Standing on the dragon, it shows spirit having mastered the chaotic first matter. The image is among the most reproduced in the whole tradition, from the *Rosarium Philosophorum* onward.

## For artists & game designers
A perfect "fusion" reward sprite: two characters merged into one crowned figure standing on the beast they overcame.

title: *Scala Philosophorum* Plate (*Geheime Figuren*, 1785)
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: After Hinricus Madathanus (Adrian von Mynsicht)
summary:
A *Scala Philosophorum* (ladder of the philosophers) plate as reproduced in the hand-coloured *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer* (Altona, 1785), here after the emblems of Hinricus Madathanus (Adrian von Mynsicht).

## Iconography
A ladder rises through an emblematic landscape, its rungs marked as the successive degrees of the work; figures climb from the base matter toward the perfected stone at the summit. Bright transparent watercolour over the engraved line is characteristic of this luxuriant compilation.

## Significance
The [[motif:ladder|scala philosophorum]] images the *opus* as a graded ascent — each rung a chemical operation and a spiritual degree — a cousin of the [[motif:alchemical mountain|mountain of the adepts]]. Its appearance in the *Geheime Figuren* shows how older emblem-series were absorbed into the great coloured Rosicrucian corpus that became the most widely reproduced body of alchemical imagery.

## For artists & game designers
A literal level-ladder: rungs as numbered stages the player climbs, each gated by an operation.

citations.text: *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer* (Altona, 1785–88), after Hinricus Madathanus.

title: *Scrutinium Chymicum* — Emblem (Michael Maier, 1687)
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Michael Maier (emblems); after Matthäus Merian
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An emblem from Michael Maier's *Scrutinium Chymicum* (Frankfurt, 1687), the later emblem-only re-issue of his celebrated *Atalanta Fugiens*.

## Iconography
A single engraved emblem pairs a motto and a Latin epigram with a figurative scene drawn from the alchemical repertoire — sun and moon, the philosophical egg, the union of king and queen — in the precise copper-plate manner of the Merian workshop.

## Significance
Where the 1618 *Atalanta Fugiens* added music (the famous fugues) to its fifty emblems, the *Scrutinium Chymicum* distilled the same images into a purely emblematic textbook of the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]]. It carried Maier's synthesis of myth, chemistry and music into the later seventeenth century and shows how a major emblem-series was repackaged for new readers.

## For artists & game designers
A clean motto-plus-picture card layout — a model for emblem cards that pair a one-line riddle with an illustration.

title: *Tabula Smaragdina* (Emerald Tablet), engr. Matthäus Merian (1618)
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Matthäus Merian (after Hermes Trismegistus tradition)
summary:
Matthäus Merian's famous 1618 engraving of the *Tabula Smaragdina*, the Emerald Tablet of Hermes Trismegistus, presented as a landscape emblem.

## Iconography
The Hermetic axioms are set within an emblematic landscape: sun and moon above, a mountain or globe at centre, the four elements and the dragon below. Latin text of the tablet frames the scene, integrating word and image so the whole doctrine can be "read" from the picture.

## Significance
The [[motif:emerald tablet|Tabula Smaragdina]] is the foundational text of Hermetic alchemy, source of the maxim *quod est superius est sicut quod est inferius* — "[[motif:as above so below|as above, so below]]." Merian's plate, made for the same Frankfurt milieu that produced Maier's *Atalanta Fugiens*, became the standard visual rendering of the tablet and circulated across Europe for centuries.

## For artists & game designers
A lore-tablet screen done right: cosmic axioms wrapped around a readable landscape of sun, moon, mountain and elements.

citations.text: *Tabula Smaragdina* (Emerald Tablet of Hermes); engraving by Matthäus Merian, 1618.

title: The Fifth Key of Basil Valentine
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Engraving after Basilius Valentinus (Wellcome Collection)
rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The fifth of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Wellcome Collection engraving.

## Iconography
Winged creatures — an eagle or winged dragon — embody the rising spirit, set against a grounded figure that holds the body fast. The emblem images the play of sublimation (ascent) and fixation (descent) by which the volatile is made to settle into the fixed.

## Significance
The alternation of [[motif:ascent and descent|ascent and descent]], *solve et coagula*, is the rhythmic engine of the Great Work. The fifth key marks the binding of spirit to body — the volatile eagle brought back down to the king — a step the alchemists regarded as the securing of the gains won in the earlier dissolutions.

## For artists & game designers
An up-down mechanic: catch the volatile bird and pin it to the body to lock in progress.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *The Twelve Keys*; Wellcome Collection M0012398.

title: The First Key of Basil Valentine (*Musaeum Hermeticum*, 1678)
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Engraving after Basilius Valentinus; *Musaeum Hermeticum* (Frankfurt)
summary:
The first of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, here in the high-resolution engraving from the 1678 *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum*.

## Iconography
A crowned king and queen stand at the centre while a grey wolf — the antimony that devours and purifies the king (gold) — is consumed by fire. Subsidiary figures of sun and moon flank the scene, and a banderole carries the key's motto. Each of the twelve keys encodes one operation of the work.

## Significance
The *Twelve Keys* (*Zwölf Schlüssel*) are among the most influential alchemical allegories of the seventeenth century, read as the sequential stages by which base metal is brought to the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]]. The wolf-and-king image is the classic emblem of purification by antimony, dramatising the alchemical maxim that the matter must "die" before it is reborn.

## For artists & game designers
A twelve-step quest template: each key is one boss-stage (wolf, dragon, eagle...) with its own motto and reward.

title: The Fourth Key of Basil Valentine
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Engraving after Basilius Valentinus (Wellcome Collection)
rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The fourth of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Wellcome Collection engraving — the great death-emblem of the series.

## Iconography
A skeleton stands or reclines amid the apparatus of the work, often on a black ground with a torch or fire nearby. The image declares the *mortificatio* — the death and blackening that the matter must undergo before it can be resurrected.

## Significance
The fourth key is the emblem of the [[motif:nigredo|nigredo]], the blackness of putrefaction in which the old form dies so a new one may arise. Carl Jung made this death-and-rebirth sequence central to his psychological reading of alchemy, but the image is rooted in centuries of laboratory allegory equating chemical decay with spiritual transformation.

## For artists & game designers
The "death gate" — a memento-mori checkpoint the player must pass through (lose something) to unlock the later stages.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *The Twelve Keys*; Wellcome Collection M0012397.

title: The Second Key of Basil Valentine
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Engraving after Basilius Valentinus (Wellcome Collection)
rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The second of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the crisp Wellcome Collection plate.

## Iconography
A winged and a wingless dragon — the volatile and fixed principles — confront one another beneath sun and moon, while Mercury (often shown with his caduceus) governs the union. The emblem represents the conjunction of opposites that must precede the work's advance.

## Significance
Where the first key purifies, the second [[motif:conjunction|conjoins]]: the marriage of the [[motif:sun and moon|sun and moon]], of fixed and volatile, of male and female mercury. The dragon-pair is one of the oldest figures of alchemical *coniunctio*, descending from Greco-Egyptian sources through the medieval Latin tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Two dragons, one winged one not, locked beneath a sun-moon arch — an instantly legible "merge two opposites" puzzle icon.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *The Twelve Keys*; Wellcome Collection M0012395.

title: The Third Key of Basil Valentine
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Engraving after Basilius Valentinus (Wellcome Collection)
rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The third of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Wellcome Collection engraving.

## Iconography
A dragon, fire and emblematic birds mark the stage of dissolution and the appearance of the many-coloured *cauda pavonis* (peacock's tail). Mottoed banderoles label the operation, and the king's matter passes through fire toward renewal.

## Significance
The third key concerns the destruction and recombination of the principles — the [[motif:putrefaction|putrefaction]] that the alchemists called the nigredo passing into the iridescent peacock's tail. The colour-sequence (black, then the rainbow, then white and red) was read as the visible proof that the work was advancing toward the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]].

## For artists & game designers
The "colour-shift" beat: a fire stage that turns a dull substance iridescent, signalling progress to the player.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *The Twelve Keys*; Wellcome Collection M0012396.

title: Rebis from the *Viatorium Spagyricum* (1625)
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Herbrandt Jamsthaler
summary:
The famous rebis engraving from Herbrandt Jamsthaler's *Viatorium Spagyricum* (Frankfurt, 1625), perhaps the single best-known image of the alchemical hermaphrodite.

## Iconography
The two-headed crowned figure stands on a winged dragon, holding a compass and a set-square in its two hands and flanked by sun and moon. The male half bears solar attributes, the female half lunar; numbered emblematic scenes surround the central figure.

## Significance
Jamsthaler's plate fixes the [[motif:rebis hermaphrodite|rebis]] standing on the [[motif:dragon|dragon]] as the canonical image of the completed *opus*: the union of opposites holding the geometer's tools that signify the measured, lawful ordering of nature. The *Viatorium Spagyricum* ("spagyric wayfaring") presents the work as a guided journey, and this figure is its destination.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive end-boss-defeated tableau: crowned dual-being on a subdued dragon, tools of mastery in hand.

title: The Four Elements, *Viridarium Chymicum* (Stolcius)
work: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: Daniel Stolcius von Stolcenberg
summary:
An emblem of the four elements from Daniel Stolcius's *Viridarium Chymicum* ("Chemical Garden," Frankfurt, 1624), a richly illustrated anthology of alchemical figures.

## Iconography
Earth, air, fire and water appear as four personified or attribute-bearing figures arranged in a balanced emblematic field, with a motto and verse keyed to the engraving. The composition presents the elemental tetrad as the substrate of all transmutation.

## Significance
The [[motif:four elements|four elements]] underlie the entire theory of the work: the alchemist separates and recombines them to produce the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]]. Stolcius's *Viridarium* gathered emblems from Maier, Mylius and others into a single "garden" of figures, and through it many otherwise scattered images reached a wide early-modern readership.

## For artists & game designers
A clean elemental-class set: four legible personifications ready to map onto an earth/air/fire/water mechanic.

title: The Antikythera Mechanism (National Archaeological Museum, Athens)
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Hellenistic Greek); photo by Joyofmuseums
place: found in the Antikythera shipwreck (now National Archaeological Museum, Athens)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Joyofmuseums). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The corroded bronze gear-train of the Antikythera mechanism, the earliest known geared astronomical computer, which modelled the heavens against a zodiac dial.

## Iconography
The surviving fragments show interlocking [[motif:bronze gears|bronze gears]] and inscribed dial plates. The front dial carried a ring of the twelve [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs against an Egyptian calendar; pointers tracked the sun, moon (with its phase) and very likely the five planets. The back dials displayed the [[motif:metonic cycle|Metonic]] 19-year cycle, the Saros [[motif:eclipse prediction|eclipse]] cycle, and the four-year Olympiad calendar.

## Significance
Recovered from a 1st-century-BCE shipwreck in 1901 and decoded over the 20th–21st centuries (Derek de Solla Price; the Antikythera Mechanism Research Project), the device proves that Hellenistic astronomers could embody their mathematical astronomy in gearwork — a sophistication not matched again until medieval astronomical clocks.

## For artists & game designers
A brass orrery with a zodiac face is the definitive 'ancient high-tech' prop — clockwork that predicts eclipses and tracks the planets.

citations.text: Derek de Solla Price, *Gears from the Greeks* (1974).
Tony Freeth et al., 'Decoding the Antikythera Mechanism', *Nature* (2006).

title: Beth Alpha synagogue zodiac mosaic with Helios
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Marianos and Hanina (mosaicists)
place: Beth Alpha synagogue, Jezreel Valley, Israel
summary:
The best-preserved of the late-antique synagogue zodiac floors, signed by the artisans Marianos and his son Hanina, placing the sun god Helios at the centre of a Jewish house of worship.

## Iconography
The central panel is a wheel: an inner medallion shows [[motif:helios|Helios]] driving his four-horse chariot across a starry ground, ringed by the twelve [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs each labelled in Hebrew, with personifications of the four seasons in the corners. Adjacent panels depict the Binding of Isaac and a Torah-shrine flanked by menorot and lions.

## Significance
The juxtaposition of a pagan solar deity with biblical scenes has driven decades of scholarship on Jewish accommodation of Greco-Roman astral imagery. Rachel Hachlili and others read the zodiac as a liturgical calendar marking the agricultural year and the priestly courses rather than as astrological worship. The floor was excavated by E. L. Sukenik in 1929.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled zodiac wheel with a charioteer hub is an instantly readable 'calendar of the heavens' motif for menus or time-of-day mechanics.

citations.text: Eleazar L. Sukenik, *The Ancient Synagogue of Beth Alpha* (1932).
Rachel Hachlili, *Ancient Synagogues — Archaeology and Art* (2013).

title: The Dendera Zodiac (Hathor Temple ceiling, Louvre)
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Ptolemaic Egypt)
place: Temple of Hathor, Dendera, Egypt (now Musée du Louvre, Paris)
summary:
A bas-relief ceiling from the Temple of Hathor at Dendera, the only complete map of the ancient sky surviving from Egypt, fusing the Egyptian decans with the imported Greek zodiac.

## Iconography
The circular planisphere is upheld by four standing goddesses and eight kneeling falcon-headed figures. Within the disc the twelve [[motif:zodiac|zodiacal]] constellations appear in their classic Greek forms (Ram, Bull, Scorpion, etc.) interwoven with native Egyptian constellations and the 36 [[motif:egyptian decans|decans]] — ten-degree star-segments that governed the hours of the night. Planets are shown as deities in their positions of exaltation.

## Significance
Carved around 50 BCE, the relief documents the moment when Babylonian-Greek astrology was absorbed into Egyptian temple religion. It was removed from the temple in 1821 and is now in the Louvre. Jean-Baptiste Biot and later Otto Neugebauer used the planetary configuration to argue a 1st-century-BCE date; the eclipse and planetary positions remain a touchstone of *Hellenistic astronomy* studies.

## For artists & game designers
The upheld star-disc is a perfect 'cosmic map' prop — a circular relief that doubles as a navigable star chart.

citations.text: Otto Neugebauer & Richard Parker, *Egyptian Astronomical Texts* (1960–69).

title: Farnese Atlas — celestial globe (Naples)
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman copy after a Hellenistic original); photo by Lalupa
place: Roman copy (now Museo Archeologico Nazionale, Naples)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Lalupa). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Roman marble statue of the Titan Atlas stooping under a celestial globe — the oldest surviving depiction of the classical Greek constellations as a set.

## Iconography
The kneeling [[motif:atlas|Atlas]] bears on his shoulders a sphere carved in relief with some 41 [[motif:constellations|constellations]] as their mythological figures, together with the celestial circles — equator, tropics, [[motif:ecliptic|ecliptic]] band of the [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]], and colures. The stars themselves are not marked; the globe shows the constellation figures and the coordinate grid.

## Significance
Bradley Schaefer (2005) argued that the constellation positions encode the star catalogue of Hipparchus (c. 129 BCE), making the globe a witness to lost Hellenistic astronomy — a claim that remains contested. Either way the Farnese Atlas is the principal visual source for the pre-Ptolemaic Greek sky and the canonical 48 constellations.

## For artists & game designers
A figure straining beneath a carved star-globe is the archetypal 'world-bearer' prop and a literal, sculptable celestial sphere.

citations.text: Bradley E. Schaefer, 'The Epoch of the Constellations on the Farnese Atlas', *Journal for the History of Astronomy* (2005).

title: The Grand Camée de France (Great Cameo of France)
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman, Julio-Claudian); photo by Carole Raddato
place: Roman Empire (now Cabinet des Médailles, BnF, Paris)
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (photo: Carole Raddato). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The largest cameo to survive from antiquity, a five-layered sardonyx whose three registers stage Julio-Claudian dynastic power as a celestial ascent.

## Iconography
The three tiers move from earth to heaven: captives below, the enthroned imperial family in the centre, and in the upper register the [[motif:apotheosis|deified]] Augustus borne aloft amid winged and astral figures — an image of [[motif:astral kingship|astral kingship]] in which the dead emperor joins the stars. The carving exploits the layered sardonyx for tonal contrast between figures and ground.

## Significance
Dated about 23 CE, the *Gemma Tiberiana* embodies the Roman ideology of *catasterism* — the ruler translated to the heavens — that underlies imperial astral propaganda from Caesar's comet onward. It passed through the Sainte-Chapelle treasury and is now in the BnF. The dynastic identifications are debated, but the cosmic-apotheosis programme is clear.

## For artists & game designers
A three-tier composition (underworld / mortal / celestial) on a single carved gem is a compact way to show a vertical cosmos and a soul's ascent to the stars.

citations.text: Wolf-Rüdiger Megow, *Kameen von Augustus bis Alexander Severus* (1987).

title: Hammat Tiberias synagogue zodiac mosaic with Helios
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman-Byzantine Palestine)
place: Hammat Tiberias synagogue, Sea of Galilee, Israel
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Praisethelorne). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The earliest and most finely executed of the Galilean synagogue zodiac floors, where Helios appears in full classical style at the hub of the celestial wheel.

## Iconography
The mosaic centres on [[motif:helios|Helios]]-Sol crowned with a solar nimbus and rays, holding the celestial globe and whip, riding his quadriga. Around him run the twelve [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs with Hebrew names, and the four seasons occupy the spandrels as winged busts. The naturalistic modelling reflects a high-quality urban workshop.

## Significance
Dated to the 4th century, the floor belongs to a synagogue associated with the patriarchal house at Tiberias. Its sophistication argues that the zodiac scheme entered synagogue art at the top of the social scale. Scholars such as Lee Levine and Zeev Weiss treat the Helios-zodiac as a cosmological emblem of divine order and the calendar rather than literal sun worship.

## For artists & game designers
The radiant charioteer with globe and whip is a compact icon of 'the sun as ruler of time' — useful for solar-themed UI or deities.

citations.text: Lee I. Levine, *Visual Judaism in Late Antiquity* (2012).

title: Marino Mithraeum fresco of the tauroctony
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman Empire)
summary:
One of the few painted tauroctonies to survive in situ, the fresco of the Marino Mithraeum preserves the colour and narrative side-panels lost on stone reliefs.

## Iconography
The back wall of the cave-sanctuary shows [[motif:mithras|Mithras]] in red cloak and Phrygian cap performing the [[motif:tauroctony|bull-slaying]], with the dog, serpent and scorpion, Sol and Luna above, and the torchbearers [[motif:cautes|Cautes]] and Cautopates. Small lateral scenes narrate the cult myth — Mithras born from rock, hunting and feasting with Sol — that fuller monuments only hint at.

## Significance
Discovered in 1962, the Marino sanctuary is prized because painted Mithraea are rare; the colours and the framing narrative episodes give a sense of how initiates experienced the *spelaeum* (cave) as a model of the cosmos. It complements the sculpted tauroctonies that dominate the corpus.

## For artists & game designers
The cave-temple with a painted cosmic scene and side-panel 'myth comics' is a ready environment design for an underground cult shrine.

citations.text: Manfred Clauss, *The Roman Cult of Mithras* (2000).

title: Mithraic leontocephaline (Chronos-Aion) — 19th-c. drawing of the Vienne relief
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Drawing after the Roman relief (perhaps Félix Lajard, 19th c.)
date: Roman original 2nd–3rd c. CE; drawing 19th c.
place: relief from Vienne, France (Musée Saint-Pierre); drawing 19th c.
summary:
A 19th-century engraving recording a Mithraic relief of the lion-headed god — the leontocephaline, identified with Aion or Zurvan, the deity of cosmic time — from Vienne in Gaul.

## Iconography
The winged, [[motif:lion-headed god|lion-headed]] figure stands frontally, entwined from foot to head by a [[motif:serpent coils|serpent]] whose coils mark the path of the sun. He holds [[motif:keys|keys]] and a sceptre, and his body or surrounding frame carries the signs of the [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]]; the wings often bear the four seasons. The serpent's spiralling course evokes the ecliptic and the turning year.

## Significance
The leontocephaline is the most enigmatic Mithraic image. Franz Cumont linked it to Zurvan/[[motif:aion|Aion]], boundless Time; later scholars (R. L. Gordon, Howard Jackson) debate whether it personifies Time, the cosmos, or a guardian of the gates of the heavens. The keys associate him with control over the soul's passage through the planetary spheres.

## For artists & game designers
A serpent-wound, lion-headed, key-bearing 'lord of time' is a complete boss or gatekeeper design encoding the zodiac on its very body.

citations.text: Franz Cumont, *Textes et monuments figurés relatifs aux mystères de Mithra* (1896–99).
Howard M. Jackson, 'The Meaning and Function of the Leontocephaline', *Numen* (1985).

title: Bronze plaque of Mithras slaying the bull (tauroctony)
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman Empire)
place: Roman Empire (now Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York)
summary:
A bronze cult plaque of the tauroctony — Mithras killing the cosmic bull — the central icon of the Roman mystery cult and an encoded star map.

## Iconography
[[motif:mithras|Mithras]], in Phrygian cap and billowing cloak, kneels on the [[motif:bull-slaying|bull]] and plunges a dagger into its neck. A [[motif:dog|dog]] and [[motif:serpent|serpent]] lap the blood, a [[motif:scorpion|scorpion]] grips the bull's genitals, a raven perches above, and the twin torchbearers Cautes and Cautopates flank the scene; the bust of Sol and Luna occupy the upper corners.

## Significance
Since Karl Stark (1869) and especially David Ulansey's *Origins of the Mithraic Mysteries* (1989), the tauroctony has been read as a celestial diagram: bull = Taurus, dog = Canis, serpent = Hydra, scorpion = Scorpius, raven = Corvus — the constellations along the celestial equator at a former spring equinox, with Mithras as the power that moves the cosmos (the precession of the equinoxes). The reading remains debated but dominant.

## For artists & game designers
Every element is a constellation cipher — a template for designing 'a scene that is secretly a star map' that rewards the player who decodes it.

citations.text: David Ulansey, *The Origins of the Mithraic Mysteries* (1989).
Manfred Clauss, *The Roman Cult of Mithras* (2000).

title: Walbrook Mithraeum marble tauroctony with zodiac border
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman Britain); photograph by Carole Raddato
place: Walbrook Mithraeum, Londinium (now Museum of London)
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (photo: Carole Raddato). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A white-marble tauroctony from the London Walbrook Mithraeum (CIMRM 810-811) whose roundel is encircled by a band of the twelve zodiac signs.

## Iconography
The central medallion holds the standard [[motif:tauroctony|tauroctony]]: [[motif:mithras|Mithras]] slaying the [[motif:bull-slaying|bull]] with dog, serpent and scorpion, flanked by Cautes and Cautopates and the busts of Sol and Luna. Running around the medallion is a continuous ring of the twelve [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs, making explicit the cosmological reading that is only implicit in plainer tauroctonies.

## Significance
Catalogued in Vermaseren's *Corpus Inscriptionum et Monumentorum Religionis Mithriacae* (CIMRM), the relief was found in the 1954 excavation of the Temple of Mithras beside the Walbrook stream. The encircling zodiac frames the bull-slaying as an event at the centre of the turning heavens, supporting astronomical interpretations of the cult.

## For artists & game designers
A central action-scene wrapped in a zodiac ring is a clean composition for a 'cosmic event at the hub of the wheel of the year'.

citations.text: M. J. Vermaseren, *Corpus Inscriptionum et Monumentorum Religionis Mithriacae* (1956–60).
John Shepherd, *The Temple of Mithras, London* (1998).

title: Na'aran synagogue zodiac mosaic
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman-Byzantine Palestine)
place: Na'aran synagogue, near Jericho, West Bank
rights: CC BY 4.0 (photo: Bukvoed). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The damaged zodiac floor of the Na'aran synagogue near Jericho, whose figural imagery was systematically defaced in antiquity.

## Iconography
The surviving outlines show the familiar wheel of twelve [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs around a central [[motif:helios|Helios]] medallion, with seasons in the corners and Hebrew labels. Crucially, the animal and human figures have been picked out and removed by later hands while the Hebrew inscriptions were left intact — a vivid record of an iconoclastic episode within the Jewish community.

## Significance
Na'aran (biblical Naarah) demonstrates both the wide diffusion of the Helios-zodiac scheme and the later ambivalence toward figural art. The deliberate sparing of text over image is often cited in discussions of Jewish (and parallel Byzantine) iconoclasm. The site was first uncovered during World War I when a shell exposed the mosaic.

## For artists & game designers
The deliberately effaced figures are a striking 'censored relic' aesthetic — a once-rich image reduced to ghosted outlines and surviving captions.

title: Temple of Bel, Palmyra — planetary/astral ceiling of the northern thalamos
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Palmyrene); photo by Gerard Degeorge
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Gerard Degeorge). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The carved stone ceiling of the northern shrine (thalamos) of the Temple of Bel at Palmyra, a rare surviving image of the heavens as a coffered cosmic dome.

## Iconography
The ceiling is organized as concentric bands within a square: a central rosette ringed by the busts of the [[motif:planetary deities|seven planetary]] gods, enclosed in turn by a band of the twelve [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs. The scheme presents the temple roof as a model of the cosmos, with the planet-gods and zodiac arrayed around the sky's centre above the cult image of [[motif:bel|Bel]].

## Significance
Dating to the early 1st century CE, the Bel ceiling is one of the clearest Near-Eastern statements of the seven-planets-plus-zodiac cosmology, blending Babylonian astral religion with Greco-Roman form. The temple was largely destroyed by ISIS in 2015, making such photographs important records. The astral programme is discussed by Henri Seyrig and later Palmyrene scholarship.

## For artists & game designers
A coffered ceiling with a planet-and-zodiac ring is a template for a 'cosmic dome' interior — the sky rendered as architecture overhead.

title: Roman relief — attendant of Mithras with signs of the zodiac (Walters)
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman Empire)
place: Roman Empire (now Walters Art Museum, Baltimore)
summary:
A Roman marble relief of a Mithraic torchbearer surrounded by the signs of the zodiac, linking the cult's attendant figures to the cosmic frame.

## Iconography
A standing [[motif:torchbearer|torchbearer]] — one of the twin attendants [[motif:cautes|Cautes]] or Cautopates who flank Mithras — is shown amid carved [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs. The torchbearers personify the rising and setting sun (the equinoxes), so their pairing with the zodiacal band reinforces the astral symbolism that pervades Mithraic art.

## Significance
Held by the Walters Art Museum, the relief illustrates how thoroughly the zodiac saturated Mithraic iconography beyond the central tauroctony. Cautes (torch up) and Cautopates (torch down) embody the ascending and descending courses of the sun across the ecliptic, a reading developed in the cosmological studies of Roger Beck and others.

## For artists & game designers
A single attendant figure framed by zodiac signs is an economical way to signal 'this minor character belongs to a cosmic order'.

citations.text: Roger Beck, *The Religion of the Mithras Cult in the Roman Empire* (2006).

title: Astronomical ceiling, Tomb of Senenmut (facsimile, MET)
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Charles Wilkinson (facsimile) after the New Kingdom original
date: original c. 1479–1458 BCE (18th Dynasty); facsimile 20th c.
place: Tomb of Senenmut (TT353), Deir el-Bahari, Egypt; facsimile at the Met
summary:
A Metropolitan Museum facsimile of the earliest known star map ceiling, from the tomb of Senenmut, steward of Queen Hatshepsut.

## Iconography
The ceiling is divided into a southern and northern panel. The southern half charts the [[motif:decans|decans]] and the deities of the planets and of [[motif:sah orion|Sah]] (Orion) and Sopdet (Sirius); the northern half shows the circumpolar constellations grouped around a central bull's-leg (Meskhetiu, the Great Bear) and a grid of the lunar months. The figures double as a star-clock for the night hours.

## Significance
Dated to the early 15th century BCE, this is the oldest surviving Egyptian astronomical ceiling and a foundational document of [[motif:egyptian astronomy|Egyptian sky-lore]]. Neugebauer and Parker analysed its decanal lists; it predates the Greek zodiac and shows the indigenous Egyptian framework that the Dendera zodiac would later fuse with Hellenistic signs.

## For artists & game designers
A grid of star-deities and a bull's-leg pole make an authentically pre-zodiacal 'sky table' — useful when you want a star map that feels older than the familiar twelve signs.

citations.text: Otto Neugebauer & Richard Parker, *Egyptian Astronomical Texts* III (1969).

title: Sepphoris (Tzippori) synagogue zodiac mosaic
work: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: Anonymous (Roman-Byzantine Palestine)
summary:
The zodiac band from the synagogue floor at Sepphoris, unusual for replacing the figure of Helios with the sun-disc and chariot alone.

## Iconography
The wheel preserves the twelve [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs, each named in both Hebrew and the corresponding month, with the four seasons in the corners. At the centre, rather than an anthropomorphic [[motif:helios|Helios]], the mosaicists set the radiant sun-disc and quadriga without a driver — a notable aniconic adaptation. The signs are paired with their Hebrew month names, reinforcing a calendrical reading.

## Significance
Excavated by Zeev Weiss and Ehud Netzer in the 1990s, the Sepphoris floor is the most richly programmatic of the synagogue mosaics, integrating the zodiac into a larger cycle of biblical and Temple imagery. The omission of Helios' body is read as a deliberate softening of the pagan model while keeping its cosmic-calendar function.

## For artists & game designers
The driverless solar chariot shows how to keep a recognizable sun-icon while avoiding a deity figure — handy for abstract or aniconic design constraints.

title: Distillation Apparatus of Zosimos of Panopolis
work: Greek Alchemy — Apparatus & Diagrams
creator: after Zosimos of Panopolis
summary:
A diagram of the distillation apparatus described by **Zosimos of Panopolis** (fl. c. 300 CE), the earliest alchemical author whose works survive in substantial quantity. Zosimos' treatises — collectively the *Cheirokmeta* ("things made by hand"), one part subtitled *On Apparatus and Furnaces* — are preserved through Byzantine manuscript copies, above all the great alchemical codex **Marcianus graecus 299** in Venice, the same manuscript that transmits the [[motif:ouroboros]] of Kleopatra.

## Iconography
The plate shows the working instruments of the Greek alchemists: the **alembic** (*ambix*) with its still-head and beak, the **tribikos** — a three-armed still for collecting distillate, whose invention Zosimos credits to **Maria the Jewess** (Maria Prophetissa) — and the **kerotakis**, a sealed vessel for exposing metals to corrosive vapours. Furnaces (*kerotakis* heat sources) sit beneath. These are not allegory but engineering: the apparatus by which sublimation, distillation, and "divine water" (*theion hydor*) were actually produced.

## Significance
Zosimos fused practical chemistry with Gnostic and Hermetic spirituality, reading the torments of metals in the furnace as a drama of death and rebirth — his famous *Visions* describe a priest dismembered and recomposed on an altar-shaped apparatus. He thus stands at the head of both lineages this archive follows: the **technical** ([[motif:furnace]], [[motif:vessel]], distillation) and the **symbolic** (the spiritualisation of matter). Maria the Jewess, credited here, is the earliest named woman in the history of the exact sciences; the *bain-marie* (water-bath) still bears her name.

## For artists & game designers
Authentic ancient laboratory glassware and furnaces — alembics, three-armed stills, sealed vessels over fire. Ideal reference for a Hellenistic or proto-scientific lab, an apothecary, or a "first alchemist" setting.

[PLACEHOLDER: confirm which manuscript/edition this particular redrawing derives from (Berthelot's *Collection des anciens alchimistes grecs*, 1887–88, is the standard source of such figures).]

citations.text: M. Berthelot & C.-É. Ruelle, *Collection des anciens alchimistes grecs* (Paris, 1887–1888).
F. S. Taylor, "The Origins of Greek Alchemy", *Ambix* 1 (1937), 30–47.

title: Ouroboros of the Chrysopoeia of Kleopatra
work: The Ouroboros — Earliest Witnesses
creator: attr. Kleopatra the Alchemist
rights: Public domain (PD Mark 1.0). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
This is, in effect, where the visual history of alchemy begins. The drawing of a serpent devouring its own tail, ringed with the words **ἓν τὸ πᾶν** (*hen to pan*, "the All is One"), comes from the *Chrysopoeia* ("gold-making") attributed to **Kleopatra the Alchemist**, one of the few named women of the Hellenistic alchemical tradition. The text was probably composed in the first centuries CE; the image survives on a single leaf of a 10th–11th-century Byzantine manuscript, **Biblioteca Marciana, MS gr. Z. 299**, in Venice.

## Why it matters
The ouroboros here is the earliest surviving alchemical diagram of its kind — the seed from which the entire later iconography of the [[motif:ouroboros]] grows, by way of [[work:atalanta_fugiens]] Emblem XIV and a thousand other plates. The motto *hen to pan* states the doctrine the whole tradition turns on: the unity of all matter, the circularity of the Work, the identity of beginning and end.

## For artists & game designers
The ur-ouroboros: a two-tone (light/dark) serpent-ring with Greek lettering. Unbeatable reference for an "ancient sigil", a loading-glyph, a cyclical-time mechanic, or the oldest-artifact tier of an esoteric set.

[PLACEHOLDER: a higher-resolution capture direct from the Marciana digitisation would improve print use.]

title: Ouroboros of Theodoros Pelekanos (1478)
work: The Ouroboros — Earliest Witnesses
creator: Theodoros Pelekanos (copyist)
summary:
The famous **coloured ouroboros** drawn by the scribe **Theodoros Pelekanos** in 1478, in a copy of an older alchemical treatise (the *Synosius* attributed to the circle of Zosimos), now Codex Parisinus graecus 2327. The serpent is rendered half light, half dark, encircling the same motto, **ἓν τὸ πᾶν**.

## Why it matters
This is the image most people picture when they think "alchemical ouroboros" — a direct descendant of the [[creator:michael-maier]]-era emblem tradition's ultimate source. Its two-tone body makes explicit what the [[motif:ouroboros]] encodes: the union of opposites (volatile/fixed, spirit/body, day/night) in a single self-renewing circle.

## For artists & game designers
A clean, symmetrical, two-tone serpent-ring on parchment — arguably the single most reusable occult emblem in existence. Drops straight into a sigil, a faction crest, a time-loop icon, or a crafting-circle UI.

title: The Apocalypse Tapestry of Angers
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Designed by Jean Bondol; woven by Nicolas Bataille's workshop
rights: CC BY 2.0. Photo Jean-Pierre Dalbéra. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A panel of the *Apocalypse Tapestry* of Angers, the largest surviving medieval tapestry set, illustrating the visions of the Book of Revelation.

## Iconography
In the tapestry's signature format, [[motif:john of patmos|St John]] watches each vision from a small architectural niche at the side while the main field stages the scene — here among the cycle's monsters: the seven-headed dragon, the beasts from sea and earth, and the angels of judgement, woven in red, blue, and gold wool across vast registers.

## Significance
Commissioned c. 1375 by Louis I, Duke of Anjou, designed by the court painter Jean Bondol (Hennequin de Bruges) after illuminated Apocalypse manuscripts, and woven in Paris by Nicolas Bataille's workshop (1377–82), the set originally ran some 140 metres. It is the supreme monumental rendering of Revelation and a landmark of Gothic narrative art, preserved at the Château d'Angers.

## For artists & game designers
The 'witness in a niche beside the action' device is an elegant way to embed a point-of-view observer in every scene of a narrative cycle.

title: The Bamberg Apocalypse (miniature, after the Ottonian manuscript)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Reichenau school (Ottonian); reproduced in a 1893 history of art
rights: No known restrictions. Via Wikimedia Commons (Internet Archive Book Images).
summary:
A reproduction (from an 1893 *History of Medieval Art*) of a miniature from the *Bamberg Apocalypse*, the great Ottonian illustrated Revelation.

## Iconography
The image preserves the monumental, gold-ground manner of the [[motif:ottonian|Ottonian]] Reichenau school: large hieratic figures, deep saturated grounds, and a stately presentation of the apocalyptic vision, with the seer and the heavenly powers set against shimmering fields rather than landscape.

## Significance
The *Bamberg Apocalypse* (Bamberg, Staatsbibliothek, Msc. Bibl. 140) was produced around 1000–1020 at the island monastery of Reichenau, probably for Emperor Otto III or Henry II, and is among the masterpieces of Ottonian book painting and a UNESCO 'Memory of the World' manuscript. Its grand, abstracting style gives Revelation an imperial, liturgical solemnity distinct from later narrative cycles.

## For artists & game designers
The Ottonian preference for gold voids over landscape is a strong stylistic choice for scenes meant to feel timeless and sacred rather than located.

citations.text: Gude Suckale-Redlefsen & Bernhard Schemmel, *Die Bamberger Apokalypse* (2000).

title: The Number of the Beast is 666 (The Great Red Dragon)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: William Blake
rights: Public domain. Rosenbach Museum & Library. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
William Blake's watercolour *The Number of the Beast is 666*, from his series illustrating the Book of Revelation, showing the [[motif:number of the beast|number of the beast]] and the apocalyptic beasts.

## Iconography
The great seven-headed beast rises from the sea while the two-horned beast of the earth gestures below the inscription of the [[motif:666|number 666]]; worshippers gather before the monsters. Blake's flame-like, muscular figures and visionary colour transform the grotesque into the sublime, part of the same cycle as his celebrated *Great Red Dragon* watercolours.

## Significance
Blake produced some eighty biblical watercolours c. 1800–1805 for his patron Thomas Butts, including a sequence on Revelation. As a visionary who claimed direct converse with angels and prophets, he read the Apocalypse less as future history than as a present spiritual drama — the war of imaginative energy against tyranny. His apocalyptic beasts are among the most powerful Romantic images of Revelation, later made famous through the 'Great Red Dragon' series.

## For artists & game designers
Blake shows how to make a monster *sublime* rather than merely ugly — muscular, flame-lit, and awe-inspiring rather than grotesque.

citations.text: Martin Butlin, *The Paintings and Drawings of William Blake* (1981).

title: The Cloisters Apocalypse — John on the Island of Patmos (f. 3r)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Anonymous (Norman workshop)
rights: Public domain. The Metropolitan Museum of Art (The Cloisters). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The opening illustration (f.3r) of the *Cloisters Apocalypse*, showing John exiled on the island of Patmos at the start of his visions.

## Iconography
[[motif:john of patmos|St John]] is shown on the rocky island of Patmos, set apart by the sea, at the moment he is 'in the Spirit on the Lord's day' and hears the great voice. The elegant Gothic figure-style, delicate architecture, and tinted drawing typify the deluxe French Apocalypse manuscripts of the early 14th century.

## Significance
The *Cloisters Apocalypse* (Metropolitan Museum, The Cloisters, MS 68.174), made in Normandy c. 1330, belongs to the rich family of illustrated Anglo-French Apocalypses that flourished after c. 1250. These books paired Revelation's text with a continuous picture-cycle for aristocratic devotional reading, making the seer's exile and visions vividly present.

## For artists & game designers
The island-of-exile establishing shot — a lone seer set apart by water before the visions begin — is a clean way to frame a 'received prophecy' prologue.

citations.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, The Cloisters, MS 68.174.

title: The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Albrecht Dürer's *The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse*, the most famous sheet of his *Apocalypse* woodcut series, depicting Revelation 6's riders unleashed at the opening of the first four seals.

## Iconography
Four horsemen charge in a compressed diagonal sweep: the crowned bowman (Conquest), the swordsman (War), the rider with the merchant's scales (Famine), and gaunt Death on a sickly nag, beneath whom a hellmouth devours an emperor and the trampled multitude. An angel hovers above the surge of figures.

## Significance
Dürer's *Apocalypse* (published 1498 in Latin and German) was the first book ever produced and published by an artist himself, and it transformed the [[motif:apocalypse|apocalyptic]] visual tradition. The compression of the four riders into a single thundering rank — earlier cycles spaced them across separate scenes — became the definitive image of the Horsemen and shaped doom imagery for centuries amid the millennial anxieties around the year 1500.

## For artists & game designers
The template for an 'all four at once' charging-cavalry composition; each rider is individuated by a single attribute (bow, sword, scales, scythe) for instant legibility.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: St John Devouring the Book
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Dürer's *St John Devouring the Book* from the *Apocalypse* series, illustrating Revelation 10 — the seer eating the angel's little scroll.

## Iconography
A colossal angel, 'clothed with a cloud,' with a rainbow upon his head, a face like the sun, and feet as pillars of fire, descends with one foot on the sea and one on the land, holding open a little book. [[motif:john of patmos|St John]] kneels and takes the book to eat it — 'sweet as honey' in his mouth, bitter in his belly — while a hand gives him the measuring reed.

## Significance
The eating of the scroll (Rev. 10:8–11) is the prophet's commissioning to 'prophesy again,' echoing Ezekiel's scroll-eating. Dürer renders the overwhelming scale of the cloud-angel with dramatic foreshortening, a tour de force of the woodcut medium that makes the act of consuming divine revelation physically vivid.

## For artists & game designers
A giant celestial NPC straddling sea and land, dwarfing the human witness — an effective scale-contrast for a revelation/quest-giving beat.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: St Michael Fighting the Dragon
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Dürer's *St Michael Fighting the Dragon* from the *Apocalypse* series, illustrating the War in Heaven of Revelation 12.

## Iconography
The archangel Michael, flanked by angelic comrades, drives his long spear down into the writhing, multi-limbed [[motif:dragon|dragon]] amid a tangle of falling demonic beasts. Below the celestial battle, a serene, finely observed landscape with a town and broad river stretches in luminous calm — a deliberate contrast between cosmic violence above and earthly peace below.

## Significance
Revelation 12:7–9 describes Michael and his angels casting down 'the great dragon... that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan.' Dürer fuses the late-medieval iconography of the dragon-slaying archangel with his new naturalism; the radiant landscape beneath the fray is one of the earliest such passages in printmaking and exemplifies how the *Apocalypse* married visionary subject to Renaissance observation.

## For artists & game designers
A masterclass in staging an aerial boss fight: chaotic massed combat above a tranquil establishing landscape grounds the supernatural scale.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: The Beast with Two Horns Like a Lamb
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Albrecht Dürer
rights: CC0 / Public domain. National Gallery of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Dürer's *The Beast with Two Horns Like a Lamb* from the *Apocalypse* series, illustrating the second beast — the false prophet — of Revelation 13.

## Iconography
The two-horned [[motif:beast from the earth|beast from the earth]] commands the people to worship the first, seven-headed beast (visible at the upper left), while fire falls from heaven at its bidding and martyrs are slain. The composition pairs the deceiving 'lamb-horned' beast that 'spake as a dragon' with the great wounded beast it serves.

## Significance
Revelation 13:11–18 describes a second beast 'coming up out of the earth' with 'two horns like a lamb' who works deceiving signs and enforces the mark and [[motif:number of the beast|number of the beast]], 666. Later identified with the false prophet and the Antichrist's herald, the two-horned beast supplied a key symbol for apocalyptic exegesis. Dürer's plate distils the deception-and-coerced-worship theme.

## For artists & game designers
The 'false lamb' is a deceiver archetype — a meek-seeming herald enforcing the true monster's will, useful for a cult-enforcer NPC.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: The Opening of the Seventh Seal (The Trumpet Angels)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
A leaf of Dürer's *Apocalypse* (Houghton Library copy) showing the opening of the seventh seal and the seven angels with trumpets of Revelation 8.

## Iconography
After the [[motif:seventh seal|seventh seal]] is opened and 'there was silence in heaven,' seven angels receive their trumpets; an angel casts a censer of fire to earth, and at the first blasts hail and fire mingled with blood fall while a third of creation is struck. An eagle flies crying 'Woe, woe, woe' to the inhabitants of the earth.

## Significance
Revelation 8 marks the hinge between the seals and the trumpet-plagues, a cascade of cosmic catastrophe. Dürer's dense, swirling design conveys the escalating violence of the seven trumpets, integrating the censer-angel, the falling fire, and the woe-crying eagle into one charged sheet of his transformative woodcut cycle.

## For artists & game designers
A reference for staging escalating disaster: stacked celestial events (silence → trumpets → falling fire) read as successive 'waves' of an apocalypse sequence.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: The Whore of Babylon
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Dürer's *The Whore of Babylon* from the *Apocalypse* series, illustrating Revelation 17 — the great harlot enthroned on the seven-headed beast.

## Iconography
The richly dressed [[motif:whore of babylon|Whore of Babylon]] rides the scarlet seven-headed, ten-horned beast, raising her golden cup 'full of abominations.' A crowd of kings and worldly powers gazes up at her, while an angel above shows St John the destruction of the great city as fire consumes it in the background. Dürer reputedly modelled her costume on a Venetian courtesan he had sketched.

## Significance
The harlot 'with whom the kings of the earth have committed fornication' (Rev. 17:2) was, in Reformation polemic that soon followed, read as a figure for corrupt worldly power and (in Protestant use) for Rome. Dürer's image fixed the visual type of the cup-bearing woman on the [[motif:seven-headed beast|seven-headed beast]] for the whole early-modern period.

## For artists & game designers
A seductive antagonist mounted on a many-headed monster — pair an alluring figure with a grotesque beast to externalise corruption.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: The Woman Clothed with the Sun and the Seven-Headed Dragon
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Dürer's *The Woman Clothed with the Sun*, illustrating Revelation 12 — the celestial woman menaced by the great red dragon.

## Iconography
The [[motif:woman clothed with the sun|Woman]] stands radiant, 'clothed with the sun, and the moon under her feet, and upon her head a crown of twelve stars,' her newborn man-child caught up to God's throne above. Below, the seven-headed, ten-horned dragon sweeps a third of the stars down with its tail and waits to devour the child; in the upper register God receives the infant while angels look on.

## Significance
The sun-clothed woman of Revelation 12 was read both as the Church persecuted by the dragon and, in Marian devotion, as the Virgin — the source of the *Immaculata* / Apocalyptic Madonna iconography on the crescent moon. Dürer's compact design joins the cosmic-woman motif to the [[motif:dragon|seven-headed dragon]], one of the most enduring of all apocalyptic pairings.

## For artists & game designers
A luminous protagonist over a coiling many-headed dragon — a built-in vertical composition of light above, monster below.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: The Flood and Noah's Ark (Gerona Beatus)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Emeterius and the nun Ende (En/Ende)
summary:
A folio from the *Gerona Beatus* (975), one of the great illuminated copies of Beatus of Liébana's *Commentary on the Apocalypse*, here showing Noah's Ark and the Flood.

## Iconography
The Ark rides a register of waves choked with the drowned and the swimming dead; Noah leans from a window to receive the returning dove (and the wandering raven), the animals housed within. The flat gold and vivid bands of [[motif:mozarabic|Mozarabic]] colour, and the schematic, frontal figures are hallmarks of the Spanish Beatus style.

## Significance
Beatus of Liébana compiled his *Commentary on the Apocalypse* c. 776 in northern Spain; its richly illustrated copies are the supreme monuments of Mozarabic art. The Gerona codex was illuminated in 975 by the painter Emeterius and the nun **Ende** (*En depintrix*), one of the earliest named women artists in Europe. The Flood here prefigures the apocalyptic deluge of judgement that the commentary expounds.

## For artists & game designers
Mozarabic palette and flat banded grounds offer a distinctive non-naturalistic visual key for a 'sacred manuscript' aesthetic.

citations.text: Beatus of Liébana, *Commentary on the Apocalypse* (Gerona codex, 975).

title: The Last Judgement (Scrovegni Chapel)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Giotto di Bondone
summary:
Giotto's *Last Judgement* on the west wall of the Scrovegni (Arena) Chapel in Padua, c. 1306 — the climactic vision toward which the whole fresco cycle leads.

## Iconography
Christ in Majesty sits within a great mandorla amid the apostles, raising the saved on his right and condemning the damned on his left. At the lower right a blue, monstrous [[motif:satan|Satan]] devours and excretes sinners amid the rivers of Hell, where demons torment the naked damned. The donor Enrico Scrovegni offers a model of the chapel to the Virgin below the Cross.

## Significance
Giotto's fresco is a pivotal reworking of the Byzantine and medieval Doom into the new naturalism of the early Trecento. The huge, swallowing Satan and the orderly hierarchy of the blessed give Revelation's judgement monumental form; the chapel was built in part to expiate the sin of usury, making the scene of reckoning intensely personal for its patron.

## For artists & game designers
The colossal soul-devouring Satan at the base of a vertical judgement composition is the archetypal 'pit boss' anchoring a moral hierarchy.

citations.text: Anne Derbes & Mark Sandona, *The Usurer's Heart: Giotto, Enrico Scrovegni, and the Arena Chapel* (2008).

title: Hell (Hortus Deliciarum)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: After Herrad of Landsberg
summary:
A plate of *Hell* from the *Hortus Deliciarum* of Herrad of Landsberg, surviving through 19th-century tracings after the manuscript destroyed in 1870.

## Iconography
Hell is shown as a teeming pit of torment: registers of the damned — kings, clergy, usurers, the lustful — are bound, boiled, and tormented by horned [[motif:demons|demons]], with Satan enthroned among the flames and the [[motif:hellmouth|jaws of the abyss]] gaping. Each sinner is punished according to his offence in a vivid catalogue of damnation.

## Significance
The *Hortus Deliciarum* ('Garden of Delights') was an illustrated encyclopaedia compiled c. 1180 by Abbess **Herrad of Landsberg** for the nuns of Hohenburg — one of the earliest known works authored and illustrated by a woman. Its Hell, with the structured torments and the devouring abyss, is a key medieval witness to the apocalyptic geography of damnation. The original burned in the siege of Strasbourg (1870); the imagery survives only through earlier copies.

## For artists & game designers
A tiered catalogue of sin-specific torments is a ready bestiary/level structure — each circle keyed to a vice and its matching punishment.

citations.text: Rosalie Green et al., *Herrad of Hohenbourg: Hortus Deliciarum*, reconstruction (1979).

title: St John on the Island of Patmos
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Anonymous (Spanish Baroque)
summary:
A Spanish Baroque painting of *St John on the Island of Patmos* from the Convent of Santa Clara, Montilla, depicting the seer receiving the Apocalypse.

## Iconography
[[motif:john of patmos|St John the Evangelist]], a book and pen at hand, sits in visionary reverie on the rocky shore of Patmos, his attribute the eagle nearby. He gazes upward toward the heavenly vision he is commanded to write down — the standard Baroque formula for the inspired author of Revelation, exiled and rapt 'in the Spirit.'

## Significance
The iconography of John writing his Revelation on Patmos, often with the Woman Clothed with the Sun or the apocalyptic heaven appearing above, was hugely popular in Counter-Reformation Spain, where it affirmed the authority of inspired Scripture. Such convent paintings made the visionary moment of the Apocalypse an object of devotional contemplation for enclosed religious communities.

## For artists & game designers
The inspired-scribe pose — pen poised, eyes lifted to an unseen vision — is the classic way to depict a character receiving prophecy.

citations.text: Émile Mâle, *L'art religieux après le Concile de Trente* (1932).

title: The Adoration of the Lamb (and the Hymn of the Chosen)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Albrecht Dürer
rights: CC0 / Public domain. National Gallery of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Dürer's *The Adoration of the Lamb* from the *Apocalypse* series, illustrating the heavenly worship around the throne (Revelation 5 and 7).

## Iconography
The slain yet living [[motif:lamb of god|Lamb]] stands on the book sealed with seven seals at the throne of God; the four-and-twenty elders cast down their crowns, the four living creatures and angels surround the throne, and the sealed multitude of the redeemed adore. Above, the censing angels and the elders' golden vials of incense rise as prayer.

## Significance
Revelation 5 presents the Lamb 'as it had been slain' as alone worthy to open the seven-sealed scroll, the pivot of the whole Apocalypse. Dürer's design organises the celestial liturgy — throne, elders, creatures, sealed saints — into a luminous hierarchy, the redemptive counterpart to the cycle's monsters and plagues.

## For artists & game designers
The radial throne-room of adorers in concentric ranks is a strong layout for a sacred 'sanctum' hub contrasting with the apocalypse's chaos scenes.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: The Last Judgement (Memling triptych)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Hans Memling
rights: Public domain. National Museum, Gdańsk. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Hans Memling's monumental *Last Judgement* triptych (National Museum, Gdańsk), one of the great Northern Renaissance visions of the end of days.

## Iconography
Christ sits enthroned on a rainbow in glory; below, the archangel [[motif:saint michael weighing souls|Michael]] weighs naked resurrected souls in his scales. The blessed ascend a crystal stair to the gate of Heaven at the left wing, while at the right the [[motif:demons|demons]] drag, claw, and hurl the damned into a fiery, churning Hell. The risen dead climb from the earth across the central panel.

## Significance
Painted in Bruges c. 1467–71 for the Florentine banker Angelo Tani, the triptych was famously seized by a Hanseatic privateer and taken to Danzig (Gdańsk). Memling fuses the medieval Doom — the weighing of souls, the divided dead, the mouth of Hell — with the jewel-like naturalism of Early Netherlandish painting, distilling the Apocalypse's final judgement into an intimate yet vast spectacle.

## For artists & game designers
The soul-weighing scale is a perfect interactive 'judgement' mechanic; the bifurcated stair-to-heaven / fall-to-hell layout maps cleanly to a branching outcome screen.

citations.text: Dirk De Vos, *Hans Memling: The Complete Works* (1994).

title: The Las Huelgas Beatus (Morgan MS M.429)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Anonymous (Castilian workshop)
summary:
A folio (f.149) of the *Las Huelgas Beatus*, the latest and largest of the dated Beatus manuscripts, now Morgan Library & Museum MS M.429.

## Iconography
The page carries one of the cycle's apocalyptic scenes within the strong framing bands and saturated grounds of the Beatus tradition, the figures set against fields of flat colour in the conservative Spanish style that preserved Mozarabic conventions long after Romanesque naturalism had spread elsewhere.

## Significance
Made in 1220 for the royal Cistercian convent of Santa María la Real de [[motif:beatus of liebana|Las Huelgas]] near Burgos, this is the last firmly dated illustrated Beatus and the most monumental in scale. Acquired by J. P. Morgan, it stands at the end of a four-century tradition of illuminating Beatus of Liébana's *Commentary on the Apocalypse*, the great Iberian vehicle for visualising Revelation's beasts, angels, and cosmic diagrams.

## For artists & game designers
The persistence of an archaic, gold-grounded 'old style' into the 13th century is a model for worlds where a sacred visual idiom resists fashion.

citations.text: Morgan Library & Museum, MS M.429 (Las Huelgas Beatus, 1220).

title: The Saint-Sever Beatus (f. 159r)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Stephanus Garsia (Stephen Garsia) and others
summary:
A folio (f.159r) of the *Saint-Sever Beatus*, the only Beatus *Commentary on the Apocalypse* produced outside Spain, made in Gascony and now in the Bibliothèque nationale de France.

## Iconography
The scene unfolds across the Beatus tradition's coloured registers, illustrating one of Revelation's visions with the energetic, expressive figure-style of its principal illuminator. The French setting introduces a distinct, more dynamic line to the inherited [[motif:mozarabic|Mozarabic]] models.

## Significance
Illustrated around 1060–70 at the Gascon abbey of Saint-Sever under abbot Gregory Muntaner, and signed in part by the painter **Stephanus Garsia**, this is the unique non-Iberian copy of Beatus of Liébana's commentary. Its hybrid of Spanish iconography and Romanesque French drawing made it famous; Picasso is reputed to have studied its grimacing faces. It transmits the apocalyptic cycle — beasts, angels, the cosmic Jerusalem — to a French audience.

## For artists & game designers
A case study in style transfer: the same iconographic program rendered in a new regional hand yields fresh energy without changing content.

citations.text: Bibliothèque nationale de France, MS lat. 8878 (Saint-Sever Beatus).

title: The Sermon and Deeds of the Antichrist
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Luca Signorelli
summary:
Luca Signorelli's fresco *The Sermon and Deeds of the Antichrist* in the San Brizio Chapel of Orvieto Cathedral, a rare extended depiction of the [[motif:antichrist|Antichrist]].

## Iconography
The Antichrist — given Christ's own features — preaches to a crowd while a devil stands at his back, whispering into his ear and even guiding his hand, the very image of false revelation. Around him unfold the deceptions and atrocities of his reign: false miracles, executions, and the seduction of the faithful. Signorelli and his patron appear as onlookers at the lower left.

## Significance
Painted c. 1499–1502 amid the apocalyptic fervour stirred by Savonarola and the approaching half-millennium, the Orvieto cycle is among the most ambitious Renaissance treatments of the Last Days. The motif of Satan animating a Christ-like impostor visualises the theology of the Antichrist as counterfeit messiah, a deceiver who apes the true. The fresco profoundly impressed later artists, Michelangelo among them.

## For artists & game designers
The 'puppet-master devil behind the charismatic leader' is a potent visual for a deceiver antagonist whose evil is hidden in plain sight.

citations.text: Jonathan B. Riess, *Luca Signorelli: The San Brizio Chapel, Orvieto* (1995).
Orvieto Cathedral, Cappella di San Brizio.

title: The Four Evangelist Symbols (Book of Kells, f. 27v)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Anonymous (Insular / Columban monks)
summary:
The four evangelist symbols arranged as a cross-quartered page (f.27v) in the *Book of Kells* — the man, lion, ox, and eagle of the apocalyptic [[motif:tetramorph|tetramorph]].

## Iconography
Within an interlace-bordered cross, four winged creatures occupy the quarters: the **man** (Matthew), the **lion** (Mark), the **ox/calf** (Luke), and the **eagle** (John), each haloed and bearing a book. The dense Insular ornament and gold typify the supreme Columban gospel-book.

## Significance
The four living creatures 'full of eyes' around the throne (Revelation 4:6–8, after Ezekiel 1) were assigned by the Church Fathers — definitively by Jerome — to the four Evangelists, becoming the **tetramorph** of Christian art. The *Book of Kells* presents them as a unified emblem of the fourfold Gospel encircling Christ, one of the most influential treatments of the motif that recurs across Apocalypse imagery in the Maiestas Domini.

## For artists & game designers
The man-lion-ox-eagle quartet is a ready-made set of four faction emblems with built-in elemental/animal symbolism.

citations.text: Revelation 4:6–8; Ezekiel 1; Jerome, prologue to the Gospels.

title: The Trier Apocalypse (fol. 11v)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: Anonymous (Carolingian)
summary:
A folio (fol. 11v) of the *Trier Apocalypse*, the earliest surviving fully illustrated cycle of the Book of Revelation.

## Iconography
The miniature presents one of Revelation's visions in the spare, large-figured Carolingian manner, the scene laid out clearly against a plain ground so the narrative reads at a glance — an early, foundational attempt to give the Apocalypse a complete picture-cycle.

## Significance
The *Trier Apocalypse* (Trier, Stadtbibliothek, Cod. 31) was made in the first half of the 9th century, probably in a northern French scriptorium, and is the oldest extant illustrated Apocalypse. Its 74 miniatures became a fountainhead for the medieval iconography of Revelation, transmitted through later Carolingian and Romanesque copies. It marks the beginning of the long Western tradition of [[motif:apocalypse|apocalyptic]] picture-cycles.

## For artists & game designers
The earliest cycle shows how to establish a visual vocabulary for a complex prophetic text from scratch — one clear scene per beat, minimal background.

citations.text: Richard Laufner & Peter K. Klein, *Trierer Apokalypse* facsimile commentary (1975).

title: The Whore of Babylon (Blake)
work: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: William Blake
rights: Public domain. British Museum. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
William Blake's *The Whore of Babylon* (1809, British Museum), his visionary watercolour of Revelation 17's harlot enthroned on the seven-headed beast.

## Iconography
The [[motif:whore of babylon|Whore of Babylon]] sits high upon the scarlet seven-headed, ten-horned beast, lifting her golden cup; from the cup or her person issue the swarming forms of war and lust, and the kings and armies of the earth muster below, dwarfed by the monstrous mount. Blake's swirling, energetic line makes the abominations almost literally pour forth.

## Significance
This sheet belongs to Blake's biblical watercolours of c. 1800–1809. For Blake the Whore embodied 'Mystery, Babylon the Great' — corrupt religion and empire, the moral and political tyranny his prophetic books opposed. His treatment intensifies the medieval and Dürer traditions into a personal mythology in which the apocalyptic harlot stands for the fallen state of 'Mystery' that the imagination must overthrow.

## For artists & game designers
Letting a figure's corruption visibly emanate as swarming forms from her cup is a striking way to externalise an antagonist's contaminating influence.

citations.text: Martin Butlin, *The Paintings and Drawings of William Blake* (1981).

title: The Planet Jupiter and His Children (De zeven planeten series)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Jupiter** presiding over his children from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, part of the Children of the Planets tradition.

## Iconography
The benign greater fortune rides his **chariot** above, his rulership signs (Sagittarius and Pisces) at hand. Below, his children pursue the dignified, prosperous occupations Jupiter governs — judges, scholars, clergy, hunters, and men of law and government — the well-born and fortunate.

## Significance
In the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] doctrine, Jupiter is the **greater benefic**: he confers honour, piety, justice, and worldly success, and rules the sanguine temperament. Where Saturn's children labour and suffer, Jupiter's prosper. The contrast between the two planets structures the moral geography of the whole series.

## For artists & game designers
Jupiter's register of judges, priests and nobles is a ready 'faction of the fortunate' — the lawful, prosperous counterpart to Saturn's underclass.

title: The Planet Mars and His Children (De zeven planeten series)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Mars** above a scene of his violent children from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, part of the Children of the Planets tradition.

## Iconography
The armed war-god rides his **chariot** over a landscape of his works, the signs of his rulership (Aries and Scorpio) beside him. Below, his children make war, burn, rob, and butcher — soldiers, executioners, brigands, and smiths at the forge — the violent, choleric, and iron-working trades.

## Significance
Mars, the **lesser malefic**, governs war, fire, surgery, butchery, and the choleric temperament in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme. The image of the planet presiding over arson and slaughter visualizes astrological determinism at its harshest: born under Mars, one inherits a life of iron and blood.

## For artists & game designers
Mars's burning, pillaging register is the canonical 'war faction' tableau — soldiers, smiths, and executioners under one red banner.

title: The Planet Mercury and His Children (De zeven planeten series)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Mercury** above his industrious children from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, part of the Children of the Planets tradition.

## Iconography
The planet of intellect rides his **chariot** over a busy workshop-town, the signs of his rulership (Gemini and Virgo) beside him. Below, his children practise the skilled and learned arts — scholars, clockmakers, painters, sculptors, goldsmiths, organists, scribes, and merchants — the trades of hand and mind.

## Significance
Mercury governs eloquence, learning, craft, commerce, and dexterity in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme; his temperament is mutable, taking on the quality of whatever planet he joins. His register is the most 'civilized' of the series — the world of the studio, the shop, and the school.

## For artists & game designers
Mercury's town of artisans and scholars is the archetypal 'crafts and guilds faction' — the workshop as a planetary domain.

title: The Planet Saturn and His Children (De zeven planeten series)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Saturn** riding above the trades and people he governs, from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, the early-modern descendant of the medieval Children of the Planets cycle.

## Iconography
The aged, scythe-bearing planet rides his **chariot** drawn by the appropriate beasts across the upper register, the zodiac signs of his rulership (Capricorn and Aquarius) beside him. Below, his 'children' pursue the saturnine occupations — labourers, diggers, tanners, cripples, prisoners, and the poor — the lowly, cold, and melancholic trades.

## Significance
The [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] schema holds that each planet shapes a temperament and a class of human work. Saturn, slowest and coldest, governs old age, agriculture, mining, and the [[motif:saturn|melancholic]] — a doctrine analysed in Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl's *Saturn and Melancholy*. The printed series fixed for a broad public the link between planet and social type.

## For artists & game designers
The planet-in-chariot-above, children-below composition is a clean two-register layout for showing a ruling power and the lives it shapes.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

title: The Planet Venus and Her Children (De zeven planeten series)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Venus** above her amorous children from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, part of the Children of the Planets tradition.

## Iconography
The goddess rides her **chariot** over a garden of pleasure, the signs of her rulership (Taurus and Libra) at hand. Below, her children feast, dance, embrace, bathe, and make music — lovers, musicians, and revellers in a scene of courtship and delight.

## Significance
Venus, the **lesser benefic**, governs love, beauty, music, ornament, and the pleasures of the flesh in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme. The pleasure-garden register is the most sensuous of the series, the planetary opposite of saturnine toil and martial violence.

## For artists & game designers
Venus's garden of lovers and musicians is a self-contained 'pleasure faction' — feasts, music, and courtship as a planet's domain.

title: Jupiter and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children

**Jupiter** with his 'children' from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), the page of the greater benefic.

## Iconography
Jupiter presides over the dignified pursuits he governs — judges, scholars, clergy, hunters, and men of law and government, the well-born and fortunate at their honourable occupations.

## Significance
In the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] doctrine, Jupiter is the **greater benefic**, conferring honour, piety, justice, and worldly success. His page in the *De Sphaera* is the courtly, prosperous counterpart to saturnine toil and martial violence — the cycle visualizing astrological determinism as a hierarchy of social fortunes.

## For artists & game designers
Jupiter's register of judges, priests and nobles is a ready 'faction of the fortunate' — the lawful, prosperous order of society under its planet.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

title: Mars and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
