summary:
**Mars** with his 'children' from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), the martial page of the Sforza planet-cycle.

## Iconography
The armed war-god presides over a scene of violence and arms — soldiers fighting, a town put to fire and sword, the trades of iron and blood. His children inherit a life of war, butchery, and the forge.

## Significance
Mars, the **lesser malefic**, governs war, fire, surgery and the choleric in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme. The *De Sphaera* renders the planet's harsh domain with courtly precision: born under Mars, one's lot is iron. The cycle is among the most sumptuous surviving examples of the genre.

## For artists & game designers
The Mars page is a self-contained 'war faction' tableau — soldiers, sieges, and smiths under one planetary lord.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

title: Mercury and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The planet **Mercury** with his 'children' from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), the most famous page of the cycle.

## Iconography
Mercury presides over the trades and arts he governs — scholars, clockmakers, painters, organists, scribes and merchants busy in a contemporary urban scene. This is the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] schema: each planet rules a temperament and a set of human occupations.

## Significance
The *De Sphaera* (c. 1470), associated with the Sforza court of Milan, presents each planet as a crowned ruler attended by the people born under it. Mercury's page — the world of the studio, the shop, and the school — is the most often reproduced, an emblem of the learned and skilled arts under their planetary patron.

## For artists & game designers
Mercury's town of artisans and scholars is the archetypal 'crafts and guilds faction' — the workshop as a planetary domain.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

title: Chronos / Saturn, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Saturn** as Chronos from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), the planet of age and time in the Sforza cycle.

## Iconography
The aged planetary king is shown with the attributes of [[motif:saturn|Saturn]] — the **scythe** of time and harvest, the heavy melancholic bearing. As Chronos he is devouring Time itself, lord of the slow, the cold, and the saturnine.

## Significance
Saturn, the **greater malefic**, governs old age, agriculture, mining, hardship, and the melancholic temperament in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme. The *De Sphaera* gives the planet's grim domain its courtly treatment; the linked themes of Saturn, Time, and melancholy are the subject of Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl's classic study.

## For artists & game designers
The scythe-bearing elder of Saturn is the canonical 'lord of time and decay' — a melancholic patron or boss with a built-in mythology.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).
A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

title: Sol and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The **Sun** with his 'children' from the luxurious *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), a high point of the Children-of-the-Planets tradition.

## Iconography
[[motif:sol|Sol]], the crowned solar ruler, presides at the top of the page over the people and pursuits he governs — kings, wrestlers and athletes, fencers, and men of honour and display in a contemporary Lombard scene.

## Significance
The *De Sphaera* (c. 1470), associated with the Sforza court of Milan, presents each of the seven planets as a crowned king attended by those born under it. The cycle visualizes astrological determinism in social terms: your planet shaped your character and your craft. It is one of the most sumptuous surviving examples of the genre.

## For artists & game designers
A planet-as-king presiding over a townful of its 'children' is a vivid model for a faction, guild, or origin system keyed to seven celestial archetypes.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

title: Venus and her children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209, f. 9v)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Venus** with her 'children' (f. 9v) from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), among the most beautiful of all Children-of-the-Planets pages.

## Iconography
The goddess presides over a courtly garden of pleasure: her children make music, dance, embrace, and bathe together in a fountain or bathhouse. The scene gathers the lovers, musicians and revellers Venus governs into one ravishing Lombard pleasure-park.

## Significance
In the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme, Venus rules love, beauty, music, and bodily delight. The *De Sphaera* page is celebrated for its tender, sensuous treatment of the planet's domain, the courtly counterpart to the popular printed Planetenkinder.

## For artists & game designers
The Venus pleasure-garden — music, bathing, courtship around a fountain — is a ready scene for a leisure or romance space keyed to a single ruling power.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

title: Constellation woodcuts, Hyginus, Poeticon Astronomicon
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: After Hyginus; early printed edition (Venice tradition)
summary:
A page of constellation woodcuts from an early printed edition of the *Poeticon Astronomicon* attributed to **Hyginus**, the standard classical handbook of the star-myths.

## Iconography
The **constellations** are drawn as classical **personifications** — mythological figures with the stars of each constellation marked upon their bodies. The *Poeticon Astronomicon* gathered the Greek catasterism legends, explaining how heroes and beasts were set among the stars.

## Significance
The text ascribed to Hyginus (Augustan era) was, with Aratus, the great ancient source for the figures of the sky; its richly woodcut Venetian editions (Ratdolt, 1482, and successors) became the visual canon of the constellations for the Renaissance. These figures are also the raw material of the [[motif:paranatellonta|paranatellonta]] — the constellations that rise alongside the zodiac and underlie the decan imagery.

## For artists & game designers
Classical constellation-personifications with stars pricked onto them are a beautiful template for a 'living sky' or star-summoned creature bestiary.

title: Rhetorica (E23), so-called Tarocchi of Mantegna (E-series)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Anonymous North Italian engraver ('Master of the E-Series')
summary:
**Rhetorica** (card E23), one of the fifty engravings of the so-called *Tarocchi of Mantegna* (the E-series), a North Italian cosmological print cycle of c. 1465 — not in fact tarot cards nor by Mantegna.

## Iconography
The sheet personifies **Rhetoric** as an allegorical woman with her attributes, one of the seven Liberal Arts in the cycle. The full series ascends a Neoplatonic ladder from the conditions of men, through Apollo and the Muses, the Liberal Arts and Virtues, the seven **planets**, and the spheres up to the *Prima Causa*.

## Significance
The E-series is a complete diagram of the hierarchical cosmos: its planet-cards (Luna to Saturn, then the Eighth Sphere and Prime Mover) place the [[motif:planetary deities|planetary rulers]] within a graded chain of being. It is a key Renaissance synthesis of astrology, the arts, and Neoplatonic cosmology, hugely influential on later print culture (Dürer copied from it).

## For artists & game designers
A fifty-card ladder from common men to the Prime Mover is a ready 'rank / cosmic tier' system — each step a clean allegorical figure.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch 24: Early Italian Masters* (the 'Tarocchi').
Jay A. Levenson et al., *Early Italian Engravings from the National Gallery of Art* (1973).

title: Saturn (Francesco Albani planet series), Rijksmuseum
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Engraver after Francesco Albani
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Saturn** as a planetary deity, an engraving after a painted series by Francesco Albani, in the Rijksmuseum — a Baroque reception of the planet-rulers.

## Iconography
The aged god is shown with the attributes of [[motif:saturn|Saturn]] — the **scythe** of harvest and time, the brooding aspect of age. As Chronos / Saturn he embodies devouring Time, the cold and dry, and the melancholic temperament.

## Significance
The identification of Saturn with melancholy, age, agriculture, and Time runs from antiquity through the medieval Children-of-the-Planets cycles into Renaissance and Baroque art (the great study is Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy*). Albani's elegant mythological series shows the planetary king surviving as a living artistic type well past the Middle Ages.

## For artists & game designers
The scythe-bearing elder is the definitive 'lord of time and decay' figure — directly usable for a saturnine boss, deity, or melancholic patron.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

title: Apollo (Sol) with the constellation Leo (Seven Planets and Constellations series)
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Netherlandish printmaker (Seven Planets and Constellations series)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
**Apollo as Sol**, the Sun, paired with his zodiacal house **Leo**, from a Netherlandish Seven Planets and Constellations engraving series in the Rijksmuseum.

## Iconography
The radiant solar deity is shown with the attributes of [[motif:sol|Sol]] — the blazing face, the sun-disc — set beside the **lion** of Leo, the sign of the Sun's domicile. The print belongs to a series joining each planet to its constellation and its 'children'.

## Significance
The Sun rules royalty, gold, magnanimity, vitality, and the choleric-sanguine in astrological doctrine; Leo is its sole domicile. As Apollo, Sol carries the mythic dignity of the planet-deity into the [[motif:children of the planets|Planetenkinder]] tradition, where the Sun's children are kings, athletes, and men of honour.

## For artists & game designers
A single sheet binding a deity, its sign, and its solar attributes is a tidy 'planet card' template for a celestial-faction or zodiac system.

title: Astrological hall of the Salone, Palazzo della Ragione, Padua
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: After Giotto's lost cycle; repainted by Nicolò Miretto and Stefano da Ferrara
summary:
The vast hall of the **Palazzo della Ragione** in Padua, whose walls carry one of the largest medieval-Renaissance astrological fresco cycles in existence.

## Iconography
The enormous **Salone** is frescoed in registers running the length of the hall: the **zodiac signs**, the planets, the months and their labours, the apostles, and the **decan**-related figures of an astrological calendar, organising the year and the heavens around the room.

## Significance
The Paduan cycle derives from a lost programme designed under the influence of **Pietro d'Abano**, the city's great astrologer-physician (d. c. 1316), and originally painted by Giotto's workshop; it was repainted in the fifteenth century after a fire by Nicolò Miretto and Stefano da Ferrara. With the Schifanoia *Salone dei Mesi*, it is a principal monument of the Italian astrological hall, binding [[motif:decans|decan]] and calendar imagery into a single civic cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
An entire room as a walk-through calendar-cosmos is an architectural model for an astrological 'world map' space the player physically moves through.

citations.text: Eva Frojmovič, 'Giotto's Allegories of Justice and the Commune,' *JWCI* (1996).

title: Allegory of April (Triumph of Venus, Taurus), Salone dei Mesi, Palazzo Schifanoia
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Francesco del Cossa
summary:
The **April** wall — the **Triumph of Venus** over the sign **Taurus** — from Francesco del Cossa's frescoes in the *Salone dei Mesi*, Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara.

## Iconography
The three-band scheme is fully legible here: Venus rides in triumph above in a garden of lovers (Mars kneeling in her chains), the bull of [[motif:taurus|Taurus]] and its three **decan** figures occupy the middle band, and the Este court takes its pleasures below. The triumphs follow the planet that rules each sign — Venus for Taurus.

## Significance
The April panel is the best-preserved and most studied of the whole cycle, the keystone of Aby Warburg's reconstruction of how Egyptian-Indian [[motif:decans|decan]] figures, transmitted through Arabic and medieval Latin astrology, surfaced in Quattrocento Ferrara. It is the single most important monument of Renaissance astrological iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A full month-wall — ruling deity above, sign-and-decans in the middle, courtly life below — is a complete 'celestial governs the world' tableau in one frame.

citations.text: Aby Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology in the Palazzo Schifanoia' (1912).
Kristen Lippincott, 'The Frescoes of the Salone dei Mesi,' (PhD thesis, 1987).

title: Allegory of July (detail, washerwomen), Salone dei Mesi, Palazzo Schifanoia
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Ferrarese workshop (Salone dei Mesi)
summary:
A detail of washerwomen (*lavandaie*) from the lower band of the **July** wall in the *Salone dei Mesi*, Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara.

## Iconography
This detail comes from the lowest of the three registers — the band of Este court and civic life that runs beneath the [[motif:decans|decan]] figures and the triumph of each month. Here women launder at a basin, a vignette of everyday labour set under the rule of the month's sign.

## Significance
The lower band grounds the astrological cosmos of the *Salone* in the lived world of Ferrara: the heavens above (deity-triumph and sign-with-decans) govern the human activity below. The cycle thus joins the abstract [[motif:decans|decan]] system to a vivid panorama of fifteenth-century life, a structure Warburg read as the meeting of high astrology and local court culture.

## For artists & game designers
The genre-detail register shows how to anchor a cosmic scheme in ordinary life — celestial rule made concrete in small scenes of work and play.

citations.text: Aby Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology in the Palazzo Schifanoia' (1912).
Kristen Lippincott, 'The Frescoes of the Salone dei Mesi,' (PhD thesis, 1987).

title: Allegory of March (Aries), Salone dei Mesi, Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Francesco del Cossa
summary:
A section of the **March** fresco, with the sign **Aries** and its decans, from Francesco del Cossa's contribution to the *Salone dei Mesi* at the Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara — the supreme surviving cycle of astrological decan imagery.

## Iconography
Each month's wall is built in three horizontal bands: a triumph of a pagan deity above, the **zodiac sign** with its three **decan** figures in the middle, and scenes of Este court life below. Here March carries [[motif:aries|Aries]] the ram and the strange, unexplained decan-figures of its three ten-degree faces.

## Significance
The Schifanoia decans descend, through Pietro d'Abano's *Astrolabium* and the *Liber Astrologiae* of Georgius Zothorus (Abu Maʿshar's tradition), from the Egyptian and Indian **paranatellonta** — the figures rising with each ten degrees of the zodiac. Aby Warburg's famous 1912 lecture decoded the middle band as this migrated, half-forgotten decan iconography, founding the modern study of astrological image-transmission.

## For artists & game designers
The three-decan band gives 36 enigmatic 'sub-sign' figures — a ready bestiary of celestial minor powers beneath the twelve signs.

citations.text: Aby Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology in the Palazzo Schifanoia' (1912).
Kristen Lippincott, 'The Frescoes of the Salone dei Mesi,' (PhD thesis, 1987).

title: Allegory of December (Triumph of Vesta), Salone dei Mesi, Palazzo Schifanoia
work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: Ferrarese workshop (Salone dei Mesi)
summary:
The **December** wall, with its **Triumph of Vesta**, from the *Salone dei Mesi* at the Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara. (The Commons filename's '01 dicembre' fixes this as the December, not September, panel.)

## Iconography
The upper band carries the triumph of the deity who rules December's sign — here **Vesta** — above the zodiacal band that would hold [[motif:capricorn|Capricorn]] and its three **decan** figures, with the Este court below. This wall is among the more damaged of the cycle.

## Significance
The *Salone dei Mesi* assigns each month a presiding pagan deity drawn from Manilius's scheme of the tutelary gods of the signs, set over the [[motif:decans|decan]] figures that descend from the Egyptian-Arabic [[motif:paranatellonta|paranatellonta]]. The December panel completes the year's round of triumphs in the great programme Aby Warburg made the founding case-study of astrological iconology.

## For artists & game designers
A triumph-of-a-deity upper register is a clean 'patron god of the month' device for a calendar or seasonal-festival system.

citations.text: Aby Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology in the Palazzo Schifanoia' (1912).
Marco Bertozzi, *La tirannia degli astri: Aby Warburg e l'astrologia di Palazzo Schifanoia* (1985).

title: Bloodletting scene, Codex germanicus monacensis 340
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons (Bayerische Staatsbibliothek).
summary:
A bloodletting illustration from Codex germanicus monacensis 340 in the Bayerische Staatsbibliothek, depicting the practice of *Aderlass* rather than the schematic vein-man.

## Iconography
The scene shows the act of phlebotomy itself — a practitioner opening a vein on a seated or standing patient, the blood collected in a bowl. The image belongs to the practical apparatus of the German medical manuscript, complementing the schematic [[motif:aderlassmann|Aderlassmann]] figures with a picture of the procedure.

## Significance
Where the vein-man is a diagram, this folio records the lived reality of [[motif:phlebotomy|letting blood]], the routine therapy of humoral medicine that the astrological calendars sought to time. Such scenes connect the abstract sign-and-planet apparatus to the barber-surgeon's daily work in the German lands.

## For artists & game designers
A depiction of the procedure (not just the chart) gives the 'cutscene' to pair with the diagram — useful for grounding an arcane medical system in visceral practice.

title: Aderlassmann (Bloodletting Man), 1491
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous (German woodcut)
summary:
A 1491 German woodcut *Aderlassmann* — the Bloodletting Man — labelling the veins to be opened and the signs that govern the timing.

## Iconography
The nude male figure stands with arms slightly raised; numerous veins are marked along the arms, legs, neck and head, each labelled or keyed to the part of the body and the ailment it serves. The surrounding apparatus ties the bleeding-points to the [[motif:zodiac man|zodiacal]] rulership of the limbs.

## Significance
The *Aderlassmann* is the German vernacular form of the bleeding-figure, central to the *Aderlasskalender* (bloodletting calendars) printed in great numbers after Gutenberg. These cheap broadsheets and almanacs told barber-surgeons and laypeople alike which days the heavens deemed safe for [[motif:phlebotomy|letting blood]] from which vein — among the earliest mass-market applications of astrology to daily life.

## For artists & game designers
The labelled-vein figure makes a perfect 'anatomy unlock screen': tapping each marked vein could reveal its associated humour, ailment and auspicious sign.

citations.text: Peter Murray Jones, *Medieval Medicine in Illuminated Manuscripts* (1998).

title: Aderlassmann (Bloodletting Man), c. 1500
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous (German woodcut)
summary:
A turn-of-the-century German *Aderlassmann*, a more elaborate woodcut of the Bloodletting Man with its full apparatus of marked veins and labels.

## Iconography
The figure is shown frontally, the body's surface crowded with named bleeding-points; banners or labels gloss each vein and its therapeutic use. The richer cutting and denser annotation mark the maturing of the German almanac woodcut around 1500.

## Significance
This exemplar shows how quickly the [[motif:aderlassmann|Aderlassmann]] standardised once it entered print. Sold in the *Aderlasskalender* and the broadsheet almanacs, the figure carried the doctrine of astrologically timed [[motif:phlebotomy|bloodletting]] into ordinary households, where the laity used it without a physician. Such prints are key evidence for the popular reach of medical astrology in the Holy Roman Empire.

## For artists & game designers
Compare with the 1491 cut as an 'era progression' asset — same schematic, more ornament — useful for showing a tradition hardening into convention.

citations.text: Charles Talbot, 'Astrology and Medicine', in *Medicine in Medieval England* (1967).

title: Aderlassmann, Codex Palatinus germanicus 644
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons (Bibliotheca Palatina).
summary:
A manuscript Bloodletting Man from Codex Palatinus germanicus 644, one of the Heidelberg Bibliotheca Palatina medical codices.

## Iconography
The figure stands with arms extended; the veins of head, neck, arms, hands, legs and feet are picked out and labelled in German, identifying which vein to open for which complaint. The hand-drawn, coloured form precedes the standardised printed *Aderlassmann*.

## Significance
The Heidelberg *Cpg* medical manuscripts preserve the manuscript ancestry of the printed bleeding-figure. As a witness from the [[motif:aderlassmann|Aderlassmann]] tradition in its handwritten stage, this folio shows the vernacular German vein-map that the early presses would later fix and multiply, linking [[motif:phlebotomy|phlebotomy]] to the wider scheme of astrological timing.

## For artists & game designers
The rougher manuscript hand gives a 'field-notebook' texture distinct from the crisp woodcut — good for a found-document or lore-page aesthetic.

title: Nude figure of the Astrological Man (14th century)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection).
summary:
A fourteenth-century manuscript miniature of the Astrological Man, a nude figure across which the zodiacal rulership of the body is mapped.

## Iconography
The naked body stands frontally, the twelve signs disposed along it from the ram at the brow to the fishes at the feet. Lines or labels connect each sign to the [[motif:melothesia|member]] it governs. The early date gives the figure the stiff, hieratic posture of Gothic manuscript art rather than the modelled nudes of the later woodcut tradition.

## Significance
Among the earlier surviving witnesses to the [[motif:homo signorum|Homo signorum]], this figure shows the doctrine already fully formed in the fourteenth century, well before its mass diffusion through print. Such miniatures accompanied the prognostic and phlebotomy texts that guided a physician's calendar of safe and dangerous days for intervention on each part of the body.

## For artists & game designers
A spare Gothic line-figure reads as an 'arcane medical schematic' more convincingly than a polished anatomical plate — good reference for a hand-drawn diegetic medicine screen.

citations.text: Roger French, 'Astrology in Medical Practice', in *Practical Medicine from Salerno to the Black Death* (1994).

title: Bloodletting Man showing the influence of zodiac and planets
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection).
summary:
A manuscript Bloodletting Man marking the points of phlebotomy on the body together with the zodiacal signs and planets that govern them.

## Iconography
A standing figure is ringed by the twelve signs in their head-to-foot order; lines run from the signs and from planetary roundels to the precise spots on the body at which a vein may be opened. The figure unites two schemes — the [[motif:zodiac man|Homo signorum]] of sign-rulership and the practical map of bleeding-points used by the surgeon.

## Significance
Bloodletting was the central therapy of humoral medicine, and its timing was governed by the heavens: a vein should be opened when the Moon and the governing planets stood favourably and not while the Moon transited the sign ruling the affected member. Figures like this one served as bedside reference, fusing astrological prognosis with the [[motif:phlebotomy|phlebotomy]] of the practising physician.

## For artists & game designers
The convergence of zodiac roundels, planetary glyphs and anatomical bleeding-points on one figure is an exemplary 'occult procedure diagram' — busy, labelled, and unmistakably operative.

citations.text: Linda Voigts, 'The Golden Table of Pythagoras', in *Popular and Practical Science of Medieval England* (1994).

title: Fasciculus medicinae, illustrated leaf (BSB)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Johannes de Ketham
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons (Bayerische Staatsbibliothek).
summary:
An illustrated leaf from a Bayerische Staatsbibliothek copy of Ketham's *Fasciculus medicinae*, one of the work's full-page diagrammatic figures.

## Iconography
The page presents one of the book's schematic bodies or instructional scenes within its woodcut border and Latin text — the *Fasciculus*'s house format, in which a single emblematic figure is wrapped in explanatory letterpress.

## Significance
The survival of multiple early printings in libraries such as the BSB lets scholars trace the *Fasciculus*'s evolving illustration across editions. Each leaf belongs to the integrated programme — [[motif:zodiac man|zodiac]], [[motif:bloodletting man|bleeding]], wound, urine — by which the volume taught the whole of practical medicine through pictures, and by which medical astrology reached its widest early-modern audience.

## For artists & game designers
The text-wrapped full-page figure is a ready 'codex page' layout — central diagram framed by dense letterpress, ideal for an in-game tome spread.

title: Uroscopy (Harnschau), Gart der Gesundheit
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous (German woodcut)
summary:
A *Harnschau* (urine-inspection) woodcut from the German herbal-medical tradition of the *Gart der Gesundheit*, the physician reading the flask.

## Iconography
A physician holds the urine-flask aloft for inspection, the woodcut rendering the *Harnschau* — the German term for uroscopy — as a discrete diagnostic act. The bold early-printed line is typical of the Mainz medical incunabula.

## Significance
The *Gart der Gesundheit* (Mainz, 1485) was among the most influential printed herbals and household-medicine books of the German Renaissance, and its [[motif:uroscopy|Harnschau]] image carried urine-diagnosis to a broad vernacular readership. The picture shows the practice as a fixed ritual of the German physician, alongside the [[motif:bloodletting man|bloodletting]] and zodiac apparatus of the same medical culture.

## For artists & game designers
The lone physician-with-flask in crisp woodcut line is a clean icon asset — high contrast, instantly readable as 'diagnosis'.

title: Ketham, Fasciculus medicinae (title figure)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Johannes de Ketham
summary:
A woodcut from the *Fasciculus medicinae* of Johannes de Ketham, the first printed medical book to contain anatomical illustrations.

## Iconography
The plate shows one of the *Fasciculus*'s signature schematic figures or scenes — physicians, the lecturing master, or one of the diagrammatic bodies — rendered in the bold early-Venetian woodcut line of the de Gregori brothers' workshop.

## Significance
First printed at Venice in 1491 and famously expanded in 1493–95, the *Fasciculus medicinae* gathered the standard apparatus of late-medieval medicine — the [[motif:zodiac man|Zodiac Man]], the [[motif:bloodletting man|Bloodletting Man]], the [[motif:wound man|Wound Man]], the Disease Woman, the [[motif:uroscopy|uroscopy]] charts and the dissection scene — into one illustrated volume. It became the most widely diffused medical picture-book of the age and fixed the canonical form of these figures for European print.

## For artists & game designers
The *Fasciculus* is the single best source-book for a Renaissance medical aesthetic: one volume supplies the zodiac body, the vein-man, the wound-man and the urine-wheel in a coherent house style.

citations.text: Karl Sudhoff & Charles Singer, *The Fasciculo di Medicina, Venice 1493* (1925).

title: Joannes de Ketham, Fasciculus medicine (Wellcome leaf)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Johannes de Ketham
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection).
summary:
A high-resolution leaf from the Wellcome Collection's copy of Joannes de Ketham's *Fasciculus medicine*, showing one of the work's celebrated illustrations.

## Iconography
The plate depicts one of the *Fasciculus*'s major scenes — among them the consultation of physicians, the anatomy lecture and dissection, or the diagrammatic bodies — in the strong woodcut line of the Venetian editions.

## Significance
The Wellcome's holdings of the *Fasciculus* are among the most studied, their high-resolution digitisation underpinning modern scholarship on the birth of the illustrated medical book. The volume's union of [[motif:uroscopy|uroscopy]], [[motif:bloodletting man|phlebotomy]] and astrological prognosis in a single woodcut programme made it the template through which Renaissance Europe pictured the medicine of the [[motif:zodiac man|star-ruled body]].

## For artists & game designers
The consultation and dissection scenes supply the human, narrative counterpart to the schematic figures — useful when an arcane-medicine setting needs people, not just diagrams.

citations.text: Tiziana Pesenti, *Il 'Fasciculus medicinae' ovvero le metamorfosi del libro umanistico* (2001).

title: The planets and signs of the zodiac send down their influence
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection).
summary:
A figure showing the planets and zodiac signs casting their influence down upon the human body, the doctrine of celestial 'influence' made literal.

## Iconography
From the heavens above, the seven planets and the twelve signs direct lines or rays of influence onto the parts of a human figure below, each celestial body linked to the organ or member it governs. The image visualises *influentia* — the in-flowing virtue by which the stars were thought to act on the sublunary body.

## Significance
The picture renders the central premise of medical astrology: that the planets and signs continuously shape the [[motif:melothesia|microcosmic body]], so that health and sickness, temperament and crisis all answer to the sky. This is the macrocosm–microcosm doctrine that underwrites the [[motif:zodiac man|Zodiac Man]] and the whole apparatus of astrological prognosis.

## For artists & game designers
Rays of planetary 'influence' descending onto a body is a clean status-effect metaphor — each planet a buff/debuff source streaming down onto the player's anatomy.

title: Constantine the African examining urine (uroscopy)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A manuscript miniature of Constantine the African examining a flask of urine, the archetypal image of medieval uroscopy.

## Iconography
The physician holds a *matula* — the round-bottomed urine flask — up to the light, scrutinising its colour, clarity and contents to reach a diagnosis. The patient or an attendant stands by. The single uplifted flask became the universal emblem of the doctor.

## Significance
Constantine the African (d. c. 1099), the Monte Cassino translator who carried Arabic medicine into Latin Europe, stands here for the science of [[motif:uroscopy|uroscopy]] — diagnosis by the inspection of urine. For centuries the urine-flask defined the physician in art, and the reading of the *matula* was woven together with astrological timing and humoral theory into a complete diagnostic and prognostic system.

## For artists & game designers
The doctor-with-flask is the instantly legible icon of the medieval healer — a single prop that signals 'physician' the way a staff signals 'mage'.

citations.text: Michael McVaugh, 'Constantine the African', in *Dictionary of Scientific Biography*.

title: Illustration of uroscopy, 15th century
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection).
summary:
A fifteenth-century illustration of uroscopy in practice, the physician judging a flask brought by or for the patient.

## Iconography
The scene shows the consultation around the urine-flask: the doctor examines the *matula* while the patient or a messenger looks on, the social ritual by which diagnosis was performed and a prognosis pronounced.

## Significance
Uroscopy was not only a technique but a public performance of medical authority: patients sent their urine to the physician, who could (it was claimed) diagnose at a distance from the flask alone. The practice, fused with astrological and humoral reasoning, defined the late-medieval consultation — and drew satire when over-claimed. Here it is shown straight, as the central diagnostic act of [[motif:uroscopy|uroscopic]] medicine.

## For artists & game designers
The flask-consultation is a compact 'diagnosis encounter' tableau — two figures and a prop carry an entire medical interaction.

title: Western Uroscopy Chart
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection).
summary:
A Western uroscopy chart — the wheel of urine flasks ringed by their diagnostic colours, the physician's reference for reading the *matula*.

## Iconography
A circular arrangement of urine flasks, each tinted a different shade from pale to dark and keyed to a humoral condition, disease or prognosis. The colour, sediment and clarity of the urine were correlated through the wheel to the patient's state.

## Significance
The [[motif:uroscopy|uroscopy]] wheel (the *Harnschau* chart) systematised diagnosis by urine into a colour-coded instrument. Twenty or more named shades, from *albus* to *niger*, indexed the balance of the humours and the course of an illness; combined with astrological prognosis, the wheel let the physician judge both disease and its critical days. The motif became so emblematic that the flask-wheel stands for medieval medicine itself.

## For artists & game designers
The radial colour-coded flask-wheel is a perfect diegetic UI: a literal diagnostic dial the player rotates to match a sample's hue to a condition.

title: Uroscopy (Hortus sanitatis)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous (woodcut)
summary:
A uroscopy woodcut from the *Hortus sanitatis* tradition, the physician inspecting urine within the late-fifteenth-century medical encyclopaedia.

## Iconography
The scene shows the urine-flask held up for examination, the diagnostic gesture set within the bordered woodcut of the printed *Hortus*. The high-resolution scan preserves the fine cutting of the early German press.

## Significance
The *Hortus sanitatis* (Garden of Health, Mainz 1491) was a sprawling natural-medical encyclopaedia whose woodcuts shaped how the early-modern reader imagined plants, animals and the practice of medicine. Its [[motif:uroscopy|uroscopy]] image situates urine-diagnosis within the wider compendium of healing arts, the flask once more standing as the physician's defining instrument.

## For artists & game designers
The encyclopaedic woodcut style — figure within ruled border — is a strong template for 'bestiary/codex entry' framing applied to a medical procedure.

title: Uroscopy, Wellcome MS 784
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection, MS 784).
summary:
A finely detailed uroscopy page from Wellcome MS 784, a high-resolution manuscript witness to the urine-diagnosis tradition.

## Iconography
The leaf presents the apparatus of uroscopy — ranks or a wheel of tinted flasks and their diagnostic glosses — in a richly executed manuscript hand, the colours of the urine carefully differentiated for the reader.

## Significance
MS 784 belongs to the substantial body of [[motif:uroscopy|uroscopic]] manuscripts that circulated as practical reference for physicians and apothecaries. The care lavished on the colour gradations underscores how technical the art aspired to be: each shade a sign, the whole a diagnostic and prognostic code read alongside the patient's stars and humours.

## For artists & game designers
The high-resolution manuscript flasks are excellent close-up reference for rendering convincing 'sample vials' with authentic period colour vocabulary.

citations.text: Peter Murray Jones, *Medieval Medicine in Illuminated Manuscripts* (1998).

title: Zodiac / Vein Man (National Library of Medicine)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: No known restrictions. Via Wikimedia Commons (U.S. National Library of Medicine, History of Medicine).
summary:
A Zodiac / Vein Man from the U.S. National Library of Medicine, marking both the zodiacal rulership and the bleeding-veins of the body.

## Iconography
The standing nude figure carries the twelve signs disposed head-to-foot and, distributed across the limbs, the network of veins indicated for phlebotomy. The 'Vein Man' designation emphasises the practical bleeding-map; the 'Zodiac Man' label its astrological frame — the two were routinely combined in one figure.

## Significance
The NLM holds one of the richest collections of medical-astrological imagery, and this composite figure typifies the genre's double duty: the physician read off both *when* (the sign and planet then ruling) and *where* (the vein) to intervene. It belongs to the same family as the [[motif:aderlassmann|Aderlassmann]] and the [[motif:zodiac man|Homo signorum]].

## For artists & game designers
The explicit vein network over a zodiac body is a strong 'circulatory + arcane' overlay — combine a vascular layer with a sign layer for a two-mode anatomy inspector.

citations.text: U.S. National Library of Medicine, *Historical Anatomies on the Web*.

title: Wound Man, Pseudo-Galen Anathomia (Wellcome MS 290)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection, MS 290).
summary:
A manuscript Wound Man from a Pseudo-Galenic *Anathomia* (Wellcome MS 290), a high-resolution witness to the figure in its handwritten form.

## Iconography
The figure bears the full repertory of wounds — bladed weapons embedded in head, chest, arms and legs, arrows, animal bites and surface lesions — each linked to the surrounding text on diagnosis and cure. The painted manuscript form gives the body colour and modelling absent from the printed cuts.

## Significance
This [[motif:wound man|Wound Man]] shows the figure circulating in manuscript anatomies attributed to Galen, the great authority whose corpus underwrote medieval medicine. As one of the finest surviving manuscript exemplars, it documents the genre before and alongside its diffusion through Ketham's *Fasciculus*, and its place within the broader teaching of [[motif:anatomy|anatomy]] and surgery.

## For artists & game designers
The painted, full-colour manuscript Wound Man is the premium reference for a richly illustrated injury codex — far more detailed than the line-cut versions.

title: Wound Man with injuries, German legend
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection).
summary:
A Wound Man with a German legend, the weapon-pierced figure surrounded by vernacular text keying each injury to its remedy.

## Iconography
The man is shown beset by the standard catalogue of wounds — embedded blades, arrows, a spear, animal bites and lesions — with German captions naming each injury and pointing to its treatment in the surrounding surgical text.

## Significance
The German-legended [[motif:wound man|Wundenmann]] belongs to the vernacular surgical handbooks of the German lands, where works like Gersdorff's *Feldtbuch der Wundartzney* carried the figure to barber-surgeons working in the field. The vernacular captions mark the genre's passage from learned Latin anatomy to practical, craft-level instruction.

## For artists & game designers
Vernacular captions on the wounds turn the figure into a labelled tooltip sheet — a model for an in-world injury glossary written in the 'local tongue'.

citations.text: Sandra Cavallo & Tessa Storey, *Healthy Living in Late Renaissance Italy* (2013).

title: Wound Man, Gersdorff Feldtbuch der Wundartzney (1517)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Hans von Gersdorff
summary:
The Wound Man from Hans von Gersdorff's *Feldtbuch der Wundartzney* (Field Book of Wound Surgery, Strasbourg 1517), one of the most celebrated of all Wound Man woodcuts.

## Iconography
A bearded man stands transfixed by a dense array of weapons — sword, dagger, spear, arrows, a thrown club, a flail — and afflicted by animal bites and lesions. Gersdorff's cut, attributed to the Strasbourg workshop of Hans Wechtlin, is unusually crowded and energetic, the body bristling with implements.

## Significance
Gersdorff was a battlefield surgeon, and his *Feldtbuch* distilled decades of practical [[motif:wound man|wound]] and amputation surgery into the most influential German surgical manual of the early sixteenth century. Its Wound Man became the canonical image of the genre, reproduced and adapted for generations. The book joined the same shelf as the astrological-medical figures, surgery and the stars sharing one body-schematic logic.

## For artists & game designers
Gersdorff's exuberantly over-armed figure is the definitive 'maximum damage' diagram — the reference to reach for when an injury screen needs to feel overwhelmed.

title: Wundenmann (Wound Man), Fasciculus medicinae 1491
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Workshop of Johannes de Ketham
summary:
The *Wundenmann* (Wound Man) woodcut from the 1491 Venice *Fasciculus medicinae* of Johannes de Ketham, the figure pierced by every weapon and affliction the surgeon must treat.

## Iconography
A standing man is struck simultaneously by a catalogue of injuries: swords and daggers in the torso and limbs, arrows and a spear, a club, thorns, the bites of dog, snake and scorpion, abscesses and skin lesions. Each wound keys to a passage of the surgical text explaining its treatment.

## Significance
The [[motif:wound man|Wound Man]] is a visual index of trauma surgery, gathering on one body every case the practitioner might face. First printed in Ketham's *Fasciculus*, the figure became a fixture of the surgical handbooks of the German and Italian Renaissance. Though not itself astrological, it shares the schematic 'mapped body' logic of the [[motif:zodiac man|Homo signorum]] with which it travelled in the same volumes.

## For artists & game designers
The Wound Man is the original 'damage diagram' — a single figure annotated with every injury type, an ideal template for an injury/affliction codex screen.

citations.text: Jack Hartnell, *Medieval Bodies: Life and Death in the Middle Ages* (2018).

title: The Zodiac Man (Homo signorum)
work: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A diagrammatic Zodiac Man from a medical-astrological manuscript, the human body annotated with the twelve signs and instructions on the importance of astrology to the physician.

## Iconography
A standing figure is overlaid or surrounded by the twelve zodiac signs in the canonical head-to-foot order of [[motif:melothesia|melothesia]]: **Aries** ruling the head, **Taurus** the neck, **Gemini** the shoulders and arms, **Cancer** the chest, **Leo** the heart, **Virgo** the belly, **Libra** the kidneys and loins, **Scorpio** the genitals, **Sagittarius** the thighs, **Capricorn** the knees, **Aquarius** the shins, and **Pisces** the feet. Surrounding text explains the medical use of the scheme.

## Significance
The *Homo signorum* expressed the doctrine that each part of the microcosmic body answered to a sign of the macrocosm. A physician consulting the figure judged whether the Moon stood in a sign governing the affected limb before bleeding, purging, or operating — one should not, the rule ran, 'touch with iron' a member whose sign the Moon then occupied. The scheme descends from Manilius and the *Tetrabiblos* tradition and saturated the calendars and almanacs of the later Middle Ages.

## For artists & game designers
The head-to-foot zodiacal body is a ready-made 'status overlay' — a paper-doll whose twelve regions light up with their ruling sign, perfect for an astrology-driven medicine or affliction system.

citations.text: Charles Clark, 'The Zodiac Man in Medieval Medical Astrology', *Journal of the Rocky Mountain Medieval and Renaissance Association* (1982).

title: The Planet Mars and His Children
work: Astrology & the Stars
creator: after the Planetenkinder tradition
summary:
An engraving from the **'Children of the Planets'** (*Planetenkinder*) tradition — **Mars**, the red planet of war, riding his chariot above the human types he governs.

## Iconography
The planetary deity rides a zodiac-marked chariot (Mars draws Aries and Scorpio); below, his 'children' act out his nature — soldiers, butchers, surgeons, brawlers, burning towns — pairing macrocosm (god in his sphere) with microcosm (lives he rules).

## Significance
The Children of the Planets series — spread by the 15th-c. *Hausbuch* and blockbooks — is the most popular Renaissance image of **astrological determinism**: the seven planets each shape a class of humanity (Iconclass 24C21). It is the everyday face of the cosmology underlying alchemy and magic.

## For artists & game designers
A complete 'planet-as-archetype' design — deity, chariot, zodiac, ruled world. Ideal for a planetary-magic system.

title: Melencolia I (Albrecht Dürer)
work: Astrology & the Stars
creator: Albrecht Dürer
rights: Public domain. Google Art Project / Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Dürer's 1514 engraving — perhaps the most analysed print in Western art, and a dense compendium of Renaissance occult thought.

## Iconography
A winged genius sits in brooding inaction amid the tools of geometry — compass, scales, hourglass, a truncated polyhedron, a magic square, a sleeping hound, a bat-winged banner reading MELENCOLIA I. A comet and lunar rainbow burn over the sea.

## Significance
The image fuses the doctrine of the four humours (melancholy, ruled by [[tradition:astrology|Saturn]]) with Ficino's Neoplatonic idea that the melancholic temperament is the seat of genius. The 4×4 **magic square** (a Jupiter *kamea*, encoding the date 1514) is a talisman against Saturn's gloom. Klibansky/Panofsky/Saxl's *Saturn and Melancholy* made it the locus classicus of esoteric iconology.

## For artists & game designers
The archetype of the blocked, brilliant adept amid arcane instruments — perfect for a wizard's study or an occult-geometry puzzle.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (London, 1964).

title: Volvelle from Apianus's *Astronomicum Caesareum*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Petrus Apianus (Peter Apian)
summary:
A **volvelle** (rotating paper instrument) from Petrus Apianus's sumptuous *Astronomicum Caesareum* (Ingolstadt, 1540).

## Iconography
Concentric **rotating discs**, hand-coloured and threaded with silk **pointers**, are layered on the page to form a working calculator. Bands of the **zodiac**, planetary glyphs, and degree-scales are printed on the moving wheels so the user can read off a planet's position by turning the dials.

## Significance
Apianus's book is the masterpiece of the **volvelle** form — dozens of movable paper computers that let a reader compute planetary longitudes, eclipses, and the data an [[motif:astrology|astrologer]] needed to cast a horoscope, without tables or an astrolabe. Made for the Emperor Charles V, it stands at the luxurious height of Renaissance astronomical-astrological print.

## For artists & game designers
The layered, thread-and-disc 'analogue computer' is irresistible UI inspiration for a craftable star-calculating gadget.

citations.text: Owen Gingerich, 'Apianus's Astronomicum Caesareum and its Leipzig Facsimile,' *Journal for the History of Astronomy* 2 (1971).

title: Cancer, Leo, and Gemini — Bartsch's *Planisphaerium*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Jacob Bartsch
summary:
A constellation plate by **Jacob Bartsch** (Kepler's son-in-law) showing the zodiac figures of **Cancer**, **Leo**, and **Gemini**.

## Iconography
Three **zodiac signs** share the sheet: the crab of **Cancer**, the lion of **Leo**, and the twins of **Gemini**, each drawn as its living figure with the brighter stars marked. Neighbouring constellations and the band of the Milky Way thread between them along the **ecliptic**.

## Significance
Bartsch's *Planisphaerium stellatum* and *Usus astronomicus* (1624) continued the tradition of figured star-maps and introduced several new constellations. As Johannes Kepler's son-in-law and editor, he stood at the meeting point of the new astronomy and an older [[motif:zodiac|astral]] iconography that astrology still relied on.

## For artists & game designers
Three zodiac emblems on one sheet — convenient reference art for a signs-of-summer set or a multi-sign UI.

citations.text: Deborah J. Warner, *The Sky Explored: Celestial Cartography 1500–1800* (1979).

title: Andromeda, from Bayer's *Uranometria*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Johann Bayer (plates engraved by Alexander Mair)
summary:
The constellation **Andromeda** as figured in Bayer's *Uranometria* (1603), the 'chained woman' of Greek myth set among her stars.

## Iconography
Andromeda is drawn as a woman with her arms bound by **chains**, the brightest stars marking her head, girdle, and feet; the star at her head, **Alpheratz** (Sirrah), is shared with Pegasus. The figure floats over a ruled **coordinate grid** with the stars graded by **magnitude** and lettered with **Bayer designations**.

## Significance
Andromeda belongs to the great Perseus cycle of catasterised myths — Cepheus, Cassiopeia, Perseus, Cetus — that fill the northern autumn sky. Bayer's elegant plate carried these ancient [[motif:zodiac|star-myths]] into a precise, lettered atlas usable by astronomer and astrologer alike.

## For artists & game designers
The bound-figure-as-constellation is striking concept art for a 'captive star' quest or a sky-myth bestiary.

citations.text: Deborah J. Warner, *The Sky Explored: Celestial Cartography 1500–1800* (1979).

title: Cetus, folio 34v of Bayer's *Uranometria*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Johann Bayer (copperplate by Alexander Mair)
summary:
**Cetus**, the sea-monster, on folio 34v of Johann Bayer's *Uranometria* (Augsburg, 1603).

## Iconography
A scaly **sea monster** with gaping jaws and coiling body fills the plate, its stars marking snout, body, and tail. The figure lies over a ruled **coordinate grid**, with magnitudes shown by symbol size and **Bayer designations** lettering the principal stars.

## Significance
Cetus is the beast sent to devour Andromeda in the Perseus myth, and its great sprawl of faint stars dominates the watery quarter of the autumn sky. Bayer's engraving (cut by Alexander Mair) is a characteristic page of the first whole-sky atlas, carrying a classical [[motif:zodiac|catasterism]] into a precisely lettered chart.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made celestial sea-monster — drop-in concept art for a sky leviathan or a 'devourer constellation'.

citations.text: Deborah J. Warner, *The Sky Explored: Celestial Cartography 1500–1800* (1979).

title: Orion, from Bayer's *Uranometria*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Johann Bayer (plates engraved by Alexander Mair)
summary:
The constellation **Orion** as engraved for Johann Bayer's *Uranometria* (Augsburg, 1603), the first true star atlas to cover the whole celestial sphere.

## Iconography
The **hunter** strides across the plate, club raised and a **lion skin** over his outstretched arm, his belt and sword marked by the bright stars. A fine **coordinate grid** is laid behind the figure, and each star is sized by **magnitude** and lettered with Bayer's Greek-letter **designations**.

## Significance
Bayer's *Uranometria* fused beautiful figure-drawing with the new rigour of Tycho Brahe's star positions, and introduced the **Bayer designations** (α, β, γ…) still used today. Its constellation figures, derived from the classical [[motif:zodiac|catasterisms]], carried the mythological sky into the age of precise observation.

## For artists & game designers
A museum-grade constellation hero-figure with labelled stars — perfect for a star-lore codex or an astrology talent tree.

citations.text: Deborah J. Warner, *The Sky Explored: Celestial Cartography 1500–1800* (1979).

title: Scorpius, from Bayer's *Uranometria*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Johann Bayer (plates engraved by Alexander Mair)
summary:
**Scorpius**, the Scorpion, one of the twelve zodiacal constellations, as engraved for Bayer's *Uranometria* (1603).

## Iconography
The **scorpion** sprawls across the plate, claws spread and tail curling to its sting; the red heart-star **Antares** glows at its body. The figure straddles the **ecliptic**, the stars graded by **magnitude** and lettered with Bayer's Greek **designations**.

## Significance
As a **zodiac sign**, Scorpius is one of the twelve through which the Sun, Moon, and planets pass; in astrology it governs a swathe of correspondences and was held a fixed water sign. Bayer's plate shows the working machinery of the [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] in atlas form — a sign that is also a precisely mapped patch of sky.

## For artists & game designers
A zodiac beast rendered with real star positions — equally usable as constellation art or as a Scorpio sign emblem.

citations.text: Deborah J. Warner, *The Sky Explored: Celestial Cartography 1500–1800* (1979).

title: *Coeli Stellati Christiani Haemisphaerium Prius* — the Christianised sky
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Andreas Cellarius
summary:
Cellarius's hemisphere of the **Christianised heavens**, in which the pagan constellations are replaced by biblical and saintly figures.

## Iconography
The familiar mythological constellations give way to scriptural ones: apostles, biblical ships, and sacred objects mapped onto the same stars. The plate follows the radical scheme of Julius Schiller's *Coelum Stellatum Christianum* (Augsburg, 1627), here drawn as a full engraved **hemisphere**.

## Significance
Schiller's project — and Cellarius's homage to it — sought to baptise the sky, expelling the heroes and beasts of the [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] in favour of a Christian iconography. It is a vivid episode in the **reception** of classical astral imagery and the Counter-Reformation impulse to sanctify even the constellations; the experiment never displaced the traditional names.

## For artists & game designers
A 'reskinned night sky' concept made literal — useful for a faction that has rewritten the heavens in its own image.

citations.text: Robert H. van Gent, *Andreas Cellarius: Harmonia Macrocosmica of 1660* (Taschen, 2006).

title: *Planisphaerium Copernicanum* — the Copernican system
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Andreas Cellarius
summary:
Cellarius's *Planisphaerium Copernicanum*, the plate of the *Harmonia Macrocosmica* depicting the Sun-centred cosmos of Copernicus.

## Iconography
The **Sun** sits at the centre of a system of concentric **planetary orbits** — Mercury, Venus, Earth with its Moon, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn — each carrying its planetary glyph and, for Earth, the band of the seasons. The whole is encircled by a **zodiac ring** of the twelve signs and watched over by **putti** bearing astronomical instruments.

## Significance
Nearly 120 years after *De revolutionibus* (1543), Cellarius gave the [[motif:heliocentric system|heliocentric model]] a place of honour beside the Ptolemaic and Tychonic plates — a visual register of a cosmos in dispute. The retained zodiac ring shows how astrological geometry survived the move of the Earth from the centre.

## For artists & game designers
Clean nested-orbit diagram with a glowing centre — ideal for an orrery mechanic or a 'systems of the world' selector.

citations.text: Robert H. van Gent, *Andreas Cellarius: Harmonia Macrocosmica of 1660* (Taschen, 2006).

title: Northern stellar hemisphere, *Harmonia Macrocosmica*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Andreas Cellarius (engraved Schenk, Valck, Van Loon)
summary:
A plate from Andreas Cellarius's *Harmonia Macrocosmica* (first published Amsterdam, 1660), the most lavish celestial atlas of the Baroque age.

## Iconography
A circular **planisphere** projects the heavens onto the plane, its grid of meridians and the slanting band of the **ecliptic** crossing a field crowded with classical **constellation figures** — bears, serpents, heroes, and the twelve zodiac beasts drawn as living bodies. The disc is framed by an ornamental cartouche with **putti**, instruments, and a Latin title-banner.

## Significance
Cellarius gathered the cosmology of his century into 29 hand-coloured copperplates. Though presented as astronomy, the atlas preserves the figured sky on which judicial [[motif:astrology|astrology]] still depended: the constellations and the zodiac that gave the planets their dignities. Re-issued by Schenk and Valck, it became the defining image of the early-modern heavens.

## For artists & game designers
The figure-packed disc inside a heavy cartouche is a perfect template for a star-map UI or a 'sky atlas' inventory screen.

citations.text: Robert H. van Gent, *Andreas Cellarius: Harmonia Macrocosmica of 1660* (Taschen, 2006).

title: *Scenographia systematis mundani Ptolemaici*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Andreas Cellarius
summary:
The *Scenographia systematis mundani Ptolemaici*, Cellarius's perspectival 'stage-set' rendering of the Earth-centred Ptolemaic cosmos.

## Iconography
Unlike the flat planispheres, this plate tilts the universe into **perspective**: nested transparent **celestial spheres** carry the planets in order — Moon, Mercury, Venus, Sun, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn — around a central **Earth**, the outermost sphere bearing the **zodiac** and fixed stars. Robed **astronomers** observe from the foreground.

## Significance
The **Ptolemaic** geocentric cosmos was the frame within which classical and medieval [[motif:astrology|astrology]] had always operated: the spheres of the seven planets and the eighth of the fixed stars. Cellarius's *scenographia* presents this ancient world-picture as a grand theatrical machine, even as rival systems pressed upon it.

## For artists & game designers
The see-through nested spheres in dramatic perspective are a ready model for a 'celestial machine' set-piece or boss arena.

citations.text: Robert H. van Gent, *Andreas Cellarius: Harmonia Macrocosmica of 1660* (Taschen, 2006).

title: *Planisphaerium Braheum* — the Tychonic system
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Andreas Cellarius
summary:
Cellarius's *Planisphaerium Braheum*, the plate setting out the compromise cosmos of **Tycho Brahe**.

## Iconography
The **Earth** holds the centre, circled by the Moon and Sun; the five planets are shown orbiting the **Sun** as it in turn travels around the Earth — the hybrid **geo-heliocentric** arrangement. A **zodiac ring** bounds the diagram and **putti** with instruments fill the corners.

## Significance
Tycho's system (proposed 1588) kept a motionless Earth, as scripture and physics then seemed to require, while granting the elegance of Sun-centred planetary motion. Cellarius's plate enshrines it as a serious third option in the seventeenth-century debate, and its preserved [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] frame shows astronomy and astrology still sharing one map of the sky.

## For artists & game designers
A visibly 'in-between' cosmology — useful when you want a world-model that is neither fully old nor fully new.

citations.text: Robert H. van Gent, *Andreas Cellarius: Harmonia Macrocosmica of 1660* (Taschen, 2006).

title: Zodiac hemisphere map, *Harmonia Macrocosmica*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Andreas Cellarius
summary:
A Cellarius hemisphere centred on the band of the **zodiac**, isolating the twelve signs from the wider star-atlas.

## Iconography
The disc is organised around the **ecliptic**, the Sun's annual path, along which the twelve **signs of the zodiac** are drawn as their living figures — Aries the ram, Taurus the bull, Gemini the twins, and so on around the circle — with celestial latitude lines fanning out from the ecliptic poles.

## Significance
The zodiac was the working instrument of astrology: the twelvefold division of the ecliptic that located planets, assigned dignities, and framed the houses of a horoscope. By giving it a dedicated hemisphere, Cellarius made explicit the [[motif:zodiac|zodiacal]] backbone shared by astronomy and astrology alike.

## For artists & game designers
A clean, ready-made wheel of the twelve signs around an ecliptic — drop-in art for a calendar, sign-selector, or destiny mechanic.

citations.text: Robert H. van Gent, *Andreas Cellarius: Harmonia Macrocosmica of 1660* (Taschen, 2006).

title: *Imagines coeli septentrionales* — Dürer's northern star map
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Albrecht Dürer's *Imagines coeli septentrionales* (Nuremberg, 1515), the northern sheet of the first printed European star charts.

## Iconography
A circular map of the **northern hemisphere** is filled with classical **constellation figures** and the twelve **zodiac** signs ringing the ecliptic, drawn in Dürer's confident woodcut line. Four famous **astronomers** of antiquity — including Ptolemy and Aratus — occupy the corners as authorities for the sky depicted.

## Significance
Planned with the astronomers Johannes Stabius and Conrad Heinfogel, Dürer's pair of charts (north and south) translated Ptolemy's catalogue into a beautiful, reproducible image and set the visual standard for two centuries of [[motif:zodiac|figured star maps]] from Bayer to Hevelius. It marks the start of printed celestial cartography.

## For artists & game designers
The foundational 'old star chart' look — corner sages and a figure-packed disc — endlessly reusable as a lore map.

citations.text: Deborah J. Warner, *The Sky Explored: Celestial Cartography 1500–1800* (1979).
Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: Scutum (Sobieski's Shield), from Hevelius
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Johannes Hevelius
summary:
**Scutum**, the Shield, one of the new constellations introduced by **Johannes Hevelius** in his *Firmamentum Sobiescianum* (Danzig, 1687).

## Iconography
A heraldic **shield** bearing a cross is set among a sparse field of stars — the arms of King Jan III Sobieski of Poland, Hevelius's patron, raised into the heavens. The figure is drawn in the firm engraved line characteristic of Hevelius's atlas.

## Significance
Hevelius mapped the sky with naked-eye instruments to the end of the telescopic age, and invented several constellations that survive — Scutum, Lynx, Sextans, Vulpecula. Naming the **Shield** for Sobieski (victor of Vienna, 1683) shows how seventeenth-century patronage literally rewrote the constellations, a late chapter in the figured-sky tradition that fed [[motif:astrology|astrology]].

## For artists & game designers
A coat-of-arms set in the stars — a neat model for a faction sigil written into the night sky.

citations.text: Deborah J. Warner, *The Sky Explored: Celestial Cartography 1500–1800* (1979).

title: Southern hemisphere, Hevelius's *Firmamentum Sobiescianum*
work: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: Johannes Hevelius
summary:
The southern celestial hemisphere (*Emisfero Australe*) from **Johannes Hevelius**'s *Firmamentum Sobiescianum, sive Uranographia* (Danzig, 1687).

## Iconography
A full **planisphere** shows the constellation figures of the southern sky drawn, in Hevelius's manner, as if seen from *outside* the celestial sphere — so the figures face away from the viewer. Beasts, ships, and mythic forms crowd the disc along the band of the **zodiac** and the southern circles.

## Significance
Hevelius's posthumous atlas (edited by his widow Elisabetha) was among the last great hand-figured star maps and the last major one drawn from naked-eye observation. Its 'external' convention links it to the celestial-globe tradition, and its mythic figures preserve the [[motif:zodiac|astral iconography]] on which astrology depended.

## For artists & game designers
An 'inside-out' sky seen from the outside of the sphere — a fresh perspective for a celestial map or a god's-eye view.

citations.text: Deborah J. Warner, *The Sky Explored: Celestial Cartography 1500–1800* (1979).

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Frontispiece: The Race of Atalanta and Hippomenes
summary:
The engraved title page to Michael Maier's *Atalanta Fugiens* (Oppenheim, 1617; engraving by Matthäus Merian the Elder) gathers four scenes built around golden apples: the Garden of the Hesperides with its guardian dragon, Hercules reaching for the fruit, Aphrodite handing the apples to Hippomenes, and — at center — the foot-race in which the swift virgin Atalanta stoops to gather a dropped apple and so loses.

## Alchemical meaning
Maier reads the myth as a parable of the Work. Atalanta is the volatile principle (mercury, the fugitive spirit); Hippomenes the fixing principle (sulphur); the three golden apples are the interventions of art that slow the flight of the volatile so that fixation can occur. The book's title — 'Atalanta Fleeing' — names this chase between the volatile and the fixed that the fifty emblems elaborate.

## For artists & game designers
A dense classical tableau: a running maiden, a pursuing youth, a goddess, a hero, an orchard guarded by a serpent-dragon, scattered apples. Excellent reference for a mythic title-card, a foot-race set-piece, or a Hesperides garden environment.

[PLACEHOLDER: confirm whether your copy is the 1617 first or 1618 second issue from the plate state.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem I: Portavit eum ventus in ventre suo (The Wind Carried Him in Its Belly)
summary:
Motto: *Portavit eum ventus in ventre suo* — 'The wind carried him in its belly.' The engraving shows Boreas, the north wind, his belly swollen with the embryonic child of the philosophers, set against a wide landscape; the infant is being carried on the air.

## Alchemical meaning
The line is drawn from the *Tabula Smaragdina* (the Emerald Tablet): 'the wind carried it in its belly.' Maier glosses it as the volatile, airy phase in which the nascent stone is borne by 'wind' — that is, by vapour and spirit — before it can be born onto the earth (the subject of Emblem II). It opens the sequence on the principle that the matter must first be sublimed and spiritualised.

## For artists & game designers
A personified wind-god with a gravid belly over open country — useful for an air-elemental, a weather deity, or a 'spirit carried on the wind' motif.

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (Brill, 1969), pp. 58-63.
Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 1

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem II: Its nurse is the Earth
motifs: person + cauldron + skeleton + dog lamb + tree + lamb frog + tower chimney + anvil + hearth + book + castle building + child
summary:
Motto: *Its nurse is the Earth.*

## Reading
Maier continues the the me of Emblem I, now emphasizing that the earth is specifically the nurse, not the mother, of the philosophical substance. He distinguishes between generation (which occurs through the volatile principle, the wind) and nourishment (which requires the fixed, earthy body). The discourse argues that just as human food must be converted into the substance of the body it feeds, so the alchemical earth must be assimilated into the growing Stone. Maier draws a careful analogy between the stages of infant feeding — blood in utero, milk after birth, solid food later — and the progressive fixation of the volatile philosophical substance.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Nigredo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Rosarium, Tabula Smaragdina.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (Brill, 1969), pp. 64-66.
Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 2

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem III: Go to the woman who washes the sheets
motifs: cauldron + sword axe + scepter cauldron + castle building house + hearth + wall + chimney + cave + sword axe + flower
summary:
Motto: *Go to the woman who washes the sheets and do as she does.*

## Reading
Maier argues that the alchemical purification process mirrors the everyday work of washing dirty linen: just as earth-stains on fabric are removed by water and then dried by sun and wind, so the impurities of the philosophical substance must be cleansed through repeated washing and exposure to the elements. The discourse develops the principle that what comes from earth is cleaned by the next element in the sequence — water purifies earth, air dries what water has cleaned. Maier presents the was her woman as a figure for the alchemist who must patiently repeat the cycle of dissolution and drying, an operation the tradition calls the albedo or whitening stage.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Albedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Pseudo Aristotle, Rosarium, Turba.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 3

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem IV: Coniunge fratrem cum sorore (Join the Brother with the Sister)
summary:
Motto: *Coniunge fratrem cum sorore et propina illis poculum amoris* — 'Join the brother with the sister and offer them the cup of love.' The plate shows a paired man and woman — the royal siblings Sol and Luna — being brought together, often with a cup.

## Alchemical meaning
This is the *coniunctio*, the chymical wedding of the solar (sulphur, masculine, fixed) and lunar (mercury, feminine, volatile) principles — the central operation of the Work, inherited from the *Rosarium Philosophorum*. The 'incestuous' union of brother and sister signals that the two must spring from one common root (the prima materia) for the marriage to be fertile.

## For artists & game designers
A regal couple / divine-twins motif — adaptable to a marriage tableau, a sun-and-moon pairing, or paired patron deities.

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (Brill, 1969), pp. 72-76.
Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 4

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem V: Appone mulieri super mammas bufonem (Set a Toad to the Woman's Breast)
summary:
Motto: *Appone mulieri super mammas bufonem, ut ablactet eum* — 'Set a toad upon the woman's breasts that she may suckle it; let it grow fat on her milk and let the woman die.' A reclining woman gives suck to a cold, swollen toad.

## Alchemical meaning
The toad is the earthy, cold, poisonous body of the matter — heavy and chthonic; the woman's milk is the nourishing volatile or 'philosophical' moisture. As the toad fattens, the woman (the active volatile) is exhausted and 'dies': the volatile is fixed into the body. The image is a memorable figure for putrefaction and the transfer of life from spirit to body.

## For artists & game designers
A striking, slightly grotesque image — a giant toad nursing at a human breast. Strong reference for a witch's familiar, a poison/earth motif, or body-horror alchemy.

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (Brill, 1969), pp. 76-80.
Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 5

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem VI: Sow your gold in the white foliated earth
motifs: sky + tree + window + building house + flower + hearth
tower chimney + castle + castle building temple + tower chimney
summary:
Motto: *Sow your gold in the white foliated earth.*

## Reading
Maier develops an extended agricultural analogy: just as the farmer must sow grain in prepared soil to reap a harvest, the alchemist must 'sow' philosophical gold in 'white foliated earth' — a specially prepared mercurial substrate — to produce the Stone. Drawing on Plato's division of the civic body into physicians and farmers, Maier argues that the alchemist combines both roles, healing metals while cultivating them. The discourse establishes a key principle: the seed (gold) must be of the same species as what it produces, and the earth (mercury) must be receptive and properly tempered. The white, foliated quality of the earth indicates the albedo stage, where the purified substrate is ready to receive the active seed.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Albedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Jodocus Greverus, Lullius, Pseudo Aristotle, Rosarium, Senior.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]
