title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem VII: The Eaglet Falls Back into the Nest
summary:
Motto (paraphrased from Maier): a young eaglet beats its wings to leave the nest but tires and falls back into it. The plate shows a fledgling eagle and the nest from which it cannot yet escape.

## Alchemical meaning
The eagle is the volatile spirit attempting sublimation — the ascent of the purified substance. Its repeated failure and fall back into the nest figures the cycle of *solve et coagula*: the matter is raised by heat and falls again as it cools, the rising and re-descending repeated many times ('reiteration') until the spirit is fixed enough to fly free. The eagle is one of alchemy's most common ciphers for volatile mercury.

## For artists & game designers
Classic heraldic eagle plus nest — good for a phoenix/ascension beat, a bird-of-prey sigil, or a 'first flight' narrative image.

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (Brill, 1969), pp. 86-89.
Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 7

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem VIII: Accipe ovum et igneo percute gladio (Take the Egg and Strike It with a Fiery Sword)
summary:
Motto: *Accipe ovum et igneo percute gladio* — 'Take the egg and smite it with a fiery sword.' A standing figure strikes a great egg with a flaming blade.

## Alchemical meaning
The 'philosophical egg' is the sealed vessel — and, by extension, the chaos of the prima materia containing all elements in potential, like the four parts of an egg (shell, membrane, white, yolk). The fiery sword is the penetrating, dividing action of fire that opens the egg and separates the elements so the Work can proceed. It is a classic emblem of *separatio*.

## For artists & game designers
An instantly legible icon — a hero cleaving a giant egg with a burning sword. Ideal for a quest item, a 'world egg' creation myth, or a boss-reveal beat.

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (Brill, 1969), pp. 89-92.
Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 8

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem IX: Emblem IX
motifs: hearth + cave + star + person + flower + skeleton + crown + person
summary:
Motto: *Fix the old man to the tree in the garden of the dew, and the old man becomes young again.*

## Reading
Maier argues that the old man represents the fixed, aged substance of the philosophical body that must be rejuvenated through exposure to celestial dew — the volatile, purifying spirit descending from above. He develops a parallel between this image and classical accounts of rejuvenation through divine intervention, including the myth of Aeson restored to youth by Medea. The discourse emphasizes that the tree functions as the apparatus of fixation: the old body is held in place while the volatile moisture penetrates and renews it. Maier cites the Turba Philosophorum and the pronouncement of Balgus as authorities for this doctrine of rejuvenation through the marriage of fixed and volatile.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Nigredo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Merlini, Senior, Solomonic, Turba.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (Brill, 1969), pp. 92-95.
Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 9

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem X: Emblem X
motifs: altar + man old man + hearth + castle building house + gate + person + person + crown + crown + building
##rodite skeleton + star + wolf hare + sword axe torch
summary:
Motto: *Give fire to fire, Mercury to Mercury, and that is enough for you.*

## Reading
Maier develops the principle that like produces like: fire must be added to fire, and mercury to mercury, for the philosophical work to succeed. He argues against the common error of mixing incompatible substances, insisting that the alchemist must recognize the common nature hidden beneath different appearances. The discourse draws on the allegory of Saturn devouring his children, which Maier interprets as the dissolution of metals back into their mercurial prima materia. He cites the Turba Philosophorum's dictum that the Stone is nourished only by its own kind, establishing the principle of philosophical homogeneity that governs the entire citrinitas stage.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Citrinitas**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Aurora, Turba.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (Brill, 1969), pp. 95-99.

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XI: Emblem XI
motifs: person + flower + man old man + chimney + man old man + child + flower + hearth + cauldron globe drum
summary:
Motto: *Make Latona white and tear up the books.*

## Reading
Maier commands two simultaneous actions: whiten Latona and tear up the books. He develops Latona as a figure for the imperfect philosophical body — an alloy of Sol and Luna stained with black impurities — that must be purified through the albedo. De Jong traces the name Latona to 'laton,' meaning electrum or yellow silver, connecting the mythological goddess to the metallurgical process of whitening a dark alloy. The instruction to destroy books signals Maier's insistence that neither theory alone nor book-learning can substitute for practical laboratory work — the alchemist must act on matter directly, not merely read about it. The discourse argues that both intellect and manual labor are necessary, but that practice takes precedence.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Albedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Rosarium, Solomonic, Tabula Smaragdina.

## Reading
Maier continues the argument of Emblem XI, now emphasizing the bewildering variety and obscurity of alchemical writings as the reason books must be abandoned. He argues that the sheer volume of contradictory texts drives seekers to despair, and that the only path forward is direct engagement with the material. The discourse develops the Latona allegory further: she is an impure body of mixed Sol and Luna that must be found in a humble place, immersed in dung where she becomes white lead, from which red lead originates. Maier presents this as the beginning and end of the opus — the albedo whitening that initiates the final reddening toward the rubedo.

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XIII: Dropsical ore washed in Jordan

## Reading
Maier draws on the biblical story of Naaman the leper, healed by washing seven times in the Jordan, as an allegory for the purification of the philosophical ore. He argues that the 'dropsical' stone — swollen with impurities — must undergo repeated washing to be cleansed, just as Naaman's leprosy was cured only through sevenfold immersion. The discourse connects this to the albedo purification and to the sacramental symbolism of baptism as a prefiguration of alchemical cleansing. Maier cites the Clangor Buccinae as the source of the dropsical ore metaphor, linking it explicitly to the Latona-whitening sequence of the preceding emblems.

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XIV: Hic est Draco caudam suam devorans (The Dragon Devouring Its Tail)
summary:
Motto: *Hic est Draco caudam suam devorans* — 'Here is the dragon devouring its own tail.' Maier's engraving (Merian) shows the winged dragon curled into a ring, its jaws closed on the tip of its own tail — the **ouroboros**, the most ancient of alchemical signs, whose earliest surviving form appears in the *Chrysopoeia of Kleopatra*.

## Alchemical meaning
The self-devouring dragon figures the closed, self-sustaining circulation of the Work: the matter feeds upon itself, dies, and is reborn from its own substance ('one is all, and by it all, and to it all, and if it does not contain all, it is nothing'). It encodes both the unity of the prima materia and the endless turning of *solve et coagula*. Maier's epigram: 'The hungry dragon, feeding on his tail, his fainting soul relieves... eat and devour, kill and revive anew.'

## For artists & game designers
The canonical ouroboros — a ready-made emblem for cycles, eternity, infinity loops, time magic, or a serpent-ring sigil. One of the single most reusable images in the whole corpus.

## Reading
Maier presents the potter's craft as a model for the alchemical work, arguing that the artisan who shapes clay from wet and dry elements teaches the principles governing the philosophical operation. The discourse emphasizes that Nature provides the model, but art must apply it: the potter does not invent the properties of clay but learns to work with them. ## Reading
Maier develops the two lions as figures for the volatile and fixed principles of the alchemical work. The winged lion represents the philosophical mercury — fugitive, aerial, unstable — while the wingless lion is the philosophical sulphur — earthy, fixed, enduring. He argues that mastery requires capturing the winged lion and joining it to its wingless counterpart, producing a stable union of volatile and fixed. The discourse connects the lion's magnanimity in classical natural history to the nobility of the alchemical substances, and draws on the solar symbolism of the lion as the king of beasts to establish its connection to gold and the citrinitas stage of yellowing.

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XVII: Fourfold fire-ball
motifs: window + vessel + moon + cauldron + lamp globe + cauldron + cauldron + river sea + gate door + chimney + tree + man old man + building + window + book + cloud + chimney
summary:
Motto: *A fourfold fire-ball controls this work.*

## Reading
Maier argues that the alchemical work requires not one fire but four distinct types, each governing a different phase of the operation. He identifies these as the fire of the lamp (external heat), the fire of the ash-bath (gentle, sustained warmth), the fire of the substance it self (intrinsic reactive heat), and the fire of the water-bath (moist, temperate heat). The discourse develops the principle that fire transforms by making things fiery — communicating its own nature — but can not make gold directly, only prepare the conditions for gold's emergence. Maier cites this fourfold fire as the master key to the practical art, without which even correct materials will fail.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Citrinitas**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Senior.

## Reading
Maier develops the idea that the four elements or components of the philosophical substance are so intimately connected that destroying one annihilates all. He argues that this demonstrates their essential unity — they are not truly four separate things but four aspects of a single body. The discourse draws on the myth of Geryon, the three-bodied king of Spain, and his pure-bred cattle guarded by the dog Orthrus, developing this as an allegory for the composite nature of the prima materia. Maier uses this to warn the alchemist against violent separation: the elements must be gently distinguished, not forcibly torn apart, lest the entire work be destroyed.

Maier articulates one of alchemy's foundational maxims: Nature teaches Nature, Nature conquers Nature, Nature rules Nature. He argues that the alchemist must learn by observing natural processes, then apply those lessons to guide and finally master the transformations of matter. The discourse connects this to Democritus's principle that art imitates nature, and extends it: the alchemical art does not merely copy natural processes but accelerates and perfects them. Maier cites the washing of dirty linen (Emblem III) as an example — Nature provides rain and sun, and the was her woman learns to replicate these cleansing agents.

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XXI: The Squared Circle of the Philosophers' Stone
summary:
One of the most famous images in all of alchemy. A philosopher-geometer, giant compasses in hand, inscribes on a wall the figure of a man and woman standing inside a small circle, enclosed by a square, enclosed by a triangle, enclosed in turn by the great outer circle.

## Alchemical meaning
The motto encodes the recipe as geometry: 'Make of the man and woman a circle, then a square, out of the square a triangle, make again a circle, and you shall have the Stone of the Philosophers.' The nested figures map the reduction of the matter through the four elements (square), the three principles — sulphur, mercury, salt (triangle), the body-soul-spirit, and back to unity (the final circle). John Michell and Allan Brown noted that the design also encodes a near-perfect squaring of the circle (equal areas), tying the alchemical to the geometric quest.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'sacred geometry' diagram — circle/square/triangle nesting with a compass-bearing sage. Perfect for a ritual circle, a puzzle glyph, a crafting-recipe UI, or an arcane blueprint.

## Reading
Maier instructs that once the white lead (the product of the albedo) has been obtained, the alchemist must perform 'women's work' — that is, cook the substance with steady, patient heat. He develops cooking as the central metaphor for the post-albedo operations, arguing that the white substance must be slowly heated and matured, just as food is transformed by cooking from raw to nourishing. The discourse emphasizes patience and temperature control: the philosophical substance must simmer, not boil. Maier draws on the herms — three-forked road markers — as guides that show the way to straying travelers, an allegory for the textual guideposts left by earlier alchemists.

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XXIV: The Wolf Devours the King, and Fire Restores Him
summary:
Motto: *Regem lupus voravit, et vita sibi reddidit* — 'A wolf devoured the king, and being burnt restored him to life again.' The plate shows a grey wolf consuming a crowned king, with a fire nearby in which the wolf is to be burned.

## Alchemical meaning
The 'grey wolf' is *lupus metallorum* — stibnite/antimony, used to purify gold (the King). The wolf devours the king (antimony dissolves and carries off impure gold); the wolf is then burnt away, leaving the king reborn purer than before. It is a vivid figure for purification by an aggressive solvent and the death-and-rebirth of the noble metal.

## For artists & game designers
A crowned king being devoured by a wolf beside a fire — potent imagery for a downfall-and-return arc, a beast-king confrontation, or an antimony/poison crafting motif.

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XXXVI: Emblem XXXVI
motifs: tower chimney + window + horse + sword axe + tower chimney + flower + old man + castle temple + window + old man + river sea + altar + building house + eagle peacock butterfly + phoenix + tree + sky
summary:
Motto: *The Stone has been thrown on to the earth and lifted on to the mountains, and it dwells in the air and feeds in the river.*

## Reading
Maier develops the paradox that the philosopher's stone has been thrown on to the earth (despised, found in humble places) yet lifted on to mountains (exalted, valued above all things). He argues that the stone dwells in the air and feeds in the river — occupying all four elements simultaneously — because it is the quintessence that participates in all elemental natures without being limited to any one. The discourse draws on the biblical metaphor of the rejected cornerstone and connects it to the alchemical tradition that the prima materia is found every where yet recognized by none. De Jong identifies a nationalistic element in Maier's treatment, connecting the earth-as-mother imagery to German territorial symbolism.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Nigredo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Solomonic.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

citations.text: Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 36

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XXXVII: The Green Lion
summary:
Motto: 'Three things suffice for the mastery: the white fume, that is water; the green lion, that is the brass (or 'glass') of Hermes; and the stinking water (aqua faetida).' The plate features the **Green Lion** among the agents of the Work.

## Alchemical meaning
The Green Lion is one of alchemy's richest ciphers: variously green vitriol, the crude unripe matter, or the powerful solvent that 'devours the sun' (dissolves gold) — frequently pictured elsewhere eating the sun and bleeding. Here it stands among the three essentials of the magistery alongside the volatile 'white fume' and the corrosive 'stinking water.' Greenness signals unripeness and vital, untamed power.

## For artists & game designers
The Green Lion is a fan-favourite alchemical creature — ideal for a verdant beast, an acid/solvent monster, or an emblem of raw untamed prime-matter.

[PLACEHOLDER: confirm the exact figural composition of this plate against your scan — the green lion is sometimes shown devouring the sun in related prints.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XXXVIII: Standing hermaphrodite
motifs: man old man + child + sword axe + person old man + person + cave
summary:
Motto: *The Hermaphrodite, born of two mountains, of Mercury and Venus.*

## Reading
Maier presents the standing Hermaphrodite — born of the two mountains of Mercury and Venus — as the completed product of the alchemical work. He develops Socrates' declaration that he was a citizen not of one city but of the world as an analogy for the Hermaphrodite who inhabits two domains simultaneously. The discourse argues that the Rebis has been successfully produced: the philosophical Mercury and Venus have been permanently united, and the resulting substance stands on its own — no longer needing the fire that revived it from darkness (Emblems XXX, XXXIII). Maier frames this as the culmination of the rubedo, where the joined substance achieves autonomous stability.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Rubedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Turba.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XXXIX: Emblem XXXIX
motifs: castle + castle + tower chimney + tower chimney + tower chimney + crown
summary:
Motto: *Oedipus, having conquered the Sphinx and having killed his father Laius, married his mother Jocasta.*

## Reading
Maier retells the Oedipus myth as an alchemical allegory: conquering the Sphinx (solving the riddle of Nature) leads to killing the father (destroying the original metallic form) and marrying the mother (reuniting with the prima materia from which the metal was born). He argues that this seemingly transgressive sequence — patricide and incest — is actually the normal order of alchemical transformation, where the product must dissolve its parent substance and recombine with its own origin. The discourse frames the Sphinx's riddle as the central question of alchemy: what walks on four legs, then two, then three? Maier reads this as the three stages of the Stone — unstable, bipedal, and finally supported by the 'third leg' of the tincture.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Albedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Lullius, Senior, Turba.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XL: Emblem XL
motifs: tower chimney + building house + door + castle + cave
crown + sword axe + person man old man + altar + hourglass + hearth
summary:
Motto: *Make one water out of two waters, and it will be the water of holiness.*

## Reading
Maier instructs the alchemist to make one water from two waters, producing the 'water of holiness.' He develops this as the final mercurial conjunction: the two philosophical waters — one volatile and ascending, the other fixed and descending — must be combined into a single, unified menstruum. The discourse connects this to the Hermetic tradition of the two serpents of the caduceus and to the mystical waters of baptism that cleanse and renew. Maier argues that this united water is the universal solvent that can dissolve all metals and serve as the medium for the final projection. The rubedo is achieved when the two contrary waters cease to separate and remain permanently joined.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Rubedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Pseudo Aristotle.

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLI: Emblem XLI
motifs: lamb frog + sword spear axe + person + mirror + cauldron
summary:
Motto: *Adonis is killed by a boar, and Venus, rushing up to him, painted the roses red with her blood.*

## Reading
Maier retells the death of Adonis — beloved of Venus, killed by a boar, his blood dyeing white roses red — as an allegory for the transition from albedo to rubedo. He argues that the beautiful, white philosophical substance (Adonis/the white stone) must be 'killed' — subjected to a final violent transformation — and that the resulting reddening (Venus's blood on the roses) produces the red tincture. The discourse develops the boar as a figure for the aggressive sulphurous agent that destroys the passive, beautiful white form. Maier cites Ovid's Metamorphoses as his primary source, while De Jong traces the alchemical reading of this myth through the Rosarium tradition.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Nigredo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Ovid, Rosarium, Solomonic.

citations.text: Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 41

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLII: Emblem XLII
summary:
Motto: *What Nature, Reason, Experience and Reading teach you, let that be the fire, the vessel, the water and the earth.*

## Reading
Maier develops the Tabula Smaragdina's teaching by identifying four guides that together constitute the alchemical method: Nature provides the raw material and model, Reason supplies theoretical understanding, Experience offers practical knowledge gained through trial, and Reading transmits the wisdom of earlier masters. He argues that these four correspond to the fire, the vessel, the water, and the earth of the work — each indispensable, none sufficient alone. The discourse emphasizes the balance between textual learning and hands-on practice, a the me running through the entire Atalanta Fugiens. Maier presents this emblem as a methodological summation of the principles scattered across the preceding forty-one.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Rubedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Aurora, Lambsprinck, Lullius, Ovid, Pseudo Aristotle, Solomonic, Tabula Smaragdina.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLIII: Emblem XLIII
summary:
Motto: *Listen to the screech owl's voice, which it produces it self, and the water and do not heed any bird that cries in the evening.*

## Reading
Maier instructs the alchemist to listen to the screech owl's voice and the sound of running water while ignoring the cries of evening birds — a parable about discerning true signals from false. He develops the owl as a figure for philosophical mercury (the lunar, nocturnal principle) whose call reveals the correct path, while the other birds represent misleading doctrines and false alchemists. The discourse connects the biblical story of Naaman (Emblem XIII) to this emblem, reprising the the me of purification through water. Maier argues that the alchemist must develop sensory discernment — learning to hear and see what Nature reveals — rather than following the loud but empty promises of charlatans.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Albedo**.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLIV: Typhon Dismembers Osiris; Isis Gathers Him
summary:
Motto: 'Typhon kills Osiris by treachery and scatters his limbs, but renowned Isis gathers them together again.' The plate depicts the Egyptian myth of the dismembered god and the goddess who reassembles him.

## Alchemical meaning
Maier — true to the 'mytho-alchemical' method of his *Arcana Arcanissima* — reads the Osiris myth as the Work: the body of the matter is broken apart and scattered (*separatio*, *putrefactio*) by the destructive principle (Typhon/Set), then patiently re-collected and made whole (*coagulatio*) by the conserving principle (Isis). Death and reintegration are the rhythm of the magistery.

## For artists & game designers
A dismemberment-and-restoration myth — useful for a resurrection quest, a 'gather the scattered pieces' mechanic, or an Egyptian-deity tableau.

citations.text: Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 44

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLV: Emblem XLV
summary:
Motto: *Sol and his shadow complete the work.*

## Reading
Maier argues that Sol and his shadow together complete the work — the visible, noble gold (Sol) must be joined with its shadowy, hidden counterpart (the impure, dark aspect) to produce the philosopher's stone. He develops the principle that the stone requires both the light and dark aspects of the same substance, and that ignoring the shadow — the nigredo remainder — makes completion impossible. The discourse draws on the Consilium Conjugii and the Massa Solis et Lunae tradition to argue that the two stones spoken of by the philosophers are actually the manifest and hidden aspects of a single substance. Maier presents this penultimate teaching as the key to unlocking the entire series.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Rubedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Lambsprinck, Pseudo Aristotle.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLVI: Emblem XLVI
motifs: crown + old man + sword axe + sword axe + mountain hill + castle + wall window + ring
summary:
Motto: *Two eagles meet, the one from the East, the other from the West.*

## Reading
Maier develops the meeting of two eagles — one from the East, the other from the West — as an allegory for the conjunction of the two philosophical principles that originate from opposite ends of the alchemical process. He identifies the Eastern eagle with sulphur (the hot, solar principle born from fire) and the Western eagle with mercury (the cool, lunar principle born from water). Their meeting in mid-air represents the volatile conjunction where both principles abandon their fixed natures and unite in the aerial, spiritual realm. The discourse connects the circumnavigation imagery — east to west, west to east — to the Hermetic principle of universal circulation, where all things return to their origin.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Citrinitas**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Pseudo Aristotle.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLVII: Emblem XLVII
motifs: angel king man witch + hearth + angel old man + bridge
summary:
Motto: *The wolf coming from the East, and the Dog coming from the West, have bitten each other.*

## Reading
Maier parallels the eagle meeting of Emblem XLVI with a more violent version: the wolf from the East and the dog from the West bite each other in mutual destruction. He argues that while the eagles' conjunction was aerial and spiritual, the wolf-and-dog encounter is terrestrial and material — the same union of opposites enacted at a lower, more physical register. The discourse develops the wolf as the more aggressive, dissolving agent (philosophical mercury in its corrosive aspect) and the dog as the resistant, fixed body that must be broken down. Their mutual biting — each wounding the other — represents the reciprocal action of dissolution, where both substances are transformed by their encounter.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Nigredo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Lambsprinck, Pseudo Aristotle, Senior.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLVIII: King Duenech receives potion
motifs: cup + altar + person man old man + woman + hearth + sword axe + hourglass + globe + person + castle building house + cup + sword axe torch + star + crown + window + door window + window
summary:
Motto: *The king, fallen ill from drinking, is restored to health by a physician.*

## Reading
Maier develops the Merlini allegory of King Duenech in its final phase: the king, fallen ill from drinking foul waters, is restored to health by physicians who administer the correct elixir. He draws on Xerxes drinking muddy water in the desert as an analogy for the philosophical substance contaminated by impurities. The discourse describes the king's cure through successive stages — first Egyptian, then Alexandrian physicians — corresponding to different alchemical traditions' approaches to purification. Bernard of Treves's allegory describes the restored king dressed in a black cuirass, white tunic, and purple-red cloak — the three colors of the opus (nigredo, albedo, rubedo) worn simultaneously, signaling the completion of the work.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Rubedo**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Lullius, Merlini.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem XLIX: Emblem XLIX
motifs: crown + cauldron anvil + person + man old man + woman + person + man old man + scepter + person + woman
summary:
Motto: *The Philosophical Child acknowledges three fathers, just as Orion.*

## Reading
Maier argues that the philosopher's stone, like the mythological Orion, acknowledges three fathers — three distinct sources or principles that contribute to its generation. He develops this as an allegory for the tria prima: the stone is born from salt (the bodily principle), sulphur (the soul principle), and mercury (the spirit principle), each 'fat he ring' a different aspect of the complete substance. The discourse draws on the Orion myth — conceived from the combined seed of Zeus, Poseidon, and Hermes — to argue that no single source suffices for the generation of the philosophical child. Maier presents this as one of the final teachings: the stone's triple origin explains why it can not be produced from any single starting material.

## In the Work
Alchemical phase: **Citrinitas**. Maier's acknowledged sources here include Lullius, Ovid, Robertus Vallensis.

[PLACEHOLDER: cross-check against H. M. E. de Jong's source commentary and add a note on the distinct visual elements present for asset use.]

citations.text: Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 49

title: Atalanta Fugiens — Emblem L: The Dragon and the Woman Slay Each Other and Bathe in Blood
summary:
Motto (the fiftieth and final emblem): 'The dragon slays the woman and she him, and together they are drenched in blood.' The closing plate shows the mutual slaying of the winged dragon and the woman.

## Alchemical meaning
The last emblem seals the sequence with a final, reciprocal *coniunctio*: the volatile (dragon) and the fixed/feminine (woman) destroy one another and are dissolved together in the 'blood' — the red tincture. Their mutual death is the death that produces the Stone; the bath of blood signals the *rubedo*, the reddening that crowns the Work. After fifty emblems of chase, the fleeing Atalanta is at last caught.

## For artists & game designers
A climactic dragon-versus-woman duel ending in a bath of blood — a natural finale image: a final-boss tableau, a blood-magic rite, or the red 'completion' card of a set.

citations.text: Furnace and Fugue — digital edition, Emblem 50

title: Aaron's Rod Becomes a Serpent before Pharaoh
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Anonymous (The Bible Panorama, 1891)
rights: No restrictions. Via Wikimedia Commons (Internet Archive Book Images).
summary:
A late-Victorian book illustration of the rod-to-serpent miracle, from *The Bible Panorama* (1891).

## Iconography
Moses and Aaron stand before the enthroned Pharaoh; Aaron's cast-down rod has become a serpent on the palace floor while the court magicians prepare their answer. The engraving renders the Exodus contest in the crisp narrative style of nineteenth-century scripture-illustration.

## Significance
The scene visualizes Exodus 7, the opening move of the plague cycle, in which the [[motif:miracle contest|sign-contest]] between Moses and Pharaoh's wise men begins. Mass-produced devotional and educational picture-Bibles like this one carried the iconography of biblical magic into Victorian households, fixing how generations imagined the showdown at the Egyptian court.

## For artists & game designers
The popular-print clarity here — single serpent, reacting court — is useful reference for staging a magic duel readably, without Baroque clutter.

title: Balaam and the Ass
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Rembrandt van Rijn
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons (Musée Cognacq-Jay).
summary:
Rembrandt's early *Balaam and the Ass* (Musée Cognacq-Jay, Paris), the seer beating his donkey before the unseen angel.

## Iconography
The pagan diviner Balaam raises a staff to strike his stalled donkey, which turns its head to speak; above, an angel with a drawn sword — visible to the beast but not the man — bars the road. Rembrandt heightens the comedy and terror of the moment.

## Significance
Numbers 22 tells of Balaam, a Mesopotamian [[motif:seer|seer]] hired by Moab to curse Israel, whose [[motif:talking ass|donkey]] perceives the blocking angel that the professional diviner cannot. Forced instead to bless Israel, Balaam became the ambiguous figure of the prophet-for-hire whose occult sight is humbled by a beast's. The talking ass is one of scripture's strangest supernatural moments.

## For artists & game designers
The creature that sees the hidden threat its master cannot is a fine "animal companion senses the unseen" device — perception inverted between human and beast.

title: Belshazzar's Feast
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Rembrandt van Rijn
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons (National Gallery, London).
summary:
Rembrandt's *Belshazzar's Feast* (National Gallery, London), the moment a disembodied hand writes the doom of Babylon on the wall.

## Iconography
The Babylonian king Belshazzar lurches back from the table, eyes wide and goblet spilling, as a glowing hand inscribes Hebrew/Aramaic letters in mid-air; courtiers and a startled woman recoil. The famous inscription is set vertically, reflecting a rabbinic explanation for why the king's own sages could not read it.

## Significance
Daniel 5 recounts the *mene mene tekel upharsin* — the spectral [[motif:writing on the wall|writing on the wall]] at Belshazzar's sacrilegious banquet, which only Daniel can interpret as the kingdom's imminent fall. Rembrandt drew the inscription's unusual arrangement from his neighbour, the scholar Menasseh ben Israel. The scene is the supreme biblical image of a supernatural omen.

## For artists & game designers
The glowing, free-floating hand writing a cryptic doom is an iconic "prophetic omen" effect — a single supernatural intrusion that reframes an entire scene.

title: Moses and the Brazen Serpent
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Sébastien Bourdon
summary:
Sébastien Bourdon's classicizing *Moses and the Brazen Serpent*, a measured French treatment of the healing miracle.

## Iconography
Amid a landscape strewn with serpent-bitten Israelites, Moses directs the afflicted toward the bronze serpent on its standard; the figures are arranged in Poussin-like friezes of grief and supplication. The palette is cool and the staging legible.

## Significance
Bourdon, a founder of the French Académie, treats Numbers 21 with the restraint of seventeenth-century classicism, subordinating Baroque turbulence to clear narrative order. The [[motif:brazen serpent|raised serpent]] remains the contested object — at once divinely commanded cure and the future idol *Nehushtan* — here presented as a lesson in obedient faith.

## For artists & game designers
Compare with Rubens's version: Bourdon shows how the same magical-healing episode can be staged for calm legibility rather than visceral chaos.

title: The Brazen Serpent (Sistine Chapel pendentive)
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Michelangelo Buonarroti
summary:
Michelangelo's pendentive fresco of the Brazen Serpent in the Sistine Chapel, a roiling mass of bodies bitten and healed.

## Iconography
On the right, Israelites writhe and die under an attack of fiery serpents; on the left, those who turn to gaze upon the bronze serpent raised on a pole by Moses are saved. The two halves split the composition between agony and deliverance.

## Significance
Numbers 21 recounts that God sent venomous snakes against the murmuring Israelites, then bade Moses raise a [[motif:brazen serpent|bronze serpent]] (the *Nehushtan*) whose mere sight cured the bitten — a healing image so potent it was later worshipped as an idol and destroyed (2 Kings 18). The Gospel of John reads it as a type of the Crucifixion. The episode hovers between sanctioned talisman and forbidden idol.

## For artists & game designers
The split-field design — death on one side, salvation on the other, hinged on a single raised object — is a powerful layout for any "look upon the relic to be cured" set-piece.

title: Moses and the Brazen Serpent
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Peter Paul Rubens
summary:
Rubens's Baroque *Moses and the Brazen Serpent* (National Gallery, London), a dramatic crowd of the stricken turning toward the raised bronze sign.

## Iconography
Moses gestures toward the serpent coiled on its pole; below, muscular figures entwined with snakes twist in pain, while others lift their faces toward the healing image. Rubens floods the scene with diagonal energy and chiaroscuro.

## Significance
The painting renders Numbers 21, in which gazing on the [[motif:brazen serpent|bronze serpent]] cures snakebite — an image medieval and Reformation theologians treated as a type of Christ "lifted up." Rubens, the great Counter-Reformation narrator, emphasizes the bodily drama of plague and rescue, making the talismanic sign the visual hinge of life and death.

## For artists & game designers
Rubens's tangle of serpent-wrapped bodies is prime reference for a poison/curse outbreak crowd, with a single elevated object as the salvation focal point.

title: Daniel Interpreting Nebuchadnezzar's Dream
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: W. A. Spicer (illustration)
summary:
A book illustration of Daniel revealing and interpreting King Nebuchadnezzar's dream of the great statue.

## Iconography
Daniel stands before the Babylonian king, gesturing as he expounds the dream the king's own magicians could not recover; behind or above looms the colossal image with its head of gold and feet of clay. The picture stages prophecy triumphing over court divination.

## Significance
Daniel 2 sets the Hebrew seer against the Chaldean "magicians, enchanters, sorcerers, and astrologers" who fail to tell the king his forgotten dream. Daniel succeeds not by [[motif:divination|divinatory art]] but by revelation, interpreting the statue as a succession of empires. The chapter became a key biblical statement that true foreknowledge comes from God, not occult technique.

## For artists & game designers
The "interpreter who reads the vision the court experts cannot" is a strong NPC beat — knowledge as the decisive power in a throne-room scene.

title: Elymas the Sorcerer Struck Blind
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: after Raphael (Rijksmuseum print)
summary:
An engraving after Raphael's tapestry cartoon *The Blinding of Elymas*, depicting the sorcerer struck blind by St Paul at Paphos.

## Iconography
Elymas (Bar-Jesus) stands at center, arms outstretched and eyes darkened, groping in sudden blindness while the seated proconsul Sergius Paulus and his court react. Paul, at left, pronounces the judgment. The print transmits Raphael's monumental design from the Sistine tapestry series.

## Significance
In Acts 13 the magus Bar-Jesus, attached to the Roman proconsul, opposes Paul and is struck with temporary blindness — a sign that converts the official. The scene is a touchstone for the Renaissance theme of the [[motif:false prophet|false prophet]] overthrown; Raphael's cartoons (now V&A) and the tapestries woven from them in Brussels were among the most influential images of the era.

## For artists & game designers
Elymas's groping, light-deprived stance is a textbook "blinded" status pose — outstretched, off-balance, hands searching the air.

title: The Shade of Samuel Invoked by Saul (Witch of Endor)
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Salvator Rosa
summary:
Salvator Rosa's *The Shade of Samuel Invoked by Saul at the Cave of Endor* (Louvre), one of the most famous necromancy paintings of the Baroque.

## Iconography
The medium of Endor recoils as the shrouded ghost of the prophet Samuel rises in pale light; King Saul prostrates himself in terror on the cave floor while attendants shrink back. Rosa's signature gloom and ragged figures heighten the dread.

## Significance
In 1 Samuel 28 the doomed Saul, having banned mediums, secretly consults the woman of Endor to raise Samuel, who foretells his death in battle. The episode is the Bible's central [[motif:necromancy|necromancy]] text and was endlessly debated — was it the true Samuel, a demon, or fraud? Rosa, himself drawn to witchcraft subjects, made it a meditation on forbidden knowledge and fate.

## For artists & game designers
The rising shroud-wrapped revenant against a cowering king is a master template for a summoned-prophecy beat: the conjuror gets an answer, but it is a sentence of doom.

title: The Adoration of the Golden Calf
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Nicolas Poussin
summary:
Nicolas Poussin's *The Adoration of the Golden Calf* (National Gallery, London), the Israelites' ecstatic idol-dance below Sinai.

## Iconography
A circle of figures dances around the gilded calf raised on a pedestal while Aaron, in white, presides; in the far distance Moses descends the mountain and hurls down the tablets. Poussin choreographs the apostasy as a frenzied bacchic round.

## Significance
Exodus 32 narrates how, in Moses's absence, the people compel Aaron to cast a [[motif:golden calf|golden calf]] and worship it with revelry — the archetypal act of biblical idolatry, conflated in later art with pagan rite and magical fertility-cult. Poussin draws explicitly on antique bacchanal imagery, making the scene a study of false worship as intoxicated misrule.

## For artists & game designers
The ring-dance around a raised idol is the definitive "cult ritual in progress" composition — circular motion, central effigy, a presiding officiant.

title: Jacob's Dream (The Ladder of Jacob)
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: after Jusepe de Ribera (Rijksmuseum photo-reproduction)
rights: CC0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Rijksmuseum reproduction of the Prado painting).
summary:
A reproduction of Jusepe de Ribera's *Jacob's Dream* (Prado), the patriarch asleep beneath a vision of angels.

## Iconography
Jacob lies sleeping against a bank, head on a stone, while a soft burst of light and the dim forms of ascending angels open above him. Ribera, unusually, downplays the architectural ladder in favour of a luminous, almost mystical haze.

## Significance
Genesis 28 recounts Jacob's dream at Bethel of a [[motif:jacobs ladder|ladder]] set between earth and heaven with angels ascending and descending, and the Lord above promising the land — a [[motif:theophany|theophany]] that made the site "the gate of heaven." The ladder became a central symbol in mystical and Kabbalistic thought of graded ascent between worlds. Ribera renders the revelation as inward, contemplative experience.

## For artists & game designers
The sleeping figure beneath a vertical shaft of light and rising figures is a model for a "dream-portal" vision scene — the threshold between realms shown as luminance, not machinery.

title: The Journey of the Magi
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Sassetta (Stefano di Giovanni)
rights: CC0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Metropolitan Museum of Art).
summary:
Sassetta's *The Journey of the Magi* (Metropolitan Museum), the wise men following the star toward Bethlehem.

## Iconography
The Magi and their retinue wind down a hillside; above, the star of Bethlehem streaks across a gold sky, sometimes rendered as a comet. The richly dressed travellers gaze upward, guided by the celestial sign.

## Significance
Matthew 2 calls them *magoi* — the term for Persian-Zoroastrian priest-[[motif:astrologers|astrologers]] — who read a new [[motif:star of bethlehem|star]] as the sign of a king's birth. The episode is the Bible's most positive image of astral divination, and Christian tradition long debated whether it sanctioned astrology or merely used it as a vehicle of revelation. Their star inspired Giotto's comet and centuries of cometary iconography.

## For artists & game designers
The star-led caravan is a clean "follow the sign" travel motif: an unmistakable celestial waypoint drawing a procession across the landscape.

title: The Rods of Moses and the Magicians Turned into Serpents
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Gerard Hoet
summary:
Gerard Hoet's engraving of the contest before Pharaoh, where Aaron's rod becomes a serpent that devours those of the Egyptian sorcerers.

## Iconography
In Pharaoh's court, rods cast to the ground writhe into serpents; Aaron's snake swallows those conjured by the magicians Jannes and Jambres, while Moses and Aaron stand before the enthroned king. The Dutch print transmits the scene as a stately courtroom confrontation.

## Significance
Exodus 7 stages the plagues as a [[motif:miracle contest|magic contest]]: Pharaoh's wise men replicate the rod-to-serpent wonder "by their secret arts," yet Aaron's serpent consumes theirs. Later tradition named the magicians Jannes and Jambres (cited in 2 Timothy 3:8 and a lost apocryphon). The episode was the classic proof-text for distinguishing divine miracle from demonic imitation.

## For artists & game designers
The duel of transmuted staffs — one snake eating the others — is an elegant "my summon beats your summon" mechanic rendered as pure spectacle.

title: The Queen of Sheba Meets Solomon
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Piero della Francesca
summary:
Piero della Francesca's fresco of the Queen of Sheba meeting Solomon, from the *Legend of the True Cross* cycle at Arezzo.

## Iconography
The scene splits into two: the queen's procession kneels before the wood of a future cross, then she meets the enthroned Solomon amid attendant courtiers. Piero's grave, geometric figures and clear light render the encounter as solemn ceremony.

## Significance
1 Kings 10 tells how the Queen of Sheba comes to test Solomon with "hard questions" — riddles and enigmas — and is awed by his God-given [[motif:wisdom|wisdom]]. In later legend (and the *True Cross* tradition Piero illustrates) she becomes a prophetess who venerates the sacred wood. Sheba accrued associations with arcane knowledge, riddle-lore, and, in some traditions, occult power.

## For artists & game designers
The "contest of riddles before the wise king" is a ready quest frame: a powerful visitor arrives to test the hero's knowledge rather than fight.

citations.text: *1 Kings* 10:1–13; Jacobus de Voragine, *Golden Legend* (True Cross).

title: The Fall of Simon Magus (and the Conversion of St Paul)
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Jan Rombouts (attributed)
rights: CC0. Via Wikimedia Commons (Museum M, Leuven).
summary:
A Southern-Netherlandish panel attributed to Jan Rombouts pairing the Fall of Simon Magus with the Conversion of St Paul.

## Iconography
Simon, the sorcerer of Samaria, is shown crashing earthward as the demons who bore him aloft scatter; below, the apostles Peter and Paul kneel in prayer, their intercession breaking the magician's flight. The composition stages the contest as a vertical plunge from the sky.

## Significance
The story, told in Acts 8 (where Simon offers money for the apostles' power — the origin of "simony") and elaborated in the apocryphal *Acts of Peter*, became the archetypal image of false [[motif:sorcery|sorcery]] overthrown by true miracle. Simon's flight before Nero, sustained by demons until Peter's prayer fells him, was read for centuries as the defeat of magic by apostolic grace.

## For artists & game designers
The falling levitator with his demon-bearers dispersing is a compact visual for a "power source severed" defeat — the magician is undone not by force but by the withdrawal of his unholy support.

citations.text: *Acts of the Apostles* 8:9–24; apocryphal *Acts of Peter*.

title: The Transfiguration
work: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio)
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons (Vatican Museums).
summary:
Raphael's final masterpiece *The Transfiguration* (Vatican Museums), uniting Christ's radiant ascent with the failed exorcism below.

## Iconography
In the upper register Christ hovers in dazzling light between Moses and Elijah while the apostles fall back blinded; in the lower, a convulsing possessed boy is brought to the helpless disciples, who point upward for aid. The two halves contrast divine glory and earthly affliction.

## Significance
The painting fuses two adjacent Gospel episodes (Matthew 17): the [[motif:transfiguration|Transfiguration]], where Christ's nature shines forth amid the appearance of the dead prophets, and the [[motif:exorcism|exorcism]] of the demoniac boy the apostles could not cure. Together they juxtapose the supreme theophany with a demonic affliction healed only by the descending power — a meditation on the spectrum of the supernatural.

## For artists & game designers
The two-tier composition — luminous power above, a crisis demanding it below — is a superb template for staging the relationship between a source of power and the need that calls it down.

title: Saul and the Witch of Endor (van Oostsanen)
work: Magic in the Bible
creator: Jacob Cornelisz. van Oostsanen
summary:
**Jacob Cornelisz. van Oostsanen's** 1526 panel — one of the earliest and richest paintings of the **Witch of Endor** (1 Samuel 28), the Bible's great scene of necromancy.

## Iconography
King Saul kneels before the witch, who works inside a magic **circle** strewn with bones and inscribed with names; the ghost of the prophet **Samuel** rises in a shroud to foretell the king's doom. Van Oostsanen crowds the background with a full [[tradition:witchcraft|witches' sabbath]] — flights, demons, cauldrons.

## Significance
The Endor episode (Iconclass 71H31) was *the* scriptural warrant for debating the reality of magic: did the witch truly raise Samuel, or did a demon counterfeit him? Painted across the whole history of art (van Oostsanen, Rosa, West, Blake), it is the keystone of **biblical-magic iconography**.

## For artists & game designers
A complete necromancy tableau — circle, bones, rising ghost, terrified king.

citations.text: Iconclass 71H31 — Saul and the witch of Endor.

title: *Albion Rose* (Glad Day)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: c.1793–1796 (colour-printed etching, from *A Large Book of Designs*)
summary:
*Albion Rose*, also called *Glad Day*, depicts the youth Albion springing up in a radiant burst of light with arms flung wide.

## Iconography
A naked young man stands on a rocky height in a cruciform pose, arms spread and one foot lifted as if dancing, surrounded by a brilliant rainbow nimbus of light. At his feet a moth and a worm-like creature emerge. His face is rapt and joyful.

## Significance
Albion is Blake's personification of England and of universal humanity; his rising is an emblem of spiritual awakening and liberation from materialist bondage. An inscription on one impression reads that Albion 'rose from where he labour'd at the Mill with Slaves, / Giving himself for the Nations he danc'd the dance of Eternal Death,' fusing [[motif:imagination as the divine body|self-sacrifice]] with resurrection. The pose echoes the Vitruvian man, reclaiming Renaissance proportion for visionary ends.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'awakening / ascension' pose: a figure exploding into radiance, arms wide, the instant the spirit breaks free.

title: The Ancient of Days (frontispiece to *Europe a Prophecy*)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: 1794 (this copy K, relief etching with watercolour)
summary:
*The Ancient of Days*, Blake's frontispiece to *Europe a Prophecy* (1794), shows the god-figure Urizen crouching within a blazing sun-disc and reaching down with a golden compass to circumscribe the dark deep below.

## Iconography
A muscular, white-bearded old man kneels inside a solar orb, his hair and beard streaming sideways in a cosmic wind. He leans out of the disc and lowers his left hand, the two splayed fingers forming a pair of compasses that cast rays of dividing light into the formless dark. The composition is severely symmetrical and architectonic.

## Significance
The figure is [[motif:urizen the measurer|Urizen]], Blake's embodiment of bounding reason and law, performing the act described in Proverbs 8:27, 'when he set a compass upon the face of the depth.' For Blake this is not pure creation but a fall into limitation — the imposition of measure, number, and weight upon the infinite. The image fuses the Demiurge of Gnostic thought with the geometer-God of Newtonian deism; it is the visual antithesis of [[motif:imagination as the divine body|imaginative liberty]]. Blake reportedly saw the design hovering at the top of his staircase.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive icon of the cosmic architect / world-bounding demiurge — a god who creates by measuring rather than loving.

title: Behemoth and Leviathan (Book of Job, plate 15)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
From Blake's Book of Job illustrations, this design shows God displaying to Job the two primeval monsters Behemoth and Leviathan in a circular vision of earth and sea.

## Iconography
God and angels appear above in an arc of cloud, gesturing downward to a roundel that frames Behemoth, a vast land-beast, above Leviathan, a coiling sea-serpent. Job and his companions watch from below. The encircling composition divides the cosmos into heaven, land, and the watery deep.

## Significance
The scene illustrates God's answer from the whirlwind in Job 40–41, in which the Almighty points to Behemoth and Leviathan as proof of a power beyond human comprehension. Blake reads the two beasts as embodiments of war and tyranny — the brute forces of the fallen world — contained within God's encompassing vision. The watercolour belongs to the Butts set that Blake later engraved as the celebrated *Illustrations of the Book of Job* (1826), studied by Joseph Wicksteed.

## For artists & game designers
A cosmic 'menagerie of monsters' framing device: land-beast over sea-beast, both displayed within a divine circle.

citations.text: Joseph Wicksteed, *Blake's Vision of the Book of Job* (1910).

title: *The First Book of Urizen*, Plate 22
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
A full-page design from *The First Book of Urizen* (1794), Blake's parody of Genesis recounting the self-isolation and fall of the god Urizen.

## Iconography
A figure is shown amid Blake's characteristic flowing, organic forms — bowed bodies, enveloping nets or webs, and the dark, cavernous spaces of Urizen's self-created world. The hand-tinted plate sets a sculptural human form against swirling colour.

## Significance
*The First Book of Urizen* is Blake's mock-Bible, narrating how [[motif:urizen the measurer|Urizen]] — 'the primeval Priest' — withdraws from the Eternals, writes his iron book of laws, and falls into the divided, mortal cosmos, dragging humanity after him into the 'Net of Religion.' It is among Blake's darkest prophecies, the negative counterpart to the liberatory *Marriage of Heaven and Hell*. The relief-etched plates were printed in very few copies, each uniquely coloured; the book is central to study of Blake's private mythology.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made fallen-creator mythology: a god who legislates the universe into existence and ensnares his creatures in nets of law.

title: The Circle of the Traitors: Dante's Foot Striking Bocca degli Abati
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake (engraving; photograph by Daderot)
summary:
An engraving from Blake's Dante series showing the frozen circle of traitors, where Dante's foot strikes the head of the betrayer Bocca degli Abati.

## Iconography
Dante and Virgil move across the icy plain of Cocytus, where traitors are frozen to the neck. Dante's foot collides with the head of one damned soul — Bocca degli Abati — who is locked in the ice. The composition is stark, the figures sharply outlined in Blake's late linear engraving style.

## Significance
The scene illustrates *Inferno* XXXII, set in the ninth and lowest circle, where betrayers are entombed in the ice of Cocytus. Blake undertook the Dante engravings for John Linnell in the last years of his life, completing only seven of an intended large series before his death in 1827. They represent the culmination of his career as an [[motif:prophetic illuminated book|engraver]], translating his watercolour designs into severe, expressive line.

## For artists & game designers
A frozen-hell tableau: betrayers locked neck-deep in ice, the protagonist literally stumbling over the damned.

citations.text: Albert S. Roe, *Blake's Illustrations to the Divine Comedy* (1953).

title: *Elohim Creating Adam*
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: 1795/c.1805 (colour print finished in ink and watercolour)
summary:
*Elohim Creating Adam*, a 1795 colour print, shows the creator-god Elohim forming Adam from the earth as a serpent already coils about the man's leg.

## Iconography
A stern, winged Elohim hovers horizontally above the prostrate Adam, pressing him into being with weary, almost grieving effort. Adam lies pinned to the soil, a serpent winding around his thigh. Both figures share an expression of suffering rather than triumph; sun and water lie at the horizon.

## Significance
For Blake the creation of the natural, mortal body was itself a kind of fall — Adam is bound to earth and already entangled with the serpent of mortality at the very moment of his making. Elohim labours under the burden of creating fallen matter, an idea coloured by Blake's reading of Gnostic and Behmenist sources. The work belongs to the same 1795 set as [[motif:fallen reason|Newton]] and *Nebuchadnezzar*.

## For artists & game designers
A creation scene reframed as tragedy: the maker exhausted, the made already doomed and serpent-bound.

citations.text: Anthony Blunt, *The Art of William Blake* (1959).

title: *The Ghost of a Flea*
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: c.1819–1820 (tempera and gold on mahogany panel)
summary:
*The Ghost of a Flea* is a small visionary tempera depicting the soul of a flea as a muscular, reptilian humanoid striding between curtains, holding a bowl to catch blood.

## Iconography
A bald, scaly, heavily muscled creature moves across a small stage between drawn curtains spangled with stars. It carries a thorn or dagger in one hand and a cupped acorn-bowl for drinking blood in the other; its tongue darts hungrily from its mouth. The figure is bathed in an eerie spotlight.

## Significance
The image arose from Blake's 'visionary heads,' drawn for the astrologer John Varley during late-night séance-like sessions. Blake claimed the spirit told him that fleas were inhabited by the souls of bloodthirsty men, confined to such tiny bodies lest they lay waste to humankind — a grim joke on [[motif:contrary states of the soul|metempsychosis]]. Varley recorded the encounter; the panel is glittered with gold to suggest its supernatural light.

## For artists & game designers
The perfect 'tiny evil made monstrous' design — a microscopic predator's soul rendered as a stage-lit blood-drinking demon.

citations.text: John Varley, *A Treatise on Zodiacal Physiognomy* (1828).

title: God Judging Adam
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: 1795 (colour print finished in ink and watercolour)
summary:
*God Judging Adam*, a 1795 colour print, shows an enthroned, flame-wreathed god pronouncing judgement on a grey, aged Adam who mirrors him.

## Iconography
A bearded god sits in a chariot ringed by flames, holding a scroll and extending his hand toward Adam, who stands before him bowed and old. The two figures strikingly resemble each other, both white-bearded; horses of fire rear behind the throne. The palette is hot and oppressive.

## Significance
Long mistitled 'God Creating Adam,' the print actually shows the giving of the law and the sentence of mortality. The near-identity of judge and judged expresses Blake's conviction that the punitive law-giving god — [[motif:urizen the measurer|Urizen]] — is a projection of fallen man, not a transcendent other. The fiery chariot recalls Ezekiel's vision turned tyrannical. It belongs to the famous 1795 colour-print group studied by Butlin and Bindman.

## For artists & game designers
Judge and condemned rendered as mirror images — the oppressor revealed as the self.

title: The Great Red Dragon and the Woman Clothed with the Sun
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
From Blake's series illustrating the Book of Revelation, this watercolour depicts the Great Red Dragon of chapter 12 looming over the Woman Clothed with the Sun.

## Iconography
A monstrous winged dragon, seen from behind, rears up with vast bat-like wings spread across the whole sheet and a serpentine tail coiling. Far below, the radiant Woman lies with the crescent moon beneath her feet, her arms flung out as the dragon prepares to devour her child. The dragon's muscular, almost heroic back dominates the design.

## Significance
The scene illustrates Revelation 12, where the dragon — identified as Satan — waits to consume the child of the sun-crowned woman. Blake renders cosmic evil as overwhelming physical power and beauty, a tension central to his vision of the [[motif:contrary states of the soul|contraries]]. The series was commissioned by Thomas Butts. The image gained pop-cultural notoriety through Thomas Harris's *Red Dragon*; art-historically it is studied via Martin Butlin's catalogue raisonné.

## For artists & game designers
A masterclass in depicting a monster from behind, so the viewer shares its menacing vantage over a helpless victim below.

citations.text: Martin Butlin, *The Paintings and Drawings of William Blake* (1981).

title: Hecate, or *The Night of Enitharmon's Joy*
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: c.1795 (colour print finished in ink and watercolour)
summary:
This 1795 colour print depicts a triple female figure — the witch-goddess Hecate, or Blake's own Enitharmon — presiding over a nocturnal world of dark creatures with her hand on an open book.

## Iconography
A seated woman rests her hand on a large open book, while two further figures press against her back, forming the triple form of Hecate. Around them lurk creatures of the night: an owl, a bat-winged reptile, a braying ass, a thistle, and a serpent. The scene is steeped in lunar gloom.

## Significance
The classical Hecate, goddess of witchcraft, crossroads, and the moon, is here fused with [[motif:female dominion|Enitharmon]], a figure of Blake's mythology whose 'night of joy' is the eighteen-hundred-year reign of female-dominated, restrictive religion. The open book signals occult or doctrinal authority; the surrounding beasts embody fear and superstition. Scholars including Kathleen Raine read the work through Blake's ambivalence toward the feminine and toward organised religion.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive triple-goddess / night-witch tableau: one figure, three aspects, ringed by familiars and a book of dark lore.

title: *Jerusalem, The Emanation of the Giant Albion*, Plate 51
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary: Plate 51 of *Jerusalem*, Blake's longest and most ambitious illuminated prophecy, showing three seated figures sunk in despair beneath thrones of error.

citations.text: Morton D. Paley, *The Continuing City: William Blake's Jerusalem* (1983).

title: *The Marriage of Heaven and Hell* (title plate)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: c.1790–1793 (copy G; relief etching with watercolour)
summary:
The title plate of *The Marriage of Heaven and Hell*, Blake's prose-poem of visionary inversion, with figures embracing across the divide of cloud and flame.

## Iconography
The title lettering arches over a scene split between an upper region of trees and clouds and a lower region of fire. Tiny couples embrace and figures stride through the flames; flowering branches and birds animate the margins. The hand-coloured plate glows in ochres, blues and reds.

## Significance
*The Marriage of Heaven and Hell* (c.1790–93) is Blake's most quoted prophetic book, home to the 'Proverbs of Hell' and the doctrine that 'Without Contraries is no progression.' It inverts conventional morality: energy is 'Eternal Delight,' and the [[motif:contrary states of the soul|contraries]] of reason and desire must wed rather than war. The book exemplifies Blake's relief-etched 'illuminated printing,' in which text and image are etched together from a single copperplate. It was produced amid the ferment of the French Revolution.

## For artists & game designers
The foundational text of moral inversion — a world where 'hell' is creative energy and the two opposed realms must be married, not conquered.

citations.text: Joseph Viscomi, *Blake and the Idea of the Book* (1993).

title: *Milton: A Poem in Two Books*, Plate 4 (copy C)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
A plate from *Milton: A Poem*, in which the spirit of the dead poet John Milton descends to earth and into Blake himself to correct his own errors.

## Iconography
The relief-etched plate combines Blake's illuminated text with marginal and interlinear figures — striding, falling, or reaching forms wreathed in flame and vine — set into the body of the verse so that words and bodies share the page.

## Significance
*Milton* (c.1804–1811) tells how the poet Milton, troubled in eternity, undertakes a journey of [[motif:imagination as the divine body|self-annihilation]] back into the world, entering Blake through the foot to redeem his Puritan misconceptions of God and gender. The book's preface contains the lyric 'And did those feet in ancient time' (later set as the hymn *Jerusalem*) with its 'dark Satanic Mills.' It is a meditation on poetic inspiration and the correction of error, exemplifying Blake's fusion of literary criticism and prophecy.

## For artists & game designers
A model for the 'mentor-spirit possesses the hero' device — a dead master returning through the living artist to undo his own mistakes.

citations.text: Susan Fox, *Poetic Form in Blake's Milton* (1976).

title: When the Morning Stars Sang Together (Book of Job, plate 14)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
A central plate of Blake's *Illustrations of the Book of Job*, depicting the moment in God's whirlwind speech when 'the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy.'

## Iconography
God stretches out his arms at the centre, flanked by a row of jubilant angels with raised arms — the singing morning stars. Below, in horizontal registers, the sun-god drives his four horses, the moon-goddess rides her serpent-drawn car, and Job's family looks up. The whole forms a tiered cosmic theatre.

## Significance
The plate illustrates Job 38:7 and stands at the spiritual climax of Blake's Job cycle, the turning from suffering to renewed vision. The 'sons of God' become a choir of angels; the personified sun and moon set the rejoicing within a fully animated, imaginative cosmos rather than a mechanical one — Blake's answer to [[motif:fallen reason|Newtonian]] astronomy. Engraved in 1826, the *Illustrations of the Book of Job* are regarded as the summit of his printmaking.

## For artists & game designers
A layered cosmic-rejoicing composition: a choir of angels above, personified sun and moon driving their chariots below.

citations.text: Joseph Wicksteed, *Blake's Vision of the Book of Job* (1910).

key_concepts: Illustrations of the Book of Job (1826)

title: *Nebuchadnezzar*
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: 1795/c.1805 (colour print finished in ink and watercolour)
summary:
*Nebuchadnezzar*, one of the 1795 large colour prints, shows the Babylonian king reduced to a beast crawling on all fours after God's judgement.

## Iconography
A naked, heavily muscled king crawls on hands and knees, his eyes wide with terror and madness, his hair and beard grown long and matted, his fingernails curled into talons. He stares out from a dark cavern of vegetation, more animal than man.

## Significance
The subject is from Daniel 4: the proud king Nebuchadnezzar is driven from men 'and did eat grass as oxen' until he acknowledged heaven's rule. For Blake the image dramatises the degradation of the rational, tyrannical mind — the same fallen-reason theme as [[motif:urizen the measurer|Urizen]] and Newton — into pure bestiality. The terrified animal stare is among the most psychologically intense faces in British art. The print descends from a small design in *The Marriage of Heaven and Hell*.

## For artists & game designers
The template for the proud ruler broken into a feral, grass-eating beast — sanity stripped to instinct.

title: *Newton*
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: 1795/c.1805 (colour print finished in ink and watercolour)
summary:
*Newton* is one of Blake's twelve 'large colour prints' of 1795, showing the natural philosopher Isaac Newton as a nude figure bent double on the floor of the sea, drawing a diagram with a compass.

## Iconography
A powerful young nude crouches on a dark, encrusted rock at the bottom of the ocean. Oblivious to the living, jewel-like coral around him, he bends his whole body toward a white scroll and inscribes a geometric figure with a pair of compasses. His eye is fixed on the abstract diagram alone.

## Significance
Newton here is the personification of what Blake called 'single vision and Newton's sleep' — reason and measurement divorced from imagination. The compass links him directly to [[motif:urizen the measurer|Urizen]] in *The Ancient of Days*; both reduce the living cosmos to mechanical geometry. That Newton sits at the bottom of the sea of materialism, blind to the organic splendour behind him, is Blake's critique of Enlightenment rationalism and Lockean empiricism. The work was a touchstone for later readings of Blake by S. Foster Damon and Northrop Frye.

## For artists & game designers
The archetype of the brilliant-but-blinkered rationalist: genius turned inward on abstraction while the living world glows ignored behind him.

citations.text: Northrop Frye, *Fearful Symmetry: A Study of William Blake* (1947).

title: *Pity*
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
date: c.1795 (colour print finished in ink and watercolour)
summary: *Pity* is a 1795 colour print that literally visualises a simile from Shakespeare's *Macbeth*, in which pity is 'like a naked new-born babe, striding the blast.'

title: The Great Red Dragon and the Woman Clothed with the Sun (Brooklyn variant)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
rights: Public domain ('No restrictions'). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A second of Blake's Revelation watercolours on the Great Red Dragon theme, here showing the dragon frontally with the woman below.

## Iconography
The seven-headed, ten-horned dragon faces the viewer with arms raised and wings outspread, crowned with diadems, hovering above the prostrate Woman Clothed with the Sun who clutches her gown amid streaming light. The frontal, cruciform pose makes the dragon a dark parody of a blessing or crucified figure.

## Significance
Blake painted several treatments of Revelation 12 for Thomas Butts; this frontal composition contrasts with the better-known rear view. The dragon's diademed heads literalise Revelation's 'seven heads and ten horns, and upon his heads seven crowns,' presenting Satanic tyranny as a counterfeit sovereignty. The image belongs to Blake's late biblical watercolour project, his most sustained engagement with [[motif:fourfold vision|apocalyptic prophecy]].

## For artists & game designers
The frontal, arms-raised monster reads as blasphemous icon — evil posed in the body-language of worship.

citations.text: Martin Butlin, *The Paintings and Drawings of William Blake* (1981).

title: Satan Arousing the Rebel Angels
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
From Blake's watercolour series illustrating Milton's *Paradise Lost*, this design (the Butts set) shows Satan rallying his fallen angels.

## Iconography
A radiant, youthful Satan stands at the centre with arms outstretched, a starburst of light behind his head, holding spear and shield. Around and below him the rebel host rouses from the burning lake, their bodies surging upward in answer to his call. Satan is still beautiful, his fall not yet legible in his form.

## Significance
Blake illustrated *Paradise Lost* for Thomas Butts in 1808. His Satan is deliberately glorious — consistent with Blake's famous remark in [[motif:contrary states of the soul|The Marriage of Heaven and Hell]] that Milton 'was of the Devil's party without knowing it.' The radiant rebel embodies energy and revolt, qualities Blake prized even as he traced their tragic limits. The series is catalogued by Martin Butlin.

## For artists & game designers
The charismatic fallen leader at the moment of rallying his army — beauty and defiance before ruin sets in.

citations.text: Martin Butlin, *The Paintings and Drawings of William Blake* (1981).

title: The Sick Rose (*Songs of Experience*)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
The plate of 'The Sick Rose,' a brief, dark lyric of *Songs of Experience* about an invisible worm destroying a rose's life.

## Iconography
A thorny rose-stem curves across the page; the bloom droops downward and a small human figure or worm sinks into its heart, while caterpillars and a despairing female form cling to the bending stem and thorns. The verse sits within and beside the foliage.

## Significance
The poem — 'O Rose thou art sick. / The invisible worm... / Has found out thy bed / Of crimson joy' — compresses Blake's vision of corrupted, secretive [[motif:contrary states of the soul|love in the fallen world of Experience]] into eight lines. The 'invisible worm' has been read as jealousy, disease, concealment, and the deformation of desire by repressive morality. The integration of the figures into the literal plant is a model instance of Blake's fusion of word and design. It pairs with the open joy of *Innocence*.

## For artists & game designers
A compact emblem of hidden corruption: beauty visibly drooping while the rot works unseen at its core.

title: *Songs of Innocence and of Experience* (combined title page)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
The combined title page of *Songs of Innocence and of Experience*, Blake's paired lyric cycle 'Shewing the Two Contrary States of the Human Soul.'

## Iconography
The decorative title is interwoven with flame-like lettering and figures; this combined title page shows Adam and Eve, cowering beneath the flames of expulsion, set amid Blake's characteristic foliate scrolls and small human and animal forms climbing through the letters.

## Significance
The *Songs* (Innocence 1789; combined with Experience 1794) present matched poems — 'The Lamb' against 'The Tyger,' the two 'Chimney Sweeper' and 'Nurse's Song' pairs — to dramatise the [[motif:contrary states of the soul|two contrary states]] through which the soul must pass. Innocence is not naivety to be discarded but a vision to be regained on the far side of Experience. Produced by Blake's relief-etched illuminated printing and hand-coloured, the book is his most widely loved work; mystical plates such as 'The Divine Image' make the human form itself divine.

## For artists & game designers
The paired-states structure is a design pattern in itself: every motif rendered twice, once in innocence and once in experience.

title: The Tyger (*Songs of Experience*)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
The illuminated plate of 'The Tyger,' Blake's most famous lyric, in which the poet asks what immortal hand could frame the tiger's 'fearful symmetry.'

## Iconography
The text of the poem fills the plate beneath a slender tree, at the foot of which stands a small, oddly mild tiger — its meekness in pointed tension with the ferocity of the verse. Foliage and a star-touched sky frame the hand-coloured page.

## Significance
'The Tyger' is the dark counterpart, in *Songs of Experience*, to 'The Lamb' of *Innocence*: 'Did he who made the Lamb make thee?' The poem poses the problem of evil and creative energy — the same maker forging both gentleness and terror — and is the lyric heart of Blake's doctrine of [[motif:contrary states of the soul|contraries]]. The almost tame depicted tiger has long provoked comment, as if the image deliberately withholds the terror the words invoke. The plate is a touchstone of English Romantic art and poetry alike.

## For artists & game designers
The productive gap between fearsome text and meek image is itself a technique — let the words carry the dread the picture refuses.

citations.text: Hazard Adams, *William Blake: A Reading of the Shorter Poems* (1963).

title: The Whirlwind of Lovers (Dante, *Inferno* Canto V)
work: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
summary:
From Blake's unfinished illustrations to Dante's *Divine Comedy*, this design shows the whirlwind of carnal sinners in the second circle of Hell, with Paolo and Francesca.

## Iconography
