citations.text: Roberta J. M. Olson & Jay M. Pasachoff, *Fire in the Sky* (1998).

title: The Three Estates under the Stars (Lichtenberger, Prognosticatio, 1492)
work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
creator: Johannes Lichtenberger
summary:
A woodcut of the three estates beneath a planetary conjunction from Johannes Lichtenberger's *Prognosticatio* (Mainz, 1492), the most influential astrological prophecy of the age.

## Iconography
The [[motif:three estates|three estates]] — clergy, emperor, and commons — stand beneath the heavens where a [[motif:conjunction|planetary conjunction]] (Saturn and Jupiter) presides, the celestial alignment hanging over the social order as its determining sign.

## Significance
Lichtenberger's *Prognosticatio* (first issued 1488) built its prophecies on the great [[motif:conjunction|Saturn–Jupiter conjunction]] of 1484, foretelling upheaval, a false prophet, and reform; it ran through scores of editions and was reprinted by Luther himself with a preface. It is the archetype of the printed [[motif:prognostication|prognostication]] — astrology mobilised as political and religious prophecy, the conjunction read as the engine of history.

## For artists & game designers
Social ranks arrayed beneath a ruling planetary conjunction is a clean visual thesis that 'the stars govern the fate of the realm.'

title: Theatrum Cometicum Title Page (Lubieniecki)
work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
creator: Stanisław Lubieniecki (Stanislaus Lubienietzki)
summary:
The engraved title page of Stanisław Lubieniecki's *Theatrum Cometicum* (1668), the monumental catalogue of all comets recorded in history.

## Iconography
An ornate allegorical frontispiece frames the title with figures of astronomy and history, comets streaking through the upper register. The architecture of the 'theatre' presents the comet-catalogue as a grand stage of cosmic history.

## Significance
Lubieniecki, a Socinian exile, compiled the *Theatrum Cometicum* as an exhaustive [[motif:comet catalogue|comet catalogue]] — over 400 comets back to the Flood — soliciting observations of the 1664–65 comets from astronomers across Europe by letter. The work straddles the era's tension: a quasi-scientific census of comets that still treats them within a framework of historical and theological meaning. Together with Hevelius it defines the great age of the printed comet-atlas.

## For artists & game designers
An allegorical 'theatre' frontispiece — figures, architecture, and comets framing a title — is a model for an in-world tome's grand opening page.

title: Lunar Eclipse of 1566 (Facial Chronicle)
work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
creator: Anonymous (Russian manuscript illuminators)
summary:
A miniature recording the [[motif:lunar eclipse|lunar eclipse]] of 1566 from the *Illustrated (Facial) Chronicle of Ivan the Terrible*.

## Iconography
Within the chronicle's stacked-architecture style, figures observe a darkened or reddened moon above a city, the eclipse marked as a noted event in the historical narrative. The composition embeds the celestial sign into the flow of recorded history.

## Significance
The *Litsevoy letopisny svod* (Facial Chronicle), compiled in the 1560s–70s for Ivan IV, is one of the largest illustrated manuscripts ever made, with some 16,000 miniatures. Its inclusion of an [[motif:lunar eclipse|eclipse]] among wars and coronations shows the Muscovite court, like the Latin West, reading celestial events as part of providential history — eclipses as signs woven into the chronicle of the realm.

## For artists & game designers
An eclipse slotted between battle scenes in a chronicle shows how to treat a sky-omen as one entry in a running historical record.

citations.text: *Litsevoy letopisny svod* (Illustrated Chronicle of Ivan the Terrible), 1560s–70s.

title: Comet Plates (Hevelius, Prodromus Cometicus)
work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
creator: Johannes Hevelius
summary:
Plates A and B from Johannes Hevelius' *Prodromus Cometicus* (1665), his observational study of the [[motif:comet of 1664|comet of 1664–65]].

## Iconography
Two engraved plates chart the comet's nightly positions and changing tail against star fields and reference grids, the comet head drawn at successive points along its track. The images are precise observational records rather than ominous broadsheets.

## Significance
The *Prodromus Cometicus* preceded Hevelius' larger *Cometographia* and documented the bright comet of 1664, one of a pair (1664 and 1665) that triggered a wave of [[motif:celestial portent|portent]] literature across Europe. Hevelius' careful plotting represents the astronomical response to the same comets that the broadsheets read as harbingers of plague — the Great Plague of London (1665) was widely blamed on them.

## For artists & game designers
A multi-position 'time-lapse' of a comet across a star chart shows motion and study — a scholarly counterpoint to the single dramatic omen image.

title: Vädersolstavlan: The Sun-Dogs over Stockholm, 1535
work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
creator: Urban målare (copy after); attributed
summary:
The *Vädersolstavlan* ('Sun-Dog Painting'), depicting the [[motif:parhelion|parhelic]] halo display of mock-suns seen over Stockholm on 20 April 1535.

## Iconography
Above a meticulous early panorama of Stockholm, a complex of glowing rings, arcs, and [[motif:sun-dogs|mock-suns]] (*vädersolar*, 'weather-suns') fills the sky — multiple bright suns linked by haloes encircling the true sun. The city below is the oldest surviving image of Stockholm.

## Significance
The sun-dog display was read in the tense politics of the Swedish Reformation: opponents of King Gustav Vasa interpreted the apparition as a divine warning against him, prompting the king to commission a recording of the [[motif:celestial portent|portent]] partly to neutralise it. The surviving 1636 copy preserves the lost original. It is a paradigmatic case of an [[motif:atmospheric optics|atmospheric optical]] phenomenon read as a political omen.

## For artists & game designers
A full halo-complex (multiple suns, rings, tangent arcs) over a detailed cityscape is the richest possible 'wrong sky' set-piece.

citations.text: Sven Lidman & others on the *Vädersolstavlan*; Storkyrkan, Stockholm.

title: *Agnus Dei* wax disc with seal of Pope Martin
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (papal workshop, Rome)
date: wax sacramental bearing a papal seal; surviving example
summary:
An *Agnus Dei*, the blessed wax disc impressed with the Lamb of God and a papal seal, the most prestigious of Catholic protective sacramentals.

## Iconography
A small oval or round disc of **wax** — traditionally made from the remnants of the Paschal candle — stamped on one face with the **Lamb of God** (*Agnus Dei*) bearing the banner of the Resurrection, and on the reverse with a saint and the **seal of the reigning pope**.

## Significance
The **Agnus Dei** was solemnly blessed by the pope, by custom in the first year of his reign and every seventh year after, and distributed as a [[motif:sacramental|sacramental]]. Worn or kept in the home, it was believed to guard against fire, flood, tempest, plague, sudden death, and the assaults of the Devil — a protective virtue catalogued in devotional handbooks and prayers attached to the object. It is the official Church counterpart to the charms of folk magic.

## For artists & game designers
A blessed wax token stamped with a lamb and a papal seal — a 'consecrated relic' item whose protection comes from the highest sacred authority.

citations.text: Megan Holmes, *The Miraculous Image in Renaissance Florence* (2013).

title: Man of Sorrows with the *arma Christi* (Schmerzensmann)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Michil (devotional printmaker)
summary:
A hand-coloured *Schmerzensmann* (Man of Sorrows) surrounded by the *arma Christi*, the instruments of the Passion, a devotional and protective image.

## Iconography
The wounded **Christ** stands or sits, crowned with thorns and displaying his **five wounds**, while around him hover the **arma Christi** — the cross, lance, sponge, scourges, nails, dice, the pillar, the column, and other tools of his Passion — each meditated upon in turn. The bleeding **side wound** is prominent.

## Significance
The **arma Christi** ('weapons of Christ') were not only objects of Passion meditation but were credited with apotropaic power: contemplating or carrying the image of the wounds and instruments was believed to shield the devout from sudden death and evil. The **side wound**, often reproduced at its 'true measure', became a charm in its own right. Such prints sit at the heart of late-medieval and early-modern devotional protection.

## For artists & game designers
A suffering Christ ringed by floating Passion-tools — a dense iconographic 'ward of the wounds' for a sacred-relic motif.

citations.text: Rudolf Berliner, 'Arma Christi,' *Münchner Jahrbuch der bildenden Kunst* (1955).

title: Medal of St Benedict (CSPB / VRSNSMV)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (Benedictine sacramental)
date: the Jubilee (1880) form of a long tradition
summary:
The Medal of St Benedict, a Catholic sacramental whose cryptic letters form a verse-charm against evil.

## Iconography
One face shows **St Benedict** holding a cross and his Rule; the reverse bears a large **cross** set in a field of initials — **C S P B** in the angles (*Crux Sancti Patris Benedicti*) and around the rim the abbreviations **V R S N S M V — S M Q L I V B**, standing for a Latin verse of exorcism. The border reads *Vade retro Satana*.

## Significance
The **Benedictine medal** is the best-known Christian [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] charm in letter form: its abbreviations spell a rhymed adjuration — *'Begone, Satan! Never tempt me with your vanities; what you offer is evil, drink the poison yourself!'* Blessed as a sacramental, it was worn, buried in fields, and set in walls to drive off the Devil, disease, and storm. It descends from the older *Zachariassegen* and was standardised in the Jubilee medal of 1880.

## For artists & game designers
A cross whose every margin is packed with abbreviated holy initials — a real 'letter-coded ward' that reads as occult cipher yet is wholly Christian.

citations.text: Prosper Guéranger, *Essai sur la médaille de saint Benoît* (1862).
Don C. Skemer, *Binding Words: Textual Amulets in the Middle Ages* (2006).

title: Devotional folded book-amulet (*Breverl*)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (convent sister, southern Germany or Austria)
date: early modern devotional packet; surviving example
summary:
A *Breverl*, a folded paper amulet-packet of holy images, blessings, and tiny relics, made in a southern-German or Austrian convent.

## Iconography
An intricately **folded paper packet** that opens to reveal a grid of small printed **holy images**, hand-coloured saints, fragments of blessed objects, and protective texts — the whole assembled and stitched in the fine convent craft known as *Klosterarbeit*.

## Significance
The **Breverl** (from *breve*, a 'brief' or letter) is a Catholic-Bavarian protective amulet: carried on the body or hung in the home, its concentrated holy contents — images of the [[motif:agnus dei|Agnus Dei]], the [[motif:benedict medal|Benedictine]] charm, relics, and blessings — were believed to guard the bearer against illness, accident, witchcraft, and evil spirits. Convent sisters produced them in great numbers as devotional gifts and pilgrimage souvenirs.

## For artists & game designers
A folded paper charm that unfolds into a miniature shrine of saints and relics — a tactile 'protective packet' rich in collage detail.

citations.text: Nina Gockerell, *Bilder und Zeichen der Frömmigkeit* (1995).
Don Yoder, 'Hex Signs and the Pennsylvania German,' in *Discovering American Folklife* (1990).

title: Friar with the Cross of Caravaca (Pieter Snyers)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Pieter Snyers
summary:
Pieter Snyers's painting of a friar holding the double-barred Cross of Caravaca, one of the most widespread Catholic protective crosses.

## Iconography
A **friar** displays a **double-barred (patriarchal) cross** — the **Cross of Caravaca** — its two transverse arms the mark of a reliquary cross said to contain a fragment of the True Cross. Often the cross is flanked by two angels.

## Significance
The **Caravaca cross** takes its name from the Spanish town of Caravaca de la Cruz, where a relic of the True Cross was venerated. Reproduced as medals and pendants throughout the Hispanic and German Catholic worlds, the double cross was carried as an [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] charm against the Devil, storm, and illness, and pressed to the sick. Its form links it to the Eastern patriarchal cross and the relic-cross tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A twin-barred relic cross with its own legend — a distinctive 'holy artefact' silhouette instantly distinguishable from the plain Latin cross.

citations.text: William A. Christian Jr., *Local Religion in Sixteenth-Century Spain* (1981).

title: St Christopher mural, Völkermarkt parish church
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (medieval wall-painter); photograph by Raul de Chissota
summary:
A large medieval mural of St Christopher in the parish church of Völkermarkt in Carinthia, a second example of the saint as a daily protective image.

## Iconography
The outsized **St Christopher** strides through water bearing the **Christ Child**, his cloak billowing, a flowering staff in hand. Like most such murals it is placed where it could be seen on entering or passing the church.

## Significance
This Carinthian mural illustrates how standardised and ubiquitous the **protective St Christopher** image was across the German-speaking lands: painted monumentally on church walls so that a single glance might secure the viewer against [[motif:apotropaic|unprepared death]] for the day. The repetition of the type — giant saint, river, Christ Child — across hundreds of churches shows it functioning as a recognised public charm, not merely decoration.

## For artists & game designers
The same saint, the same pose, repeated church to church — a useful reference for how a 'protective icon type' spreads as a visual standard.

title: The *Drudenfuß* (pentagram / pentalpha)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Schematic diagram (after the folk figure)
summary:
The *Drudenfuß*, the unicursal five-pointed star drawn on thresholds and cradles in German folk belief to ward off the night-spirit, the Drude.

## Iconography
A **pentagram** (*pentalpha*) — a five-pointed star drawn in a single unbroken line, its five segments here marked to show its golden-ratio proportions. The figure has no beginning or end.

## Significance
The **Drudenfuß** ('Drude's foot') was scratched or carved onto **doors, bedsteads, and cradles** to bar the *Drude* (or *Trud*) — the Alpine night-hag who pressed and suffocated sleepers, the German nightmare-spirit. Like the folk [[motif:hexafoil|hexafoil]], its protective power lay in being drawn in one continuous stroke, leaving no gap for the spirit to enter. Goethe famously has Mephistopheles trapped by an imperfectly drawn *Drudenfuß* in *Faust*. The same figure carries deep occult and Pythagorean associations.

## For artists & game designers
The classic single-stroke star-ward: instantly legible as 'protective sigil', with a built-in fail-state (an unclosed line lets the demon through).

citations.text: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, *Faust I* (1808), the Drudenfuß scene.

title: *Hagelkreuz* (hail-cross), Eschweiler
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (folk custom); photograph by NDG
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: NDG). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A *Hagelkreuz* at Eschweiler in the Rhineland, a wayside cross raised specifically to protect the fields against destructive hail.

## Iconography
A **wayside cross** on the field-edge, named *Hagelkreuz* ('hail-cross'). Many such crosses mark the route of the annual procession by which the parish blessed its land.

## Significance
The **Hagelkreuz** is the hail-specific form of the field-cross: at it, and on the rogation days, the priest pronounced the **weather-blessing** (*Wettersegen*) against [[motif:apotropaic|hail and tempest]] that threatened the crops. Such crosses, often endowed after a ruinous storm, anchored a whole landscape of [[motif:wetterkreuz|protective]] piety in which the cross, holy water, blessed palms, and the ringing of consecrated bells were all marshalled against the weather.

## For artists & game designers
A named 'hail-cross' marking where a community begged protection from the sky — vivid evidence that even the weather was a thing to be warded.

citations.text: Adolph Franz, *Die kirchlichen Benediktionen im Mittelalter* (1909).

title: *Haussegen* (house-blessing broadside), Baumann of Neuruppin
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Samuel Baumann (publisher), Neuruppin
summary:
A printed *Haussegen* or house-blessing broadside from the Neuruppin press of Samuel Baumann, hung in the home as a protective charm.

## Iconography
A decorative printed **broadside** framing a **blessing for the house** — verses invoking God's protection over the dwelling and its inhabitants, set among ornamental borders, scripture, and often a vignette of the holy family or guardian angel.

## Significance
The **Haussegen** ('house-blessing') was a staple of the German and Pennsylvania-German home: hung on the wall, the printed blessing was held to guard the household against fire, lightning, theft, and misfortune, much like the [[motif:himmelsbrief|Himmelsbrief]]. The Neuruppin *Bilderbogen* presses turned out vast numbers of such cheap protective and devotional sheets in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

## For artists & game designers
A framed printed blessing on the parlour wall that quietly wards the home — domestic 'protective decor' grounded in real popular print.

citations.text: Don Yoder, 'The Himmelsbrief,' in *Discovering American Folklife* (1990).

title: St Bernardino of Siena with the IHS Christogram
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (after Bernardino's emblem); Rijksmuseum print
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A print of St Bernardino of Siena displaying his IHS tablet, the radiant monogram of the Holy Name promoted as a protective emblem.

## Iconography
The Franciscan friar **Bernardino of Siena** holds up a tablet bearing the letters **IHS** — the first letters of the Greek name of Jesus — set within a blazing **sunburst** of twelve rays. The scene shows one of his miracles.

## Significance
Bernardino (1380–1444) popularised the **IHS Christogram** in a golden sunburst as a devotion to the **Holy Name of Jesus**, urging citizens to set it over their doors in place of factional badges. The radiant monogram quickly took on [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] force: the Name of Jesus was held to put demons to flight, and the emblem guarded the threshold and the household. It became the badge of the Jesuit order and a ubiquitous protective sign.

## For artists & game designers
A blazing three-letter monogram that wards by the holy Name alone — a clean, heraldic 'sacred sigil' for a doorway or banner.

citations.text: Iris Origo, *The World of San Bernardino* (1962).
Cynthia Polecritti, *Preaching Peace in Renaissance Italy* (2000).

title: The Fourteen Holy Helpers (altarpiece, Millstatt)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (Baroque painter)
summary:
A Baroque altarpiece of the Fourteen Holy Helpers from Millstatt in Carinthia, the collective of saints invoked together against every kind of harm.

## Iconography
The **Fourteen Holy Helpers** (*Vierzehn Nothelfer*) gathered around the armoured **St George** with his dragon: among them St Giles with the hind, St Eustace, St Vitus with his cauldron, [[motif:saint christopher|St Christopher]] with the Child, St Barbara with her tower, St Catherine with her wheel, St Margaret, St Blaise, St Erasmus, St Cyriacus, St Denis, St Pantaleon, and St Acacius.

## Significance
The cult of the **Fourteen Holy Helpers**, which crystallised in fifteenth-century Franconia (and the shrine of Vierzehnheiligen), bundled fourteen saints into a single [[motif:apotropaic|protective]] devotion: each was specialist patron against a particular peril — sudden death, plague, fever, throat-ailments, fire, storm — and together they covered every emergency. Invoking the whole company at once made the most comprehensive protective charm in late-medieval and Baroque Catholicism.

## For artists & game designers
Fourteen patrons in one image, each with a distinct attribute and 'specialty' against a named danger — a ready-made roster of protective figures.

citations.text: Hellmut Rosenfeld, 'Vierzehn Nothelfer,' *Lexikon der christlichen Ikonographie* (1968–76).

title: The Fourteen Holy Helpers (Franz Anton Maulbertsch)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Franz Anton Maulbertsch
rights: Public domain. National Museum in Warsaw, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Franz Anton Maulbertsch's Baroque painting of the Fourteen Holy Helpers, the Austrian master's vision of the protective company of saints.

## Iconography
The **Fourteen Holy Helpers** swirl in a luminous Baroque heaven, each identifiable by attribute — dragon, wheel, tower, cauldron, hind — arranged around the central martyrs in Maulbertsch's characteristically loose, glowing brushwork.

## Significance
Maulbertsch (1724–1796), the leading painter of the late Austrian Baroque, here gives monumental form to the same protective devotion seen in the [[motif:fourteen holy helpers|Millstatt altarpiece]]. By his day the cult of the *Vierzehn Nothelfer* was a central Habsburg-lands devotion, its [[motif:apotropaic|collective intercession]] sought against illness, accident, and sudden death. The painting shows how a folk-rooted protective charm was elevated into high ecclesiastical art.

## For artists & game designers
The same protective fourteen rendered as a swirling Baroque vision — a study in turning a 'list of patrons' into dynamic heavenly composition.

title: St Christopher wall painting, Untergermaringen
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (medieval wall-painter); photograph by Vivarius
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Vivarius). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A monumental medieval wall-painting of St Christopher in the Romanesque church of St George at Untergermaringen, a protective image against sudden death.

## Iconography
The giant **St Christopher** wades through a river, leaning on a staff, carrying the **Christ Child** on his shoulder. The figure is painted at great size, typically on a wall facing the church door so it could be seen from outside.

## Significance
A widespread late-medieval belief held that whoever **looked upon an image of St Christopher** would not die a [[motif:apotropaic|sudden or unprepared death]] that day — captured in the rhyme *Christophori faciem die quacumque tueris, illa nempe die non morte mala morieris*. This made his colossal image a daily-renewed protective charm, painted near doors and gates so travellers could catch sight of it. He is the protective patron *par excellence* among the [[motif:fourteen holy helpers|Holy Helpers]].

## For artists & game designers
A giant saint painted to be glimpsed in passing, granting a day's protection from a single look — a built-in 'glance-blessing' mechanic.

citations.text: Émile Mâle, *Religious Art in France: The Late Middle Ages* (1908).

title: Christ appoints St Roch patron of the plague-stricken
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (after a Netherlandish design); Rijksmuseum print
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A devotional print of Christ appointing St Roch patron of plague sufferers, inscribed *Sancte Roche ora pro nobis* — a protective image against pestilence.

## Iconography
**St Roch**, dressed as a **pilgrim** with staff and scallop, lifts his robe to show the **plague-sore** on his thigh, accompanied by his dog with a loaf. Above, **Christ** designates him protector; the legend asks his intercession.

## Significance
In the recurring plague years of early-modern Europe, the protective saints **Roch** and [[motif:saint sebastian|Sebastian]] were the chief heavenly defenders against pestilence. Prints and altarpieces of St Roch were displayed and carried as [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] images, his exposed sore turning his own suffering into a guarantee of protection. He is one of the [[motif:fourteen holy helpers|Fourteen Holy Helpers]] invoked in time of need.

## For artists & game designers
A pilgrim-saint who shows his own plague-wound as a badge of protection — a striking 'survivor-patron' figure for an epidemic setting.

citations.text: Sheila Barker, 'The Making of a Plague Saint,' in *Piety and Plague* (2007).

title: St Sebastian, plague-saint (engraving, British Museum)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (engraver)
rights: Public domain. British Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving of St Sebastian pierced with arrows, the soldier-martyr invoked across Europe as a protector against the plague.

## Iconography
**St Sebastian** bound to a tree or column, his body pierced by **arrows**, often shown nude and youthful. Sometimes an angel attends or the archers stand by.

## Significance
By a long-standing association of arrows with the 'darts' of pestilence, **St Sebastian** became, with [[motif:saint roch|St Roch]], the foremost [[motif:apotropaic|protective]] plague-saint of Catholic Europe; his image was carried in procession and set up in churches and homes to turn aside the disease. The arrow-pierced martyr who yet survived his wounds offered a model of protection through endurance. Cheap devotional prints spread his protective cult to every household in plague years.

## For artists & game designers
The arrow-struck martyr whose wounds ward off the 'darts of plague' — a potent visual link between suffering and immunity.

citations.text: Sheila Barker, 'The Making of a Plague Saint,' in *Piety and Plague* (2007).

title: Four *Schluckbildchen* (devotional prints to be swallowed)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (devotional printer)
summary:
Four *Schluckbildchen*, the tiny printed holy images that were literally swallowed as a healing and protective charm.

## Iconography
A strip of **very small printed devotional images** — saints, the [[motif:agnus dei|Agnus Dei]], the names of Jesus and Mary, or short blessings — printed many to a sheet and cut apart into pellet-sized squares.

## Significance
A **Schluckbild** ('swallow-picture') was a sacramental taken internally: the little image, sometimes blessed at a shrine, was rolled up and **swallowed** by a sick person or animal, often dissolved in water or hidden in feed, so that its holy virtue might work directly on the body. The practice — a literal ingestion of the sacred — was widespread in folk Catholicism against fever, possession, and cattle-disease, and stands at the very border of devotion and magic.

## For artists & game designers
A holy image meant to be eaten — a startling 'consumable blessing' that fuses relic and medicine.

citations.text: Adolf Spamer, *Das kleine Andachtsbild vom XIV. bis zum XX. Jahrhundert* (1930).
Don Yoder, 'Folk Medicine,' in *Folklore and Folklife* (1972).

title: Tau cross of St Antony (Antoniuskreuz), Heyroth
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous; photograph by Thomas Hummel
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Thomas Hummel). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Tau cross associated with St Antony the Great, the T-shaped cross worn and displayed as protection against 'St Antony's Fire'.

## Iconography
A cross in the shape of the Greek letter **tau (T)** — the *crux commissa* — here in a church dedicated to St **Antony**. The Tau was the emblem of the Antonine order and the badge worn by its hospitaller brothers.

## Significance
The **Tau cross** carries protective force on two grounds: in Ezekiel and patristic reading, the *tau* marked on the forehead is the sign that **spares** the faithful from destruction (the apotropaic mark *par excellence*), and it became the emblem of St Antony, patron against **ergotism** ('St Antony's Fire') and other afflictions. Worn as an amulet and set on Antonine hospitals, the Tau guarded against fire, plague, and that burning sickness. St Francis too adopted it as his protective sign.

## For artists & game designers
The oldest 'sparing mark' — a single T-shape that signs the protected from the doomed, with deep biblical authority.

citations.text: Adalbert Mischlewski, *Grundzüge der Geschichte des Antoniterordens* (1976).
Gerhard Ladner, 'The Origin and Significance of the Tau,' in *Images and Ideas in the Middle Ages* (1983).

title: Chalked Epiphany door-blessing (20 ✝ C ✝ M ✝ B ✝ 24)
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (folk custom); photograph by Brian Snelson
rights: CC BY 2.0 (photo: Brian Snelson). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A doorway chalked with the Epiphany blessing of the Three Kings, the annual threshold-charm against harm.

## Iconography
Written in **blessed chalk** above a **doorway**: the year split around the letters **C ✝ M ✝ B**, e.g. *20 + C + M + B + 24*, with small crosses between. The letters stand both for the traditional names of the Magi — **C**aspar, **M**elchior, **B**althasar — and for *Christus Mansionem Benedicat* ('May Christ bless this house').

## Significance
The **Three Kings' blessing** (*Dreikönigssegen*) marks the house at Epiphany (6 January) with a [[motif:apotropaic|protective]] inscription meant to guard the household for the coming year against storm, fire, illness, and evil. Blessed chalk, holy water, and incense are used in the rite. In German lands the *Sternsinger* (star-singers) carry the blessing door to door — a living survival of Christian threshold-magic.

## For artists & game designers
A charm written in chalk over a door, renewed once a year — a perfect seasonal 'house ward' the player can inscribe.

title: *Ulrichskreuz* of the Battle of the Lechfeld
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous
date: associated with St Ulrich (d. 973); later devotional form
summary:
The *Ulrichskreuz*, the double cross associated with St Ulrich of Augsburg and the victory at the Lechfeld, cast as a protective amulet.

## Iconography
A **double-barred cross**, often shown with Christ on one bar and the Virgin or relics on the other, linked in legend to the cross St **Ulrich** of Augsburg was said to have received from an angel before the **Battle of the Lechfeld** (955).

## Significance
The **Ulrichskreuz** became one of the standard German Catholic [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] crosses: cast in metal and blessed, it was carried and hung in homes and stables as a guard against the Devil, disease of cattle, storm, and sudden death. Augsburg pilgrims spread it widely, and like the [[motif:caravaca cross|Cross of Caravaca]] and the [[motif:benedict medal|Benedictine medal]] it belongs to the family of protective relic-crosses.

## For artists & game designers
A battle-blessed double cross tied to a famous medieval victory — a 'relic of triumph' charm with a martial backstory.

citations.text: Adolph Franz, *Die kirchlichen Benediktionen im Mittelalter* (1909).

title: The *Vehmgericht* (secret tribunal), 'League of the Holy Court'
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous
date: depiction of a medieval institution; later print
summary:
A depiction of the *Vehmgericht*, the secret 'holy court' of medieval Westphalia, whose sworn members judged in the name of God and the Emperor.

## Iconography
Hooded or assembled **judges** (*Freischöffen*) sit in a **secret tribunal**, the emblems of their justice — a **rope** and a **dagger or knife** — laid before them, the accused brought to a hidden court convened on 'free soil'.

## Significance
The **Vehmgericht** (Femegericht) was the dread secret tribunal of late-medieval **Westphalia**, whose initiates were bound by terrible oaths and who could summon, try, and execute in the Emperor's name. Though a judicial institution rather than a charm, it belongs to the borderland of sacred dread: its rites of secrecy, oath, and the *Heilige Vehme* ('holy court') invested ordinary justice with an almost numinous, apotropaic terror that lingered in German legend and Romantic imagination. The rope-and-dagger emblem became a byword for hidden, inescapable judgment.

## For artists & game designers
A hooded secret court that judges in God's name with rope and blade — a ready-made 'sacred tribunal' faction steeped in oath and dread.

citations.text: Henry Charles Lea, *A History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages* (1888).

title: *Wetterkreuz* (weather-cross), Tauberbischofsheim
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (folk custom); photograph by Triplec85
summary:
A *Wetterkreuz* near Tauberbischofsheim, one of the wayside weather-crosses raised in the fields to guard the harvest against storm and hail.

## Iconography
A **wayside crucifix or cross** set in open farmland, often on a rise. Where a full crucifix, the corpus of Christ faces the fields; many bear an inscription invoking protection from bad weather.

## Significance
The **Wetterkreuz** ('weather-cross') belongs to the agrarian [[motif:apotropaic|protective]] landscape of Catholic German lands: erected and blessed to shield crops and village from **storm, lightning, and hail**, it was a focus of the rogation processions and weather-blessings (*Wettersegen*) by which the clergy and people sought to turn aside destructive weather. It stands alongside the [[motif:hagelkreuz|Hagelkreuz]] in the open-field cult of protection.

## For artists & game designers
A lone cross standing in a field against the sky — a quiet environmental marker of folk weather-magic and rural piety.

citations.text: Adolph Franz, *Die kirchlichen Benediktionen im Mittelalter* (1909).

title: *Zachariassegen* (Blessing of St Zachary) plaque
work: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: Anonymous (Kloster St. Mang, Füssen)
date: early modern protective inscription; surviving example
summary:
A *Zachariassegen*, the cross-of-letters Blessing of St Zachary, displayed at the monastery of St Mang in Füssen — the medieval ancestor of the Benedictine medal.

## Iconography
A panel of crosses, each followed by an initial letter, arranged as a litany: a sequence of **+Z +D +I +A +B +I +Z +S +A +B +Z** and a longer chain of abbreviated Latin invocations, often headed by the cross and holy names.

## Significance
The **Zachariassegen** is a plague-charm attributed to St Zachary (Pope Zacharias), each cross-and-letter standing for the opening of a Latin prayer of protection against pestilence, sudden death, and the Devil. Cut on plaques, printed on slips, and set over doors, it was among the most popular [[motif:apotropaic|protective]] inscriptions of Catholic German lands during plague years, and its abbreviation-charm directly shaped the later [[motif:benedict medal|Medal of St Benedict]].

## For artists & game designers
A wall of crosses-plus-initials that reads as an enciphered prayer — a historically grounded 'letter litany' ward against plague.

citations.text: Don C. Skemer, *Binding Words: Textual Amulets in the Middle Ages* (2006).
Adolph Franz, *Die kirchlichen Benediktionen im Mittelalter* (1909).

title: Boehme's *Philosophische Kugel* (Philosophical Globe)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: After Jakob Böhme (theosophical diagram)
summary:
The *Philosophische Kugel* or 'Wonder-Eye of Eternity,' the central theosophical diagram of Jakob Böhme's mystical cosmology.

## Iconography
Two interlocking circles or hemispheres form an eye-shaped figure crossed by a heart; around and within it are arrayed the divine qualities and the names of God, mapping the eternal Nature's procession out of the groundless [[motif:abyss|*Ungrund*]] into the manifest world of light and dark. The whole is read as the eye in which God beholds himself.

## Significance
Böhme (1575–1624), the 'Teutonic theosopher' shoemaker of Görlitz, described creation as the self-revelation of God out of the *Ungrund* (groundless abyss) through seven source-spirits and the tension of wrath and love. This diagram — printed in editions of his works and in William Law's English edition — condenses that [[motif:theosophy|theosophy]] into interlocking circles of emanation, parallel to the cabalist sefirot and the Neoplatonic procession. Böhme shaped Pietism, Romanticism, and figures from Newton to Hegel; Andrew Weeks and Arthur Versluis are guides.

## For artists & game designers
A self-contained cosmogram: an eye made of interlocking rings holding the names of every divine force — a striking title-screen sigil or scrying device.

citations.text: Jakob Böhme, *Mysterium Magnum* (1623); *Theosophical Works*, ed. William Law (1764–81).

title: Portrait of Jakob Böhme
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous engraver (Rijksmuseum impression)
summary:
An engraved portrait of Jakob Böhme (1575–1624), the Görlitz shoemaker whose visionary theosophy became a wellspring of Western mysticism.

## Iconography
A bust portrait of the mystic, in the devotional-scholarly format used for the frontispieces of his collected works; later impressions often surround the likeness with emblems of his doctrine and Latin verses.

## Significance
Böhme, the *Philosophus Teutonicus*, claimed a sudden illumination (gazing into the sheen of a pewter dish) that revealed the inner structure of God and Nature, which he expounded in *Aurora* (1612) and *Mysterium Magnum*. His doctrine of the [[motif:theosophy|*Ungrund*]], the seven source-spirits, and the divine *Sophia* fed Pietism, Quietism, Romanticism, and Western [[motif:theosophy|theosophy]] — Gichtel, William Law, Saint-Martin, Blake, Hegel, and Schelling all drew on him. His work runs parallel to Christian Cabala in reading creation as graduated divine self-disclosure. Andrew Weeks and Arthur Versluis are standard.

## For artists & game designers
The humble-artisan mystic: a tradesman granted cosmic vision — a relatable sage NPC whose wisdom outranks his station.

citations.text: Arthur Versluis, *Wisdom's Children: A Christian Esoteric Tradition* (1999).

title: The Divine Tetractys (decad of dots)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Sneedledee (modern diagram)
summary:
A clean explanatory diagram of the 'divine tetractys,' labelling the symbolic meaning of each of its four rows.

## Iconography
The ten dots of the [[motif:tetractys|tetractys]] are grouped row by row and glossed: the apex unit as the [[motif:monad|monad]] or divine source, the second row as duality, the third as harmony/the surface, the fourth as the material world — the whole resolving into the [[motif:decad|decad]] of ten.

## Significance
The diagram makes explicit the metaphysics the Pythagoreans read into the figure: a procession from undivided unity through increasing multiplicity to the completed world, recovered in the Pythagorean number-theology that Renaissance Christian cabalists mapped onto the emanation of the sefirot from *Ein Sof*. (This is a modern teaching diagram, not a historical plate.)

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made tiered cosmology: four labelled levels of being descending from a single source — drop straight into an emanation or skill-tier system.

citations.text: Nicomachus of Gerasa, *Introduction to Arithmetic*; Theon of Smyrna.
S. K. Heninger, *Touches of Sweet Harmony* (1974).

title: *Integrae Naturae Speculum* (Fludd)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Robert Fludd; engraved by Matthäus Merian / Johann Theodor de Bry
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Robert Fludd's engraving *Integrae Naturae Speculum, Artisque Imago* ('The Mirror of the Whole of Nature and the Image of Art') from the *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617) — a Neoplatonic chain of being drawn as linked concentric circles.

## Iconography
A chain runs from the Hebrew Name of God in the clouds to the hand of a winged maiden, *Natura*, the [[motif:world soul|anima mundi]], whose own hand chains the [[motif:ape of art|ape of Art]] below. Around her radiate [[motif:concentric circles|concentric bands]]: the angelic hierarchies, the planetary spheres, and the elemental and mineral worlds — a single graduated procession from the One to brute matter.

## Significance
Fludd's plate is the most famous early-modern image of the Neoplatonic [[motif:chain of being|great chain]] as emanation: God's Name above, Nature as mediating world-soul, and the descending circles of being, with human art (the ape) imitating nature at the bottom. It fuses Hermetic, cabalist, and Pythagorean cosmology. Frances Yates, Joscelyn Godwin, and (on its design) the Warburg tradition discuss it. (Distinct from Fludd's separate sefirot and monochord plates.)

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'great chain' visual: a literal chain from God to ape threading concentric realms — a perfect map screen for a layered cosmos.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin, *Robert Fludd: Hermetic Philosopher and Surveyor of Two Worlds* (1979).

title: The Ape of Nature (Fludd)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Robert Fludd; engraved by Theodor de Bry
summary:
A detail of the 'ape of nature' from Robert Fludd's cosmological engravings — human art and science figured as an ape imitating Nature.

## Iconography
A seated [[motif:ape of art|ape]] points a compass at a globe, surrounded by the disciplines it practises — arithmetic, geometry, music, perspective, the mechanical arts. The creature is *ars simia naturae*, Art the ape of Nature, copying at one remove the works that Nature copies in turn from God.

## Significance
Fludd's ape encapsulates the Renaissance Neoplatonic ladder of imitation: God → Nature (the world-soul) → human Art, each level an ape of the one above. The disciplines ringing the ape are organized by [[motif:number mysticism|Pythagorean]] number and proportion, the quadrivium of arithmetic, music, geometry, and astronomy. The motif descends from the medieval *simia* topos and recurs across emblem literature. Godwin and the Warburg scholarship discuss it.

## For artists & game designers
A single figure that diagrams a whole knowledge-system: a craftsman-ape ringed by the skills it can perform — an instant 'crafting menu' character.

citations.text: H. W. Janson, *Apes and Ape Lore in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance* (1952).

title: *De Harmonia Mundi Totius* (Francesco Giorgio)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Francesco Giorgio (Zorzi); printed by Bernardino de Vitalibus
summary:
The title page of Francesco Giorgio's *De Harmonia Mundi Totius* (Venice, 1525), the great Venetian synthesis of Pythagorean harmony, Neoplatonism, and Christian Cabala.

## Iconography
The printed title page of the *cantica* in which the Franciscan friar Giorgio (Zorzi, 1466–1540) expounds the universe as a single musical [[motif:world harmony|harmony]] built on Pythagorean numerical ratios and the cabalist names of God.

## Significance
*De Harmonia Mundi* fused the [[motif:number mysticism|Pythagorean]] doctrine of cosmic musical proportion with Ficinian Neoplatonism and the [[motif:sefirot|Kabbalah]] of Pico and Reuchlin, presenting creation as a graduated concord from God down through the angelic and celestial worlds to matter. It deeply shaped Renaissance ideas of universal harmony — influencing Agrippa, English architectural theory, and (per Frances Yates and Rudolf Wittkower) the proportional thinking of Palladio. A foundational text of the harmonic strand of Christian Cabala.

## For artists & game designers
A design thesis in one book: the cosmos as tuned intervals — score your world's planes to musical ratios for a literally harmonious cosmology.

citations.text: Francesco Giorgio, *De Harmonia Mundi Totius Cantica Tria* (Venice, 1525).
Rudolf Wittkower, *Architectural Principles in the Age of Humanism* (1949).

title: Kircher's Tree of Life (Sephirotic diagram)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
Athanasius Kircher's diagram of the cabalist Tree of Life — the version that fixed the standard layout of ten sefirot and twenty-two connecting paths.

## Iconography
Ten circular [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]], each labelled with its Hebrew name and a divine name, are joined by the [[motif:twenty-two paths|twenty-two paths]] of the Hebrew alphabet, arranged in the three-pillar configuration descending from *Keter* to *Malkhut*. Kircher frames the scheme with Latin correspondences.

## Significance
Published in Kircher's *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* (1652–54), this is the most reproduced Christian-cabalist Tree of the early modern period and the direct ancestor of the diagram used by the 19th-century occult revival and the Golden Dawn. Kircher (1602–1680), the Jesuit polymath, read the sefirot as a node in his universal theology linking Hebrew, Egyptian, and Pythagorean wisdom. (The image is closely related to but distinct from his circular 'names of God' diagram.)

## For artists & game designers
The canonical ten-node, twenty-two-edge graph — a ready skill-tree topology already weighted with names, colours, and elemental tags.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin, *Athanasius Kircher's Theatre of the World* (2009).

title: Title page of Llull's *Ars Magna* (printed edition)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Ramon Llull (printed edition)
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The title page of an early printed edition of Ramon Llull's *Ars Magna, generalis et ultima* — the combinatorial 'Great Art' that Renaissance Christian cabalists embraced as a method for combining the divine names.

## Iconography
The ornate Latin title page of the *Ars magna generalis et ultima*, naming Llull's art as the 'master-key and door' to all arts and sciences.

## Significance
Ramon Llull (ca. 1232–1316) built an *ars combinatoria* of rotating figures and tables that combined a fixed set of divine 'dignities' (Goodness, Greatness, Eternity, Power, Wisdom…) to generate all true propositions and to convert unbelievers by reason. Renaissance thinkers — Pico, Agrippa, Bruno, and later Kircher — fused Lullism with [[motif:christian cabala|Christian Cabala]] and Pythagorean number-art, treating the wheels of [[motif:divine names|divine names]] as a cabalist combinatory. Frances Yates traced this 'Lullism' as a major current of Renaissance occult thought. (This printed title page is distinct from the held Llull diagram plates.)

## For artists & game designers
The original 'logic engine': a fixed alphabet of divine attributes combined mechanically to generate every truth — a ready mechanic for a deduction or spell-combination system.

citations.text: Ramon Llull, *Ars magna generalis et ultima* (ca. 1305; early printings).
Frances A. Yates, *Lull and Bruno: Collected Essays* (1982).

title: Pentagrams with the Pentagrammaton (Guaita)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Stanislas de Guaita
summary:
The pair of pentagrams from Stanislas de Guaita's *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897), inscribed with the five Hebrew letters of the Pentagrammaton.

## Iconography
Two opposed [[motif:pentagram|pentagrams]] — one point-up, one point-down — each carrying around its five points the Hebrew letters *Yod-He-Shin-Vav-He* (*YHShWH*), the [[motif:pentagrammaton|Pentagrammaton]] of Yeshua. The upward star aligns the letters with the harmonious microcosm; the inverted star, with the infernal. A human figure is fitted to the rays.

## Significance
Guaita's diagram is the late-occult-revival crystallization of Reuchlin's Christian-cabalist idea: the Name of Jesus as Tetragrammaton-plus-*Shin* set into the five-pointed star, the upright star marking divine order and the reversed one its perversion — the source of the modern association of the inverted pentagram with evil. It synthesizes Éliphas Lévi's pentagram-as-microcosm with the older Name-magic. Lévi's and Guaita's works are the key sources.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'good star / evil star' pair, each labelled with the same sacred name — a built-in alignment switch for a magic symbol.

citations.text: Stanislas de Guaita, *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897).
Éliphas Lévi, *Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie* (1855–56).

title: Portrait of Pico della Mirandola (Rubens)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Peter Paul Rubens (after an earlier likeness)
rights: CC0 (Museum Plantin-Moretus, Antwerp). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A portrait of Giovanni Pico della Mirandola (1463–1494), the founder of Christian Cabala, attributed to Peter Paul Rubens.

## Iconography
The young philosopher is shown in three-quarter view, long-haired and richly dressed — the conventional 'phoenix of the wits' likeness of Pico that circulated through Renaissance and Baroque copies, including this version in the Plantin-Moretus collection.

## Significance
Pico's *Conclusiones* (the '900 Theses,' 1486) and his *Oratio de hominis dignitate* were the first systematic attempt to read the Hebrew [[motif:sefirot|Kabbalah]] as a confirmation of Christianity, arguing 'no science gives us more certainty of Christ's divinity than magic and Cabala.' Drawing on the converts Flavius Mithridates and on Pythagorean number-theology, he launched the whole tradition of Christian Cabala later carried by Reuchlin, Agrippa, and Postel. Frances Yates and Chaim Wirszubski are the standard guides.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal Renaissance magus-scholar: youthful, aristocratic, surrounded by the aura of forbidden synthesis — a perfect questgiver of secret wisdom.

citations.text: Chaim Wirszubski, *Pico della Mirandola's Encounter with Jewish Mysticism* (1989).
Frances A. Yates, *Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition* (1964).

title: Portrait of Guillaume Postel
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous engraver (Rijksmuseum impression)
summary:
An engraved portrait of Guillaume Postel (1510–1581), the French Hebraist, orientalist, and visionary who carried Christian Cabala to its most radical extreme.

## Iconography
A bust portrait of the bearded scholar (here catalogued as an 'astronomer'), in the conventional learned-man format of 16th-century engraving.

## Significance
Postel was the first to translate the [[motif:zohar|*Zohar*]] and the *Sefer Yetzirah* into Latin and the great prophet of a coming [[motif:universal religion|universal religion]] (*concordia mundi*) that would unite all peoples under a restored Christianity grounded in [[motif:christian cabala|Cabala]]. He proclaimed the Venetian holy woman 'Mother Joanna' the female messiah and read the cabalist *Shekhinah* into her — getting himself declared 'mad rather than heretical' by the Inquisition. A central, eccentric figure of the Christian-cabalist tradition; Marion Kuntz and Frances Yates study him.

## For artists & game designers
The heterodox prophet-scholar archetype: brilliant linguist, world-reforming visionary, and borderline heretic — a rich NPC of dangerous ideas.

citations.text: Marion L. Kuntz, *Guillaume Postel: Prophet of the Restitution of All Things* (1981).

title: Proclus, *Elements of Theology* (Patrizi, 1583)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Proclus; Latin trans. Francesco Patrizi
summary:
The 1583 Latin edition of Proclus's *Elements of Theology* and *Elements of Physics*, translated by Francesco Patrizi — the Neoplatonic metaphysics that underlies Renaissance cosmological diagrams of the One's procession into concentric and interlocking circles.

## Iconography
The printed title page of Patrizi's edition; the *Elements of Theology* itself is a chain of 211 propositions deducing, more geometrico, the whole order of being from the [[motif:the one|One]] through the [[motif:henads|henads]] and triads down to bodies and souls.

## Significance
Proclus (412–485), last great head of the Athenian Academy, gave Neoplatonism its most rigorous form: all things proceed from and return to the One ([[motif:procession and return|*proodos* and *epistrophē*]]) through ranked triads of [[motif:henads|henads]], each level mirrored in interlocking circles of cause and effect. Mediated by Patrizi and by Pico's reading in the *Heptaplus*, this scheme shaped Christian-cabalist and Renaissance cosmic diagrams (e.g. the Proclan-cosmos model on the cover of Crofton Black's study of Pico). The *Elements* also reached the Middle Ages disguised as the *Liber de Causis*.

## For artists & game designers
The source-code of emanation cosmology: a strictly ranked descent from a single One through nested triads — a clean spec for a tiered metaphysical world-structure.

citations.text: Proclus, *The Elements of Theology*, ed. & trans. E. R. Dodds (1933; 2nd ed. 1963).
Crofton Black, *Pico's Heptaplus and Biblical Hermeneutics* (2006).

title: Proclus, *Platonic Theology* (MS. Laud. gr. 18)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Proclus (Greek manuscript)
summary:
A Greek manuscript page of Proclus's *Platonic Theology* (Bodleian MS. Laud. gr. 18), the fullest exposition of the Neoplatonic hierarchy of divine orders.

## Iconography
A leaf of the Greek text in a Byzantine hand. The *Platonic Theology* unfolds the ranks of gods as a graduated descent from the [[motif:the one|One]] through the [[motif:henads|henads]] (unitary gods) and successive [[motif:triads|triads]] — intelligible, intelligible-and-intellective, and intellective orders — each reflecting the whole at its own level.

## Significance
Proclus's system of nested triads and henads is exactly the cosmos that Renaissance Neoplatonists pictured as interlocking circles: each divine order both proceeds from and reverts upon its cause, so the whole is a set of concentric, mutually-mirroring rings of being. Pico drew on this Proclan model in the *Heptaplus*; Patrizi, Ficino, and the Christian cabalists transmitted it as the metaphysical armature beneath the sefirot and the chain of being. E. R. Dodds and H. D. Saffrey are the standard editors.

## For artists & game designers
A manuscript prop for a Neoplatonic sourcebook — and, in its content, a blueprint for self-similar nested realms, each tier a smaller copy of the whole.

citations.text: Proclus, *Théologie platonicienne*, ed. H. D. Saffrey & L. G. Westerink (1968–97).
Crofton Black, *Pico's Heptaplus and Biblical Hermeneutics* (2006).

title: Leaf from Reuchlin's *De Arte Cabalistica*
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Johannes Reuchlin
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A leaf from Johannes Reuchlin's *De Arte Cabalistica* (1517), the first full Latin treatise on Kabbalah by a Christian and the founding textbook of Christian Cabala.

## Iconography
The printed page sets Latin exposition beside [[motif:hebrew letters|Hebrew]] words and divine names, in the dialogue form Reuchlin used to expound the doctrine of the sefirot, the permutation of letters, and the messianic reading of the Name.

## Significance
Reuchlin (1455–1522), the great Christian Hebraist, followed Pico in arguing that the Kabbalah secretly confirms Christ. *De Arte Cabalistica*, dedicated to Pope Leo X, systematized [[motif:gematria|gematria]] and the cabalist Name-doctrine for European scholars and tied it to [[motif:number mysticism|Pythagorean arithmology]], casting Pythagoras as a transmitter of Hebrew wisdom. With his earlier *De Verbo Mirifico* it set the agenda for two centuries of Christian Kabbalah. Wilhelm Schmidt-Biggemann surveys the tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A dense bilingual grimoire page — Latin learning shot through with Hebrew names — for the look of a scholar's forbidden study.

citations.text: Johannes Reuchlin, *De Arte Cabalistica libri tres* (Hagenau, 1517).

title: *De Verbo Mirifico* (binding, Reuchlin)
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Johannes Reuchlin
summary:
The binding of an early edition of Reuchlin's *De Verbo Mirifico* ('On the Wonder-Working Word,' 1494), the book that introduced the Pentagrammaton to Christian Cabala.

## Iconography
The blind-tooled cover of the volume; the work itself is a dialogue arguing that the unpronounceable Hebrew [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]] YHWH becomes pronounceable and salvific when the letter *Shin* is inserted to spell *YHShWH* — Yeshua, Jesus.

## Significance
Reuchlin's 'wonder-working word' is the [[motif:pentagrammaton|Pentagrammaton]]: the Name of Jesus as the Tetragrammaton plus *Shin*, presented as the supreme word of power that makes the ineffable Name effable and redemptive. This Christian-cabalist conceit, first set out here in 1494, recurs across Renaissance magic — in Agrippa, in the pentagram-with-Hebrew-letters, and in countless later occult diagrams. Frances Yates and Charles Zika trace its influence.

## For artists & game designers
The 'true name unlocked' trope: a forbidden word made speakable by adding one secret letter — a tidy puzzle-key mechanic.

citations.text: Charles Zika, 'Reuchlin's De Verbo Mirifico and the Magic Debate of the Late Fifteenth Century,' *Journal of the Warburg and Courtauld Institutes* (1976).

title: Rose Cross with Tetragrammaton and Pentagrammaton
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: RootOfAllLight (after a Golden Dawn design by Frater5)
summary:
A Rose-Cross lamen carrying the Tetragrammaton and, at its centre, the Pentagrammaton — the Golden Dawn's emblem of Christian-cabalist Name-magic.

## Iconography
The ornate rose-and-cross bears the four letters of the [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]] on its arms and the five-fold [[motif:pentagrammaton|Pentagrammaton]] *YHShWH* at the heart of the rose, surrounded by the Hebrew alphabet, the elements, planets, and zodiac in coloured correspondence — the whole a compressed map of the cabalist cosmos.

## Significance
The Rose Cross of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn distils the long Christian-Cabala tradition: Reuchlin's salvific Name of Jesus (Tetragrammaton + *Shin*) placed at the centre of a Rosicrucian symbol of resurrection, ringed by the sefirotic and astrological correspondences inherited from Knorr von Rosenroth and the occult revival. (This is a modern redrawing of the order's design.) Israel Regardie's *The Golden Dawn* documents the lamen.

## For artists & game designers
A single insignia that encodes an entire correspondence system — name at the centre, alphabet and elements radiating out: a ready faction-sigil with built-in lore.

citations.text: Israel Regardie, *The Golden Dawn* (1937–40), on the Rose Cross lamen.

title: The Tetragrammaton-Tetractys
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous (after the Pythagorean and cabalist tradition)
summary:
The Pythagorean tetractys fused with the Hebrew Tetragrammaton — the emblem in which Christian Cabala wedded Greek number-mysticism to the ineffable Name of God.

## Iconography
A triangle of the four letters *Yod–He–Vav–He* arranged in the four rows of the [[motif:tetractys|tetractys]]: one *Yod* at the apex, then *Yod-He*, then *Yod-He-Vav*, then the full *Yod-He-Vav-He* — so that the cumulative letters sum to the divine Name while the cumulative dots (1+2+3+4) sum to the perfect [[motif:decad|decad]], ten.

## Significance
The Pythagorean tetractys was the sacred figure of the decad, the sum of the first four numbers and the root of cosmic harmony, sworn upon by the Pythagoreans. Renaissance Christian cabalists — Pico, Reuchlin, Agrippa — noted that the four-letter Name laid out triangularly likewise yields ten, binding [[motif:number mysticism|Pythagorean arithmology]] to Hebrew [[motif:divine names|name-theology]]. The figure became a standard proof-emblem of the supposed unity of all wisdom.

## For artists & game designers
A perfect convergence glyph: stack a sacred word so its accumulating rows resolve into a single perfect number — instant 'hidden unity' iconography.

citations.text: Johannes Reuchlin, *De Arte Cabalistica* (1517), on the Name and number.
S. K. Heninger, *Touches of Sweet Harmony: Pythagorean Cosmology and Renaissance Poetics* (1974).

title: The Pythagorean Tetraktys
work: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous (Pythagorean tradition)
summary:
The bare tetraktys — the triangular array of ten points in four rows that was the central sacred symbol of Pythagorean number-mysticism.

## Iconography
Ten dots in four ascending rows (1, 2, 3, 4) form an equilateral [[motif:triangle of ten dots|triangle]]. The single point at the apex is the [[motif:monad|monad]] (unity); the four rows generate the point, line, plane, and solid, and their sum is the [[motif:decad|decad]], ten — held to contain all number and so all things.

## Significance
The [[motif:tetractys|tetraktys]] was the figure by which Pythagoreans swore their oath, 'by him who gave to our soul the tetraktys.' It encoded the musical ratios (2:1 octave, 3:2 fifth, 4:3 fourth) and thus the harmony of the cosmos. Transmitted through Theon of Smyrna, Nicomachus, and Iamblichus, it became foundational to Neoplatonic and Renaissance number-theology, where the decad mirrors the ten sefirot.

## For artists & game designers
The minimalist sacred symbol: ten dots that silently encode unity, the four elements, and musical harmony — a clean occult sigil for a numerological system.

citations.text: Iamblichus, *Life of Pythagoras*; Theon of Smyrna, *Mathematics Useful for Reading Plato*.
Walter Burkert, *Lore and Science in Ancient Pythagoreanism* (1972).

title: Psyche Entering Cupid's Garden
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: John William Waterhouse
rights: Public domain. Harris Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Waterhouse's *Psyche Entering Cupid's Garden* (1903), illustrating the tale of Cupid and Psyche embedded in Apuleius's *Golden Ass*.

## Iconography
**Psyche** pushes open the gate into the enchanted **garden** of the unseen god, hesitating on the **threshold** of the invisible palace where her mysterious bridegroom awaits. The lush, secret garden and her cautious step evoke the wonder and dread of the marvellous tale.

## Significance
The story of **Cupid and Psyche** is the central inset of Apuleius's *Metamorphoses* (the *Golden Ass*), the same Latin novel that ends with the hero Lucius's initiation into the mysteries of [[motif:isis|Isis]]. Already in antiquity the tale was read allegorically as the soul's (*psyche*'s) trials, suffering and final union with divine Love — a reading revived by the Platonists and beloved of Victorian painters. Waterhouse treats it as a parable of the questing soul.

## For artists & game designers
The threshold of the enchanted unseen palace — the archetypal 'step into the magic place' moment, all anticipation and concealed presence.

title: The Wine of Circe
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Edward Burne-Jones
summary:
Burne-Jones's *The Wine of Circe*, the enchantress dosing the wine-cups as Odysseus's ships appear on the horizon.

## Iconography
**Circe** leans gracefully over two golden wine-jars, dropping her **potion** into the cups laid for guests. Two black **panthers** — themselves transformed men — crouch at her feet, and through the window the **ships** of the approaching Greeks ride on a green sea. The mood is one of poised, decorative menace.

## Significance
A key Aesthetic-movement image of [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|Circe]], the design moved D. G. Rossetti to write a sonnet. Burne-Jones reads the Homeric witch as a figure of beautiful, languid danger, the meal itself the trap; the panthers literalize the metamorphosis to come. It distils the Victorian fascination with the antique sorceress as *femme fatale*.

## For artists & game designers
The poisoned banquet as ambush — the threat is in the hospitality, with already-transformed victims standing guard as beasts.

citations.text: D. G. Rossetti, sonnet 'For The Wine of Circe by Edward Burne-Jones'.
F. MacCarthy, *The Last Pre-Raphaelite: Edward Burne-Jones* (2011).

title: The Cumaean Sibyl (from the Famous Persons cycle, Villa Carducci)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Andrea del Castagno
summary:
Andrea del Castagno's Cumaean Sibyl from his fresco cycle of Famous Persons (*uomini e donne famosi*) at the Villa Carducci, c. 1450.

## Iconography
The **Sibyl** strides forward in a feigned niche, holding an open **scroll** and gesturing, posed like a living statue among the cycle's heroes and heroines. Castagno's sharp, sculptural early-Renaissance line gives her a vivid, almost monumental presence.

## Significance
The **Cumaean Sibyl** was admitted to a gallery of exemplary figures because, like the Hebrew prophets, she was held to have foretold Christ — the same logic that placed sibyls on church walls and ceilings across Italy. Castagno's cycle is an early Florentine instance of the humanist *uomini famosi* tradition, into which the pagan [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|prophetess]] is naturally absorbed.

## For artists & game designers
The prophetess as one of a hall of heroes — showing how an oracle can be presented as a celebrated historical personage rather than an uncanny outsider.

title: Cumaean Sibyl (after Domenichino)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Angelica Kauffmann, after Domenichino
summary:
Angelica Kauffmann's copy after Domenichino's famous *Cumaean Sibyl*, the serene Baroque type of the antique prophetess.

## Iconography
The young **Sibyl** wears an exotic embroidered **turban** and gazes upward in inspiration, an **open book** and a sheet of **musical notation** before her — the Baroque convention linking prophecy with divine harmony. Her beauty and calm contrast sharply with Michelangelo's brawny seeress.

## Significance
Domenichino's original (Galleria Borghese) became the most copied image of a [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|sibyl]] in European art, and the young Kauffmann's faithful copy testifies to its fame and to how prophetesses were studied as models of ideal female beauty. The book and music encode the belief that the [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|sibyls]] sang the future in inspired verse.

## For artists & game designers
The gentle, turbaned oracle — prophecy as beautiful reverie rather than violent possession; a softer template for a seeress NPC.

title: Sibilla Cumana (the original, Galleria Borghese)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Domenichino (Domenico Zampieri)
rights: CC BY 3.0 (photo: Sailko). Galleria Borghese, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Domenichino's own *Sibilla Cumana* of 1616–17 in the Galleria Borghese, the prototype behind countless later copies.

## Iconography
The **Cumaean Sibyl** sits in a richly coloured **turban**, holding a sheet of **music** and turning her eyes heavenward, with the neck of a **viol** and a **book** beside her. Domenichino's classicizing Baroque gives her a poised, luminous gravity.

## Significance
This is the source image whose fame made the turbaned, music-bearing [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|Cumaean Sibyl]] a fixture of European galleries — copied by Kauffmann among many others. The pairing of prophetic book and musical score reflects the long association of sibylline [[motif:divination|divination]] with inspired song, from Virgil's *Aeneid* VI to the Renaissance idea of cosmic harmony.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical Baroque oracle: study it next to its copies to see how one design propagates and mutates through reproduction.

title: Circe (or Melissa)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Dosso Dossi
rights: CC BY 3.0 (photo: Sailko). Galleria Borghese, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Dosso Dossi's enigmatic enchantress in a wood, traditionally called Circe but perhaps the benevolent sorceress Melissa of Ariosto, c. 1520 (Galleria Borghese).

## Iconography
A richly dressed **sorceress** sits in a dark landscape holding an inscribed **tablet** of magical characters, an open **book** of spells beside her, surrounded by **beasts** and birds — men she has transformed, or whom she is releasing. Suits of armour hang in the trees.

## Significance
The figure hovers between two readings: the cruel Homeric/Ovidian [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|Circe]] who turns men to animals, and the kindly enchantress **Melissa** of Ariosto's *Orlando Furioso*, who reverses Alcina's spells. Dosso, court painter at Ferrara, paints magic as a learned, bookish art — the witch as scholar with grimoire and inscribed plaque — reflecting the Este court's taste for chivalric romance.

## For artists & game designers
The enchantress-as-scholar: a spellcaster defined by an open grimoire and an inscribed tablet rather than by wand and potion.

citations.text: P. Humfrey & M. Lucco, *Dosso Dossi: Court Painter in Renaissance Ferrara* (1998).

title: Medea
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Anselm Feuerbach
summary:
Anselm Feuerbach's grand 1870 *Medea*, the sorceress as brooding tragic mother on the eve of her revenge.

## Iconography
**Medea** sits massive and still on the **seashore**, her two **children** asleep against her, while in the distance Jason's **ship** is readied. Rather than the act of witchcraft, Feuerbach paints the heavy silence before the catastrophe — the abandoned wife contemplating the murder that will be her sorcery's last and worst work.

## Significance
The German classicist Feuerbach treats [[motif:medea-the-sorceress|Medea]] in the noble, Raphaelesque idiom of his 'ideal' style, drawing on Euripides. Here the magic recedes and the human tragedy dominates: the enchantress is above all a betrayed woman, her supernatural powers the instrument of a wholly human grief and rage.

## For artists & game designers
A counter-model to the spell-casting witch: power held in reserve, all menace conveyed through stillness and the sleeping children who will become its victims.

citations.text: J. Ecker, *Anselm Feuerbach: Leben und Werk* (1991).

title: Saturn Devouring His Son
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Francisco de Goya
rights: Public domain. Museo del Prado, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Goya's nightmarish *Saturn Devouring His Son*, one of the Black Paintings from the Quinta del Sordo, c. 1819–23 (Prado).

## Iconography
The wild-eyed god **Saturn** (Greek Kronos) crouches in darkness, clutching the bloodied, half-eaten body of one of his **children** and cramming it into his mouth. The image is reduced to its raw horror: a black void, a crazed giant, and the act of **devouring** itself.

## Significance
The myth — Kronos eating his offspring to forestall the prophecy that one would overthrow him — was read in the astrological tradition through the planet **Saturn**, lord of [[motif:melancholy|melancholy]], time, and devouring age (see Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy*). Goya strips away Renaissance allegory to expose the [[motif:saturn-devouring|astral god]] as pure, lightless dread, an emblem of Time consuming all it creates.

## For artists & game designers
The purest 'devouring titan' image in Western art — proof that horror intensifies as ornament is removed: one figure, one act, total darkness.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

title: The Delphic Sibyl (engraving after Michelangelo's Sistine fresco)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Giorgio Ghisi (and others), after Michelangelo
date: engraving (16th c.), after the fresco of 1508–12
rights: Public domain (CC0). The Met, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving after Michelangelo's Delphic Sibyl, the most celebrated of his Sistine prophetesses, associated with the oracle of Apollo at Delphi.

## Iconography
The youthful **Delphic Sibyl** turns sharply, unrolling a **scroll** of prophecy, her wide eyes and windblown drapery suggesting the seizure of inspiration. Of all Michelangelo's sibyls she is the most idealized and serene.

## Significance
Linked to the [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|sibylline]] tradition and to the great oracle of **Delphi**, she stands among the pagan seeresses Michelangelo enrolled as unwitting witnesses to Christian revelation. The placing of these classical prophetesses alongside the Hebrew prophets is the supreme Renaissance statement of the idea that pagan **divination** had glimpsed the truth. Engraved series spread her image across Europe.

## For artists & game designers
The inspired moment of prophecy — body twisting, scroll snapping open — captures oracular possession as a single dynamic pose.

title: The Cumaean Sibyl (engraving after Michelangelo's Sistine fresco)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Giovanni Volpato, after Michelangelo
date: engraving (later 18th c.), after the fresco of 1508–12
rights: Public domain (CC0). The Met, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving after Michelangelo's Cumaean Sibyl on the Sistine ceiling, the pagan prophetess who was believed to have foretold Christ.

## Iconography
The aged, powerfully muscular **Cumaean Sibyl** pores over a great **open book** of prophecy, attended by two **putti**. Her formidable physical bulk — Michelangelo's famous *terribilità* — conveys the weight of the oracular knowledge she carries.

## Significance
The **sibyls** were pagan prophetesses whom Christian tradition, following Augustine and the *Sibylline Oracles*, credited with foreseeing the coming of Christ; Michelangelo therefore set five of them, the [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|Cumaean]] among them, beside the Hebrew prophets on the Sistine ceiling. The Cumaean Sibyl is the one who, in Virgil's *Aeneid* VI, guides Aeneas to the underworld. Engravings such as Volpato's carried Michelangelo's design across Europe.

## For artists & game designers
The oracle as monumental scholar: prophecy embodied not in a frail seeress but in a colossal, book-wielding sage.

citations.text: Virgil, *Aeneid* VI; Augustine, *City of God* XVIII.

title: Erichtho (Sextus Pompeius consulting the Thessalian witch)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: John Hamilton Mortimer
summary:
John Hamilton Mortimer's image of Erichtho, the Thessalian necromancer of Lucan's *Pharsalia*, the most extreme witch of Roman epic.

## Iconography
The haggard **Erichtho** works over a fresh **corpse** on the **battlefield**, reanimating a dead soldier so that he may prophesy the outcome of the civil war to **Sextus Pompeius**, who comes to consult her. Mortimer renders the scene with Romantic gloom — gaunt figures, ruined landscape, the horror of the reawakened dead.

## Significance
**Erichtho** (Lucan, *Pharsalia* VI) is classical literature's darkest sorceress: she robs graves, compels the gods, and forces a slain man back to life for a single, hideous prophecy. As a study of [[motif:necromancy|necromancy]] she stands behind Dante's and Goethe's witches. Mortimer, an English history-painter drawn to violent and macabre subjects, helped revive her for the Gothic imagination.

## For artists & game designers
The battlefield reanimator: a witch who raises the freshly dead to extract information — necromancy as forced interrogation of a corpse.

citations.text: J. Sunderland, *John Hamilton Mortimer: His Life and Works* (1988).

title: Saul and the Witch of Endor
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Jacob Cornelisz van Oostsanen
rights: Public domain. Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Jacob Cornelisz van Oostsanen's 1526 panel, which fuses the biblical Witch of Endor with a Northern-Renaissance witches' sabbath (Rijksmuseum).

## Iconography
In the foreground the kneeling old **witch** raises the shrouded prophet **Samuel** within a **magic circle** before the fainting **King Saul**. Around them swarms a full demonological landscape: **naked witches** brewing and **flying**, cavorting demons, and a smoking cauldron — the sabbath imagery of Oostsanen's own age grafted onto the Old Testament story.

## Significance
This is the earliest dated Netherlandish painting of the subject and a landmark in the iconography of [[motif:witchcraft|witchcraft]]: it reads the [[motif:witch-of-endor|Witch of Endor]] (1 Samuel 28) through the contemporary witch-craze, much as Hans Baldung Grien did in prints. The biblical necromancer becomes a fully fledged sabbath-witch, documenting how antiquity's magic was re-imagined in early-modern terms.

## For artists & game designers
A masterclass in 'period drift': a single canvas layering an ancient summoning with a whole contemporary witch-world of flight, cauldrons, and demons.

citations.text: D. Carasso-Kok & J. Levy-van Halm (eds.), *Jacob Cornelisz van Oostsanen* (2014).

title: Pythagoras (detail from The School of Athens)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Raphael
summary:
Raphael's Pythagoras from the *School of Athens* (Stanza della Segnatura), the sage of sacred number absorbed in writing while a tablet displays his harmonic ratios.

## Iconography
**Pythagoras** sits writing in a great book, his brow furrowed, while a disciple holds before him a **tablet** showing the *epogdoon* and the **tetractys** — the diagram of musical ratios (the octave, fifth and fourth) and the sacred triangle of the first four numbers whose sum is ten. Onlookers crane to copy his wisdom.

## Significance
Pythagoras was revered in the Renaissance as the founder of the doctrine that number underlies the cosmos — the **harmony of the spheres** and the mystical [[motif:sacred-number|tetractys]]. Through Ficino and the Neoplatonists his arithmology fed directly into Hermetic and [[motif:kabbalah|Kabbalistic]] number-mysticism. Raphael places him among the ancestors of philosophy in the most famous of all images of the antique sages.

## For artists & game designers
