The scholar-mystic defined by a single prop: the harmonic tablet that turns a writing figure into the patriarch of sacred mathematics.

citations.text: S. K. Heninger, *Touches of Sweet Harmony: Pythagorean Cosmology and Renaissance Poetics* (1974).
I. Pagliara, in *Raffaello nell'appartamento di Giulio II e Leone X* (1993).

title: Saul and the Witch of Endor (etching, Rijksmuseum)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Anonymous (after a Baroque composition); Rijksmuseum print
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Baroque print of Saul and the Witch of Endor (Rijksmuseum), depicting the most famous necromancy of the Bible.

## Iconography
The **Witch of Endor** conjures within a dim, torchlit chamber as the spectral form of the prophet **Samuel** rises from the ground. **King Saul**, who has come in disguise, collapses in terror at the apparition's prophecy of his doom. The scene is staged with Baroque flourishes of smoke, gesture and dramatic light.

## Significance
The episode (1 Samuel 28) — the medium of En-dor raising Samuel's shade at the king's command — was the great biblical proof-text for [[motif:necromancy|necromancy]] and was endlessly debated by demonologists: was it the true Samuel, a demon, or fraud? Painters from van Oostsanen to Salvator Rosa and West returned to it as the supreme scene of forbidden conjuring sanctioned by Scripture itself.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'summon the dead' tableau: kneeling witch, rising ghost, and a powerful man brought low — staged in a single torchlit room.

citations.text: S. Schreuder, in Rijksmuseum online catalogue, RP-P-OB-63.922.

title: Moses and the Brazen Serpent
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Giuseppe Nuvolone
summary:
Giuseppe Nuvolone's Baroque *Moses and the Brazen Serpent*, the Old Testament scene of a healing image set up against a plague of snakes.

## Iconography
**Moses** raises the **bronze serpent** coiled upon a **pole** while the Israelites, attacked by fiery **serpents**, writhe in agony below; those who look up at the metal snake are healed. The composition swirls with tangled bodies, struck and saved, around the upright standard of the cure.

## Significance
In Numbers 21 God commands Moses to make a [[motif:brazen-serpent|bronze serpent]] (*Nehushtan*) so that the bitten may look on it and live — an image at the very border of sanctioned wonder and forbidden idol, later destroyed by Hezekiah. Christian typology read it as a prefiguration of the Crucifixion (John 3:14). The motif fascinated artists as a rare biblical instance of an apotropaic, quasi-magical healing object.

## For artists & game designers
The healing totem amid catastrophe: a raised standard that cures those who behold it — a built-in 'look to be saved' mechanic.

citations.text: M. Rossi & A. Rovetta, *I Nuvoloni: pittori a Milano nel Seicento* (exhibition literature).

title: Saturn Devouring His Son
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Peter Paul Rubens
rights: Public domain. Museo del Prado, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Rubens's *Saturn Devouring His Son* (Prado), the learned Baroque treatment of the myth, painted c. 1636–38.

## Iconography
The aged, bearded **Saturn**, leaning on his **scythe**, bites into the breast of a writhing infant **son**. Above gleams a band of **three stars** — Rubens's reference to the contemporary telescopic 'triple' appearance of the planet Saturn (its rings, not yet understood). The old man's wings and sickle identify him equally as Time.

## Significance
Rubens fuses three identities long entwined in the tradition: the Titan **Kronos** who eats his children, the planet **Saturn** of the [[motif:astrology|astrologers]], and **Time** (Chronos) the all-devourer. The starry detail shows the painter's awareness of Galilean astronomy, tying the [[motif:saturn-devouring|myth]] directly to the heavens. The work stands behind Goya's later, bleaker version.

## For artists & game designers
A design where a single celestial detail (the three stars) anchors a mythological scene to a real planet — myth and cosmology in one frame.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

title: Medea
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Frederick Sandys
summary:
Frederick Sandys's *Medea*, the Colchian sorceress in the act of mixing a fatal enchantment, painted 1866–68.

## Iconography
**Medea** stands wild-eyed before her brazier, fingering a **coral necklace** at her throat as she prepares a **potion**; a **toad**, a shell, and ritual paraphernalia lie before her, and behind glints the **Golden Fleece** and a Japanese-screen frieze of Argonauts. Her clenched, trance-like intensity conveys the moment of incantation.

## Significance
The subject is the sorceress of Euripides and Ovid who helps **Jason** win the Fleece, then destroys his bride with a poisoned gift. Sandys, the most technically dazzling of the Pre-Raphaelite circle, paints [[motif:medea-the-sorceress|Medea]] as the supreme witch — barbarian, learned in drugs, consumed by passion. Famously rejected by the Royal Academy in 1868, the picture became a cause célèbre.

## For artists & game designers
The spell-mixing close-up: a single figure, a brazier, and a clutter of grim ingredients (toad, shell, coral) communicate active witchcraft without any fantastical effect.

citations.text: B. Elzea, *Frederick Sandys 1829–1904: A Catalogue Raisonné* (2001).

title: Morgan le Fay
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Frederick Sandys
summary:
Frederick Sandys's *Morgan le Fay* (1864), the Arthurian enchantress caught in the frenzy of casting a destructive spell.

## Iconography
**Morgan le Fay** twists in mid-incantation, throwing on the enchanted, flame-coloured **robe** she has woven on the **loom** behind her to destroy King Arthur. Talismanic objects, a leopard-skin, and runic decoration crowd the chamber; her contorted pose and glaring eyes convey the violence of the working **spell**.

## Significance
Though medieval and Arthurian rather than antique, Morgan belongs to the same 19th-century re-imagining of the powerful magical woman as Sandys's [[motif:medea-the-sorceress|Medea]] and Waterhouse's [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|Circe]] — the *enchantress* descended ultimately from Circe and Medea. Sandys renders her with the same jewelled, claustrophobic intensity, making her a Pre-Raphaelite icon of female sorcery.

## For artists & game designers
Spell-casting as physical performance: a witch shown mid-gesture donning her enchanted garment, the magic dramatized through the body rather than through effects.

citations.text: B. Elzea, *Frederick Sandys 1829–1904: A Catalogue Raisonné* (2001).

title: The Tabula Cebetis (Table of Cebes) — allegorical print
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum print
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A detail from an engraved *Tabula Cebetis* (Table of Cebes), the allegorical map of human life derived from a Greek text ascribed to the Pythagorean Cebes.

## Iconography
The print lays out the **allegory of human life** as a series of **concentric enclosures**: souls enter the gate of life past **Fortune** and Deceit, pass through the precincts of false and **true education**, and ascend toward the citadel of Happiness. Each personified figure is labelled, making the whole a walkable moral diagram.

## Significance
The *Tabula Cebetis* (*pinax*), a dialogue of the 1st century CE attributed to a follower of [[motif:pythagoras|Pythagoras]], describes a votive panel in a temple of Kronos that maps the soul's pilgrimage. Rediscovered and printed throughout the Renaissance, it became a favourite subject for emblematic engravings uniting Platonic-Pythagorean philosophy with [[motif:hermetic|Hermetic]] and Stoic moral teaching.

## For artists & game designers
Literally a level-map of life: nested rings of trials and false paths leading to a final citadel — an allegory that is already a game board.

citations.text: *Tabula Cebetis* (Pseudo-Cebes), ed. J. T. Fitzgerald & L. M. White (1983).

title: Circe Offering the Cup to Ulysses
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: John William Waterhouse
summary:
Waterhouse's monumental Circe, the archetypal Pre-Raphaelite image of the antique enchantress, painted in 1891.

## Iconography
**Circe** sits enthroned and frontal, raising in one hand the drugged **cup** and in the other the **wand** with which she turns men to beasts. At her feet a transformed companion lies as a **swine**; behind her a **mirror** reflects the tiny approaching figure of **Ulysses**. Lions and a pool of spilled enchantment complete the composition.

## Significance
The scene is from *Odyssey* X, where the goddess-witch of Aiaia drugs Odysseus's crew. Waterhouse makes her the calm, commanding [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|sorceress]] who became the Victorian type of the dangerous magical woman, descended from Homer through Ovid. The mirror device — showing the hero she cannot yet see — is the painter's own theatrical addition.

## For artists & game designers
A seated boss-enchantress, cup-and-wand in hand, with a victim already transformed at her feet: a complete 'beast-maker' encounter in one frame.

title: Circe Invidiosa
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: John William Waterhouse
summary:
Waterhouse's 1892 *Circe Invidiosa* ('jealous Circe'), the enchantress poisoning the sea-pool of her rival Scylla.

## Iconography
**Circe** stands tall in an iridescent green-blue gown, pouring a stream of **poison** from a shallow bowl into the water below, where the half-glimpsed form of **Scylla** begins her monstrous **metamorphosis**. A grotesque sea-creature rises beneath her feet; the whole canvas is bathed in venomous green.

## Significance
The subject is from Ovid's *Metamorphoses* XIV: spurned by Glaucus, who loves Scylla, Circe out of [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|jealousy]] poisons the nymph's bathing-place and transforms her into a monster. Waterhouse condenses the Ovidian tale into a single vertical icon of cold, deliberate sorcery — magic as envy made visible.

## For artists & game designers
A vertical poisoning ritual rendered almost monochrome green — a study in how a single colour can encode toxicity and malice.

title: Saul and the Witch of Endor (etching, Rijksmuseum)
work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum print
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A second Rijksmuseum print of Saul and the Witch of Endor, showing a different compositional treatment of the conjuring of Samuel.

## Iconography
The **Witch of Endor** stands or kneels at her conjuring as the prophet **Samuel** appears; the disguised **King Saul** and his companions recoil. The print emphasizes the moment of the **apparition** and the king's dismay at hearing his coming defeat foretold.

## Significance
The story of En-dor (1 Samuel 28) generated a long print tradition across the Low Countries and beyond, since it offered the rare spectacle of a sanctioned biblical [[motif:necromancy|necromancy]]. Such engravings carried the imagery of the [[motif:witch-of-endor|raising of Samuel]] into Bibles and emblem books, fixing the visual formula of witch, rising shade, and stricken king.

## For artists & game designers
Variant blocking of the same beat — useful for studying how the 'summoner / apparition / horrified witness' triangle can be re-staged.

title: The Antikythera mechanism (main fragment)
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Anonymous (Hellenistic Greek); photograph by Joyofmuseums
place: National Archaeological Museum, Athens (found off Antikythera)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Joyofmuseums). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The largest surviving fragment ('Fragment A') of the Antikythera mechanism, a Hellenistic geared device recovered in 1901 from a shipwreck off the island of Antikythera and now in the National Archaeological Museum, Athens.

## Iconography
What survives is a mass of corroded **bronze gear-wheels** fused into a lump, with the teeth of one large wheel clearly visible. X-ray and CT imaging have revealed at least thirty interlocking gears and inscriptions. Reconstructions show it as a hand-cranked box whose dials displayed the **lunisolar calendar**, the phase and position of the Moon, the dates of **eclipses** (via the Saros and Exeligmos cycles), the Metonic cycle, and probably the motions of the planets and the timing of the Panhellenic games.

## Significance
The Antikythera mechanism is the oldest known analogue computer and the direct ancestor of the medieval astronomical clock: proof that the Greeks could embody astronomical *theory* in gearwork. Its sophistication was so unexpected that it reshaped the history of ancient technology (work by Derek de Solla Price, and the modern Antikythera Mechanism Research Project).

## For artists & game designers
A corroded green lump that turns out to be a precision computer is the perfect 'ancient impossible artefact' — its mundane ruin is exactly what makes the reveal land.

citations.text: D. de Solla Price, *Gears from the Greeks* (1974).
T. Freeth et al., 'Decoding the Antikythera Mechanism', *Nature* (2006).

title: Brass astronomical compendium / pocket instrument
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Anonymous instrument-maker; photograph by Rosie Summers
summary:
A small engraved brass instrument of the kind carried as a portable **astronomical compendium** — a pocket case combining several instruments on hinged leaves.

## Iconography
The piece is built around a finely **engraved brass dial** with a graduated rim and a magnetic compass for orientation. Compendia of this type typically fold open to reveal, on successive leaves, a portable **sundial** (often with a string gnomon), a list of city latitudes, a lunar volvelle for converting Sun-time to Moon-time at night, a wind-rose, and a table of the zodiac — the whole sky-reckoning kit of a Renaissance traveller in one brass box.

## Significance
The astronomical compendium is the personal, portable cousin of the great fixed clocks: where the cathedral clock modelled the heavens for a whole city, the compendium let one well-off owner reckon time, latitude and the [[motif:zodiac-signs|zodiac]] anywhere. Such objects (Augsburg and Nuremberg were the great centres) blur instrument, status-symbol and curio.

## For artists & game designers
A brass box that unfolds into five different instruments is a compact 'multi-tool' object — each leaf a different mini-mechanic the player can flip to.

title: Volvelle from Apian's Astronomicum Caesareum (1540)
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Petrus Apianus (Peter Apian)
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons (Houghton Library).
summary:
A leaf (f. 2r) of Petrus Apianus's *Astronomicum Caesareum*, printed at Ingolstadt in 1540 — one of the most lavish scientific books ever produced, here in the Houghton Library copy.

## Iconography
The page is dominated by a **volvelle**: layered, hand-coloured paper discs pinned at the center and fitted with silk threads and tiny seed-pearl markers, which the reader rotates to read off astronomical quantities. Apian's volvelles function as **paper equatoria** and computers — set the discs for a date and they give the geocentric longitude of a planet, the phase of the Moon, the time of an eclipse, or the position of the lunar dragon's head and tail.

## Significance
The *Astronomicum Caesareum*, dedicated to the Emperor Charles V (hence 'Caesarean'), is the high-water mark of the printed astronomical instrument: it puts a working planetary calculator into the hands of a reader who owns no brass. It belongs to the same impulse as the great clocks — to make the [[motif:planetary-motion|motions of the planets]] mechanically obtainable — but achieves it in paper and thread.

## For artists & game designers
A book whose pages are working machines (spin the disc, read the fate) is a tactile model for an in-game grimoire that is also a tool, not just lore text.

citations.text: O. Gingerich, 'Apianus's Astronomicum Caesareum', *Journal for the History of Astronomy* (1971).

title: Dials of the Besançon astronomical clock
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Auguste-Lucien Vérité (1858–60); photograph by PCouton
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: PCouton). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An array of dials on the astronomical clock of Besançon Cathedral, built 1858–60 by Auguste-Lucien Vérité.

## Iconography
The clock is a cabinet of some **70 dials and indicators** distributed over a tall neo-Gothic case. They report not only the hour but the **tides** at major French ports, the rising and setting of Sun and Moon, the lengths of day and night, the movements of the **planets**, eclipses, leap years and a perpetual calendar. Small **automata** representing the Resurrection and the days of the week animate at intervals.

## Significance
Besançon (Vérité also built the comparable Beauvais clock) is the 19th-century culmination of the cathedral-clock idea: an industrial-age machine that gathers astronomy, tide-tables and the liturgical calendar into one devotional instrument. It belongs to a moment when precision clockwork could finally *compute* the heavens it had long only symbolised.

## For artists & game designers
Seventy dials on one cabinet is a design lesson in information density — a single object that rewards close reading because every face tells a different truth.

citations.text: A. Ungerer, *Les Horloges astronomiques et monumentales* (1931).

title: The mechanism of the Eise Eisinga Planetarium, Franeker
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Eise Eisinga (1774–1781); photograph by Vera de Kok
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Vera de Kok). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The driving mechanism, mounted in the attic above the living-room ceiling, of the Eise Eisinga Planetarium in Franeker — the oldest working planetarium in the world, built by the wool-comber Eise Eisinga between 1774 and 1781.

## Iconography
The photograph shows the **wooden gear-train** of hand-cut oak wheels, driving-weights and a pendulum clock that powers the display. On the ceiling of the room below, gilded discs representing the **planets** ride in grooved tracks across a painted sky, moving in real time to scale; pointers give the date, the phase of the Moon, the signs of the zodiac and the hour.

## Significance
Eisinga built his **heliocentric** orrery partly to calm neighbours panicked by a 1774 pamphlet predicting that a planetary conjunction would destroy the Earth — a machine made to answer astrological dread with Newtonian order. It is the supreme survival of the domestic planetarium and a monument to Enlightenment self-education: a labourer's living room rebuilt as a model of the solar system.

## For artists & game designers
A secret machine-room above a humble parlour, full of slowly turning wooden gears that move the painted heavens below, is an irresistible set-piece for a hidden-mechanism reveal.

citations.text: A. van der Werf, *Eise Eisinga's Planetarium* (1981).

title: Model of the Lund Cathedral astronomical clock (Horologium mirabile)
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Model after the medieval clock; built under Theodor Wåhlin, c. 1913
rights: CC0 (public domain dedication). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A model (made c. 1913, in connection with the restoration overseen by Theodor Wåhlin) of the *Horologium mirabile Lundense*, the astronomical clock of Lund Cathedral, originally built around 1424.

## Iconography
The clock has two faces. The upper **astronomical dial** is an astrolabe showing the hours, the [[motif:zodiac-signs|zodiac]], and the positions of Sun and Moon over a fixed Earth; mechanical **knights** clash above it. The lower **calendar dial** is a vast computus disc for finding the dates of Easter and the feasts. At noon and 3 p.m. the **Magi** process before the Virgin and Child while the organ pipes play *In dulci jubilo*.

## Significance
Lund's medieval clock — rediscovered and reconstructed in the early 20th century — is the great Scandinavian survivor of the type. The byname *Horologium mirabile* ('the wonderful clock') captures the medieval reception of these machines as marvels at the edge of the miraculous.

## For artists & game designers
The paired dials — one for the sky, one for the calendar — are a clean two-screen UI metaphor: 'where the stars are' versus 'what day it is'.

citations.text: A. Ungerer, *Les Horloges astronomiques et monumentales* (1931).

title: The astronomical clock of Lyon Cathedral
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Medieval, much rebuilt (14th–17th c.); photograph by Zairon
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Zairon). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The astronomical clock in the north transept of the Primatiale Saint-Jean-Baptiste in Lyon, one of the oldest in Europe, its origins medieval and the present case largely 17th-century.

## Iconography
Within a tall painted Gothic case an **astrolabe dial** shows the positions of Sun, Moon and stars over Lyon and an ecclesiastical calendar good for movable feasts to the 2nd millennium's end. On the hour an **Annunciation** plays out above — God the Father, doves and angels move, a **rooster** at the summit beats its wings and crows three times.

## Significance
Lyon's clock is among the earliest astronomical clocks of France, repeatedly rebuilt (notably after damage in the Wars of Religion). Like its peers it married the *computus* — the church's calculation of Easter — to an astrolabe model of the sky, turning the cathedral transept into an observatory of sacred time.

## For artists & game designers
The crowing automaton rooster is a recurring motif across these clocks; as a sound-cue it makes a memorable, diegetic 'the hour has turned' signal.

citations.text: A. Ungerer, *Les Horloges astronomiques et monumentales* (1931).

title: The astronomical clock of Münster Cathedral
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Dietrich Tzwyvel & Nikolaus Windemaker (1540–43); photograph by Dietmar Rabich
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Dietmar Rabich). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The astronomical clock in the ambulatory of St Paul's Cathedral, Münster, built 1540–43 after the destruction of an earlier clock in the 1534 Anabaptist rising.

## Iconography
The richly painted face turns **anticlockwise**. A large central dial maps the positions of the **planets** in the zodiac on a geocentric scheme, surrounded by painted panels of the [[motif:zodiac-signs|signs]], the planetary gods and a learned border of astronomical figures. Below runs a perpetual calendar reaching to 2071. On the hour the **Three Magi** and a servant bow before the Virgin and Child while a carillon plays.

## Significance
Münster's clock is a Renaissance encyclopaedia of the sky painted in brilliant colour: a teaching machine that shows where every planet stands among the stars on any day. Its anticlockwise motion follows the apparent westward drift of the heavens — a learned choice that turns the whole face into a slow image of the turning firmament.

## For artists & game designers
An anticlockwise clock is a small uncanny detail that signals 'this place keeps time by older, stranger rules' without a word of exposition.

citations.text: E. Zinner, *Deutsche und niederländische astronomische Instrumente* (1956).

title: Nocturnal by Erasmus Habermel, Prague c. 1600
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Erasmus Habermel; photograph by Daderot
summary:
A brass **nocturnal** made by the master instrument-maker Erasmus Habermel in Prague around 1600, now in the Mathematisch-Physikalischer Salon, Dresden.

## Iconography
The nocturnal is a hand-held disc with a central **sighting hole** and a long rotating arm. To use it one sights **Polaris** through the hole, sets an outer ring to the date, then swings the arm onto the **pointer stars of the Big Dipper** (or Little Dipper); the arm's position against the hour-scale gives the time. Habermel's piece is finely engraved and pierced in the Mannerist court style of Rudolf II's Prague.

## Significance
The nocturnal turns the slow rotation of the [[motif:celestial-mechanism|circumpolar sky]] into a clock you carry: the heavens themselves are the moving part, the instrument merely reads them. Made for Rudolf II's celebrated court of astronomers and alchemists, it links the world of practical star-time to the same milieu that produced the Prague Orloj and the imperial astrological enterprise.

## For artists & game designers
A pocket device that requires you to find a real star before it works ties an in-game tool to the actual night sky — a satisfying loop of look-up-then-read.

citations.text: R.J.W. Evans, *Rudolf II and His World* (1973).

title: The astronomical clock of Olomouc (historical postcard)
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Anonymous (postcard); medieval clock, much altered
date: clock 15th c., rebuilt; postcard early 20th c.
summary:
A historical postcard of the astronomical clock set into the tower of the Town Hall of Olomouc in Moravia, showing the clock as it appeared before its 20th-century remodelling.

## Iconography
The tall niche holds an **astrolabe-type dial** of the heavens above a **calendar dial**, framed by Gothic tracery and ranks of painted and sculpted figures — saints, allegories and craftsmen. The postcard preserves a religious, late-medieval iconography that the present clock (rebuilt in a Socialist-Realist style in 1955, with mosaic workers replacing the saints) no longer shows.

## Significance
Olomouc belongs to the Central European family of civic astronomical clocks alongside Prague, displaying the same marriage of [[motif:astrolabe-dial|astrolabe]] and calendar on a town-hall façade. The image is valuable precisely as a record of a lost state: a reminder that these 'eternal' machines are repeatedly remade in the image of the regimes that own them.

## For artists & game designers
A before/after pair (sacred clock vs. its ideological rebuild) is a ready-made environmental-storytelling device about who controls public time.

citations.text: A. Ungerer, *Les Horloges astronomiques et monumentales* (1931).

title: The Salone of the Palazzo della Ragione, Padua
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Astrological fresco cycle after Pietro d'Abano (14th c.; repainted by Niccolò Miretto, 15th c.); photograph by Armin Kleiner
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Armin Kleiner). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The interior of the *Salone*, the immense first-floor hall of the Palazzo della Ragione in Padua, whose walls carry one of the largest astrological fresco cycles of the Middle Ages.

## Iconography
Under a vast keel-shaped wooden vault the walls are divided into hundreds of panels arranged as a great **astrological calendar**: the twelve [[motif:zodiac-signs|signs]] of the zodiac, their associated **decans** and planetary rulers, the **labours of the months**, apostles and saints, all keyed to a now-lost program devised by the physician-astrologer **Pietro d'Abano**. The cycle was repainted by Niccolò Miretto and assistants after a fire of 1420.

## Significance
The Salone is a building turned into a cosmic machine: a civic court of justice roofed over with the whole working astrology of the year, so that human law sat literally beneath the rule of the stars. With the Hall of the Months at Schifanoia (Ferrara), it is the supreme monumental survival of medieval astrological iconography, much studied since Aby Warburg.

## For artists & game designers
A single room whose walls *are* the calendar — every panel a date and its ruling power — is a model for a 'hub' space that doubles as the game's almanac.

citations.text: A. Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology' (1912).

title: The calendar dial of the Prague Orloj
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Calendar plate after Josef Mánes (1865); photograph by George Groutas
rights: CC BY 2.0 (photo: George Groutas). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The lower, calendar dial of the Prague Orloj, set beneath the astronomical face. The painted disc rotates once a year.

## Iconography
A great circular plate carries, in concentric rings, the 365 days of the year with their **saints' names**, the twelve [[motif:zodiac-signs|signs of the zodiac]], and twelve medallions of the **Labours of the Months** — agricultural and seasonal scenes (the present paintings are a 19th-century medallion cycle after the Czech painter Josef Mánes, the original now in a museum). At the very center are the arms of Prague's Old Town. Gilded figures of a chronicler, an angel, an astronomer and a philosopher flank the dial.

## Significance
Where the upper dial maps the *sky*, the calendar dial maps the *year* as lived on Earth: the liturgical round of feast days bound to the turning of the zodiac and the rhythm of the fields. Together the two faces present the medieval cosmos as one geared whole, sacred time and star-time on a single wall.

## For artists & game designers
The Labours-of-the-Months ring is a compact visual calendar of seasonal activity — useful shorthand for a farming or festival cycle in a game year.

title: The astronomical dial of the Prague Orloj
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Mikuláš of Kadaň & Jan Šindel (1410); later restorers; photograph by Godot13
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Godot13). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The astronomical dial of the Prague Orloj, the oldest astronomical clock still in operation, mounted on the south wall of the Old Town Hall and first installed in 1410.

## Iconography
The dial is a working **astrolabe** projected onto the clock face. A fixed background shows the Earth at the center, the blue sky, and the orange band of night; a rotating golden [[motif:zodiac-ring|zodiac ring]] carries the ecliptic. Three hands track the **Sun**, the **Moon** (a sphere that turns to show its phase), and the stars. Concentric scales give the time in several systems at once — Central European, Old Bohemian (counted from sunset), Babylonian (unequal day-hours), and sidereal star-time.

## Significance
The Orloj is the supreme survival of the medieval conviction that the cosmos is a [[motif:celestial-mechanism|machine]] and that machine can be re-built in brass. Its design is credited to the astronomer Jan Šindel with the clockmaker Mikuláš of Kadaň; the figures of Death and the procession of the Apostles were added later. It fuses astronomy, astrology and memento-mori theatre on a single face.

## For artists & game designers
A single dial that reports four different time-systems at once is a beautiful model for an in-world clock that means different things to different cultures sharing one city.

title: Detail of the Strasbourg Cathedral astronomical clock
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Jean-Baptiste Schwilgué (1842 mechanism); photograph by Tangopaso
summary:
Detail of the celebrated astronomical clock in the south transept of Strasbourg Cathedral — the third clock on the site, the mechanism rebuilt by Jean-Baptiste Schwilgué and completed in 1842.

## Iconography
The towering painted Renaissance case (its decoration from the 1571–74 clock) frames a stack of indicators: a perpetual ecclesiastical **calendar** computing the movable feasts, dials for the **planets** in their geocentric order, a globe of the Moon's phases, and a small orrery. Carved and gilded **automata** perform — the Ages of Man pass before Death who strikes the hours, a cock crows, and at noon the Apostles process before Christ.

## Significance
Strasbourg's clock is the grand baroque heir to the cathedral-clock tradition: a single machine made to compute the date of Easter forever, to model the heavens, and to preach the brevity of life. Schwilgué's 1842 *comput ecclésiastique* is a famous early mechanical calculator. The clock fascinated visitors as proof that the universe could be wound up and made to run.

## For artists & game designers
A machine whose whole purpose is to compute one shifting date (Easter) forever is a wonderful quest object — a device that answers a question no human can hold in their head.

citations.text: H. Bach & J.-P. Rieb, *Les Trois Horloges Astronomiques de la Cathédrale de Strasbourg* (1992).

title: The torquetum of Regiomontanus (engraving)
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: After Johannes Regiomontanus / Petrus Apianus tradition; engraving reproduced via DPLA
summary:
An engraving of the **torquetum**, a medieval and Renaissance observing instrument here associated with the astronomer Regiomontanus.

## Iconography
The torquetum is built as a stack of **inclined graduated discs**, each plane representing one of the great coordinate systems of the sky — the **horizon**, the **celestial equator**, and the **ecliptic** — hinged so that an observer can pass a measured position from one system to another by mechanical reading rather than calculation. A plumb-line and sighting alidades complete it. The engraving labels the parts in the manner of a Renaissance instrument-book.

## Significance
The torquetum is a three-dimensional model of the heavens' geometry: it physically embodies the transformations between ecliptic, equatorial and horizon coordinates that astronomers otherwise did by spherical trigonometry. Described by Bernard of Verdun and Peter Apian's *Astronomicum Caesareum*, it stands between the [[motif:astrolabe-dial|astrolabe]] and the great clocks as a tool that *computes the sky's geometry* by turning brass.

## For artists & game designers
An instrument whose nested tilted rings literally are the planes of the sky is gorgeous, legible machinery — ideal for an astronomer-character's desk or a puzzle of 'align the rings'.

citations.text: E. Zinner, *Regiomontanus: His Life and Work* (trans. 1990).

title: Zodiac and dial of the Torre dell'Orologio, Venice
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Gian Paolo & Gian Carlo Rainieri (1493–99); photograph by Zairon
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Zairon). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The great enamelled clock face of the Torre dell'Orologio above the entrance to the Merceria on the Piazza San Marco, built 1493–99 by Gian Paolo and Gian Carlo Rainieri.

## Iconography
A disc of deep **blue and gold** carries an outer ring of 24 Roman hour-numerals, an inner ring of the twelve [[motif:zodiac-signs|zodiac signs]], and at the center the **Earth** with the **Sun** riding a golden pointer around it. The phase of the **Moon** is shown by a revolving half-gilt sphere. Above the dial stand a winged Lion of St Mark and, on the terrace, the two bronze **Moors** who strike the bell.

## Significance
The Venetian clock fused civic pride, navigation and astrology: a merchant republic announcing that it kept time by the same stars its ships sailed under. The zodiacal dial let any passer-by read the Sun's place in the ecliptic — astrology as public utility. The bronze Mori striking the hours became one of Venice's signature spectacles.

## For artists & game designers
The blue-and-gold celestial dial is an instantly readable shorthand for 'a city that measures itself against the heavens' — strong establishing-shot iconography.

citations.text: G. Brusa, *L'Orologio della Torre di San Marco* (1999).

title: The astronomical clock of Wells Cathedral
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Medieval (c. 1390, dial); photograph by Tilman2007
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Tilman2007). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The astronomical clock of Wells Cathedral, one of the oldest surviving clock faces in the world, the dial dating to around 1390.

## Iconography
The great painted dial presents an explicitly **geocentric** universe. The Earth sits at the center; the Sun-hand and a separate ball showing the **phases of the Moon** travel around a 24-hour ring numbered twice (I–XII, I–XII). An inner dial counts the **minutes**, and another shows the age of the Moon in days. Above the dial, on the quarters, jousting knights spin in a tournament while a figure called **Jack Blandifers** kicks bells with his heels.

## Significance
Wells embodies the late-medieval clock as a working model of the [[motif:ptolemaic-universe|Ptolemaic cosmos]] — the heavens turning about a fixed Earth, made visible and audible to a congregation. The original mechanism survives (now in the Science Museum, London) and is among the earliest geared clocks anywhere; the dial here is a faithful replacement of the medieval face.

## For artists & game designers
The combat automaton — knights endlessly jousting on the hour — is a tidy idea: a clock whose moving parts are a frozen story replayed forever.

title: The Hampton Court astronomical (zodiac) clock
work: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: Nicholas Oursian (1540, for Henry VIII); photograph by Mike Cattell
rights: CC BY 2.0 (photo: Mike Cattell). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The astronomical clock in the Anne Boleyn Gateway of Hampton Court Palace, made in 1540 by Nicholas Oursian for Henry VIII.

## Iconography
The great **blue-and-gold dial** shows, on a fixed Earth at the center, the **Sun** travelling through the twelve [[motif:zodiac-signs|zodiac signs]] and the 365 days of the year, the **phase and age of the Moon**, and — uniquely useful to a riverside court — the time of **high water at London Bridge**, which governed when one could travel to the city by barge. Its scheme is frankly **pre-Copernican**, the Sun circling the Earth.

## Significance
The Hampton Court clock is a rare survival of a Tudor astronomical dial in situ, a court instrument that read at a glance both the heavens and the tides. As a practical-astrology object it shows the genre at its most worldly: the cosmos consulted not for prophecy but for catching the next boat.

## For artists & game designers
A celestial clock whose killer feature is a tide-table is a lovely reminder that 'cosmic' instruments earned their keep with mundane utility — good texture for a grounded fantasy court.

title: Geocentric Universe of the Spheres, Apian's *Cosmographia*
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Petrus Apianus (Peter Apian)
summary:
A diagram of the geocentric cosmos from Peter Apian's *Cosmographia*, the immensely popular Renaissance handbook of astronomy and geography.

## Iconography
The earth, with its concentric shells of water, air, and fire, sits at the centre. Around it run the spheres of the seven planets (Moon, Mercury, Venus, Sun, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn), then the sphere of the fixed stars marked with the zodiac, the crystalline heavens, the *primum mobile*, and the outermost **Empyrean** of God and the elect.

## Significance
Apian's *Cosmographia* (first ed. 1524), later revised by Gemma Frisius, codified the Ptolemaic [[motif:celestial spheres|sphere model]] for generations of students and was famous for its movable paper *volvelles*. The nested-spheres image is the standard Renaissance picture of the universe just before the Copernican turn — a finite, ordered, God-crowned cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'orrery of nested spheres' — a clean template for a star-map UI or a layered cosmology screen.

citations.text: S. K. Heninger, *The Cosmographical Glass: Renaissance Diagrams of the Universe* (1977).

title: Cover and volvelle apparatus, Apian's *Cosmographia* (Cosmograph.)
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Petrus Apianus & Gemma Frisius
summary:
A page from an edition of Apian's *Cosmographia* (here in the Latin *Cosmograph.*, with Gemma Frisius), the cosmographic handbook celebrated for its working paper instruments.

## Iconography
The leaf presents the cosmographic apparatus of the book — its framed title and the rotating-disc *volvelles* by which the reader computes positions of sun, moon, and stars, finds latitudes, and tells time. The printed circles are designed to be cut and pinned so the diagram becomes a literal calculating machine.

## Significance
The [[motif:volvelle|volvelle]] — a paper instrument of nested rotating discs — was a Renaissance speciality, and Apian's *Cosmographia* is its most famous showcase. These movable diagrams turn cosmology into computation, embodying the period's conviction that the heavens are governed by a knowable, calculable order. Gemma Frisius's revisions made the work a European standard for decades.

## For artists & game designers
A paper-machine aesthetic: pinned rotating dials that turn a static diagram into an interactive astronomical computer.

citations.text: Peter Apian, *Cosmographia*, rev. Gemma Frisius (16th c.).
Suzanne Karr Schmidt, *Interactive and Sculptural Printmaking in the Renaissance* (2017).

title: Byrhtferth's Diagram (the harmony of the months, elements, and seasons)
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Byrhtferth of Ramsey
summary:
A version of Byrhtferth's diagram, the great computistic schema correlating the four elements, four cardinal directions, four seasons, solstices and equinoxes, ages of man, and the letters of Adam's name.

## Iconography
The page is organized as nested lozenges and circles. At the centre stand the four elements — fire, air, water, earth — each tied to its qualities (hot, cold, moist, dry) and to a cardinal point, a season, a solstice or equinox, and a humour. The outer frame carries the twelve signs and the two solstice/equinox axes, binding the year and the world into one balanced tetradic figure.

## Significance
Byrhtferth of Ramsey (fl. c. 986–1016) composed his *Enchiridion* as a teaching manual for computus, the reckoning of time. The diagram is the visual heart of that work: an argument that the same fourfold harmony — the doctrine of [[motif:four elements|the four elements]] and their qualities — governs the cosmos, the year, and the human body. It is among the earliest and most influential English microcosm–macrocosm figures.

## For artists & game designers
A self-contained elemental-correspondence wheel: four elements × qualities × seasons × directions, ideal for an alchemy or affinity system.

citations.text: Byrhtferth, *Enchiridion*, ed. Peter S. Baker & Michael Lapidge (EETS, 1995).
Faith Wallis, 'Byrhtferth's Diagram,' in *Medieval Manuscripts and the Order of Knowledge*.

title: The Ptolemaic System, Cellarius *Harmonia Macrocosmica*
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Andreas Cellarius (engr. Jan van Loon)
summary:
The plate of the Ptolemaic (geocentric) system from Andreas Cellarius's *Harmonia Macrocosmica* (1660), the most lavish celestial atlas of the baroque age.

## Iconography
A central earth is encircled by the spheres of the Moon, the planets, and the Sun, ringed by the band of the **zodiac** and the firmament, all rendered in vivid colour and hung about with putti, astronomers, and decorative cartouches. The scheme is the classical Ptolemaic order of nested heavens, presented as a single harmonious machine.

## Significance
Cellarius's atlas displays the competing world-systems of the seventeenth century — Ptolemaic, Copernican, and Tychonic — as grand allegorical plates. The geocentric plate preserves the ancient picture of [[motif:celestial spheres|concentric heavens]] even as the Copernican revolution was overtaking it; the very title, *Harmonia Macrocosmica*, asserts the old conviction that the macrocosm is an ordered harmony. Engraved by Jan van Loon, published by Johannes Janssonius.

## For artists & game designers
A richly ornamented cosmology poster — the gold standard for a 'baroque star atlas' visual treatment.

citations.text: Robert van Gent, *The Celestial Atlas of Andreas Cellarius* (2006).

title: *Anima Mundi* / Integrae Naturae, Robert Fludd's macrocosm
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Robert Fludd
summary:
The famous *Integrae Naturae Speculum* / *Anima Mundi* engraving from Robert Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617), depicting the whole macrocosm as a chained order under God.

## Iconography
A naked female figure — **Natura**, the world-soul — stands upon the elemental and mineral earth, her head among the stars; one hand is chained to the cloud-borne hand of God above, the other holds a chain leading down to an ape (*ars*, art/the human) seated upon the globe. Concentric bands rise from minerals, plants, and animals through the planetary spheres and the angelic hierarchies to the divine name at the summit.

## Significance
Fludd (1574–1637), the great English Paracelsian and Rosicrucian apologist, makes this the frontispiece-statement of his system: nature is God's intermediary, art is the 'ape of nature,' and the cosmos is a single chained continuum from matter to deity — a Hermetic fusion of the [[motif:great chain of being|Great Chain]] with the [[motif:anima mundi|world-soul]]. It is among the most reproduced of all esoteric cosmological images.

## For artists & game designers
A vertical 'world-soul' axis linking God, Nature, and human Art by literal chains — a striking cosmology splash or progression spine.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin, *Robert Fludd: Hermetic Philosopher and Surveyor of Two Worlds* (1979).

title: The Great Chain of Being (*scala naturae*), Diego Valadés
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Diego Valadés (Didacus Valades)
summary:
A *scala naturae* or Great Chain of Being from Diego Valadés's *Rhetorica Christiana* (1579), arranging all creation as a graded ladder.

## Iconography
A vertical ladder or chain ascends from inanimate matter at the foot — minerals and the elements — upward through plants, animals, and humankind, then through the angelic orders, to God at the summit. Each rung is a degree of being and perfection; the whole is a single continuous hierarchy from the basest body to the divine.

## Significance
The Great Chain of Being is one of the most durable structures of Western cosmology: the idea, traced by A. O. Lovejoy, that the universe is a full, continuous, and graded hierarchy of forms. Rooted in Plato and Aristotle and elaborated by Neoplatonism and scholasticism, it dominated medieval and Renaissance thought about [[motif:hierarchy|cosmic order]]. Valadés, a Franciscan of mixed Spanish-Indigenous descent, printed this influential version to teach the order of creation.

## For artists & game designers
A literal vertical hierarchy of being — usable as a progression ladder, a moral alignment axis, or a cosmology of tiers.

citations.text: Arthur O. Lovejoy, *The Great Chain of Being* (1936).

title: The Cosmic Man, Hildegard of Bingen, *Liber Divinorum Operum*
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Hildegard of Bingen (school of)
summary:
The cosmic-man vision opening Hildegard of Bingen's *Liber Divinorum Operum* (Book of Divine Works), in which a human figure is set at the centre of the wheeling universe.

## Iconography
A standing human figure stands with arms outstretched at the centre of a series of concentric rings — bands of fire, ether, air, and water studded with stars, planets, and the heads of the winds blowing from the cosmic circumference. Above and behind, a vast red figure (Caritas, divine Love) embraces the whole wheel. The body of the inner man is dimensioned to the cosmos: its limbs reach to the elemental zones.

## Significance
The image is the classic medieval statement of [[motif:microcosm-macrocosm|microcosm and macrocosm]] — that the human being is a 'little world' whose proportions answer to the structure of the great one. Hildegard (1098–1179) frames it theologically: the cosmos and the body are bound together in God's love. The composition descends from late-antique and Carolingian cosmic diagrams and looks forward to the Renaissance Vitruvian and Fluddian cosmic men.

## For artists & game designers
A ready cosmology screen: concentric elemental rings with a player-figure at the hub, wind-heads marking the cardinal directions.

citations.text: Barbara Newman, *Sister of Wisdom: St. Hildegard's Theology of the Feminine* (1987).
Hildegard of Bingen, *Liber Divinorum Operum*, ed. A. Derolez & P. Dronke (CCCM 92, 1996).

title: Philosophy and the Seven Liberal Arts, *Hortus Deliciarum*
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Herrad of Landsberg
summary:
The wheel of Philosophy and the Seven Liberal Arts from the *Hortus Deliciarum* (Garden of Delights), compiled by Herrad of Landsberg for the nuns of Hohenburg.

## Iconography
A crowned **Philosophia** is enthroned at the centre of a great circle, with the springs of wisdom issuing from her. Around her, in seven arched compartments, sit the personified liberal arts — Grammar, Rhetoric, Dialectic, Music, Arithmetic, Geometry, Astronomy — each with her tools. Socrates and Plato write below; poets and magicians, inspired by 'unclean spirits,' are cast outside the holy circle.

## Significance
The diagram makes knowledge itself a *cosmos*: a concentric, hierarchical wheel descending from divine Wisdom through the ordered disciplines. Herrad (d. 1195) and her community built the *Hortus Deliciarum* as an encyclopaedic teaching manuscript; the twelfth-century original burned in 1870, but careful tracings preserve its imagery. It is a key witness to the medieval ordering of the [[motif:seven liberal arts|liberal arts]] as a structured whole.

## For artists & game designers
A radial skill-tree archetype: a central authority figure ringed by seven discipline 'classes,' each with iconographic kit.

citations.text: Rosalie Green et al., *Herrad of Hohenbourg: Hortus Deliciarum* (1979).
Fiona Griffiths, *The Garden of Delights: Reform and Renaissance for Women in the Twelfth Century* (2007).

title: Diagrammatic T-O World Map (after Isidore of Seville)
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: after Isidore of Seville
summary:
A twelfth-century *T-O* world map of the type devised by Isidore of Seville, schematizing the inhabited earth as a wheel divided by a T into three continents.

## Iconography
A circle (the *O*, the encircling Ocean) is split by a *T* of water: the horizontal bar is the Don–Nile axis, the vertical stroke the Mediterranean. The three resulting fields are labelled **Asia** (the top half), **Europe**, and **Africa**, traditionally assigned to the three sons of Noah — Shem, Japheth, and Ham. East (Asia, the site of Paradise) is placed at the top.

## Significance
Isidore of Seville (c. 560–636) set out this diagram in his *Etymologiae* and *De natura rerum*; copied for a millennium, the [[motif:t-o map|T-O scheme]] is less a geography than a cosmological *rota* — a wheel-figure asserting that the world's structure is rational, tripartite, and divinely ordered. It belongs to the same diagrammatic family as the elemental and seasonal *rotae*.

## For artists & game designers
The minimal, instantly legible 'three-realm wheel' makes an excellent stylized world map or faction emblem.

citations.text: Isidore of Seville, *Etymologiae*, Book XIV; *De natura rerum*.
Evelyn Edson, *Mapping Time and Space: How Medieval Mapmakers Viewed Their World* (1997).

title: Cosmological folio from Lambert's *Liber Floridus*
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Lambert of Saint-Omer
summary:
A folio from the *Liber Floridus* (Book of Flowers), the encyclopaedic compilation assembled by Lambert, canon of Saint-Omer, around 1120.

## Iconography
The page shows the author-scribe at work amid the diagrammatic apparatus of his book — the circular figures, spheres, and *rotae* through which the *Liber Floridus* organizes the heavens, the elements, the winds, and the ages of the world. The manuscript is famous for its wheel-diagrams of the cosmos and its planetary and zodiacal schemes.

## Significance
Lambert's *Liber Floridus* is one of the great medieval visual encyclopaedias, gathering Isidore, Bede, and apocalyptic and computistic lore into an interlocking set of diagrams. Its cosmological *rotae* — concentric rings of spheres, elements, and winds — transmit the [[motif:microcosm-macrocosm|microcosm–macrocosm]] worldview to the high Middle Ages and influenced later encyclopaedic illumination. The autograph survives at Ghent University Library.

## For artists & game designers
A model 'wizard's compendium' page: a scribe surrounded by the spinning diagrams of his own cosmology.

citations.text: Albert Derolez, *The Autograph Manuscript of the Liber Floridus* (1998).
Lambert of Saint-Omer, *Liber Floridus* (Ghent UB MS 92).

title: *Arbor Scientiae* (Tree of Knowledge), Ramon Llull
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: after Ramon Llull
summary:
The *Arbor Scientiae* (Tree of Knowledge / Tree of Science), Ramon Llull's vast botanical diagram of all the sciences, in an early-printed form.

## Iconography
A single tree carries the whole of knowledge: its roots are labelled with the nine 'divine dignities' and Llullian principles (goodness, greatness, eternity, power, wisdom…); its successive trunks and branches grow into the trees of the elements, the vegetal, the sensual, the imaginal, the human, the moral, the celestial, the angelic, the eternal, and the divine. Each level bears its leaves, flowers, and fruits of sub-topics.

## Significance
Ramon Llull (c. 1232–1316) devised the *Arbor Scientiae* (1295–96) as a more accessible presentation of his *Ars* — a combinatorial, encyclopaedic system meant to derive all truths from a few divine attributes. The tree is at once a cosmology and an epistemology: the ladder of being (elemental → angelic → divine) doubles as the ladder of the sciences. Llull's diagrams shaped later Renaissance combinatorics and the art of memory.

## For artists & game designers
A literal tech/knowledge tree, rooted in 'principles' and branching into tiers of being — directly mappable to a skill or research system.

citations.text: Anthony Bonner, *The Art and Logic of Ramon Llull: A User's Guide* (2007).

title: Llullian Combinatorial Wheel (figure of the *Ars Magna*)
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: modern recreation after Ramon Llull
summary:
A clean modern recreation of one of the rotating combinatorial figures of Ramon Llull's *Ars Magna* (Great Art), the volvelle-wheel that generates combinations of fundamental principles.

## Iconography
Concentric lettered circles (the letters B–K standing for the divine dignities and relational principles) can be rotated against one another so that every term is paired with every other. The diagram is a working device: by turning the inner wheel one reads off all the binary (and higher) combinations of Llull's basic concepts.

## Significance
Llull's *Ars* (from c. 1274; the *Ars Magna* / *Ars Generalis Ultima*, 1305–08) sought a universal method for demonstrating truths by mechanically combining a small alphabet of principles. Its [[motif:volvelle|rotating wheels]] are ancestors of the art of memory, of Bruno's and Kircher's combinatorial schemes, and — by a celebrated line through Leibniz — of formal logic and computation. This image is a present-day redrawing (CC BY-SA), not a historical manuscript.

## For artists & game designers
A functioning crafting/spell-combination dial: rotate lettered rings to enumerate every pairing of base 'principles.'

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *The Art of Memory* (1966), ch. on Lull.

title: Schematic of the Ptolemaic Geocentric System
work: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: Anonymous (modern schematic)
summary:
A simplified labelled schematic of the Ptolemaic geocentric system, showing the canonical order of the planetary spheres around a central earth.

## Iconography
Concentric rings radiate from the central earth outward through the spheres of the Moon, Mercury, Venus, Sun, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn, to the sphere of the fixed stars (the zodiac) and the *primum mobile*. The diagram strips the cosmos to its bare geometric structure — the nested-spheres order codified in Ptolemy's *Almagest*.

## Significance
The geocentric model of [[motif:celestial spheres|concentric heavens]] set out by Claudius Ptolemy (2nd c. CE) governed Western and Islamic astronomy for some 1,400 years and underlay all medieval and Renaissance cosmological diagrams in this collection. This clean modern redrawing makes the planetary ordering — the backbone of astrological correspondence — immediately legible; it is a present-day schematic, not a historical print.

## For artists & game designers
The minimal reference diagram of planetary order — handy as a base layer for any astrological or cosmological UI.

citations.text: Olaf Pedersen, *A Survey of the Almagest* (1974).

title: Integrae Naturae Speculum Artisque Imago (Fludd's Great Chain)
work: Cosmological Diagrams
creator: Robert Fludd (engr. Matthäus Merian)
summary:
The most celebrated single image in [[creator:robert-fludd]]'s *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia* (Oppenheim, 1617; engraved by Matthäus Merian, who also cut [[work:atalanta_fugiens]]). Its title — *Integrae Naturae Speculum Artisque Imago* — means "The Mirror of the Whole of Nature and the Image of Art."

## Iconography
At the top right, the **hand of God** emerges from cloud and the Tetragrammaton, holding a chain. The chain descends to a nude woman — **Natura**, the *anima mundi* or World-Soul — who stands upon the elemental earth, one foot in water, a sun on her breast and a moon on her belly. From *her* left hand a second chain runs down to an **ape** seated on the globe: *Ars*, Art (human craft, including alchemy), which imitates Nature as Nature imitates God. Around them are arrayed, in concentric rings, the realms of creation: the elements, the mineral, vegetable, and animal kingdoms, the mechanical arts, the liberal arts, the planetary spheres, and the angelic hierarchies.

## Significance
The plate is the definitive visualisation of the **Great Chain of Being** and the Hermetic axiom *as above, so below*: divine power is transmitted, link by link, from God through the World-Soul to nature and finally to human art. It encapsulates Fludd's entire programme — a cosmos legible as a hierarchy of correspondences ([[tradition:hermetic]]) rather than as Kepler's mathematics, a disagreement that became one of the defining methodological quarrels of the seventeenth century.

## For artists & game designers
A complete cosmological map in one frame — divine hand, chained World-Soul, ape of Art, nested spheres of elements, planets, and angels. Unmatched reference for a tech/magic tree, a cosmology screen, a "chain of being" progression, or an arcane wall-mural.

citations.text: Robert Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia*, vol. I (Oppenheim: de Bry, 1617), frontispiece.
J. Godwin, *Robert Fludd: Hermetic Philosopher and Surveyor of Two Worlds* (Thames & Hudson, 1979).

title: The Divine Monochord (Robert Fludd)
work: Cosmological Diagrams
creator: Robert Fludd
summary:
**Robert Fludd's** 'Divine Monochord' (*Monochordum mundi*), from the *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617) — the universe drawn as a single tuned string.

## Iconography
A monochord stretches from earth at the base to the empyrean at the top, its bridge tuned by the **hand of God** from a cloud. Along the string the elements, planets, and angelic spheres are marked off as musical intervals (diapason, diapente, diatessaron), so the whole cosmos sounds as one octave.

## Significance
The purest statement of the **music of the spheres** — the cosmos ordered by harmonic proportion, the macrocosm a chord. It belongs to Fludd's quarrel with Kepler over whether the universe is read by [[tradition:hermetic|symbolic harmony]] or mathematics, and pairs with Fludd's [[work:cosmology|Great Chain]] already in this archive.

## For artists & game designers
Cosmos-as-instrument — a vivid metaphor for a music-magic system or a harmony-of-the-spheres UI.

citations.text: Robert Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia*, vol. I (Oppenheim, 1617).
J. Godwin, *Harmonies of Heaven and Earth* (1987).

title: Ars moriendi (block-book woodcut)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Anonymous (printed by Conrad Kachelofen)
summary:
A woodcut from the *Ars moriendi* ("The Art of Dying"), the immensely popular fifteenth-century manual on the good death, here in an edition printed by Conrad Kachelofen.

## Iconography
A dying man lies on his bed surrounded by the unseen combatants for his soul: demons crowd in with temptations — to despair, impatience, pride, avarice and loss of faith — while angels, saints and Christ offer consolation. The deathbed becomes the stage of a final spiritual contest.

## Significance
The *Ars moriendi*, circulating both as a blockbook and in typeset editions from the 1450s onward, taught the laity to die well by resisting the five temptations and embracing their consoling counter-images. It is a cornerstone of late-medieval death culture, complementing the danse macabre's communal *memento mori* with a private, dramatised psychomachia at the bedside, as studied by Mary Catharine O'Connor and others.

## For artists & game designers
Staging an invisible tug-of-war of demons and angels around a single vulnerable figure is a clean template for a "contest for the soul" encounter.

citations.text: Mary Catharine O'Connor, *The Art of Dying Well: The Development of the Ars Moriendi* (1942).
*Ars moriendi* (German lands, late 15th c., printed Conrad Kachelofen).

title: The Triumph of Death (Pieter Bruegel the Elder)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Pieter Bruegel the Elder
summary:
Pieter Bruegel the Elder's panoramic panel *The Triumph of Death* (c. 1562, Museo del Prado), a vast apocalyptic landscape in which armies of skeletons overwhelm the living.

## Iconography
Legions of the dead drive humanity toward a great coffin-trap; a skeleton on a pale horse mows the crowd with a scythe, bells toll, a king's treasure is plundered, lovers play on oblivious. Every social rank — cardinal, soldier, peasant, gambler — is engulfed in a blasted, fire-lit world.

## Significance
Bruegel fuses the medieval *Triumph of Death* and *danse macabre* into a single eschatological panorama, drawing on the Italian frescoes and on Netherlandish print culture. The work reads both as universal *memento mori* and, many scholars argue, as a coded response to the religious violence of the sixteenth-century Low Countries. It is the most ambitious painted version of the theme.

## For artists & game designers
Massing identical skeletons as a relentless army — rather than a single reaper — converts a moral emblem into a horror set-piece of overwhelming scale.

citations.text: Pilar Silva Maroto (ed.), *Bruegel: The Triumph of Death* (Prado, 2018).

title: The Triumph of Death (Camposanto, Pisa — fragment)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Buonamico Buffalmacco (attributed)
rights: Public domain (CC0, Rijksmuseum reproduction). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A fragment of the great *Trionfo della Morte* fresco in the Camposanto Monumentale at Pisa, generally attributed to Buonamico Buffalmacco and dated to around 1336–1341 — before the Black Death — and badly damaged by fire in 1944.

## Iconography
The cycle combines several mortuary themes: an elegant hunting party recoils before three open coffins of decaying corpses (the [[motif:three living and three dead|Three Living and the Three Dead]]); a scythe-bearing Death swoops over heaps of the dead; and beggars vainly implore Death to take them. This reproduction preserves a portion of the composition.

## Significance
The Pisan *Triumph of Death* is one of the earliest and most ambitious survivals of the theme and a touchstone for its iconography, influencing later treatments down to Bruegel. Its early date shows that the meditation on universal mortality predates the plague, though the epidemic intensified it. Scholars such as Millard Meiss read such frescoes as expressions of a penitential, death-haunted Tuscan piety.

## For artists & game designers
Stacking three death-motifs in one scene — the corpse-confrontation, the reaping figure, the unanswered plea — builds a single image that rewards close reading.

citations.text: Millard Meiss, *Painting in Florence and Siena after the Black Death* (1951).
Luciano Bellosi, *Buffalmacco e il Trionfo della Morte* (1974).

title: La grande danse macabre des hommes et des femmes
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Anonymous (popular print tradition)
date: 1862 reissue of an early-modern woodcut series
summary:
A page from *La grande danse macabre des hommes et des femmes*, a long-lived French popular-print version of the Dance of Death (this copy an 1862 reissue) that extends the procession to the female estates.

## Iconography
Woodcut figures of Death lead women of differing ranks — queen, abbess, bourgeoise, bride, nurse — alongside the men, each accompanied by verses. The *danse macabre des femmes* gives the genre its full social and gendered range.

## Significance
The Dance of Death of women, developed in the later fifteenth century (notably in editions following Marchant), added the estates of women to a tradition that had begun with men only. These cheap, repeatedly reprinted *bibliothèque bleue*-style chapbooks kept the medieval *memento mori* alive into the nineteenth century, long after elite art had moved on, as historians of popular print have shown.

## For artists & game designers
Extending an archetype-procession to a parallel cast (here, women's estates) is a low-cost way to double a roster while keeping one coherent visual system.

citations.text: *La danse macabre des femmes* (Paris, late 15th c.; many later reissues).
Ann Tukey Harrison (ed.), *The Danse Macabre of Women* (1994).

title: Death and the Maiden (Hans Baldung Grien)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Hans Baldung Grien
summary:
Hans Baldung Grien's panel *Death and the Maiden* (c. 1518–1520), one of several treatments by the artist of a young woman seized by a putrefying corpse.

## Iconography
A beautiful nude young woman, often with mirror or flowing hair, is grasped from behind by a decaying cadaver who pulls her toward the grave; she recoils in dread. Youth, sensuality and vanity are confronted directly by physical corruption.

## Significance
*Death and the Maiden* is a German Renaissance offshoot of the *danse macabre*, narrowing the universal dance to a single, erotically charged pairing of *Eros* and *Thanatos*. Baldung, a pupil of Dürer, made the theme his own, using the contrast of flawless flesh and rotting body to preach the transience of beauty — a gendered intensification of the *memento mori* that scholars such as Joseph Koerner have analysed at length.

## For artists & game designers
The beauty-and-corpse embrace compresses an entire mortality sermon into a single two-figure tableau charged with dread and desire.

citations.text: Joseph Leo Koerner, *The Moment of Self-Portraiture in German Renaissance Art* (1993).

title: The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (Dürer)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Albrecht Dürer's woodcut *The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse* from his *Apocalypse* series (c. 1498), the defining image of the riders of Revelation, with Death on the pale horse at the front.

## Iconography
Four riders — Conquest, War, Famine and, lowest and gauntest, Death astride a pale horse and brandishing nothing but a pitchfork — trample humanity, from emperor to commoner, beneath their hooves; the maw of Hell opens to swallow the fallen. The riders surge diagonally with terrifying momentum.

## Significance
Dürer's *Apocalypse*, published in 1498 amid pre-millennial anxiety, gave Revelation 6 its most powerful visual form and made the [[motif:death on a pale horse|pale-horse rider]] a permanent fixture of Western death-imagery. The series fused the eschatological horseman with the leveller of the *Triumph of Death*: emperors and peasants perish together. Its technical brilliance also marked the woodcut's arrival as a vehicle for ambitious art.

## For artists & game designers
Giving the most lethal rider the meanest weapon — a farm pitchfork, not a sword — makes Death's inevitability feel casual and therefore more chilling.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: Death with the Scythe (allegorical engraving, Pufendorf title-page)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Anonymous engraver (Rijksmuseum impression)
rights: Public domain (CC0, Rijksmuseum). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An allegorical engraved title-page (1732) for an edition of Samuel von Pufendorf's *Introduction à l'histoire générale et politique de l'univers*, in which Death appears as a skeleton with scythe beside the muse of history, Clio.

## Iconography
A skeletal figure of Death wields the great scythe, the agricultural reaping-tool that became its standard attribute, set in an allegorical assembly with the personified muse who records the deeds and falls of nations. Time and mortality frame the writing of history.

## Significance
The skeleton with the scythe — and often an hourglass — is the fully crystallised early-modern personification of Death, the "Grim Reaper," descended from the reaping Death of the *Triumph* and the *danse macabre*. Its appearance on a history book's frontispiece shows how thoroughly the *memento mori* had entered the visual grammar of learned culture, reminding readers that empires too are mown down.

## For artists & game designers
The scythe reads instantly as "Death" even in silhouette — the single most recognisable attribute for personifying mortality.

citations.text: Samuel von Pufendorf, *Introduction à l'histoire générale et politique de l'univers* (1732 ed.).

title: Knight, Death and the Devil (Dürer)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Albrecht Dürer
rights: Public domain (CC0, Metropolitan Museum). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Albrecht Dürer's master engraving *Ritter, Tod und Teufel* (Knight, Death and the Devil, 1513), one of his three *Meisterstiche*, in which a mounted knight rides resolutely past Death and a swinish devil.

## Iconography
A Christian knight in full armour advances through a gloomy ravine. Death, a crowned, snake-wreathed corpse on a pale nag, raises an hourglass to remind him his time is short; a horned, hog-snouted devil shambles behind. The knight neither flinches nor looks aside, fixed on the distant fortress.

## Significance
Usually read in light of Erasmus's *Enchiridion militis Christiani* (the "Handbook of the Christian Soldier"), the print shows steadfast faith riding unmoved through the terrors of death and temptation. The hourglass-bearing corpse is a quintessential *memento mori*, but here the lesson is turned to courage rather than despair. Panofsky's reading of the engraving remains classic.

## For artists & game designers
Death as a heckler holding up an hourglass — present but powerless against a resolute hero — flips the usual dynamic and characterises the protagonist through what he ignores.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: The Bishop and the Squire (Danse Macabre, Marchant tradition)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: after Guyot Marchant (anonymous woodcutter)
summary:
A *Danse Macabre* woodcut in the tradition of Guyot Marchant's Parisian editions (first printed 1485), reproducing the pairing of the Bishop and the Squire; this plate appears in Johan Huizinga's *The Waning of the Middle Ages*.

## Iconography
Two cavorting corpses lead a mitred bishop and a young squire into the dance. Each living figure is partnered by a lively skeleton, and a verse exchange (in the original) has Death address the victim and the victim lament his fate.

## Significance
Marchant's 1485 *Danse Macabre*, drawn from the lost mural painted around 1424–25 in the cemetery of the Saints-Innocents in Paris, was the first printed edition of the theme and spread it across Europe. The procession descends the social ladder, insisting that office and youth alike are powerless before death — the central lesson Huizinga made famous in his account of late-medieval mortuary culture.

## For artists & game designers
Pairing each victim with an animated, almost gleeful corpse turns a grim subject into a darkly comic dance — tone as much as theme.

citations.text: Johan Huizinga, *The Waning of the Middle Ages* (1919; Eng. trans. 1924).

title: The Expulsion from Paradise (Holbein, Dance of Death series)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Hans Holbein the Younger (designer); Hans Lützelburger (cutter)
summary:
An opening woodcut from Hans Holbein the Younger's *Les simulachres & historiees faces de la mort* (the "Dance of Death"), designed in Basel around 1526 and first published at Lyon in 1538, here showing the Expulsion from Paradise that brings death into the world.

## Iconography
An angel drives Adam and Eve from Eden while a skeletal figure of Death, already accompanying them, plays a stringed instrument — situating the entire series as the consequence of the Fall. The German caption *Vßtribung Ade Eue* names the scene.

## Significance
Holbein's miniature woodcuts, cut by Hans Lützelburger, transformed the older mural *Danse Macabre* into a portable book in which Death seizes each estate — pope, emperor, merchant, ploughman — at its daily business. The series became the most influential treatment of the theme; scholars from Werner Gundersheimer to the editors of the Dover facsimile trace its diffusion across confessional Europe. Beginning with the Fall frames death as universal punishment rather than mere chance.

## For artists & game designers
Opening a death-themed sequence with origin-of-death imagery, rather than a generic skeleton, gives the whole set a narrative spine.

citations.text: Hans Holbein the Younger, *Les simulachres & historiees faces de la mort* (Lyon, 1538).
Werner L. Gundersheimer (ed.), *The Dance of Death by Hans Holbein the Younger* (1971).

title: Death and the Knight (Holbein, Dance of Death)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: after Hans Holbein the Younger
summary:
A plate from Holbein's *Dance of Death* in which Death overcomes an armoured knight, one of the social estates seized in turn throughout the series.

## Iconography
A skeleton attacks a fully armoured nobleman, running him through despite his plate and weapons; the soldier's martial strength is shown to be useless against the universal leveller. The composition continues the book's procession through every rank of society.

## Significance
The *Danse Macabre* asserts that death erases the hierarchy of estates — emperor, bishop, knight, peasant fall alike. The knight's plate, the ultimate sign of worldly power and protection, is pierced as easily as a beggar's rags, dramatising the medieval commonplace [[motif:memento mori|*memento mori*]]. Holbein's individualised, action-filled scenes broke from the static earlier mural tradition and fixed the leveller motif in the European imagination.

## For artists & game designers
Defeating the most heavily armoured archetype with the cheapest enemy is a compact way to signal that ordinary rules no longer apply.

citations.text: Hans Holbein the Younger, *The Dance of Death* (Lyon, 1538).
James M. Clark, *The Dance of Death in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance* (1950).

title: The Lübeck Totentanz (Marienkirche Dance of Death)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: after Bernt Notke (1701 copy by Anton Wortmann)
summary:
A panoramic record of the *Totentanz* (Dance of Death) once in the Marienkirche at Lübeck, originally painted by Bernt Notke in 1463 and known through a 1701 repainting by Anton Wortmann; the medieval original was destroyed in 1942.

## Iconography
A long frieze shows skeletal figures of Death leading an alternating chain of the living — pope, emperor, cardinal, king, down to the peasant and the infant — each clasped by a corpse in a grim dance. Verse dialogues once accompanied each pairing.

## Significance
The Lübeck mural was among the most celebrated north-German *Totentänze* and a key witness to the genre that flourished after the Black Death. Its alternation of the powerful and the humble embodies death as the great leveller; the dancing dead are not the deceased themselves but Death personified, summoning each estate. The loss of Notke's original in the wartime bombing makes such copies the principal evidence, as art historians of the *Totentanz* tradition emphasise.

## For artists & game designers
A horizontal procession alternating finely dressed figures with identical corpses reads instantly as "everyone, no exceptions."

citations.text: Hartmut Freytag (ed.), *Der Totentanz der Marienkirche in Lübeck* (1993).
James M. Clark, *The Dance of Death in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance* (1950).

title: Doctor Schnabel von Rom (the Plague Doctor)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Paul Fürst (publisher); after 'I. Columbina'
summary:
*Der Doctor Schnabel von Rom* ("Doctor Beak of Rome"), an engraving published by Paul Fürst of Nuremberg around 1656, the most famous image of the beaked plague doctor.

## Iconography
A gowned physician is encased head to foot in a waxed leather costume with a long bird-like beak (packed with aromatics against miasma), gloves, hat and a pointing wand. A mocking macaronic verse surrounds him, deriding doctors who profit from the dead while doing little for the living.

## Significance
The costume responded to the miasma theory of contagion during the great seventeenth-century plagues, the beak filled with herbs to filter "corrupt air." The print is at once documentary and satirical, a grim joke on the impotence of medicine before pestilence — a recurring partner of the *memento mori*, since the plague was the great quickener of death-imagery from the danse macabre onward. Astrological and divine causes of pestilence were debated alongside such remedies.

## For artists & game designers
The beaked silhouette is one of the most instantly evocative period costumes in existence — pure mood with a built-in story of fear and quackery.

citations.text: Christine M. Boeckl, *Images of Plague and Pestilence* (2000).
