Paul Fürst, *Der Doctor Schnabel von Rom* (Nuremberg, c. 1656).

title: The Plague of Florence in 1348 (after Boccaccio)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Anonymous (Wellcome Collection impression)
rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An etching depicting *The Plague of Florence in 1348* as described in the introduction to Boccaccio's *Decameron*, from the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The streets and interiors of Florence are strewn with the dead and dying; the living flee, mourn or carry off corpses while the social order collapses under the Black Death. The scene visualises Boccaccio's harrowing account of the epidemic that frames his hundred tales.

## Significance
The Black Death of 1347–1351 killed perhaps a third of Europe and is the historical engine behind the whole macabre tradition — the danse macabre, the *Triumph of Death* and the transi tomb all intensify in its wake. Boccaccio's eyewitness preface became the canonical literary description, repeatedly illustrated. Contemporaries sought causes in divine wrath, corrupt air and adverse planetary conjunctions, binding pestilence to the astrology of disaster.

## For artists & game designers
A city scene of collapsing social order — not just individual death — conveys catastrophe at a scale that a single skeleton cannot.

citations.text: Christine M. Boeckl, *Images of Plague and Pestilence* (2000).

title: The Three Living and the Three Dead (Master of the Dresden Prayer Book)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Master of the Dresden Prayer Book (or workshop)
summary:
A Flemish manuscript illumination of *The Three Living and the Three Dead*, attributed to the Master of the Dresden Prayer Book or his workshop (active c. 1480–1515).

## Iconography
Three richly dressed young noblemen, often out hunting, come face to face with three corpses in varying stages of decay, who warn them: *"As you are, so once were we; as we are, so shall you be."* The living recoil at the mirror of their own future.

## Significance
This *exemplum*, current in Europe from the thirteenth century in poetry and wall-painting, is a direct ancestor of the danse macabre: it confronts youth, rank and vitality with their inevitable corruption. The encounter-of-three structure, frequently placed in Books of Hours beside the Office of the Dead, drives home the personal *memento mori*. The motif is documented across French, English and Italian art by scholars of medieval death imagery.

## For artists & game designers
The mirror-encounter — confronting the player-character with rotting versions of themselves — is a stark, economical way to deliver a mortality theme without exposition.

citations.text: Ashby Kinch, *Imago Mortis: Mediating Images of Death in Late Medieval Culture* (2013).
Paul Binski, *Medieval Death: Ritual and Representation* (1996).

title: Le Transi de René de Chalon (Ligier Richier)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Ligier Richier
summary:
Ligier Richier's *transi* (cadaver effigy) of René de Chalon, Prince of Orange, carved around 1545–1547 for the church of Saint-Étienne at Bar-le-Duc — one of the supreme *memento mori* sculptures.

## Iconography
In place of a serene recumbent effigy, the prince is shown as a partly decomposed, flayed standing corpse, skin hanging in tatters, who raises his left hand to offer his own withered heart heavenward. The figure is upright and almost defiant, gazing toward God.

## Significance
The *transi* or cadaver tomb, which flourished from the late fourteenth century, depicts the deceased not in idealised repose but in graphic decay, fusing aristocratic commemoration with the universal lesson of bodily corruption. Richier's masterpiece pushes the type to a sculptural extreme; Erwin Panofsky and Kathleen Cohen treat such monuments as the funerary counterpart of the danse macabre. The offered heart converts horror into an act of devotion.

## For artists & game designers
A standing, active corpse that performs a gesture of devotion is far more unsettling — and characterful — than a passive skeleton lying down.

citations.text: Kathleen Cohen, *Metamorphosis of a Death Symbol: The Transi Tomb in the Late Middle Ages and the Renaissance* (1973).

title: The Triumph of Death (Palazzo Sclafani / Palazzo Abatellis, Palermo)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Anonymous (Maestro del Trionfo della Morte)
summary:
A large detached fresco of *Il Trionfo della Morte* (the Triumph of Death), painted around 1446 for the courtyard of the Palazzo Sclafani in Palermo and now in the Galleria Regionale di Palazzo Abatellis.

## Iconography
A skeletal Death rides a gaunt, ghostly horse, loosing arrows into a company of the rich, young and powerful — courtiers, lovers, a pope and emperor — while the poor and infirm at the left, who beg for death, are pointedly spared. The composition is among the most powerful realisations of the theme.

## Significance
The *Triumph of Death*, a theme spread by Petrarch's *Trionfi* and the trauma of the Black Death, shows Death conquering all earthly estates at once rather than in sequence. The Palermo fresco's mounted archer became iconic; its modern fame was amplified when Picasso is said to have studied it. It belongs to the same fifteenth-century mortuary culture as the danse macabre and the transi tomb.

## For artists & game designers
A mounted skeletal archer who spares the suffering and strikes the fortunate inverts the usual logic of mercy — an unsettling, memorable rule.

citations.text: Alberto Tenenti, *Il senso della morte e l'amore della vita nel Rinascimento* (1957).
Joël Saugnieux, *Les danses macabres de France et d'Espagne* (1972).

title: Vanitas Still Life with Skull, Hourglass and Globe (Gijsbrechts)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Cornelis Norbertus Gijsbrechts
summary:
A *vanitas* still life by the Flemish painter Cornelis Norbertus Gijsbrechts, assembling a skull, hourglass, guttering candle, violin, globe, sheet music and playing cards on a draped table.

## Iconography
Each object is an emblem of transience: the skull and extinguished candle for death, the hourglass for fleeting time, the bubble-like instruments, music and cards for fragile worldly pleasure and chance. Together they preach the vanity of earthly things — *vanitas vanitatum*.

## Significance
The Dutch and Flemish *vanitas* of the seventeenth century translated the medieval *memento mori* into the language of the bourgeois still life, where the skull beside the hourglass distils centuries of death-imagery into a single contemplative arrangement. The genre is rooted in Ecclesiastes' "all is vanity" and studied as a meditation on mortality and the futility of accumulation. Gijsbrechts was a noted specialist of the type and of *trompe-l'œil*.

## For artists & game designers
The skull-plus-hourglass pairing is the most compact, universally legible death symbol in Western art — instant shorthand for "time is running out."

citations.text: Alberto Veca, *Vanitas: il simbolismo del tempo* (1981).
Ingvar Bergström, *Dutch Still-Life Painting in the Seventeenth Century* (1956).

title: The Wheel of Fortune (Carmina Burana manuscript)
work: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: Anonymous (Carmina Burana illuminator)
summary:
The celebrated *Rota Fortunae* (Wheel of Fortune) illumination from the *Carmina Burana* manuscript (c. 1230), illustrating the goddess Fortuna turning her wheel.

## Iconography
Four figures ride the rim, each labelled: *regnabo* ("I shall reign"), *regno* ("I reign"), *regnavi* ("I have reigned") and *sum sine regno* ("I am without a kingdom"). A crowned king rides the summit while another tumbles headlong, as blind Fortune spins all from glory to ruin.

## Significance
The Wheel of Fortune, given its enduring form by Boethius's *Consolation of Philosophy*, became the medieval emblem of worldly mutability and a close kin of the *memento mori*: like Death, Fortune levels the proud and reminds the mighty of their fall. The *Carmina Burana* image is the genre's most reproduced witness, opening a manuscript whose verses lament fate's caprice — the same fatalism that underlies the danse macabre.

## For artists & game designers
A rotating wheel whose four stations spell rise, rule, fall and ruin is a ready-made visual loop for systems about fortune, status, or impermanence.

citations.text: Howard R. Patch, *The Goddess Fortuna in Mediaeval Literature* (1927).

title: Abraxas
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: After J. Collin de Plancy
summary:
*Abraxas* as engraved for the *Dictionnaire Infernal* — the cock-headed, serpent-legged power of late-antique Gnosticism re-presented among the demons.

## Iconography
Abraxas is the classic *anguipede*: a warrior with the head of a rooster, a cuirassed human torso, and two serpents for legs, bearing a shield and a whip or flail. The form is copied from ancient [[motif:abraxas|magical gems]] (the so-called Abraxas stones).

## Significance
In the system of the Gnostic teacher Basilides (2nd c. Alexandria), *Abraxas* was the supreme Archon whose name totals 365 by Greek isopsephy — one for each of the 365 heavens. Engraved on amulets as a protective deity, he was later absorbed into demonological and occult literature, and C. G. Jung invoked him in the *Seven Sermons to the Dead* as a god uniting opposites. Collin de Plancy lists him among the infernal powers.

## For artists & game designers
The rooster-headed, snake-legged soldier is one of the oldest composite-deity designs in Western magic — instantly evocative for amulet or boss art.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed.).
Irenaeus on Basilides; C. G. Jung, *Septem Sermones ad Mortuos* (1916).

title: Adramelech
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Adramelech*, grand chancellor of Hell, shown with a mule's body and peacock tail in the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Adramelech appears with a man's torso, a crowned bestial head, the lower body of a mule (or sometimes a peacock's fanned tail). The regalia mark his office as chancellor and president of the High Council of devils.

## Significance
Adramelech (Adrammelech) is named in 2 Kings 17 as a god of Sepharvaim to whom colonists 'burned their children in fire.' Demonologists such as those compiled by Collin de Plancy made him chancellor of the infernal order and superintendent of Satan's wardrobe. His association with [[motif:child sacrifice|child sacrifice]] links him to Moloch in the biblical roster of condemned deities.

## For artists & game designers
The crowned man-mule-peacock hybrid is an ornate courtly demon — cast him as a scheming infernal bureaucrat rather than a frontline fighter.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).

title: Amon (Aamon)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Amon*, a marquis of Hell who reconciles disputes, in the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Amon is depicted as a wolf-headed figure with a serpent's tail, sometimes vomiting flame. The composite follows the Goetia, which describes him appearing as a wolf with a serpent's tail breathing fire, or as a man with the teeth of a dog.

## Significance
Amon (Aamon), likely echoing the Egyptian *Amun* or the Carthaginian *Baal-hammon*, is the seventh spirit of the *Ars Goetia*, a Great Marquis commanding forty legions. He discerns past and future and [[motif:reconciler|procures love and reconciliation]] between friends and foes — one of the demons valued for social rather than martial powers.

## For artists & game designers
The wolf-head/serpent-tail combination is a clean two-animal hybrid; the 'reconciler' role suits a non-combat NPC who brokers truces.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
*The Lesser Key of Solomon: Ars Goetia*, spirit 7.

title: Andras
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Andras*, the owl-headed demon of discord, from the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Andras is shown with the head of an owl (or raven) on a winged angelic body, brandishing a sharp sword and riding upon a strong black wolf. The image follows the Goetia exactly, including the wolf mount and drawn blade.

## Significance
Andras is the sixty-third spirit of the *Ars Goetia*, a Great Marquis commanding thirty legions, who sows [[motif:discord|discord and slaughter]]. The grimoire issues a stark warning: he comes to kill the careless conjurer and his companions, making him one of the most dangerous spirits in the catalogue — a demon to be bound with great caution.

## For artists & game designers
The owl-headed swordsman on a black wolf is a sleek, aggressive design — ideal for a high-threat assassin demon with a 'kills the unwary' gimmick.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
*The Lesser Key of Solomon: Ars Goetia*, spirit 63.

title: Asmodeus
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Louis Le Breton's *Asmodeus*, the three-headed king of demons, from the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Asmodeus is depicted with three heads — a bull, a man, and a ram — webbed feet, a serpent's tail, and a lance, riding upon an infernal dragon. The composite heads correspond to the description in the *Ars Goetia*, where he breathes fire and rides a dragon while bearing a lance and banner.

## Significance
Asmodeus (Aeshma-daeva) enters Jewish tradition through the deuterocanonical *Book of Tobit*, where he slays Sarah's bridegrooms before being driven off by Tobias and the angel Raphael. In the *Testament of Solomon* he is bound to manufacture the Temple. He became the demon of [[motif:lust|lust]] and discord, and one of the seventy-two spirits ruling thirty-six legions. Le Breton synthesized these textual cues into a single iconic portrait.

## For artists & game designers
The tri-headed, lance-bearing dragon-rider is a maximalist demon-king design — combine bestial heads to signal his rank without text.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).

title: Astaroth
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Louis Le Breton's engraving of *Astaroth*, the Great Duke of Hell, for the 1863 edition of Collin de Plancy's *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Astaroth is shown as a nude, ugly figure riding an infernal dragon or wyvern, gripping a viper in his left hand. The image follows the literary description in which he appears mounted, holding a serpent, with a fetid breath the conjurer must guard against by holding a magical ring to the face.

## Significance
Astaroth derives from the ancient Near-Eastern goddess Astarte, demoted in Christian demonology to a male duke of the infernal hierarchy. He appears among the [[motif:infernal hierarchy|seventy-two spirits]] of the *Ars Goetia* and in the *Pseudomonarchia Daemonum* of Johann Weyer, where he answers questions on the past, present, and future and discourses on the fall of the rebel angels. Le Breton's plates fixed the modern visual vocabulary of named demons.

## For artists & game designers
The dragon-mounted duke is a ready boss archetype: a humanoid commander riding a serpentine beast, paired with a poison/breath hazard mechanic.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).

title: Bael (Baal)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Bael*, the first king of the *Ars Goetia*, rendered as a tripartite monster in the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Bael appears with three heads — a toad, a man, and a cat — set upon a body supported by spider-like legs. This precisely follows the Goetic description of a king who appears 'with three heads, the first like a toad, the second like a man, the third like a cat,' speaking with a hoarse voice.

## Significance
Bael descends from the Canaanite storm-god *Baal* (Ba'al, 'lord'), the great rival of Yahweh in the Hebrew Bible, demonised in Christian tradition. In the *Ars Goetia* he is the first of the seventy-two spirits, commanding sixty-six legions and granting [[motif:invisibility|invisibility]]. Le Breton's spider-legged composite became the canonical modern image of the demon.

## For artists & game designers
The toad-man-cat triad on spider legs is a striking 'wrongness' silhouette — assemble mismatched animal heads to make an unsettling first-tier boss.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
*The Lesser Key of Solomon: Ars Goetia*.

title: Beelzebub
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Beelzebub*, the 'Lord of the Flies' and a supreme chief of the infernal empire, from the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
The demon is rendered as a colossal winged fly, an explicit visual pun on the name *Baal-zebub*, 'lord of the flies.' The insect form distils the demonological tradition that Beelzebub presided over swarms of plague-bearing flies.

## Significance
Beelzebub originates as *Baal-Zebul* ('Baal the prince'), the Philistine god of Ekron in 2 Kings, deliberately mangled into *Baal-Zebub* by Hebrew scribes. In the Gospels he is named 'prince of the demons,' and later demonologists ranked him second only to Satan, even identifying him as the supreme ruler of Hell. He is one of the chief tempters in the [[motif:infernal hierarchy|classification of demons]] by sin.

## For artists & game designers
The giant-fly form is a vivid, instantly legible monster — pair with a swarm/plague mechanic that fragments into lesser flies.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).

title: Behemoth
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Behemoth*, the gluttonous demon-beast, from the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Behemoth is shown as an enormous elephant-headed demon with a swollen belly, standing upright on stout legs. The grotesque potbelly and beast-head encode his role as the demon of [[motif:gluttony|gluttony]] and brute appetite.

## Significance
Behemoth derives from the primordial land-monster of the Book of Job (40:15–24), counterpart to the sea-beast Leviathan. Christian demonology recast him as the demon presiding over gluttony and the pleasures of the belly, master of Hell's feasts and revels. He stands among the great mythic monsters that demonologists folded into the infernal court.

## For artists & game designers
A hulking, slow elephant-demon is an ideal heavy 'tank' enemy whose gluttony theme can drive a swallow/devour attack.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).

title: Belphegor
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's grotesque *Belphegor*, the demon of sloth and ingenious discoveries, enthroned on a privy in the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
The horned, bearded, claw-handed demon sits upon an open latrine — a deliberately scatological throne. The image visualises the medieval etymology connecting Belphegor with the Moabite *Baal-Peor*, a god whose rites were imagined to involve excrement.

## Significance
Belphegor (from Baal-Peor of Numbers 25) was reinterpreted in Christian demonology as the demon who tempts mankind with ingenious inventions and the promise of effortless wealth, and was paired with the deadly sin of [[motif:sloth|sloth]]. The legend that he was sent up from Hell to test whether married happiness existed on earth was popularised by Machiavelli's tale and later by Milton among the fallen powers.

## For artists & game designers
A comic-grotesque demon: rank, lazy, and bound to a filthy throne — perfect for a sin-themed bestiary slot that reads as repulsive rather than fearsome.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).

title: Berith (Beal)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Berith*, the soldier-duke who rides a red horse, from the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Berith is depicted as a soldier in red armour wearing a golden crown, mounted on a red horse. The martial dress and crimson colouring follow the Goetic description of a 'great and terrible duke' appearing as a red-clad soldier riding a red horse.

## Significance
Berith is the twenty-eighth spirit of the *Ars Goetia*, a Great Duke commanding twenty-six legions. He answers truly of past, present, and future, can [[motif:alchemy|turn all metals into gold]], and gives dignities — yet the grimoire warns he is a notorious liar who must not be trusted. His name echoes the Canaanite *Baal-Berith* of Judges 8–9.

## For artists & game designers
A crowned red knight on a red steed is a clean, heraldic boss; the alchemical gold-making power supplies a tempting but treacherous reward hook.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
*The Lesser Key of Solomon: Ars Goetia*, spirit 28; Judges 8:33.

title: Buer
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's celebrated *Buer*, the wheel-shaped Goetic president, from the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal* — one of the most recognisable demon images ever printed.

## Iconography
Buer is shown as a lion-faced head ringed by five radiating goat-like legs arranged like the spokes of a wheel, allowing him to roll in any direction. The bizarre rotational anatomy follows the Goetic note that he appears 'when the Sun is in Sagittarius.'

## Significance
Buer is the tenth spirit of the *Ars Goetia*, a Great President who teaches natural and moral philosophy, logic, and the virtues of herbs and plants, and who [[motif:healing|heals all distempers]] of man. He commands fifty legions. Le Breton's wheel-of-legs design has become an icon of demonological illustration, widely reproduced and parodied.

## For artists & game designers
The radially symmetric, rolling 'wheel beast' is a memorable enemy whose locomotion can drive unusual movement AI.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
*The Lesser Key of Solomon: Ars Goetia*, spirit 10.

title: The Devil (Le Diable), Tarot Trump XV
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Tarot World Project (after the Marseille pattern)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A detail of *Le Diable* (The Devil), trump XV of the Tarot de Marseille, holding the infernal torch.

## Iconography
The [[motif:devil|Devil]] of the Marseille pattern stands frontal with bat-wings, stag or goat horns, a torch in one hand, and clawed feet on a pedestal to which two small horned, tailed figures are chained by the neck. The downward torch and the willing captives encode bondage to material desire.

## Significance
In the standard Tarot de Marseille (codified in 18th-century France from earlier Italian *trionfi*), trump XV follows Temptation/Death and Tower in the sequence of the soul's trials. Esoteric tarotists from Éliéphas Lévi onward read the card as Baphomet, matter, and the bondage of the appetites; in divination it signals attachment, addiction, or the shadow. It is the tarot's principal demonic image.

## For artists & game designers
A compact, symbol-dense devil — wings, torch, horns, chained captives — that reads as 'bondage/temptation' at a glance for card or tile art.

citations.text: Éliphas Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1856).

title: Abigor (Eligos)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's mounted knight-demon, the war-counsellor of the *Ars Goetia*, from the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal* (the Commons file is titled *Abigor*; the figure embodies the warrior-duke type also borne by Eligos).

## Iconography
The demon appears as an armoured knight bearing a lance, banner, or sceptre and riding a war-horse — in some printings a skeletal or monstrous steed. The chivalric panoply marks a duke who commands the arts of war.

## Significance
The warrior-dukes of the Goetia (Eligos, spirit 15, and Abigor/Eligor in variant lists) foresee the outcome of wars, reveal hidden things, and [[motif:favour of lords|procure the favour of lords]] and the loyalty of soldiers. They typify the grimoire's military spirits, consulted for strategy and command — a reminder that Renaissance and early-modern magic served princely as well as scholarly ambitions.

## For artists & game designers
A lance-bearing demon knight on a war-horse is the quintessential cavalry boss; tie his powers to morale and recruitment mechanics.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
*The Lesser Key of Solomon: Ars Goetia*, spirit 15.

title: Seal of Botis (Goetic Sigil)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Anonymous (after the Lemegeton)
summary:
The *Seal of Botis*, one of the seventy-two demon sigils of the *Ars Goetia*, the abstract glyph by which a spirit is summoned and bound.

## Iconography
The seal is a compact emblem of intersecting lines, loops, small crosses, and points enclosed within or beside a circle. Each Goetic sigil is unique, a non-figurative 'signature' or [[motif:demon seal|character]] of its spirit, meant to be engraved (ideally on the proper metal) and worn or displayed during the operation.

## Significance
The *Ars Goetia*, first book of the *Lemegeton* (Lesser Key of Solomon, 17th c.), assigns to each of its seventy-two spirits a name, rank, legions, office, and such a seal. Botis is the seventeenth spirit, a president and earl who appears first as a viper, then as a horned man with a sword, reconciling friends and foes. S. L. MacGregor Mathers and Aleister Crowley's 1904 edition standardised the seals for the modern occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
These sigils are a ready alphabet of summoning glyphs — assign one per creature for instantly readable 'binding circle' UI.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia: The Lesser Key of Solomon the King* (1904).

title: Witches' Sabbath (The Great He-Goat)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Francisco Goya
summary:
Francisco Goya's *Witches' Sabbath* (*El aquelarre* / *The Great He-Goat*), one of the Black Paintings from the Quinta del Sordo, c. 1821–23.

## Iconography
A monstrous silhouetted [[motif:black goat|he-goat]] — the Devil presiding as goat — looms at the left before a huddled, grimacing crowd of witches gathered in the dark. The frieze-like mass of frightened, deformed faces conveys collective hysteria rather than orgiastic revelry.

## Significance
Goya returned repeatedly to the sabbath theme (an earlier 1798 *Aquelarre* and the *Caprichos*), drawing on Spanish witch-lore and the inquisitorial trials of Logroño. Painted directly on his villa walls and never meant for public view, the Black Painting transforms the folkloric sabbath into a bleak meditation on superstition, fanaticism, and the herd. It is the most famous post-Enlightenment image of the goat-devil and his coven.

## For artists & game designers
A study in mood over monster: the Devil is mostly shadow, the horror carried by the crowd's faces — useful for atmospheric cult scenes.

citations.text: Francisco Goya, *El aquelarre / The Great He-Goat* (Black Paintings, c. 1821–23), Museo del Prado.

title: The Pact with the Devil (Compendium Maleficarum)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Francesco Maria Guazzo
summary:
A woodcut from Francesco Maria Guazzo's *Compendium Maleficarum* (1608) depicting a stage in the witch's pact with the Devil.

## Iconography
The scene shows the demon and the initiate enacting the formal compact — the new witch renouncing the faith, being inscribed in the [[motif:book of death|devil's book]], or receiving the diabolical mark. The crude, forceful woodcut style suits the inquisitorial subject.

## Significance
Guazzo, an Ambrosian friar, compiled the *Compendium Maleficarum* as a practical handbook for witch-hunters, illustrated with a famous series of woodcuts cataloguing the eleven stages of the pact: the renunciation of baptism, the *osculum infame*, the new name, the surrender of a piece of clothing, the oath within the circle, enrolment in the book of death, and more. These images crystallised the early-modern theology of the demonic pact that underwrote the witch trials.

## For artists & game designers
A primary source for ritual-pact imagery — use the staged 'contract' beats as a cutscene template for a soul-bargain quest.

title: Satan Cast into Hell (Hell-mouth)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A print of *Satan thrown into Hell* at the Last Judgement, deploying the medieval [[motif:hellmouth|Hell-mouth]] motif (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
Hell is figured as the gaping, fanged maw of a vast beast — the jaws of Leviathan — into which Satan and the damned are hurled amid flames and tormenting demons. The animal mouth swallows sinners whole, its teeth framing the fiery interior.

## Significance
The Hell-mouth, derived from the Leviathan of Job and Isaiah's image of Sheol opening its jaws, became one of the most enduring inventions of medieval Christian art — from Anglo-Saxon manuscripts to the *Hortus Deliciarum*, doom paintings, and mystery-play stage machinery that belched smoke and sinners. It localised damnation as ingestion by a monster, a vivid pedagogical terror linked to the Last Judgement and the fall of the rebel angels.

## For artists & game designers
The ur-design for a 'gate to Hell' — a literal monstrous mouth as portal, ideal for a dungeon entrance or boss arena.

citations.text: Gary D. Schmidt, *The Iconography of the Mouth of Hell* (1995).

title: The Nightmare (Der Alp)
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Henry Fuseli (Johann Heinrich Füssli)
summary:
Henry Fuseli's *Nightmare* (here the 1810 variant, *Der Alp leaves the bed of two sleeping women*), the iconic Romantic image of the incubus demon.

## Iconography
A squat, ape-like [[motif:incubus|incubus]] crouches on the breast of a sleeping woman flung backward in abandon, while a blind-eyed spectral horse — the 'night-mare' — thrusts its head through the curtains. The demon's weight on the chest visualises the crushing oppression of sleep paralysis.

## Significance
Fuseli's first *Nightmare* (1781) was a sensation; he repeated the theme in several versions. The painting fuses folklore — the *Mahr*/*Alp* night-demon that sits on sleepers and the incubus that assaults women in their sleep — with a charged erotic and psychological subtext later prized by Freud (a print of it hung in his study). It remains the defining modern depiction of demonic nocturnal oppression.

## For artists & game designers
The template for a sleep/paralysis horror beat — a heavy demon perched on a victim's chest, paired with an uncanny animal omen.

citations.text: Henry Fuseli, *The Nightmare* (1781 and later versions).

title: Léonard, Grand Master of the Sabbath
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Léonard*, the great black goat who presides over the witches' sabbath, in the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Léonard appears as a three-horned goat with a fox-like or human face, standing upright. The three horns — one burning between the others — and the goat's body crystallise the inquisitorial stereotype of the devil worshipped at the [[motif:witches sabbath|sabbath]].

## Significance
In French witch-trial demonology Léonard (the 'Grand Master Léonard') was the inspector-general of black magic and demon of the sabbath, before whom witches performed the *osculum infame*, the obscene kiss. He embodies the early-modern construction of the goat-devil presiding over the nocturnal assembly, a figure elaborated by demonologists such as Pierre de Lancre. Le Breton fixed the visual type later echoed by Goya and Baphomet imagery.

## For artists & game designers
The upright black goat is the archetypal cult-leader devil — use for a sabbath-boss whose ritual buffs lesser witch enemies.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
Pierre de Lancre, *Tableau de l'inconstance des mauvais anges* (1612).

title: Lucifer
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Lucifer*, the fallen light-bringer and emperor of Hell, in the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Lucifer is rendered as a winged, horned demon, his angelic origin still legible in his form even as bat-wings and bestial features mark his fall. The image presents him as a ruler rather than a tormentor.

## Significance
Lucifer ('light-bearer') translates the Hebrew *Helel ben Shahar*, the 'morning star' of Isaiah 14 cast down for [[motif:pride|pride]]. Conflated with Satan by patristic writers and immortalised as the tragic rebel of Milton's *Paradise Lost*, he heads many demonological hierarchies as emperor of Hell, distinct in some schemes from Satan and Beelzebub. The Le Breton plate gave the literary archfiend a definite Victorian-era face.

## For artists & game designers
The ruined-angel design — beautiful proportions corrupted by bat-wings and horns — is the template for a sympathetic final antagonist.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).

title: The Devil of the Codex Gigas
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Herman the Recluse (attrib.)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. National Library of Sweden. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The famous full-page portrait of the Devil in the *Codex Gigas* ('Giant Book' / Devil's Bible), the largest surviving medieval manuscript.

## Iconography
The [[motif:devil|Devil]] squats frontally, crowned with two great horns, a green face with red eyes, a forked red tongue, clawed hands and feet, and an ermine loincloth. The crouching, symmetrical pose and bald cranium give him a uniquely heraldic, almost frog-like menace, set against an empty arched frame.

## Significance
Produced in a Bohemian Benedictine monastery in the early 13th century, the *Codex Gigas* contains the entire Vulgate plus Josephus, Isidore, exorcism formulae, and a medical compendium. Legend held that a monk facing death walled-up sold his soul to finish it in a single night, hence the devil portrait facing a page on confession and exorcism. It is the most reproduced of all medieval manuscript devils.

## For artists & game designers
A rare full-figure medieval devil with a distinctive ermine-and-horns costume — an authentic alternative to the generic red demon.

citations.text: *Codex Gigas* (early 13th c.), National Library of Sweden, MS A 148.

title: Moloch
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's crowned and horned *Moloch*, the demon-prince associated with child sacrifice, in the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Moloch is shown as a crowned, regal demon with horns, an aged bearded face, and clawed hands. The crown and bearing signal his rank as a great prince of Hell; the iconography draws on the Bible's account of a deity to whom children were 'passed through the fire.'

## Significance
Moloch (Molech) appears in Leviticus and Kings as an Ammonite god condemned for the sacrifice of children in the valley of Hinnom (Gehenna). Milton ranks him first among the fallen angels in *Paradise Lost*, 'horrid king besmeared with blood / Of human sacrifice.' In modern usage he became a byword for systems that devour the young, a reading amplified by [[motif:child sacrifice|the brazen-idol legend]].

## For artists & game designers
A somber, regal antagonist tied to a sacrificial-altar mechanic — Moloch works as a cult deity rather than a brawler.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
Leviticus 18:21; J. Milton, *Paradise Lost*, Book I.

title: A Scholar in His Study ('Faust')
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Rembrandt van Rijn
rights: CC0 / Public domain. The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Rembrandt's enigmatic etching long known as *Faust*, the supreme image of the scholar-magician confronted by a conjured vision.

## Iconography
A scholar in his cluttered study turns from his desk toward a window where a radiant disc has appeared, inscribed with a cabbalistic anagram (INRI / mystical letters) and pierced by a hand pointing to a mirror. The luminous apparition stands for the [[motif:magic apparition|spirit or revelation]] summoned by the magus.

## Significance
Though Rembrandt gave it no title, the print was associated with the Faust legend by the 18th century and chosen by Goethe to illustrate the first edition of his *Faust*. Scholars (e.g. H. van de Waal) read the glowing monogram as a meditation on divine versus diabolical knowledge — the very ambiguity at the heart of the pact narrative, where forbidden learning is bought at the price of the soul.

## For artists & game designers
A masterclass in depicting a summoning by light alone — the unseen spirit is implied by a glowing sigil-disc, not a monster.

citations.text: H. van de Waal, studies on Rembrandt's 'Faust' etching.

title: The Temptation of St Anthony
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Martin Schongauer
summary:
Martin Schongauer's celebrated engraving of *The Temptation of St Anthony*, in which a swarm of demons lifts and torments the desert hermit in mid-air.

## Iconography
St Anthony is borne aloft, clutching his book, while a ring of grotesque [[motif:hybrid monsters|hybrid demons]] — feathered, scaled, beaked, horned, and clawed — beat, bite, and drag at him. Each fiend is a virtuoso assemblage of animal parts, the whole forming a churning wheel of torment.

## Significance
The scene illustrates the Life of St Anthony by Athanasius, the foundational text of Christian eremitic struggle against demonic assault. Schongauer's print (c. 1470s) was so admired that the young Michelangelo copied it in paint. It became the template for centuries of demon-swarm imagery, from Grünewald's Isenheim Altarpiece to Callot, and exemplifies the late-medieval imagination of the devil as a multiplicity of monstrous forms.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive reference for designing a 'demon swarm' encounter — every monster is a unique kitbash of beast parts.

citations.text: Martin Schongauer, *The Temptation of St Anthony* (engraving, c. 1470–75).

title: Stolas
work: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: Louis Le Breton
summary:
Le Breton's *Stolas*, the crowned-owl prince of the *Ars Goetia*, from the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal*.

## Iconography
Stolas is shown as a great horned owl or long-legged crowned night-raven. The owl form follows the Goetic description of a powerful prince who first appears 'in the form of a night raven' before taking human shape.

## Significance
Stolas (Stolos) is the thirty-sixth spirit of the *Ars Goetia*, a Great Prince commanding twenty-six legions, who teaches [[motif:astronomy|astronomy]] and the properties of herbs and precious stones. He belongs to the class of demons prized as tutors of natural science rather than as warriors, reflecting the grimoire tradition's fascination with hidden knowledge.

## For artists & game designers
A dignified owl-demon makes an excellent scholarly familiar or quest-giver who trades arcane lore for a price.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed., ill. Louis Le Breton).
*The Lesser Key of Solomon: Ars Goetia*, spirit 36.

title: The Cage of the Sacred Chickens (Auspices)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Anonymous (after a Roman model)
summary:
An engraving of the *cage of the sacred chickens* used by Roman augurs to take the auspices, the *auspicia ex tripudiis*.

## Iconography
The plate shows the portable coop in which the sacred poultry were kept. In practice the *pullarius* (chicken-keeper) released the birds and scattered grain: how eagerly they ate, and whether food fell from their beaks to strike the ground (the *tripudium solistimum*), determined whether the gods approved an undertaking.

## Significance
Roman [[motif:augury|augury]] was a state discipline: no major public action — battle, election, assembly — proceeded without favourable auspices read from the flight, cries, or feeding of birds. The chicken-omen was the form most used on campaign for its portability. The famous anecdote of the consul Publius Claudius Pulcher, who threw the unwilling chickens overboard before his defeat at Drepana (249 BCE), shows how seriously the omens were taken.

## For artists & game designers
A low-tech, high-stakes oracle: feed the birds, read the result — a clean binary omen mechanic with rich flavour.

title: The Fortune Teller (Caravaggio)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Caravaggio (Michelangelo Merisi)
summary:
Caravaggio's *The Fortune Teller* (*La Buona Ventura*, c. 1595), in which a young gallant has his palm read — and his ring quietly stolen — by a smiling Romani woman.

## Iconography
A finely dressed youth offers his hand to a [[motif:palmistry|palm-reader]] who gazes into his eyes as she traces his palm; her other hand slips the ring from his finger. The exchange of glances and the hidden theft make the deception the true subject. The half-length, dramatically lit figures are a hallmark of Caravaggio's early naturalism.

## Significance
The picture launched a hugely influential genre of fortune-teller and cardsharp scenes across Baroque Europe (Vouet, Valentin, La Tour, the Le Nain). It reflects the early-modern stereotype of the wandering Romani diviner as a charming swindler, and the moralising theme of youth gulled by sensual distraction. Caravaggio painted at least two versions (Capitoline, Rome; Louvre, Paris).

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'con artist diviner' beat — fortune-telling as misdirection while the real theft happens in plain sight.

citations.text: Caravaggio, *The Fortune Teller* (c. 1595), Pinacoteca Capitolina, Rome.

title: Crystal Gazing (fortune-telling poster)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Anonymous (advertising lithograph)
summary:
A turn-of-the-century advertising poster for a 'crystal gazing' fortune-telling act ('Chandra Yogi'), illustrating the popular stage-magic afterlife of crystallomancy.

## Iconography
A performer in 'oriental' costume gazes into a crystal ball, the standard prop of [[motif:crystal gazing|crystallomancy]]. The exoticising imagery — turban, robes, an air of Eastern mystery — is typical of the commercial seer and mind-reading acts that toured theatres and fairs.

## Significance
While crystal-gazing descends from learned Renaissance scrying (Dee's shew-stones) and folk practice, by the nineteenth century it had become a fixture of spiritualist parlours, fairground fortune-tellers, and vaudeville mentalism. Posters like this document the popularisation and theatrical commodification of divination — the crystal ball cemented as the universal shorthand for telling the future. The 'Oriental' branding reflects the era's orientalist marketing of occult wonder.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'fortune-teller booth' visual language — crystal ball, robes, exotic signage — for a carnival-seer scene.

citations.text: On stage scrying and mentalism, see histories of spiritualism and the magic theatre.

title: John Dee's Obsidian Mirror (case)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Aztec; later owned by John Dee
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The obsidian mirror associated with the Elizabethan magus John Dee, an Aztec ritual object later used for scrying (British Museum; the photograph shows the mirror in its case).

## Iconography
The object is a disc of polished black volcanic glass with a small pierced handle-tab. Its mirror-dark surface is the classic [[motif:scrying|scrying]] speculum — a 'shew-stone' into which the seer gazes to summon visions. Such mirrors were also kept in fitted cases, as here.

## Significance
The mirror is a *tezcatlipoca* device — Aztec obsidian mirrors were sacred to the 'Smoking Mirror' god — brought to Europe after the conquest of Mexico. It entered the collection of John Dee, who with his scryer Edward Kelley conducted the 'angelic conversations' that produced the Enochian system. Long held in the British Museum and once owned by Horace Walpole, it is the most famous physical instrument of Renaissance crystallomancy and a striking case of New-World ritual objects absorbed into European occult practice.

## For artists & game designers
A real, dateable scrying artefact — a black glass disc as a 'vision portal' prop with a genuine cross-cultural backstory.

citations.text: British Museum, obsidian mirror (Dee); on Dee & Kelley, D. Harkness, *John Dee's Conversations with Angels* (1999).

title: Human and Animal Physiognomies (della Porta)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: After Giambattista della Porta
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A plate after Giambattista della Porta's *De humana physiognomonia* (1586), juxtaposing human profiles with animal heads to argue a correspondence of form and character (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
Human faces are set beside the heads of beasts — a man paired with a ram, an ox, a lion, an owl — so that shared contours (a heavy brow, a snub nose, a jutting jaw) suggest a shared temperament. The comparison is the signature device of [[motif:physiognomy|zoomorphic physiognomy]].

## Significance
della Porta revived the ancient pseudo-Aristotelian *Physiognomonica*, systematising the reading of character from outward form and especially the human-animal analogy. His plates fixed a visual method that ran on through Charles Le Brun's lectures to Lavater's *Essays on Physiognomy* and, much later, into the discredited racial 'sciences' of the nineteenth century. As divination, physiognomy claimed to read fate and disposition written in the face.

## For artists & game designers
The original 'beast-aspect' character chart: pair each face with an animal to telegraph temperament at a glance.

title: Sikidy Geomantic Divination
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Anonymous (Malagasy)
summary:
A photograph (c. 1900) of *sikidy*, the Malagasy geomantic divination in which a diviner generates a tableau of binary figures from arranged seeds.

## Iconography
The diviner lays out rows of seeds or beans, reducing each pile to a one-or-two remainder to build columns of [[motif:geomancy|geomantic figures]] — the same odd/even dot-patterns used across the Old World geomantic family. The resulting grid (the *toetry*) is then read for the questioner's fate.

## Significance
Sikidy is the East African and Malagasy branch of the geomantic tradition that runs from Arabic *'ilm al-raml* ('the science of the sand') through medieval Latin *geomantia* (Hugh of Santalla, Pietro d'Abano) to European fortune-telling. All share the sixteen tetragram figures generated by tallying marks into odd or even. The example shows the living, non-European face of a divinatory system Europe imported and Latinised, and underlines how widely the binary geomantic method travelled.

## For artists & game designers
A pure binary oracle — random marks collapse to sixteen named figures; trivial to implement and historically deep.

citations.text: M. Bloch, on Malagasy sikidy; S. Skinner, *Geomancy in Theory and Practice*.

title: Haruspices Interpreting Caesar's Dream
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Anonymous (manuscript illumination)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. British Library. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A manuscript miniature (British Library, Royal 16 G VIII) showing diviners interpreting an omen or dream for Julius Caesar.

## Iconography
Caesar is depicted attended by [[motif:haruspicy|haruspices]] or soothsayers who read the signs and deliver their counsel. The medieval illuminator dresses the antique diviners in contemporary costume, transposing the Roman art of reading entrails and dreams into a chivalric setting.

## Significance
The scene belongs to a tradition of vernacular histories of Caesar (the *Faits des Romains* and related texts) much copied in the later Middle Ages, in which classical divination is retold for a courtly audience. It illustrates the long medieval afterlife of Roman *haruspicy* and *oneiromancy*, and recalls the warnings — the soothsayer's 'Beware the Ides of March,' Calpurnia's dream — that Caesar famously disregarded.

## For artists & game designers
A template for the 'court diviner' scene: a ruler flanked by seers delivering a portent he may heed or ignore.

citations.text: London, British Library, Royal MS 16 G VIII, f. 39.

title: The Fortune Teller (Georges de La Tour)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Georges de La Tour
rights: Public domain. The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Georges de La Tour's *The Fortune Teller* (c. 1630s, Metropolitan Museum of Art), a richly costumed scene of a young man robbed while his fortune is told.

## Iconography
An old [[motif:fortune-telling|fortune-teller]] takes the youth's coin to 'cross his palm with silver' while, on either side, her young accomplices pick his pocket and cut a medal from its chain. The youth's distracted gaze and the converging hands of the thieves stage a slow, theatrical robbery in brilliant brocades and frozen glances.

## Significance
La Tour reworks the Caravaggesque fortune-teller theme that had spread north from Rome, turning it into a tableau of multiple deception and worldly warning. The painting carries a partly hidden inscription naming the artist and Lunéville. It exemplifies the moralised genre of the duped innocent and remains one of the most celebrated treatments of the diviner-as-trickster — an image of fortune-telling as social danger rather than true prophecy.

## For artists & game designers
A masterclass in staging a coordinated theft inside a divination scene — every accomplice's hand has a job.

citations.text: Georges de La Tour, *The Fortune Teller* (c. 1630s), The Metropolitan Museum of Art.

title: Lavater, Essays on Physiognomy (design plate)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: After Johann Caspar Lavater
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A design plate from an English edition of Johann Caspar Lavater's *Essays on Physiognomy*, the work that made face-reading a fashionable late-Enlightenment science (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The plate presents a row of profile heads and silhouettes for comparative analysis — the slope of a forehead, the line of a nose, the set of a mouth read as indices of intellect and morality. Lavater favoured the cut-paper [[motif:silhouette|silhouette]] as the purest record of a profile's contour.

## Significance
Lavater's *Physiognomische Fragmente* (1775–78), translated and lavishly engraved across Europe, claimed that inner character is legibly written in outward form, especially the face in profile. Hugely popular, it drew sharp criticism from Lichtenberg and others, yet seeded both the silhouette portrait craze and the later quasi-sciences of phrenology and criminal anthropology. As a divinatory art it promised to read a person's soul and destiny from their features.

## For artists & game designers
Silhouette-as-character: a profile cut-out carrying moral meaning is a strong stylised portrait device for a 'judge the stranger' mechanic.

citations.text: J. C. Lavater, *Essays on Physiognomy* (Eng. ed., 1789–98).

title: Petit Lenormand Fortune-Telling Cards
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: After Marie Anne Lenormand (tradition)
summary:
A card from the *Petit Lenormand*, the 36-card cartomantic deck named for the celebrated French fortune-teller Marie Anne Lenormand.

## Iconography
Each Lenormand card bears a single emblematic symbol — the Ship, the Tree, the Scythe, the Tower, the Stars, the Clover — often with a small inset playing card. The symbols are read in combination, neighbouring cards modifying one another, rather than through the elaborate single-card symbolism of tarot.

## Significance
Marie Anne Lenormand (1772–1843) was the most famous fortune-teller of Napoleonic France, reputed to have read for Joséphine and Robespierre. The decks published after her death under her name codified a distinct system of [[motif:cartomancy|cartomancy]] — simpler, more concrete, and grid-read (the *Grand Tableau*) — that remains popular across Europe alongside tarot. It represents the nineteenth-century commercialisation of card divination for a mass public.

## For artists & game designers
A compact symbol-set (36 plain emblems) ideal for a readable, modular divination minigame distinct from tarot's heavier iconography.

citations.text: On Lenormand cartomancy, see histories of 19th-c. fortune-telling decks.

title: Misero (The Beggar) — Mantegna Tarocchi
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi (so-called 'Mantegna')
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Rijksmuseum. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Misero* (the Beggar), the lowest of the fifty cards of the so-called *Mantegna Tarocchi*, an engraved didactic series of c. 1465 (Rijksmuseum impression).

## Iconography
A ragged beggar leans on a staff, accompanied by two dogs — the bottom rung of the 'Conditions of Man.' He opens the first decade (E1) of the series, beneath the Servant, Artisan, Merchant, and so up to the Emperor and Pope. The image is plain, labelled, and emblematic.

## Significance
The *Mantegna Tarocchi* is not a true playing tarot but an encyclopaedic engraved set of fifty cards in five decades — the [[motif:estates of man|estates of man]], the Muses and Apollo, the Liberal Arts, the cosmic principles, and the planets and spheres. Wrongly attributed to Mantegna, it is a North-Italian humanist teaching device of the Ferrarese circle, and an essential witness to how the early image-systems behind tarot encoded a hierarchical, divinatory map of the cosmos. *Misero* fixes the bottom of that ladder of fortune.

## For artists & game designers
A clean labelled 'rank card' from the very dawn of tarot imagery — useful as a historic, pre-Waite alternative to modern decks.

title: XIII (Death / l'Arcane sans nom) — Tarot de Marseille
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Trump XIII of the Tarot de Marseille — the skeletal reaper traditionally called Death, the 'arcane sans nom,' in the Nicolas Conver pattern of 1760.

## Iconography
A black-boned skeleton wields a scythe, mowing a field strewn with severed heads, hands, and feet that sprout afresh from the dark soil. In the Marseille tradition the card is famously left *unnamed* on many decks (hence *l'arcane sans nom*), reinforcing its taboo. The sprouting limbs hint at regeneration as much as ending.

## Significance
Trump XIII belongs to the tarot's central sequence of trials and transformations and descends from the medieval *Danse Macabre* and *memento mori* tradition. Esoteric tarotists from Court de Gébelin and Éliphas Lévi onward read it not as literal death but as [[motif:transformation|transformation]], the clearing-away that precedes renewal. It is the most iconic and most superstitiously regarded card of the deck.

## For artists & game designers
The scythe-skeleton over a sprouting field is the classic 'death = change' symbol — useful where literal mortality should read as transition.

citations.text: A. Court de Gébelin, *Le Monde primitif*, vol. 8 (1781).
É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1856).

title: La Lune (The Moon) — Tarot de Marseille XVIII
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*La Lune* (The Moon), trump XVIII of the Tarot de Marseille, in the Nicolas Conver pattern of 1760.

## Iconography
A great face-in-the-moon sheds drops of light over a landscape where two dogs (or a dog and a wolf) bay upward, two towers flank the horizon, and a crayfish or crustacean crawls from a pool below. The composition reads as a nocturnal world of instinct, water, and threshold.

## Significance
The Moon, second of the celestial triad, traditionally signifies [[motif:illusion|illusion]], the unconscious, dreams, and the deceptive light that obscures as much as it reveals. The baying dogs and the creature emerging from the deep were read by esoteric tarotists as the animal soul and the path through dark waters. In divination it warns of confusion, hidden enemies, and the uncertain path that must nonetheless be travelled.

## For artists & game designers
The baying-dogs-and-crayfish moonscape is a ready 'uncanny night / illusion' tableau for a dream or threshold scene.

citations.text: É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1856).

title: L'Étoile (The Star) — Tarot de Marseille XVII
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*L'Étoile* (The Star), trump XVII of the Tarot de Marseille, in the Nicolas Conver pattern of 1760.

## Iconography
A nude woman kneels by a stream, pouring water from two ewers — one onto the land, one back into the water — beneath a great central star surrounded by seven lesser stars, with a bird perched in a tree. The endless pouring evokes renewal, flow, and replenishment.

## Significance
The Star comes after the Tower in the sequence, beginning the celestial triad of [[motif:hope|Star, Moon, and Sun]] that follows catastrophe with hope and guidance. Esoteric tarotists associated her with the goddess pouring the waters of life, with hope and inspiration, and with astral influence descending to earth. The eight-star arrangement was variously read as the planets, the Pleiades, or the waters above and below.

## For artists & game designers
The water-pouring star-maiden is the tarot's 'hope/renewal' card — a serene counterpoint to the Tower's disaster.

citations.text: É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1856).

title: La Maison-Dieu (The Tower) — Tarot de Marseille
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Anonymous (Tarot de Marseille pattern, drawing)
rights: Public domain. Bibliothèque nationale de France. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*La Maison-Dieu* (The Tower), trump XVI of the Tarot de Marseille, here a drawing after the standard pattern (Bibliothèque nationale de France).

## Iconography
A tall tower is struck at its crown by a bolt from the heavens; the crenellated top flies off and two figures tumble headlong amid a shower of coloured discs or flames. The French name *La Maison-Dieu* ('the House of God') is an old crux — possibly a corruption referring to a hospice, an almshouse, or the fallen Tower of Babel.

## Significance
Trump XVI follows the Devil in the sequence and signals sudden [[motif:catastrophe|catastrophe]] — the violent overthrow of pride, false structures, or illusion by a stroke from above. Esoteric readers linked it to Babel, to the lightning of divine judgement, and to the necessary shattering that precedes spiritual renewal (the Star, Moon, and Sun follow). It is the tarot's image of crisis and abrupt revelation.

## For artists & game designers
The lightning-struck tower with falling figures is the universal 'sudden disaster' icon — drop it on any moment of structural collapse.

title: La Roue de Fortune — Tarot de Marseille X
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*La Roue de Fortune*, trump X of the Tarot de Marseille, in the pattern of Nicolas Conver's 1760 Marseille deck.

## Iconography
A great wheel turns, its rim ridden by hybrid creatures: one ascends, one descends head-down, and a crowned beast (often sphinx-like) is poised at the top with a sword. The image fuses the [[motif:wheel of fortune|Wheel of Fortune]] of medieval *Rota Fortunae* iconography with the tarot trump's animal riders.

## Significance
The Tarot de Marseille is the standardised woodcut pattern that crystallised in 17th–18th-century France and became the basis of all Western occult tarot. Trump X transmits the ancient and medieval image of Fortune's wheel — the blind goddess raising and casting down kings (Boethius, *De consolatione*) — into the tarot's allegorical sequence, where it signals cycles, chance, and reversal of fate. The Conver deck of 1760 is the most influential single edition of the Marseille pattern.

## For artists & game designers
The purest 'turn of fortune' card: a wheel with rising/falling figures, instantly legible as a luck or random-event icon.

title: Metoposcopia (Forehead-Line Divination)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Samuel Fuchs
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A face diagram from Samuel Fuchs's *Metoposcopia* (1615), in which the lines of the forehead are read as planetary signs of character and fate (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
A human face is overlaid with horizontal [[motif:forehead lines|forehead lines]], each assigned to one of the seven planets — Saturn highest, then Jupiter, Mars, Sun, Venus, Mercury, and the Moon lowest near the brows. The depth, breaks, and crossings of these lines are interpreted as the heavens' imprint on the individual.

## Significance
Metoposcopy — a specialised offshoot of physiognomy — was given its classic form by Girolamo Cardano, whose posthumous *Metoposcopia* (1658) supplied hundreds of forehead diagrams with prognostications. Fuchs's earlier treatise belongs to the same tradition that mapped the seven planetary lines across the brow. It exemplifies the early-modern conviction that astral influence is inscribed on the body and can be read like a horoscope from the skin itself.

## For artists & game designers
A seven-line 'planetary forehead' overlay is a compact, exotic divination graphic for a fortune-teller NPC.

title: Chiromancy Hand (De Occulta Philosophia)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
summary:
A chiromantic hand diagram associated with Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa's *De Occulta Philosophia*, mapping the lines and mounts of the palm to the planets.

## Iconography
The open palm is overlaid with named [[motif:chiromancy|lines]] (life, head, heart, the Girdle of Venus) and the fleshy *mounts* at the base of each finger and the thumb, each mount assigned to a planet — Jupiter, Saturn, Apollo/Sun, Mercury, Venus, the Moon, and the Mars regions. The diagram turns the hand into an astrological microcosm.

## Significance
Chiromancy (palmistry) treated the hand as a legible text written by the heavens at birth. Agrippa's three-book *De Occulta Philosophia* (1533) systematised Renaissance magic and gave the palmistry-astrology correspondence its standard learned form, building on earlier authors such as Bartolommeo della Rocca (Cocles). The planetary mounts remain the backbone of palmistry to this day.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical palm-map — labelled mounts and lines tied to planets — works directly as a diegetic divination chart or character-sheet overlay.

citations.text: H. C. Agrippa, *De Occulta Philosophia libri tres* (1533).

title: The Liver of Piacenza
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Etruscan (anonymous)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *Fegato di Piacenza*, a life-size Etruscan bronze model of a sheep's liver inscribed as a diviner's reference chart, c. 100 BCE.

## Iconography
The convex bronze is divided into regions by incised lines, each cell bearing the abbreviated name of an Etruscan deity. The outer rim carries sixteen divisions corresponding to the sixteen houses of the Etruscan sky; the central lobes (the *processus pyramidalis* and gall-bladder) are also labelled. It is, in effect, a map of the cosmos projected onto an organ.

## Significance
The object is the key surviving artefact of Etruscan [[motif:haruspicy|hepatoscopy]] — divination by reading the liver of a sacrificed animal, the discipline the Romans inherited as the *haruspices*' art. Its sixteenfold division of the heavens parallels the cosmological scheme described by Martianus Capella and Pliny, and it links Etruscan practice to the older Mesopotamian liver-omen tradition. It remains the primary teaching model for understanding how ancient diviners correlated celestial regions with portions of the entrails.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made 'divination interface': a labelled organ-map whose zones map to gods and sky-houses — perfect for an augury minigame UI.

citations.text: L. B. van der Meer, *The Bronze Liver of Piacenza* (1987).

title: Thalia — Mantegna Tarocchi (Muse)
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi (so-called 'Mantegna')
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Cleveland Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Thalia*, muse of comedy and pastoral poetry, from the second decade (Apollo and the Muses) of the *Mantegna Tarocchi*, c. 1465 (Cleveland Museum of Art).

## Iconography
Thalia stands as a graceful classical figure, her attribute marking her as the [[motif:muse|muse]] of comedy and bucolic verse. She belongs to the 'D' decade (E11–E20) that ranges Apollo and the nine Muses, the second tier of the fifty-card system above the estates of man.

## Significance
The Muse decade displays the Ferrarese humanists' revival of classical learning within an ordered, ascending scheme — from the conditions of mortal life up through the arts, the cosmos, and the heavens. The fine engraving, once credited to Mantegna himself, transmits the antique iconography of the Muses to Renaissance Europe and shows tarot's early imagery as a curriculum of knowledge rather than a mere game. Albrecht Dürer is known to have copied figures from the series.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic 15th-century Muse figure — period reference for allegorical 'patron of an art' cards.

citations.text: J. A. Levenson et al., *Early Italian Engravings from the National Gallery of Art* (1973).

title: Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot card
work: The Arts of Divination
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
A card from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) tarot, c. 1440s, among the earliest surviving hand-painted tarot decks, attributed to Bonifacio Bembo for the Milanese court.

## Iconography
The card is hand-painted on a tooled [[motif:gold ground|gold ground]], in the luxurious International Gothic manner of the Visconti-Sforza workshop. The Cary-Yale deck is unusually expanded, including extra court figures (a Female Knight and Page alongside the male) and theological-virtue trumps not found in the standard later pattern.

## Significance
The Visconti and Visconti-Sforza decks are the foundational monuments of tarot: bespoke *trionfi* (triumph) cards made for the rulers of Milan in the mid-fifteenth century, long before any documented use for fortune-telling. They preserve the original trump iconography — the Wheel, Death, the Tower, the World — that the printed Tarot de Marseille would later standardise and that occultists would, from the 18th century, reinterpret as a divinatory book of wisdom.

## For artists & game designers
The gold-ground, courtly origin of tarot — a reference for 'luxury commissioned deck' art distinct from cheap printed cards.

title: Bagua Mirror Talismans (Eight Trigrams)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Chinese; photo Physicist1204)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Three *bagua* mirror talismans, octagonal protective charms displaying the Eight Trigrams arranged around a central mirror.

## Iconography
Each charm is an octagon bearing the eight three-line [[motif:eight trigrams|trigrams]] (☰ Qian, ☷ Kun, ☵ Kan, ☲ Li, and the rest) around its rim, usually framing a *Taijitu* and a small convex, flat, or concave mirror at the centre. The arrangement shown is typically the 'Later Heaven' (King Wen) sequence used in feng shui.

## Significance
The *bagua* — eight trigrams formed from broken and unbroken lines — is the combinatorial heart of the *I Ching* and of Chinese correlative cosmology, each trigram keyed to a direction, element, season, family member, and bodily organ. Mounted with a mirror above a doorway, the bagua becomes an [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] device that deflects malign *sha* energy in feng-shui practice. The object thus fuses divinatory cosmology with everyday domestic magic.

## For artists & game designers
An eight-fold directional key (trigram = element + compass point) usable as a ward icon or an attunement wheel.

citations.text: Richard J. Smith, *Fathoming the Cosmos and Ordering the World: The Yijing* (2008).

title: Fifty-Eight Wrathful Deities of the Bardo Thodol
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Tibetan; rendering by Djampa)
summary:
A diagram of the fifty-eight wrathful deities encountered in the intermediate state (*bardo*) according to the *Bardo Thodol*, the so-called Tibetan Book of the Dead.

## Iconography
Ranks of flame-wreathed [[motif:wrathful deity|wrathful deities]] — *herukas* in union with consorts, animal-headed goddesses, and gatekeepers — are arrayed in their fixed iconographic order. With the forty-two peaceful deities they form the 'hundred peaceful and wrathful' that arise to the consciousness of the dying and dead.

## Significance
The *Bardo Thodol* ('Liberation Through Hearing in the Intermediate State') is a treasure-text (*terma*) of the Nyingma tradition, read aloud to guide the deceased through the visionary states between death and rebirth. The peaceful and wrathful deities are taught to be projections of the person's own mind; recognising them as such brings liberation, while reacting with fear drives one toward another rebirth. The text became globally famous through W. Y. Evans-Wentz's 1927 edition and shaped Western reception of Tibetan thought, including C. G. Jung's commentary.

## For artists & game designers
A fixed roster of 58 ordered apparitions — a ready 'judgement of the dead' gauntlet with named, designed forms.

citations.text: W. Y. Evans-Wentz (ed.), *The Tibetan Book of the Dead* (1927).

title: Bhavachakra (Wheel of Life), Tawang Monastery
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Tibetan; photo Kingshuk Mondal)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo Kingshuk Mondal). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A *Bhavachakra*, the Buddhist 'Wheel of Life,' painted at the entrance of Tawang Monastery — a diagram of cyclic existence held in the jaws of the lord of death.

## Iconography
The great wheel is clutched by [[motif:yama|Yama]], lord of death. At its hub spin the three poisons (cock, snake, pig: greed, hatred, delusion); the next ring shows beings rising and falling by karma; the broad band is divided into the [[motif:six realms|six realms]] of rebirth (gods, demigods, humans, animals, hungry ghosts, hells); the outer rim runs the twelve links of dependent origination (*nidanas*). Outside, a Buddha points to the moon of liberation.

## Significance
The *Bhavachakra* is Buddhism's master-diagram of *samsara* — the round of birth, death, and rebirth driven by craving and ignorance — and a teaching device traditionally painted at monastery gates. It compresses the doctrines of karma, the realms of rebirth, and the twelve-fold chain of causation into a single legible image, with the Buddha's gesture marking the only exit. It is among the most widely reproduced of all Buddhist instructional paintings.

## For artists & game designers
A nested wheel of realms and causes — a full reincarnation/karma system rendered as one diagram.

citations.text: Robert Beer, *The Encyclopedia of Tibetan Symbols and Motifs* (1999).

title: Yogin in Meditation with Chakras and Kundalini
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Indian)
summary:
An Indian painting of a yogin in meditation, his body marked with the *chakras* and the rising *kundalini* serpent of the subtle anatomy.

## Iconography
The seated figure is overlaid with a vertical column of lotus-[[motif:chakras|chakras]] strung along the central channel, from the root at the perineum to the thousand-petalled lotus at the crown. The coiled [[motif:kundalini|kundalini]] serpent, the dormant 'serpent power,' rises through them, and the side channels (*ida* and *pingala*) twine around the central *sushumna*.

## Significance
This is the classic visual scheme of *kundalini yoga* and tantric physiology: a [[motif:subtle body|subtle body]] of channels (*nadis*) and energy-centres (*chakras*) superimposed on the physical form. Through breath, posture, and visualisation the practitioner awakens the kundalini at the base and draws it upward to union at the crown, attaining liberation. Systematised in texts like the *Sat-Chakra-Nirupana* (and popularised in the West by Arthur Avalon's *The Serpent Power*, 1919), this map of the inner body parallels the Daoist neidan charts as Asia's other great science of energetic self-cultivation.

## For artists & game designers
A vertical 'energy ladder' of named centres with a rising-power mechanic — a ready progression/charge system overlaid on a body.

title: Diamond World Mandala (Kongōkai), Mandala of the Two Worlds
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Japanese; Chester Beatty Library copy)
date: later copy of the esoteric (Shingon) type
summary:
The *Kongōkai* (Diamond World) mandala, one half of the paired Mandala of the Two Worlds central to Japanese Shingon esoteric Buddhism (Chester Beatty Library copy).

## Iconography
The Diamond World is laid out as a three-by-three grid of nine 'assemblies,' each a sub-[[motif:mandala|mandala]] of Buddhas and bodhisattvas centred on the cosmic Buddha [[motif:vairocana|Vairocana]] (Dainichi). The rigid lattice expresses the adamantine, indestructible wisdom aspect of reality.

## Significance
With its partner the *Taizōkai* (Womb World), the *Kongōkai* forms the *ryōkai* mandala that Kūkai brought from China in 806 to found the Shingon school. The Diamond World represents the unchanging wisdom of Vairocana; the Womb World the dynamic compassion that pervades phenomena. Hung facing each other in the ritual hall, the pair maps the entire esoteric cosmos and structures Shingon initiation and visualisation. The nine-assembly grid is one of East Asian Buddhism's most influential diagrammatic schemes.

## For artists & game designers
A 3×3 grid of self-contained sub-worlds, each centred on the same lord — a fractal, modular map structure.

citations.text: Adrian Snodgrass, *The Matrix and Diamond World Mandalas in Shingon Buddhism* (1988).

title: Vishnu Riding Garuda (Nepalese schist)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Nepalese)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Art Institute of Chicago. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Nepalese schist sculpture (16th–17th c.) of the god Vishnu riding his mount *Garuda*, the eagle-being who is a potent protective and serpent-quelling deity (Art Institute of Chicago).

## Iconography
Vishnu sits enthroned upon [[motif:garuda|Garuda]], the great bird-man with wings, beak or eagle features, and human torso, carved in dark stone. Garuda is shown bearing his divine rider aloft, the canonical *vahana* (vehicle) pose.

## Significance
Garuda, sworn enemy of the *nagas* (serpents), is invoked across Hindu and Buddhist Asia as a protector against snakebite, poison, and malign serpent-spirits; Garuda *mantras* and amulets are a staple of protective magic from India and Nepal to Tibet, Southeast Asia, and beyond. In the Vajrayana he also appears as a wrathful enlightened form. As Vishnu's mount he links the great Hindu pantheon to the everyday apotropaic practice of warding off poison and disease.

## For artists & game designers
A winged bird-rider deity with a clear function (anti-poison, anti-serpent) — a ready protective-summon design.

title: Nine-Deity Mandala of Two-Armed Hevajra
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Tibetan)
summary:
A painted mandala of the two-armed *Hevajra* in his nine-deity configuration, a Vajrayana meditational diagram (Google Art Project).

## Iconography
At the centre of a square palace within concentric protective rings stands [[motif:hevajra|Hevajra]] in union with his consort Nairatmya, surrounded by eight attendant *yogini* goddesses set in the petals of a lotus — the 'nine deities' of the title. The four-gated [[motif:mandala|mandala]] is enclosed by rings of vajras and flame.

## Significance
Hevajra is the central deity of the *Hevajra Tantra*, a major 'mother tantra' especially important to the Sakya school and historically influential at the Mongol Yuan court. The mandala is not merely a picture but a meditative architecture: the practitioner visualises themselves entering the palace and identifying with the central deity and retinue. The nine-deity scheme — a centre plus eight — is a recurrent tantric pattern echoing the Luoshu's nine cells and the eightfold lotus.

## For artists & game designers
A centre-plus-eight 'party formation' inside a gated palace — a compact mandala template for a hub-and-ring layout.

title: Fuxi Order of the 64 Hexagrams (Grid and Circle)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: After Shao Yong; engraving in James Legge, *The Yi King* (1882)
summary:
The 'Former Heaven' (Fuxi) arrangement of the sixty-four *I Ching* hexagrams as both a square grid and a surrounding circle, reproduced in James Legge's 1882 translation.

## Iconography
Sixty-four six-line [[motif:hexagrams|hexagrams]] are laid out in a square block and again around a great ring. Read in the Fuxi order they follow a strict doubling pattern, so that the sequence is, in effect, a binary count from 0 to 63 expressed in broken and unbroken lines.

## Significance
