This is the diagram, devised by the Song philosopher Shao Yong, that the printed broadsheet of which Joachim Bouvet sent to Leibniz — who recognised in its order his own binary arithmetic. The *I Ching* (*Book of Changes*) is the foundational Chinese divination classic, its hexagrams generated by yarrow stalks or coins and read for guidance. The Fuxi circle-and-grid makes visible the mathematical regularity beneath the oracle and became a key node in the early-modern European reception of Chinese thought.

## For artists & game designers
A 64-state binary lattice — every hexagram an address — ideal for a procedurally ordered oracle or map key.

citations.text: James Legge, *The Yî King* (Sacred Books of the East, vol. 16, 1882).
Richard J. Smith, *The I Ching: A Biography* (2012).

title: Kalachakra Sand Mandala (Namgyal monks)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Namgyal Monastery monks (photo Wonderlane)
rights: CC BY 2.0 (photo Wonderlane). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Tibetan Buddhist monks of the Namgyal Monastery constructing the *Kalachakra* ('Wheel of Time') sand mandala, the most elaborate of the Vajrayana mandalas.

## Iconography
Coloured sand is laid grain by grain into a vast circular [[motif:mandala|mandala]] — a square palace of four gates set within concentric rings of fire, vajra, lotus, and protective deities, all keyed to the Kalachakra deity at the centre. The whole is a two-dimensional ground-plan of a three-dimensional celestial palace.

## Significance
The *Kalachakra* tantra integrates cosmology, the calendar, subtle-body yoga, and the figure of the messianic kingdom of Shambhala. Its mandala is built over days for a major public initiation, then ceremonially swept away and poured into water — a deliberate enactment of [[motif:impermanence|impermanence]]. The Dalai Lamas, whose personal monastery is Namgyal, are especially associated with conferring the Kalachakra empowerment. As ritual technology the sand mandala is a temporary cosmos created to be entered in meditation and then dissolved.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical four-gated palace plan — concentric protective rings around a central deity, a ready map for a sacred-architecture level.

title: Leibniz's Diagram of the I Ching Hexagrams (1701)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Joachim Bouvet / G. W. Leibniz (annotated)
summary:
The diagram of the *I Ching* hexagrams in the arrangement of Shao Yong, owned and annotated by Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, sent to him in 1701 by the Jesuit Joachim Bouvet.

## Iconography
A circular and square arrangement of the sixty-four [[motif:hexagrams|hexagrams]] of the *Book of Changes*, with Leibniz's marginal numbering correlating each figure to a value in his binary notation — broken lines as 0, unbroken as 1.

## Significance
This sheet is the famous artefact of cross-cultural mathematics: Bouvet, working in Beijing, recognised that the Fuxi order of the hexagrams matched the binary arithmetic Leibniz had developed, and sent him the diagram. Leibniz published the connection in 1703, reading the ancient Chinese oracle as an unwitting expression of dyadic (base-2) numeration — and, in his Christian-philosophical framing, of creation from 0 (nothing) and 1 (God). It is a touchstone of the European Enlightenment's encounter with Chinese cosmology and a landmark in the prehistory of binary computing.

## For artists & game designers
The historical moment binary met divination — a prop for any 'ancient code = modern machine' narrative.

citations.text: G. W. Leibniz, 'Explication de l'arithmétique binaire' (1703).

title: Feng-Shui Compass (Luopan)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Chinese)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Chinese *luopan*, the geomancer's compass used in *feng shui* to determine the auspicious siting and orientation of buildings and graves (lacquer on wood and brass, 20th c.).

## Iconography
A magnetic needle sits at the centre of a disc ringed by many concentric bands, each inscribed with a layer of cosmological data — the eight [[motif:eight trigrams|trigrams]], the twenty-four directions, the sixty *jiazi* cycle, the lunar mansions, the five phases — read outward from the needle to correlate a site with the heavens.

## Significance
The *luopan* is the working instrument of *kanyu* (feng shui), the Chinese geomantic art of harmonising human structures with the flow of *qi* across the landscape. Its many rings compress the whole apparatus of correlative cosmology into a single hand-held device, and its central magnetised needle reflects China's early mastery of the lodestone compass — first developed, in fact, for divination and siting before navigation. Each school of feng shui favours its own ring configuration.

## For artists & game designers
A dense concentric dial encoding direction, element, and cycle — a perfect diegetic 'cosmic instrument' UI.

citations.text: Stephen Skinner, *Guide to the Feng Shui Compass* (2008).

title: Luoshu (Magic Square of Order Three)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Chinese; diagram)
summary:
A diagram of the *Luoshu* ('Writing of the Luo River'), the 3×3 magic square in which every row, column, and diagonal sums to fifteen, foundational to Chinese numerological cosmology.

## Iconography
The nine digits 1–9 are arranged so that the central cell is 5 and each line sums to 15; in traditional form the numbers appear as clusters of dark (yin, even) and light (yang, odd) dots, said to have been seen on the shell of a sacred turtle emerging from the Luo River.

## Significance
The [[motif:luoshu|Luoshu]], paired with the *Hetu* ('River Chart'), is one of the two primal diagrams of Chinese cosmology. Its nine cells became the 'Nine Palaces' mapped onto the compass directions, the trigrams, the five phases, and the calendar, and it underlies *feng shui*, divination, and ritual orientation. As the earliest documented magic square, it also occupies a place in the global history of recreational and esoteric mathematics, mirrored later in Islamic and European magic squares.

## For artists & game designers
A 3×3 numeric grid where every line balances — a built-in puzzle lattice and a directional cosmology in nine cells.

citations.text: Frank J. Swetz, *Legacy of the Luoshu* (2002).

title: Mongolian Shaman's Instruments
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Mongolian; photo Gary Todd)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A display of Mongolian shaman's instruments — the drum, costume, and ritual implements used to enter trance and engage the spirit world.

## Iconography
The objects include the broad single-headed frame [[motif:ritual drum|drum]] (the shaman's 'mount' to the other world), a fringed and tasselled spirit-costume hung with metal pendants, bells, and bronze mirrors (*toli*), and assorted ritual gear. Their materials and ornament identify the wearer's helping spirits.

## Significance
Mongolian shamanism, rooted in *Tengrism* (the cult of the sky-father Tengri and the spirits of land and ancestors), centres on the shaman (*böö*) who drums into ecstatic trance to journey among spirits, heal, divine, and mediate with the dead. The drum carries the shaman in flight; the mirror deflects harmful forces and 'sees' spirits; the costume's pendants give voice to the helpers. Surviving alongside and beneath Tibetan Buddhism, Mongolian shamanism is a key living branch of the North-Asian shamanic complex studied by Mircea Eliade and others.

## For artists & game designers
A complete shaman kit — drum as travel-portal, mirror as spirit-sight, costume as spirit-roster — a ready toolkit of ritual items.

citations.text: Mircea Eliade, *Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy* (1951).

title: Neijing Tu (Chart of the Inner Landscape)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Chinese, Daoist)
date: 19th c. (after a Qing stele in the White Cloud Monastery, Beijing)
summary:
The *Neijing Tu* ('Chart of the Inner Landscape / Inner Warp'), a Daoist diagram of the human body imagined as a mountain landscape through which the vital breath circulates in the practice of internal alchemy.

## Iconography
The seated body is shown in profile as a vertical world: the head a peaked mountain with sun and moon (the eyes), the spine a river or watercourse, the lower belly a terraced field where an ox ploughs and a child treads a water-wheel to raise the energy. Pagodas, weaving maidens, and a herd-boy mark the three *dantian* (cinnabar fields) and the gates along the spinal pathway.

## Significance
The chart visualises [[motif:neidan|neidan]], Daoist 'internal alchemy,' in which the adept refines *jing*, *qi*, and *shen* by circulating breath up the spine and down the front in the 'microcosmic orbit.' The body is read as a sealed cosmos — a [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]] mirroring the heavens — so that the alchemical Work performed by outer adepts on cinnabar and mercury is here turned inward. The best-known version derives from a stone stele preserved at the Baiyun Guan (White Cloud Monastery) in Beijing.

## For artists & game designers
A complete 'body-as-landscape' map: organs become locations and qi a resource to route — a ready inner-world level design.

citations.text: Catherine Despeux, *Taoïsme et corps humain: le Xiuzhen tu* (1994).

title: Ofuda Paper Talisman (Yanagidani Kannon)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Japanese; Yanagidani Kannon temple)
summary:
An *ofuda*, a Japanese paper (or wooden) household talisman, issued by the Yanagidani Kannon temple.

## Iconography
The tall strip bears the name or seal of the enshrined deity or Buddha — here associated with [[motif:kannon|Kannon]], the bodhisattva of compassion — in bold calligraphy, often with a temple stamp. It is meant to be enshrined in the home, typically on a *kamidana* or *butsudan*, or pasted by the door.

## Significance
The [[motif:ofuda|ofuda]] carries the presence and protective power of the deity into the household, warding misfortune, fire, and illness over the year before being returned and renewed. Issued by both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, ofuda exemplify Japanese 'practical religion,' in which sacred names and seals function as portable, renewable instruments of blessing and protection — the everyday end of the same talismanic logic seen in Daoist fú and Buddhist *dharani* slips.

## For artists & game designers
A renewable, place-based blessing token — a clean 'shrine buff' item with an annual expiry mechanic.

citations.text: Ian Reader & George J. Tanabe, *Practically Religious* (1998).

title: Omamori Amulet (Tachiki Kannon)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Japanese; Tachiki Kannon temple)
summary:
An *omamori*, a Japanese portable amulet in a small brocade pouch, issued by the Tachiki Kannon temple.

## Iconography
The charm is a little drawstring bag of patterned silk brocade, printed or embroidered with the temple's name and purpose. Sealed inside is a slip of paper or wood (a miniature *ofuda*) bearing the sacred name; the pouch is never meant to be opened.

## Significance
The [[motif:omamori|omamori]] is the portable counterpart of the household ofuda — carried on the person, hung from a bag or phone, or kept in a car for a specific blessing: traffic safety, success in study, easy childbirth, love, or general protection. Like the ofuda it embodies the deity's protective power and is renewed yearly. Omamori are among the most visible everyday objects of Japanese religious life and a vivid example of talismanic practice thriving in a modern, commercial setting.

## For artists & game designers
A wearable single-purpose charm in a sealed pouch — a tidy equippable buff item with thematic variants.

citations.text: Ian Reader & George J. Tanabe, *Practically Religious* (1998).

title: Wrathful Deities of the Guhyagarbha Tantra (chart)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Tibetan; via Himalayan Art Resources)
summary:
An iconographic chart of the wrathful deities of the *Guhyagarbha Tantra*, the root tantra of the Nyingma 'Mahayoga' that underlies the peaceful-and-wrathful deity scheme.

## Iconography
The deities are laid out as a labelled key — the central *herukas* and their retinues, gatekeepers, and goddesses set in ordered registers, in the manner of a reference sheet rather than a devotional thangka. This is the deity assembly from which the [[motif:bardo thodol|Bardo Thodol]] visions are drawn.

## Significance
The *Guhyagarbha Tantra* ('Secret Essence') is the foundational scripture for the mandala of the hundred peaceful and wrathful deities in the Nyingma tradition; the *Bardo Thodol* and the *zhitro* ('peaceful-wrathful') practices elaborate its assembly. A systematic chart like this serves practitioners as an iconographic and liturgical map — fixing the names, attributes, and positions of each deity in the mandala. It exemplifies the precise, encoded visual taxonomy of Tibetan tantra.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled deity-roster sheet — exactly the kind of reference key for building a coherent pantheon with fixed attributes.

citations.text: Gyurme Dorje (trans.), *The Guhyagarbha Tantra* and commentaries.

title: Yant Paed Tidt (Eight-Direction Thai Yantra)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Thai)
summary:
*Yant Paed Tidt* ('Yantra of the Eight Directions'), a classic Thai *sak yant* design used as a protective tattoo or cloth/paper amulet.

## Iconography
The geometric [[motif:sak yant|yantra]] radiates protection toward the eight compass directions, its cells filled with sacred *Khom* (Khmer) script syllables and topped by spiralling *unalome* and rows of mantra-letters. Each direction and syllable carries a specific guarding power.

## Significance
*Sak yant* is the Thai (and broader Southeast Asian) tradition of sacred yantra tattooing, practised by Buddhist monks and lay masters (*ajarn*), fusing Theravada Buddhist devotion with older animist and Brahmanic magic. The designs confer protection, charisma, invulnerability, or luck, activated by the master's recited *kata* (mantra). The *Yant Paed Tidt* in particular shields the bearer from danger arriving from any of the eight directions. The same yantras appear on cloth (*pha yant*) and rolled-metal *takrut* amulets.

## For artists & game designers
A directional ward (protection from eight headings) rendered as inscribed geometry — a strong tattoo/sigil asset.

citations.text: On sak yant, see studies of Thai protective tattooing and Buddhist magic.

title: Abe no Seimei (Onmyōji with the Seiman Pentagram)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Suzuki Masanao (Japan, 1815–1890)
rights: Public domain. LACMA. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A nineteenth-century Japanese print of *Abe no Seimei*, the legendary Heian-era *onmyōji* (diviner-magician) whose emblem is the five-pointed *Seiman* star (Suzuki Masanao; LACMA).

## Iconography
The sage is shown in courtly robes amid the apparatus of his art. His signature device, the [[motif:seiman pentagram|Seiman]], is a unicursal five-pointed star whose five points stand for the five phases — wood, fire, earth, metal, water — and their cycles of generation and conquest, used as a protective seal.

## Significance
Abe no Seimei (921–1005) is the most famous practitioner of *onmyōdō* ('the way of yin and yang'), the Japanese court discipline blending Chinese five-phase cosmology, calendrical science, divination, and apotropaic ritual. Around him gathered legends of commanding *shikigami* (spirit-servants) and defeating curses. The Seiman pentagram and the grid-like *Dōman* remain his emblems, still sold as protective talismans at the Seimei Shrine in Kyoto. He embodies the East Asian counterpart to the learned ritual magus.

## For artists & game designers
A court-sorcerer archetype with a ready sigil (five-element pentagram) and spirit-servant mechanic.

citations.text: Shigeta Shin'ichi, on onmyōdō and Abe no Seimei.

title: Sri Yantra (Engraved Copper Plate)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Indian)
summary:
A *Sri Yantra* (Shri Chakra) engraved on a copper plate, the supreme diagram of the Hindu Shakta tantric tradition.

## Iconography
Nine interlocking [[motif:interlocking triangles|triangles]] — five pointing down (the goddess) and four up (the god) — interpenetrate to generate forty-three smaller triangles around a central point (*bindu*), the whole enclosed by lotus petals and a square 'earth-citadel' with four gates. The intricate symmetry radiates from, and resolves into, the central dot.

## Significance
The [[motif:sri yantra|Sri Yantra]] is the geometric body of the goddess Lalita Tripura Sundari and the central icon of the Shri Vidya school of tantra. It is read as a cosmogram — the unfolding of the manifest universe from the undifferentiated *bindu* and its reabsorption — and serves as a support for worship and meditation, the adept ascending its nine enclosures (*chakras*) inward toward the centre. Its mathematically demanding construction has fascinated geometers as well as devotees.

## For artists & game designers
A recursive nested-triangle cosmogram converging on a single point — a striking sacred-geometry asset and meditation-target motif.

citations.text: Madhu Khanna, *Yantra: The Tantric Symbol of Cosmic Unity* (1979).

title: Taijitu (Yin-Yang Diagram) with Laozi
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Chinese)
summary:
The *Taijitu* ('Diagram of the Supreme Ultimate'), the familiar yin-yang symbol of interlocked light and dark, here paired with an image of Laozi.

## Iconography
A circle is divided by an S-curve into a dark ([[motif:yin yang|yin]]) and a light (yang) field, each carrying a seed-dot of its opposite. The rotational symmetry expresses ceaseless interchange: each pole at its fullest already contains the germ of the other.

## Significance
The *Taiji* ('Supreme Ultimate') is the undivided whole from which yin and yang, and then the 'ten thousand things,' unfold — a cosmogonic principle systematised by the Song Neo-Confucian Zhou Dunyi in his *Taijitu Shuo*. As the master-symbol of Daoist correlative cosmology it underlies the *I Ching*, internal alchemy, medicine, and feng shui. The associated figure of Laozi marks the diagram's place within the Daoist tradition that took the *Daodejing* as scripture.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'balance/duality' icon — instantly legible as paired opposing forces with embedded reversal.

citations.text: Zhou Dunyi, *Taijitu Shuo* ('Explanation of the Diagram of the Supreme Ultimate').

title: Chinese Fú Talisman (Han-dynasty bamboo strip)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Chinese)
summary:
A Chinese *fú* (符) talisman written on an excavated Han-dynasty bamboo slip, an early example of the Daoist charm-script tradition.

## Iconography
The slip bears columns of contorted, quasi-calligraphic graphs — the 'cloud-script' or 'celestial writing' of [[motif:fulu|fú-lù]] talismans — in which characters are deliberately distorted, doubled, or fused into sigils legible to spirits rather than ordinary readers. Such marks command, summon, or repel unseen powers.

## Significance
The *fú* (often paired with *lù*, registers) is the core instrument of Daoist ritual magic: a written contract or command issued in the name of celestial bureaucrats, used to heal, exorcise, protect a household, or empower an adept. Believed to descend from heavenly archetypes, fú talismans were burned, ingested in water, or pasted at thresholds. This excavated example shows the practice already current in the Han, anchoring a tradition that runs unbroken into living Daoist and folk use.

## For artists & game designers
A real ancient 'spell scroll' — distorted glyphs as a commandable script system for an in-world magic language.

title: Four Mandalas of the Vajravali Series (Ngor Monastery)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Tibetan, Tsang; Ngor Monastery, Sakya order)
summary:
A thangka from the *Vajravali* series of the Ngor Monastery (Sakya order), showing four mandalas arranged in a grid on a single painting.

## Iconography
Four complete [[motif:mandala|mandalas]] are set in a two-by-two arrangement, each a four-gated palace within rings of flame and vajra, populated by the deities of a particular tantric cycle. The geometric precision and rich mineral pigments are characteristic of the Ngor workshop's mandala sets.

## Significance
The *Vajravali* ('Garland of Vajras') is a compendium of tantric mandalas compiled by the Indian master Abhayakaragupta, and the Ngor Monastery — a major Sakya seat founded in 1429 — produced celebrated painted sets illustrating its cycles. Multi-mandala thangkas like this functioned as visual indexes to whole liturgical systems, each diagram a palace the initiate could enter in visualisation. They are prime documents of the systematised, encyclopedic side of Tibetan tantra.

## For artists & game designers
Four palace-plans on one sheet — a tileset of mandala variations to mix and recombine.

citations.text: Martin Brauen, *The Mandala: Sacred Circle in Tibetan Buddhism* (1997).

title: Vajrayogini Thangka
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Tibetan, commissioned in Dharamsala)
summary:
A thangka of *Vajrayogini*, the red tantric goddess (*dakini*) central to many Vajrayana meditation practices.

## Iconography
The deity is a vivid red, naked but for bone ornaments, dancing in a ring of flame. She holds a curved flaying-knife (*kartika*) and a skull-cup (*kapala*) brimming with blood, a tantric staff (*khatvanga*) at her shoulder, and treads on prone figures — the standard form of the [[motif:dakini|dakini]] who embodies blissful wisdom.

## Significance
[[motif:vajrayogini|Vajrayogini]] is a 'highest yoga tantra' deity whose practice transmutes desire and the energies of the subtle body into the realisation of emptiness and bliss. As the archetypal *dakini* — a female embodiment of awakened energy — she is a principal meditational deity (*yidam*) in the Sakya, Kagyu, and Gelug traditions. Her fierce, sensual iconography encodes the tantric principle that the passions themselves, skilfully handled, become the path to liberation.

## For artists & game designers
A flame-haloed dancing figure with knife and skull-cup — a complete character design for a fierce-wisdom archetype.

citations.text: Elizabeth English, *Vajrayogini: Her Visualizations, Rituals, and Forms* (2002).

title: Womb World Mandala (Taizōkai), Buddha-in-Lotus detail
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Japanese; Kyōōgokoku-ji / Tō-ji)
summary:
A detail of the central lotus from the *Taizōkai* (Womb World) mandala, the ninth-century Mandala of the Two Realms preserved at Kyōōgokoku-ji (Tō-ji), Kyoto.

## Iconography
The detail shows the heart of the Womb World: the Buddha [[motif:vairocana|Vairocana]] (Dainichi) seated at the centre of an eight-petalled red lotus, each petal bearing a Buddha or bodhisattva. From this 'Central Dais of Eight Petals' the compassion of the cosmic Buddha radiates outward through the mandala's enclosing courts.

## Significance
The *Taizōkai* ('Womb-Store World') is the matrix-half of the Shingon two-world [[motif:mandala|mandala]], representing the principle (*ri*) and compassionate unfolding of reality, complementary to the Diamond World's wisdom. The eight-petalled lotus at its core is among the most reproduced of all Buddhist diagrams, a model of how a single centre generates an ordered cosmos. This Tō-ji set is one of the oldest surviving versions, descending from Kūkai's transmission of esoteric Buddhism to Japan.

## For artists & game designers
The eight-petal lotus with a central lord and eight aspects — a clean radial hub for a skill wheel or pantheon ring.

citations.text: Adrian Snodgrass, *The Matrix and Diamond World Mandalas in Shingon Buddhism* (1988).

title: Diverse Forms of Mahakala and Other Protectors
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Tibetan)
summary:
A Tibetan thangka depicting diverse forms of *Mahakala* and other *dharmapala* (protector) deities, the wrathful guardians of the Buddhist teaching.

## Iconography
Dark-bodied [[motif:wrathful deity|wrathful figures]] crowd the painting: Mahakala in his several forms — multi-armed, fanged, wreathed in flame, crowned with skulls, trampling enemies and brandishing chopper and skull-cup — surrounded by lesser protectors. The terrifying aspect, far from demonic, expresses the ferocity of compassion against ignorance.

## Significance
*Dharmapalas* such as [[motif:mahakala|Mahakala]] guard the Dharma and its practitioners; Mahakala (a Buddhist transformation of the Hindu Shiva-Mahakala) is among the most important protectors across Tibetan, Mongolian, and East Asian Vajrayana, and a special guardian of particular lineages and the Mongol and Manchu courts. The wrathful iconography is read as the enlightened mind's power to destroy obstacles, not as malevolence — a key principle of tantric symbolism.

## For artists & game designers
A full bestiary sheet of flame-wreathed guardian forms — directly usable as boss/protector design reference.

citations.text: Robert Beer, *The Encyclopedia of Tibetan Symbols and Motifs* (1999).

title: Xiuzhen Tu (Chart for the Cultivation of Perfection)
work: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: Anonymous (Chinese, Daoist)
summary:
The *Xiuzhen Tu* ('Chart for the Cultivation of Perfection'), a Daoist anatomical-alchemical diagram detailing the inner organs, channels, and energetic centres used in self-cultivation.

## Iconography
A tall frontal chart of the trunk shows the viscera rendered as palaces and deities, the spinal passage with its named gates, columns of explanatory text, and the circulating routes of breath. Where the *Neijing Tu* is a landscape, the *Xiuzhen Tu* is closer to a labelled medical-mystical anatomy.

## Significance
The chart codifies the inner geography of [[motif:neidan|internal alchemy]], naming the bodily 'palaces' inhabited by tutelary spirits and the pathways along which *qi* is refined toward the embryonic 'immortal' body. It belongs to the same lineage as the *Neijing Tu* and was studied by Catherine Despeux in the foundational scholarly treatment of these body-cosmos diagrams. Such charts united Daoist physiology, ritual, and the quest for transcendence in a single image.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled 'skill-tree of the body' — viscera as upgradeable palaces, each with a resident spirit and a stat.

citations.text: Catherine Despeux, *Taoïsme et corps humain: le Xiuzhen tu* (1994).

title: Weighing of the Heart, Papyrus of Ani
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egyptian scribes and painters)
summary:
The judgement scene from the *Papyrus of Ani*, the most celebrated copy of the Egyptian Book of the Dead, now in the British Museum.

## Iconography
Jackal-headed Anubis weighs the scribe Ani's heart against the feather of Maat on a great balance. Ibis-headed Thoth records the verdict, while the composite monster Ammit — part crocodile, lion and hippopotamus — waits to devour the heart of the unjust. Ani and his wife look on at the left.

## Significance
The *psychostasia* enacts spell 125 of the Book of the Dead, in which the deceased recites the "negative confession" before forty-two assessor gods. The scene is the visual heart of Egyptian funerary belief: the heart (*ib*), seat of conscience, must balance against cosmic order ([[motif:maat feather|Maat]]) for the dead to be reborn. Edited and translated by E. A. Wallis Budge (1895), the Ani papyrus shaped the modern Western image of Egyptian afterlife.

## For artists & game designers
The balance-scale judgement is a ready morality mechanic: weigh a character's deeds against a single feather, with a devourer waiting for failure.

citations.text: E. A. Wallis Budge, *The Book of the Dead: The Papyrus of Ani* (1895).

title: Apotropaic Wand ("Birth Tusk") Fragment (MET 55475)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A fragment of an Egyptian apotropaic wand, or "birth tusk," of carved hippopotamus ivory, in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## Iconography
A curved, flat blade of ivory (following the shape of a hippopotamus canine) incised with a procession of protective beings: Taweret, Bes, lions, snakes, knife-wielding genii and composite demons who ward off danger.

## Significance
These Middle Kingdom "magic knives" were used to draw a protective circle around a mother and newborn — traces of wear at the tips suggest they were dragged across the ground or a bed. The parade of fierce guardians, several gripping knives, formed a frontier no hostile spirit could cross. They are the principal evidence for [[motif:apotropaic magic|domestic protective magic]] around childbirth, studied in detail by Hartwig Altenmüller and Geraldine Pinch.

## For artists & game designers
A wand whose engraved monsters are the spell — a portable bestiary that doubles as a ward — is an evocative magic-item concept.

title: Faience Amulet of the God Bes (Museo Egizio C 615)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A glazed faience amulet of the dwarf-god Bes in the Museo Egizio, Turin.

## Iconography
A squat, frontal dwarf deity with bandy legs, a lion-like bearded face, protruding tongue and a feathered crown. The deliberately grotesque face — meant to frighten away evil — is Bes's signature.

## Significance
Bes was a benevolent household protector, guarding sleepers, women in childbirth and young children against snakes, scorpions and malevolent spirits. Unlike most Egyptian gods he is shown full-face, confronting the viewer (and the threat) directly. His image proliferated on amulets, beds, headrests and mirror handles from the Middle Kingdom onward and survived into Greco-Roman [[motif:apotropaic magic|apotropaic magic]], underpinning the composite Bes-Pantheos figures.

## For artists & game designers
Bes is the archetype of the friendly-grotesque guardian: ugliness as a feature, protection through intimidation.

title: Djed Pillar Amulet (Metropolitan Museum)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A *djed*-pillar amulet in the Metropolitan Museum of Art, one of the core funerary amulets prescribed by the Book of the Dead.

## Iconography
A columnar form crowned by four stacked horizontal bars or capitals. Variously interpreted as a stylised tree-trunk, a bundle of stalks, or — most enduringly — the backbone of Osiris.

## Significance
The [[motif:djed pillar|djed]] hieroglyph means "stability/endurance," and as the spine of Osiris it promised the deceased a stable, re-membered body and resurrection. Book of the Dead spell 155 calls for a gold djed at the throat of the mummy so "you raise up your backbone, O weary-hearted one." The "raising of the djed" was also a major royal festival affirming cosmic order. It is, with the wedjat and the tyet, one of the most frequently buried amulets.

## For artists & game designers
A spine-as-pillar glyph signifying "stability" is a strong icon for a fortification, anchor, or resurrection mechanic.

title: Vignettes from the Greenfield Papyrus (Sheet 12)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egyptian scribes and painters)
summary:
A strip of vignettes from the *Greenfield Papyrus*, the Book of the Dead of the priestess Nestanebetisheru, in the British Museum.

## Iconography
A register of small painted scenes (vignettes) illustrating the spells of the Book of the Dead — deities, the deceased in adoration, and ritual episodes — running above or beside columns of hieratic text.

## Significance
At over 37 metres, the Greenfield Papyrus is the longest known Book of the Dead. Made for a high-ranking woman of the Theban priestly elite around 950 BCE, it is a key witness to the 21st-Dynasty recension of funerary [[motif:book of the dead|spells]], when such papyri were produced for an aristocratic clientele. Its vignettes are an invaluable corpus of Egyptian afterlife iconography, named for Edith Mary Greenfield, who donated it in 1910.

## For artists & game designers
A scrolling band of captioned vignettes is a natural template for an in-world illuminated codex or a side-scrolling ritual sequence.

citations.text: E. A. Wallis Budge, *The Greenfield Papyrus in the British Museum* (1912).

title: Heart Scarab in Egyptian Blue (Museo Egizio C 6016)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A large heart scarab of Egyptian blue in the Museo Egizio, Turin, the type of funerary amulet placed over the mummy's heart.

## Iconography
A carved beetle (a dung-beetle, *Scarabaeus sacer*) with detailed wing-cases and legs; the flat underside of such scarabs was inscribed with a magical spell. Heart scarabs are notably larger than ordinary scarab seals.

## Significance
The heart scarab carried spell 30B of the Book of the Dead, addressing the heart: "do not stand as a witness against me" at the weighing of the heart. By magically silencing the heart's potential self-incrimination, the amulet insured the deceased against failing the judgement. The scarab form itself evoked Khepri, the self-renewing morning sun, making the object a double charm for [[motif:rebirth|rebirth]]. Carol Andrews's catalogue treats it as the funerary amulet par excellence.

## For artists & game designers
An object that literally bribes a character's own conscience to stay silent at judgement is a wonderfully ambiguous talisman.

title: Heart Scarab of Bak-en-Djehuti (Walters 42380)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
The inscribed heart scarab of a man named Bak-en-Djehuti, in the Walters Art Museum, Baltimore.

## Iconography
A carved scarab beetle whose flat base bears a hieroglyphic inscription naming the owner and giving the heart-spell. The naming personalises the otherwise standardised amulet for a specific deceased individual.

## Significance
This scarab carries the version of Book of the Dead spell 30B that begins "O my heart which I had from my mother...," pleading with the heart not to betray its owner before the divine tribunal. Named heart scarabs let Egyptologists tie magical objects to particular biographies and to the spread of the spell across the New Kingdom. The Walters example is a clear teaching specimen of how text and amulet fused into a single funerary safeguard.

## For artists & game designers
Inscribing the owner's name turns a generic protective item into a bespoke relic — a small detail that makes loot feel personal.

citations.text: John H. Taylor (ed.), *Journey through the Afterlife* (2010).

title: Cippus with Pataikos on the Crocodiles (MET 30.8.87)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A magical stela (cippus) in the Metropolitan Museum on which the dwarf-god Pataikos replaces the usual Horus-child standing on the crocodiles, flanked by Isis and Nephthys.

## Iconography
A frontal apotropaic figure stands on two crocodiles, grasping serpents and noxious animals; here the body is the squat form of Pataikos rather than the youthful Horus. The protective goddesses Isis and Nephthys stand to either side, with magical inscriptions covering the field.

## Significance
The substitution of Pataikos shows how flexible the [[motif:horus on the crocodiles|Horus-on-the-crocodiles]] formula was: the protective power lay in the composition — mastery over dangerous creatures — more than in any single deity. Such cippi, poured over with water to heal snakebite, are catalogued among Egyptian healing magic and demonstrate the syncretic crowding of protective gods (Bes, Pataikos, Horus) into one apotropaic image.

## For artists & game designers
A single "slot" in a protective composition that different guardian figures can fill is a neat modular design pattern for ward objects.

citations.text: Heike Sternberg-el Hotabi, *Untersuchungen zur Überlieferungsgeschichte der Horusstelen* (1999).

title: Child Horus Standing on Crocodiles (Healing Stela)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A photograph (Tupper / Boston Public Library) of a Horus-cippus showing the child god triumphant over crocodiles and venomous creatures.

## Iconography
The naked Horus-child, with the side-lock of youth, stands frontally upon two crocodiles. In his outstretched hands he grips serpents, scorpions and other dangerous animals, demonstrating total mastery over the agents of poison.

## Significance
The "Horus on the crocodiles" type (*Horus cippi* or *Horus stelae*) was the standard Egyptian device against snakebite and scorpion sting. Its logic is sympathetic: because the infant Horus survived every venomous threat under Isis's protection, water charged by the inscribed stela could transfer that immunity to a patient. Thousands survive, from monumental stelae to pocket amulets, making it one of the most widespread forms of practical [[motif:healing magic|Egyptian magic]].

## For artists & game designers
The child-who-grips-snakes-and-survives is a compact emblem of immunity — useful for an antidote item or a poison-resistant character sigil.

title: Judgement Scene from the Papyrus of Hunefer
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egyptian scribes and painters)
summary:
The weighing-of-the-heart vignette from the *Papyrus of Hunefer*, a royal scribe of Seti I, held in the British Museum.

## Iconography
Anubis leads Hunefer to the scales and adjusts the balance; the heart is checked against Maat's feather while Thoth notes the result and Ammit crouches beneath. At right, falcon-headed Horus presents the vindicated Hunefer to enthroned Osiris, flanked by Isis and Nephthys.

## Significance
The Hunefer scene is the textbook illustration of [[motif:funerary judgement|Egyptian judgement of the dead]], reproduced in nearly every survey of Egyptian art. It compresses spell 125 of the Book of the Dead into a continuous narrative: trial, verdict and presentation to Osiris, lord of the underworld. The crisp Ramesside draughtsmanship makes it the standard comparandum to the Ani papyrus.

## For artists & game designers
The left-to-right "trial then presentation to the throne" sequence is a clean storyboard for an afterlife judgement cutscene.

citations.text: John H. Taylor (ed.), *Journey through the Afterlife: Ancient Egyptian Book of the Dead* (2010).

title: The Metternich Stela (Cippus of Horus)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt); commissioned by the priest Esatum
date: c. 360–343 BCE (30th Dynasty, reign of Nectanebo II)
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo Eb.hoop). Object public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *Metternich Stela*, the largest and finest of the Horus-cippi, now in the Metropolitan Museum of Art (Fletcher Fund, 1950).

## Iconography
The young god Horus-the-child stands frontally on two crocodiles, grasping serpents, scorpions, a lion and an oryx in his fists, surmounted by the grotesque mask of Bes. The whole surface is densely covered with hieroglyphic spells against the bites of snakes, scorpions and other dangerous creatures.

## Significance
Cippi of Horus were healing devices: water poured over the carved spells absorbed their power and was drunk or applied to neutralise venom. The Metternich Stela preserves a long magical narrative in which Isis heals the poisoned infant Horus, the mythic precedent guaranteeing the cure. Studied by C. E. Sander-Hansen and central to Egyptian medical-magical practice, it shows [[motif:healing magic|magic and medicine]] as one continuous craft.

## For artists & game designers
A carved stone whose inscriptions "charge" poured water is a vivid, tactile healing-item mechanic.

citations.text: C. E. Sander-Hansen, *Die Texte der Metternichstele* (1956).

title: Scarab Engravings, *The Sacred Beetle* (1902)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: John Ward (author/illustrator of the treatise)
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons / Internet Archive.
summary:
An illustration from John Ward's *The Sacred Beetle: A Popular Treatise on Egyptian Scarabs in Art and History* (1902), documenting types of scarab.

## Iconography
A printed antiquarian plate showing rows of scarabs in profile and underside view, recording the variety of forms, inscriptions and designs of these beetle amulets.

## Significance
Ward's treatise was part of the turn-of-the-century antiquarian enthusiasm that turned the scarab into the emblem of ancient Egypt for Western collectors. Such typological plates both popularised the [[motif:scarab|scarab]] and fed the Egyptian-revival and reception interest that later flowed into occult and Theosophical circles. The image is thus a useful witness to how Egyptian magical objects were catalogued, reproduced and re-enchanted in the modern era.

## For artists & game designers
A period engraved "catalogue plate" of magical objects is a handy in-world artefact: a collector's reference sheet the player can consult.

title: Scarab Seal of Imeni (Walters 42.21)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A steatite scarab seal naming a man called Imeni, in the Walters Art Museum, Baltimore.

## Iconography
A small beetle carved in the round on top, its flat underside engraved with a name and decorative design for use as a stamp seal. Pierced lengthwise, it could be strung as a ring or pendant.

## Significance
The scarab was simultaneously seal, amulet and sign: its dung-beetle form embodied Khepri, the self-generating sun, so even a humble administrative seal carried connotations of [[motif:rebirth|rebirth]] and good fortune. Middle Kingdom name- and title-scarabs like Imeni's document the merging of bureaucratic identity with protective magic. Scarab seals are among the most abundant Egyptian artifacts and a backbone of dating and prosopography for Egyptologists.

## For artists & game designers
An object that is at once an ID badge, a lucky charm and a signature stamp compresses identity, magic and authority into one tiny prop.

title: Taweret Figure Vase with Human Head (MET)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A figure-vase in the form of the goddess Taweret (here with a human head) in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## Iconography
Taweret combines the body of a pregnant hippopotamus standing upright, the limbs and paws of a lion, the back and tail of a crocodile, and pendulous breasts and belly; this vessel substitutes a human head for the usual hippo head. She often rests a paw on the *sa* hieroglyph of protection.

## Significance
[[motif:taweret|Taweret]] ("the Great One") was, with Bes, the chief protector of women in pregnancy and childbirth and of infants. Her fearsome hybrid form channelled the danger of the hippopotamus into a guardian against the demons that threatened mother and child. Figure vases like this likely held milk or protective liquids used in birth ritual, fusing object and deity into a single magical implement.

## For artists & game designers
Taweret models the "dangerous animal turned protector" archetype: terrifying anatomy repurposed to guard the vulnerable.

title: Red Jasper Tyet Amulet (Knot of Isis, MET)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A red jasper *tyet* amulet — the "Knot of Isis" or "Blood of Isis" — in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## Iconography
A looped knot resembling the ankh but with its two side-arms folded downward, like a sash tied at the waist. The deep red of jasper or carnelian was the prescribed material.

## Significance
The [[motif:tyet|tyet]] is closely paired with the djed: where the djed gives Osirian stability, the tyet invokes the protective power of Isis. Book of the Dead spell 156 requires a tyet of red jasper at the mummy's throat so that "the power of Isis is the protection of this great one." Its red colour was read as the blood of Isis, a potent safeguarding fluid. The tyet, djed and wedjat together form the standard amuletic triad of Egyptian burial.

## For artists & game designers
Material-specified magic (it only works in *red* stone) is a clean crafting constraint: the colour is part of the spell.

title: Faience Overseer Ushabti (Metropolitan Museum)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A glazed faience "overseer" *ushabti* in the Metropolitan Museum of Art — a magical servant figure for the afterlife.

## Iconography
A small mummiform (or, as an overseer, kilted and whip-bearing) figurine of bright faience, inscribed with a spell. Overseer ushabtis are shown in the dress of the living to command the gangs of worker ushabtis.

## Significance
Ushabtis ("answerers") were animated by spell 6 of the Book of the Dead: when the deceased was called to labour in the [[motif:field of reeds|Field of Reeds]], the figurine would answer "Here I am!" and do the work in their stead. Wealthy burials held hundreds — ideally 365 workers plus 36 overseers. They are the clearest case of Egyptian magic as labour-saving technology, substituting an enchanted proxy for the owner's own toil.

## For artists & game designers
A figurine that answers a summons and works on your behalf is a ready-made minion or familiar mechanic, complete with overseer hierarchy.

title: Faience Wedjat-Eye Amulet (Museo Egizio C 1108)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A glazed faience *wedjat*-eye amulet — the Eye of Horus — in the Museo Egizio, Turin.

## Iconography
The stylised falcon's eye combines a human eyebrow and lid with the markings of a lanner falcon: the curling "teardrop" and the spiral below the eye. It is among the most instantly recognisable of all Egyptian symbols.

## Significance
The [[motif:wedjat|wedjat]] ("the whole / sound one") embodies the eye Horus lost in battle with Seth and had restored by Thoth — hence it signifies healing, wholeness and protection. Worn by the living and laid on mummies (often over the embalming incision), it was the commonest of all Egyptian amulets. Its six geometric parts were later read as fractions in scribal arithmetic, linking magic to measure.

## For artists & game designers
The wedjat is a near-universal "protection" glyph; its restored-from-injury backstory makes it a natural icon for a healing or shield power-up.

title: Pectoral with Winged Scarab (Museo Egizio C 6850)
work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A pectoral in the form of a winged scarab, in the Museo Egizio, Turin, of the type sewn onto the mummy's chest wrappings.

## Iconography
A central scarab beetle spreads broad outstretched wings to either side, often pushing or framing a solar disk. The winged scarab is a single composite emblem of the reborn sun.

## Significance
The scarab pushing the sun across the sky personified Khepri, "he who comes into being," the morning aspect of the sun-god and a guarantee of daily renewal. Placed as a winged pectoral over the heart, it transferred that promise of [[motif:rebirth|solar rebirth]] to the deceased. Winged scarab pectorals are among the most spectacular pieces of Egyptian funerary jewellery, fusing precious craft with potent resurrection symbolism.

## For artists & game designers
The winged-scarab-and-sun is a clean heraldic device for a faction of rebirth or dawn; it reads instantly even at small scale.

title: Emblem 'Potentissimus affectus amor' (Alciato, 1531)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Andrea Alciato; woodcut by Hans Schäufelin after Jörg Breu the Elder
summary:
A page from the first edition of Andrea Alciato's *Emblematum liber* (Augsburg, 1531), the book that founded the entire emblem genre.

## Iconography
The leaf carries the canonical three-part emblem structure: a Latin **motto** (*Potentissimus affectus amor*, 'love, the most powerful passion'), a **woodcut** picture, and an explanatory verse **epigram**. The image shows winged **Cupid** mastering wild beasts, the visual proof that love subdues even the strongest creatures.

## Significance
Alciato's collection assembled short Latin epigrams to which the Augsburg printer Heinrich Steyner attached woodcuts, accidentally inventing the [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|emblem]] as a fixed form of *motto + picture + poem*. The book ran to hundreds of editions across Europe and made the encoding of a moral or abstract idea in a decodable picture a central Renaissance art. Every later emblem book — sacred, amorous, Hermetic — descends from it.

## For artists & game designers
The three-slot template (title / image / caption) is a ready-made card layout: a single icon that 'means' a virtue, vice, or power, with motto and flavour text built in.

citations.text: P. M. Daly, *Literature in the Light of the Emblem* (1998).

title: Alciati Emblemata cum commentariis (Padua, 1621) — illustrated page
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Andrea Alciato, with commentary by Claude Mignault and Lorenzo Pignoria
summary:
A page from the great annotated *Alciati Emblemata cum commentariis*, the heavily glossed Paduan edition that turned Alciato's slim emblem book into a vast scholarly encyclopedia.

## Iconography
An engraved **emblem** picture sits within dense columns of humanist **commentary** by Claude Mignault (Minois) and Lorenzo Pignoria, surrounded by sources, parallels and moral exposition. The image proper has shrunk to a small framed scene embedded in a sea of learned text.

## Significance
By the early 17th century the [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|Alciato]] corpus had accreted layer upon layer of commentary, treating each emblem as a riddle whose hidden meaning required scholarly decoding — the very [[motif:hieroglyph-and-hidden-meaning|theory of the hidden image]] in action. This edition shows the emblem as object of erudite interpretation, midway between poetry and the Hermetic 'hieroglyphics' of Valeriano.

## For artists & game designers
A model for layered lore: a small symbolic image surrounded by expanding tiers of annotation, where the picture is the key and the text is the unlockable codex entry.

citations.text: A. Alciato, *Emblemata cum commentariis Claudii Minois* (Padua, 1621).
D. Russell, *Emblematic Structures in Renaissance French Culture* (1995).

title: Symbolicarum Quaestionum libri quinque (Bocchi, 1574) — engraved symbol
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Achille Bocchi; engravings by Giulio Bonasone (after Prospero Fontana)
summary:
An engraved 'symbol' from Achille Bocchi's *Symbolicarum Quaestionum libri quinque*, one of the most intellectually ambitious and Hermetic of all Renaissance emblem books.

## Iconography
A finely engraved allegorical scene by **Giulio Bonasone** is paired with a Latin **symbolum** and verse, the whole posed as a *quaestio* — a learned 'question' whose meaning the reader must resolve. The figures and attributes are arranged as a deliberate visual puzzle of Neoplatonic and moral ideas.

## Significance
Bocchi, founder of a Bolognese academy, conceived his 150 'symbolic questions' as serious philosophical play (*quas serio ludebat*), steeped in [[motif:hieroglyph-and-hidden-meaning|hieroglyphic]] and Hermetic thought of the kind associated with the Egyptian wisdom of Hermes Trismegistus. The Bonasone plates are among the finest emblem engravings of the century and place Bocchi at the esoteric, Neoplatonic end of the [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|emblem]] tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A gold standard for 'cryptic device' art: tightly composed allegorical engravings that present as solvable riddles rather than illustrations.

citations.text: E. See & others on Bocchi's academy; cf. M. Watson, *Achille Bocchi and the Emblem Book as Symbolic Form* (1993).

title: Frontispiece to Robert Fludd's Utriusque Cosmi Historia (1617)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Robert Fludd; engraved frontispiece (Theodore de Bry workshop, Oppenheim)
summary:
The engraved title page of Robert Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi... Historia* (1617–19), the encyclopedic Hermetic work on the 'two worlds', macrocosm and microcosm.

## Iconography
The **allegorical frontispiece** frames the title in an architectural portal flanked by personified figures and emblematic devices that announce the book's theme: the correspondence of the great world (**macrocosm**) and the little world of man (**microcosm**), bound by cosmic harmony.

## Significance
Fludd, English physician and Rosicrucian apologist, produced through the De Bry press at Oppenheim a series of the most spectacular [[motif:allegorical-frontispiece|occult title pages]] of the age. The frontispiece is itself a Hermetic emblem writ large: the whole argument of the book compressed into one decodable image, exemplifying the [[motif:hieroglyph-and-hidden-meaning|theory of the hidden image]] applied to the title page of a printed book.

## For artists & game designers
The architectural title-portal: an ornate frame whose personifications and symbols pre-load the entire 'world' the reader is about to enter — a splash-screen that is also a thesis.

citations.text: J. Godwin, *Robert Fludd: Hermetic Philosopher and Surveyor of Two Worlds* (1979).

title: Hadrianus Junius, Emblemata (1565) — illustrated emblem
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Hadrianus Junius (Adriaen de Jonghe); printed by Christopher Plantin
summary:
An emblem from the *Emblemata* of Hadrianus Junius (Antwerp, 1565), a learned humanist contribution to the genre printed by the great Plantin press.

## Iconography
A neatly engraved **device** is set beneath a Latin **motto** and above a Neo-Latin **epigram**, in the classic Alciatan arrangement. Junius's images are compact allegorical conceits, each unfolding a moral or philosophical commonplace.

## Significance
Junius, a Dutch physician and polymath, was among the humanist authors who, in the generation after [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|Alciato]], expanded the emblem into an instrument of erudite display. Issued by **Christopher Plantin**, his *Emblemata* belongs to the dense Netherlandish production of the 1560s (alongside Sambucus) that made Antwerp the European capital of the printed emblem and refined the picture-as-hidden-meaning into a polished scholarly art.

## For artists & game designers
A clean, repeatable emblem template from the genre's classic period — useful as a reference for period-accurate device design and layout.

citations.text: C. Heesakkers, in *The Emblem in Renaissance and Baroque Europe* (1993).

title: Plate from Khunrath's Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae (1595/1609)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Heinrich Khunrath
summary:
An engraved plate from Heinrich Khunrath's *Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae* ('Amphitheatre of Eternal Wisdom'), among the most celebrated theosophical-alchemical emblem plates of the era.

## Iconography
The large circular composition stages an emblematic 'amphitheatre' of divine **wisdom**: figures, inscriptions, and ritual or laboratory apparatus arranged so that the whole functions as a meditative diagram uniting prayer and alchemical work (*ora et labora*).

## Significance
Khunrath's plates — most famously the 'oratory-laboratory' — fuse Christian piety, Paracelsian [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] and proto-Rosicrucian [[motif:rosicrucian|theosophy]] into single image-arguments. They are emblems on a monumental scale, where each inscription and figure is a station in a contemplative ascent toward eternal wisdom, exemplifying how the [[motif:allegorical-frontispiece|allegorical plate]] could serve as a tool of esoteric meditation.

## For artists & game designers
A full circular 'temple of knowledge' tableau: a single ornate scene that doubles as a map of stations to be visited in order.

title: Frontispiece of Athanasius Kircher's Oedipus Aegyptiacus (1652)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Athanasius Kircher; engraved frontispiece
summary:
The allegorical frontispiece of Athanasius Kircher's *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* (Rome, 1652–54), his vast attempt to 'decipher' Egyptian hieroglyphs as a hidden Hermetic theology.

## Iconography
The **frontispiece** marshals Egyptianizing emblems — obelisks, sphinxes, personified figures and hieroglyphic devices — around the title, casting Kircher as the new **Oedipus** who will solve the riddle of Egyptian wisdom just as the original solved the Sphinx's.

## Significance
Kircher believed the **hieroglyphs** encoded a secret philosophy descending from [[motif:hermetic|Hermes Trismegistus]], and his work is the grand Baroque monument to the Renaissance [[motif:hieroglyph-and-hidden-meaning|theory of the hidden image]] — the conviction that pictures could carry esoteric truths legible only to initiates. His decipherments were almost wholly wrong, but the frontispiece perfectly embodies the dream of the readable sacred symbol.

## For artists & game designers
The 'cipher-puzzle world' announced visually: obelisks and sphinxes as promise of a script to be cracked — the title page as the game's central riddle in miniature.

citations.text: D. Stolzenberg, *Egyptian Oedipus: Athanasius Kircher and the Secrets of Antiquity* (2013).

title: Frontispiece to Burton's Anatomy of Melancholy (1638)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Robert Burton; engraved frontispiece by Christian Le Blon (Christof Le Blon)
summary:
The famous emblematic frontispiece to Robert Burton's *The Anatomy of Melancholy* (1638 edition), a title page that is itself a chart of the melancholic temperament.

## Iconography
The **frontispiece** is divided into compartments, each an emblem of a melancholic type — Jealousy, Solitude, the Lover, the Hypochondriac, the superstitious and the maniac — flanked by the planet **Saturn** above and the figures of Democritus and the author below, with explanatory verses ('The Argument of the Frontispiece').

## Significance
Burton's title page is a model of the [[motif:allegorical-frontispiece|emblematic frontispiece]]: a grid of personified states that diagrams the whole book's subject before a word of text. It ties **melancholy** to its astral governor [[motif:saturn|Saturn]] in the tradition analysed by Klibansky, Panofsky and Saxl, presenting a 'hidden image' of the diseased soul that the reader decodes compartment by compartment.

## For artists & game designers
A gridded character-select screen avant la lettre: each panel a distinct 'type' with its own emblem, framed by the governing planet and presiding sages.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

title: Quarles, Emblems Divine and Moral (1736 ed.) — engraved emblem
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Francis Quarles (after William Marshall / Boetius à Bolswert designs)
summary:
An engraved emblem from Francis Quarles's *Emblems, Divine and Moral* (here the 1736 edition; first published 1635), the most popular English emblem book.

## Iconography
A religious **emblem** pairs an engraved scene — often the soul (*Anima*) and Divine Love (the child Christ) acting out a spiritual conceit — with a scriptural motto and a meditative verse. The picture renders an inward, devotional truth as an outward, decodable image.

## Significance
Quarles adapted his designs from Continental Jesuit emblem books (the *Pia Desideria* of Hugo and the *Typus Mundi*), Protestantizing them for an English audience. *Emblems* became a household devotional classic, carrying the [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|emblem]] tradition deep into English religious culture and demonstrating the form's power to make the soul's hidden states visible. The full title's 'hieroglyphicks of the life of man' explicitly invokes the [[motif:hieroglyph-and-hidden-meaning|hidden-image]] theory.

## For artists & game designers
The soul as a recurring little protagonist: an inner spiritual journey externalized as a sequence of symbolic vignettes.

citations.text: F. Quarles, *Emblems, Divine and Moral* (London, 1635; 1736 ed.).

title: Personification of 'Academy' (Ripa tradition, Wellcome plate)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Anonymous (after Cesare Ripa's Iconologia)
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraved plate (Wellcome Collection) personifying **Academy** in the manner of Ripa's *Iconologia*, illustrating the allegory of organized learning.

## Iconography
A female **personification** of Academy is shown with attributes signifying study, the liberal arts and disciplined enquiry — books, instruments, and emblematic devices that mark her as the embodiment of an institution of learning rather than a single abstract virtue.

## Significance
The figure belongs to the long afterlife of Cesare Ripa's [[motif:personification-of-abstractions|Iconologia]], whose method of clothing abstractions in fixed attributes was copied and extended in countless later engraved series. 'Academy' personified shows how the system reached beyond classical virtues to embody the very institutions of Renaissance and early-modern intellectual life, including those (academies, *studia*) that nurtured Hermetic and natural-philosophical pursuits.

## For artists & game designers
Proof that the Ripa kit scales to institutions, not just virtues: a guild, college, or order can be given a single allegorical avatar with its own attribute set.

title: Ripa's Iconologia (1603) — illustrated page of personifications
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Cesare Ripa
summary:
A page from the 1603 illustrated *Iconologia* of Cesare Ripa, the standard handbook for personifying abstract ideas in Western art.

## Iconography
A woodcut **personification** stands with its diagnostic **attributes** — the objects, animals, postures and colours by which an abstraction (a virtue, art, passion, or place) is to be recognized — beneath a heading and an explanatory description of why each attribute is chosen.

## Significance
Ripa's *Iconologia* (text 1593, illustrated 1603) codified the visual vocabulary of [[motif:personification-of-abstractions|allegory]]: for two centuries painters, sculptors and designers of frontispieces consulted it to give bodily form to Wisdom, Melancholy, Alchemy, the Elements, the Liberal Arts and countless other concepts. It is the indispensable key to reading the personified figures that crowd Baroque ceilings, title pages and occult emblems.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'attribute kit' for allegory: look up an abstraction and get the exact props and colours that will make it legible at a glance.

citations.text: E. Mandowsky, *Ricerche intorno all'Iconologia di Cesare Ripa* (1939).

title: Personification of Africa (Ripa's Iconologia, 1603)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Cesare Ripa
summary:
The personification of **Africa** from Cesare Ripa's *Iconologia* (1603), a characteristic example of how Ripa embodies an abstraction through fixed attributes.

## Iconography
Africa appears as a woman wearing the famous **elephant-head** headdress and accompanied by attributes such as a scorpion, a lion or serpent, and a cornucopia — the signs by which Ripa's reader was to identify the continent and its supposed qualities of heat and wildness.

## Significance
The four continents were among the most-copied of Ripa's [[motif:personification-of-abstractions|personifications]], and his Africa-with-elephant-headdress became the canonical formula, repeated on fountains, ceilings and title pages across Baroque Europe (most famously by Tiepolo). The figure shows the *Iconologia*'s method exactly: an abstract idea (a continent) made into a readable body through a checklist of emblematic attributes.

## For artists & game designers
A textbook 'faction personified' design: one figure whose headdress and companions instantly encode a whole region's identity.

citations.text: S. Poeschel, *Studien zur Ikonographie der Erdteile* (1985).

title: Emblem 'Non dolo sed virtute' (Sambucus, Emblemata)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Joannes Sambucus (János Zsámboky)
motifs: not by guile but by virtue
summary:
The emblem *Non dolo sed virtute* ('not by guile but by virtue') from the *Emblemata* of Joannes Sambucus, a leading humanist emblematist of the 1560s.

## Iconography
The **device** illustrates the motto *Non dolo sed virtute* — a scene contrasting honest valour with trickery — accompanied by an epigram drawing the moral that worthy ends should be won by virtue and not by deceit.

## Significance
Sambucus, a Hungarian humanist working in the orbit of the Plantin press, produced one of the most reprinted [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|emblem books]] of the century, valued for its classical learning and elegant Latin. Emblems such as this typify the genre's ethical core: a memorable picture fixing a moral maxim in the mind, the *hidden* lesson made legible through image and motto.

## For artists & game designers
A clean maxim-emblem: a single contrast-scene that locks an aphorism to an image — the model for a 'proverb card' system.

citations.text: A. S. Q. Visser, *Joannes Sambucus and the Learned Image* (2005).

title: Tabula Cebetis (Carta Vitae) — allegorical print (detail)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum print
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A detail (upper right corner) of an engraved *Tabula Cebetis* (*Carta Vitae*, 'map of life'), the allegorical picture-diagram of human existence (Rijksmuseum).

## Iconography
The print lays out the **allegory of life** as nested **enclosures**: souls enter the gate of life past **Fortune** and Deceit, traverse the regions of false and **true education**, and climb toward the citadel of Happiness, each personification labelled so the whole reads as a walkable moral map.

## Significance
The *Tabula Cebetis*, a Greek dialogue of the 1st century CE ascribed to a follower of [[motif:pythagoras|Pythagoras]], describes a votive panel mapping the soul's pilgrimage. Rediscovered and endlessly printed in the Renaissance, it became a favourite emblematic subject uniting Pythagorean-Platonic ethics with [[motif:hermetic|Hermetic]] and Stoic teaching — the supreme example of the picture that is also a readable map of the inner life.

## For artists & game designers
Literally a level map of life: nested rings of trials and false paths leading to a final citadel — an allegory that already is a game board.

citations.text: *Tabula Cebetis* (Pseudo-Cebes), ed. J. T. Fitzgerald & L. M. White (1983).

title: 'Ghewensten Strijdt / Combat Heureux' (Vaenius, Amorum Emblemata, 1608)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Otto van Veen (Vaenius)
summary:
An emblem from Otto van Veen's *Amorum Emblemata* (Antwerp, 1608), the influential picture-book of secular love-emblems.

## Iconography
The engraving shows winged **Cupid** (Amor) acting out a conceit of love — here a 'happy combat' (*Ghewensten Strijdt / Combat Heureux*) — beneath mottoes given in several languages, with verses expanding the amorous lesson.

## Significance
Vaenius, Rubens's teacher, produced in his *Amorum Emblemata* the model secular [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|emblem book]] of love, in which Cupid enacts the paradoxes of desire across a hundred plates. The work was hugely popular and helped spread the emblem form into vernacular and amorous registers across Europe; it stands beside his moral *Emblemata Horatiana* as the secular wing of the tradition that Alciato founded.

## For artists & game designers
A cohesive 'recurring mascot' device: a single character (Cupid) re-posed across dozens of plates to dramatize an abstract theme, with multilingual captioning.

citations.text: K. Porteman & M. B. Smits-Veldt, *Een nieuw vaderland voor de muzen* (2008).

title: Moral emblem 'Socrates and Xanthippe' (Vaenius tradition)
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Otto van Veen (Vaenius)
summary:
A moral emblem of **Socrates and Xanthippe** by Otto van Veen, illustrating a philosophical lesson in patience drawn from the Neo-Stoic emblem tradition.

## Iconography
The philosopher **Socrates** endures the rage of his wife **Xanthippe**, who in the familiar anecdote drenches him with water; Socrates remains calm. The scene serves as the picture of an emblem teaching forbearance — that after thunder (scolding) comes the downpour, met with equanimity.

## Significance
Vaenius's moral emblem books, above all the *Emblemata Horatiana*, translated the ethics of Horace and the Stoics into [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|picture-mottoes]] for a wide readership. The Socrates-and-Xanthippe exemplum was a stock image of the wise man's imperturbability, showing how the [[motif:emblem-motto-picture-epigram|emblem]] form carried not only love and Hermetic mystery but practical ethical instruction by visual anecdote.

## For artists & game designers
A compact 'virtue lesson' scene: a single famous anecdote staged so its moral (patience) reads instantly — useful as illustrative loading-screen wisdom.

citations.text: M. Praz, *Studies in Seventeenth-Century Imagery* (2nd ed., 1964).

title: Valeriano's Hieroglyphica (1556) — illustrated page
work: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: Pierio Valeriano (Giovanni Pierio Valeriano Bolzani)
summary:
A page from Pierio Valeriano's *Hieroglyphica, sive De sacris Aegyptiorum literis commentarii* (1556), the great Renaissance encyclopedia of symbolic meaning.

## Iconography
A woodcut **symbolic image** (typically an animal, object, or natural sign) accompanies commentary explaining its hidden significations, drawn from ancient authors and from the supposed 'sacred letters' (*sacrae literae*) of the Egyptians. Each entry treats a thing as a sign with layered meanings.

## Significance
Valeriano systematized the Renaissance belief, founded on the recovery of [[motif:hieroglyph-and-hidden-meaning|Horapollo]], that the world is a book of symbols and that the Egyptian **hieroglyph** was a perfect picture-language of ideas. His vast *Hieroglyphica* supplied artists, emblematists and Hermetic philosophers with a reference work of decodable images, and stands directly behind the theory that an abstract concept can be carried by a sacred picture.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made symbol dictionary: each creature or object keyed to a meaning, ideal for a consistent iconographic language across a project.

citations.text: K. Giehlow, *The Humanist Interpretation of Hieroglyphs in the Allegorical Studies of the Renaissance* (Eng. trans. 2015).

title: The Monas Hieroglyphica (John Dee)
work: John Dee & Enochian Magic
creator: John Dee
summary:
The **Monas Hieroglyphica** — the single composite glyph **John Dee** unveiled in his cryptic 1564 treatise, claiming it encoded the unity of the cosmos.

## Iconography
A built-up sigil: the **crescent moon** over the **circle-and-point of the sun**, set on a **cross** (the four elements), with the horned sign of **Aries** (fire) at the base. Dee derives it across 24 'theorems' as the root from which every planetary and alchemical sign can be drawn.

## Significance
The Monas fuses [[tradition:hermetic|Hermetic]], alchemical, astrological, and [[tradition:kabbalah|Kabbalistic]] symbolism into one mark — a claim that all the cosmos springs from a single hieroglyph. It is the intellectual seed of Dee's later angelic ('Enochian') work.

## For artists & game designers
A self-contained 'master sigil' — clean line-art that reads as the ultimate arcane rune.

citations.text: John Dee, *Monas Hieroglyphica* (Antwerp, 1564); trans. C. H. Josten, *Ambix* 12 (1964).

title: Portrait of John Dee (Ashmolean Museum)
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Unidentified painter (English school)
summary:
Oil portrait of the English mathematician, astrologer and magus John Dee (1527–1608/9), held by the Ashmolean Museum, Oxford, and long regarded as the standard likeness of the man who attempted to converse with angels.

## Iconography
Dee is shown in old age, half-length, against a dark ground: a gaunt face with a long white beard, a black scholar's skullcap and a plain white ruff over dark robes. The image radiates sober gravity rather than occult drama — the costume is that of a learned Tudor gentleman and natural philosopher. A later tradition holds that a ring of skulls once surrounded the figure and was painted out, a [[motif:memento mori|memento mori]] consistent with the sitter's reputation.

## Significance
Dee was the most learned Englishman of his generation — adviser to Elizabeth I, author of the *Monas Hieroglyphica*, owner of the greatest private library in England — yet from 1582 he devoted himself to the "angelic conversations" recorded with Edward Kelley, the seed of the Enochian system. This portrait fixes the public memory of the [[motif:renaissance magus|renaissance magus]] who bridged mathematics, navigation and ceremonial magic. Modern scholarship (Deborah Harkness, *John Dee's Conversations with Angels*, 1999) reads the angelic project as natural philosophy by other means.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive "wise old magus" reference: skullcap, white beard, ruff, austere palette — instantly legible as a Renaissance scholar-mage without a single occult prop.

citations.text: Deborah E. Harkness, *John Dee's Conversations with Angels* (Cambridge UP, 1999).

title: John Dee (line engraving by Francis Cleyn, 1658)
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Francis Cleyn (Franz Klein)
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A line engraving of John Dee by Francis Cleyn, dated 1658 — produced half a century after Dee's death, in the decade that saw his angelic diaries finally reach print.

## Iconography
The bust portrait, often set within an oval, repeats the established Dee type: aged scholar, long beard, skullcap and ruff, rendered now in the crisp parallel hatching of mid-seventeenth-century engraving. As a print it was designed for reproduction and circulation in books rather than for a wall.

## Significance
The date is telling: in 1659 Meric Casaubon published *A True & Faithful Relation of What Passed for Many Years Between Dr John Dee... and Some Spirits*, the first printing of the angelic conversations, intended to discredit Dee as a deluded enthusiast. Engraved likenesses such as Cleyn's belong to this moment of renewed, sceptical public interest, when Dee became a cautionary figure in the debate over [[motif:angelic conversation|spirit communication]] and credulity. The portrait thus marks the transition of Dee from living philosopher to printed legend.

## For artists & game designers
A period-accurate "engraved frontispiece" asset — ideal for a discovered book or grimoire whose author's face stares out from the title page in hatched black line.

title: John Dee's gold disc (Sigillum Aemeth fitting), British Museum
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Anonymous goldsmith (for John Dee)
rights: CC0. Photograph by Vassil, British Museum. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A small engraved gold disc associated with John Dee's angelic practice, now in the British Museum — distinct from the large wax *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* discs, and engraved with a vision Kelley reported in the crystal.

## Iconography
The thin gold roundel is incised with a schematic scene: a central cross or arrangement of figures within a circle, recording one of the visions "shown" to Edward Kelley in the [[motif:scrying crystal|shew-stone]] — traditionally identified with the four watchtowers or castles seen during the angelic sessions. The metalwork is fine and the engraving deliberately diagrammatic rather than pictorial.

## Significance
Dee's surviving ritual apparatus in the British Museum — the obsidian mirror, the crystal, the wax seals and this gold disc — constitutes the rare case of a Renaissance magical practice preserved as physical objects. The disc materialises the principle that the angels did not merely speak but *displayed* structured images to be copied down, the visual logic that produced the Enochian tablets. It connects the abstract diagrams to a tangible, precious instrument fit for a [[motif:seal of god|seal of God]].

## For artists & game designers
A luxe quest-object: an engraved gold talisman whose incised diagram is itself a clue — the picture on the disc is a map of somewhere the player must go.

citations.text: British Museum, *John Dee's gold seal/disc* (collection object).

title: Letter in the hand of John Dee, 1588
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: John Dee
summary:
An autograph letter written by John Dee in 1588, during or just after the years of his continental travels with Edward Kelley among the courts of the Holy Roman Empire and Poland.

## Iconography
A single leaf of laid paper covered in Dee's tight Elizabethan secretary hand, closing with his signature. The document is a piece of working correspondence rather than a magical text, but it presents the magus through the most intimate evidence of all — his own handwriting.

## Significance
1588 was a pivotal year: the famous "cross-matching" episode, in which the angels reportedly instructed Dee and Kelley to share their wives, had strained the partnership, and Dee was preparing his return to an England transformed by the Armada. Surviving autographs anchor the angelic diaries (the *Libri Mysteriorum*, today Sloane MSS in the British Library) to a real, datable hand, and let palaeographers test which manuscripts are genuinely Dee's. The letter is a reminder that the [[motif:renaissance magus|magus]] was also a courtier, diplomat and tireless writer of business.

## For artists & game designers
A convincing "handwritten document" prop: dense secretary hand and a flourished signature read instantly as an authentic period letter discovered among a scholar's papers.

citations.text: Glyn Parry, *The Arch-Conjuror of England: John Dee* (Yale UP, 2011).

title: Portrait of John Dee (Wellcome Collection)
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
