creator: Unidentified artist (after the Ashmolean type)
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A portrait of John Dee preserved in the Wellcome Collection, London, one of several derivatives of the canonical Ashmolean likeness that carried Dee's face into the history of medicine and science.

## Iconography
Dee appears half-length and frontal, an old man with a flowing white beard, the black skullcap of a scholar and a white ruff at the throat, set against a plain dark ground. The treatment is restrained and documentary, presenting him as a grave physician–philosopher rather than a conjuror.

## Significance
The Wellcome holds extensive material on Dee because his reputation straddles medicine, astrology and magic; his casting of horoscopes, his work on calendar reform and his angelic experiments were all of a piece in the Elizabethan understanding of natural knowledge. The survival of multiple portrait versions testifies to the long fascination with the man whom later ages cast as the model wizard. On the angelic side his great monument is the *Libri Mysteriorum* — the diaries of his sessions with [[motif:angelic conversation|the angels]].

## For artists & game designers
Use alongside the Ashmolean portrait to show how a single "face of the magus" propagates through copies — handy for a lore item that exists in several slightly different painted versions.

title: The Holy Table (*Mensa Sancta*), *A True & Faithful Relation*
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: John Dee (engraved for Meric Casaubon's edition)
summary:
The design of the Holy Table (*Mensa Sancta*), the consecrated table on which Dee and Kelley performed the angelic actions, as printed in Meric Casaubon's *A True & Faithful Relation* (London, 1659) from Dee's own records of 1582–83.

## Iconography
A large square table-top bordered on all four edges by a continuous band of [[motif:enochian script|Enochian (angelic) letters]]. Within the border are set further characters and, at the centre, a grid or compartmented square; in practice the wax *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* rested at the centre and smaller seals supported the legs. The whole is a working diagram: every glyph had to be reproduced exactly for the table to function.

## Significance
The Holy Table is the physical stage of Enochian magic. Its border script was among the first deliverances of the angels, and the table organised the apparatus — seals, shew-stone, candles — into a single sanctified field. Casaubon printed these diagrams to expose Dee, but in doing so created the source from which the nineteenth-century [[motif:ceremonial magic|Golden Dawn]] reconstructed the system. It is the architectural plan of the angelic experiments.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made ritual-circle floor: a square bordered in alien script with a seal at its heart — drop props onto the marked positions and the table becomes an interactive summoning device.

title: Edward Kelley (coloured aquatint)
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Anonymous (coloured aquatint)
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A coloured aquatint of Edward Kelley in the Wellcome Collection, a richer, tinted counterpart to the line and stipple portraits of Dee's scryer.

## Iconography
Kelley is shown half-length as a robed, bearded figure in a skullcap, the soft aquatint ground washed with colour to give warmth and depth. As with every Kelley likeness the features are conjectural; the colouring serves to make the alchemist vivid and collectible rather than to record a true face.

## Significance
The survival of Kelley in line engraving (Cooper), antiquarian print (Pennant) and coloured aquatint shows how thoroughly the print trade mythologised the pair. Kelley's alchemical fame — the claim that he transmuted metals before the court of [[motif:reception|Rudolf II]] in Prague — kept him in demand among collectors of "famous adepts," independent of the angelic work. The coloured plate is the most decorative phase of that afterlife.

## For artists & game designers
A tinted character portrait ready for a card frame — the colour palette (warm robes, shadowed ground) is a usable reference for an alchemist NPC.

title: Edward Kelley (stipple engraving by R. Cooper)
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Richard Cooper (stipple engraving)
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A stipple-engraved portrait of Edward Kelley by Richard Cooper, in the Wellcome Collection — a refined nineteenth-century likeness of Dee's scryer and alchemical partner.

## Iconography
A bust portrait, usually within an oval, shows Kelley as a bearded man in a close skullcap, gazing aside. The soft tonal dots of [[motif:reception|stipple engraving]] lend the face a smooth, almost sentimental finish far removed from any documented Elizabethan source; like all Kelley portraits it is essentially invented, since no life-portrait survives. Tradition held that Kelley's ears had been cropped as punishment for forgery, and some images allude to the skullcap that hid them.

## Significance
Edward Kelley (1555–1597/8) was the medium who actually "saw" in the shew-stone while Dee recorded; without his visions the Enochian system would not exist. His shadowed biography — convicted coiner, alchemist, knighted and then imprisoned by Rudolf II — made him irresistible to print-shops cataloguing famous magi. This portrait belongs to the antiquarian and biographical literature (Thomas Pennant, the alchemical compilers) that kept the [[motif:scryer|scryer]]'s memory alive.

## For artists & game designers
A polished "wanted-poster" likeness for the roguish medium of the pair — pair it with the necromancy print to show the gap between elegant portrait and lurid legend.

citations.text: Wellcome Collection, *Edward Kelley*, stipple engraving by R. Cooper (V0003198).

title: Edward Kelley Raising the Dead (Sibly engraving)
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Ebenezer Sibly (engraving)
summary:
An engraving from Ebenezer Sibly's *A New and Complete Illustration of the Occult Sciences* (London, 1780s–90s) depicting Edward Kelley raising the dead — the image that fixed Kelley's reputation as a necromancer (German captions title it *Totenbeschwörung*).

## Iconography
In a moonlit churchyard two cloaked figures stand within a protective [[motif:magic circle|magic circle]] inscribed with names and characters; one holds a torch or wand and an open book while a shrouded corpse rises stiffly from an opened grave before them. Tombs, a ruined church and night sky complete the gothic setting. The scene is wholly imaginary — an eighteenth-century fantasy projected back onto the Elizabethan scryer.

## Significance
Though Kelley's documented work with Dee was angelic, not funerary, this print made him the archetype of the graveyard necromancer and became one of the most reproduced images of Renaissance magic. It marks the [[motif:reception|reception]] of Dee and Kelley by the Romantic occult revival, which preferred lurid sorcery to the tedious tabulation of angelic names. Sibly's engraving is the visual ancestor of every later "two magicians and a rising corpse" illustration.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical necromancy tableau: circle, torch, book, open grave, rising shroud — a complete spell-scene composition you can restage almost beat for beat.

citations.text: Ebenezer Sibly, *A New and Complete Illustration of the Occult Sciences* (London, c. 1790).

title: Edward Kelly, Prophet or Seer to Dr Dee
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: After Thomas Pennant
summary:
An antiquarian print labelled "Edward Kelly, Prophet or Seer to Dr Dee," associated with the collections of the Welsh antiquary Thomas Pennant — an image that names Kelley precisely by his function in the partnership.

## Iconography
A bust portrait of a bearded man in a skullcap, captioned with his name and the telling title "prophet or seer to Dr Dee." The plate sits among eighteenth-century antiquarian portraits of notable Britons; its lettering does the interpretive work, fixing Kelley's identity as the [[motif:scryer|seer]] of the pair rather than as an independent magus.

## Significance
The caption captures the division of labour that defined Enochian magic: Dee, the learned director, posed questions and wrote everything down; Kelley, the gifted (and suspect) medium, reported what appeared in the crystal. Calling him "prophet or seer" — with that hedging "or" — also registers the perennial doubt over whether Kelley genuinely scryed or fabricated his visions, a question argued from his own century to ours. The print belongs to the antiquarian [[motif:reception|reception]] that historicised the duo.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled NPC card: the same bearded face, but the caption assigns a role — "Seer" — turning a portrait into a class identity for the party's medium.

citations.text: Thomas Pennant (antiquarian collection); see Glyn Parry, *The Arch-Conjuror of England* (2011).

title: John Dee Memorial Plaque, St Mary the Virgin, Mortlake
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Modern memorial (photograph by Robert Smith)
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0. Photo by Robert Smith. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A memorial plaque to John Dee in the church of St Mary the Virgin, Mortlake, where the magus was buried after his death in 1608/9 — the only fixed monument to a man whose grave itself is lost.

## Iconography
An inscribed commemorative plate mounted in the parish church, naming Dee and recording his association with Mortlake. The plaque is modern; Dee's actual tomb and gravestone have not survived, so the memorial stands in for a vanished burial.

## Significance
Mortlake on the Thames was Dee's home and the site of his celebrated library — the largest in Elizabethan England, with thousands of volumes and scientific instruments — to which scholars, courtiers and the queen herself came. It was here that much of the angelic work was conducted and here that, during his absence abroad, his collection was plundered. The plaque marks the geographical anchor of Dee's life and the [[motif:reception|continuing local memory]] of the "Mortlake conjuror."

## For artists & game designers
A quiet "end of the trail" location: a church plaque that confirms a legendary figure was a real, buried person — good for a reflective late-game discovery.

citations.text: William H. Sherman, *John Dee: The Politics of Reading and Writing in the English Renaissance* (1995).

title: John Dee House, Mortlake High Street
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Photograph by Philip Jeffrey
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0. Photo by Philip Jeffrey. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A house on Mortlake High Street associated with John Dee, marking the riverside site of his home and famous library on the Thames west of London.

## Iconography
A street-level photograph of a building on Mortlake High Street linked by local tradition to Dee. The structure is unremarkable domestic architecture; its significance is entirely commemorative, pinning a legendary household to an actual address.

## Significance
Dee's Mortlake house was an intellectual powerhouse of Elizabethan England: it held his vast library, his alchemical laboratories and his instruments, and it was here, in the early 1580s, that he and Edward Kelley began the [[motif:angelic conversation|angelic conversations]] over the shew-stone. The library was a resource of national importance, consulted on navigation, calendar reform and natural philosophy, and its dispersal during Dee's continental years was a real loss to English learning. The surviving site keeps the [[motif:reception|memory]] of where the Enochian system was first received.

## For artists & game designers
A hub location reference: the unassuming exterior of the magus's house — the contrast between mundane street frontage and the wonders worked inside is a usable narrative beat.

citations.text: William H. Sherman, *John Dee: The Politics of Reading and Writing in the English Renaissance* (1995).

title: The Order of the *Inspirati*, *A True & Faithful Relation*
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: John Dee (engraved for Meric Casaubon's edition)
summary:
A diagram titled "The Order of the *Inspirati*" from Casaubon's *A True & Faithful Relation* (1659), one of the structured tables by which Dee organised the angelic names and hierarchies received through Edward Kelley.

## Iconography
The plate is a ruled chart: rows and columns of names, letters and titles arranged to display rank and relationship among the angelic and spirit beings of the system. Latin headings frame compartments filled with the transliterated angelic names, the inspired ones (*inspirati*) set in their proper order. It is information design, not pictorial art — a taxonomy of invisible powers.

## Significance
The angelic conversations did not deliver loose oracles but an entire administrative cosmos: governors, kings and ministering angels each with a name, a seal and a sphere of authority. Charts like this one show Dee the mathematician imposing order on revelation, tabulating heaven as he might tabulate a star catalogue. Such tables of [[motif:angelic hierarchy|angelic hierarchy]] became the backbone of later Enochian practice, supplying the names invoked in the calls of the Aethyrs.

## For artists & game designers
A lore codex page: a grid of named entities with ranks and sigils — perfect as a discoverable "bestiary of angels" the player decodes one row at a time.

title: Order of Emperor Rudolf II for the Arrest of Edward Kelley
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: Imperial chancery of Rudolf II
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A document signed by the Holy Roman Emperor Rudolf II ordering the arrest of Edward Kelley, preserved in the Wellcome Collection — the bureaucratic record of the scryer's spectacular fall in Bohemia.

## Iconography
A leaf of formal chancery script bearing the imperial signature and authority, its weight conveyed by layout and subscription rather than by image. As a state paper it stands in sharp contrast to the fanciful portrait prints: this is the real administrative trace of a magician's life.

## Significance
After Dee returned to England in 1589, Kelley remained on the Continent and won the patronage of Rudolf II of Prague — emperor, melancholic and the greatest collector-patron of [[motif:alchemy|alchemy]] of his age — even being knighted on the strength of his promised transmutations. When the gold failed to materialise, favour turned to imprisonment; Kelley is said to have died after a fall while attempting escape. This order documents the dangerous economy of court alchemy, where a failed adept could be jailed at imperial command. It closes the historical arc of the Dee–Kelley partnership.

## For artists & game designers
A "bounty warrant" prop with real stakes: an imperial arrest order for your alchemist NPC, signed and sealed — a quest hook the moment the player finds it.

citations.text: R. J. W. Evans, *Rudolf II and His World* (Oxford, 1973).

title: Angelic Chart from *A True & Faithful Relation*
work: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: John Dee (engraved for Meric Casaubon's edition)
summary:
One of the lettered charts reproduced in Casaubon's *A True & Faithful Relation* (1659), recording a grid of characters dictated to Dee and Kelley during the angelic actions.

## Iconography
A squared field subdivided into many small cells, each holding a single letter or character. The reader is meant to extract names and words from the grid by prescribed paths — across rows, down columns, or along marked diagonals. This is the form in which much of the angelic language and the Enochian tablets were delivered: as letter-by-letter dictation into a matrix.

## Significance
The great tables of Enochian magic — the four elemental watchtowers and the unifying Tabula Recensa — are precisely such lettered squares, from which the names of governing angels are read out to compose the "calls." Charts like this preserve the raw deliverance before interpretation, showing how revelation arrived as structured data. The slow, error-prone copying of these grids (and the angels' insistence on exactness) is a recurring drama of the [[motif:angelic conversation|conversations]]. The same combinatorial logic links Dee's project to the [[motif:enochian script|Kabbalistic letter-mysticism]] he knew.

## For artists & game designers
A cipher puzzle in object form: a letter-grid whose hidden words the player must trace along the right path to unlock an angel's name.

title: Portrait of John Dee (the Ashmolean Portrait)
work: Portraits of the Adepts
creator: Anonymous (16th-c. English school)
rights: Public domain. Ashmolean Museum, Oxford; via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The best-known likeness of **[[creator:john-dee]]** (1527–1608/9) — Elizabethan mathematician, astrologer, and angel-summoner — painted late in his life and now in the Ashmolean Museum, Oxford. Dee is shown in the dark gown and skullcap of a scholar, his beard white, an austere and slightly haunted presence.

## Why it matters
Dee is the hinge between Renaissance science and ceremonial magic: author of the *Monas Hieroglyphica*, adviser to Elizabeth I, and (with Edward Kelley) recorder of the **Enochian** angelic system. A portrait of the magus himself anchors the human side of the archive — the faces behind the diagrams.

## For artists & game designers
A textbook "Renaissance magus / court astrologer" reference: black gown, white ruff, skullcap, grave expression. Ideal for an NPC scholar, a wizard advisor, or a portrait-in-a-frame asset.

[PLACEHOLDER: the painting was later trimmed; some versions show a border of skulls — note if you source that state.]

title: The air-thermometer of the *Philosophia Moysaica*
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; Gouda printing
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving of Fludd's **air-thermometer** (weather-glass) from the *Philosophia Moysaica* (1638).

## Iconography
A glass instrument with an air-filled head over a liquid column stands graduated against a scale; as the trapped **air** expands and contracts with heat and cold, the liquid moves. The plate ties the apparatus to Fludd's cosmic physics of expansion and contraction.

## Significance
Fludd's thermoscope is one of the most-cited early thermometers. He read it cosmologically: the swelling of the air demonstrates the active, dilating principle of [[motif:divine-light|light]] against the contracting dark. The device sits at the junction of experimental instrument and Hermetic demonstration.

## For artists & game designers
The same prop as the *Mosaicall* weather-glass in an earlier, higher-resolution state — good reference for a richly engraved scientific instrument.

citations.text: F. Sherwood Taylor, 'The Origin of the Thermometer', *Annals of Science* (1942).

title: Frontispiece of *Anatomiae Amphitheatrum* (1623)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; Frankfurt printing
repository: Biblioteca Europea di Informazione e Cultura (BEIC)
summary:
The engraved frontispiece (*antiporta*) of Fludd's *Anatomiae Amphitheatrum* (Frankfurt, 1623).

## Iconography
An **anatomical amphitheatre** is presented as an architectural frame for the title — the theatre of dissection reimagined as a stage on which the spiritual as well as physical anatomy of man is displayed.

## Significance
Fludd's *Amphitheatrum* is not ordinary anatomy but a **spiritual and cosmic anatomy of the microcosm**, treating the body's structure as a map of the soul's faculties and the cosmos's powers. It belongs to the same project as his memory theatre — knowledge displayed as architecture.

## For artists & game designers
An anatomy theatre repurposed as a 'stage of the soul' is a strong setting for an introspective or surgical level.

title: The anatomy of the eye (*De Naturae Simia*, optics)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An **anatomy of the eye** from the optics section of Fludd's *De Naturae Simia* (1618).

## Iconography
The **eye** is shown in section with its coats and humours, together with the geometry of **vision** — rays entering and forming images. The plate sits among Fludd's treatments of perspective and optics in the 'technical history' of the macrocosm.

## Significance
For Fludd, the eye is the bodily instrument of [[motif:light|light]] and so a privileged microcosmic organ: the same divine light that creates the world is received and ordered by sight. His optics blends practical perspective with the metaphysics of illumination.

## For artists & game designers
A period eye-section paired with ray geometry is solid reference for a 'vision/perception' upgrade or a light-puzzle.

title: Arithmetica memorialis: the memory of numbers
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram of *arithmetica memorialis* — the art of memorising numbers — from Fludd's *De Naturae Simia* (1618).

## Iconography
The plate lays out a **mnemonic scheme for numbers**, associating digits and figures with memorable images or places so that calculations and quantities can be held in the mind. It belongs to the same memory-art apparatus as the *Oculus imaginationis* and the theatre.

## Significance
Fludd extended the classical [[motif:art-of-memory|art of memory]] into arithmetic and music, treating mnemonics as a universal technique for compressing knowledge into images. These plates document the early-modern conviction that the trained imagination could store the encyclopaedia of the arts.

## For artists & game designers
A system that turns numbers into images is a ready puzzle mechanic — encode a combination as a remembered picture.

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *The Art of Memory* (1966).

title: The brain and the inner senses according to Fludd
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: de Bry edition, Utriusque Cosmi Historia]
summary:
Fludd's diagram of the **brain and its inner senses**, mapping the mental faculties onto the chambers of the head.

## Iconography
The head is sectioned to show the seat of the **common sense**, **imagination**, **cogitation/reason** and **memory** in the cerebral ventricles, with channels linking the outer senses inward. Worlds and faculties are labelled in the Fluddian manner.

## Significance
This is the medical-psychological companion to the great threefold-soul plate: the medieval **cell doctrine** of brain function (sensus communis, imaginativa, memorativa) absorbed into Fludd's [[motif:microcosm|microcosmic]] scheme, where each inner sense answers to a level of the cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
A cutaway brain labelled by faculty is a clean reference for a 'mental stats' diagram or a neuro-fantasy interface.

title: Catoptrum meteographicum: the meteorological mirror
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; Frankfurt workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *catoptrum meteographicum* ('meteorological mirror') from Fludd's *Philosophia Sacra* (1626).

## Iconography
A diagram mapping the **winds, clouds and weather** of the sublunary air, organising meteorological phenomena into a mirror-like scheme of correspondences. Directions, qualities and exhalations are arranged around a central axis.

## Significance
Fludd's *Philosophia Sacra* develops a **Mosaic meteorology** — a theory of weather drawn from Genesis rather than Aristotle, in which the winds and waters obey the same divine light-and-darkness physics as the cosmos at large. The plate is a key document of his 'sacred philosophy' of nature.

## For artists & game designers
A wind-and-weather correspondence wheel is a usable HUD for a game where climate is read like an oracle.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: The palm of the hand: chiromancy
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A **chiromancy** (palmistry) plate showing the lines and mounts of the hand, from *De Praeternaturali Utriusque Mundi Historia* (1621).

## Iconography
The open **palm** is mapped with its major **lines** (life, head, heart) and the **planetary mounts** at the base of each finger (the mount of Jupiter, Saturn, Apollo, Mercury, the Moon, Venus, Mars). The hand becomes a small astrological chart.

## Significance
Fludd's [[motif:chiromancy|chiromancy]] integrates palmistry into his macro–microcosm system: the planets that rule the heavens are written into the flesh of the hand, so reading the palm is reading the stars' imprint on the individual. It is among the most fully Hermetic of the early-modern palmistry treatments.

## For artists & game designers
A palm whose mounts are named for the planets is a self-contained 'read your fate' diagram for a fortune-telling interaction.

title: Fludd's colour wheel
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: Fludd, Utriusque Cosmi Historia / Anatomiae Amphitheatrum]
summary:
Robert Fludd's **colour wheel**, one of the earliest circular arrangements of colour, derived from his physics of light and darkness.

## Iconography
Colours are set in a **ring** running between **white** (light) and **black** (darkness), the intermediate hues generated by the proportional mixture of the two extremes. The diagram makes colour a function of the same light–dark contest that drives Fludd's cosmos.

## Significance
Fludd's wheel is a landmark in the prehistory of **colour theory**: rather than treating colours as arbitrary, he orders them on a scale from light to dark, anticipating later circular colour systems. It ties his optics directly to his cosmology of [[motif:light-and-darkness|light and darkness]].

## For artists & game designers
A colour wheel literally built from 'light vs dark' is an evocative palette device for a game whose whole aesthetic is a war of brightness and shadow.

title: The dark chaos before creation (*Et sic in infinitum*)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The celebrated 'dark chaos' page from *De metaphysico macrocosmi ortu* in Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617).

## Iconography
A solid **black rectangle** fills the page, its four edges inscribed *Et sic in infinitum* ('and so on to infinity'). It depicts the state **before** creation: the boundless, lightless prime matter or void out of which God will call the worlds.

## Significance
Often cited as a startlingly modern image, Fludd's black field is a serious cosmological statement — the *materia prima* as pure unformed darkness, the negative ground against which divine [[motif:light-and-darkness|light]] will act. It is among the most reproduced single images in the history of esoteric printing.

## For artists & game designers
A black page captioned 'and so on to infinity' is a masterclass in saying everything with nothing — perfect for a 'before the world' loading screen.

title: The faculties of man (*De Praeternaturali Utriusque*)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram of the **faculties of man** from *De Praeternaturali Utriusque Mundi Historia*, the second volume of Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*.

## Iconography
The plate sets out the human powers — **vegetative, sensitive and rational** — and their organs, frequently branching as a tree or ladder from the body up to the divine intellect, with the spirits (*spiritus*) mediating between body and soul.

## Significance
In this 'preternatural' volume Fludd treats the powers that lie between the natural and the supernatural in man. The faculties-plate shows how the human soul forms a graded bridge from matter to God — the ascent that the whole *microcosm* is built to make.

## For artists & game designers
A branching tree of vegetative-to-rational powers maps neatly onto a tiered ability tree from instinct to intellect.

title: Portrait of Robert Fludd
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Engraved by Balthasar Moncornet
summary:
An engraved **portrait of Robert Fludd** (1574–1637), English physician and Hermetic philosopher, by Balthasar Moncornet.

## Iconography
A bust-length author **portrait** in a framed cartouche, Fludd shown in the sober dress of a learned physician, with an inscription naming him and his titles. The format is the standard frontispiece-portrait of a seventeenth-century *philosophus*.

## Significance
Fludd, an Oxford-trained doctor and Fellow of the College of Physicians, was the great English systematiser of [[motif:hermeticism|Hermetic]], Paracelsian and Rosicrucian thought. He defended the Rosicrucian manifestos, debated Kepler, Mersenne and Gassendi, and produced in the de Bry volumes the most lavishly illustrated occult philosophy of the age.

## For artists & game designers
A dignified period author-portrait is the perfect 'mentor/loremaster' face for the figure whose books your game's cosmology is built on.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: Forma Theatri: the form of the memory theatre
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *Forma Theatri* ('form of the theatre') from the *Ars Memoriae* in Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*.

## Iconography
The plate shows a **theatre stage** with a tiered façade, doors and a forestage — an architectural building meant to be filled with memory-images. The reader is to walk this remembered stage to recall ordered knowledge.

## Significance
Frances Yates argued the *Forma Theatri* reflects the **public playhouse** of Fludd's London (possibly the Globe) and is crucial evidence in the reconstruction of the Elizabethan stage. As a mnemonic device it embodies the classical 'memory palace' tradition raised to Hermetic ambition: the theatre as a model of the cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
A literal stage you populate with vivid figures to store knowledge is a directly playable mechanic — a 'memory palace' minigame.

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *The Art of Memory* (1966).
Frances A. Yates, *Theatre of the World* (1969).

title: Hostilis munimenti typus: the fortress besieged by disease
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; Frankfurt printing
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *Hostilis munimenti typus* ('figure of the hostile fortification') from Fludd's *Integrum Morborum Mysterium* (1631).

## Iconography
The plate diagrams the human body as a **fortress** ringed by **bastions and defences**, beset by the assaults of disease. Hostile celestial and demonic forces lay siege from without while the vital powers man the walls within.

## Significance
Fludd's siege-emblem is the visual core of his **military model of pathology**: sickness is invasion, the physician a commander, health the successful defence of the microcosmic citadel against malign macrocosmic influence. It is among the most striking images in early-modern medical illustration.

## For artists & game designers
The body literally drawn as a star-fort under attack is a directly playable diagram for a defend-the-organism game.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: Geomancy: the figures of the earth-divination
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: de Bry edition, De Naturae Simia, De Geomantia]
summary:
A geomancy plate from the *De Geomantia* section of Fludd's *De Naturae Simia* (*Utriusque Cosmi Historia*).

## Iconography
The plate presents the **sixteen geomantic figures** — patterns of single and double dots (Puer, Amissio, Via, Populus and the rest) — and the apparatus for casting and reading them: the random marks made in earth or on paper, reduced to figures and assigned to the houses of an astrological chart.

## Significance
Fludd was the most important early-modern Latin systematiser of [[motif:geomancy|geomancy]], the art of divining from random earth-marks. His treatment links the sixteen figures to the planets, signs and astrological houses, giving the technique a full cosmological scaffolding within his Hermetic encyclopaedia.

## For artists & game designers
Sixteen dot-figures cast at random and read against a chart is a self-contained divination minigame with real historical rules.

citations.text: Robert Fludd, *De Geomantia* in *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617–18).

title: The interaction between God and matter
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: de Bry edition, Utriusque Cosmi Historia]
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A plate depicting the interaction between God and matter from Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*.

## Iconography
Divine power — figured as a **cloud, name or radiant source** above — acts upon the world below. Rays or a descending influence link the supreme principle to the receptive material sphere, dramatising how the formless is shaped by the divine.

## Significance
The meeting of God and matter is the central event of Fludd's cosmogony: the active divine light informing passive [[motif:prime-matter|prime matter]]. Fludd often signals the unnameable God by the Hebrew Tetragrammaton set in a cloud, refusing any direct image of the deity.

## For artists & game designers
An unseen power above acting on raw matter below is the archetypal 'creation' composition — top-down divine influence made literal.

title: The hierarchy of angels (*De Integra Microcosmi Harmonia*)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram of the **hierarchy of angels** from *De Integra Microcosmi Harmonia*, Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*.

## Iconography
The **nine angelic orders** — Seraphim, Cherubim, Thrones, Dominions, Virtues, Powers, Principalities, Archangels, Angels — are arranged in tiers descending from the divine, each order governing a level of the cosmos and a corresponding power in man.

## Significance
Fludd adopts the **Pseudo-Dionysian celestial hierarchy** and threads it through his macro–microcosm scheme: the angelic orders are the channels by which divine influence descends to the planetary spheres and to the human soul. Angelology becomes part of his physics of mediation.

## For artists & game designers
Nine ranked angelic orders, each tied to a sphere, is a ready faction ladder for a celestial bureaucracy.

title: Nature abhors a vacuum
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A plate illustrating that **'nature abhors a vacuum'**, from the technical/physical section of Fludd's *De Naturae Simia* (1618).

## Iconography
An apparatus — vessels, tubes and columns of **water and air** — demonstrates how nature resists empty space, liquid rising or holding to prevent a void. Nature may be personified as the agent that 'fears' the vacuum.

## Significance
The *horror vacui* was a central doctrine of pre-mechanical physics, and Fludd uses such pneumatic demonstrations within his cosmology of the contest between expansive and contractive principles. These experiments connect to his weather-glass and to the great seventeenth-century debates on the void.

## For artists & game designers
A period 'nature fears emptiness' apparatus is good set-dressing for an alchemist's bench or a pressure puzzle.

title: Title page of *Integrum Morborum Mysterium* (1631)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; Frankfurt printing
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The engraved title page of Fludd's *Integrum Morborum Mysterium* ('The Complete Mystery of Diseases', 1631), the first part of his great medical treatise.

## Iconography
An emblematic frontispiece frames the title with images of the **cosmic combat of health and disease** — the wholesome and the hostile influences that besiege the body. It introduces Fludd's military metaphor of the body as a fortress under attack.

## Significance
*Integrum Morborum Mysterium* sets out Fludd's **iatromathematical medicine**: disease as the invasion of the human [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]] by malign celestial winds and spirits, healing as the restoration of cosmic harmony. It contains the celebrated 'fortress of health' diagram.

## For artists & game designers
A medical book that frames the body as a besieged fortress is a perfect premise for a tower-defence health system.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: Light in darkness: the first act of creation
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A creation diagram, 'light in darkness', from *De metaphysico macrocosmi ortu* in Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617).

## Iconography
The plate shows divine **light** breaking into and dividing the primordial **darkness** — the first day of Genesis rendered as a diagram of penetrating radiance against a field of black. Concentric or radiating forms mark how light orders the formless deep.

## Significance
Fludd's cosmogony makes the contest of [[motif:light-and-darkness|light and darkness]] the engine of creation: God's light enters the dark abyss (the *aqua* or chaos) and from their proportion the worlds are formed. This polarity underlies his famous twin pyramids of form and matter.

## For artists & game designers
Literal light cutting into a black void is the cleanest possible 'genesis' visual — a single high-contrast image for a creation cutscene.

title: The macrocosm: the cosmic spheres of *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: de Bry edition, Utriusque Cosmi Historia]
summary:
A small diagram of the macrocosm — the 'great world' — from Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*.

## Iconography
Concentric rings descend from the divine and empyrean heaven through the planetary [[motif:celestial-spheres|spheres]] to the sublunary world of the four elements at the centre. The whole is a compact picture of the geocentric, hierarchically ordered universe that Fludd took as the pattern for everything below it.

## Significance
Fludd's cosmology is emphatically **Mosaic and Hermetic** rather than Copernican: a layered cosmos emanating from God, in which light descends and darkness rises. Such ring-diagrams are the skeleton on which his grander allegories (the monochord, *Integra Naturae*) are built.

## For artists & game designers
A clean nested-rings cosmos is the most legible possible 'world map' for a game whose levels are heavens to ascend through.

title: Fludd on magnetism: the magnetic cross of the cosmos
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; Frankfurt/Gouda printing
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram of Fludd's **doctrine of magnetism**, expounded in his Mosaic philosophy.

## Iconography
The plate maps **poles, axes and lines of attraction** as a cross or grid of magnetic influence running through the cosmos. Directions of sympathy and antipathy are labelled, the lodestone's behaviour generalised into a universal law of cosmic pull.

## Significance
Fludd built a whole **magnetic cosmology**: all things are governed by attraction toward the divine north (light) and repulsion from darkness, the lodestone being a visible token of the world-soul's sympathies. His magnetic and weapon-salve writings drew him into famous polemics with Mersenne and the mechanical philosophers.

## For artists & game designers
A cosmos crossed by visible magnetic lines of sympathy and antipathy is a tidy systems-design metaphor for factions that attract or repel.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: Man, the ape and the chain of imitation
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A plate showing **man, the ape and the chain of imitation** from *De Praeternaturali Utriusque Mundi Historia* (1621).

## Iconography
The figure links **God, Nature, man and the ape** in a descending series, each imitating the one above: Nature apes God, man apes Nature, and the ape (figuring human art) apes man. Lines or a chain connect the tiers.

## Significance
This is the doctrinal kernel behind Fludd's famous *Ape of Nature* engraving: a hierarchy of [[motif:imitation|imitation]] running down the chain of being. Art is a copy of a copy, valuable precisely because, far down the chain, it still reflects the divine pattern.

## For artists & game designers
A visible 'chain of imitation' from god to ape is a neat way to justify a crafting system where every made thing is a degraded echo of an ideal.

title: The Vitruvian macrocosm: man inscribed in the cosmic circle
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: de Bry edition, Utriusque Cosmi Historia]
summary:
A plate from Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* showing the human figure spread within a great circle, the body set into correspondence with the surrounding cosmos.

## Iconography
In the manner of the Vitruvian figure, a man stands with limbs extended to touch a circle — but here the circle is the **cosmos**, its rings the elements and heavens. The proportions of the body are presented as the same proportions that govern the world, so that the human frame becomes a measured map of the universe.

## Significance
This is the visual heart of Fludd's doctrine: *homo* as [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]], the little world that repeats the great. Fludd inherited the squared-circle man from Vitruvius and Agrippa and recharged it with Hermetic and Paracelsian meaning — every organ answering to a planet, every part to a region of the sky.

## For artists & game designers
The figure literally diagrams 'as above, so below' — ideal iconography for a character sheet where body zones map to cosmic powers.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin, *Robert Fludd: Hermetic Philosopher and Surveyor of Two Worlds* (1979).

title: Oculus imaginationis: the eye of imagination (Ars Memoriae)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: de Bry edition, Utriusque Cosmi Historia, Ars Memoriae]
summary:
The *Oculus imaginationis* ('eye of the imagination') from the *Ars Memoriae* section of Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617).

## Iconography
A single great **eye** represents the inner faculty of imagination in which memory-images are seen. In Fludd's art of memory, the mind builds a theatre of vivid places and figures viewed by this inward eye.

## Significance
Fludd's **art of memory** is the subject of Frances Yates's most famous chapters: she argued his memory 'theatre' echoed the Globe playhouse and embodied the Hermetic claim that the trained imagination can mirror the whole cosmos. The eye is the threshold between outer world and inner [[motif:memory-theatre|memory theatre]].

## For artists & game designers
A giant disembodied eye standing for 'the place where you imagine' is a haunting interface metaphor for a memory or dream level.

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *The Art of Memory* (1966).

title: The mental faculties of man
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A plate of the **mental faculties of man** from the *Tomus secundus* (microcosm volume) of Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*.

## Iconography
The diagram arranges the powers of the soul — sense, imagination, reason, intellect and memory — in ordered relation, often radiating from or stacked within the human head, each tied to a corresponding region of the cosmos.

## Significance
One of several closely related Fluddian psychology plates, it expresses his core thesis that the [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]] of the mind replicates the macrocosm: the same hierarchy of worlds that structures the universe structures human cognition.

## For artists & game designers
Use alongside the threefold-soul and brain plates as a coherent visual set for a faculties-of-mind progression system.

title: The metaphysical spheres of the macrocosm
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: de Bry edition, De metaphysico macrocosmi ortu, 1617]
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram from the tract *De metaphysico macrocosmi ortu* (on the metaphysical origin of the great world) in Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*.

## Iconography
Nested spheres rise from the material world to the metaphysical and divine, each level labelled in Latin. The figure charts the **descent of being** from God through the intelligible and celestial worlds into matter — and, read upward, the soul's return.

## Significance
The *De metaphysico* opens Fludd's whole system by deriving the cosmos from God's act. It belongs with the Neoplatonic ladder of emanation: a structured hierarchy through which divine [[motif:divine-light|light]] flows downward into ever grosser substance.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled ladder of worlds is ready-made as a progression tree — each sphere a tier of ascent.

title: The weather-glass (thermoscope) from the *Mosaicall Philosophy*
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; London printing
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving of Fludd's **weather-glass** (thermoscope) from the English *Mosaicall Philosophy* (London, 1659).

## Iconography
The instrument is a tall glass with a bulb and a column of **water**, marked with a scale; the rise and fall of the liquid registers changes in the surrounding **air** — heat, cold and the 'spirit' of the weather. The plate shows the device and the cosmic forces it is meant to reveal.

## Significance
Fludd used the weather-glass not merely as a meteorological gauge but as proof of his **two-principle physics**: the contest of expansive heat (the divine *Nort*/light) and contracting cold (darkness) made visible in a tube. It is among the earliest depicted air-thermoscopes and a touchstone in histories of the thermometer.

## For artists & game designers
An instrument that claims to make an invisible cosmic war (heat vs cold) visible in a glass tube is a beautiful diegetic 'detector' prop.

citations.text: F. Sherwood Taylor, 'The Origin of the Thermometer', *Annals of Science* (1942).

title: The Ape of Nature (*De Naturae Simia*)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The famous **Ape of Nature** plate from Fludd's *De Naturae Simia seu Technica Macrocosmi Historia* (the technical history of the great world), part of *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*.

## Iconography
A great wheel arranges the human **arts and sciences** — arithmetic, geometry, music, optics, art of war, fortification, the art of memory, cosmography, astrology, geomancy and more — around a central figure. An **ape** (art), chained to **Nature**, who is in turn linked to God, sits among them: *ars* imitates *natura* as the ape imitates the human.

## Significance
The plate is the table of contents of Fludd's entire 'technical' volume and the emblem of his theory of art: human skill is the [[motif:ape-of-nature|ape of Nature]], Nature the ape of God. It is one of the most reproduced images of Renaissance encyclopaedism.

## For artists & game designers
An ape chained between Nature and God, ringed by every art, is a perfect skill-tree hub: each spoke a discipline to master.

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *The Art of Memory* (1966).

title: Engraved title page of *De Naturae Simia* (1618)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The engraved title page of *De Naturae Simia seu Technica Macrocosmi Historia*, the second tract of the first volume of Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (Oppenheim, 1618).

## Iconography
An architectural title frame presents the work as the 'technical history of the macrocosm' — the survey of the human arts by which man imitates the world. The design is in the ornate de Bry manner.

## Significance
This volume contains Fludd's celebrated treatments of the [[motif:art-of-memory|art of memory]], music, the cosmic monochord, fortification, perspective and machines — the practical, image-rich heart of the *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* and the source of many of his most copied plates.

## For artists & game designers
A frontispiece announcing 'the technical history of the great world' is an apt cover for an in-game compendium of crafts.

title: Frontispiece of the *Philosophia Moysaica* (1638)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; published Gouda
summary:
The frontispiece of Fludd's posthumously published *Philosophia Moysaica* (Gouda, 1638), the Latin basis of the English *Mosaicall Philosophy* (1659).

## Iconography
An emblematic title design declares a philosophy whose 'cornerstone' is Christ, deriving the wisdom of creation from Moses. The image program announces a physics read out of the Book of Genesis.

## Significance
The *Philosophia Moysaica* is Fludd's final statement of **Mosaic philosophy** — the claim that true natural knowledge is contained in Scripture, especially the creation account. It transmits his doctrine of the two principles (light and dark, *Nort* and the divine wind) and his magnetic and weather-glass experiments to a later English readership.

## For artists & game designers
A deathbed book that claims all science is hidden in one sacred text is a strong quest-artifact premise.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: Title page of Fludd's *Philosophia Sacra* (1626)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; Frankfurt workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The engraved title page of Robert Fludd's *Philosophia Sacra et vere Christiana seu Meteorologia Cosmica* (Frankfurt, 1626).

## Iconography
Within a framed cartouche the long Latin title announces a 'sacred and truly Christian philosophy, or cosmic meteorology'. The design follows the sober, text-dominated frontispiece style of Fludd's later Frankfurt volumes.

## Significance
*Philosophia Sacra* is Fludd's systematic **meteorology grounded in Scripture**, opposing Aristotle and the new mechanists with a Genesis-based physics of wind, water and weather. It belongs to the polemical late phase in which Fludd defended his Mosaic philosophy against Mersenne, Gassendi and Kepler.

## For artists & game designers
A frontispiece naming a whole alternative physics ('cosmic meteorology') is good worldbuilding texture for an in-game heretical treatise.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: Universale pulsuum systema: the universal system of the pulse
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; Frankfurt printing
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *Universale pulsuum systema* ('universal system of the pulse') from Fludd's *Pulsus, seu nova et arcana pulsuum historia* (1631), part of his medical works.

## Iconography
A systematic **diagram of the pulse**, correlating its varieties and rhythms with the cosmic and bodily forces that govern them. The plate organises sphygmology into a single ordered scheme of correspondences.

## Significance
Fludd's pulse treatise is notable as one of the **first printed endorsements of William Harvey's circulation of the blood**, which he interpreted within his own cosmic-medical framework of solar and lunar tides in the body. The work marries empirical medicine to Hermetic macro–microcosm theory.

## For artists & game designers
A chart that reads the body's pulse as an echo of cosmic tides is good flavour for a healer-class diagnostic mechanic.

citations.text: Robert Fludd, *Pulsus, seu nova et arcana pulsuum historia* (1631).
William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: Calculating disc (Rechenscheibe): the arithmetic instrument
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: Fludd, De Naturae Simia, arithmetic section]
summary:
A **calculating disc** (German cataloguers' *Rechenscheibe*) from the arithmetic and proportion section of Fludd's *De Naturae Simia* (1618).

## Iconography
Concentric graduated **rings of numbers** form a rotary computing device — a volvelle-like disc for working proportions and calculations by turning rather than reckoning on paper. Scales of figures run around the circumference.

## Significance
Fludd's arithmetic plates present number and proportion as foundations of the [[motif:macrocosm|macrocosm]]'s harmony; the calculating disc is the practical instrument of that Pythagorean conviction, a hand-tool embodiment of cosmic ratio. It belongs to the same instrument tradition as the printed volvelles of Apian.

## For artists & game designers
A rotary number-disc is a satisfying tactile 'set the dials' mechanic for an in-world calculator or cipher.

title: The Sun in the work of creation
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A plate showing the Sun within the creation sequence of *De metaphysico macrocosmi ortu*, Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617).

## Iconography
The **Sun** appears as a radiant disc set within the cosmic spheres, its beams ordering the surrounding heavens. In Fludd's scheme the Sun is the visible vehicle of the divine light, placed at the heart of the created world.

## Significance
Fludd's solar theology is intensely Hermetic: the [[motif:sun|Sun]] is the *tabernacle* of God in the cosmos, the secondary cause through which the supreme light is mediated to the planets and to man. This solar centrality is symbolic and metaphysical, not Copernican.

## For artists & game designers
The Sun-as-divine-vessel is a strong central icon for a cosmology where one luminous body is the source of all power.

title: Engraved title page of Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*, vol. I (1617)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; engraved by Matthäus Merian / de Bry workshop
summary:
The engraved title page of the first volume of Robert Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* ('The History of the Two Worlds'), printed by Johann Theodor de Bry at Oppenheim in 1617.

## Iconography
Within an elaborate architectural frame the long Latin title announces a history of both the *maior* (great) and *minor* (small) world — the macrocosm and the microcosm of man — 'metaphysical, physical and technical'. Allegorical figures and emblematic devices fill the border in the dense Mannerist manner of the de Bry press.

## Significance
The *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* is the central monument of Fludd's project: a vast illustrated encyclopaedia arguing that man (the [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]]) mirrors the universe (the [[motif:macrocosm|macrocosm]]), and that both are bound by divine harmony. Its plates — the divine monochord, *Integra Naturae*, the two pyramids of light and darkness — became the most reproduced images of English Hermeticism. Frances Yates placed Fludd at the heart of the 'Rosicrucian Enlightenment'.

## For artists & game designers
A single engraved frontispiece that promises 'the history of two worlds' is a perfect title-screen device for any game built on a correspondence between the heavens and the body.

title: The kabbalistic Tree of Life after Fludd (modern redrawing)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: After Robert Fludd; modern vector reconstruction
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: modern reconstruction after Fludd, via Wikimedia Commons]
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A modern vector redrawing of the **kabbalistic Tree of Life** as transmitted by Robert Fludd, whose works helped carry the Sephirotic tree into English Christian Cabala.

## Iconography
The **ten Sephiroth** (Kether to Malkuth) are arranged on three pillars and joined by paths, the divine emanations descending from the crown to the kingdom. The figure is the standard map of the kabbalistic worlds.

## Significance
Fludd absorbed **Christian Cabala** (via Reuchlin, Agrippa and the Lurianic currents reaching northern Europe) into his Hermetic synthesis, treating the [[motif:sephiroth|Sephiroth]] as the divine architecture behind his cosmic spheres. This redrawing presents that tree in clean modern form for reference. *(Image is a modern reconstruction, not an original Fludd plate.)*

## For artists & game designers
The ten-node, three-pillar tree is one of the most reusable esoteric skill-graphs in existence — nodes as powers, paths as transitions.

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *The Occult Philosophy in the Elizabethan Age* (1979).

title: The triangle in the circle: the Trinity and the cosmos
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The 'triangle in a circle' diagram from *De metaphysico macrocosmi ortu*, Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617).

## Iconography
A **triangle** is inscribed within concentric **circles** of the cosmos, its three points reaching toward the bounds of the created spheres. The figure binds the **triune God** to the geometry of the universe; divine names or rays mark the corners.

## Significance
Fludd routinely fuses Christian Trinitarian doctrine with Pythagorean–Hermetic number: the triangle is at once the Trinity, the three-fold structure of being, and the generative figure of geometry. The pairing of triangle and circle expresses God (unbounded circle) acting through threefold form.

## For artists & game designers
A glowing triangle locked inside cosmic rings reads instantly as 'a divine signature on the world' — useful as a sigil for a creator-deity faction.

title: The threefold soul and its faculties (consciousness diagram)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
repository: [PLACEHOLDER: de Bry edition, Utriusque Cosmi Historia, Tomus secundus]
summary:
Fludd's celebrated diagram of the **faculties of the soul** mapped onto the human head, from the second volume of *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (often called the 'consciousness' or *triplici animae visione* plate).

## Iconography
A man's **head in profile** is divided into spheres of inner faculty: the *mundus sensibilis* (sensible world) enters through the senses to the **imagination**, passes to **reason** (*intellectus*) and the rational and divine worlds, and is stored in **memory** at the back of the skull. Concentric worlds — sensible, imaginable, intellectual, divine — are drawn radiating from the head.

## Significance
The plate is the supreme image of Fludd's **psychology of the microcosm**: the mind itself is a little cosmos, its faculties arranged as a ladder from sense to God. It is one of the most reproduced diagrams in the history of psychology and consciousness studies.

## For artists & game designers
A head whose interior is a tiered cosmos of perception, reason and memory is a ready blueprint for a 'mind level' UI.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: Title page with winds and angels (*Integrum Morborum Mysterium*)
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry / Frankfurt workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraved title page bearing winds and angels, from Fludd's medical work *Integrum Morborum Mysterium* (1631).

## Iconography
Blowing **winds** (often personified faces at the corners) and **angelic figures** frame the title. The winds carry the celestial influences and pestilential vapours that, in Fludd's meteorological medicine, bring health or disease to the human microcosm.

## Significance
For Fludd, weather is theology: the [[motif:four-winds|four winds]] are the agents by which the macrocosm acts on the body, and angels govern the contest of good and evil influences. His meteorological pathology made the sickbed a battlefield of cosmic forces.

## For artists & game designers
Personified winds at the four corners blowing influence inward is a compact way to show 'the weather has agency' in a world where climate is a moral force.

citations.text: William H. Huffman, *Robert Fludd and the End of the Renaissance* (1988).

title: The weighted ziggurat: a demonstration of statics
work: Robert Fludd: Utriusque Cosmi Historia & the Engraved Hermetic Cosmos
creator: Robert Fludd; de Bry workshop
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A **weights-and-mechanics** demonstration (the 'weighted ziggurat') from Fludd's *De Naturae Simia* (1618).

## Iconography
A stepped, **ziggurat-like structure** is loaded with **weights** to illustrate principles of balance, proportion and statics. The diagram belongs to Fludd's survey of the mechanical arts.

## Significance
In the 'technical history of the macrocosm' Fludd catalogues the human mechanical arts — levers, balances, pumps, lifting-gear — as part of man's imitation of nature. These plates show the practical, engineering face of his vast Hermetic encyclopaedia, the [[motif:ape-of-nature|ape of Nature]] at work.

## For artists & game designers
A stepped tower of weights is a clean physics-puzzle prop with a striking silhouette.

title: Abracadabra triangle amulet diagram
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (after Serenus Sammonicus)
date: charm of late antiquity; later diagram
summary:
The classic abracadabra charm written as a diminishing triangle, a word-amulet against fever and disease.

## Iconography
The word **ABRACADABRA** written on successive lines, each dropping a final letter, so the text **tapers** to a single A and forms an inverted triangle. The whole was inscribed on parchment and worn at the neck.

## Significance
The **abracadabra** charm is first recorded in the *Liber Medicinalis* of Quintus Serenus Sammonicus (3rd c. CE), who prescribed the diminishing triangle to drive out fever: as the word shrank, so the illness was to dwindle and depart. It is a textbook case of [[motif:word magic|word-magic]] — the form of the writing, not just its meaning, doing the work — and circulated as a [[motif:apotropaic|protective]] amulet through the Middle Ages and into early-modern charm-books.

## For artists & game designers
A spell that 'counts down' a disease by shedding a letter each line — an elegant visual mechanic for a tapering healing charm.

citations.text: Quintus Serenus Sammonicus, *Liber Medicinalis* (3rd c. CE).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Cimaruta (sprig-of-rue charm), from Elworthy
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Frederick Thomas Elworthy (illustration)
rights: Public domain (Frederick Thomas Elworthy, 1895). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The cimaruta, the silver 'sprig of rue' charm of Italian folk magic, hung over cradles against the evil eye, as illustrated in Elworthy's *The Evil Eye*.

## Iconography
A branching silver charm shaped like a sprig of **rue**, from whose three stems hang a cluster of protective symbols: a **crescent moon**, a **key**, a **serpent**, a flower, a hand, a fish, and others. Each pendant adds its own apotropaic virtue.

## Significance
The **cimaruta** (*cima di ruta*, 'top of rue') compounds many charms into one: rue itself was a protective herb, and the moon, key, and serpent invoke Diana, Hecate, and older guardian powers. Hung over an infant's bed, it guarded against the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] and witches. Elworthy's 1895 study made it the type-specimen of the Italian compound amulet, later central to discussions of survivals of pagan goddess-cult.

## For artists & game designers
A single charm that bundles a dozen protective symbols on one stem — a richly detailed 'composite amulet' for close study.

citations.text: Charles Godfrey Leland, *Etruscan Roman Remains in Popular Tradition* (1892).

title: Cornicello (Italian horn charm)
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Italian tradition); photograph by Antonio Cavallo
rights: Public domain (photo: Antonio Cavallo). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A cornicello, the twisted red horn-shaped amulet of southern Italian folk magic, worn against the malocchio.

## Iconography
A slender, **twisted horn** tapering to a point, traditionally of red **coral**, gold, or silver, pierced for suspension. The form recalls an animal horn or a chilli pepper.

## Significance
The **cornicello** ('little horn') is the classic Neapolitan charm against the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] (*malocchio*) and against *iettatura*, ill-luck cast by an envious glance. Its protective force draws on the ancient apotropaic power of the horn and of red coral, both attested in Roman amulets. Frederick Elworthy and later folklorists traced its descent from antique horn-and-phallus charms.

## For artists & game designers
A small red twisted horn on a chain — a wearable luck-charm with deep Mediterranean roots and a striking silhouette.

citations.text: Alan Dundes (ed.), *The Evil Eye: A Casebook* (1981).

title: Ethiopian magic scroll (prayers against ailments), Wellcome
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Ethiopian dabtara / scribe)
rights: Public domain. Wellcome Collection, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An Ethiopian protective scroll comprising prayers against various ailments, from the Wellcome Collection — a body-length amulet of the Ethiopian magico-religious tradition.

## Iconography
A long, narrow strip of **parchment** written in **Ge'ez** with prayers, divine and angelic names, and bordered by **talismanic paintings** — staring guardian faces, geometric 'net' designs, and angels — meant to bind demons and the spirits of disease.

## Significance
Such **magic scrolls** (*ya branna ṭelsam*) were made by the *dabtara* (clergy-scribes) to the height of their owner, to heal and protect against disease, the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]], and possession by spirits such as the *buda*. The interlaced eyes and faces are characteristic [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] devices. They form one of the richest living traditions of [[motif:goetia_grimoire|written]] protective magic, studied by Jacques Mercier.

## For artists & game designers
A scroll measured to the owner's own body, painted with watching eyes — a deeply personal 'protective relic' with a vivid graphic language.

title: The 'Evil Eye' / Lucky Hunchback mosaic, Antioch
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Carole Raddato
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (photo: Carole Raddato). Hatay Archaeology Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Roman floor mosaic from Antioch showing the evil eye under attack, a threshold-charm against the malevolent gaze.

## Iconography
A large **eye** is assailed from every side — pierced by a **trident** and sword, set upon by a **scorpion**, serpent, dog, and birds — while a horned, ithyphallic dwarf (the 'Lucky Hunchback') strides away. The composition shows the destruction of the eye's power.

## Significance
Laid at a doorway, this mosaic is monumental [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] art: the assembled creatures and weapons turn the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] against itself, while the hunchback (*gibbus*) and phallus add their own luck-bringing, eye-deflecting force. It is among the most cited ancient depictions of the belief, a direct ancestor of the later [[motif:nazar|nazar]] and horn charms.

## For artists & game designers
A whole bestiary marshalled to stab a single eye — a vivid template for an 'anti-curse ward' rendered as a scene rather than a symbol.

citations.text: Katherine M. D. Dunbabin, *Mosaics of the Greek and Roman World* (1999).

title: Roman phallic amulet (fascinum)
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Roman); recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (Portable Antiquities Scheme, Colchester & Ipswich Museum). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Roman copper-alloy phallic pendant or fascinum, recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme, worn as protection against the evil eye.

## Iconography
A small cast **copper-alloy** amulet modelled as a **phallus**, pierced or looped for suspension. Many such pieces combine the phallus with a fist or the *mano fica* on the reverse, doubling the apotropaic charge.

## Significance
The **fascinum** was the Roman embodiment of the divine apotropaic power that bore the same name; phallic charms were hung on children, soldiers, and doorways to deflect the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] (*invidia*) and the harm of envy. Pliny records the practice, and the type underlies the medieval and modern Italian [[motif:cornicello|horn]] and [[motif:mano cornuta|hand]] charms. Such finds are common across the Roman provinces.

## For artists & game designers
An ancient pocket charm that wards by drawing the eye to a shocking image — the historical ancestor of later luck-horns.

citations.text: Pliny the Elder, *Natural History*, on the fascinum.
Catherine Johns, *Sex or Symbol: Erotic Images of Greece and Rome* (1982).

title: The hag-stone / adder-stone (holed stone charm)
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (folk tradition); diagram via Wikimedia
summary:
An illustration concerning the hag-stone or adder-stone, the naturally holed pebble used across Britain and northern Europe as a protective charm.

## Iconography
The figure concerns the **holed stone** — a pebble or flint naturally pierced by water-wear. Such stones were hung on bedposts, stable doors, and keys, or peered through.

## Significance
The **hag-stone** (also 'adder-stone', *Hühnergott*) was held to guard against the night-hag or [[motif:nightmare|Mara]] who pressed sleepers, against witches who rode horses by night, and against the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]]; looking through the hole was said to grant sight of fairies or the truth behind glamour. The naturally formed hole made it a charm 'made by nature', and it remained one of the commonest free-found amulets in European folk magic.

## For artists & game designers
A found pebble with a hole that reveals the unseen when looked through — a perfect low-magic 'true-sight' item.

citations.text: Christina Hole, *A Dictionary of British Folk Customs* (1976).
Ralph Merrifield, *The Archaeology of Ritual and Magic* (1987).

title: Pennsylvania barn with hex signs, Oley (HABS)
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Pennsylvania German tradition; HABS photograph by Charles Dornbusch
rights: Public domain (HABS, Charles Dornbusch). Library of Congress, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Pennsylvania-German bank barn at Oley painted with hex signs, the geometric 'barn stars' of Pennsylvania-Dutch folk decoration.

## Iconography
Large circular **rosette stars** painted on the barn's gable and façade — radiating points within concentric rings, in bright colour. The motifs descend from the compass-rosette and Fraktur ornament of the Rhineland settlers.

## Significance
The **hex sign** (*Hexefoos*) is the best-known American folk-magical mark, though its protective meaning is much debated: nineteenth-century barn stars were partly decorative and partly, in popular belief, [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] — guarding the barn, the livestock, and the harvest. The term 'hex' links them in the popular imagination to the Pennsylvania-German pow-wow (*Brauche*) tradition. Scholarship by Don Yoder and Thomas Graves disentangles ornament from charm.

## For artists & game designers
Bold painted star-rosettes on a rural building: an immediately legible regional 'folk ward' aesthetic.

citations.text: Don Yoder & Thomas E. Graves, *Hex Signs: Pennsylvania Dutch Barn Symbols and Their Meaning* (2000).
Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS), Library of Congress.

title: Chalk hexafoil (daisy-wheel) apotropaic mark
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (folk tradition); photograph by Violetcabra
rights: Public domain (CC0, photo: Violetcabra). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A hexafoil or 'daisy wheel', the six-petalled rosette compass-scribed onto buildings as a protective 'witch mark'.

## Iconography
A circle within which six arcs, struck from points on the circumference at the radius, interlace to form a **six-petalled flower**. The figure is drawn in a single continuous compass operation, leaving no gap for a spirit to enter.

## Significance
The **hexafoil** is the commonest of the British [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] marks catalogued by surveys of ritual-protection graffiti (notably Matthew Champion's work on medieval church graffiti). Cut into door-frames, beams, and fireplaces, the unbroken interlace was held to trap or repel malevolent spirits and the evil eye. Its solar-rosette geometry links it to far older protective sun-wheels.

## For artists & game designers
A single-stroke geometric ward that a character can scratch anywhere with a compass or knife — instantly readable as 'protective sigil'.

citations.text: Matthew Champion, *Medieval Graffiti: The Lost Voices of England's Churches* (2015).
Timothy Easton, 'Apotropaic Symbols and Other Measures for Protecting Buildings against Misfortune,' in *Physical Evidence for Ritual Acts* (2016).

title: Himmelsbrief (heaven-letter), c. 1800
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Druck: Oemigke & Riemschneider, Neuruppin
summary:
A coloured Himmelsbrief or 'letter from heaven', a printed protective broadside of German and Pennsylvania-German folk belief.

## Iconography
A printed sheet headed by a coloured **angel**, beneath which runs the text of a **letter purportedly sent from heaven** (often said to have fallen at Holstein or written by Christ's own hand), promising protection to whoever carries or copies it.

## Significance
The **Himmelsbrief** ('heaven-letter') is a Christian protective charm in epistolary form: kept in the house or carried on the body, it was believed to guard the bearer against fire, flood, weapons, and sudden death. Hugely popular in German lands and carried by emigrants to Pennsylvania, where it joined the *Brauche* (pow-wow) repertoire alongside the [[motif:long lost friend|Long Lost Friend]]. It belongs to the wider family of amuletic protective texts.

## For artists & game designers
A holy letter that protects simply by being carried — a charm that is pure text plus an angel, ideal as a 'blessed document' item.

citations.text: Don Yoder, 'The Himmelsbrief,' in *Discovering American Folklife* (1990).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Lucky horseshoe over a door
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (folk tradition); photograph by Man vyi
rights: Public domain (photo: Man vyi). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A horseshoe nailed over a doorway, the most familiar of European luck-and-protection charms.

## Iconography
An iron **horseshoe** fixed above a **door**, conventionally with the points turned **upward** (so the luck 'does not run out') — though points-down, to pour protection over those entering, is equally traditional.

## Significance
The **horseshoe** unites several strands of folk belief: the protective virtue of **iron** against witches and fairies, the sanctity attached to the smith and to Saint Dunstan (who legendarily shod the Devil), and the lucky crescent shape. Nailed at the [[motif:apotropaic|threshold]], it guards the house against bewitchment. It is among the longest-surviving and most widespread of everyday charms.

## For artists & game designers
The humblest possible ward: a piece of iron over a door whose orientation carries meaning — easy environmental storytelling.

citations.text: Edward A. Armstrong, 'The Symbolism of the Horseshoe,' *Folklore* (1944).
Owen Davies, *Popular Magic: Cunning-folk in English History* (2003).

title: Krampus masks of Austria
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Alpine mask-carvers); photograph by Naturpuur
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Naturpuur). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
