Carved wooden Krampus masks from Austria, the horned figures of the Alpine midwinter processions.

## Iconography
Fiercely carved **wooden masks** with great curling **horns**, bared fangs, lolling tongues, and wild fur, worn with bells and birch switches. Each mask is hand-carved and individual.

## Significance
**Krampus** is the horned companion of St Nicholas in the Alpine *Krampuslauf*, punishing the wicked while Nicholas rewards the good. He belongs to the same midwinter complex as the [[motif:perchten|Perchten]]: masked, noise-making processions that drive out winter and evil spirits. Though Christianised, the custom preserves much older apotropaic mumming, in which the terrifying mask both embodies and repels malevolent forces. Studied by John Honigmann and folklorists of Alpine custom.

## For artists & game designers
A gallery of hand-carved horned masks — a deep reference for terrifying-yet-protective folk monster design.

citations.text: Al Ridenour, *The Krampus and the Old, Dark Christmas* (2016).

title: Mano cornuta amulet (horned-hand gesture)
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Italian tradition); photograph by Ra Boe
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Ra Boe). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An amulet in the form of the mano cornuta, the 'horned hand' whose extended index and little finger make the protective corna sign.

## Iconography
A cast **hand** with the index and little finger extended and the middle two folded under the thumb — the *corna* or 'horns'. Rendered in metal or coral, it is worn as a pendant; as a living gesture it is made with the hand pointed downward or outward.

## Significance
The **mano cornuta** is one of the principal Italian gestures and amulets against the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]]. The horned silhouette echoes the apotropaic horn (as in the [[motif:cornicello|cornicello]]); pointed at a suspected *jettatore*, it deflects the malevolent gaze. It belongs with the *mano fico* (fig-hand) in the repertory of protective hand-signs catalogued since Andrea de Jorio's study of Neapolitan gesture.

## For artists & game designers
A frozen protective gesture cast in metal — a charm that encodes a ritual movement, good for a 'gesture of warding' motif.

citations.text: Andrea de Jorio, *La mimica degli antichi investigata nel gestire napoletano* (1832).

title: Nazar (evil-eye) glass amulet, Turkey
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Turkish glassmaker); photograph by Zorka Sojka
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Zorka Sojka). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A nazar boncuğu, the blue glass eye-bead hung throughout the eastern Mediterranean to ward off the evil eye.

## Iconography
A flattened disc of **blue glass** marked with **concentric rings** — white, light blue, and a dark central pupil — forming a stylised staring **eye**. The cobalt blue is itself held to be protective.

## Significance
The **nazar** answers the ancient and widespread fear of the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] (*nazar*, *baskania*, *malocchio*): a covetous or envious glance believed to bring harm. By presenting an eye that 'looks back', the amulet reflects or absorbs the malevolent gaze. The belief is documented from antiquity (Plutarch, the *Mishnah*) to the present and crosses the Islamic, Jewish, and Christian Mediterranean alike.

## For artists & game designers
The glowing blue eye-bead is one of the world's most recognisable protective charms — a compact 'anti-curse' object with instant cultural legibility.

citations.text: Alan Dundes (ed.), *The Evil Eye: A Casebook* (1981).

title: Perchten masks, Salzburg
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Alpine mask-carvers)
summary:
Perchten masks from Salzburg, the masked figures of the Alpine winter processions associated with the spirit Frau Perchta.

## Iconography
Grotesque carved **masks** — some beautiful (*Schönperchten*), some hideous and horned (*Schiachperchten*) — worn in processions through the dark midwinter weeks.

## Significance
The **Perchten** take their name from **Frau Perchta**, the Alpine winter spirit who rewards the diligent and disembowels the slovenly during the *Rauhnächte* ('rough nights') between Christmas and Epiphany. Their masked runs are [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] rites meant to drive out winter, banish evil spirits, and ensure fertility for the coming year. Beautiful and ugly Perchten embody the year's two faces. The custom is a key case-study in the survival of pre-Christian winter ritual, discussed by Carlo Ginzburg.

## For artists & game designers
Paired 'fair' and 'foul' masks for a single winter-spirit — a built-in design dichotomy for a seasonal entity.

citations.text: Carlo Ginzburg, *Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath* (1991).

title: Doll pierced with pins, Museum of Witchcraft
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (folk-magical artefact); photograph by Midnightblueowl
rights: Public domain (CC0, photo: Midnightblueowl). Museum of Witchcraft, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A cloth poppet stuck with pins, the classic image-magic doll, in the Museum of Witchcraft and Magic at Boscastle.

## Iconography
A crude **fabric figure** in human shape, into which **pins** have been driven. The doll stands in for a specific person; what is done to it is meant to befall them.

## Significance
The **poppet** (also 'mommet' or 'corp creadh') is the European instrument of sympathetic [[motif:image magic|image magic]], used both to curse — piercing to cause pain — and, inverted, to heal or protect. Such dolls appear in early-modern witch-trial evidence and survived in cunning-folk practice into the twentieth century. The Boscastle museum holds one of the richest collections of these objects, studied by Owen Davies and others.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal curse-object: a pin-stuck doll that visibly links harm to a victim through likeness — instantly readable image magic.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Popular Magic: Cunning-folk in English History* (2003).
Museum of Witchcraft and Magic, Boscastle.

title: *Der lange verborgene Freund* (The Long Lost Friend), Hohman
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Johann Georg Hohman
summary:
The title page of Johann Georg Hohman's *Der lange verborgene Freund* (1820), the foundational charm-book of Pennsylvania-German pow-wow magic, later famed in English as *The Long Lost Friend*.

## Iconography
A plain letterpress **title page** in German, naming the book and its compiler, **Johann Georg Hohman**, printed at Reading, Pennsylvania. The book itself is a pocket-sized collection of recipes and charms.

## Significance
*The Long Lost Friend* is the most important printed source of **pow-wow** (*Brauche*), the Christian folk-healing and protective magic of the Pennsylvania Germans. It gathers charms against fire, theft, illness, and bewitchment — including the [[motif:sator square|SATOR square]] and biblical formulae — drawn from older German books like the *Romanus-Büchlein*. It became a near-talismanic object in its own right, said to protect the house that held it. Studied by Don Yoder and David Kriebel.

## For artists & game designers
A humble pocket book that is itself believed to protect its owner — a 'living grimoire' artifact rooted in real Americana.

citations.text: Johann Georg Hohman, *Der lange verborgene Freund* (Reading, PA, 1820).
David W. Kriebel, *Powwowing among the Pennsylvania Dutch* (2007).

title: SATOR square, carved stone at Oppède
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (medieval mason); photograph by M Disdero
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: M Disdero). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A SATOR square carved in stone at Oppède, the famous five-word Latin palindrome used as a protective charm.

## Iconography
A 5×5 grid of letters spelling **SATOR / AREPO / TENET / OPERA / ROTAS** — a square that reads the same across, down, and (reversed) both ways. Here it is incised into a block of building stone.

## Significance
The **SATOR square** (or ROTAS square) is one of the most enduring [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] word-charms of the West. Its letters can be rearranged into a cross of *PATERNOSTER* flanked by A and O (Alpha and Omega), giving it a Christian reading, while its palindromic perfection made it a charm against fire, illness, and the Devil from antiquity onward. It was cut into walls, written on bread and slips, and copied in charm-books across Europe.

## For artists & game designers
A self-mirroring letter-grid that works as a charm by its very symmetry — a ready-made 'word-puzzle ward'.

citations.text: Duncan Fishwick, 'On the Origin of the Rotas-Sator Square,' *Harvard Theological Review* (1964).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: SATOR (ROTAS) square graffito, Pompeii
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Roman); recorded by Matteo Della Corte
rights: Public domain (record: Matteo Della Corte). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A record of the SATOR/ROTAS word-square scratched at Pompeii, among the earliest known examples of the charm, buried by Vesuvius in 79 CE.

## Iconography
The drawing reproduces the **5×5 Latin word-square** (here in the ROTAS-first orientation) recorded from the Palaestra and other Pompeian walls (CIL IV 8623, 8622).

## Significance
Because Pompeii was destroyed in **79 CE**, its SATOR graffiti prove the [[motif:sator square|word-square]] existed in the first century, long before its medieval Christian use — fuelling debate over whether its origins were Jewish, Christian, Mithraic, or simply a clever Latin palindrome later given sacred meaning. As the earliest dated witnesses, the Pompeian examples anchor the entire history of this travelling charm. Recorded by the archaeologist Matteo Della Corte.

## For artists & game designers
An ancient wall-scratch that is the 'first appearance' of a charm later found across Europe — a great deep-history Easter egg.

citations.text: Duncan Fishwick, 'On the Origin of the Rotas-Sator Square,' *Harvard Theological Review* (1964).
Rebecca Benefiel, 'Magic Squares and the Pompeian Graffiti,' in studies of ancient graffiti.

title: Schiachperchten table-mask, Goldegg Perchtenlauf
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (Alpine mask-carver); photograph by Wald1siedel
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Wald1siedel). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A towering Perchten table-mask (*Tafelmaske*) from the Goldegg Perchtenlauf in Salzburg, an elaborate form of the Alpine procession mask.

## Iconography
A huge, vertically built **frame-mask** rising far above the wearer's head, crowned with horns, fur, mirrors, and carved faces — a *Tafelmaske* (table-mask) of monumental scale carried in the run.

## Significance
This form belongs to the *Perchtenlauf* of the Pongau region, where the massive masks are displayed and paraded during the *Rauhnächte*. Like the simpler [[motif:perchten|Perchten]] and [[motif:krampus|Krampus]] masks it is [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] — its noise, height, and terrifying faces meant to drive out winter and evil and to bring luck and fertility. The Goldegg run is among the best-known surviving examples of the tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A mask that becomes architecture — a person-sized 'walking shrine' of horns and faces, perfect for a festival-boss design.

citations.text: Carlo Ginzburg, *Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath* (1991).
Al Ridenour, *The Krampus and the Old, Dark Christmas* (2016).

title: Taper burn marks, Haddon Hall
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: Anonymous (folk tradition); photograph by Delusion23
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Delusion23). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Deliberate taper burn-marks on timber at Haddon Hall, a form of apotropaic charring made to guard a building.

## Iconography
Clusters of **teardrop-shaped scorch marks** burned into oak beams and door-jambs with the flame of a candle or taper. The marks are shallow, repeated, and concentrated around openings — doorways, windows, and especially fireplaces.

## Significance
Long mistaken for accidental scorching, these **taper burn-marks** are now recognised by historians of vernacular building as a deliberate [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] practice: by symbolically 'burning' the timber, householders were thought to inoculate it against destructive fire and against the malevolent spirits that travelled by chimney and threshold. Timothy Easton's fieldwork established their intentional, patterned distribution.

## For artists & game designers
Scorch-marks deliberately burned around a hearth as a fire-ward — atmospheric environmental lore that hints at unseen folk belief.

citations.text: John Dean & Nick Hill, 'Burn Marks on Buildings,' *Vernacular Architecture* (2014).

title: Glass witch-ball (New England Glass Company), Met
work: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: New England Glass Company
rights: Public domain (CC0). The Metropolitan Museum of Art, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A blown-glass witch-ball with its vase, a reflective sphere hung in windows to repel witchcraft and the evil eye.

## Iconography
A hollow, often **iridescent or coloured glass sphere**, here paired with a matching vase. Its mirror-bright, curved surface throws back a distorted reflection of whatever approaches.

## Significance
The **witch-ball** worked by reflection and fascination: hung in a window, its shining surface was believed to dazzle and turn back the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] or to trap a witch's malevolent gaze, much as the threads inside a glass 'spirit ball' were said to ensnare spirits. The form spread from English folk practice into American decorative glass, blurring charm and ornament — kin to the silvered 'gazing ball'.

## For artists & game designers
A pretty reflective orb that doubles as a ward by bouncing back a curse — decorative object with a hidden protective function.

citations.text: Ralph Merrifield, *The Archaeology of Ritual and Magic* (1987).
Metropolitan Museum of Art, witch-ball and vase.

title: The Stages of Life
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Anonymous
rights: Public domain. British Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A popular print of *The Stages of Life*, the staircase or pyramid of the human ages from cradle to grave (British Museum).

## Iconography
The lifespan is arranged as an ascending and descending **staircase** (*Lebenstreppe*): an infant at the lowest step, the figure rising in vigour by decades to a crowned prime at the summit (often age fifty), then declining stage by stage into stooped old age and the grave. Emblems of mortality—skeletons, an hourglass—frequently frame the scene.

## Significance
The **Ages of Man** schema, dividing life into symmetrical rising and falling decades, descends from medieval and classical age-theory and flourished as a broadside print across Europe. The staircase form makes the lifespan a [[motif:wheel-of-fortune|rise-and-fall]] like Fortune's wheel, a [[motif:vanitas|memento mori]] reminding the viewer that ascent is inevitably followed by decline.

## For artists & game designers
The stepped-pyramid lifespan is a ready-made progression bar: each step a decade with its own costume, vigour and emblem.

citations.text: J. A. Burrow, *The Ages of Man: A Study in Medieval Writing and Thought* (1986).

title: The Wheel of Fortune (French Boccaccio manuscript)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Anonymous (French illuminator)
summary:
A fifteenth-century French illumination of Fortune's wheel in the tradition of Boccaccio's *De casibus virorum illustrium*.

## Iconography
**Fortuna** turns the **wheel** on which crowned and noble figures rise, reign, and are flung down. The mood is tragic: princes who climbed to the summit are shown in the act of falling, illustrating the *casus*—the catastrophic reversal—of the great.

## Significance
Boccaccio's *De casibus virorum illustrium* ('On the Fates of Famous Men', c. 1360) catalogued the downfalls of the illustrious as warnings against trusting [[motif:wheel-of-fortune|Fortune]]. Translated by Laurent de Premierfait and into English by Lydgate, it made the falling-prince wheel a staple of late-medieval moral illustration and a root of Renaissance tragedy.

## For artists & game designers
The 'fall of princes' framing turns the wheel into a roster of cautionary boss-figures, each defined by the height from which they drop.

citations.text: H. R. Patch, *The Goddess Fortuna in Mediaeval Literature* (1927).

title: The Wheel of Fortune (Consolation of Philosophy)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Anonymous (French illuminator)
summary:
A French manuscript illumination of Fortune turning her wheel, illustrating Boethius's *De consolatione philosophiae*.

## Iconography
**Fortuna**, often blindfolded, stands at the hub and cranks the great **wheel** to which four crowned figures cling: one rises (*regnabo*, 'I shall reign'), one sits enthroned at the top (*regno*, 'I reign'), one tumbles head-down (*regnavi*, 'I have reigned'), and one is crushed beneath (*sum sine regno*, 'I am without a kingdom'). The cycle of rise and fall is read clockwise around the rim.

## Significance
Boethius's sixth-century *Consolation*, written in prison awaiting execution, gave the Middle Ages its master-image of [[motif:wheel-of-fortune|mutability]]: Lady Philosophy teaches that Fortune's gifts are by nature inconstant, and her turning wheel became the visual emblem of worldly instability in countless manuscripts. The blindfold marks her indifference to merit.

## For artists & game designers
A self-contained 'status wheel': four stations (rising, ruling, falling, fallen) that can drive a board, a luck mechanic, or a fate UI.

citations.text: H. R. Patch, *The Goddess Fortuna in Mediaeval Literature* (1927).

title: Nemesis (The Great Fortune)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Dürer's monumental engraving known as *Nemesis* or *Das grosse Glück* ('The Great Fortune'), the winged goddess of measured retribution balanced on a sphere above the world.

## Iconography
A powerful winged **figure** stands on a floating **sphere** high above a meticulously rendered Alpine **landscape**, holding a **goblet** (reward) and a **bridle** (restraint). Her body is rendered with deliberate, unidealized realism after Vitruvian proportion. The sphere beneath her feet marks the instability of fortune.

## Significance
Dürer fuses **Fortuna** with **Nemesis**, the goddess of just retribution, following Politian's Latin poem *Manto*: she rewards and punishes, dispensing the cup and the curb. Standing on the rolling [[motif:fortuna|sphere]] rather than the wheel, she is the humanist, classicized reworking of medieval Fortune into an agent of measured cosmic justice.

## For artists & game designers
The reward-and-restraint pairing (cup vs. bridle) is a compact icon for a balancing power that both grants and reins in—a karma or luck deity.

citations.text: E. Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: Father Time (design for a funerary monument)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Anonymous (after a French ornament-print series)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A design featuring Father Time (Vader Tijd) from a printed series of patterns for tabernacles, altars and epitaphs (Rijksmuseum).

## Iconography
**Father Time** appears as a winged old man bearing a **scythe** and an **hourglass**, here incorporated into a funerary monument design. The scythe mows down life; the hourglass measures its running-out; the wings show time's flight. As a memorial ornament he presides over mortality.

## Significance
**Father Time** is the late descendant of **Chronos / Saturn**, his scythe and hourglass marking him as the devourer and measurer of all things. The conflation of Greek Chronos ('Time') with the Titan Kronos / [[motif:saturn-chronos|Saturn]] produced the familiar bearded reaper of [[motif:vanitas|vanitas]] imagery, ubiquitous on tombs and clocks from the Baroque onward.

## For artists & game designers
Scythe + hourglass + wings is the irreducible 'time/death' kit—instantly legible on a clock face, a tomb, or a reaper NPC.

citations.text: E. Panofsky, 'Father Time', in *Studies in Iconology* (1939).

title: Fortuna (Luck)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Hans Sebald Beham
rights: Public domain. Statens Museum for Kunst, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Hans Sebald Beham's small engraving *Fortuna* of 1541, the goddess of luck poised on a sphere amid the winds.

## Iconography
The nude **Fortuna** balances precariously on a rolling **sphere**, her hair and drapery caught by the **winds** that drive her this way and that, sometimes holding a billowing sail or cloth. Her unstable footing on the ball is the essential sign of fortune's inconstancy.

## Significance
Beham, one of the German *Kleinmeister* ('Little Masters') and a pupil-follower of Dürer, distilled [[motif:fortuna|Fortuna]] into the Renaissance emblematic type that supplanted the medieval wheel: a nude figure on a sphere or globe, blown by wind, never still. The image circulated widely as a moral commonplace on the precariousness of worldly success.

## For artists & game designers
The 'figure on a rolling ball in the wind' is the most economical possible icon of instability—one body, one sphere, motion implied.

citations.text: H. R. Patch, *The Goddess Fortuna in Mediaeval Literature* (1927).
G. Pauli, *Hans Sebald Beham: Ein kritisches Verzeichnis seiner Kupferstiche* (1901).

title: Isis-Fortuna with Rudder and Cornucopia
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
rights: Public domain. Walters Art Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Roman-Egyptian bronze statuette of Isis-Fortuna in the Walters Art Museum, fusing the Egyptian goddess Isis with the Roman Fortuna.

## Iconography
The goddess holds a **rudder** (the steering of destiny) set upon a small **globe**, and cradles a **cornucopia** (the horn of plenty) in the other arm; she often wears the lunar disk and horns of **Isis**. The rudder and globe steer fate; the cornucopia pours out abundance.

## Significance
The rudder, globe and cornucopia are the classic attributes of **Fortuna / Tyche**: she steers the course of human affairs and dispenses prosperity. In Roman Egypt she merged with **Isis** in the syncretic cult that Apuleius celebrates, making [[motif:fortuna|Fortuna]] both cosmic helmsman and giver of plenty. This is the antique root of the later Fortuna-with-rudder iconography.

## For artists & game designers
The attribute trio—rudder, globe, cornucopia—reads instantly as 'controls destiny and grants abundance', ideal for a luck or harvest deity.

title: Atropos (The Fates)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Francisco de Goya
rights: Public domain. Museo del Prado, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Goya's *Atropos* (*Las Parcas*), one of the Black Paintings from the Quinta del Sordo, showing the Fates hovering over a dark landscape, c. 1819–23 (Prado).

## Iconography
Three (or four) **Fates** float against a leaden sky: one holds the **scissors** of **Atropos**, one a magnifying glass, one a small effigy or newborn-like figure that may be the spun life, and a bound male figure watches helplessly. The grouping hovers weightless above a void, the thread of life reduced to nightmare.

## Significance
Goya transforms the classical [[motif:the-three-fates|Parcae]] into a hallucinatory image of inexorable destiny. Where Renaissance Fates spin elegantly, Goya's drift like specters, indifferent and grotesque; **Atropos**, 'the inflexible', with her shears, presides over a fate that cannot be appealed. The picture belongs to the despairing private murals of his late years.

## For artists & game designers
Proof that the same three-Fates motif can flip from decorative allegory to pure dread—weightlessness and a dim void do the work that ornament cannot.

title: The Wheel of Fortune (Hortus Deliciarum)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Herrad of Landsberg (workshop)
summary:
The Wheel of Fortune from Herrad of Landsberg's *Hortus Deliciarum*, the great twelfth-century monastic encyclopedia (known through facsimiles after the original burned in 1870).

## Iconography
A large **wheel** carries figures up one side, over a crowned **king** at the summit, and down the other as he is toppled and cast to the ground. **Fortuna** turns the mechanism, and accompanying labels moralize each stage of the ascent and fall.

## Significance
Compiled by the abbess **Herrad of Landsberg** for the nuns of Hohenburg, the *Hortus Deliciarum* ('Garden of Delights') set the [[motif:wheel-of-fortune|Wheel of Fortune]] within a Christian teaching program on the vanity of earthly power. Its destruction in the siege of Strasbourg makes the surviving copies precious witnesses to one of the earliest fully developed medieval wheels.

## For artists & game designers
The wheel as moral diagram with captioned stages — a model for an annotated 'rise-and-ruin' sequence.

citations.text: R. Green et al., *Herrad of Hohenbourg: Hortus Deliciarum* (1979).

title: Occasio arrepta (Opportunity Seized)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Joannes David (Jan David), S.J.
summary:
An emblematic engraving from Jan David's *Occasio arrepta, neglecta* (Antwerp, 1605), depicting Occasio—Opportunity, the Latinized Kairos—as the seizable fleeting moment.

## Iconography
**Occasio** is the figure with a long **forelock** at the front of the head but **bald behind**, often poised on a rolling wheel or sphere with **winged feet** and a **razor**: she can be grasped by the hair only as she approaches, never once she has passed. The young figures who seize or miss her forelock dramatize opportunity taken and lost.

## Significance
The image derives from the Greek **Kairos** ('the right moment'), the youthful god sculpted by Lysippos with a forelock and razor, Latinized in the Renaissance as **Occasio** and codified by Alciato's emblem *In occasionem*. Jesuit emblem books like David's used [[motif:kairos-occasio|Occasio]] as a moral lesson on seizing the fleeting chance for grace. The proverbial 'taking time by the forelock' descends directly from this figure.

## For artists & game designers
The forelock-and-bald-back design is a perfect 'limited-time event' icon: graspable only in a narrow window, ungraspable once it has passed.

title: Lebenstreppe (The Staircase of Life)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Anonymous (Verlag Gustav Kühn)
summary:
A German *Lebenstreppe* ('staircase of life') broadside published by Gustav Kühn of Neuruppin in 1886, the German form of the Ages of Man.

## Iconography
A symmetrical **staircase** of ten steps carries a figure (or a couple) from a cradled **infant** at age ten, up through the decades to the **summit** at fifty, then down again to the bent figure of ninety and the grave at one hundred. Each decade is captioned with its character; emblems of death often close the descent.

## Significance
The **Lebenstreppe** was a staple of the cheap, hand-coloured Neuruppiner *Bilderbogen* (picture broadsides) mass-produced for popular consumption in 19th-century Germany. It crystallized the centuries-old [[motif:ages-of-man|Ages of Man]] into a domestic moral image of the [[motif:wheel-of-fortune|rise and fall]] of a single life—a secular *memento mori* for the parlour wall.

## For artists & game designers
The captioned ten-decade staircase is an explicit life-progression UI, each step a labelled life-stage with its own pose and mood.

citations.text: J. A. Burrow, *The Ages of Man* (1986).
On Neuruppiner Bilderbogen: K. Riha, *Bänkelsang und Moritat* (literature).

title: The Norns
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Johannes Gehrts
summary:
Johannes Gehrts's *Die Nornen* (1889), a Romantic depiction of the three Norse goddesses of fate at the foot of the world-tree.

## Iconography
The three **Norns**—**Urd** (the past), **Verdandi** (the present) and **Skuld** (the future)—are shown by the **Well of Urd** beneath the great ash **Yggdrasil**, weaving or tending the **thread of fate** that determines the destinies of gods and men. The world-tree rises behind them.

## Significance
In Norse cosmology the [[motif:norns|Norns]] water Yggdrasil from the Well of Urd and shape the fate of every being, the Northern counterpart to the Greek [[motif:the-three-fates|Moirai]]. Gehrts, a German history-painter active in the 19th-century Norse revival, gives them the romantic, monumental treatment that fed the Wagnerian and later fantasy imagination of the weird sisters of fate.

## For artists & game designers
A past/present/future triad bound to a sacred tree and well—a self-contained mythic site where destiny is literally cultivated.

citations.text: H. R. Ellis Davidson, *Gods and Myths of Northern Europe* (1964).

title: The Wheel of Fortune (Lydgate's Troy Book)
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Anonymous (English illuminator)
summary:
A detail of Fortune's wheel from a manuscript of John Lydgate's *Troy Book*, the great English verse history of the siege of Troy.

## Iconography
**Fortuna** turns the **wheel** bearing the figures of the rising, reigning, and falling, here applied to the heroes and kings of the Trojan War whose glory is overturned. The image gathers the whole story's tragedies under the single sign of inconstant Fortune.

## Significance
Lydgate, a Benedictine monk and the most prolific English poet of the fifteenth century, framed his *Troy Book* (1412–20) within the *De casibus* tradition he inherited from Boccaccio: the fall of Troy becomes the supreme exemplum of how [[motif:wheel-of-fortune|Fortune]] humbles even the greatest. The motif passed from such manuscripts into the 'Mirror for Magistrates' and Elizabethan drama.

## For artists & game designers
A literary-historical wheel—useful for showing how a single fate-icon can index an entire epic of reversals.

citations.text: H. R. Patch, *The Goddess Fortuna in Mediaeval Literature* (1927).

title: The Planet Saturn and His Influence on the World
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Anonymous (Netherlandish engraver)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A 'children of the planets' engraving showing Saturn and the human activities, ages and temperaments governed by his influence (Rijksmuseum).

## Iconography
**Saturn** rides across the sky in his chariot bearing a **scythe**, with his zodiacal houses **Capricorn** and **Aquarius**; below, the 'children of Saturn' labour at the harshest tasks—digging, tanning, imprisonment, old age and death—the melancholic, earthbound lives ruled by the planet.

## Significance
The **Planetenkinder** ('children of the planets') series mapped each planet's astral influence onto the trades and temperaments of those born under it. As lord of [[motif:melancholy|melancholy]], cold, age and toil, [[motif:saturn-chronos|Saturn]]—simultaneously the devouring Titan Kronos and the planet of time—governed the lowest and most laborious lives, as analyzed in Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl's *Saturn and Melancholy*.

## For artists & game designers
A single sheet that links a celestial body to a whole social stratum—useful for a planet-rules-its-people worldbuilding mechanic.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

title: The Three Parcae (Fates) with a Grieving Woman
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Giovanni Francesco Romanelli
rights: Public Domain Mark. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Giovanni Francesco Romanelli's drawing of the three Parcae (Fates) attending a grieving female figure, a Baroque treatment of the spinners of destiny.

## Iconography
The three **Fates**—**Clotho** with the distaff who spins the thread of life, **Lachesis** who measures it, and **Atropos** with the **shears** who cuts it—work the single **thread** that is a mortal's lifespan, while a sorrowing woman looks on, her destiny in their hands.

## Significance
The Greek **Moirai** (Roman **Parcae**)—Clotho, Lachesis and Atropos—spin, allot and sever the thread of every human life, a fate to which even the gods submit. Romanelli, a pupil of Pietro da Cortona, renders the classical [[motif:the-three-fates|trio]] in elegant Baroque line; the grieving woman makes explicit that the spinning of the thread is also the spinning of grief and mortality.

## For artists & game designers
The spin–measure–cut division of labour gives three distinct character roles (creator, allotter, ender) for a single life-and-death mechanic.

title: The Tyche of Antioch
work: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: Roman copy after Eutychides of Sicyon
date: Roman copy (c. 1st–2nd c. CE) after a bronze of c. 300 BCE
rights: Public domain. Vatican Museums, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Roman marble copy (Vatican) of Eutychides's celebrated bronze *Tyche of Antioch*, the personified Fortune of the city.

## Iconography
**Tyche** sits on a rock wearing a **mural crown** (a circuit of city walls) and holding sheaves of grain or a **cornucopia**; at her feet swims the youthful **river-god Orontes**, the river of Antioch. Her seated, turning pose conveys the protective good fortune of a great city.

## Significance
Created c. 300 BCE by **Eutychides**, a pupil of Lysippos, for the new Seleucid capital, the *Tyche of Antioch* became the model for the civic **Tyche / Fortuna** of cities throughout the Greco-Roman world. The mural crown marks her as the embodied [[motif:fortuna|Fortune]] and guardian of the polis—fate localized as the luck of a place.

## For artists & game designers
The mural-crowned, river-god-at-the-feet formula is a ready template for a personified 'genius of the city' or settlement-guardian figure.

citations.text: Pausanias on Eutychides; T. Dohrn, *Die Tyche von Antiochia* (1960).

title: Euclid I.47 (The 47th Problem) — Vatican Manuscript
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Euclid (manuscript copyist)
summary:
A manuscript page of Euclid's *Elements*, Book I, Proposition 47 — the Pythagorean theorem — which Freemasonry venerates as the '47th Problem of Euclid' and the badge of the Past Master.

## Iconography
The diagram shows a right-angled triangle with a square erected on each of its three sides, the classic 'windmill' figure that demonstrates the equality of the square on the hypotenuse to the sum of the squares on the other two sides, accompanied by Greek demonstrative text.

## Significance
Masonic tradition, following the legend that Pythagoras cried *Eureka!* on discovering the proof, adopted the [[motif:47th problem of euclid|47th Problem]] as the emblem of the Past Master and a symbol of the love of the arts and sciences. It encapsulates the Craft's reverence for [[motif:geometry|geometry]] as the foundational and divine science 'whereon Masonry is founded.'

## For artists & game designers
A geometry-as-power glyph: the three-square figure can serve as a craftable emblem whose correct construction unlocks a 'master' tier.

title: Illustration from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Malcolm C. Duncan
summary:
A diagram from Malcolm C. Duncan's *Masonic Ritual and Monitor* (1866), the celebrated printed exposure that illustrated the signs, words, and ceremonies of the degrees.

## Iconography
The figure presents ritual instruction in printed form — a hieroglyphic or word-diagram (here relating to the Royal Arch word) set among explanatory text describing the postures, grips, and passwords of a degree.

## Significance
Duncan's *Monitor* is the best-known of the nineteenth-century printed 'exposures' that, despite the Craft's secrecy, set its rituals and [[motif:ritual monitor|monitorial lectures]] in cheap, widely circulated books. Whatever their accuracy, such works fixed in popular print the gestures, mottoes, and degree-lore of Freemasonry, and remain a primary source for the form of the American ritual.

## For artists & game designers
An 'unlocked secret' document: a diagram of signs and passwords the player can study to bluff their way past gatekeepers.

citations.text: Malcolm C. Duncan, *Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor* (1866).

title: The All-Seeing Eye of Providence (Lodge Decoration)
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (photographed by Michael Coghlan)
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (Michael Coghlan). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Masonic lodge ornament of the All-Seeing Eye of Providence, the radiant single eye that watches over the assembled brethren.

## Iconography
A single human eye, set within a triangle or burst of golden rays and clouds, gazes outward; the motto bids the visitor welcome under its watch. The eye is surrounded by a 'glory' of beams, the standard emblem of divine effulgence.

## Significance
The [[motif:all-seeing eye|All-Seeing Eye]] represents the ever-watchful providence of the Great Architect, reminding the Mason that thoughts and deeds are observed by the Most High. Though far older than the Craft — rooted in Renaissance and biblical imagery of the divine eye — it was adopted by Freemasonry in the later eighteenth century and, via the Great Seal of the United States, passed into the broadest popular iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A surveillance/conscience motif: a luminous eye that reacts to the player's moral choices, opening wider or clouding over.

citations.text: Albert G. Mackey, *An Encyclopædia of Freemasonry* (1874).

title: Hiram Abiff and the Pillar Boaz
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
An illustration of Hiram Abiff, the legendary master builder of Solomon's Temple, shown with one of the great pillars — the central figure of the Third Degree drama.

## Iconography
A robed craftsman stands beside the pillar Boaz, holding or gesturing toward the tools of his office. The composition identifies him as the master of the work, set against the architecture of the Temple he directs.

## Significance
[[motif:hiram abiff|Hiram Abiff]], the 'widow's son' and chief artificer of the Temple, is murdered (in the Masonic legend) for refusing to betray the secrets of a Master Mason; his death, burial, and raising form the allegorical heart of the Craft. Though the elaborated legend is a speculative-era construction built around the biblical Hiram of Tyre, it furnishes Freemasonry with its myth of fidelity, loss, and recovery.

## For artists & game designers
A tragic-mentor archetype: a master craftsman NPC whose death sets the central quest — recovering 'that which was lost' — in motion.

title: The Brazen Pillars Jachin and Boaz
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (architectural reconstruction)
summary:
A reconstruction of the two brazen pillars, Jachin and Boaz, that stood at the porch of Solomon's Temple and which Freemasonry sets at the entrance of every symbolic lodge.

## Iconography
Two free-standing columns rise side by side, each crowned by a capital decorated with lily-work, pomegranates, and chains of network, surmounted by a terrestrial and a celestial globe. The pillars are figured exactly as described in the Books of Kings and Chronicles.

## Significance
[[motif:jachin|Jachin]] ('He shall establish') and [[motif:boaz|Boaz]] ('In strength') frame the entrance to the lodge as they framed the Temple porch; the Mason passes between establishment and strength to reach the holy place. The crowning globes, terrestrial and celestial, were read as emblems of the universality of Masonry and the comprehensiveness of its science.

## For artists & game designers
A threshold gate: two named pillars the player must pass between, each granting a paired attribute (stability vs. force) that shapes the route ahead.

citations.text: 1 Kings 7:15–22; Albert G. Mackey, *Encyclopædia of Freemasonry* (1874).

title: Léo Taxil, *La Bible amusante* (Anti-clerical/Anti-Masonic Hoaxer)
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Léo Taxil (Gabriel Jogand-Pagès)
summary:
An illustration from a work by Léo Taxil (Gabriel Jogand-Pagès), the French writer notorious for the great anti-Masonic 'Palladism' hoax of the 1890s.

## Iconography
The caricatural plate (from his anticlerical *La Bible amusante*) shows Taxil's mocking satirical manner — the same pen later turned to lurid, invented exposés of Masonic 'devil worship.'

## Significance
[[motif:leo taxil|Léo Taxil]] first attacked the Church, then publicly 'converted' and fabricated an elaborate myth of a satanic Masonic inner order, *Palladism*, complete with a fictitious priestess, [[motif:diana vaughan|Diana Vaughan]]. In 1897 he triumphantly confessed the whole thing was a hoax designed to ridicule credulous anti-Masons and clerics alike. The episode is the classic case of the [[motif:devil worship myth|satanic-Freemasonry myth]], a conspiracy fantasy whose imagery still circulates.

## For artists & game designers
The unreliable-source trope made literal: a propaganda pamphlet whose 'revelations' the player slowly discovers were invented to manipulate them.

citations.text: Eugen Weber, *Satan franc-maçon: la mystification de Léo Taxil* (1964).

title: Masonic Lodge Interior with Mosaic Pavement
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (photographed by UpdateNerd)
summary:
The interior of a Masonic lodge room, showing the chequered floor and the arrangement of the symbolic temple in which the degrees are worked.

## Iconography
The room is laid out as a symbolic [[motif:temple of solomon|Temple]]: a black-and-white [[motif:mosaic pavement|mosaic pavement]] underfoot, the Master's chair raised 'in the East,' the Wardens' stations to West and South, and pillars or columns marking the officers' places, the whole bordered with tessellated ornament.

## Significance
Every Craft lodge is furnished as a microcosm of Solomon's Temple and, beyond it, of the ordered cosmos. The [[motif:mosaic pavement|mosaic pavement]] of alternating light and dark squares represents the mingling of good and evil in human life; the orientation toward the East, source of light, governs the placement of officers and the movement of ritual. The room is itself a three-dimensional tracing board.

## For artists & game designers
A ready blueprint for a ritual chamber: a checker-floored hall with cardinal stations the player circumambulates in a fixed ceremonial order.

title: Masonic Apron of Major William Christy
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (American lodge regalia)
summary:
The personal Masonic apron of Major William Christy, a painted lambskin regalia apron crowded with the Craft's emblems.

## Iconography
The white apron bears a flap and a square field painted with a dense programme of symbols — the [[motif:square and compasses|square and compasses]], the [[motif:all-seeing eye|All-Seeing Eye]] in its glory, the two pillars, columns, the beehive, and instruments of the work — bordered with rosettes and tassels.

## Significance
The lambskin apron is the first and most enduring badge presented to a Mason, emblem of the innocence and labour proper to the Craft and said to be 'more ancient than the Golden Fleece.' Hand-painted aprons like this one, common in early American lodges, served as portable tracing boards, the wearer carrying the whole symbolic cosmos on his person.

## For artists & game designers
A wearable inventory of insignia — an apron whose painted emblems can be 'earned' and added one by one as the character advances.

citations.text: Aimee E. Newell, *The Badge of a Freemason: Masonic Aprons from the Scottish Rite Masonic Museum* (2015).

title: Masonic Certificate, Grand United Order of Odd Fellows
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Popular Graphic Arts (lithographer)
summary:
An engraved fraternal membership certificate of the Grand United Order of Odd Fellows, bearing the motto *Amicitia, Amor et Veritas* (Friendship, Love and Truth).

## Iconography
The sheet frames a central text panel with allegorical personifications and dense emblematic ornament — clasped hands, hearts, the all-seeing eye, tools and regalia of the order — arranged in a symmetrical border around the motto and the member's name.

## Significance
Fraternal certificates and diplomas were the portable proof of initiation across the nineteenth-century lodge societies — Masonry, Odd Fellowship, and their many affiliates — that shared a common visual vocabulary of clasped hands, mottoes, and moral emblems. The G.U.O.O.F. was a major African-American fraternal order; its handsome engraved certificates show how the [[motif:fraternal certificate|emblematic diploma]] carried the order's ideals of [[motif:friendship love truth|friendship, love, and truth]].

## For artists & game designers
An ornate in-world credential — a printed diploma whose emblem-border can be 'read' for the bearer's affiliations and rank.

citations.text: Mark A. Tabbert, *American Freemasons: Three Centuries of Building Communities* (2005).

title: Master Mason's Chart (Third Section)
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A Master Mason's chart illustrating the monitorial emblems of the Third Degree's lecture, the moving symbols of mortality and virtue.

## Iconography
The chart sets out the classic Third Degree emblems in sequence: the [[motif:pot of incense|pot of incense]], the beehive, the book of constitutions guarded by the Tyler's sword, the sword pointing to a naked heart, the all-seeing eye, the anchor and ark, the [[motif:hourglass|hourglass]], the scythe, and the three steps — each a 'moving image' of human life and duty.

## Significance
These emblems derive from the monitors of Thomas Smith Webb and Jeremy Cross, who systematised the American 'monitorial' lectures around 1800. The chart compresses the degree's meditation on time, death, and moral watchfulness into a memorable visual catechism — the [[motif:hourglass|hourglass]] and scythe marking the brevity of life, the anchor and ark the hope of a well-spent one.

## For artists & game designers
A panel of status icons each tied to a virtue or mortality theme — a ready-made HUD of moral 'meters' for a contemplative game.

citations.text: Jeremy L. Cross, *The True Masonic Chart, or Hieroglyphic Monitor* (1819).

title: Emblematic Chart and Masonic History (Ramsey, Millett & Hudson)
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Ramsey, Millett & Hudson Steam Lith. Co.
summary:
A large lithographed 'Emblematic Chart and Masonic History of Free and Accepted Masons,' a compendious wall print gathering the Craft's symbols into a single didactic composition.

## Iconography
The chart arrays the working tools, degree emblems, pillars, the all-seeing eye, the ark, the beehive, and scenes of Temple history across a panelled field, each cell captioned, so that the entire symbolic system can be surveyed at a glance.

## Significance
Mass-produced chromolithographs like this one, issued by commercial steam-lithography firms in the Gilded Age, democratised Masonic iconography, hanging the whole [[motif:compendium of symbols|compendium of symbols]] on lodge and parlour walls. They functioned as printed encyclopedias of the Craft, fixing a standard repertoire of emblems in the popular American imagination.

## For artists & game designers
A reference plate for an entire symbol-set: useful as an in-game 'codex' screen where each emblem is a tappable lore entry.

title: Past Master's Jewel (Mount Anthony Lodge No. 13)
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous silversmith
summary:
A Past Master's jewel presented to Judge Charles H. Darling on his retirement as Master of Mount Anthony Lodge No. 13, Bennington, Vermont, in 1899.

## Iconography
The pendant jewel carries the emblem proper to the office of Past Master — a pair of compasses opened on a quadrant (or a representation of the 47th Problem of Euclid) — suspended from a ribbon and engraved with a presentation dedication.

## Significance
Masonic [[motif:officers jewel|jewels]] are the metal badges of lodge office, each office distinguished by its emblem: the square for the Master, the level for the Senior Warden, the plumb for the Junior Warden, and the [[motif:47th problem emblem|compasses-and-quadrant]] or Euclidean figure for the Past Master. Presentation jewels like this commemorated faithful service, blending the symbolic vocabulary of the Craft with the personal honour of an individual brother.

## For artists & game designers
A rank-insignia object: a pendant whose distinct emblem instantly signals an NPC's office and history within the lodge hierarchy.

title: Masonic Triangle (Bicentennial Plate, 1789–1989)
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Jebulon
summary:
A French Masonic plate built around the luminous triangle (the *delta*), commemorating the bicentennial 1789–1989 with the symbols of the Craft.

## Iconography
A radiant triangle — often enclosing an eye or the divine name — sits at the centre, flanked and underlaid by the working tools: square, compasses, level, and plumb. The arrangement is geometric and heraldic, the triangle crowning the assembly of emblems.

## Significance
The [[motif:masonic triangle|luminous delta]] is the Masonic figure of deity and of geometric perfection — three sides for the ternary that recurs throughout the Craft (the three degrees, three lights, three principal officers). In French Freemasonry especially, the radiant triangle over the Master's chair focuses the lodge's contemplation of the Great Architect, and its conjunction here with 1789 ties the fraternity to the Enlightenment and the Revolution.

## For artists & game designers
A glowing triangular emblem suited to a 'sanctum' marker; rotating its tools could encode different rites or factions.

title: *Freemasonry Exposed* (William Morgan), Front Cover
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Capt. William Morgan
summary:
The front cover of *Freemasonry Exposed* (often *Illustrations of Masonry*), the 1826 exposure attributed to Captain William Morgan whose suppression ignited the American anti-Masonic movement.

## Iconography
A plain printed title page announces the disclosure of the secrets of the Craft 'by one of the fraternity,' the typographic cover promising to lay bare the hidden signs, grips, and ceremonies of the order.

## Significance
Morgan's threatened publication led to his abduction and presumed murder in western New York in 1826 — the [[motif:morgan affair|Morgan affair]] — which provoked a wave of public outrage, the founding of the [[motif:anti-masonic movement|Anti-Masonic Party]] (America's first third party), and a sharp decline in lodge membership. The book and the scandal around it made Freemasonry, for a generation, the focus of a national controversy over secrecy and republican virtue.

## For artists & game designers
A forbidden book whose mere existence triggers a faction-wide manhunt — a plot device for an exposure-and-pursuit storyline.

title: The Square and Compasses
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (modern rendering)
date: symbol c. 18th c.; this rendering modern
summary:
The Square and Compasses, the single most recognisable emblem of speculative Freemasonry, here in a clean graphic rendering of the interlaced instruments.

## Iconography
A carpenter's square and a pair of compasses are interlocked to form a lozenge, the square opening upward and the compasses straddling it; in many versions the letter **G** sits at the centre. The two working tools — borrowed from operative stonemasonry — are arranged so that the points and arms enclose a shared field.

## Significance
The emblem condenses the Craft's [[motif:moral allegory|moral allegory]]: the square teaches the Mason to act 'on the square' (uprightly), the compasses to circumscribe his passions within due bounds. The central **G** is glossed as both Geometry — the divine science — and God, the Great Architect of the Universe. Adopted across Anglo-American and Continental lodges by the later eighteenth century, it became the badge of the fraternity worldwide.

## For artists & game designers
A faction sigil that reads instantly at any size; the openness of the compass legs can encode rank (the degree is signalled by which limb sits above the square).

citations.text: Albert G. Mackey, *An Encyclopædia of Freemasonry* (1874).

title: First Degree Tracing Board (Engraving)
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous engraver
summary:
An engraving of a First Degree (Entered Apprentice) Masonic tracing board, the illustrated teaching-chart laid before candidates in the opening degree (c. 1850–1900).

## Iconography
The board crowds the lodge's emblems into one scene: the two pillars at the porchway, a chequered [[motif:mosaic pavement|mosaic pavement]], Jacob's Ladder ascending to a [[motif:blazing star|blazing star]] amid clouds, the three lesser lights, and assorted working tools. A border of indented tessels frames the whole.

## Significance
Tracing boards descend from the chalk designs once drawn on the lodge floor and erased after the meeting; by the nineteenth century they had become painted or engraved panels carrying the symbolic 'furniture' of each degree. The First Degree board maps the moral cosmos the [[motif:entered apprentice|Entered Apprentice]] is taught to read — ladder of virtues, pillars of strength and establishment, the pavement of light and dark.

## For artists & game designers
A single image that doubles as a level legend: every emblem on the board corresponds to a lesson or station the player will later encounter in sequence.

citations.text: Julian Rees, *Tracing Boards of the Three Degrees in Craft Freemasonry Explained* (2009).

title: Second Degree Tracing Board, Josiah Bowring
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Josiah Bowring (1757–1832)
summary:
The Second Degree (Fellowcraft) tracing board painted by Josiah Bowring, whose designs became standard models for the Emulation working (Museum of Freemasonry, London).

## Iconography
A [[motif:winding staircase|winding staircase]] of fifteen steps rises between the two great pillars toward a doorway, a flow of water and an ear of corn set beside it. The stair's divisions allude to the three, five, and seven, and the porch leads to the 'Middle Chamber' where the Fellowcraft receives his wages.

## Significance
The Fellowcraft degree dramatises the pursuit of knowledge: the ascent of the staircase past the orders of architecture and the [[motif:seven liberal arts|seven liberal arts]] toward hidden wisdom. Bowring's boards, produced in the early nineteenth century, fixed the visual canon of the English Craft so thoroughly that later lodges commissioned copies of his compositions for generations.

## For artists & game designers
The winding stair is a ready-made progression mechanic: a numbered ascent (3-5-7) where each landing gates a new skill or revelation.

citations.text: Julian Rees, *Tracing Boards of the Three Degrees in Craft Freemasonry Explained* (2009).

title: Third Degree Tracing Board (Master Mason)
work: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
The Third Degree (Master Mason) tracing board, centred on the grave of Hiram Abiff and the symbols of mortality and raising (Museum of Freemasonry, London).

## Iconography
The panel is dominated by a [[motif:coffin|coffin]] or open grave, often inscribed with emblems of mortality — [[motif:skull and bones|skull and crossbones]] — and marked by a [[motif:sprig of acacia|sprig of acacia]]. Compasses, a setting maul, and other instruments of the Hiramic legend lie about; the whole is rendered in sombre tones against a dark ground.

## Significance
The Master Mason's degree enacts the death and raising of [[motif:hiram abiff|Hiram Abiff]], the architect of Solomon's Temple, as an allegory of fidelity, the inevitability of death, and the hope of a 'raising' to come. The acacia marking the grave signals both discovery of the body and the immortality of the soul. This board is the lodge's *memento mori*, the visual climax of the Craft system.

## For artists & game designers
A death-and-resurrection set piece: a grave scene whose scattered tools are clues to a reconstruction puzzle the player solves to be 'raised'.

citations.text: Julian Rees, *Tracing Boards of the Three Degrees in Craft Freemasonry Explained* (2009).

title: Ficino's *Pimander* — title page of *De Potestate et Sapientia Dei*
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Marsilio Ficino (translator); early printed edition
summary:
The title page of Marsilio Ficino's Latin *Pimander* — *Mercurii Trismegisti Liber de Potestate et Sapientia Dei* — the version through which the *Corpus Hermeticum* re-entered the Western imagination.

## Iconography
A letterpress title page declaring the work of Hermes Trismegistus 'on the power and wisdom of God', translated by Ficino. The page is typographic rather than pictorial — the artefact's importance is bibliographic, marking the printed transmission of the Hermetic dialogues.

## Significance
Cosimo de' Medici had Ficino break off his Plato to translate a Greek Hermetic manuscript first; the resulting *Pimander*, completed in 1463 and first printed in 1471, was a publishing sensation that ran through dozens of editions. It cast [[motif:hermes trismegistus|Hermes Trismegistus]] as the fount of a *prisca theologia* and made the [[motif:corpus hermeticum|Corpus Hermeticum]] central to Renaissance Neoplatonism — until Casaubon redated the texts to late antiquity in 1614.

## For artists & game designers
A convincing 'forbidden translation' book-prop: the moment a lost ancient text is rendered legible and unleashed upon a culture.

title: *Tabula Smaragdina* frontispiece, Khunrath's *Amphitheatrum* (Houghton copy)
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Heinrich Khunrath (engraving in his *Amphitheatrum*)
summary:
An engraved plate from Heinrich Khunrath's *Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae* presenting the *Tabula Smaragdina* (Emerald Tablet) within his theosophical-alchemical programme (Harvard Houghton copy).

## Iconography
The plate sets the sentences of the Emerald Tablet amid Khunrath's characteristic apparatus of divine names, radiant geometry, and emblematic frame, expounding the Hermetic axiom that the things above answer the things below. Latin captions key the tablet's clauses to the stages of the alchemical work.

## Significance
The *Tabula Smaragdina* — 'that which is above is as that which is below' ([[motif:as above so below|as above, so below]]) — was the compressed creed of alchemy, and Khunrath wove it into the Christian-theosophical synthesis of his *Amphitheatrum*. (This is a distinct engraving from the Deutsche-Fotothek Emerald-Tablet emblem; here the tablet appears within Khunrath's own published frame.) Peter Forshaw's studies anchor the modern reading of the work.

## For artists & game designers
A single emblem-card combining a terse axiom-text with radiant geometry — a 'law of the world' artifact the player can study to unlock correspondences.

citations.text: Peter Forshaw, studies on Heinrich Khunrath (2000s).

title: *Mercurius Trismegistus*, engraving from Boissard's *Tractatus posthumus*
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Johann Theodor de Bry (engraver), after J. J. Boissard
summary:
An engraved portrait of *Mercurius Trismegistus* from Jean-Jacques Boissard's posthumous *Tractatus … de divinatione et magicis praestigiis*, engraved by the de Bry workshop (c. 1615).

## Iconography
Hermes appears as a dignified bearded sage in antique dress, set within an emblematic frame and identified by inscription as Mercurius Trismegistus. He is shown as the prototype magus and lawgiver, an attribute-tablet or book to hand, in the refined burin style of Johann Theodor de Bry.

## Significance
Boissard's antiquarian compendium gathered portraits and lore of the magical and divinatory traditions; its placement of [[motif:hermes trismegistus|Hermes Trismegistus]] at the head of the diviners reflects the early-modern view of him as founder of magic and *prisca theologia*. The de Bry firm at Oppenheim, also responsible for engraving Fludd and Maier, made such Hermetic portraits a staple of the learned esoteric book.

## For artists & game designers
A portrait-card template for a 'founder of the arcane arts' character: antique robes, framing cartouche, identifying scroll.

citations.text: J. J. Boissard, *Tractatus posthumus de divinatione et magicis praestigiis* (Oppenheim, c. 1615).

title: *Trismegistos* — printed depiction of Hermes Trismegistus
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (early-modern print)
summary:
An early-modern printed image of *Trismegistos* — Hermes the Thrice-Great — representing the mythic author of the Hermetic corpus.

## Iconography
Hermes is figured as a venerable robed sage, the conventional attributes of the Hermetic author — book or tablet and an air of ancient authority — establishing his identity. The image belongs to the dense print tradition that disseminated his likeness across early-modern learned literature.

## Significance
The epithet *Trismegistus*, 'thrice-greatest', marked [[motif:hermes trismegistus|Hermes]] as the supposed Egyptian master of philosophy, priesthood, and kingship and the fountainhead of the [[motif:corpus hermeticum|Hermetica]]. Such portraits multiplied through the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, cementing his role as patron of alchemy, astrology, and magic in the European esoteric imagination.

## For artists & game designers
A simple, legible 'archmage of antiquity' silhouette — robe, beard, tablet — for use as a faction emblem or lore portrait.

title: Hermes Trismegistus, Siena Cathedral pavement (Giovanni di Stefano)
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Giovanni di Stefano (designed by, attrib.)
summary:
The marble-intarsia panel of *Hermes Trismegistus* set into the pavement of Siena Cathedral (1488), the most monumental Renaissance image of the legendary Egyptian sage.

## Iconography
A robed, bearded Hermes hands a tablet to two figures while resting his other hand on a raised book or slab inscribed in Latin. The flanking inscription hails him *Hermes Mercurius Trismegistus contemporaneus Moysi* — "contemporary of Moses" — and a second tablet declares that he foretold the coming of Christianity. The whole is executed in inlaid coloured marble (*commesso*).

## Significance
The panel embodies the Renaissance doctrine of *prisca theologia*: the belief, encouraged by Ficino's translation of the [[motif:hermes trismegistus|Hermetica]], that an ancient gentile wisdom anticipated Christian truth. Placing Hermes at the very entrance of the cathedral, ahead of the Sibyls along the nave, monumentalised the conviction — only later overturned by Isaac Casaubon — that the Corpus Hermeticum was older than Moses.

## For artists & game designers
A grand foyer-floor motif: a robed lawgiver presenting a tablet, ideal for a temple threshold that frames the player's whole quest as ancient prophecy.

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition* (1964).

title: Hermes Trismegistus and the *Table d'Émeraude*, decorated initial
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (early printed book; Library of Congress)
summary:
An early printed page joining a portrait of *Hermes Trismegistus*, 'father of philosophy', to the *Table d'Émeraude* (Emerald Tablet) within a decorated historiated initial L (Library of Congress).

## Iconography
A portrait of the sage is worked into an ornamented capital letter, set beside the text of the [[motif:emerald tablet|Emerald Tablet]] presented as his foundational utterance. The page weds figural portrait and decorated typography in the manner of an apologetic preface defending the Hermetic art.

## Significance
Here Hermes is explicitly cast as *father of philosophy* and author of the Emerald Tablet, the terse axiom-text ('as above, so below') that the alchemical tradition ascribed to him. The pairing typifies how early-modern printers framed alchemy with Hermetic authority, lending the practical art the dignity of a primordial revelation. The standard study of the tablet's textual history is Julius Ruska's.

## For artists & game designers
A decorated drop-cap device — a portrait nested in an illuminated letter — useful for chapter-headers in an in-game grimoire.

title: Jung Codex (Nag Hammadi Codex I) — *Gospel of Truth* papyrus
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (Coptic scribe)
summary:
A papyrus leaf (page 17) of Nag Hammadi Codex I — the celebrated *Jung Codex* — carrying part of the *Gospel of Truth*, a Valentinian meditation often attributed to Valentinus himself.

## Iconography
A single tall column of Coptic majuscule fills the leaf, the fibres of the papyrus running visibly behind the ink. There is no illustration; the artefact's interest is textual and material — a fragment of the most lyrical of Gnostic writings.

## Significance
Codex I acquired the name *Jung Codex* because it was purchased in 1952 and presented to C. G. Jung, whose Zürich institute first published it before it was reunited with the rest of the library in Cairo. The *Gospel of Truth* expounds [[motif:gnosis|gnosis]] as the dispelling of ignorance — error personified flees as the saving knowledge of the Father is revealed — making it a key witness to [[motif:sophia and the aeons|Valentinian]] theology.

## For artists & game designers
A lore-fragment prop: a torn page of glowing script whose recovery unlocks a faction's true creation-myth.

citations.text: Harold W. Attridge (ed.), *Nag Hammadi Codex I (The Jung Codex)* (1985).

title: Mandaean *masbuta* in the Karun River, Ahvaz, Iran (2018)
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Mehdi Pedramkhoo (photographer)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Mehdi Pedramkhoo). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A modern colour photograph of the Mandaean *masbuta* performed in the Karun River at Ahvaz, Iran, in 2018 — a living continuation of the rite shown in the 1925 image.

## Iconography
Congregants in the white *rasta* ritual garment wade into the river as a priest performs the baptism in the flowing water. The contemporary setting demonstrates that the [[motif:masbuta|masbuta]] remains the centre of Mandaean devotional life nearly a century after the early ethnographic photographs.

## Significance
The Mandaean community, scattered by modern upheavals in Iraq and Iran, still anchors its identity in baptism in [[motif:living water|flowing water]] and in the *Ginza Rabba*. As the last surviving ancient Gnostic religion — venerating John the Baptist and rejecting the demiurgic powers — its persistence is of singular importance to the study of Gnosticism, much of which is otherwise known only from antiquity.

## For artists & game designers
A contemporary counterpoint for any 'ancient sect still practising' worldbuilding: modern dress at the riverbank, the rite unchanged.

citations.text: Jorunn J. Buckley, *The Mandaeans: Ancient Texts and Modern People* (2002).

title: Mandaean Triple Baptism (*masbuta*), photographed in Iraq, 1925
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (E. S. Drower-era documentary photograph)
summary:
An early documentary photograph of the Mandaean *masbuta* (baptism) in Iraq, here the threefold baptism by which a ritually polluted priest is purified.

## Iconography
White-robed Mandaeans stand in flowing river water as a priest administers the rite; the scene captures the indispensable role of [[motif:living water|living water]] (*yardna*) in Mandaean practice. Photographs of this kind were gathered in the early twentieth century during fieldwork on the community.

## Significance
The Mandaeans of southern Iraq and Iran are the only Gnostic religion to survive continuously from antiquity, revering John the Baptist and condemning the false creator-powers. Their frequent baptism in running water enacts a cosmology of light and the World of Light against the dark. The classic studies are by E. S. Drower, who edited the Mandaean scriptures and documented the rites; the *Ginza Rabba* is their central scripture.

## For artists & game designers
A living ritual reference: white-robed celebrants and a moving-water font, the antithesis of the stagnant-pool 'corruption' trope.

citations.text: E. S. Drower, *The Mandaeans of Iraq and Iran* (1937).

title: Manichaean *Diagram of the Universe* (Detail) — the spheres of the cosmos
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (Chinese Manichaean painter)
summary:
A horizontal detail strip from the Chinese Manichaean *Diagram of the Universe*, isolating one band of the cosmic machine through which the light is purified and raised.

## Iconography
The detail shows a register of the firmaments crowded with deities, registers of cloud, and the conduits along which redeemed light-particles travel. Figures process across the band in the conventions of Chinese religious painting, the whole forming one storey of the great vertical cosmogram.

## Significance
The complete scroll diagrams the Manichaean myth of salvation as a cosmic apparatus: the [[motif:sun and moon vessels|sun and moon]] are ships that ferry liberated light upward toward the [[motif:redemption of light|New Paradise]]. Such painted teaching-pictures realised Mani's own claim to have illustrated his doctrine, a tradition reconstructed by Zsuzsanna Gulácsi from the dispersed Chinese and Central Asian remains.

## For artists & game designers
A conveyor-belt cosmology: a horizontal track of cloud-tiers along which collected 'light' resources flow upward to be banked.

title: *Daênâ with Her Attendants*, Manichaean Sermon silk painting
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (Chinese Manichaean painter)
summary:
A detail of *Daênâ with her attendants* from a Chinese Manichaean 'sermon' silk painting, depicting the radiant soul-maiden who meets the elect after death.

## Iconography
A crowned female light-divinity stands haloed amid smaller attendants, all in flowing robes painted in fine Chinese line and colour. She personifies the Daênâ — the heavenly self or 'Light-Maiden' — who greets the righteous soul and conducts it upward, a motif Manichaeism inherited from the Iranian religious world.

## Significance
The Manichaean 'sermon' paintings were didactic icons used in preaching, dramatising the fate of the soul. The figure of [[motif:daena|Daênâ]], the soul's own purified image come to meet it, links Mani's church to older Zoroastrian eschatology and to the wider Gnostic theme of the heavenly twin or [[motif:soul-maiden|Light-Maiden]]. Gulácsi and Kósa have reconstructed these scenes from the surviving Japanese-held scrolls.

## For artists & game designers
A psychopomp NPC: a luminous guide-figure with a retinue who appears at the moment of death to escort or judge the player-soul.

citations.text: Gábor Kósa, studies on the Chinese Manichaean silk paintings (2010s).

title: Manichaean *Diagram of the Universe* (Cosmology — Heaven Scene)
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (Chinese Manichaean painter)
summary:
The upper, celestial register of the Chinese Manichaean *Diagram of the Universe*, a large Yuan-era silk hanging scroll that maps the entire Manichaean cosmos.

## Iconography
Tiered bands of palatial heavens rise toward the realm of the Father of Greatness, each storey populated by enthroned light-divinities and guardian figures painted in the idiom of Chinese Buddhist temple art. The scene belongs to a vertical cosmogram that descends through the firmaments, the wheels of sun and moon, the earthly world, and the prisons of darkness below.

## Significance
Manichaeism, founded by the prophet Mani in third-century Sasanian Mesopotamia, taught a thoroughgoing [[motif:dualism|dualism]] of Light and Darkness and an elaborate machinery for the [[motif:redemption of light|liberation of the light]] trapped in matter. This silk, rediscovered in Japan and studied by Zsuzsanna Gulácsi and Yutaka Yoshida, is the only known complete pictorial cosmology of the religion to survive.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made vertical level select: ten stacked heavens above, sun-and-moon ferries in the middle, light-eating dungeons of darkness below.

title: Nag Hammadi Codex II (leather-bound papyrus codex)
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (Coptic scribes, Upper Egypt)
summary:
Nag Hammadi Codex II, one of the thirteen leather-bound papyrus books discovered near Nag Hammadi in Upper Egypt in 1945, the single richest cache of Gnostic scripture to survive from antiquity.

## Iconography
The object itself is the icon: a thick papyrus codex in a soft tooled-leather wrapper, its quires of Coptic script opened to a written page. Codex II famously contains the *Apocryphon of John*, the *Gospel of Thomas*, the *Gospel of Philip*, and the *Hypostasis of the Archons* — the texts are unillustrated, their power lying in the surviving artefact and its cramped sloping hand.

## Significance
The Nag Hammadi library transformed the study of [[motif:gnosis|Gnosticism]], which had previously been known almost only through the polemics of its opponents. Buried, probably by monks of the nearby Pachomian monasteries, around the time Athanasius's festal letter fixed the biblical canon, the codices preserve Sethian, Valentinian, and Hermetic works in Coptic translation. The standard edition is the Coptic Gnostic Library produced under James M. Robinson.

## For artists & game designers
The ur-image of the 'forbidden library' trove: a jar of leather books pulled from the desert, each volume a different heresy waiting to be opened.

citations.text: James M. Robinson (ed.), *The Nag Hammadi Library in English* (1977).

title: *Holy Wisdom* (Sophia) — Russian icon, 1812
work: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: Anonymous (Russian icon painter)
summary:
A Russian icon of *Holy Wisdom* (Sophia) from 1812, depicting Divine Wisdom as a crowned, winged figure enthroned in fire — a Christian counterpart to the Gnostic Aeon Sophia.

## Iconography
Sophia is enthroned at the centre as a crowned, fiery-faced winged being within a red aureole, flanked by Christ, the Virgin, and ranks of angels. The composition derives from the 'Sophia, Wisdom of God' iconographic type developed at Novgorod, in which abstract Wisdom is personified as a regal angelic presence.

## Significance
In the Gnostic systems, [[motif:sophia|Sophia]] is the last of the [[motif:sophia and the aeons|Aeons]] whose fall and longing precipitate the material cosmos and the false creator. The Eastern Christian veneration of Holy Wisdom — and the later Russian *sophiology* of Solovyov and Bulgakov — keeps alive, in orthodox form, the image of Wisdom as a divine feminine hypostasis mediating between God and creation.

## For artists & game designers
A luminous, winged 'Wisdom' deity-portrait: enthroned, fire-faced, flanked by an angelic court — a high-tier patron figure.

citations.text: Kurt Rudolph, *Gnosis: The Nature and History of Gnosticism* (1977).

title: The 72 Demons of the Ars Goetia (composite plate of seals)
work: Seals of the 72 Spirits (Ars Goetia)
creator: after the Lemegeton (Lesser Key of Solomon)
summary:
A composite plate arraying the seals of all seventy-two demons of the *Ars Goetia* in a single grid.

## Iconography
Seventy-two distinct line-sigils, each labelled with its spirit's name, ranked from Bael through Andromalius — the full roster of the *Lesser Key of Solomon*'s first book.

## Significance
The *Ars Goetia* catalogues 72 spirits, each with a name, a rank ([[motif:king|king]], [[motif:duke|duke]], prince, marquis, president, earl or knight), an office, a number of legions, and a proper seal used to conjure and bind it. The system descends from Johann Weyer's *Pseudomonarchia Daemonum* (1577) and was codified in the *Lemegeton*, popularised by Mathers and Crowley (1904).

## For artists & game designers
A single reference sheet for a full demon-summoning roster — 72 ranked entities each keyed to a unique glyph.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & Aleister Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: The Seal of Aim (Duke of the Goetia)
work: Seals of the 72 Spirits (Ars Goetia)
creator: after the Lemegeton; ed. S. L. MacGregor Mathers & Aleister Crowley
date: MS tradition 17th c.; this rendering after the 1904 edition
summary:
The seal (*sigillum*) of Aim, the duke ranked among the seventy-two spirits of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
An abstract line-glyph composed of crosses, loops, arcs and points — the character the conjurer engraves or draws to summon and bind the spirit, worn as a *Lamen* before the breast.

## Significance
In the *Ars Goetia*, the first book of the *Lesser Key of Solomon* (*Lemegeton*), Aim is a [[motif:duke|Duke]] commanding 26 legions. He also Aym or Haborym; sets cities and great places afire and makes men witty; appears three-headed, riding a viper, bearing a firebrand. The seal is his proper signature; with the Brass Vessel, the Magic Circle and Triangle, and the conjurations of S. L. MacGregor Mathers and Aleister Crowley's edition (1904), it compels his appearance.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made summoning glyph for a demon-binding system: each of the 72 spirits has a distinct seal, rank, and bargainable office.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & Aleister Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia: The Lesser Key of Solomon the King* (1904).
