title: The Seal of Bathin (Duke of the Goetia)
work: Seals of the 72 Spirits (Ars Goetia)
creator: after the Lemegeton; ed. S. L. MacGregor Mathers & Aleister Crowley
date: MS tradition 17th c.; this rendering after the 1904 edition
summary:
The seal (*sigillum*) of Bathin, the duke ranked among the seventy-two spirits of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
An abstract line-glyph composed of crosses, loops, arcs and points — the character the conjurer engraves or draws to summon and bind the spirit, worn as a *Lamen* before the breast.

## Significance
In the *Ars Goetia*, the first book of the *Lesser Key of Solomon* (*Lemegeton*), Bathin is a [[motif:duke|Duke]] commanding 30 legions. He knows the virtues of herbs and precious stones and can transport men swiftly between countries; appears mounted on a pale horse. The seal is his proper signature; with the Brass Vessel, the Magic Circle and Triangle, and the conjurations of S. L. MacGregor Mathers and Aleister Crowley's edition (1904), it compels his appearance.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made summoning glyph for a demon-binding system: each of the 72 spirits has a distinct seal, rank, and bargainable office.

title: Magical Gem Depicting Abrasax
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
summary:
An engraved magical gem (intaglio) bearing the anguipede deity Abrasax (Abraxas), the signature figure of Greco-Egyptian gem magic.

## Iconography
The composite god has a rooster's head, a human torso in armour, and two serpents for legs, usually holding a whip and a round shield. The shield or reverse often carries the name IAΩ or other [[motif:voces magicae|voces magicae]].

## Significance
The Greek letters of ABRASAX sum to 365, the days of the year, and the figure became associated with the Gnostic teacher Basilides and the cosmic ruler of the 365 heavens. As Campbell Bonner showed in *Studies in Magical Amulets*, such gems were worn for protection and were among the most reproduced magical images of antiquity, later mined by Renaissance and modern occultism.

## For artists & game designers
The rooster-snake-soldier hybrid is a ready-made arcane sigil — recognisable, weird, and rich with numerological lore (365).

title: Curse Tablets from Aquae Sulis, Bath
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Romano-British)
summary:
A display of lead curse tablets recovered from the sacred spring at Aquae Sulis (Bath), addressed to the goddess Sulis Minerva.

## Iconography
Dozens of small inscribed lead sheets, many concerning stolen property — cloaks, coins, gloves — beg the goddess to harm the thief until restitution is made. Several reverse the letters or list suspects.

## Significance
The Bath tablets, edited by Roger Tomlin, are the largest single group of Latin curse texts and the basis for the category Henk Versnel named "prayers for justice" — appeals to a deity for redress rather than purely aggressive [[motif:binding magic|binding magic]]. They are invaluable evidence for vulgar Latin and everyday religion in Roman Britain.

## For artists & game designers
The theft-curse — "whoever stole my cloak, let him pay" — is a perfect low-stakes magical quest hook grounded in real grievance.

title: Bronze Amulet of the God Bes (Museo Egizio C 672)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
summary:
A bronze figure of Bes from the Museo Egizio, Turin — the dwarf-god whose grotesque face guarded households, mothers and children, and who underlies the composite Bes-Pantheos magical figures.

## Iconography
A squat, frontal dwarf deity with a lion-like bearded face, plumed crown and protruding tongue. In magical contexts Bes proliferates into the "Bes-Pantheos": a many-armed, multi-winged, animal-headed composite gathering many gods' powers into one apotropaic body.

## Significance
Bes was protective rather than infernal — warding off snakes, demons and the dangers of childbirth — and his image survived from pharaonic Egypt into the Greco-Roman magical repertoire. The Bes-Pantheos figures on gems and papyri concentrate cosmic power for protection and were catalogued among Greco-Egyptian magical types by Bonner and the *Campbell Bonner Magical Gems Database*.

## For artists & game designers
Bes's grotesque-but-friendly face is a model for an apotropaic guardian whose ugliness is the point — the scarier the ward, the safer the home.

title: Bound Female Magical Figurine (Louvre E 27145)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
rights: CC BY 2.5 (Marie-Lan Nguyen). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Louvre's famous bound and pierced female figurine (E 27145), often loosely called a "voodoo doll," made for an erotic binding spell.

## Iconography
A kneeling nude woman of unbaked clay, arms bound behind her back, her body transfixed by thirteen needles driven into head, eyes, ears, mouth, breasts, hands, feet and genitals. She was found inside a clay vessel with a lead tablet inscribed with the spell.

## Significance
The figurine enacts PGM IV's instructions almost verbatim: pierce each part of the target so she can think of nothing but the operator. It is the most-cited object for the brutal mechanics of ancient [[motif:love magic|love magic]] and *katadesis*, discussed by Faraone, Gager and Daniel Ogden as the physical counterpart of the agōgē papyrus charms.

## For artists & game designers
A pierced figurine in a sealed jar is an instantly legible — and disquieting — magical object; the needle-per-body-part logic doubles as a ritual mini-game.

citations.text: Christopher A. Faraone, *Ancient Greek Love Magic* (1999).

title: Intaglio Gem of Chnoubis (BnF, de Clercq 3453)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
summary:
An engraved gem in the Bibliothèque nationale de France (de Clercq collection) showing Chnoubis, the lion-headed serpent of ancient healing magic.

## Iconography
A serpent with a radiate lion's head rears up, usually over a horizontal bar; the reverse frequently bears the triple-S-and-bar Chnoubis sign and his name. The figure draws on Egyptian solar and Agathodaimon imagery.

## Significance
Chnoubis gems were specifically therapeutic, worn against ailments of the stomach and digestion — Galen even mentions green jasper amulets for the stomach. As studied by Bonner, Simone Michel and Véronique Dasen, they show ancient magic operating as practical medicine, targeting the body part depicted. The recurring "Chnoubis sign" is one of the few standardised [[motif:charakteres|charaktêres]] of antiquity.

## For artists & game designers
A lion-serpent carved into green stone makes a believable healing talisman keyed to a specific affliction (the stomach).

title: Defixio Naming Rhodine and Faustus (Terme)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman)
summary:
A Roman lead curse tablet, held in the Museo Nazionale Romano (Terme di Diocleziano), that names Rhodine and Faustus.

## Iconography
The lead sheet bears a scratched Latin curse text invoking the dead or underworld powers against the named individuals. The hand is irregular, scored quickly into the metal.

## Significance
This tablet exemplifies the personal, often erotic or competitive grievances behind Roman *defixiones*: a named target is handed over to chthonic forces. The naming of specific persons makes such tablets, like the famous Rhodine curse, both legal-historical and emotional documents of ordinary Roman life, studied within Gager's and Audollent's corpora of *defixionum tabellae*.

## For artists & game designers
Use a real named target ("I bind Rhodine…") to make an in-game curse feel intimate and specific rather than generic.

title: Lead Curse Tablet (British Museum 1934,11-5.1)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Greco-Roman)
summary:
An inscribed lead *defixio* (curse tablet) in the British Museum, one of the most characteristic artifacts of ancient binding magic.

## Iconography
A thin sheet of lead is incised with a curse text, the soft metal taking a sharp stylus easily. Such tablets were typically rolled or folded, sometimes pierced with a nail, and deposited in graves, wells or sanctuaries to carry the message to the underworld.

## Significance
Defixiones (Greek *katadesmoi*) sought to "bind" rivals in love, litigation, commerce or the circus by consigning their names to chthonic powers. Thousands survive across the Roman world. John Gager's *Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World* and Henk Versnel's work established them as a primary window into everyday anxieties and the social reach of [[motif:binding magic|binding magic]].

## For artists & game designers
Lead's dull grey and the act of nailing-and-burying make a vivid, tactile mechanic for a curse the player must physically hide.

citations.text: John G. Gager, *Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World* (1992).

title: Leiden Magical Papyrus I 384 (PGM XII)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
summary:
Leiden Papyrus I 384, edited as PGM XII, a bilingual Greek-and-Demotic magical handbook from the Theban cache now in the Rijksmuseum van Oudheden.

## Iconography
The papyrus carries spell texts in both Greek and Demotic Egyptian script. Recipes cover invocations, amulet manufacture and ritual procedure, mixing Egyptian priestly knowledge with Greek formulae.

## Significance
The Leiden and London Demotic magical papyri are key evidence that the PGM grew out of Egyptian temple scriptoria. The bilingual format shows native Egyptian priests adapting their craft for a Hellenized clientele, a process documented by Jacco Dieleman (*Priests, Tongues, and Rites*). The roll belongs to the same find as Leiden I 383 and the Great Magical Papyrus of Paris.

## For artists & game designers
A bilingual scroll signals a manuscript at the seam of two cultures — useful for a relic that only a doubly-literate scribe can fully read.

title: Inscribed Gold Sheet (Lamella) Amulet
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (The British Museum / PAS). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A thin inscribed gold sheet (*lamella*) of the kind worn as a protective amulet or phylactery, recorded through the Portable Antiquities Scheme.

## Iconography
A small rectangle of beaten gold foil incised with text — typically divine names, [[motif:voces magicae|voces magicae]] and protective formulae. Such sheets were rolled or folded tightly and carried in a tubular case (*bulla* or capsule) against the body.

## Significance
Gold and silver lamellae are the high-end of ancient protective magic, used against fever, the evil eye and demons. Roy Kotansky's *Greek Magical Amulets* catalogues the genre, which links the Greco-Egyptian papyri to later Jewish and Christian phylactery traditions. The precious metal signals both wealth and the seriousness of the threat warded against.

## For artists & game designers
A rolled gold strip in a tiny capsule is a wearable charm with built-in mystery: the text is hidden until unrolled, risking its power.

title: Magical Papyrus: Love Charm (BNUS inv. 1167)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
summary:
A papyrus love charm (*charme d'amour*), one genre of the erotic-binding spells that fill the Greek magical handbooks.

## Iconography
The sheet carries a Greek spell text designed to inflame and compel a named target's desire. Such *agōgē* ("leading") charms typically name the victim and her mother, invoke chthonic and astral powers, and demand the target feel no rest until she comes to the operator.

## Significance
Erotic spells are among the best-attested categories of ancient magic, surviving on papyri, lead tablets and ostraca. They reveal the coercive, often violent emotional logic of [[motif:binding magic|binding magic]] (*katadesis*) and have been central to studies of gender and agency in antiquity by scholars such as Christopher Faraone (*Ancient Greek Love Magic*).

## For artists & game designers
A love charm makes a morally ambiguous quest item: powerful, effective, and unsettling in what it asks the caster to wish on another person.

citations.text: Christopher A. Faraone, *Ancient Greek Love Magic* (1999).

title: Leaf from the Greek Magical Papyri (PGM)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
summary:
A leaf from the corpus modern scholars call the *Papyri Graecae Magicae* (PGM), the working handbooks of late-antique ritual specialists.

## Iconography
Dense columns of Greek cursive set out a practical spell: an invocation, a list of [[motif:voces magicae|voces magicae]] (untranslatable power-words), instructions for offerings, and the desired result. Many such leaves intersperse charaktêres and divine names with the running text.

## Significance
The PGM are the single richest source for how magic was actually performed in the Greco-Roman world. Assembled chiefly from a Theban cache and published by Karl Preisendanz, they blend Egyptian, Greek, Jewish and Christian divine names into a syncretic ritual technology. Hans Dieter Betz's English translation made them central to the study of ancient religion and [[motif:syncretism|syncretism]].

## For artists & game designers
The layout — running prose broken by clusters of nonsense power-words and drawn signs — is a ready template for an in-world spellbook page.

citations.text: Hans Dieter Betz (ed.), *The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation* (1986).

title: Bronze Statuette of Harpocrates (Museo Egizio C 430)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Greco-Roman Egypt)
summary:
A bronze statuette of Harpocrates — Horus the Child — in the Museo Egizio, Turin, a deity central to Greco-Egyptian protective magic.

## Iconography
A nude boy with a side-lock of youth raises one finger to his lips, the gesture the Greeks read as a sign of silence and secrecy. He is frequently shown seated on a lotus, sometimes on the cippus of Horus that wards off venomous creatures.

## Significance
As the infant solar god triumphant over chaos and serpents, Harpocrates was invoked on amulets and the Horus-cippi for protection against snakebite and the dangers of childhood. The Greeks reinterpreted the childhood finger-gesture as enjoining silence, making him a patron of [[motif:secrecy|mystery and secrecy]] in the magical papyri, where he appears among the sun-god's daily forms.

## For artists & game designers
The finger-to-lips child-god is a perfect emblem for a cult of silence or a secret-keeping NPC: the iconography itself says "tell no one."

citations.text: Hans Dieter Betz (ed.), *The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation* (1986).

title: Chrysopoeia of Cleopatra (Ouroboros)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (attributed to Cleopatra the Alchemist)
date: Surviving copy medieval; tradition Greco-Roman (1st–3rd c. CE)
summary:
The *Chrysopoeia of Cleopatra*, an emblem from the Greco-Egyptian alchemical tradition centred on a serpent devouring its own tail.

## Iconography
A double ring encloses the [[motif:ouroboros|ouroboros]] — a serpent biting its tail, half light and half dark — around the motto *hen to pan*, "the all is one." Adjacent diagrams give the symbols and apparatus of gold-making (chrysopoeia).

## Significance
Attributed to Cleopatra the Alchemist, this is among the earliest and most influential statements of the ouroboros as a symbol of cyclical unity and material transmutation. Transmitted through the Greek alchemical corpus (Zosimos, the Marciana and Paris manuscripts), it became foundational for later alchemy and for Jung's reading of the ouroboros as a symbol of self-renewal.

## For artists & game designers
The light/dark ouroboros and "all is one" motto are a compact, ready-to-engrave emblem for an alchemical faction or transmutation system.

citations.text: Marcellin Berthelot, *Collection des anciens alchimistes grecs* (1888).

title: Sator Square, Palaestra at Pompeii
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Roman)
summary:
The Sator (Rotas) square scratched in the Palaestra at Pompeii — the earliest securely dated example of this famous magical word-square.

## Iconography
Five five-letter words, SATOR AREPO TENET OPERA ROTAS, form a grid readable identically across, down, and in both reversed directions: a perfect bidirectional palindrome built around the cross-shaped TENET.

## Significance
Buried by Vesuvius in 79 CE, the Pompeii example proves the square predates Christianity's spread in the West. Its letters can be rearranged into PATERNOSTER twice (with two each of A and O, read as alpha and omega), fuelling debate over Christian, Jewish or purely apotropaic origins. It recurs for over a millennium on amulets, walls and in folk medicine across Europe as a protective and [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] charm.

## For artists & game designers
A self-mirroring word grid is an elegant puzzle-glyph: it reads the same from every direction, perfect for a ward that must be drawn exactly.

title: Amuletic Pendant with Holy Rider (Walters 54.2653)
work: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: Anonymous (Byzantine / Eastern Mediterranean)
date: Late antique – early Byzantine (5th–7th century CE)
summary:
A late-antique amuletic pendant in the Walters Art Museum showing the "Holy Rider" — a horseman spearing a prostrate demon, an image tied to King Solomon's mastery over evil spirits.

## Iconography
A mounted figure drives a spear down into a fallen female demon beneath the horse's hooves. The reverse often bears the formula "One God who conquers evil" and the device against the *baskania* (evil eye).

## Significance
The Holy Rider amulet bridges Greco-Egyptian magic and Christian late antiquity. The rider is variously named Solomon, an angel, or a saint, trampling the child-killing demon Gyllou/Abyzou. Such amulets, studied by Bonner and Jeffrey Spier, show how the legend of Solomon as exorcist (the *Testament of Solomon*) fused with older protective gem traditions into a Christianised [[motif:binding magic|binding magic]].

## For artists & game designers
The rider-tramples-demon composition is a clean, heraldic emblem for a protective order or anti-demon faction.

citations.text: Jeffrey Spier, "Medieval Byzantine Magical Amulets," *Journal of the Warburg and Courtauld Institutes* (1993).

title: Astaroth (Dictionnaire Infernal)
work: Grimoire & Goetia Plates
creator: Louis Le Breton / Collin de Plancy
summary:
**Astaroth**, engraved by **Louis Le Breton** for the 1863 *Dictionnaire Infernal* of Collin de Plancy — the most famous illustrated catalogue of demons.

## Iconography
A Grand Duke of Hell rides an infernal beast (a dragon/wyvern), holding a serpent — the attributes the [[tradition:goetia_grimoire|Goetia]] assigns him. The 1863 edition's 69 demon portraits gave faces to the spirits of the grimoire tradition.

## Significance
Le Breton's gallery is the visual companion to the *Lemegeton* / Ars Goetia hierarchy this archive's Goetia material analyses: Astaroth is one of the 72 spirits, a 'Great Duke' of forty legions. The *Dictionnaire Infernal* is where demonology became, decisively, a body of *pictures*.

## For artists & game designers
The template demon-portrait — named spirit, infernal mount, heraldic attributes. A direct reference for a bestiary or summoning catalogue.

citations.text: J. Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal*, 6th ed., ill. L. Le Breton (Paris, 1863).

title: The Magic Circle & Triangle of King Solomon (Goetia)
work: Grimoire & Goetia Plates
creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds., 1904)
rights: Public domain (published pre-1931). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The great protective **Magic Circle** of the *Goetia* (the first book of the *Lesser Key of Solomon*, or *Lemegeton*), shown with the **Triangle of Art** into which a conjured spirit is compelled to appear. This engraving is from the 1904 edition edited by **S. L. MacGregor Mathers** and Aleister Crowley, reproducing the design of the 17th-century manuscripts (British Library, Sloane MSS).

## Reading
The magician stands within the circle, ringed by divine and angelic names that fence out danger; the spirit manifests safely in the triangle outside. It is the single most iconic object of the [[tradition:goetia_grimoire]] tradition of ceremonial magic — the practical, operative counterpart to alchemy's contemplative emblems.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive summoning circle: concentric rings, Hebrew/Latin god-names, a bounded triangle of manifestation. Drops straight into a ritual scene, a summoning minigame, a warding glyph, or a dungeon floor-seal.

title: The Beast from the Land (Valcavado Beatus)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Monk Oveco (Valcavado scriptorium)
summary:
The *Beast from the Land* (the false prophet) from the Valcavado *Beatus*, a Mozarabic Apocalypse manuscript dated 970.

## Iconography
The two-horned [[motif:apocalyptic beast|beast that rises from the earth]] of Revelation 13 is rendered in the bold, flat colour fields and abstracted forms of Mozarabic illumination. It accompanies the first Beast and the Dragon as the unholy trinity of the Apocalypse.

## Significance
The *Beatus* manuscripts transmit the *Commentary on the Apocalypse* by Beatus of Liébana (8th c.), copied and illuminated across medieval Iberia. Their monstrous beasts — the seven-headed dragon, the leopard-beast, the two-horned false prophet — supplied medieval Christendom with much of its repertoire of demonic and apocalyptic imagery, the same grotesque vocabulary later mined by grimoire and demonological illustrators.

## For artists & game designers
Mozarabic art's flat, graphic monsters are a striking stylistic alternative to naturalistic demons — study the Beatus beasts for bold heraldic creature design.

citations.text: Beatus of Liébana, *Commentary on the Apocalypse*; Valcavado Beatus (970).

title: Talisman of the Black Pullet (A Franga Preta)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A talismanic figure from *La Poule Noire* (*The Black Pullet*), the grimoire of magical rings and talismans whose hen lays golden eggs.

## Iconography
The diagram shows one of the book's talismans — a ring or figure inscribed with characters and a controlling word — by which the operator commands the spirits attached to it. Each talisman in the work is paired with a ring and a secret word of power.

## Significance
*The Black Pullet*, an 18th-century French grimoire, teaches the manufacture of [[motif:talisman|magical rings and talismans]] that grant invisibility, command of spirits, and the legendary black hen that lays golden eggs. Framed as the revelation of a Turkish mage to a French soldier in Egypt, it represents the talismanic, treasure-seeking branch of the grimoire tradition, where spirits are bound not by long conjuration but through engraved objects.

## For artists & game designers
A template for 'enchanted item' iconography — a sigil-bearing ring that doubles as the binding for a servant spirit.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: A Demon from the Compendium rarissimum
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A hand-coloured demon figure from the *Compendium rarissimum totius Artis Magicae*, an 18th-century German manuscript of necromantic and demonological lore.

## Iconography
The page presents a grotesque demon rendered in watercolour against a blank ground — a hybrid creature combining human, bestial and reptilian features with lurid colouring, in the deliberately repellent style that distinguishes this manuscript from the engraved devils of printed books.

## Significance
The *Compendium rarissimum* (sometimes catalogued with the *Magia naturalis et innaturalis* tradition) belongs to the clandestine handbooks of [[motif:necromancy|conjuration]] copied privately in the German-speaking lands. Its leering, full-colour demons were painted as visual aids to evocation, illustrating the appearances that spirits were expected to take. Such manuscripts circulated outside print, preserving a folk-magical demonology distinct from the learned Solomonic line.

## For artists & game designers
The saturated, hand-painted palette of these manuscript demons is a vivid alternative to monochrome engraving — study the colour grotesque for unsettling, non-photoreal monster design.

citations.text: *Compendium rarissimum totius Artis Magicae* (German MS, 18th c.).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: The Fall of the Rebel Angels
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: William de Brailes
summary:
William de Brailes' illumination of *The Fall of the Rebel Angels*, c. 1250 (Walters Art Museum).

## Iconography
The page shows the angels who followed Lucifer cast out of Heaven and tumbling downward, their forms darkening and growing monstrous as they fall — the very moment of [[motif:transformation|angels becoming demons]]. God and the loyal host remain above; the rebels plunge into the lower register.

## Significance
The revolt and fall of the angels, elaborated from Isaiah 14, Ezekiel 28 and Revelation 12, is the origin story for every demon in the grimoire tradition: the spirits later catalogued and conjured are understood as these fallen powers. William de Brailes, the earliest English illuminator known by name, gives the myth a vivid Gothic staging. The transformation-in-mid-air motif explains why grimoire demons retain half-angelic, half-bestial forms.

## For artists & game designers
A reference for 'corruption' visual design — show rank by how far a falling figure has mutated from angel toward beast.

title: Seal of Amdusias (Goetic Sigil)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after the Lemegeton)
summary:
The *seal of Amdusias*, the sixty-seventh spirit of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
The seal is an ornamented character of curved and crossing strokes, the [[motif:demon seal|glyph]] by which the spirit is summoned. Amdusias is described as appearing in human form, sometimes with a unicorn's head, attended by the sound of trumpets and concert music.

## Significance
Amdusias (Amduscias) is a Great Duke of the *Ars Goetia* commanding twenty-nine legions; he is the demon of music, able to make trees bend at the conjurer's will and to provide skilled musician-familiars. He belongs to the class of Goetic spirits prized for arts and harmonies rather than war — a reminder that the catalogue served aesthetic as well as martial desires. His seal completes the standard set.

## For artists & game designers
A 'bard demon' — use the seal with a unicorn-headed musician for a spirit tied to sound and enchantment mechanics.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: Seal of Asmoday (Goetic Sigil)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after the Lemegeton)
summary:
The *seal of Asmoday* (Asmodeus), the thirty-second spirit of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
The seal is a dense interlaced character, the [[motif:demon seal|glyph]] by which this great king is called. Asmoday is described in the Goetia as appearing with three heads — bull, man, and ram — with a serpent's tail and the feet of a goose, riding an infernal dragon and bearing a lance and banner.

## Significance
Asmoday is a King of the *Ars Goetia* commanding seventy-two legions, identified with the Asmodeus of the *Book of Tobit* and the *Testament of Solomon*. The grimoire counsels that the conjurer must stand bareheaded before him, for he is mighty and will otherwise show no respect; he teaches arithmetic, geometry and handicraft, gives the ring of virtues, and guards hidden treasure. Distinct from the held Le Breton portrait, this is his Solomonic *seal*.

## For artists & game designers
A powerful 'king'-tier seal — pair with the three-headed dragon-rider for a high-rank summon requiring special ritual conditions.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: Seal of Bael (Goetic Sigil)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after the Lemegeton)
summary:
The *seal of Bael*, sigil of the first king of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
The seal is a compact emblem of intersecting lines, loops and points — the abstract [[motif:demon seal|character]] of the spirit, distinct from any figural portrait. It is the glyph by which Bael is named, called, and bound.

## Significance
Bael (echoing the Canaanite storm-god *Ba'al*) is the first of the seventy-two spirits of the *Ars Goetia*, a king commanding sixty-six legions who grants invisibility. Each Goetic spirit is assigned such a unique seal, to be engraved on the proper metal and displayed during the operation. The seals were standardised for the modern occult revival by Mathers and Crowley's 1904 edition of the *Lemegeton*.

## For artists & game designers
A ready 'summoning glyph' — assign one sigil per creature for instantly legible binding-circle and UI iconography.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: Seal of Buer (Goetic Sigil)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after the Lemegeton)
summary:
The *seal of Buer*, sigil of the wheel-shaped president of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
The seal is an interlaced figure of lines and small terminals, the non-figurative [[motif:demon seal|character]] of the spirit — the counterpart to Buer's famous wheel-of-legs portrait.

## Significance
Buer is the tenth spirit of the *Ars Goetia*, a Great President who teaches natural and moral philosophy, logic, and the virtues of herbs and plants, and who heals all distempers; he appears 'when the Sun is in Sagittarius' and commands fifty legions. As with every Goetic spirit, his power is invoked through this unique engraved seal, part of the standardised apparatus of the *Lemegeton*.

## For artists & game designers
Pair this seal with the wheel-of-legs creature for a complete 'name + glyph' set, mirroring how grimoires bind image to character.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: Seal of Decarabia (Goetic Sigil)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after the Lemegeton)
summary:
The *seal of Decarabia*, the sixty-ninth spirit of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
Decarabia's seal is distinctive in incorporating a star or pentacle within its lines — the [[motif:demon seal|character]] reflecting the spirit, who first appears as a star or pentagram before taking human shape.

## Significance
Decarabia (Carabia) is a Marquis of the *Ars Goetia* commanding thirty legions; he knows the virtues of herbs and precious stones and can make the illusion of birds fly before the conjurer, even granting them as familiars. His appearance as a glowing star links him to the astral and natural-magical interests of the grimoire's compilers. The seal belongs to the canonical Goetic series.

## For artists & game designers
The star-shaped seal is among the most graphically striking of the set — ideal where a sigil must read as both glyph and 'spawn point'.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: Seal of Marchosias (Goetic Sigil)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after the Lemegeton)
summary:
The *seal of Marchosias*, the thirty-fifth spirit of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
The seal is a balanced figure of crossing lines and terminals, the engraved [[motif:demon seal|character]] used to call the spirit. Marchosias himself is described as a fierce she-wolf with griffin's wings and a serpent's tail, breathing fire.

## Significance
Marchosias is a Great Marquis of the *Ars Goetia* commanding thirty legions; he was, the grimoire says, formerly of the order of Dominations among the angels and hopes to return to the Seventh Throne after twelve hundred years — a rare note of demonic nostalgia for lost grace. He fights truly for the conjurer and answers all questions. His seal is part of the standard Goetic set.

## For artists & game designers
The 'fallen but hopeful' backstory makes Marchosias a sympathetic spirit-ally; pair seal with a winged-wolf design.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: Page from the Grand Grimoire (Dragão Vermelho)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A selected page from *O Dragão Vermelho* (*The Red Dragon*), a popular edition of the *Grand Grimoire*, showing the apparatus of spirit evocation.

## Iconography
The page combines printed conjuration text with magical figures — the diagrams, circles and characters by which the operator prepares to call and command an infernal spirit. The layout is that of a working handbook rather than an art-book.

## Significance
The *Grand Grimoire*, circulated under the lurid title *Le Dragon Rouge*, is among the most notorious of the black-magic grimoires, promising the conjuration of [[motif:spirit evocation|Lucifuge Rofocale]] and the discovery of hidden treasure. Cheap chapbook printings spread it across France and, through Portuguese and Brazilian editions like this one, into the wider Atlantic world, where it fed folk-magical practice well into the modern era.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic grimoire page — a reference for the dense mix of typeset incantation and hand-drawn sigil that signals a real working spellbook.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Shax, Spirit of the Ars Goetia
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after the Lemegeton)
summary:
A drawing of *Shax* (Chax, Scox), the stork-shaped marquis of the *Ars Goetia*.

## Iconography
Shax is depicted following the grimoire's description as a great stork or long-necked bird, speaking 'with a hoarse and subtle voice.' The avian form distinguishes him within the bestiary of the seventy-two spirits.

## Significance
Shax is a Great Marquis of the *Ars Goetia* (first book of the *Lemegeton*), commanding thirty legions. The grimoire credits him with the power to [[motif:theft|take away the sight, hearing, or understanding]] of any person, and to steal money and bring it to the conjurer — though it warns he is a deceiver who must be commanded into the triangle of art. He typifies the catalogue of named, ranked spirits that defines the Solomonic demonology.

## For artists & game designers
A bird-bodied trickster spirit — the stork form makes Shax an unusual, memorable design among horned and bestial demons.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: Lucifuge Rofocale
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after the Grand Grimoire)
summary:
*Lucifuge Rofocale*, the demon prime-minister of Hell who is conjured to seal the pact in the *Grand Grimoire*.

## Iconography
Lucifuge is shown as a horned, clawed demon of grotesque aspect, the central spirit the operator must summon and bargain with. His name — *lux fugiens*, 'fleeing the light' — marks him as a creature of darkness who shuns the day.

## Significance
In the *Grand Grimoire* (the so-called *Red Dragon*, *Dragon Rouge*), Lucifuge Rofocale is set by Lucifer over the wealth and treasures of the world, and it is with him that the magician negotiates the infamous [[motif:pact|infernal pact]]. He governs the powers of riches and treasure-finding, and the grimoire supplies the *blood pact* and the coercive 'Great Conjuration' used to compel him. He epitomises the bargain-driven black-magic strand of the grimoire tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A classic 'deal-maker' antagonist — design Lucifuge as a courtly but monstrous broker who trades treasure for a terrible price.

citations.text: *Le Grand Grimoire* / *Le Dragon Rouge* (18th–19th c. editions).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: The Apparition (La Damnation de Faust)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (after Berlioz / Faust legend)
summary:
A nineteenth-century print of an apparition scene from the Faust legend (*La Damnation de Faust*), in which a conjured vision is raised before the scholar.

## Iconography
The composition shows a [[motif:conjured vision|spectral apparition]] summoned into appearance — the vision Mephistopheles conjures to ensnare Faust. The figure materialises out of darkness, the staple image of the spirit called up by a magician's bargain.

## Significance
The Faust legend is the literary apex of the grimoire-and-pact tradition: a scholar who, dissatisfied with ordinary learning, conjures the spirit Mephistopheles and seals a [[motif:damnation|pact]] for forbidden knowledge and power. Berlioz's *La Damnation de Faust* (1846), after Goethe, gave the story its great Romantic-musical form, and its illustrations — like this apparition — fed the popular imagery of demonic summoning that surrounds the actual *Höllenzwang* grimoires attributed to Faust.

## For artists & game designers
A model for the 'summoned apparition' beat — a luminous figure conjured from shadow as the fruit (and trap) of a magical pact.

citations.text: H. Berlioz, *La Damnation de Faust* (1846), after J. W. von Goethe.
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: The Magician Conjuring a Spirit (The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century)
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous (Robert Cross Smith, ed.)
summary:
An engraving of a magician conjuring a spirit, from *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (1825), a popular English compendium of occult lore.

## Iconography
The magus stands within a protective [[motif:magic circle|circle]] amid the apparatus of evocation — wand, lamp, skull and ritual instruments — as a [[motif:raised spirit|spirit]] manifests in answer to his conjuration. The image dramatises the grimoire operation rather than a single named demon.

## Significance
*The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century*, issued by the circle of Robert Cross Smith ('Raphael'), packaged Solomonic and grimoire material for a Georgian popular audience, helping to seed the Victorian occult revival. Its plates of robed conjurers and rising spirits fixed the romantic image of the ceremonial magician at work — the practitioner who, by circle, sigil and word, calls a spirit into visible appearance.

## For artists & game designers
A complete 'summoning scene' reference — circle, tools, and apparition together — useful for staging an evocation set-piece.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Talisman from The Black Pullet
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A second talismanic plate from the *Black Pullet* grimoire, an engraved figure of binding characters.

## Iconography
The talisman is a geometric field of magical characters and letters — a self-contained 'seal' whose script encodes the [[motif:spirit seal|name and command]] of the spirit it governs. As in the rest of the book, the figure is meant to be reproduced on a ring or plate and activated with a secret word.

## Significance
The *Black Pullet* organises its magic around a series of such talismans, each promising a specific power — to see hidden treasure, to become invisible, to compel love or obedience. The reliance on engraved character-talismans rather than dramatic evocation links the work to the older Arabic and Solomonic talismanic traditions while presenting them in an accessible, almost recipe-book form.

## For artists & game designers
Clean abstract sigil art — use these character-fields as readable 'rune plates' for an in-game crafting or binding system.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: King Solomon and Two Demons
work: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: `Aliquli (attrib.)
summary:
A manuscript miniature of *King Solomon and Two Demons*, from a Persianate illustrated book (Walters Art Museum).

## Iconography
Solomon is shown enthroned in majesty with two demonic *divs* — grotesque, horned, animal-bodied spirits — brought into his presence and submission. The composition stages the king's God-given dominion over the world of [[motif:command of spirits|spirits]].

## Significance
The figure of Solomon as master of demons is the wellspring of the entire Solomonic grimoire tradition. In Qur'anic and Jewish lore alike he commands the *jinn* and *divs* by a divine ring and seal, setting them to labour on the Temple. This Islamicate miniature preserves the same root legend that, in Europe, generated the *Clavicula Salomonis* and the *Ars Goetia* — the king who binds spirits to his will.

## For artists & game designers
The enthroned-master-with-bound-monsters tableau is the original 'summoner' image — a model for depicting authority over a captive spirit retinue.

citations.text: Walters Art Museum, MS W.624 (Public domain).
Qur'an 27 (Sulayman and the jinn); *Testament of Solomon*.

title: Boethius, De institutione musica (Einsiedeln manuscript)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Anonymous scribe (after Boethius)
summary:
A two-page opening from a medieval manuscript of Boethius's *De institutione musica* (Einsiedeln, Stiftsbibliothek, Codex 358), the textbook that transmitted Greek harmonic theory to the Latin West.

## Iconography
The ruled folios carry Boethius's text with **proportion diagrams** and a divided-string [[motif:monochord|monochord]] scale in the margins, the numbers and arcs setting out the intervals of the tone, semitone and the consonances. Such figures let a reader literally measure the ratios as lengths.

## Significance
Boethius (c. 500 CE) divided music into *musica mundana* (the harmony of the spheres), *musica humana* (the concord of body and soul) and *musica instrumentalis* (audible sound), a scheme that organised the medieval [[motif:quadrivium|quadrivium]] for a thousand years. His insistence that true music is the rational science of number behind sound underlies every later image in this collection; the manuscript diagrams are the working tools of that doctrine.

## For artists & game designers
The three-fold scheme — world, human, instrument — is a clean way to tier a music system from cosmic to personal to played.

title: De Musica Mundana: the elements in proportion (Fludd)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Robert Fludd (engr. de Bry)
summary:
Robert Fludd's *De Musica Mundana* diagram (p. 97 of *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*), mapping the world's elements onto the consonant musical intervals.

## Iconography
From the dark globe of **Terra** at the bottom, wedge-shaped regions rise through **Aqua**, **Aer** and **Ignis**; concentric arcs and radiating lines are labelled with the harmonic proportions — *Proportio Dupla* (2:1, the octave), *Sesquialtera* (3:2, the fifth), *Sesquitertia* (4:3, the fourth) and *Quadrupla* (4:1, the double octave) — so the spacing of the elemental spheres is set out as a musical scale.

## Significance
The plate is Fludd's geometric proof that the cosmos is literally tuned: the distances of the elements and spheres obey the same whole-number [[motif:musical-ratios|ratios]] as the consonances, the doctrine of *musica mundana* inherited from Pythagoras, Plato and [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|Boethius]]. It is the abstract counterpart to his celebrated cosmic monochord, here without the figure of God's tuning hand.

## For artists & game designers
Elemental layers labelled with their interval ratios are a direct recipe for an ambient soundtrack that changes pitch by altitude.

citations.text: R. Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia*, vol. I, tract. 'De Musica Mundana' (1617).

title: The Temple of Music (Templum Musicae)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Robert Fludd (engr. de Bry)
summary:
Robert Fludd's *Templum Musicae* ("Temple of Music") from the music treatise within *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (vol. I, 1618), an architectural allegory that organises the whole theory of music.

## Iconography
A fantastical building rises in stages: a great **spiral** scroll dominates the central tower, while colonnaded **arches** to the sides shelter figures — a radiant musician (often read as Apollo or Timotheus) holding an instrument, and a satyr/Pan in a garden. Each architectural member is keyed to an element of music theory (the spiral to proportion, the windows and pilasters to intervals, modes and notes), so the temple is a memory-theatre of the discipline.

## Significance
Fludd casts *musica instrumentalis* as a temple whose every measure embodies harmonic [[motif:musical-ratios|proportion]], a built image of the conviction that music mirrors the architecture of the [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|cosmos]]. The plate belongs to his vast Hermetic project to show the [[motif:macrocosm|macrocosm]] and microcosm tuned by the same divine numbers.

## For artists & game designers
A building whose rooms each teach a rule of music is a finished design for a 'temple level' where architecture is the tutorial.

citations.text: R. Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia*, vol. I (Oppenheim, 1617–1618).
P. Ammann, 'The Musical Theory and Philosophy of Robert Fludd', *JWCI* (1967).

title: Apollo, the Muses and the planetary modes (Practica Musicae frontispiece)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Franchino Gaffurio (workshop woodcut)
summary:
The celebrated cosmological woodcut from Franchino Gaffurio's *Practica Musicae* (Milan, 1496), the single most influential Renaissance image of the music of the spheres.

## Iconography
**Apollo** sits enthroned at the top beneath the banner *Mentis Apollineae vis has movet undique Musas* ("the power of the Apollonian mind moves these Muses everywhere"), flanked by the three **Graces** (Euphrosyne, Aglaia, Thalia). From his throne a great **three-headed serpent** — wolf, lion and dog, Macrobius's emblem of Time — descends through the heavens. Along its coils run the planetary spheres from the *primum mobile* down to the Moon, each linked to a **Muse**, a musical mode (Hypermixolydius, Mixolydius, Lydius, Phrygius, Dorius … Hypodorius), a whole-tone or semitone interval, and a zodiac sign; the silent Earth at the base bears Thalia and the four elements.

## Significance
The plate fuses the eight tones of the [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|celestial scale]] with the Muses, the modes and the planets into one diagram, making audible Plato's and Cicero's claim that the turning heavens sound a cosmic octave. It became the canonical visual statement of *musica mundana* and was copied for over a century.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made vertical 'cosmic ladder': each rung carries a planet, a note, a mode and a Muse — a complete correspondence stack to drive a layered soundtrack.

citations.text: S. K. Heninger, *Touches of Sweet Harmony* (1974).

title: Harmonia est discordia concors (Gaffurio teaching)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Franchino Gaffurio (woodcut)
summary:
The author-and-pupils woodcut associated with Franchino Gaffurio's *De Harmonia Musicorum Instrumentorum Opus* (1518), bearing his motto on concordant discord.

## Iconography
Gaffurio stands at a **lectern** (inscribed *Franchin*) addressing a group of students; behind him a rank of **organ pipes** is labelled with the numbers 6, 4, 3 — the lengths that yield the octave, fifth and fourth. A scroll proclaims *Harmonia est discordia concors* ("harmony is concordant discord"), the Pythagorean principle that unlike sounds unite by ratio.

## Significance
The plate makes the abstract mathematics of consonance visible as measured pipe-lengths, the same whole-number ratios (2:1, 3:2, 4:3) Pythagoras was said to have discovered. By printing his motto Gaffurio binds practical instrument-tuning to the cosmic doctrine that the [[motif:musical-ratios|ratios]] reconciling discord into [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|harmony]] also govern the heavens and the soul.

## For artists & game designers
Numbered pipes (6-4-3) are a compact way to show 'tuning as math' on screen — adjust a number, hear the interval shift.

citations.text: F. Gaffurio, *De Harmonia Musicorum Instrumentorum Opus* (Milan, 1518).

title: Opening page of the Practica Musicae (Dresden copy)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Franchino Gaffurio (illuminated print)
summary:
The richly illuminated opening leaf of Gaffurio's *Practica Musicae* — *Franchini Gafori Laudensis Musicae actionis liber primus* — from the deluxe copy in the Saxon State Library, Dresden.

## Iconography
A decorated initial and grotesque-and-foliage **borders** frame the Latin text in which Gaffurio defends harmonic science against the *cessantes* who deny that music rests on theory. In the lower margin a small scene shows musicians and an experiment with sounding vessels, recalling the legendary discovery of the consonances; classical putti and candelabra ornament the side panel.

## Significance
The page presents music as a learned quadrivial science continuous with arithmetic and the [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|harmony of the cosmos]]. As a hand-painted incunable it shows how the new print culture clothed Pythagorean–Boethian music theory in humanist splendour, and stands as the gateway to the famous spheres-frontispiece that opens the same book.

## For artists & game designers
The illuminated-incipit format — text framed by living ornament with a narrative scene in the margin — is a clean template for an in-game 'grimoire page'.

title: Theorica Musicae (title woodcut, 1492)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Franchino Gaffurio (woodcut)
summary:
The title page of Gaffurio's *Theorica Musicae* (Milan, 1492), the printed source of the most-reproduced version of the Pythagoras-and-the-hammers legend.

## Iconography
The woodcut's panels show the philosopher experimenting on sounding bodies to fix the consonant ratios: striking **hammers** of different weights at a forge, sounding **bells**, plucking strings under graded weights, and blowing pipes and glasses filled to different levels. Inscribed numbers mark the 6:8:9:12 proportions that yield octave, fifth and fourth.

## Significance
The legend (transmitted by Nicomachus and [[motif:pythagoras-and-the-hammers|Boethius]]) held that Pythagoras passing a smithy heard consonances in the ringing hammers and so discovered that musical harmony reduces to simple whole-number [[motif:musical-ratios|ratios]]. Gaffurio's plate fixed the iconography for the Renaissance, even though the hammer-weight version is acoustically impossible — a myth of origin for the whole science of *musica*.

## For artists & game designers
Four 'experiment stations' (hammers, bells, strings, pipes) all yielding the same numbers make an elegant discovery-puzzle of acoustics.

title: The Harmony of the Spheres (engraving, 1589)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Anonymous (Mannerist engraver, after a Florentine intermedio)
summary:
A Mannerist engraving titled *The Harmony of the Spheres* (1589), depicting the heavens as a cosmic concert and dance.

## Iconography
In the upper register the planetary **deities** are enthroned among radiant **clouds** around a central sun-king, while in the foreground two rows of female figures — **Muses** and Hours — join hands in a stately ring **dance** across the firmament. The whole scene stages the turning of the spheres as a choreographed celestial harmony.

## Significance
The print reflects the late-Renaissance fashion (crowned in the 1589 Florentine *intermedi* for the Medici wedding) for representing [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|cosmic harmony]] as a danced and sung spectacle. Drawing on Plato's *Republic* (the Sirens on the whirling spheres) and the Neoplatonism of Ficino, it presents the ordered cosmos as a perpetual concert in which planets, Muses and Hours move in measured [[motif:cosmic-harmony|concord]].

## For artists & game designers
Gods above, dancers below, all moving to one inaudible measure — a vivid blueprint for a 'celestial ballroom' set-piece.

citations.text: D. P. Walker, *Spiritual and Demonic Magic from Ficino to Campanella* (1958).

title: Apollo with the lyre (Musurgia Universalis frontispiece)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Athanasius Kircher (engr. for the Musurgia)
summary:
An engraved frontispiece to the second volume of Athanasius Kircher's *Musurgia Universalis* (Rome, 1650), the Jesuit polymath's encyclopaedia of music and cosmic harmony.

## Iconography
A statue of **Apollo** stands on an ornamented pedestal holding his **lyre**, the three-headed **Cerberus** crouched at his feet, set in a classical landscape with columns and a distant town. The instrument and the three-headed beast recall the Gaffurio tradition in which the modes and the [[motif:three-headed-serpent|serpent of time]] descend from Apollo through the spheres.

## Significance
Kircher's *Musurgia* sought to reduce all sounding nature — voices, instruments, birdsong, echoes and the [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|harmony of the spheres]] — to a single divine *musurgia* ("work of the Muses"). The Apolline frontispiece announces music as a cosmic, theological science in which God is the supreme organist tuning creation, the culminating Baroque statement of the Pythagorean tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Apollo-with-lyre over a guardian beast on a plinth is a clean 'patron-of-harmony' shrine asset.

title: Plucked instruments and tunings (Musurgia Universalis)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Athanasius Kircher (engr. for the Musurgia)
summary:
An organological plate (*Iconismus VII*) of plucked string instruments from Kircher's *Musurgia Universalis* (1650), the practical face of his cosmic-harmony encyclopaedia.

## Iconography
Lutes, **citterns**, a theorbo and related instruments are drawn in detail, each accompanied by **tuning tables** and short staves of notation that give the pitch of every course and the intervals between strings. The numbered fret and string charts translate each instrument into the ratios of *musica instrumentalis*.

## Significance
Kircher's treatise moves continuously from such measured instruments up to the [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|harmony of the spheres]]: the same [[motif:musical-ratios|ratios]] tuning a lute string tune the planets and the soul. The plate shows how seventeenth-century scholarship grounded the grand Pythagorean cosmology in exact, empirical organology — the audible string as a model of the silent cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
Period-accurate instrument diagrams with tuning charts are drop-in reference for craftable instruments or a music-as-mechanic system.

title: The Macrobian cosmos (Dream of Scipio world map)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Anonymous (after Macrobius)
date: Macrobius c. 430 CE; printed map after his scheme
summary:
A map after Macrobius's *Commentary on the Dream of Scipio* (early 5th c.), the text that carried the ancient doctrine of the harmony of the spheres through the Middle Ages.

## Iconography
The earth is divided into the Macrobian **climatic zones** — two frigid caps, two temperate bands and a torrid equatorial belt crossed by an ocean — the scheme Macrobius derived from contemplating the cosmos in Scipio's dream. In the full vision this terrestrial globe sits at the still centre of the revolving **celestial spheres** whose motion sounds the cosmic music.

## Significance
In Cicero's *Somnium Scipionis*, expounded by Macrobius, the elder Scipio shows the dreamer the nine [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|spheres]] whose turning produces a great concord, too constant for mortal ears to notice. Macrobius's commentary made this the standard medieval account of cosmic [[motif:cosmic-harmony|harmony]] and of the soul's descent and return, shaping writers from Boethius to Dante.

## For artists & game designers
The zoned earth at the hub of singing spheres is a ready cosmology: a silent centre ringed by sounding shells.

title: Pythagoras with bells, glasses and monochord (Theorica Musicae)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Franchino Gaffurio (woodcut)
summary:
The four-panel Pythagoras illustration from Gaffurio's *Theorica Musicae* (1492), isolating the legendary experiments by which the consonances were found.

## Iconography
In successive scenes Pythagoras (and in one panel Philolaus) tests sounding bodies marked with the numbers **4, 6, 8, 9, 12, 16**: hammers at a forge, tuned **bells**, water-filled **glasses**, strings stretched by hanging weights, and graduated **pipes**. The last panel shows a divided string — the [[motif:monochord|monochord]] — the one instrument that genuinely demonstrates that halving a string yields the octave (2:1).

## Significance
The sequence is the visual catechism of Pythagorean harmonics: every consonance proceeds from ratios among the first numbers. Only the monochord experiment is physically sound; the rest are legendary, yet together they enshrine the conviction that number is the hidden cause of beauty and that the same ratios tune the [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|cosmos]].

## For artists & game designers
The numbered sounding objects are a tidy 'lab' of ratio puzzles; the monochord alone is the honest one — useful for a 'find the real law' beat.

title: The Pythagorean Tetractys
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Anonymous (diagram)
date: modern rendering of an ancient figure
summary:
A diagram of the Pythagorean *tetractys*, the triangular figure of ten points in rows of 1-2-3-4, here paired with the Hebrew Tetragrammaton.

## Iconography
Ten dots are stacked in a perfect equilateral **triangle**: one at the apex, then two, three and four below, summing to the sacred **decad** (10). The four rows correspond to point, line, plane and solid, and to the ratios 1:2, 2:3, 3:4 that generate the octave, fifth and fourth. In the kabbalistic overlay each row bears the letters of the divine Name in expanding combination.

## Significance
The [[motif:tetractys|tetractys]] was so holy to the Pythagoreans that they swore oaths upon it as 'the source and root of ever-flowing nature'. Because its first four numbers contain all the musical consonances, it is the seed of the entire doctrine of cosmic [[motif:musical-ratios|harmony]]: the same little triangle holds the scale, the dimensions of space and, for later Christian-cabalist readers, the structure of the divine Name.

## For artists & game designers
A four-row triangle that encodes both geometry and the musical scale is a perfect minimalist sigil — one shape, total cosmology.

title: Typus Arithmeticae: Boethius and Pythagoras reckon (Margarita Philosophica)
work: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: Gregor Reisch (woodcut)
summary:
The *Typus Arithmeticae* woodcut from Gregor Reisch's encyclopaedia *Margarita Philosophica* (1503), the famous contest of the new written calculation against the old counting board.

## Iconography
Lady **Arithmetica**, her gown patterned with the doubling and triple proportions (2 4 8 / 3 9 27 that generate the musical intervals), presides between two reckoners. On the left **Boethius** computes with Hindu-Arabic numerals and pen (*algorismus*); on the right **Pythagoras** works counters on an **abacus** (*abacus*). Arithmetica's gaze favours the smiling Boethius.

## Significance
Reisch personifies the foundational art of the [[motif:quadrivium|quadrivium]]: arithmetic, the science of number, parent of music, geometry and astronomy. The numbers woven into her dress are precisely the Platonic *lambda* series whose ratios yield the harmonic intervals, so the image quietly asserts that the same numbers underlie calculation and the [[motif:music-of-the-spheres|harmony of the spheres]]. It also dramatises the historical victory of written arithmetic over the abacus.

## For artists & game designers
The 'two methods, one judge' staging is a vivid template for an in-game contest between an old and a new system of power.

title: Apis, the sacred Egyptian bull (Musgrave engraving)
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: William Musgrave (antiquarian engraving)
summary:
An antiquarian engraving of Apis, the sacred bull of Memphis, from the work of the English scholar William Musgrave — a specimen of the early-modern study of Egyptian animal-gods.

## Iconography
[[motif:apis bull|Apis]] is shown as a striding bull bearing the solar disc between his horns, the distinctive markings by which the living bull-god was identified by the priests of Memphis.

## Significance
Apis, linked in death to Osiris and later assimilated to the Hellenistic god Serapis, exemplified the Egyptian animal worship that fascinated and puzzled European antiquarians. Renaissance and Baroque scholars folded such cults into their Hermetic reading of Egypt as a land of veiled theological symbols, while classical authors had already debated whether the beast-gods were idolatry or allegory.

## For artists & game designers
A sacred-beast emblem: a marked bull crowned with a sun-disc, ready as a cult-animal icon or temple guardian.

citations.text: Erik Iversen, *The Myth of Egypt and Its Hieroglyphs* (1961).

title: Egyptian obelisk woodcut from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili*
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Anonymous (woodcut); Francesco Colonna (text); Aldus Manutius (printer)
summary:
A woodcut of a monumental Egyptianising structure from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (Venice, Aldus Manutius, 1499), the dream-romance whose hero wanders a landscape of antique marvels.

## Iconography
The image presents a towering ancient monument — part of the book's parade of obelisks, hieroglyph-bearing pylons, and fantastic architecture — drawn in the spare elegant line of the celebrated Aldine woodcuts. [[motif:hieroglyphs|Hieroglyphic]] devices appear as enigmatic captions to be decoded by the dreamer Polifilo.

## Significance
The *Hypnerotomachia* fused humanist antiquarianism with invented hieroglyphs, popularising the idea that Egyptian signs were a silent language of pure image. Its woodcuts — including the famous obelisk borne by an elephant — became a touchstone for Renaissance design and for the obelisk's later career as an esoteric monument, a lineage examined by Anthony Grafton and Liane Lefaivre.

## For artists & game designers
A model for dreamlike ruin-exploration: monumental architecture inscribed with riddles the player must read to progress.

citations.text: Liane Lefaivre, *Leon Battista Alberti's Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (1997).

title: Artemis of Ephesus (the 'many-breasted' goddess) — read as Isis-Nature
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Anonymous (Roman statue); photograph by Dennis G. Jarvis
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (Dennis G. Jarvis). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A statue of the 'many-breasted' Artemis of Ephesus, the type that Renaissance and Baroque writers fused with Isis to personify veiled Nature.

## Iconography
The goddess stands rigid and frontal, her lower body a tapering sheath of animal registers, her chest covered with the rows of bulbous protuberances (variously read as breasts, eggs, or bull-testes) that gave her the epithet *multimammia*.

## Significance
Humanist mythographers identified this composite Great Mother with [[motif:isis-nature|Isis]] as *Natura* — the nourishing, many-breasted World-Soul whose face is veiled. The image became the standard allegory of Nature on Baroque frontispieces (including Kircher's), embodying the Hermetic conviction, traced by Pierre Hadot in *The Veil of Isis*, that Egyptian Isis personified the hidden order of the cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
A 'Nature incarnate' deity design: a frontal, hieratic mother-goddess sheathed in animals and overflowing with breasts — fecundity made monstrous and sacred.

title: Frontispiece to Kircher's *Mundus Subterraneus* (Theodor Matham)
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Theodor Matham (engraver), after Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The engraved frontispiece, by Theodor Matham, to Athanasius Kircher's *Mundus Subterraneus* (Amsterdam, 1665), his cosmography of the hidden world beneath the earth.

## Iconography
An allegorical title page descends from a radiant divine source through the heavens to the fiery interior of the globe, personifying the chain of causes that animates the subterranean realm. Figures of Nature and Providence mediate the influx of [[motif:divine light|divine light]] into matter.

## Significance
Though not strictly an Egyptological book, *Mundus Subterraneus* belongs to the same Hermetic-Jesuit vision: a cosmos bound by hidden correspondences and sympathetic forces, the natural-philosophical counterpart to Kircher's Egyptian theology of secret meanings. It is a key text in the history of geology, volcanology, and Baroque natural magic.

## For artists & game designers
A vertical 'as above, so below' splash image: heaven, earth's surface, and a molten underworld linked by a single shaft of light.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin, *Athanasius Kircher's Theatre of the World* (2009).

title: Hieroglyphic obelisk plate from Kircher's *Oedipus Aegyptiacus*
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
An engraved plate from the third volume of Kircher's *Oedipus Aegyptiacus*, presenting an obelisk shaft covered in hieroglyphs alongside his 'reading' of them.

## Iconography
A tall obelisk face is rendered glyph by glyph, each [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphic]] register paired with Kircher's allegorical Latin gloss. The plate belongs to his project of recovering the *prisca aegyptiorum sapientia* — the 'ancient wisdom of the Egyptians'.

## Significance
Kircher treated each sign as an ideogram encoding a whole theological proposition, producing elaborate sentences from a handful of figures. Champollion's later decipherment showed the hieroglyphs to be a mixed phonetic-logographic script, but Kircher's engravings remain the visual emblem of the Hermetic dream that Egypt held a primordial revelation.

## For artists & game designers
A reusable 'annotated monument' layout: a carved column flanked by a scholar's confident, sprawling translation — instant arcane exposition.

citations.text: Erik Iversen, *The Myth of Egypt and Its Hieroglyphs* (1961).

title: Kircher's *Sphinx Mystagoga* (1676)
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
A plate from Athanasius Kircher's late work *Sphinx Mystagoga* (1676), in which the Egyptian Sphinx is cast as the 'leader into the mysteries' (*mystagogos*).

## Iconography
The engraving displays Egyptian funerary material — mummies, sarcophagi, and inscriptions — framed by the title's promise that the [[motif:sphinx|Sphinx]] will conduct the reader into hidden truth. The plate gathers reports of mummies and tombs into Kircher's enduring symbolic-theological scheme.

## Significance
*Sphinx Mystagoga* extended Kircher's Egyptological programme to mummification and the afterlife, treating the riddling Sphinx as guardian of an initiatory wisdom. Though his decipherments were illusory, his framing of Egypt as a land of [[motif:initiation|sacred mysteries]] shaped the esoteric imagination from the Enlightenment through the modern occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
A tomb-crawl frontispiece: a Sphinx-warded chamber of mummies presented as the threshold to secret knowledge.

title: The Lateran Obelisk, Piazza San Giovanni in Laterano (18th-c. view)
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Anonymous (18th-century vedutista)
summary:
An eighteenth-century view of the Piazza San Giovanni in Laterano, dominated by the Lateran Obelisk — the tallest standing Egyptian obelisk in the world.

## Iconography
The veduta shows the colossal [[motif:obelisk|obelisk]], originally of Thutmose III/IV from Karnak, towering before the basilica where Domenico Fontana re-erected it for Pope Sixtus V in 1588, surmounted by a cross.

## Significance
Sixtus V's campaign of raising Rome's fallen obelisks topped each with a cross or papal emblem, recasting the pagan solar pillars as trophies of the triumphant Church. The engineering feat of moving them, narrated in Fontana's own treatise, made the obelisk an emblem of both Egyptian antiquity and Counter-Reformation power, as discussed by Erik Iversen.

## For artists & game designers
A cathedral-square landmark: an ancient foreign monolith repurposed and crowned with the conqueror's holy symbol.

citations.text: Erik Iversen, *Obelisks in Exile, vol. 1: The Obelisks of Rome* (1968).

title: The Mensa Isiaca (Bembine Tablet of Isis), Museo Egizio, Turin
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Anonymous (Roman workshop, Egyptianising style)
summary:
The *Mensa Isiaca* — the celebrated 'Bembine Tablet of Isis', a large bronze altar-table inlaid with silver and niello, the single most influential object in the early-modern misreading of Egypt.

## Iconography
A central shrine frames an enthroned [[motif:isis enthroned|Isis]] surrounded by registers of Egyptian and Egyptianising deities, processions, and bands of pseudo-hieroglyphic signs. Though Roman work of the imperial Isiac cult, its glyphs are largely decorative imitations rather than legible Egyptian script.

## Significance
Named for Cardinal Pietro Bembo, who owned it in the sixteenth century, the tablet was read by Renaissance scholars as a key to Egyptian theology. [[motif:hermes trismegistus|Athanasius Kircher]] devoted much of his *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* to 'translating' its imaginary inscriptions, an enterprise wholly overturned once Champollion deciphered the real hieroglyphs in 1822, exposing it as Roman pastiche.

## For artists & game designers
A master 'mysterious tablet' prop: a metal slab crowded with gods and undecipherable signs that any scholar-NPC will confidently — and wrongly — claim to read.

citations.text: Erik Iversen, *The Myth of Egypt and Its Hieroglyphs in European Tradition* (1961).

title: Plate from Kircher's *Obeliscus Pamphilius* (the Pamphilj obelisk)
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
A page (444) from Athanasius Kircher's *Obeliscus Pamphilius* (Rome, 1650), his study of the obelisk Pope Innocent X raised over Bernini's Fountain of the Four Rivers in Piazza Navona.

## Iconography
The plate reproduces the obelisk's [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphic]] faces with Kircher's interpretive apparatus. The book served as a learned prelude to the still grander *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* two years later.

## Significance
The Pamphilj obelisk is a Roman-made monument in the Egyptian manner; Kircher nonetheless 'translated' its glyphs as a Hermetic-theological message, lending Baroque papal Rome the prestige of Egyptian antiquity. The episode is central to accounts of how the Counter-Reformation papacy appropriated [[motif:obelisk|obelisks]] as Christianised solar monuments.

## For artists & game designers
A case study in monument-as-propaganda: a ruler crowns a public fountain with an ancient pillar and commissions a scholar to declare it prophesies his reign.

citations.text: Brian Curran et al., *Obelisk: A History* (2009).

title: Bernini's Fountain of the Four Rivers and the Pamphilj obelisk, Piazza Navona
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Gian Lorenzo Bernini (fountain); photograph by George M. Groutas
rights: CC BY 2.0 (George M. Groutas). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Gian Lorenzo Bernini's *Fontana dei Quattro Fiumi* (1648–51) in Piazza Navona, crowned by the Pamphilj obelisk that Athanasius Kircher 'deciphered'.

## Iconography
Four river-gods of the personified continents recline around a rocky base from which rises the slender [[motif:obelisk|obelisk]], its faces carved with [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphs]]. A dove — emblem of the Pamphilj pope Innocent X — alights at the summit, Christianising the ancient solar shaft.

## Significance
The ensemble is the supreme statement of Baroque papal Egyptomania: a Roman-made obelisk re-erected as a monument to universal Christian dominion, its glyphs glossed by Kircher in the *Obeliscus Pamphilius*. The fusion of Egyptian form and papal triumph is a central episode in Brian Curran's history of the obelisk.

## For artists & game designers
A grand civic set-piece: a watery plaza dominated by an ancient pillar that a regime has rebranded as a sign of its own destiny.

citations.text: Brian A. Curran et al., *Obelisk: A History* (2009).

title: The Flaminio Obelisk, Piazza del Popolo, Rome
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Photograph by Dudva (monument: ancient Egyptian, re-erected 1589)
summary:
The Flaminio Obelisk in the Piazza del Popolo, one of the ancient Egyptian obelisks re-erected in Rome under Pope Sixtus V.

## Iconography
The tall red-granite shaft, carved on all faces with [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphs]] of Seti I and Ramesses II, rises from a fountain base at the centre of the great oval piazza, a cross at its apex.

## Significance
Brought to Rome by Augustus for the Circus Maximus and raised again in 1589 by Domenico Fontana, the [[motif:obelisk|obelisk]] is part of Sixtus V's urban programme that turned Rome's scattered Egyptian monuments into a network of Christianised landmarks. Such obelisks fed the Hermetic-antiquarian fascination with Egypt that Kircher would systematise.

## For artists & game designers
A city-planning beacon: an ancient inscribed needle set as the focal point of a radiating public square.

citations.text: Erik Iversen, *Obelisks in Exile, vol. 1: The Obelisks of Rome* (1968).

title: Frontispiece to Kircher's *Oedipus Aegyptiacus*
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Athanasius Kircher (author)
summary:
The frontispiece to Athanasius Kircher's vast *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* (Rome, 1652–54), the Jesuit polymath's attempt to restore the 'lost' wisdom of the hieroglyphs.

## Iconography
The Baroque title page massess Egyptian emblems — sphinxes, obelisks, and ranks of [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphic signs]] — around the title, casting Kircher as the new Oedipus who will solve the riddle of the Egyptian Sphinx.

## Significance
Kircher believed the hieroglyphs were a symbolic theology concealing Hermetic and Neoplatonic truths handed down from [[motif:hermes trismegistus|Hermes Trismegistus]]. His 'translations' were entirely fanciful, yet the *Oedipus* became the encyclopedic monument of seventeenth-century Egyptomania and a cornerstone of the *prisca theologia* idea later studied by Frances Yates and Erik Iversen.

## For artists & game designers
A definitive 'tome of forbidden Egyptian lore' cover: dense, authoritative, and confidently mistaken — perfect set dressing for a scholar's study.

citations.text: Daniel Stolzenberg, *Egyptian Oedipus: Athanasius Kircher and the Secrets of Antiquity* (2013).

title: *The Pyramid of Cestius, Rome* — Hubert Robert
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Hubert Robert
summary:
Hubert Robert's painted view of the Pyramid of Cestius in Rome, the Augustan-age tomb-pyramid that kept the Egyptian form alive in the heart of the city.

## Iconography
The steep marble-faced [[motif:pyramid of cestius|pyramid]] rises amid the picturesque ruins and figures characteristic of Robert, nicknamed 'Robert des Ruines'. The Egyptian silhouette stands incongruous and arresting against the Roman setting.

## Significance
Built around 12 BCE as the tomb of Gaius Cestius, the pyramid testified to Rome's own first wave of Egyptomania under Augustus and later became a magnet for Grand Tour antiquarians and the Romantic cult of ruins. Its enduring presence helped sustain the Western fascination with Egypt as the land of death-mysteries and hidden wisdom.

## For artists & game designers
A mood-piece for 'ancient monument in a later city': an alien Egyptian form weathering among the ruins of a successor civilisation.

title: The Pyramids and Sphinx of Memphis at Sunset (engraving)
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Imprimerie impériale (after the *Description de l'Égypte*)
summary:
A general view of the pyramids of Memphis and the Great Sphinx at sunset, from the great French survey tradition that closed the era of Egyptosophy and opened scientific Egyptology.

## Iconography
The panoramic engraving sets the [[motif:sphinx|Great Sphinx]] and the pyramids of Giza against a low sun, emphasising scale and desolation in the picturesque manner of the *Description de l'Égypte*.

## Significance
Napoleon's 1798 expedition and its monumental *Description de l'Égypte* produced precise surveys that transformed Europe's image of Egypt from a realm of Hermetic riddles into an object of empirical study — even as the Sphinx and pyramids remained potent symbols of secret wisdom for the nineteenth-century occult revival. The shift from Kircher's fantasy to Champollion's decipherment runs straight through this corpus.

## For artists & game designers
A wide establishing shot for an Egyptian setting: monumental, sun-baked, and freighted with the promise of buried secrets.

citations.text: Erik Iversen, *The Myth of Egypt and Its Hieroglyphs* (1961).

title: *Tabula Smaragdina* (Emerald Tablet) — Theosophie & Alchemie engraving
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Anonymous (early-modern engraving)
summary:
An early-modern emblematic engraving of the *Tabula Smaragdina* — the Emerald Tablet — the terse alchemical axiom-text ascribed to Hermes Trismegistus and imagined as Egyptian-Hermetic revelation (Deutsche Fotothek).

## Iconography
The plate presents the tablet's sentences within an emblematic frame of theosophical and alchemical figures, expounding the maxim that 'that which is above is as that which is below'. The composition treats [[motif:hermes trismegistus|Hermes]]'s words as the foundation of the whole work of transmutation.

## Significance
Though the Emerald Tablet's earliest texts are medieval Arabic, the European tradition received it as the most ancient utterance of Egyptian Hermes, the compressed creed of [[motif:as above so below|correspondence]] between macrocosm and microcosm. Its history was untangled by Julius Ruska, who showed how thoroughly it was woven into the Hermetic myth of Egypt.

## For artists & game designers
A single 'law of the world' artifact-card: a tablet of axioms ringed by emblems, studied to unlock the correspondence mechanics of an alchemy system.

title: Thoth, the ibis-headed god (handbook engraving)
