work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Anonymous (engraving from a 19th-c. handbook of archaeology)
summary:
An engraving of Thoth, the ibis-headed Egyptian god of writing and wisdom, from a nineteenth-century handbook of archaeology — the deity the Greeks equated with Hermes.

## Iconography
[[motif:thoth|Thoth]] appears with the head of an ibis (sometimes a baboon), often bearing a scribe's palette or recording the verdict at the weighing of the heart. The engraving renders him in the conventionalised profile of Egyptian relief.

## Significance
As scribe of the gods and master of sacred letters, Thoth was identified by the Greeks with Hermes, and the compound figure of [[motif:hermes trismegistus|Hermes Trismegistus]] — 'thrice-great' — fused the two into the legendary author of the Hermetica. The whole Egyptosophical tradition, from Ficino to Kircher, traced its 'ancient theology' back to this god of hidden knowledge, as set out by Garth Fowden.

## For artists & game designers
A clean reference for a 'god of knowledge' design: animal-headed scribe with palette and stylus, patron of writing and magic.

title: Pierio Valeriano, *Hieroglyphica* (1556) — page of Egyptian symbols
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Giovanni Pierio Valeriano Bolzani
summary:
A page from Pierio Valeriano's *Hieroglyphica, sive De sacris Aegyptiorum literis commentarii* (1556), the great Renaissance dictionary of symbolic meanings 'on the sacred letters of the Egyptians'.

## Iconography
The book pairs woodcut images of animals, plants, and objects with learned commentary on their allegorical sense, treating each as a [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphic]] sign whose hidden meaning the humanist scholar must unfold.

## Significance
Building on the rediscovered *Hieroglyphica* of Horapollo, Valeriano's vast compendium reconceived Egyptian writing as a universal symbolic language and supplied a quarry of motifs for emblem books, imprese, and Baroque iconography. It is a foundational text of the Renaissance conviction, traced by E. H. Gombrich and Erik Iversen, that hieroglyphs were pictures of timeless ideas.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made symbol-lexicon: each entry is an icon plus its 'true meaning', ideal for a puzzle system where creatures and objects spell out hidden messages.

citations.text: Erik Iversen, *The Myth of Egypt and Its Hieroglyphs* (1961).

title: The Vatican Obelisk before St Peter's Basilica
work: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: Anonymous (monument re-erected by Domenico Fontana, 1586)
summary:
The Vatican Obelisk standing at the centre of St Peter's Square, the obelisk whose dramatic re-erection in 1586 inaugurated Sixtus V's obelisk campaign.

## Iconography
The smooth red-granite [[motif:obelisk|shaft]] — uniquely among Rome's great obelisks, uninscribed with [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphs]] — rises from a base of bronze lions and is topped by a cross said to enclose a relic of the True Cross.

## Significance
Domenico Fontana's feat of transporting and raising the 300-tonne monolith for Pope Sixtus V was celebrated across Europe and recorded in his own illustrated treatise. Placed before the new basilica, the pagan Egyptian pillar was exorcised and consecrated, becoming the prototype for the Christianisation of Rome's obelisks and a spur to Hermetic-Egyptian antiquarianism.

## For artists & game designers
A plaza centrepiece and an engineering-epic in one: a giant foreign monolith hauled into place as the signature achievement of a ruler's reign.

citations.text: Erik Iversen, *Obelisks in Exile, vol. 1: The Obelisks of Rome* (1968).

title: Ea/Enki flanked by fish-cloaked apkallu, basalt basin from Assur (Pergamon Museum)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Neo-Assyrian); photograph by Osama Shukir Muhammed Amin
date: Neo-Assyrian, reign of Sennacherib (7th c. BCE)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Osama Shukir Muhammed Amin). Pergamon Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A basalt water-basin from Assur carved with the god Ea/Enki flanked by fish-cloaked apkallu sages — the aquatic, purifying form of Mesopotamia's protective genii.

## Iconography
The central figure is **Ea (Sumerian Enki)**, god of the sweet waters and of wisdom and magic, identified by the streams flowing from his shoulders. He is flanked by **apkallu** wearing the whole skin of a great **fish** as a cloak, the fish-head rising above their own — the *purādu*-fish sages associated with the primordial sage Adapa/Oannes.

## Significance
The **fish-cloaked apkallu** are the bringers of civilization and the masters of purification and exorcism, who served Ea, the divine patron of incantation. Figurines of these beings were buried under floors to ward off evil, exactly as the [[motif:incantation-bowl|incantation bowls]] would later be. The basin, used in temple purification rites, ties the apotropaic sages to the cleansing power of water that runs through Mesopotamian magic. See Wiggermann (1992) and the *Bīt Rimki* ritual.

## For artists & game designers
The fish-cloak priest is a striking, under-used 'water-magic exorcist' design — half-man, half-fish, attendant on a god of the deep.

citations.text: F. A. M. Wiggermann, *Mesopotamian Protective Spirits* (1992).
S. Parpola, 'The Assyrian Tree of Life,' *JNES* 52 (1993).

title: Assyrian winged genie, Palace of Ashurnasirpal II at Nimrud
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Neo-Assyrian); photograph by Gary Todd
rights: Public domain (CC0, photo: Gary Todd). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Neo-Assyrian relief of a winged 'genie' (apkallu) from the Northwest Palace of Ashurnasirpal II at Nimrud, performing a rite of purification and protection.

## Iconography
The bearded, **winged** figure stands in profile holding a **cone** (a date-palm spathe) in one hand and a **bucket** (*banduddû*) in the other, the implements of a purifying, fertilizing aspersion. He wears a horned cap marking divine or semi-divine status. These are the **apkallu** — protective sages or genii.

## Significance
The **apkallu** were the antediluvian sages of Mesopotamian lore, guardians of secret wisdom who carried out apotropaic and purificatory rites. Lined along palace walls and buried as figurines under thresholds, they protected the king and the building from demons and disease. They embody the *benign* pole of the same protective magic whose hostile targets are the demons trapped in the [[motif:incantation-bowl|incantation bowls]]. Wiggermann's *Mesopotamian Protective Spirits* (1992) is the key study.

## For artists & game designers
The quintessential 'guardian sage' figure — winged, ritually equipped, lining the walls as a permanent protective garrison.

citations.text: F. A. M. Wiggermann, *Mesopotamian Protective Spirits: The Ritual Texts* (1992).

title: Incantation bowl with pseudo-Aramaic inscription (Metropolitan Museum)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Sasanian Mesopotamia)
rights: Public domain (CC0). The Metropolitan Museum of Art, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A terracotta incantation bowl in the Metropolitan Museum whose interior is filled with a spiral of pseudo-Aramaic writing, a clay 'demon-trap' buried beneath the floor of a Mesopotamian home.

## Iconography
The shallow earthenware bowl is inscribed on its concave inner surface with lines of script running in a **spiral** from rim to centre. Here the characters imitate Aramaic without forming a fully legible text — a *pseudo-inscription* produced by or for an illiterate client, showing that the bowl's form and the act of spiralling words were themselves felt to be efficacious.

## Significance
Mesopotamian incantation bowls (5th–7th c. CE) were inverted and set into the corners and thresholds of houses to **bind and trap** demons, the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]], curses, and the night-hag Lilith. The spell typically names the client, invokes divine and angelic authority, and 'seals' the demon — a domestic descendant of the older Akkadian exorcistic tradition. The standard corpus is J. A. Montgomery's *Aramaic Incantation Texts from Nippur* (1913), with later editions by Shaked, Ford and Bhayro.

## For artists & game designers
A buried bowl whose written spell only 'works' as a spiral is a perfect environmental trap object — the magic is in the geometry as much as the words.

citations.text: J. A. Montgomery, *Aramaic Incantation Texts from Nippur* (1913).
S. Shaked, J. N. Ford & S. Bhayro, *Aramaic Bowl Spells* (2013).

title: The Burney Relief / Queen of the Night (British Museum)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Old Babylonian); photograph by Osama Shukir Muhammed Amin
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Osama Shukir Muhammed Amin). The British Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Burney Relief, also called the 'Queen of the Night' — an Old Babylonian terracotta plaque of a winged, bird-taloned goddess long debated as Ishtar, Ereshkigal, or a Lilith-figure.

## Iconography
A nude **winged** female stands frontally on the backs of two **lions**, flanked by **owls**, her feet ending in bird **talons**. She holds the **rod-and-ring** symbols of divine authority and wears the horned crown of a deity. The talons and owls give her a nocturnal, chthonic character.

## Significance
The identity of the figure is contested: she has been read as **Ishtar/Inanna**, as her underworld sister **Ereshkigal**, or — in older and popular literature — as a *lilītu* (Lilith-type) night-demon, a reading now largely doubted by specialists. Whatever her name, the relief stands at the source of the imagery of dangerous winged female night-powers that flows down to the **Lilith** of the later [[motif:incantation-bowl|incantation bowls]]. I keep the identification open, following the British Museum and Frankfort's classic discussion.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'winged night-goddess' silhouette — talons, owls, lions — endlessly reusable for an ambiguous deity who is neither wholly good nor wholly demonic.

citations.text: H. Frankfort, 'The Burney Relief,' *Archiv für Orientforschung* 12 (1937–39).
D. Collon, *The Queen of the Night* (British Museum Objects in Focus, 2005).

title: Cylinder seal with archer and winged lion-demon (Walters Art Museum)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mesopotamia)
rights: Public domain. The Walters Art Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Mesopotamian cylinder seal engraved with an archer confronting a winged lion-demon — a contest scene that doubled as a protective device.

## Iconography
The rolled-out design shows an **archer** drawing his bow against a **winged lion** or composite demon, a version of the ancient Near Eastern 'contest' or 'hero-vs-beast' scene. Cut into hardstone, the seal both authenticated and protected, the engraved combat charging the little cylinder with warding force.

## Significance
**Cylinder seals** were Mesopotamia's signature personal objects, worn on a cord and used to sign documents, but they were also amulets: the choice of imagery — gods, protective genii, the defeat of demons — gave them apotropaic value. Scenes of a hero or god overpowering a demonic beast assert order over chaos, the same victory dramatized in the [[motif:lamashtu|Lamashtu]] plaques and later sealed into the [[motif:incantation-bowl|incantation bowls]]. Standard reference: D. Collon, *First Impressions* (1987).

## For artists & game designers
A tiny rollable object whose imprinted scene is a frozen boss-fight — identity, signature, and protective talisman in one.

citations.text: D. Collon, *First Impressions: Cylinder Seals in the Ancient Near East* (1987).

title: Incantation bowl with a bound demon drawn at the centre (Metropolitan Museum)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Sasanian Mesopotamia); photograph by Marie-Lan Nguyen
rights: Public domain (photo: Marie-Lan Nguyen). The Metropolitan Museum of Art, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An Aramaic incantation bowl with a crude figure of a fettered demon drawn in the centre, encircled by the binding spell — the classic 'devil-trap' bowl.

## Iconography
A schematic **demon** is sketched at the bowl's base: a frontal figure with wild hair, sometimes shown with bound hands and feet or chained at the neck. Around it the spell winds in a **spiral** of Aramaic letters. The image is not decorative but operative — the demon is depicted *already captured*, its likeness imprisoned by the surrounding text.

## Significance
The drawn-demon bowls make the magic visible: the threatening power (Lilith, a *lilith*, a curse-demon, or the personified [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]]) is named, depicted, and shackled, so that representation enacts capture. Such bowls were prepared by Jewish, Mandaean, Christian and pagan practitioners alike across Sasanian Babylonia, drawing on a shared repertoire of angelic names, Solomonic authority, and the formula of the *get* (writ of divorce) served on the demon.

## For artists & game designers
The 'bind the monster by drawing it in chains' mechanic in one object — depiction *is* containment.

citations.text: J. A. Montgomery, *Aramaic Incantation Texts from Nippur* (1913).
D. Levene, *A Corpus of Magic Bowls* (2003).

title: Mask of the demon Humbaba (Louvre, SB 6567)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Old Babylonian); photograph by Shonagon
date: Old Babylonian period (c. early 2nd millennium BCE)
rights: Public domain (CC0, photo: Shonagon). Musée du Louvre, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A clay mask of Humbaba (Huwawa), the monstrous guardian of the Cedar Forest slain by Gilgamesh, whose face was rendered as a single coiling line.

## Iconography
The grimacing **Humbaba** face is formed from one continuous **coiled line**, a labyrinthine pattern that ancient sources explicitly compared to the convolutions of animal **entrails**. The result is a frontal, apotropaic grotesque — a face that menaces whoever meets its gaze.

## Significance
**Humbaba/Huwawa** is the terrible guardian-demon of the *Epic of Gilgamesh*, but his single-line face had a second life in **extispicy** (divination by entrails): omen texts state that when the coils of a sheep's intestines formed 'the face of Humbaba,' it portended revolution or a usurper. The mask thus links epic myth, [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] art, and Babylonian divination. Discussed by Wiggermann and by the Gilgamesh scholarship of A. R. George.

## For artists & game designers
A monster whose entire face is one unbroken maze-line — a brilliant, instantly recognizable motif for a labyrinth-guardian or omen-sign.

citations.text: A. R. George, *The Babylonian Gilgamesh Epic* (2003).
F. A. M. Wiggermann, *Mesopotamian Protective Spirits* (1992).

title: Mandaic magical bowl from Mesopotamia (Wellcome Collection)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mandaean, Mesopotamia)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A magical bowl inscribed in Mandaic from the Wellcome Collection, another witness to the demon-binding bowls of late-antique Mesopotamia.

## Iconography
The bowl's interior carries a **spiral** of Mandaic letters. As in the wider genre, the text would name the client, summon protective powers, and pronounce a binding or expulsion against demons and the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]] threatening the household.

## Significance
Held in a medical-history collection, this bowl underlines that the spells were practical health-and-home magic: protection against miscarriage, disease, and malign spirits. Mandaic, Jewish Aramaic, and Syriac bowls share formulae and angelic names, evidence of a common Babylonian magical koine crossing religious lines. The corpus is edited by Yamauchi, Müller-Kessler, and the Shaked–Ford–Bhayro project.

## For artists & game designers
A museum-quality reference for a 'health charm' object whose protective intent is domestic and bodily rather than martial.

citations.text: S. Shaked, J. N. Ford & S. Bhayro, *Aramaic Bowl Spells* (2013).

title: Plaque of protection against Lamashtu
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mesopotamia)
summary:
An apotropaic plaque directed against Lamashtu, the lion-headed female demon who menaced pregnant women and infants in Mesopotamian belief.

## Iconography
**Lamashtu** is shown with a lion's or bird's head, ass's teeth and ears, taloned bird feet, and pendulous breasts at which a piglet and a puppy suckle; she clutches **serpents** and often stands on a donkey crossing the underworld river. The plaque, hung in the home or worn, presents her image precisely in order to repel her.

## Significance
Lamashtu (Akkadian *Lamaštu*) was the archetypal **child-snatching, fever-bringing demon**, blamed for miscarriage, crib-death, and puerperal disease. She was countered by incantation series and by amuletic plaques, frequently paired with [[motif:pazuzu|Pazuzu]], the only power thought able to drive her back. This Mesopotamian therapeutic-magical complex is a direct ancestor of the later Aramaic bowls' war on Lilith. F. A. M. Wiggermann and W. Farber have produced the standard studies.

## For artists & game designers
A fully realized 'plague-bringing hag' design — lion head, animal teeth, suckling beasts, serpents — terrifying and instantly legible.

citations.text: W. Farber, *Lamaštu: An Edition of the Canonical Series* (2014).
F. A. M. Wiggermann, 'Lamaštu, Daughter of Anu,' in M. Stol, *Birth in Babylonia* (2000).

title: Lamashtu amulet (British Museum)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mesopotamia)
rights: Public domain. The British Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Lamashtu amulet in the British Museum, a portable version of the protective plaque carried to ward the child-snatching demon from mother and infant.

## Iconography
The amulet depicts **Lamashtu** in her canonical form — leonine head, bared teeth, taloned feet, snakes in her raised hands, often suckling a piglet and pup and kneeling on her **donkey**. A perforation or loop allowed it to be worn or hung at the bedside of the vulnerable.

## Significance
Worn amulets extended the protection of the larger plaques to the body itself, especially around childbirth — the moment Lamashtu was most feared. Bearing the demon's own image turned her power against her, the same apotropaic logic that governs [[motif:pazuzu|Pazuzu]] heads and, centuries later, the drawn demons of the Aramaic [[motif:incantation-bowl|incantation bowls]]. Wiggermann and Farber remain the authorities.

## For artists & game designers
A wearable charm bearing the very monster it repels — the 'fight fire with fire' amulet, scaled down to jewelry.

citations.text: F. A. M. Wiggermann, 'Lamaštu, Daughter of Anu' (2000).

title: Plaque of conjuration against Lamashtu (Louvre, AO 22205)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mesopotamia); photograph by Tangopaso
rights: Public domain. Musée du Louvre, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Louvre 'Lamashtu plaque' (AO 22205), one of the celebrated multi-register exorcism plaques showing the demon driven down to the underworld river. (The annotated overlay labels the figures.)

## Iconography
The plaque is divided into horizontal **registers**: above, ranks of protective deities and astral symbols; in the centre, ritual acts at a sickbed; below, **Lamashtu** kneeling on her donkey as it is ferried across the river of the netherworld, surrounded by her serpents, comb, and spindle. A looming head — that of [[motif:pazuzu|Pazuzu]] — often peers over the top edge, expelling her.

## Significance
These 'Hell plaques' or *plaques de conjuration* are among the most complex apotropaic objects of the ancient Near East, compressing a whole exorcistic ritual into a single carved scene. They visually narrate the demon's defeat and banishment, functioning as a permanent, self-renewing spell. Wiggermann's studies of the *bīt mēseri* protective rites illuminate their logic.

## For artists & game designers
A single object that storyboards an entire exorcism in stacked registers — a model for environmental art that tells a ritual narrative top-to-bottom.

citations.text: F. A. M. Wiggermann, *Mesopotamian Protective Spirits* (1992).

title: Mandaic incantation bowl for Buktuya and household (Royal Ontario Museum)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mandaean, Southern Mesopotamia); photograph by Daderot
rights: Public domain (CC0). Royal Ontario Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Mandaic-language incantation bowl inscribed for a named client, Buktuya, and his household — a window onto the Mandaean branch of the Mesopotamian magic-bowl tradition.

## Iconography
The bowl carries a dense **spiral** of cursive **Mandaic** script, the sacred script of the Mandaeans of southern Iraq. The text names the beneficiary (Buktuya) and his household and invokes protection over them, sealing out the demonic forces that threaten the home.

## Significance
Mandaic bowls form one of the three main scriptural streams of the genre, alongside Jewish Babylonian Aramaic and Syriac. The Mandaeans — a surviving Gnostic baptismal community — produced bowls drawing on their own cosmology of light and darkness. Naming the client personalizes the charm, a feature exploited by scholars to reconstruct Sasanian-era onomastics and social history. Key editions are by E. M. Yamauchi (*Mandaic Incantation Texts*, 1967) and C. Müller-Kessler.

## For artists & game designers
A protective charm keyed to a *named* owner and their dependents — a model for binding magic that is personal, not generic.

citations.text: C. Müller-Kessler, *Die Zauberschalentexte in der Hilprecht-Sammlung* (2005).

title: Aramaic incantation bowl from Nippur (Oriental Institute, Chicago)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Sasanian Babylonia); photograph by Daderot
rights: Public domain (CC0). Oriental Institute Museum, University of Chicago, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An Aramaic incantation bowl excavated at Nippur, the site whose finds founded the modern study of the entire genre.

## Iconography
The earthenware bowl bears the characteristic concentric **spiral** of square Aramaic letters, the binding spell coiling inward. Bowls of this type were recovered *in situ* at Nippur, often inverted and placed at the corners of rooms or beneath floors and doorways.

## Significance
Nippur is the type-site: the University of Pennsylvania excavations of the 1880s–90s yielded the bowls that John A. Montgomery published in *Aramaic Incantation Texts from Nippur* (1913), the foundational corpus. The Sasanian date (240–641 CE) anchors the genre firmly in late antiquity, in a Babylonia shared by Jews, Mandaeans, Christians and Zoroastrians whose magical practices visibly intermingle on the bowls.

## For artists & game designers
An archaeologically grounded artifact — the buried, inverted spell-bowl as found, useful reference for an excavation or dungeon-floor discovery.

citations.text: J. A. Montgomery, *Aramaic Incantation Texts from Nippur* (1913).
M. J. Geller, 'Jewish Magic in the Sasanian Period,' in *Officina Magica* (2005).

title: Pazuzu amulet
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mesopotamia); photograph by Zunkir
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Zunkir). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A small Pazuzu amulet, the wearable form of the wind-demon that placed his protective ferocity directly on the body of the bearer.

## Iconography
The amulet reproduces **Pazuzu** in miniature — canine snout, four **wings**, talons, and **scorpion tail** — frequently as just the head, pierced for suspension. Pazuzu heads were hung at thresholds, on furniture, and worn at the neck, often by or for pregnant women.

## Significance
The proliferation of cheap Pazuzu amulets shows how thoroughly his apotropaic role saturated daily life. Worn against [[motif:lamashtu|Lamashtu]] and against the diseases borne on the wind, the amulet works by the same principle as the [[motif:incantation-bowl|incantation bowls]]: confronting a demonic threat with a more terrible, allied power. Heeßel catalogues the typology of statuettes, heads, and plaques.

## For artists & game designers
A compact 'equip the lesser evil' talisman — proof that the scariest silhouette can read as a ward, not a threat.

citations.text: F. A. M. Wiggermann, *Mesopotamian Protective Spirits* (1992).

title: Cylinder seal with the head of Pazuzu (Louvre, AO 21111)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mesopotamia); photograph by Zunkir
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Zunkir). Musée du Louvre, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Louvre cylinder seal carved with the head of Pazuzu (AO 21111), combining the apotropaic demon with the administrative-magical technology of the seal.

## Iconography
The **head of Pazuzu** — snarling canine muzzle, deep brow, bulging eyes — is engraved on the body of a **cylinder seal**, an object normally rolled across clay to authenticate documents. Here the demon's face turns the seal into a protective device as much as a signature.

## Significance
The disembodied **Pazuzu head** was itself a potent apotropaion, believed to embody the whole demon; seals and amulets bearing it carried his warding power. The crossover of seal and amulet shows how Mesopotamian magic permeated even bureaucratic objects, and prefigures the later use of seals and signs to bind demons in the [[motif:incantation-bowl|bowl spells]] and grimoire tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A stamp/seal whose impression *is* a protective demon — a neat fusion of identity-marking and warding magic.

citations.text: D. Collon, *First Impressions: Cylinder Seals in the Ancient Near East* (1987).

title: Bronze statuette of the demon Pazuzu (Louvre)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Mesopotamia); photograph by Vassil
rights: Public domain (CC0). Musée du Louvre, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A bronze statuette of Pazuzu, king of the wind-demons, shown in profile — the apotropaic demon enlisted to fight other demons.

## Iconography
**Pazuzu** has a snarling **canine or leonine face**, bulging eyes, two pairs of bird **wings**, the **talons** of a bird of prey, a **scorpion tail**, and an erect serpent-headed phallus. One hand is raised, the other lowered — the gesture of his standard formula, 'I ascended and descended.' His grotesque ferocity is precisely what makes him protective.

## Significance
Though himself a malevolent bringer of the destructive west wind and fevers, **Pazuzu** was harnessed *against* worse evils — above all [[motif:lamashtu|Lamashtu]], whom he alone could drive back to the underworld. His image guarded homes, pregnant women, and (via small heads) was worn as an amulet. Pazuzu's modern fame owes much to *The Exorcist* (1973); the scholarly study is N. Heeßel's *Pazuzu* (2002).

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'monstrous protector' — assembled from dog, eagle, scorpion and snake, terrifying yet on the player's side.

citations.text: N. P. Heeßel, *Pazuzu: Archäologische und philologische Studien* (2002).
F. A. M. Wiggermann, *Mesopotamian Protective Spirits* (1992).

title: Protective bowl with inscription and a demon (Louvre)
work: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: Anonymous (Sasanian Mesopotamia)
summary:
A *bol de protection* — a protective incantation bowl combining a spiral spell with a drawn demon, exemplifying the union of word and image that defines the genre.

## Iconography
The spell spirals across the bowl's interior while a **demon** is figured within it. Text and image work together: the [[motif:bound-demon|demon]] is named in the writing and depicted in the drawing, doubly fixing it. The inward-turning spiral mimics the act of enclosing and confining the hostile power at the bowl's centre.

## Significance
Protective bowls addressed the everyday terrors of Sasanian domestic life — sickness, miscarriage, the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]], and night-demons such as Lilith and the *lilin*. Many invoke the same angelic and divine names found in contemporary Jewish, Christian and Mandaean magic, and some deploy the legal fiction of issuing the demon a writ of divorce. The interpenetration of traditions makes the bowls a prime source for the religious history of late-antique Babylonia.

## For artists & game designers
The coiling text + caged figure is a ready-made 'sealing circle' visual: a spell whose very shape is a prison.

citations.text: D. Levene, *A Corpus of Magic Bowls* (2003).
S. Shaked, J. N. Ford & S. Bhayro, *Aramaic Bowl Spells* (2013).

title: The Budūḥ Magic Square
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Anonymous (lettrist tradition)
summary:
The famous **budūḥ** square — the 3×3 magic square whose corner letters spell the talismanic word *budūḥ*.

## Iconography
A simple **3×3 grid** holds the numbers 2-9-4 / 7-5-3 / 6-1-8 (every row, column and diagonal summing to fifteen). Read by their *abjad* letter-values, the four corner cells (b–d–w–ḥ) form the magic name **BUDŪḤ**, which was itself written as a charm in place of the full square.

## Significance
The budūḥ square is the best-known device of Islamic lettrist magic (*ʿilm al-ḥurūf*), invoked for protection, safe childbirth and the safe arrival of letters and goods. Its reduction from a numerical [[motif:magic square|wafq]] to a single pronounceable name shows how operative magic compressed a whole diagram into one potent word.

## For artists & game designers
A glyph with a self-contained internal logic (equal sums) and a 'true name' hidden in its corners — an elegant model for a rune whose power has an in-world rationale.

citations.text: Emilie Savage-Smith (ed.), *Magic and Divination in Early Islam* (2004).

title: Demons (Devils), Kitāb al-Bulhān
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Workshop of ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī (attrib.), with later additions
summary:
A page of **devils** (*shayāṭīn*) from the *Kitāb al-Bulhān* (Book of Wonders), the illustrated compendium of astrology, divination and the spirit-world made in Jalayirid Baghdad.

## Iconography
Clustered dark **demons** with horns, fangs, clawed limbs and flame-coloured or smoke-grey bodies crowd the leaf. Each belongs to the manuscript's gallery of [[motif:jinn|jinn]] and devil-chiefs, marked off from humans by bestial heads and unnatural skin so that the practitioner could recognise — and ritually master — them.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* (Bodleian MS Or. 133) gathers the divinatory lore of Abū Maʿshar and others; its Ottoman recensions ordered the spirit-rulers into a scheme of days, planets and colours. Such demon-galleries lie at the root of the talismanic tradition that, through the *Picatrix*, fed European astral magic.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made roster of named demon-chiefs with built-in planetary and chromatic correspondences — a summoning bestiary in one frame.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *The Wonders of Creation and the Singularities of Painting* (2015).

title: Spirit-King Folio (fol. 32a), Book of Wonders (Kitāb al-Bulhān)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Anonymous (Arabic collectaneous manuscript)
summary:
Folio 32a of a fifteenth-century *Kitāb al-Bulhān* (Book of Wonders), showing one of the manuscript's enthroned **spirit-kings**.

## Iconography
A commanding **jinn-king** is set centrally with smaller attendant demons, in the manuscript's recurring 'ruler-and-retinue' format. Horn-shape, body-colour and regalia mark his rank within the hierarchy of day- and planet-rulers.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* is the single richest illustrated source for the Islamicate doctrine of governable [[motif:jinn|spirit-rulers]]. Each king's image is effectively a talismanic portrait, intended to make the spirit recognisable so that incantation and timing could bind him. The leaf belongs to Bodleian MS Or. 133.

## For artists & game designers
The 'enthroned chief with demon retinue' composition is a clean boss-and-minions template lifted straight from a medieval magical handbook.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *Il Kitāb al-Bulhān di Oxford* (1988).

title: Devils Talking (Conversing Demons), Kitāb al-Bulhān
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Workshop of ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī (attrib.), with later additions
summary:
A scene of **conversing devils** from the *Kitāb al-Bulhān*, showing demons in close exchange rather than menace.

## Iconography
Two or more horned, party-coloured **jinn** lean together as if in council or conversation. The grouping belongs to the manuscript's demonological cycle, where the spirits are individuated by colour, horn-shape and posture so the reader can tell one ruler from another.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* treats the [[motif:jinn|jinn]] not as a faceless horde but as a hierarchy of named, planet-bound chiefs who could be addressed and bound. Depicting them in social converse underlines the Islamicate premise that the spirit-world was an ordered society, governable by the talismanic sciences.

## For artists & game designers
A 'demons in parley' tableau is a useful counter-image to the usual snarling monster — spirits shown scheming or negotiating among themselves.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *Il Kitāb al-Bulhān di Oxford* (1988).

title: The Jinn King of Tuesday (al-Aḥmar), Kitāb al-Bulhān
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Workshop of ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī (attrib.), with later additions
figures: al-Aḥmar (the Red King, jinn-king of Tuesday)
summary:
The **Jinn King of Tuesday** from the *Kitāb al-Bulhān*, one of the seven day-ruling spirit-kings of the manuscript's demonological scheme.

## Iconography
A large enthroned or striding **demon-king** dominates the leaf, attended by lesser spirits. As ruler of Tuesday he is tied to **Mars** and to the colour red — *al-Aḥmar*, 'the Red' — the planet of war and iron, his form coloured and armed to match.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* assigns each weekday a presiding [[motif:jinn|jinn]]-king with a planet, a colour and an angelic overseer, so a magician could time and address a conjuration to the right ruler. This seven-king scheme, systematised in the Ottoman recensions, is one of the clearest surviving visual codifications of Islamicate planetary spirit-magic.

## For artists & game designers
Seven planet-coded spirit-kings, one per weekday, give a complete calendrical summoning system — pick the day, get the king, the planet and the colour.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *The Wonders of Creation and the Singularities of Painting* (2015).

title: Page of al-Būnī's Shams al-Maʿārif (the Sun of Knowledge)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Aḥmad ibn ʿAlī al-Būnī (text); printed edition
summary:
A page from al-Būnī's *Shams al-Maʿārif* (The Sun of Knowledge), the foundational handbook of Islamic lettrist magic (*ʿilm al-ḥurūf*).

## Iconography
The page sets **gridded letter- and number-squares** among lines of Arabic text. The squares deploy the Arabic letters and their numerical (*abjad*) values, together with the [[motif:divine names|Beautiful Names of God]], to concentrate celestial and divine power into a usable talismanic device.

## Significance
Attributed to Aḥmad al-Būnī (d. c. 1225), the *Shams al-Maʿārif* systematised the science of letters: the idea that the twenty-eight Arabic letters carry the powers by which God created the world, so arranging them in squares and names channels those powers. It became the single most influential — and most feared — grimoire of the Islamicate world.

## For artists & game designers
The living-letter premise — every glyph a unit of creative power — turns an alphabet itself into a magic system, with squares as spells you literally assemble from letters.

citations.text: Noah Gardiner, 'Esotericism in a Manuscript Culture: Aḥmad al-Būnī and His Readers' (PhD diss., 2014).

title: The Fallen Angels Hārūt and Mārūt
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Anonymous (Persianate workshop)
summary:
A painting of the **fallen angels Hārūt and Mārūt**, the two angels who, in Islamic tradition, taught humankind magic at Babylon.

## Iconography
The two **angels** are shown suspended head-downward in the pit at Babel — the punishment for teaching forbidden sorcery — winged and haloed yet inverted, set against a rocky well. The composition pictures the moment that magic entered the world.

## Significance
Qurʾan 2:102 names **Hārūt and Mārūt** as the source from which men learned magic that 'could separate a man from his wife'. The pair became the mythic origin-point of the whole talismanic and astral science: angels who held the forbidden knowledge and were punished for dispensing it. Their image frames Islamicate magic as a dangerous, heaven-derived gift.

## For artists & game designers
The inverted-angels-in-a-pit motif is a striking origin-myth image for a magic system — power that came from above but at a cost, its teachers eternally punished.

citations.text: G. Vajda, 'Hārūt wa-Mārūt,' *Encyclopaedia of Islam* (2nd ed.).

title: Diagram of the Levels of Heaven (Islamic Cosmology)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Modern diagram (SPQR10) after Islamic Neoplatonic cosmology
summary:
A modern diagram of the **concentric heavens** of Islamic Neoplatonic cosmology — the model of nested spheres behind Ibn ʿArabī's and the Sufi cosmologists' circle-diagrams.

## Iconography
**Concentric circles** rise from the earthly centre through the planetary heavens to the outermost spheres, each ring labelled. The form is the classic Ptolemaic-Islamic cosmos: a graded ladder of being mapped as nested rings.

## Significance
The descent-of-being and the ranked heavens were core to the cosmologies of [[motif:emanation|Islamic Neoplatonism]], al-Ghazālī and Ibn ʿArabī, whose *Futūḥāt* circle-diagrams chart the divine names, the spheres and the 'Breath of the Merciful' radiating the cosmos into existence. This reconstruction renders that nested-spheres model clearly; it is a modern educational diagram rather than a historical manuscript leaf.

## For artists & game designers
Nested concentric heavens give a ready vertical-world map — ascend ring by ring toward the source, each sphere a level with its own ruler.

citations.text: William C. Chittick, *The Sufi Path of Knowledge: Ibn al-ʿArabī's Metaphysics of Imagination* (1989).

title: Celestial Map: Zodiac and Lunar Mansions
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Seyyid Lokman (workshop)
summary:
An Ottoman **celestial map** combining the signs of the zodiac with the twenty-eight **lunar mansions** (*manāzil al-qamar*).

## Iconography
A concentric circular diagram sets the twelve zodiac signs in an outer band and the twenty-eight **lunar mansions** — the moon's nightly stations along the ecliptic — in an inner ring, each compartment labelled and ornamented. The whole reads as a working wheel of the sky.

## Significance
The lunar mansions were central to Islamicate astral magic: each of the twenty-eight stations had its own influence, image and use, and the *Picatrix* drew on them for timing talismans. This map, associated with the workshop of the Ottoman court historian Seyyid Lokman, shows the mansion-system as a precise calendrical-astrological instrument.

## For artists & game designers
A twenty-eight-station lunar wheel gives a fine-grained timing mechanic — each night-station with its own image and effect, layered over the twelve-sign year.

citations.text: Emilie Savage-Smith (ed.), *Magic and Divination in Early Islam* (2004).

title: Mandaean Scroll of Abathur (Diwan Abatur)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Anonymous (Mandaean priestly scribe)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Ethan Doyle White). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A **Mandaean ritual scroll** illustrating the *Diwan Abatur*, the realm of the weigher-of-souls **Abathur** and the purgatories the soul passes after death.

## Iconography
The long scroll runs as a sequence of simple linear **drawings** of the *maṭarata* — the watch-houses or purgatories — and the throne of **Abathur**, who weighs souls on his scales. The schematic, register-by-register figures map the soul's astral ascent through stations of judgement.

## Significance
The Mandaeans of southern Iraq and Iran preserve a Gnostic religion with strong astral and stellar elements, long linked by outsiders to the 'Sabians' of the Qurʾan. Their cosmographic scrolls chart the soul's post-mortem journey through planetary watch-houses — a distinctive Near Eastern astral-eschatological tradition standing at the fringe of the Islamicate occult world.

## For artists & game designers
A register-by-register 'scroll of the afterlife journey' — a linear sequence of watch-houses and a soul-weighing gate — is a ready level-progression map drawn from a living tradition.

citations.text: E. S. Drower, *The Mandaeans of Iraq and Iran* (1937).

title: Astrological Figure from a Book of Nativities (after Abū Maʿshar)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: after Abū Maʿshar al-Balkhī
summary:
An illustrated leaf associated with the astrology of **Abū Maʿshar al-Balkhī** (Albumasar), the master of the *Kitāb al-Mawālīd* (Book of Nativities).

## Iconography
A robed figure — the astrologer or a personified planet — is set among the apparatus of **judicial astrology**: the casting of a birth-chart and the reading of planetary positions at the moment of nativity. The image stands for the science of computing a life from the heavens.

## Significance
Abū Maʿshar (787–886) was the most authoritative astrologer of the Islamic world; his works on nativities and great conjunctions, translated into Latin, made him the chief astrological authority of the medieval West. The *Kitāb al-Mawālīd* set out how the natal horoscope determines character and fate — the core practice of [[motif:horoscopy|judicial astrology]].

## For artists & game designers
The natal-chart astrologer is a perfect 'fate-reader' NPC: present a birth moment, receive a destiny — a built-in character-generation oracle.

citations.text: Charles Burnett & Keiji Yamamoto, *Abū Maʿšar on Historical Astrology* (2000).

title: Engraved Brass Astrolabe
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Anonymous instrument-maker
repository: Musée national de la Marine, Paris
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo Rama). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraved **brass astrolabe**, the master instrument of Islamicate astronomy and astrology, photographed against white.

## Iconography
The disc shows a pierced **rete** — an openwork star-map with curved pointers to named bright stars and a ring for the ecliptic — turning over engraved latitude plates, with degree- and hour-scales around the rim. Every line is calculation made visible: time, star positions, and the casting of horoscopes.

## Significance
Perfected by Islamic makers from the ninth century, the astrolabe let an astrologer find the **ascendant** and the planets' places for any instant — the indispensable first move in casting a [[motif:horoscopy|nativity]]. A portable model of the heavens, it fused exact science with astral divination and passed directly into the medieval Latin West. This example is in the Musée national de la Marine, Paris.

## For artists & game designers
The rotating rete is a real analog-computer UI — a turnable star-dial that can drive a time-and-fate mechanic with genuine historical pedigree.

citations.text: David A. King, *In Synchrony with the Heavens* (2004–05).

title: The Magical Circle of Solomon (Hockley)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Frederick Hockley and Henry Dawson Lea
rights: Public domain (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A nineteenth-century English **Magical Circle of Solomon**, drawn in the Solomonic grimoire tradition that descends — through the *Picatrix* and the *Key of Solomon* — from the Near Eastern lore of Solomon as master of spirits.

## Iconography
A carefully ruled **protective circle** is inscribed with divine and angelic **names**, crosses and characters, the operator's standing-ground for conjuration. The design belongs to the Western Solomonic books, here in a clean draughtsman's copy by the occultist Frederick Hockley.

## Significance
The figure of **Solomon** as commander of *jinn* and demons is shared across Islamicate and European magic; the [[motif:seal of solomon|Seal of Solomon]] (the hexagram *khātam Sulaymān*) and the conjuring-circle pass between the traditions. This English drawing represents the late reception of that current — a Solomonic image whose roots lie in the Near Eastern science of binding spirits.

## For artists & game designers
The inscribed conjuring-circle is the canonical 'safe zone' of summoning magic — a ringed ground of names that protects the caster while the spirit is bound outside it.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Orion (al-Jabbār), al-Ṣūfī's Book of Fixed Stars
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-Ṣūfī
summary:
The constellation **Orion** — *al-Jabbār*, 'the Giant' — from a 1125 Baghdad copy of al-Ṣūfī's *Kitāb ṣuwar al-kawākib al-thābita* (Book of the Fixed Stars).

## Iconography
A standing, robed **giant** holds a staff and a hide or sleeve, his catalogued **stars** marked on the body with their magnitudes. Following al-Ṣūfī's method, the figure embodies the constellation while serving as a labelled, usable star-map.

## Significance
Completed in 964 CE, al-Ṣūfī's treatise reconciled Ptolemy's catalogue with indigenous Arab star-lore and became the canonical source of constellation imagery across the Islamic world and Renaissance Europe. This early copy (Museum of Islamic Art, Doha, MS 2.1998) is among the finest surviving witnesses to his Orion. The fixed stars it maps were the raw material of both astronomy and astral magic.

## For artists & game designers
The 'giant whose body is a star-chart' is a vivid way to render a sky-figure that is at once a character and a navigational diagram.

citations.text: Emmy Wellesz, *An Islamic Book of Constellations* (1965).

title: Talismanic Scroll (Met DP119750)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
creator: Anonymous
repository: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
rights: Public domain (CC0, Metropolitan Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A long **talismanic scroll** densely inscribed with Qurʾanic text, divine names and magic squares, made to be rolled into an amulet and carried.

## Iconography
The narrow strip is filled top to bottom with minute **calligraphy** — Qurʾanic verses, the [[motif:names of god|Beautiful Names of God]], prayers and gridded **magic squares** (*wafq*) — arranged in panels, roundels and ruled bands. The whole surface is charged text, the format echoing block-printed amulet scrolls.

## Significance
Talismanic scrolls applied the lettrist principle of *ʿilm al-ḥurūf* in portable form: rolled tight and worn or hung, they carried the protective virtue of sacred words against illness, the evil eye and danger in war. Many were block-printed from the medieval period, anticipating mass-produced amulets. This example is in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## For artists & game designers
A wearable 'scroll of protection' literally built from inscribed words — unrolling it reveals stacked sigils and squares, a satisfying physical artefact.

citations.text: Yasmine Al-Saleh, 'Amulets and Talismans from the Islamic World,' *Heilbrunn Timeline of Art History*, Met Museum (2010).

title: Sagittarius (zodiac centaur), Kitāb al-Bulhān
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī (attrib.)
summary:
The sign **Sagittarius** rendered as the archer-centaur in the *Kitāb al-Bulhān*.

## Iconography
A **centaur** twists to draw a bow, the classical *qaws* (archer) of the zodiac. In the manuscript's scheme he is bound to **Jupiter**, lord of the sign, and set among the figures that personify the celestial houses.

## Significance
The archer-centaur descends from Hellenistic constellation imagery transmitted through Arabic astronomy and re-coloured in the Persianate idiom of Jalayirid Baghdad. The *Kitāb al-Bulhān*'s zodiac cycle is among the most influential medieval Islamic visualisations of the signs, mediating the iconography that later reached Europe.

## For artists & game designers
The self-aiming centaur archer is an archetype that reads instantly as a ranged-attacker class keyed to the fire element and Jupiter.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *The Wonders of Creation and the Singularities of Painting* (2015).

title: Human figure and devils, Kitāb al-Bulhān
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Workshop of ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī (attrib.)
date: late 14th c. (Jalayirid Baghdad), with later additions
summary:
A folio from the *Kitāb al-Bulhān* (Book of Wonders), an illustrated compendium of astrology, divination and the spirit-world produced in Jalayirid Baghdad.

## Iconography
A central robed figure is flanked by dark, horned and clawed **devils** (shayāṭīn) with bestial heads and flaming or smoke-like bodies. The grouping belongs to the manuscript's gallery of [[motif:jinn|jinn]] and demon-kings, each ruler associated with a day, a planet and a colour, depicted to be recognised and ritually controlled.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* (Oxford, Bodleian MS Or. 133) compiles the astrological-divinatory lore of Abū Maʿshar and others; its later Ottoman recensions systematised the demon-kings into a ruler-of-the-week scheme. Such images sit at the root of the talismanic tradition that fed, through the Picatrix, into European astral magic.

## For artists & game designers
A ready bestiary of named, colour- and planet-coded demon lords — a complete summoning roster with built-in correspondences.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *The Wonders of Creation and the Singularities of Painting* (2015).

title: Leo, Kitāb al-Bulhān
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī
summary:
The zodiac sign **Leo** as personified in the *Kitāb al-Bulhān*, where each sign is shown with its planetary lord and astrological dignities.

## Iconography
A striding **lion** is paired with a solar disc or enthroned figure, marking Leo as the domicile of the **Sun**. The painting follows the manuscript's habit of fusing the constellation-beast with the planet that rules it, so the image encodes both sign and rulership at a glance.

## Significance
The sign-and-planet pairings of the *Kitāb al-Bulhān* visualise the doctrine of [[motif:domicile|planetary domiciles]] central to Arabic and, in turn, Latin judicial astrology. The leaf is attributed to the painter ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī and survives in Bodleian MS Or. 133.

## For artists & game designers
A clean template for a 'sign + ruling planet' card set where each zodiac beast carries its governing power as a visible attribute.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *Il Kitāb al-Bulhān di Oxford* (1988).

title: Celestial Globe (silvered brass), Mughal India
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous instrument-maker
summary:
A silvered-brass **celestial globe** of the Mughal period, modelling the sphere of the fixed stars.

## Iconography
The globe's surface is engraved with the **constellation figures** and inlaid star-points, ringed by the great circles of the celestial sphere — equator, ecliptic and colures. The figures follow the al-Ṣūfī tradition, here rendered in metal to be turned in the hand.

## Significance
Islamicate celestial globes, often cast in a single seamless piece by the famed instrument-makers of Lahore, gave astronomers and astrologers a three-dimensional sky to model star risings and the framework of the horoscope. They are the sculptural counterpart of the star catalogue, and among the most refined scientific objects of the Mughal world.

## For artists & game designers
A hand-held star sphere is an evocative artifact — a turnable model of the whole heavens that an astrologer-character can consult.

title: The Seven Sleepers of Ephesus, Folio from a Falnāma (Book of Omens)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous (Safavid or Ottoman court workshop)
rights: Public domain (CC0, Metropolitan Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A large-format divinatory painting from a *Falnāma* (Book of Omens), showing the **Seven Sleepers of Ephesus** (Aṣḥāb al-Kahf) and their dog discovered in the cave.

## Iconography
The sleepers and the dog **Qiṭmīr** lie in a rocky cave while a royal party (here named for Alexander) arrives; the scene fills a monumental folio meant to face a page of omen-text. The brilliant palette and dramatic framing are typical of *Falnāma* divinatory paintings.

## Significance
The *Falnāma* was used for **bibliomancy**: a seeker opened the book at random, and the priest read the image and its facing prognostication as an omen of the querent's fortune. The genre flourished in the Safavid and Ottoman courts of the 16th–17th centuries. This folio is in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## For artists & game designers
A fully realised 'draw-a-card-of-fate' mechanic: one striking image paired to a fortune text, designed to be opened at random.

citations.text: Massumeh Farhad & Serpil Bağcı, *Falnama: The Book of Omens* (2009).

title: Geomantic Instrument of Muḥammad ibn Khutlukh al-Mawṣilī
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Muḥammad ibn Khutlukh al-Mawṣilī
summary:
The earliest known **geomantic instrument**, a brass device inlaid with silver and gold for mechanically generating the figures of *ʿilm al-raml* (the science of the sand).

## Iconography
The rectangular brass plate carries rows of rotating **dials** and sliders; turning them produces the sixteen four-line **geomantic figures** that would otherwise be made by marking dots in sand. Bands of inscription name the figures and their meanings around the apparatus.

## Significance
Geomancy (*ʿilm al-raml*) generated a chart of sixteen figures, read for divinatory answers much as a horoscope is read; it was among the most popular Islamic divinatory arts and passed to Europe as *geomantia*. Signed by Muḥammad ibn Khutlukh al-Mawṣilī and dated 639 AH (1241–42), this device (now British Museum) mechanised the casting of figures into a single elegant machine.

## For artists & game designers
A genuine 'fortune-telling machine' — turnable dials that output a fixed set of sixteen symbolic results, a ready divination minigame.

citations.text: Emilie Savage-Smith & Marion B. Smith, *Islamic Geomancy and a Thirteenth-Century Divinatory Device* (1980).

title: Alchemical Folio from a Miscellany of Magical and Alchemical Recipes
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous (Jābirian tradition)
summary:
An **alchemical** page from an Arabic recipe miscellany standing in the tradition of Jābir ibn Ḥayyān (Latin *Geber*).

## Iconography
Columns of Arabic procedural text set out an **alchemical** operation — proportions, substances and stages of the work — in the terse imperative of a laboratory recipe. Such pages rarely illustrate apparatus; the diagram is the procedure itself.

## Significance
The Jābirian corpus founded Arabic alchemy on the **sulphur–mercury** theory of metals and the doctrine of the *mīzān* (balance), aiming at the elixir that perfects base matter. Translated into Latin, this body of work became the bedrock of European alchemy. Working miscellanies like this one carried the operative tradition through the centuries.

## For artists & game designers
Procedural alchemical text is ideal flavour for a transmutation crafting system — terse, imperative, and convincingly technical.

citations.text: Syed Nomanul Haq, *Names, Natures and Things: The Alchemist Jābir ibn Ḥayyān* (1994).

title: Magic Bowl (magische schaal)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (Tropenmuseum / Nationaal Museum van Wereldculturen). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A **magic-medicinal bowl** of the Islamic type, inscribed inside and out to charge water with healing power.

## Iconography
The metal bowl is engraved with **inscriptions** — Qurʾanic verses, names, and often **magic squares** and figural or astrological signs — covering its interior. Water poured in and drunk was believed to absorb the virtue of the words and cure poisoning, fever, fright or the bite of a mad dog.

## Significance
The so-called **poison cup** or healing bowl is one of the most widespread instruments of Islamicate practical magic, made from the medieval period onward across Syria, Egypt, Iran and India. It applies the same lettrist principle as the talismanic shirt and the magic square: sacred and numerically potent text transfers power to the body, here through ingested water.

## For artists & game designers
An inscribed healing vessel — water charged by reading the bowl's words — is a tidy in-world potion mechanic with a clear ritual logic.

citations.text: H. Henry Spoer, 'Arabic Magic Medicinal Bowls,' *Journal of the American Oriental Society* (1935).

title: Shiʿi Stamped Amulet with Magic Square
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A stamped (block-printed) **amulet** carrying a **magic square** and pious inscriptions, of the kind produced for personal protection in the Shiʿi world.

## Iconography
The sheet bears a gridded numerical or lettered **magic square** (*wafq*) framed by lines of devotional text and the names of holy figures. The square's rows, columns and diagonals each sum to the same value — the property held to bind its protective force.

## Significance
The science of the *wafq* (magic squares) and the famous **budūḥ** square belong to *ʿilm al-ḥurūf*, the lettrist occult science systematised by al-Būnī. Squares were inscribed on paper, metal and cloth to heal, protect in childbirth, or guard against evil. Mass-stamped amulets like this democratised what had been an elite talismanic art.

## For artists & game designers
The magic square is a self-contained 'rune of power' glyph whose internal logic (equal sums) gives an in-world rationale for why it works.

citations.text: Emilie Savage-Smith (ed.), *Magic and Divination in Early Islam* (2004).

title: Persian Astrolabe (brass)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous instrument-maker
rights: Public domain (CC0, Huntington Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A brass **astrolabe** from Iran, the master instrument of Islamicate astronomy and astrology.

## Iconography
The disc carries a pierced **rete** — a star-map with curved **pointers** to named bright stars and a ring for the ecliptic — rotating over engraved latitude plates. Scales of degrees and hours ring the rim. Every line serves computation: time, star positions, and the casting of horoscopes.

## Significance
The astrolabe, perfected by Islamic makers from the 9th century, let an astrologer find the **ascendant** and the positions of the planets for any moment — the indispensable first step of casting a nativity. As a portable model of the heavens it embodied the union of exact science and astral divination, and its design passed directly into medieval Europe.

## For artists & game designers
The rotating rete is a beautiful real-world 'analog computer' UI — a turnable star-dial that can drive a time-and-fate mechanic.

citations.text: Emilie Savage-Smith & others; David A. King, *In Synchrony with the Heavens* (2004–05).

title: Winged Angels (and Winged Feline and Cow), al-Qazwīnī's ʿAjāʾib al-makhlūqāt
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: after Zakariyyāʾ al-Qazwīnī
summary:
A folio from a manuscript of al-Qazwīnī's *ʿAjāʾib al-makhlūqāt* (Wonders of Creation), showing two winged **angels** beside winged hybrid beasts.

## Iconography
Two robed, haloed **angels** (*malāʾika*) with broad feathered wings stand in a register above (or beside) a winged feline and a winged cow. The pairing places the angelic order of creation next to the marvellous beasts, both classes of the *ʿajāʾib* — the wonders God set in the cosmos.

## Significance
al-Qazwīnī's thirteenth-century cosmography organises the universe from the celestial spheres and their angels down through the elements, plants, animals and monstrous races. Its angelic hierarchy — angels governing the planets and the throne — supplied later Islamic and even European magical lore with a populated heaven. This leaf is LACMA M.88.213.8.

## For artists & game designers
The register layout 'angelic rank above, beast rank below' is a compact way to show a cosmic hierarchy in a single illustrated frame.

citations.text: Persis Berlekamp, *Wonder, Image, and Cosmos in Medieval Islam* (2011).

title: Cepheus and Corona Borealis (Wonders of Creation)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Muḥammad ibn Muḥammad Shākir Ruzmah-ʾi Nāthānī, after al-Qazwīnī
summary:
A constellation page from an Ottoman copy of al-Qazwīnī's *ʿAjāʾib al-makhlūqāt*, illustrating **Cepheus** and the **Corona Borealis** (Northern Crown).

## Iconography
A standing, robed figure personifies the constellation **Cepheus**, his body studded with marked stars; a jewelled crown-arc renders the **Corona Borealis**. The stars are placed on the figure to double as a usable star map and a mythic portrait.

## Significance
Islamic constellation imagery descends from al-Ṣūfī's *Book of Fixed Stars*, which reconciled Ptolemy's catalogue with indigenous Arab star-lore. Copyists such as Ruzmah-ʾi Nāthānī (this leaf dated 1717, Walters W.659) kept the tradition alive into the Ottoman period, preserving the figures that astrologers used to read celestial influence.

## For artists & game designers
The 'human body as constellation map' device — stars fixed to anatomy — is a striking way to render a sky chart that is also a character.

citations.text: Emmy Wellesz, *An Islamic Book of Constellations* (1965).

title: The Singer Ibrāhīm and the Jinn
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous (Persianate workshop)
summary:
A narrative miniature from the *ʿajāʾib* tradition depicting the singer Ibrāhīm's encounter with a **jinn**.

## Iconography
A seated musician meets a horned, fanged **jinnī** with a coloured, semi-bestial body. The composition follows the convention for human–spirit encounters: the supernatural being is marked off by horns, flame-like hair and unnatural skin tone, yet engages the human in a recognisable social scene.

## Significance
The jinn are intelligent, fiery beings of the *ʿajāʾib* and Qurʾanic cosmology, capable of music, mischief and bargains. Tales of musicians taught or tormented by jinn dramatise the porousness between the human and spirit worlds that the occult sciences sought to manage through talismans and incantations.

## For artists & game designers
A model for the 'mortal makes a deal with a spirit' set-piece — note how only a few visual cues (horns, hue, flame) flip an otherwise human figure into a jinn.

citations.text: Amira El-Zein, *Islam, Arabs, and the Intelligent World of the Jinn* (2009).

title: Ursa Major, al-Ṣūfī's Book of Fixed Stars
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-Ṣūfī
date: manuscript copy of a 964 CE text
summary:
The constellation **Ursa Major** (the Great Bear) from a copy of al-Ṣūfī's *Kitāb ṣuwar al-kawākib al-thābita* (Book of the Fixed Stars).

## Iconography
A naturalistic **bear** is drawn with its catalogued stars marked on the body, each labelled with magnitude. al-Ṣūfī characteristically shows each figure twice — as seen in the sky and as mirror-reversed on a celestial globe — fusing observation with the inherited Ptolemaic image.

## Significance
Completed in 964 CE, al-Ṣūfī's treatise corrected and updated Ptolemy's *Almagest* with fresh observations and Arab star-names, and became the canonical source for constellation imagery across the Islamic world and, eventually, Renaissance Europe. This copy is Bodleian MS Marsh 144. Its figures underlie almost all later Islamic celestial globes and star maps.

## For artists & game designers
The 'observed vs. mirrored' double-image is an elegant way to show the difference between the sky as seen and the sky as modelled on a globe.

citations.text: Emmy Wellesz, *An Islamic Book of Constellations* (1965).

title: Sagittarius, al-Ṣūfī's Book of Fixed Stars
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-Ṣūfī
date: manuscript copy of a 964 CE text
summary:
The constellation **Sagittarius** as figured in al-Ṣūfī's *Book of the Fixed Stars*.

## Iconography
The archer-**centaur** draws his bow, his catalogued **stars** marked along body and weapon. As elsewhere in al-Ṣūfī, the figure follows the Ptolemaic constellation while bearing the magnitudes and Arab star-names of his updated catalogue.

## Significance
al-Ṣūfī's constellation figures, fixed in the late tenth century, became the visual standard for the zodiac and the fixed stars throughout Islamic astronomy and astrology, and passed into European celestial cartography. Sagittarius, lord-house of Jupiter, was among the most-copied of his images.

## For artists & game designers
A reference-grade archer-centaur with stars mapped onto anatomy — a sky-figure that doubles as a labelled diagram.

citations.text: Emmy Wellesz, *An Islamic Book of Constellations* (1965).

title: Talismanic Shirt (jāmah)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous
rights: Public domain (CC0, Metropolitan Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A **talismanic shirt** (jāmah / *kamīṣ*) covered in protective inscriptions, worn under armour for supernatural defence.

## Iconography
The whole surface is gridded with minute **calligraphy** — Qurʾanic verses, the ninety-nine Beautiful Names of God, prayers and **magic squares** (*wafq*) — arranged in roundels, cartouches and borders. Every field is a charged word; the garment is text made wearable.

## Significance
Such shirts were produced across the Islamic world from the 15th century onward, especially for soldiers, who believed the divine words would turn blades and arrows. They embody the central premise of Islamicate talismanic science: that the [[motif:names of god|names of God]] and numerically potent squares channel celestial and divine power onto the body. This example is in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## For artists & game designers
A literal 'armour of words' — a wearable that grants protection through inscribed text, ideal for an enchanted-garment item.

citations.text: Maryam Ekhtiar et al., *Masterpieces from the Department of Islamic Art* (2011).

title: Ilyās (Elijah) Given a Horse to Ascend (Chester Beatty T 414)
work: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: Anonymous (Ottoman workshop)
summary:
An Ottoman manuscript painting (Chester Beatty MS T 414) showing the prophet **Ilyās** (Elijah) given a horse for his heavenly ascent, attended by **angels**.

## Iconography
The robed prophet receives a mount as winged, flame-haloed **angels** hover in a gold sky. The angelic figures follow the Persianate-Ottoman convention — multicoloured wings, scarf-like sashes — that renders the celestial hierarchy mediating between heaven and earth.

## Significance
Angels (*malāʾika*) populate Islamic cosmology as governors of the spheres, bearers of revelation and conductors of souls; the seven planetary heavens each have their presiding angel. Scenes of prophetic ascent visualise the ladder of heavens that astral and visionary lore mapped onto the cosmos. The leaf belongs to a 16th–17th-century Ottoman codex in the Chester Beatty Library.

## For artists & game designers
The 'mount granted for an ascent through the heavens' motif gives a clean visual grammar for a level-by-level vertical journey guarded by angelic figures.

citations.text: Christiane Gruber, *The Praiseworthy One: The Prophet Muhammad in Islamic Texts and Images* (2019).

title: Diagram of the Names of God (Kircher)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
Athanasius Kircher's circular diagram of the divine names, radiating the Hebrew names and epithets of God outward from the Tetragrammaton.

## Iconography
A radiant centre holds the [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]] (YHWH); around it concentric bands and spokes distribute the many Hebrew names and attributes of God, arranged so that the seventy-two-fold and other name-systems fan out from the single ineffable Name like rays from a sun.

## Significance
Kircher (1602–1680), the Jesuit polymath, devoted parts of his *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* (1652–54) to Kabbalah, reading the divine names as a key linking Hebrew wisdom to a supposed universal Egyptian theology. His name-diagrams transmitted the Kabbalistic doctrine of the *Shem ha-Mephorash* — the explicit seventy-two-letter Name — to a wide learned audience as a centerpiece of Christian Kabbalah.

## For artists & game designers
A solar 'name-wheel' UI: a single sacred word at the hub spawning rays of lesser names, each a selectable invocation.

title: Ezekiel's Vision (William Blake)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: William Blake
summary:
William Blake's watercolour of Ezekiel's vision (ca. 1803–05, Museum of Fine Arts, Boston), a Romantic-visionary reading of the *Merkabah* chariot.

## Iconography
Blake renders the prophet beneath a great cloud of fire from which the four-faced [[motif:tetramorph|living creatures]] and the eyed wheels emerge, the human figures swept up in his characteristic flowing, energetic line. The composition fuses the biblical throne-vision with Blake's own mythology of imaginative apocalypse.

## Significance
Blake (1757–1827) read the prophets as fellow visionaries and made Ezekiel's chariot a touchstone of his art and poetry; for him the Merkabah was an emblem of the imagination's power to behold the eternal. The piece exemplifies the Romantic and later esoteric reception of Jewish throne-mysticism into Western visionary art. Kathleen Raine traced Blake's debts to the esoteric tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A reference for the 'ecstatic vision' register — chaotic fire, multiple faces, and swirling motion to depict a mind overwhelmed by the divine.

title: Sefirot diagram from *Kabbala Denudata*
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Christian Knorr von Rosenroth
summary:
A sefirot diagram from Knorr von Rosenroth's *Kabbala Denudata*, presenting the ten emanations as a vertical Tree of divine attributes.

## Iconography
The ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] descend in the familiar three-column arrangement — the pillars of mercy, severity, and the middle balance — each sphere labelled with its Hebrew name and joined to its neighbours by the twenty-two connective paths. The whole reads as a single articulated body of the divine emanations.

## Significance
This plainer Tree-diagram from the *Kabbala Denudata* shows the schematic form in which Lurianic and Zoharic teaching reached Christian Europe. The three-pillar layout it transmits became the standard template for the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn and virtually all later Western occult Kabbalah. Gershom Scholem traces the historical Kabbalah behind such schemata.

## For artists & game designers
The textbook three-pillar Tree — left/right/centre columns map neatly onto a balance of opposed forces with a reconciling middle path.

title: The Sefirot (Robert Fludd)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Robert Fludd
summary:
Robert Fludd's engraved diagram of the sefirot, integrating the Kabbalistic Tree into his vast Christian-Hermetic cosmology of the two worlds.

## Iconography
The ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] are arranged with their Hebrew names as a descending order of emanation from *Ein Sof* (the Infinite), often drawn as a flaming or radiant source above, down through the named powers to the material world. Fludd surrounds the scheme with Latin glosses tying each emanation to cosmological and theological correspondences.

## Significance
Fludd (1574–1637), the English Paracelsian physician and defender of the Rosicrucians, folded Kabbalah into the macrocosm-microcosm system of his *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617–21). His sefirotic diagrams exemplify how 17th-century Christian Kabbalah read the Tree as a universal cosmogony rather than a specifically Jewish theology. Frances Yates and Joscelyn Godwin survey Fludd's project.

## For artists & game designers
A richly engraved 'cosmic ladder' — pair the ten emanations with descending planes or realms for a vertical world-map.

title: The Golem of Prague (reproduction)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Anonymous (after the Prague legend)
date: Modern reproduction of a legendary subject
summary:
A reproduction depicting the Golem of Prague, the clay man animated by holy letters in the most famous legend of practical Kabbalah.

## Iconography
The Golem is shown as a hulking humanoid of clay; in the legend it is brought to life by inscribing the word *emet* ('truth') on its forehead (or placing a name-inscribed *shem* in its mouth), and deactivated by erasing the *aleph* to leave *met* ('dead'). The [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] themselves are the source of its life.

## Significance
The creation of a golem is rooted in the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s doctrine that letters can shape matter; medieval Ashkenazi pietists describe such rituals, and the legend later attached to Rabbi Judah Loew ben Bezalel (the Maharal) of 16th-century Prague, who supposedly made a golem to defend the Jewish community. Scholem's essay 'The Idea of the Golem' is the classic treatment; the tale became foundational for modern stories of artificial life.

## For artists & game designers
The original 'craftable construct': a servant golem activated and deactivated by inscribing or erasing a single word.

citations.text: Gershom Scholem, 'The Idea of the Golem,' in *On the Kabbalah and Its Symbolism* (1965).

title: Facsimile of Aaron's Breastplate (the *Hoshen*)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Anonymous (facsimile)
date: Modern facsimile of an antique design
summary:
A facsimile of the *Hoshen*, the jewelled breastplate of the Israelite High Priest — Scripture's own oracular and protective object.

## Iconography
The breastplate is a square panel set with twelve gemstones in four rows, each engraved with the name of one of the [[motif:twelve tribes|twelve tribes]] of Israel. Folded within it lay the *Urim and Thummim*, the lots by which the divine will was inquired; the whole was worn over the heart of the High Priest when he entered the sanctuary.

## Significance
Described in Exodus 28, the *Hoshen mishpat* ('breastplate of judgement') was the Bible's licit oracle, and later Jewish and esoteric tradition treated its stones and the *Shem ha-Mephorash* hidden in it as a deeply magical object — the priest's stones flashing to give God's answer. It became a key reference for esoteric correspondences of stones, tribes, and the zodiac. (Stone identifications vary across sources; this is a reconstructed facsimile.)

## For artists & game designers
A wearable 'oracle item': twelve named gems whose lighting pattern answers yes/no questions — a built-in divination mechanic.

title: Plate from *Kabbala Denudata* (Knorr von Rosenroth)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Christian Knorr von Rosenroth
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraved plate from the *Kabbala Denudata* (Kabbalah Unveiled, 1677–84), Christian Knorr von Rosenroth's monumental Latin anthology of Kabbalistic — especially Lurianic — texts.

## Iconography
The plate diagrams the structure of the divine worlds: the [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] and their groupings into the *partzufim* (configured 'faces' or personae of the godhead), labelled in Hebrew and Latin, mapping the Lurianic account of how the broken vessels are repaired and the divine countenance reconstituted.

## Significance
Knorr von Rosenroth (1636–1689) compiled the *Kabbala Denudata* at Sulzbach with the help of Francis Mercury van Helmont, translating the *Zohar* and Lurianic treatises of Isaac Luria and Hayyim Vital for a Christian readership. It was the single most influential channel of Jewish Kabbalah into early-modern Europe and, via 19th-century occultists, into the modern Western tradition. Allison Coudert's studies are standard.

## For artists & game designers
The 'advanced tech-tree' version of the Tree: nested faces and worlds, ideal for a layered cosmology where each tier reveals a deeper structure.

citations.text: Allison P. Coudert, *The Impact of the Kabbalah in the Seventeenth Century* (1999).

title: Lilith (John Collier, 1892)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: John Collier
summary:
John Collier's 1892 painting *Lilith*, the Pre-Raphaelite image of the demonic first woman entwined with a serpent.

## Iconography
A nude woman stands embraced by a great serpent coiling about her body, her eyes closed in sensual reverie. Collier renders [[motif:lilith|Lilith]] as the archetypal *femme fatale* — beautiful, dangerous, and intimate with the serpent, conflating her with the temptation of Eden.

## Significance
In Jewish tradition — crystallized in the medieval *Alphabet of Ben Sira* — Lilith is Adam's rebellious first wife, who flees Eden and becomes a night-demon and slayer of infants, against whom childbirth amulets invoke the angels Senoy, Sansenoy, and Semangelof. The *Zohar* develops her as a queen of the demonic 'other side' (*sitra achra*). The 19th-century Romantic and esoteric revival, seen here, recast her as a symbol of dangerous female autonomy. Raphael Patai's *The Hebrew Goddess* surveys the Lilith myth.

## For artists & game designers
A ready archetype for a seductive demon-queen antagonist — beauty, serpent, and lethal allure in one figure.

citations.text: Raphael Patai, *The Hebrew Goddess* (1967), ch. on Lilith.
*Alphabet of Ben Sira* (medieval), on Lilith as Adam's first wife.

title: The Golem and Rabbi Loew (Mikoláš Aleš, 1899)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Mikoláš Aleš
summary:
Mikoláš Aleš's 1899 illustration of the Golem with Rabbi Loew, made for a collection of Prague legends.

## Iconography
The Czech artist depicts the towering [[motif:golem|Golem]] beside or commanded by the figure of Rabbi Judah Loew (the Maharal), staging the moment of the creature's animation or service. The drawing belongs to the romantic-folkloric visual tradition that crystallized the Prague Golem as a national and literary legend.

## Significance
Mikoláš Aleš (1852–1913), a central figure of the Czech national revival, illustrated the Golem for the *Věnec pražských pověstí* ('Garland of Prague Legends'). Such turn-of-the-century images — alongside Hugo Steiner-Prag's and the 1915–20 Golem films — cemented the Maharal's golem in modern culture, ancestor of every fictional artificial being from the robot to the digital homunculus. The legend's Kabbalistic root remains the letter-animation of the *Sefer Yetzirah*.

## For artists & game designers
