A period-accurate 'master and construct' composition for a summoner archetype — the small maker beside the giant he commands.

citations.text: *Věnec pražských pověstí* (Garland of Prague Legends, 1908), illustr. Mikoláš Aleš.
Gershom Scholem, 'The Idea of the Golem' (1965).

title: Ezekiel's Vision of the Merkabah (Merian)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Matthäus Merian the Elder
summary:
Matthäus Merian's engraving of Ezekiel's vision of the *Merkabah* — the divine chariot-throne that founds the oldest current of Jewish mysticism.

## Iconography
The prophet beholds the four living creatures (the [[motif:tetramorph|tetramorph]] of man, lion, ox, and eagle), the gleaming 'wheels within wheels' (*ophanim*) studded with eyes, and above them the crystalline firmament bearing the sapphire throne and the radiant likeness of a human form — the *kavod*, the Glory of God.

## Significance
Ezekiel 1 and 10 supplied the foundational imagery of *Ma'aseh Merkabah*, the 'work of the chariot,' the visionary throne-mysticism of the Hekhalot literature in which adepts ascend through heavenly palaces to behold the enthroned Glory. Scholem treated Merkabah mysticism as the wellspring of the whole Kabbalistic tradition. Merian's engraving, from a Bible-illustration cycle, gave the vision its enduring early-modern visual form.

## For artists & game designers
A blueprint for a divine 'boss-encounter': spinning eyed wheels, a four-faced guardian, and an unapproachable throne of light.

citations.text: Gershom Scholem, *Jewish Gnosticism, Merkabah Mysticism, and Talmudic Tradition* (1960).

title: Mizrah papercut (Israel Dov Rosenbaum)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Israel Dov Rosenbaum
summary:
An elaborate *Mizrah* papercut by Israel Dov Rosenbaum, marking the eastern wall toward which Jews turn in prayer.

## Iconography
The symmetrical cut-paper panel centres the word *Mizrah* ('east') amid a dense field of [[motif:hebrew letters|Hebrew]] verses, divine names, columns, crowns, and flanking lions or birds, frequently incorporating a [[motif:menorah|menorah]] and protective formulae. The intricate openwork doubles as both orientation marker and blessing for the home.

## Significance
The *Mizrah*, like the related *Shiviti*, situates the worshipper toward Jerusalem and gathers protective verses and holy names into a devotional object hung on the eastern wall. The papercut (*reyzele*) tradition flourished in 18th–19th-century Eastern European Jewish folk art, blending kabbalistic name-magic with decorative craft. Such works are studied in the scholarship on Jewish folk and ritual art.

## For artists & game designers
A decorative 'directional ward' — an intricate wall-piece that both signals orientation and confers a passive blessing on its room.

citations.text: Studies in Jewish papercut and folk art (e.g. Joseph & Yehudit Shadur, *Traditional Jewish Papercuts*, 2002).

title: Frontispiece of *Portae Lucis* (Gates of Light)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Joseph Gikatilla; Latin trans. Paulus Ricius; woodcut after Leonhard Beck
summary:
The woodcut frontispiece of *Portae Lucis* (1516), Paulus Ricius's Latin translation of Joseph Gikatilla's *Sha'arei Orah* — the image that fixed the now-canonical 'Tree of Life' arrangement of the sefirot in the European imagination.

## Iconography
A bearded sage holds a tree formed of ten labelled spheres — the [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] — linked by paths and crowned by *Keter*. Each sphere carries a Hebrew divine name and attribute, descending from the hidden crown through wisdom and understanding to the kingdom (*Malkhut*) at the base. The figure presents the diagram as a map of the godhead's self-revelation.

## Significance
Gikatilla's *Sha'arei Orah* (late 13th c.) is the great systematic treatise on the divine names attached to each sefirah. Ricius's 1516 Latin version made it a foundational text of Christian Kabbalah, and this woodcut — not any medieval Jewish source — is the direct ancestor of the schematic Tree of Life used by every later occultist. Gershom Scholem and Moshe Idel are the standard guides to the underlying Kabbalah.

## For artists & game designers
The master 'skill-tree' diagram: ten labelled nodes joined by paths, instantly legible as a system of progressive emanation or attribute-unlocks.

citations.text: Joseph Gikatilla, *Sha'arei Orah*; Latin *Portae Lucis*, trans. Paulus Ricius (Augsburg, 1516).
Gershom Scholem, *Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism* (1941).

title: *Segulot* (charms) from the *Sefer Raziel*
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Anonymous (Sefer Raziel tradition)
summary:
A page of *segulot* — practical charms and remedies — from the *Sefer Raziel HaMalakh*, showing the everyday magic side of the Kabbalistic tradition.

## Iconography
The leaf combines Hebrew instructions with small magical figures: grids of letters, name-squares, and seals prescribed as [[motif:segulot|segulot]] for concrete ends — healing, protection in childbirth, success, safety in travel. Each charm pairs a holy name or letter-arrangement with a directive for how and when to write and use it.

## Significance
*Segulot* (literally 'remedies' or 'efficacious things') form the operative core of practical Kabbalah (*kabbalah ma'asit*), where the manipulation of divine names is held to act directly on the world. The *Sefer Raziel* was the most widely owned handbook of such charms in Ashkenazi and Sephardi homes. Trachtenberg and, more recently, Gideon Bohak study this tradition of Jewish name-magic.

## For artists & game designers
A recipe book of micro-charms: short letter-figures each tied to a single buff — a compact, lore-grounded consumables system.

citations.text: Gideon Bohak, *Ancient Jewish Magic: A History* (2008).

title: The Sefirot (Paulus Ricius diagram)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Paulus Ricius (Paulus Israelita)
summary:
A diagram of the ten sefirot associated with Paulus Ricius, the converted Jewish physician and Hebraist whose Latin translations carried Kabbalah into Christian learning.

## Iconography
The ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] are set out as a branching scheme of named spheres, each glossed with its Hebrew title and divine attribute, joined by connective paths that trace the flow of emanation from the infinite down into the created world.

## Significance
Ricius (d. 1541), physician to Emperor Maximilian I, was among the most important transmitters of Kabbalah to Christian Europe; his *Portae Lucis* and *De arte cabalistica* dialogues gave Reuchlin, Agrippa, and later Christian Hebraists their working vocabulary of the sefirot. Diagrams in his orbit standardized the ordered list of emanations later absorbed into Renaissance occult philosophy. (Attribution and exact source edition should be confirmed against the Commons file page.)

## For artists & game designers
A clean alternate Tree layout — useful when you want the ten-node emanation system in a more austere, scholarly register.

title: Magical seals from the *Sefer Raziel HaMalakh* (1701)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Anonymous (attributed to the angel Raziel)
summary:
A page of magical seals and sigils from the 1701 Amsterdam printing of the *Sefer Raziel HaMalakh* (Book of Raziel the Angel), the great compendium of practical Kabbalah.

## Iconography
The page sets out rows of [[motif:magic seals|seals]] — circles, grids, and looped *characteres* filled with Hebrew divine and angelic names — each keyed to a specific protective or curative purpose: guarding the home, easing childbirth, warding off the evil eye. They were meant to be copied onto parchment and worn or hung as amulets.

## Significance
Attributed to the angel Raziel who supposedly gave the book to Adam, the *Sefer Raziel* gathers older Hekhalot and magical material; its 1701 Amsterdam printing made it a hugely popular protective object — owning the book itself was believed to ward off fire and harm. It is the principal Hebrew source behind the Latin *Liber Razielis*. Joshua Trachtenberg's *Jewish Magic and Superstition* is the classic study.

## For artists & game designers
A sheet of distinct, purpose-tagged seals — each one a craftable amulet whose engraved glyph determines its protective effect.

title: Circle diagram of the *Sefer Yetzirah*
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Monozigote (modern diagram after the Sefer Yetzirah)
summary:
A circular diagram illustrating the letter-cosmology of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Book of Formation), the earliest and most enigmatic text of Jewish speculative mysticism.

## Iconography
The twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet are arranged around a circle, divided into the three 'mother' letters (Aleph, Mem, Shin), the seven 'double' letters, and the twelve 'simple' letters, mapping the alphabet onto the elements, planets, and zodiac. The figure shows creation as a combinatorial act of [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]].

## Significance
The *Sefer Yetzirah* (composed sometime between late antiquity and the early Middle Ages) teaches that God created the world through the ten *sefirot* and the twenty-two letters — the seed of all later Kabbalistic linguistic mysticism and of the Golem legend, in which letters animate matter. Scholem and, more recently, Tzahi Weiss study its text and reception. (This image is a modern explanatory diagram, not a historical manuscript page.)

## For artists & game designers
A combinatorial 'alphabet of creation' — letters as elemental building-blocks a player arranges to formulate spells or living constructs.

citations.text: *Sefer Yetzirah*; trans. and study by Aryeh Kaplan (1990).

title: Table of the Sephiroth and *Ain Soph* the Limitless One
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Anonymous (occult-revival compiler)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A 19th-century table laying out the sefirot and the unmanifest *Ain Soph* (the Limitless), the kind of correspondence-chart that defined occult-revival Kabbalah.

## Iconography
The sheet tabulates the ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] beneath *Ain Soph*, the boundless source above all emanation, listing for each sphere its Hebrew name, divine name, archangel, planetary or elemental correspondence, and attribute — a full grid of associations rather than a pictorial Tree.

## Significance
Such tables crystallize the synthesis worked by the 19th-century occult revival, which drew the historical Kabbalah of the *Zohar* and Luria through Knorr von Rosenroth and recast it as a system of universal correspondences. The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn made these grids the backbone of their initiatory grade system and of modern ceremonial magic.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made correspondence table — every node already keyed to a name, planet, and colour, perfect for a tightly-linked magic system.

citations.text: Israel Regardie, *The Golden Dawn* (1937–40), on the sephirotic correspondences.

title: Shiviti plate (Moshe Ganbash)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Moshe Ganbash
summary:
An ornate *Shiviti* plate by Moshe Ganbash, the devotional-magical placard set before the prayer leader and hung in synagogues and homes.

## Iconography
The plate is built around the verse *Shiviti Adonai le-negdi tamid* ('I have set the Lord always before me,' Ps. 16:8) and a great [[motif:menorah|menorah]] whose seven branches are formed from the words of Psalm 67. Around and within it cluster the [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]], divine names, and protective verses, often in fine Hebrew micrography.

## Significance
The *Shiviti*-menorah composition fuses contemplative focus with apotropaic power: gazing on the Name and the psalm-menorah was a meditative practice, while the accumulated holy names made the plate a protective amulet for the space it adorned. The form flourished in 18th–19th-century Sephardi and Ottoman Jewish communities. Such plates are studied within the literature on Jewish ritual art and *kavvanah*.

## For artists & game designers
A 'focus altar' object: a text-and-light icon that doubles as both a meditation point and a ward protecting the room.

citations.text: Psalm 67 and Psalm 16:8, the textual core of the Shiviti-menorah.

title: The Tetragrammaton (Sephardi script)
work: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: Anonymous
summary:
The Tetragrammaton — the four-letter ineffable Name of God, YHWH — rendered in a Sephardi Hebrew hand.

## Iconography
The four [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] *Yod–He–Vav–He* are set out in formal script. As the *Shem ha-Meforash*, the Name was never pronounced aloud, substituted in reading by *Adonai*; its written form alone carried supreme sanctity and power.

## Significance
The [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]] stands at the centre of Jewish and Kabbalistic name-mysticism: its letters were permuted, vocalized, and meditated upon (*tzeruf*) as the very engine of creation, and its derivatives — the 42-letter and 72-letter Names — underlie much of practical Kabbalah and the wider Western magical tradition that borrowed the Name as a word of power. Scholem and Moshe Idel examine the speculative mysticism of the Name.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'true name' token — a single unspeakable word that anchors an entire name-based magic system.

title: The Sephirotic Tree of Life
work: Kabbalah — The Tree of Life
creator: Kabbalistic tradition
summary:
The **Tree of Life** (*Etz Chaim*) — the central diagram of Kabbalah, mapping the ten **Sephirot**, the divine emanations through which the hidden Godhead (*Ein Sof*) brings the cosmos into being.

## Iconography
Ten spheres in three columns (the pillars of Mercy, Severity, and the Mean), joined by twenty-two paths (one per Hebrew letter). From Keter (Crown) to Malkhut (Kingdom) the figure charts the descent of divinity into matter — and the ladder of return.

## Significance
The Tree is the master-key of [[tradition:kabbalah]]: cosmology, psychology, and map of ascent at once. Through Pico and Reuchlin it entered Renaissance **Christian Cabala**, and later the Golden Dawn, where each sphere was assigned colours, gods, and tarot trumps. *(The first printed Christian Tree appeared in the* Portae Lucis, *1516.)*

## For artists & game designers
The ur-'skill tree' — ten nodes, three pillars, twenty-two paths. A natural magic-progression system or ritual diagram.

[PLACEHOLDER: confirm the exact edition/source of this Tree diagram against the Commons file record.]

title: Frontispiece to the Arca Noë (Noah's Ark)
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The frontispiece of Kircher's *Arca Noë* (1675), his learned reconstruction of Noah's Ark — its dimensions, cargo, and engineering — as a problem in natural history.

## Iconography
The Ark rides the floodwaters beneath a rainbow of covenant, with the procession of animals, the dove, and emblems of the deluge; the title is borne aloft by allegorical figures of providence and the rescued creation.

## Significance
Kircher treated the Ark as a real vessel to be reverse-engineered: he calculated how Noah housed and fed every species, arranged the stalls deck by deck, and used the project to grapple with the new flood of animals reported from the Americas. It belongs to the early-modern effort to reconcile [[motif:natural history|natural history]] with sacred chronology, and pairs directly with his *Turris Babel*.

## For artists & game designers
A blueprint-minded 'great vessel' image — the Ark as an engineered ship full of compartments and creatures, ideal reference for an arcology-on-water or floating-bestiary setting.

citations.text: Natália Lozada Mendieta & others; cf. Findlen (ed.), *Athanasius Kircher* (2004).

title: Frontispiece to the Ars Magna Lucis et Umbrae (The Great Art of Light and Shadow)
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Anonymous engraver after Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The allegorical title plate of Kircher's *Ars Magna Lucis et Umbrae* (1646), his sprawling treatise on optics, light, shadow, and the metaphysics of illumination.

## Iconography
Light descends from a radiant divine source at the top, distributed by allegorical figures bearing mirrors and emblems of sacred and natural light, while shadow gathers below. Beams, reflections, and personifications of the powers of light fill the composition.

## Significance
Kircher's optics treatise ranged from sundials and burning mirrors to the [[motif:optics|magic lantern]] and the projection of images, all framed by a Neoplatonic theology in which physical light is the visible trace of divine light. It is a key Baroque text linking natural magic, scientific optics, and Hermetic metaphysics — the book in which Kircher described early image-projection devices.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'light vs. shadow' allegory plate — a directly usable emblem for any setting that personifies illumination and darkness as cosmic forces.

citations.text: Daniel Stolzenberg (ed.), *The Great Art of Knowing: The Baroque Encyclopedia of Athanasius Kircher* (2001).

title: The Horoscopium Catholicum (Universal Sundial), Ars Magna Lucis et Umbrae
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The *Horoscopium Catholicum Societatis Iesu* — Kircher's 'universal sundial,' an emblematic olive tree whose branches tell the hour simultaneously across all the houses of the Jesuit order worldwide.

## Iconography
A spreading tree rises at the center, each of its branches inscribed with the name of a Jesuit province; the tree casts a shadow that functions as a [[motif:gnomon|gnomon]], the dial-lines and place-names converting the single shadow into the local time of provinces from Rome to China.

## Significance
From the *Ars Magna Lucis et Umbrae*, this plate fuses gnomonics (the science of sundials) with Jesuit self-image: the Society of Jesus as a single tree casting one shadow over the whole globe. It exemplifies Kircher's habit of turning a scientific instrument into a theological emblem.

## For artists & game designers
An elegant 'world clock' conceit — one shadow telling many times at once. Reuse it as a map-spanning device that synchronizes distant locations through a single celestial pointer.

citations.text: John Edward Fletcher, *A Study of the Life and Works of Athanasius Kircher* (2011).

title: The Hanging Gardens / City of Babylon, Turris Babel
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
A reconstruction of ancient Babylon and its monuments from Kircher's *Turris Babel*, his attempt to recover the lost grandeur of the first great city.

## Iconography
The walled metropolis of Babylon is laid out in antiquarian detail — the terraced hanging gardens, monumental gates, and towering structures rendered as a Baroque vision of Mesopotamian splendour, drawn from classical descriptions rather than archaeology.

## Significance
Kircher reconstructed Babylon, its [[motif:hanging gardens|hanging gardens]], and the surrounding empire as the cradle of the post-Babel nations and of idolatry. Lacking real archaeological data (Mesopotamia would not be excavated for another two centuries), he synthesized Herodotus, scripture, and his own conjecture into an influential and wholly imaginative cityscape.

## For artists & game designers
A richly imagined ancient megacity — terraces, gardens, and ziggurats — perfect as a reference plate for a fallen Bronze-Age capital.

citations.text: Daniel Stolzenberg, *Egyptian Oedipus: Athanasius Kircher and the Secrets of Antiquity* (2013).

title: The Tower of Babel (Frontispiece), Turris Babel sive Archontologia
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: after Athanasius Kircher (Rijksmuseum impression)
summary:
The title plate of Kircher's *Turris Babel* (1679), his scholarly reconstruction of the Tower of Babel and the post-diluvian dispersal of nations and languages.

## Iconography
The great tower rises in receding storeys toward heaven amid the bustle of its construction, while above, divine intervention scatters the builders — the moment of the confusion of tongues — and below, the founder Nimrod and the genealogy of nations are set out.

## Significance
In *Turris Babel* Kircher calculated that a tower reaching the moon was physically impossible, using the engineering question as a frame for his theories on the origin of languages, idolatry, and the spread of peoples after the Flood. It connects to his Egyptological work, where he traced [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphs]] and pagan religion back to a single primordial wisdom corrupted at Babel.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive Baroque Babel: a spiralling mega-structure mid-construction under divine wrath — a complete set-piece for any 'doomed tower' or 'fractured-language' scenario.

citations.text: Daniel Stolzenberg, *Egyptian Oedipus: Athanasius Kircher and the Secrets of Antiquity* (2013).

title: Frontispiece to China Monumentis Illustrata (China Illustrated)
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The engraved frontispiece of Kircher's *China Monumentis Illustrata* (1667), Europe's most influential early compendium on the marvels, religions, and antiquities of China.

## Iconography
The Jesuit missionaries Matteo Ricci and Adam Schall present a map of China beneath the radiant name of God; emblems of the mission and of Chinese learning frame the scene, asserting the Society of Jesus's role as interpreter of the East.

## Significance
*China Illustrata* shaped the European image of China for generations, mixing reliable Jesuit reportage with Kircher's own theories — including his claim that Chinese characters and religion descended from Egyptian [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphs]] and the dispersal after Babel. It is a foundational and famously distorting text of early Sinology and of the Hermetic 'universal wisdom' thesis.

## For artists & game designers
A model 'explorer's title page': two scholars unrolling a map of a distant empire under divine sanction — instantly legible as the opening of a voyage to the unknown East.

citations.text: Florence C. Hsia, 'Athanasius Kircher's China Illustrata: An Apologia Pro Vita Sua,' in Findlen (ed.), *Athanasius Kircher* (2004).

title: The Nestorian Stele of Xi'an, China Illustrata
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher (after the Xi'an monument)
summary:
Kircher's reproduction of the Nestorian Stele of Xi'an — the Tang-dynasty monument recording the arrival of the Church of the East in China — with its Chinese and Syriac text.

## Iconography
The tall stele is shown with its surmounting cross and dense columns of Chinese characters, flanked by lines of Estrangela Syriac script; Kircher's plate transcribes and annotates the inscription for European readers.

## Significance
The stele (erected 781 CE, rediscovered c. 1625) proved that Christianity had reached China nearly a millennium before the Jesuits. Kircher published the first full European transcription and Syriac decipherment in *China Illustrata*, making the monument a centerpiece of debates over the antiquity of the [[motif:church of the east|Church of the East]] and the global reach of the faith.

## For artists & game designers
A real artifact of 'lost early Christianity in the East' — a bilingual stone whose decipherment can anchor a historical-mystery plot about a forgotten church.

citations.text: Michael Keevak, *The Story of a Stele: China's Nestorian Monument and Its Reception in the West, 1625–1916* (2008).

title: Map of the Routes to Cathay (Tabula Geodoborica), China Illustrata
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
rights: CC BY 2.0 (Biblioteca de la Universidad de Sevilla). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Kircher's *Tabula Geodoborica* — a map charting the overland routes by which various travellers reached Cathay (China), compiled for *China Illustrata*.

## Iconography
A broad map of Central Asia and the approaches to China traces the itineraries of Jesuit and earlier travellers across mountains and deserts, with place-names, routes, and cartographic ornament marking the long road to Cathay.

## Significance
Kircher assembled this map from the reports of missionaries such as Bento de Góis and Johann Grueber to demonstrate that Cathay and China were one land — and to display the Jesuit network as a global instrument of knowledge. It belongs to the same encyclopedic impulse as his [[motif:hydrophylacia|hydrographic maps]]: turning correspondence into cartography.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic overland-route map for a perilous journey east — usable as a campaign map of caravan trails through mountains and deserts.

citations.text: Florence C. Hsia, 'Athanasius Kircher's China Illustrata,' in Findlen (ed.), *Athanasius Kircher* (2004).

title: Portrait of Athanasius Kircher, Aged 53
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: A. Frölich (engraver)
motifs: the last man who knew everything
summary:
An engraved portrait of Athanasius Kircher, the Jesuit polymath, inscribed as 'aged 53' — the man behind this entire encyclopedic universe.

## Iconography
Kircher is shown in three-quarter view in Jesuit habit, set within an oval frame with a Latin inscription naming him *P. Athanasius Kircherus Fuldensis, è Societate Jesu*, in the formal convention of a learned author's portrait.

## Significance
Born in Geisa near Fulda in 1602, Kircher became the most famous scholar of his age — Egyptologist, geologist, musicologist, Sinologist, and inventor — earning the later epithet 'the last man who knew everything.' His vast, often credulous output made him both celebrated and, after the rise of stricter scientific method, a byword for over-ambitious erudition. This likeness fixes the face that presided over the [[motif:musaeum kircherianum|Roman College museum]].

## For artists & game designers
A ready 'archetypal polymath' portrait — the learned friar in an oval frame, instantly castable as the author-figure or quest-giver of an esoteric library.

citations.text: Paula Findlen (ed.), *Athanasius Kircher: The Last Man Who Knew Everything* (2004).

title: Title Page to the Mundus Subterraneus, Volume Two (1664)
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: after Athanasius Kircher (Rijksmuseum impression)
summary:
The allegorical engraved title page to the second volume of Kircher's *Mundus Subterraneus*, the great encyclopedia of the underground world.

## Iconography
A Baroque architectural frame is animated with personifications of the natural sciences, glimpses of mountains opened to reveal their fiery and watery interiors, and the divine light descending from above to order the subterranean cosmos. The book's title is set within a draped cartouche.

## Significance
Kircher's frontispieces are themselves arguments: they stage the claim that the hidden machinery of the earth ([[motif:geocosmos|the geocosmos]]) is governed by divine providence and legible to the Jesuit natural philosopher. The *Mundus Subterraneus* (1664–65) was Kircher's most influential scientific work, shaping early geology, mineralogy, and the study of volcanoes for a century.

## For artists & game designers
A textbook Baroque title-card: ornate frame, allegorical figures, and a peek at the world-machine behind the curtain — copy the layout for any 'forbidden encyclopedia' cover.

citations.text: Paula Findlen (ed.), *Athanasius Kircher: The Last Man Who Knew Everything* (2004).

title: Frontispiece to Latium (The Antiquities of Latium)
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The title plate of Kircher's *Latium* (1671), his antiquarian survey of the region of Rome — its ancient sites, ruins, and topography.

## Iconography
An allegorical figure of Latium presides over a landscape strewn with classical ruins, aqueducts, and monuments; river-gods, antiquities, and emblems of the region's mythic past frame the title.

## Significance
*Latium* combined genuine topographical and antiquarian observation with Kircher's taste for myth, tracing the area's history back to a Saturnian golden age. It is part of his broader effort to recover the sacred and esoteric past inscribed in the monuments of Italy, a counterpart to his Egyptological and [[motif:hieroglyphs|hieroglyphic]] researches closer to home.

## For artists & game designers
A model 'allegory of a haunted landscape' — a personified land surrounded by its own ruins, useful as cover art for a region defined by its lost antiquity.

citations.text: Harry B. Evans, *Exploring the Kingdom of Saturn: Kircher's Latium and Its Legacy* (2012).

title: Frontispiece to the Magnes, sive de Arte Magnetica (The Magnetic Art)
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The title plate of Kircher's *Magnes, sive de Arte Magnetica* (1641), in which magnetism becomes the master-metaphor for the force that binds the whole universe.

## Iconography
A chain of being descends from the divine source above, linking the spheres, the elements, and earthly creatures as if by magnetic attraction; emblems of the loadstone and its powers frame the central title.

## Significance
Kircher generalized the magnet into a universal principle: a *vis magnetica* of sympathy and attraction running through cosmos, society, and soul, ultimately rooted in divine love drawing all things to itself. The treatise blends genuine experiment on loadstones with Hermetic doctrines of [[motif:sympathy|sympathy and antipathy]], and was a key channel for the magnetic philosophy of the seventeenth century.

## For artists & game designers
A strong emblem for a world held together by an unseen attractive force — the 'great chain' rendered as literal magnetism, useful for any cosmology of binding sympathies.

citations.text: Martha Baldwin, 'Magnetism and the Anti-Copernican Polemic,' *Journal for the History of Astronomy* (1985).

title: A Magnetic Device, Magnes sive de Arte Magnetica
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
An engraved demonstration plate from Kircher's *Magnes*, illustrating one of the magnetic apparatuses he used to dramatize the hidden power of the loadstone.

## Iconography
A carefully drawn instrument combines magnetized elements with mechanical parts — figures, dials, or floating pointers driven by concealed magnets, presented in the diagrammatic style of a working demonstration.

## Significance
Kircher delighted in 'magnetic marvels': clocks, oracular statues, and floating figures secretly moved by loadstones, which he used both to entertain visitors to the [[motif:musaeum kircherianum|Roman College museum]] and to argue for a magnetic order of nature. Such devices blur the line between conjuring trick, scientific demonstration, and theological emblem — natural magic in the precise period sense.

## For artists & game designers
Period blueprints for 'automated wonders' — statues and clocks that move by invisible attraction. Excellent reference for a wizard's hall of self-moving curiosities.

citations.text: Paula Findlen (ed.), *Athanasius Kircher: The Last Man Who Knew Everything* (2004).

title: A Winged Dragon, Mundus Subterraneus
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
One of the dragons Kircher catalogued among the genuine inhabitants of the underground world, depicted as a clawed, bat-winged serpent at rest on the ground.

## Iconography
A long-bodied dragon with membranous wings, a crested head, and taloned forelimbs lies coiled across the engraving's foreground, rendered with the sober precision of a natural-history specimen rather than a heraldic flourish.

## Significance
Kircher devoted a serious section of the *Mundus Subterraneus* to *dracones*, treating reports of dragons in Alpine caves and Swiss mountains as credible natural history alongside fossils and minerals. The plates sit at the hinge between Renaissance bestiary and emerging empirical science — the same author who measured Vesuvius also gravely diagrammed the anatomy of dragons.

## For artists & game designers
A period-authentic 'naturalist's dragon': drawn like a real animal, not a mythic monster — ideal for a cryptozoology codex or a bestiary that insists its dragons are simply rare fauna.

citations.text: Daniel Stolzenberg, *The Great Art of Knowing: The Baroque Encyclopedia of Athanasius Kircher* (2001).

title: The Underground Water-Reservoirs (Hydrophylacium Asiae Majoris), Mundus Subterraneus
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
Kircher's map of the *hydrophylacia* of greater Asia — the hidden water-cisterns he imagined locked inside mountains, the source of every river and spring.

## Iconography
A geographical map of Asia is overlaid with great mountain ranges drawn in profile, each cut open to reveal a bulging subterranean lake. From these reservoirs rivers pour out across the continent, while the surrounding ocean feeds the system through underground channels.

## Significance
The watery counterpart to Kircher's fire-chambers: in his living [[motif:geocosmos|geocosmos]], a vast circulatory system drew seawater inland, desalinated it in mountain cisterns, and discharged it as rivers — an early, if fanciful, attempt to explain the hydrological cycle and the perennial flow of springs. Kircher gathered reports from Jesuit missionaries worldwide to populate such maps.

## For artists & game designers
A gorgeous cartographic device: a real-looking continent map whose mountains hide secret inland seas — perfect for a quest that traces a river back to an impossible source.

citations.text: Paula Findlen (ed.), *Athanasius Kircher: The Last Man Who Knew Everything* (2004).

title: The Subterranean Fire-Reservoirs (Systema Ideale Pyrophylaciorum), Mundus Subterraneus
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
Kircher's diagram of the *pyrophylacia* — the great fire-chambers he believed honeycombed the body of the earth, all fed from a central blaze at the planet's core.

## Iconography
A spherical earth is shown in cutaway, its interior threaded with branching ducts and rounded reservoirs of flame. A vast central fire occupies the core, from which smaller fire-pockets radiate outward and vent at the surface as volcanoes spitting smoke and flame across the encircling landscape.

## Significance
In the *Mundus Subterraneus* (1665) Kircher proposed a living [[motif:geocosmos|geocosmos]] whose circulating fire and water mirrored the human body's humors. The central fire connected to surface volcanoes — a theory he claimed to confirm by being lowered into the smoking crater of Vesuvius in 1638. Though pre-Newtonian and wrong in detail, it is a founding image of geology as a systematic science of the earth's interior.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made 'world's molten heart' schematic: a planet veined with fire-rivers and magma vaults that any subterranean dungeon or volcano-god setting can borrow wholesale.

citations.text: John Edward Fletcher, *A Study of the Life and Works of Athanasius Kircher* (2011).

title: The Musaeum Kircherianum (Kircher's Museum at the Roman College)
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Filippo Buonanni (after the Kircher collection)
summary:
A view of the *Musaeum Kircherianum*, the celebrated cabinet of curiosities Kircher assembled at the Collegio Romano, from Filippo Buonanni's 1709 catalogue.

## Iconography
A long gallery is crowded floor-to-ceiling with marvels: obelisks and Egyptian antiquities, hanging instruments, stuffed animals, automata, mirrors, and machines, with visitors dwarfed by the encyclopedic accumulation.

## Significance
The museum was the physical embodiment of Kircher's project — to gather all of creation into one room as a [[motif:wunderkammer|theatre of the world]]. It became a compulsory stop on the Grand Tour and a model for the encyclopedic museum, blending genuine antiquities with magnetic tricks and reconstructed wonders. Buonanni curated and catalogued it after Kircher's death.

## For artists & game designers
The ur-'museum of impossible objects': a single room that contains obelisks, automata, and beasts. Use the engraving directly as a level map for a collector-mage's hall.

citations.text: Paula Findlen, 'Scientific Spectacle in Baroque Rome: Athanasius Kircher and the Roman College Museum,' *Roma Moderna e Contemporanea* (1995).

title: Frontispiece to the Musurgia Universalis (The Harmony of the World)
work: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: Anonymous engraver after Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The allegorical frontispiece of Kircher's *Musurgia Universalis* (1650), the encyclopedia in which all of music is read as a reflection of the harmony binding the cosmos.

## Iconography
The heavens open to reveal a celestial choir and the Pythagorean cosmos as a great stringed instrument; personifications of harmony, allegories of the senses, and the music of nature surround a central scene where divine order is tuned like an instrument. Apollo and emblems of the muses appear among the registers.

## Significance
Kircher's *Musurgia* presents the universe as music: the [[motif:harmony of the spheres|harmonia mundi]] in which God composed creation as a fugue, and the world is held together by ratio and consonance. The work contained early experiments in mechanical composition and influential discussions of acoustics, the 'music of the spheres,' and even animal sound.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'cosmos as instrument' image — directly usable for any setting where reality is tuned, played, or held together by sacred music.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin, *Athanasius Kircher's Theatre of the World* (2009).

title: Shiʿi Calligraphy of ʿAlī as the Tiger of God
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous (modern redrawing)
summary:
A black-and-white zoomorphic calligram forming a striding big cat (lion/tiger) out of an Arabic invocation of **ʿAlī**.

## Iconography
Interlaced cursive letters compose the head, flank and legs of the animal; the negative spaces between strokes give the impression of dappled fur. The text is a devotional formula naming ʿAlī, the 'Lion/Tiger of God' (*Ḥaydar*).

## Significance
The piece restates the lettrist principle that a holy name can be re-bodied as its emblematic creature. As a clean line drawing it shows the underlying scaffolding of such calligrams better than gilded album leaves, making the letter-to-limb correspondences legible. It belongs to the broad family of ʿAlī-as-lion talismans tied to the *Nād-i ʿAlī* prayer.

## For artists & game designers
Useful reference sheet: the bare linework reveals how to read a creature back into its constituent glyphs.

title: Panjtan — The Five Holy Ones in Calligraphy
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Kānūn-i Hunar-i Shīʿī (Shiʿi art workshop)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Kānūn-i Hunar-i Shīʿī). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A calligraphic composition of the **Panjtan-i Āl-i ʿAbā** — the Five Holy Ones: Muhammad, ʿAlī, Fāṭima, Ḥasan and Ḥusayn — woven into a single symmetrical design.

## Iconography
The five sacred names are arranged as an interlaced star or rosette, often in mirror (muthanna) symmetry so the names reflect one another around a central axis. No faces appear; the persons are present purely as their names.

## Significance
In Shiʿi devotion the Panjtan are the locus of divine blessing and protection, and their five names are deployed together as a powerful talismanic formula — inscribed on amulets, banners and the hand-shaped *panja*. As a sacred-name [[motif:mirror calligraphy|mirror-calligram]], the piece exemplifies the lettrist principle that arranging holy names in ordered, symmetrical form concentrates their power.

## For artists & game designers
A five-fold 'patron set' — a single emblem binding five named protectors into one ward.

title: Mirror Image of 'ʿAlī walī Allāh'
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Ibrāhīm Maḥmūd
summary:
A **muthannā** (mirror) calligraphic panel of the Shiʿi testimony *ʿAlī walī Allāh* ('ʿAlī is the friend/vicegerent of God'), the phrase doubled in perfect reflection.

## Iconography
The inscription is written once and then mirrored across a vertical axis, so the two halves meet like a butterfly's wings; the symmetry makes a single phrase read as a balanced ornamental whole.

## Significance
Mirror calligraphy (*muthannā* or *aynalı*) was prized exactly because the doubling intensifies a sacred phrase into a self-contained, talismanic image. Applied to *ʿAlī walī Allāh* — the distinctive Shiʿi addition to the call to prayer — it produces a devotional sign carrying both confessional identity and protective power, a paradigm case of the lettrist transformation of a [[motif:sacred names|holy phrase]] into diagram.

## For artists & game designers
The mirror technique is a free 'sigil-doubler': reflect any inscription to make it read as an emblem.

title: The Name of God (Allāh)
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous (vector, Wikimedia 'Mr100percent')
summary:
The single word **Allāh** — the Greatest Name (*al-ism al-aʿẓam*) — rendered in bold green calligraphy.

## Iconography
The name is built from the doubled *lām* rising as twin verticals, the looping *hāʾ* below, the *alif* and the *shadda* above — a tight, instantly recognisable monogram of four written elements.

## Significance
For the lettrists the Name *Allāh* is the seed of all the others and the supreme object of meditation; its letters (alif-lām-lām-hā) were analysed for their successive disappearance down to the bare *hū* ('He'), and its abjad value computed as a key to the divine. As the [[motif:names of god|Greatest Name]], it is held to be the most powerful of all letter-talismans, the centre around which the ninety-nine Names and every magic square are organised.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'true name' token — the single glyph at the centre of every ward and rosette in the set.

title: The Ninety-Nine Beautiful Names of God
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A golden calligraphy board displaying **al-asmāʾ al-ḥusnā**, the ninety-nine Most Beautiful Names of God, each set in its own cartouche.

## Iconography
The Names — al-Raḥmān, al-Raḥīm, al-Malik, and so on — radiate around a central medallion (typically the Name *Allāh*), the whole arranged as an ordered grid or rosette of letter-talismans.

## Significance
The ninety-nine [[motif:names of god|Names]] are the heart of lettrist devotion and magic: each Name has an abjad value, a planetary and elemental correspondence, and a prescribed number of repetitions (*dhikr*) thought to release its specific power. Al-Būnī's *Shams al-maʿārif* tabulates their squares and seals. Displayed together they form a complete map of the divine attributes — a master-diagram of sacred letters.

## For artists & game designers
A 99-entry 'attribute system': each Name a distinct power with its own number, sound and correspondence.

title: Basmala (Naskh)
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous (Saddam19, Wikimedia)
summary:
A clean single-line rendering of the **basmala** in a clear naskh hand.

## Iconography
The phrase *bismillāh ar-raḥmān ar-raḥīm* is set out plainly, letter by letter, so the individual graphemes — and thus their abjad values — are easy to count and contemplate.

## Significance
Where display calligraphy dramatises the formula, a plain naskh basmala is the version the lettrist actually meditates on and computes: each letter weighed for its [[motif:abjad|abjad]] number, the totals sought in the divine Names. The legibility makes this a useful reference for the very alphabet on which the whole science of letters operates.

## For artists & game designers
A legible 'reference inscription' — the base text from which stylised and zoomorphic variants derive.

title: Bismillah in Calligraphy
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous (Laisurw2020, Wikimedia)
summary:
The **bismillah** — *bismillāh ar-raḥmān ar-raḥīm* — written as a single flowing line of display calligraphy.

## Iconography
The nineteen letters of the formula are drawn out in expansive thuluth, the elongated *sīn* of *bismi* sweeping under the whole phrase as a base-line that gathers the words into one gesture.

## Significance
The basmala opens every chapter of the Quran but one and every blessed act; its nineteen letters became a focus of lettrist numerology (the 'mystery of nineteen'), and the phrase itself is the most common subject of zoomorphic calligrams and talismanic panels. As the door-formula of speech and writing alike, it is the natural seed of the whole [[motif:sacred letters|science of letters]].

## For artists & game designers
The universal 'invocation header' — the phrase that opens every spell, document and journey.

title: Bismillah Roundel (Mirror Composition)
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous (vector, Wikimedia 'Frater5')
summary:
The **bismillah** composed into a circular seal-like medallion, the words folded to fill a roundel.

## Iconography
Black on white, the formula is arranged so its strokes radiate and interlock within a circle, producing a compact emblem rather than a running line — close to a stamp or seal.

## Significance
Compressing the basmala into a roundel is the move that turns a phrase into a [[motif:seal|seal]]: a self-contained sign that can be impressed, worn or hung as a unit of blessing and protection. The circular format aligns the sacred opening-formula with the talismanic logic of the magic-square amulet and the tughra, where holy text becomes a closed, portable device.

## For artists & game designers
A drop-in 'holy stamp' icon — the bismillah as a seal you can emboss on doors, scrolls and wax.

title: The Budūḥ Magic Square
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: after Zakariyā al-Qazwīnī
summary:
The **budūḥ** — the classic 3×3 magic square of Islamic letter-magic, here in a manuscript hand associated with the cosmographer al-Qazwīnī.

## Iconography
Nine cells hold the Arabic numerals 2-9-4 / 7-5-3 / 6-1-8 (or their letter equivalents), every row, column and diagonal summing to fifteen. The four corner letters bā-dāl-wāw-ḥā spell the talismanic word *BUDŪḤ*.

## Significance
The 3×3 square (the *wafq* of Saturn, the Chinese Lo Shu) entered Islamic magic as the [[motif:buduh|budūḥ]], invoked as a guardian word on amulets for safe travel, childbirth and protection — its mere name a charm. It is the foundational object where [[motif:abjad numerology|abjad numerology]] and the magic square meet, and it passed into Latin and Hebrew magic alike.

## For artists & game designers
The budūḥ is a portable, drawable ward: a 3×3 grid the player inscribes to seal a door or a journey.

citations.text: Edgar W. Lane / E. Doutté on the budūḥ; J. Sesiano, *Magic Squares in Islam* (2017).

title: Calligraphic Roundel of the Name of Aḥmad al-Būnī
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous (modern roundel)
summary:
A calligraphic medallion spelling the name and honorific of **al-Shaykh Aḥmad b. ʿAlī al-Būnī** (d. c. 1225), the foundational author of the Islamic science of letters.

## Iconography
White cursive script on black fills a double-ruled roundel: 'the Shaykh Aḥmad ibn ʿAlī al-Būnī, may God have mercy on him', composed as a balanced seal-like emblem.

## Significance
Al-Būnī is the towering figure of lettrist magic, credited with the *Shams al-maʿārif al-kubrā* ('Sun of Great Knowledge'), the encyclopaedia of the [[motif:names of god|divine Names]], magic squares, and the occult properties of the Arabic letters that stands behind centuries of Islamic and, via translation, European talismanic practice. Rendering his very name as a sealed roundel honours him in the medium he theorised.

## For artists & game designers
A named 'grimoire author' emblem — the sigil of a magus the player can quest to study under.

citations.text: Noah Gardiner, 'Forbidden Knowledge? Notes on the Production and Reception of the Works of al-Būnī' (2012).

title: The Buraq Worshipped by Two Princes
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous
repository: Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA)
summary:
A painting of the **Buraq**, the winged human-faced steed that carried the Prophet on the Night Journey (miʿrāj), here venerated by two kneeling princes; the verso carries calligraphy.

## Iconography
The Buraq, with a woman's face, peacock tail and equine body, is shown crowned and haloed. The pairing of image (recto) and sacred script (verso) is typical of the talismanic album leaf, where the holy beast and the holy text guard one another.

## Significance
The Buraq is itself a frequent subject of zoomorphic calligrams (the bismillah-as-Buraq), and the creature's body is treated, like the lion of ʿAlī, as a vessel for sacred text. This LACMA leaf shows the painted source-image from which the calligraphic Buraq abstracts. It belongs to the devotional, protective image-culture of [[motif:miʿraj|the Night Journey]].

## For artists & game designers
A fully designed mythic mount — human face, peacock tail, equine body, crowned — ready as a steed asset.

citations.text: Christiane Gruber, *The Praiseworthy One: The Prophet Muhammad in Islamic Texts and Images* (2018).

title: Nād-i ʿAlī Prayer Cut in the Shape of a Lion
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Mīr ʿAlī Heravī (attrib.)
summary:
An album page on which the **Nād-i ʿAlī** — a celebrated prayer of intercession to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib — is rendered as découpage (cut-paper) calligraphy in the form of a lion.

## Iconography
The entire lion, mounted on a contrasting ground, is built from the cut-out words of the prayer; the medium of paper-cutting turns the text into a solid silhouette. The lion again signals ʿAlī, the *Asad Allāh*.

## Significance
The *Nād-i ʿAlī* ("Call upon ʿAlī, the manifester of wonders") was prized as a protective and talismanic invocation across the Persianate world. Shaping it into the lion fuses the apotropaic power of the words with the iconic force of the image — a high-art instance of letter-mysticism in service of devotion. The leaf is associated with the great calligrapher Mīr ʿAlī Heravī.

## For artists & game designers
Découpage as game-asset: a talisman where the prayer text literally is the relic's silhouette.

title: Hilye-i Şerif (Calligraphic Description of the Prophet)
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous (Ottoman)
summary:
A **hilye** (Arabic *ḥilya*) — the formalised calligraphic 'portrait in words' describing the physical and moral qualities of the Prophet Muhammad.

## Iconography
The genre has a fixed architecture: a top panel (*başmakam*) with the basmala, a central medallion (*göbek*) holding the descriptive text, four corner roundels for the Rightly-Guided Caliphs, and a lower panel with a closing verse — all bordered like an icon. No face is depicted; the description *is* the likeness.

## Significance
The hilye answers the prohibition on figural images of the Prophet by substituting a verbal-visual diagram believed to carry blessing (*baraka*); to keep or gaze upon one was held to confer protection. As a sacred design made wholly of words and ordered space, it is a paradigm of lettrist devotion. The form was codified by the Ottoman master Hâfız Osman in the 17th century.

## For artists & game designers
A strict template UI: fixed panels and medallions you can re-skin as a 'relic-card' frame.

title: Hilye by Hâfız Osman
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Hâfız Osman
summary:
A **hilye** by **Hâfız Osman** (1642–1698), the Ottoman calligrapher who standardised the genre's classic layout, now in the Sadberk Hanım Museum.

## Iconography
The sheet follows the canonical scheme: basmala header, a circular central medallion containing the description of the Prophet, four caliphal roundels, and a framing border, executed in disciplined thuluth and naskh.

## Significance
Hâfız Osman's design fixed the hilye as a devotional and quasi-talismanic object reproduced for centuries; owning one was thought to bring blessing and protection. Its geometry — a sacred text organised into concentric and cornered fields — makes the page a [[motif:devotional diagram|devotional diagram]] as much as a piece of writing, the lettrist ideal of word-as-icon.

## For artists & game designers
The authoritative reference for the hilye frame: copy its proportions for an in-game holy-relic document.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

title: Calligraphic Composition (Levha) by Hasan Rıza Şumnu
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Hasan Rıza Şumnu
summary:
An Ottoman **levha** — a single-sheet calligraphic panel made to be framed and hung — signed by Hasan Rıza Şumnu and dated 1886–87.

## Iconography
A sacred phrase is laid out as a balanced symmetrical composition in bold thuluth, with the words interlocking to fill the field as a designed image rather than a line of running text.

## Significance
The levha brought monumental sacred calligraphy out of the mosque and into the home as a devotional and protective wall-image. A well-made levha of a Quranic verse or holy name was understood to carry blessing into the room it guarded — the everyday face of [[motif:sacred letters|letter-mysticism]], where a phrase becomes a talismanic object simply by being beautifully and deliberately composed.

## For artists & game designers
The 'framed inscription' as environmental prop — a hangable holy panel for interior set-dressing.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

title: Persian Wafq — a 6×6 Magic Square with Instructions
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A 16th-century Persian manuscript page giving a **wafq** (magic square) of order six, with the construction rules written out above and below the grid.

## Iconography
A 6×6 grid is filled with the numbers 1–36 so that every row and column shares a constant sum (111); the surrounding Persian text explains, cell by cell, how the figure is to be built — a working recipe, not just a finished talisman.

## Significance
The order-six square is the *wafq* of the Sun in the planetary series, and its construction was a prized branch of the lettrist mathematical sciences (*ʿilm al-awfāq*). Squares like this were engraved on amulets and astral talismans, their numbers tied through [[motif:abjad numerology|abjad]] to divine names and planetary powers. The inclusion of the method shows the practitioner's craft, descending from al-Būnī and the Brethren of Purity.

## For artists & game designers
A genuine in-world 'spell recipe': the grid plus the step-by-step text for building it.

citations.text: Jacques Sesiano, *Magic Squares in the Tenth Century* / *Les carrés magiques dans les pays islamiques* (2004).

title: Leaf from an Ottoman Calligraphy Diploma
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Muhammad Hilmi Afandi
summary:
An illuminated leaf from an Ottoman **icazet** (calligraphy licence/diploma) by Muhammad Hilmi Afandi, in the Walters Art Museum.

## Iconography
The gilt-framed page shows model lines of sacred text in alternating thuluth and naskh, with an illuminated header and floral panels — the showcase by which a master certified a pupil's mastery of the holy script.

## Significance
The icazet embodies the chain of transmission (*silsila*) by which the correct, blessed forms of the sacred letters passed master-to-pupil — a quasi-initiatic lineage that treats calligraphy as a spiritual discipline, not mere penmanship. This is the institutional backbone of the lettrist culture in which writing the [[motif:sacred letters|holy letters]] is itself an act of devotion and power.

## For artists & game designers
A 'mastery certificate' artifact — proof of a craft-lineage the player can earn or forge.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

title: The Mysterious Letters Ṭā-Hā (Surah 20)
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous (modern roundel)
summary:
A roundel displaying **Ṭā-Hā** (طه), the two disconnected 'mysterious letters' (*muqaṭṭaʿāt*) that open the 20th sura of the Quran.

## Iconography
Within a green medallion the word *sūra* and the number 20 frame the large isolated letters Ṭā and Hā, written without the surrounding text — exactly as they stand, enigmatic, at the head of the chapter.

## Significance
Twenty-nine Quranic suras begin with such [[motif:muqattaat|muqaṭṭaʿāt]] — single or grouped letters (Alif-Lām-Mīm, Yā-Sīn, Ṭā-Hā) whose meaning is undisclosed. For the lettrists they were the supreme proof that bare letters carry hidden power: commentators computed their abjad values, sought their secret names of God, and treated them as keys to the cosmos. They are the Quranic charter of the science of letters.

## For artists & game designers
The muqaṭṭaʿāt are canonical 'untranslatable runes' — sacred glyphs whose meaning is a deliberate mystery.

title: Square Kufic Bismillah Page by Ahmed Karahisari
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Ahmed Karahisari
summary:
A calligraphic page attributed to the great Ottoman master **Ahmed Karahisari** (d. 1556), combining a cursive **bismillah** with two geometric panels in **square (bannāʾī) Kufic**.

## Iconography
At centre a flowing bismillah; above and below, sacred phrases are folded into diamond grids of square Kufic, where each letter is squared off and woven into an interlocking maze that must be 'read' by tracing the path of the strokes.

## Significance
Square Kufic turns sacred text into pure geometry, the closest the Arabic script comes to a [[motif:magic square|magic square]]: the eye decodes a word from a lattice. Karahisari, founder of an influential Ottoman school, exploited this to make text legible as architecture and ornament. Such grid-letters were used on buildings and as talismanic medallions, sitting squarely within the lettrist treatment of writing as occult diagram.

## For artists & game designers
Square Kufic is a built-in 'glyph puzzle' mechanic — a word hidden in a grid the player must trace.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

title: Tughra of Sultan Süleyman the Magnificent
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Ottoman chancery (nişancı)
summary:
The illuminated **tughra** (sovereign monogram) of **Süleyman the Magnificent**, the calligraphic cipher that authenticated every Ottoman imperial decree.

## Iconography
The tughra weaves the sultan's name and titles into a fixed abstract form: a cluster of vertical staves (*tuğ*) on the right, three looping ovals (*beyze*) on the left, and a flourish sweeping outward, here filled with gold floral illumination.

## Significance
More than a signature, the tughra functioned as a quasi-magical sign of presence and authority: to bear it was to bear the sultan's word made visible, and its inimitable design protected documents like a [[motif:seal|seal]] or talisman against forgery. As a sacred-name-form abstracted into pure pattern, it sits at the meeting of calligraphy, heraldry and lettrist sign-magic.

## For artists & game designers
A ready 'royal seal' generator: a personal name compressed into an unforgeable emblem of power.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

title: Bismillah in the Shape of a Hoopoe
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
The **basmala** — *bismillāh ar-raḥmān ar-raḥīm*, 'In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful' — written so that its letters form a **hoopoe**.

## Iconography
A crested hoopoe, gilded and finely scrolled, is composed entirely of the words of the opening invocation: the crest, beak, wing and tail are each carried by a stroke of the phrase. Black calligraphy on a gold ground.

## Significance
The hoopoe (*hudhud*) is the messenger-bird of Solomon and, in ʿAṭṭār's *Conference of the Birds*, the guide of the soul's journey to the divine — a fitting body for the sacred name that opens every act and every Quranic chapter. Bird-calligrams of the [[motif:bismillah|bismillah]] are a favourite of the lettrist tradition, where the formula's letters are also counted and meditated upon for their abjad value.

## For artists & game designers
A model bismillah-bird: a single holy phrase that resolves into a guide-creature.

title: Calligram in the Shape of a Lion (ʿAlī)
work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
creator: Vesal Shirazi (attrib.)
summary:
A zoomorphic calligram in which a devotional Arabic phrase honouring **ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib** is bent into the body of a lion.

## Iconography
The whole beast — mane, paw, curling tail — is formed of cursive script, the letters doubling as anatomy. The lion is the standard emblem of ʿAlī, the *Asad Allāh* ('Lion of God'), so the image is both portrait and prayer: the holy name *becomes* the holy animal.

## Significance
Zoomorphic calligraphy belongs to the lettrist conviction that the [[motif:sacred letters|sacred letters]] are living, generative substance. By shaping a venerated name into a lion the calligrapher makes a talisman that is read and seen at once — a practice especially developed in Shiʿi and dervish circles. Such pieces circulated as protective images in 18th–19th-century Iran and India.

## For artists & game designers
A template for 'word-creatures' — sigils whose silhouette spells the spell they cast.

title: Bronze Astragalus (Knucklebone)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Greek or Roman)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A bronze *astragalus* (knucklebone) of the Greco-Roman world, the four-sided 'die' used both for games and for divination by lot (Metropolitan Museum of Art).

## Iconography
The object is a cast-bronze copy of a sheep's or goat's ankle-bone, with its characteristic four distinguishable faces of unequal value. Real bones, and metal or stone replicas like this one, were thrown in sets; each face counted as a number (commonly 1, 3, 4, 6).

## Significance
The knucklebone is the oldest randomiser of the Mediterranean world. Beyond the game of *astragaloi*, casting them was a recognised oracle: at certain shrines a throw was matched to a verse in an [[motif:astragalomancy|astragalomantic]] table (the *Anatolian dice-oracles*) that delivered a god-sanctioned answer. The astragalus thus stands at the root of the whole dice-and-lot tradition that the medieval and Renaissance lot-books would elaborate into wheels and printed books.

## For artists & game designers
The original four-sided die — an authentic ancient randomiser prop, and the historical seed of every dice-oracle table.

citations.text: On dice-oracles, see J. Nollé, *Kleinasiatische Losorakel* (2007).

title: Bollstatter Losbuch (Cgm 312)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Konrad Bollstatter
rights: Public domain. Bayerische Staatsbibliothek. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A page of the *Losbuch* of Konrad Bollstatter (Munich, Bayerische Staatsbibliothek, Cgm 312), a German vernacular lot-book of the later fifteenth century.

## Iconography
The leaf combines diagram and rubricated verse: pointing devices, named prophets or kings, and tables route the reader from a question through a chain of intermediaries to a rhymed German answer. The hand is Bollstatter's, a prolific Augsburg scribe who copied several such books.

## Significance
The German *Losbücher* are the northern counterpart of the Italian printed lot-books, descending from antique and Arabic sortilege. The querent generated a number (by dice, by a spun [[motif:wheel of fortune|wheel]], or by pricking) and followed pointers — often a series of prophets, animals, or kings — to a fortune. Bollstatter's manuscripts are key witnesses to this living medieval game of chance-and-counsel, blending entertainment with quasi-prophetic authority.

## For artists & game designers
The northern lot-book's pointer-chains (prophet → animal → answer) are a vivid model for a hand-drawn, manuscript-styled fortune mechanic.

title: Domanda (Question) Pages — Triompho di Fortuna
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Sigismondo Fanti
rights: CC0 / Public domain. The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An opening of *Domande* (questions) from Fanti's *Triompho di Fortuna* (1526), the index by which a querent enters the lot-book (Metropolitan Museum of Art).

## Iconography
The text is set as numbered *DOMANDA* entries — 'Se il paese della donna haura victoria o sera vinta', 'Se la phisonomia de l'huomo e buona o no' — each followed by an instruction such as *Va alla Fortuna di Libico alla lettera E*, with a small marginal woodcut. The reader finds their question, then is dispatched by letter and name into the wheels and houses that follow.

## Significance
These question-tables are the entry layer of the lot-book's algorithm: a fixed menu of fortunes (love, victory, journeys, character, life and death) cross-referenced into the [[motif:rota|wheels]]. The form descends from the medieval *Losbücher* and Lorenzo Spirito, and shows how thoroughly the genre systematised divination into a branching, page-jumping procedure long before any mechanical computer.

## For artists & game designers
A genuine 16th-century 'dialogue tree': pick a question, follow the letter, jump to the result — a dating-from-1526 template for branching oracle UX.

title: Rota della Charità & Rota della Fede (Triompho di Fortuna)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Sigismondo Fanti
rights: CC0 / Public domain. The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A double-page opening from Fanti's *Triompho di Fortuna* (1526) showing two answer-wheels, the *Rota della Charità* and the *Rota della Fede* (the Wheels of Charity and of Faith), flanked by attendant sages (Metropolitan Museum of Art).

## Iconography
Each page carries a paired set of [[motif:rota|wheels]]: an upper figured wheel with a virtue personified at its hub, and a lower dense ring of numbered or lettered cells. Worthies and prophets stand in the margins, named in cartouches. The reader, directed here from the question-tables, spins or reads off the ring to find the cell that yields the next step toward an answer.

## Significance
The *rote* are the working machinery of the lot-book: a randomising lookup table dressed as an allegorical wheel of Fortune. By naming the wheels for the theological virtues, Fanti folds a moral cosmology into the mechanics of chance, so that consulting Fortune becomes a passage through Faith, Charity, and the rest. It is a vivid witness to how Renaissance print turned divination into an interactive, navigable system.

## For artists & game designers
A literal spin-the-wheel UI from 1526 — concentric numbered rings with a themed hub, directly usable as a randomiser interface.

title: Triompho di Fortuna — Title Page (Sigismondo Fanti)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Sigismondo Fanti (woodcut after a design attrib. Baldassare Peruzzi)
summary:
The elaborate title-page woodcut of Sigismondo Fanti's *Triompho di Fortuna* (Venice, 1526), one of the great printed fortune-telling lot-books of the Italian Renaissance.

## Iconography
Beneath the red title a vast turning globe or drum dominates the page; the figures *Virtus* and *Voluptas* (Virtue and Pleasure) are named above, while nude genii heave at the wheel of Fortune below. At right rises an astronomical clock-tower with a zodiacal dial; an aged astrologer kneels with his instruments at lower left. The whole composition allegorises the contest of virtue, pleasure, and chance that the book proposes to read.

## Significance
Fanti's *Triompho* is a sortilege book: the reader poses one of a fixed set of questions and is sent, by dice or chance, through a chain of [[motif:wheel of fortune|wheels]] (*rote*), houses, astrologers, and prophets to a versified answer. It belongs to the same family as Lorenzo Spirito's *Libro delle Sorti* but is more lavishly astrological. The design is traditionally connected to Baldassare Peruzzi, and the book is dedicated, in its grandeur, to the courts of Italy.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made 'oracle engine' frontispiece — globe, named virtues, clockwork zodiac — perfect as the cover of an in-game book of fate.

title: Rota del Dio d'Amore — Triompho di Fortuna
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Sigismondo Fanti
rights: CC0 / Public domain. The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A page from Fanti's *Triompho di Fortuna* (1526) bearing the *Rota del Dio d'Amore* (Wheel of the God of Love) above a zodiacal wheel, attended by named sages including Plotino and Eusebio (Metropolitan Museum of Art).

## Iconography
The upper [[motif:rota|wheel]] frames a scene of the God of Love at its hub, its rim a dense band of inscribed cells; a lower wheel is marked with the [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] and further numbered divisions. In the corners stand authorities — Plotinus, Eusebius, and others — named to lend the oracle classical weight.

## Significance
Naming a wheel for the *Dio d'Amore* and pairing it with the zodiac shows how Fanti distributed the querent's possible fates across a cosmos of love, virtue, and the stars. The summoned philosophers act as guarantors of the answer, a print-era version of consulting the ancients. It is among the most decorative of the book's many wheels and a fine example of the lot-book as an astrological-allegorical machine.

## For artists & game designers
A themed result-wheel (Love) ringed by named 'experts' — a model for flavourful randomiser screens that cite in-world authorities.
key_concepts: Wheel of the God of Love

title: The Wheel of Fortune (15th-c. French manuscript)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Anonymous (French illuminator)
summary:
A fifteenth-century French manuscript miniature of the *Roue de Fortune*, the Wheel of Fortune, set in a flowered border above a column of text.

## Iconography
A great wooden [[motif:wheel of fortune|wheel]] turns at the right of a verdant scene; a small figure clings at its summit while others are raised and cast down around it. A crowd of courtiers looks on. The image personifies Fortune's indifferent turning, the ascent and ruin of the great — the visual commonplace of medieval *de casibus* literature.

## Significance
The *Rota Fortunae* is the master-image behind every lot-book and fortune-game: Boethius's blind goddess who raises kings only to dash them, a wheel that became the engine-metaphor for divinatory wheels (Fanti's *rote*, the tarot's trump X) and for the moralised histories of fallen princes. This miniature exemplifies its luxurious courtly form in late-medieval France.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical medieval luck-wheel with rising/falling figures — the ancestor of every spin-the-wheel and a clear 'fate turns' icon.

title: Wheel of Fortune (de casibus tradition)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Anonymous (manuscript illumination)
summary:
A cropped manuscript image of the Wheel of Fortune in the *de casibus* tradition, with crowned figures rising and falling on the turning wheel.

## Iconography
Fortune's [[motif:wheel of fortune|wheel]] carries kings at its four cardinal points — one ascending (*regnabo*, 'I shall reign'), one enthroned at the top (*regno*, 'I reign'), one toppling (*regnavi*, 'I have reigned'), and one crushed beneath (*sum sine regno*, 'I am without rule') — the classic four-king scheme of the medieval Wheel.

## Significance
This four-king *Rota Fortunae* visualises the cycle of worldly fortune that gave Boccaccio's *De casibus virorum illustrium* and Lydgate's *Fall of Princes* their theme. As the governing emblem of chance and mutability, it underwrites the whole lot-book enterprise: to consult Fortune is to step onto her wheel. The image is among the most copied of all medieval allegories.

## For artists & game designers
The four-king wheel (regnabo / regno / regnavi / sum sine regno) is a compact, legible status-cycle device for any rise-and-fall mechanic.

citations.text: Boccaccio, *De casibus virorum illustrium*; Boethius, *De consolatione*.

title: Geomantic Figures in Wellcome MS Arabic 823
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Arabic manuscript)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A detail from an Arabic geomantic manuscript (Wellcome Collection, MS Arabic 823) showing the dot-figures of *'ilm al-raml* set out in a working table.

## Iconography
Rows and columns of [[motif:geomancy|dot-figures]] are arranged across the page, each a tetragram of single and paired dots, interspersed with Arabic captions. The layout is the practical 'shield' or table by which a geomancer derives the daughter- and resulting figures from the four 'mothers.'

## Significance
The manuscript witnesses *'ilm al-raml* in its home tradition: a learned Arabic art of generating and combining the sixteen figures to answer questions of fortune, illness, travel, and love. Such tables are the direct ancestors of the European geomantic 'shield chart.' The Wellcome holding documents the Islamic-world geomancy that medieval Latin translators carried into Europe, where it became one of the most popular forms of bookish divination.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic Arabic geomancy worksheet — period reference for the 'shield chart' that turns four random figures into a full reading.

title: Geomantic Figure Types (the sixteen figures, named)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Phil wink (modern diagram of the traditional figures)
date: modern diagram of a medieval system
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (Phil wink). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A clear modern chart of the sixteen geomantic figures with their Latin names, illustrating the fixed vocabulary of Western *geomantia*.

## Iconography
Each [[motif:geomancy|figure]] is drawn as four rows of one or two dots and labelled — *Via*, *Cauda Draconis*, *Caput Draconis*, *Puella*, *Puer*, *Fortuna Major*, *Fortuna Minor*, *Acquisitio*, *Amissio*, *Carcer*, *Tristitia*, *Laetitia*, *Albus*, *Rubeus*, *Coniunctio*, *Populus*. The two 'Fortunes' and 'Via'/'Populus' pairs show the symmetry of the set.

## Significance
This is the Latin-European canon of the sixteen figures as transmitted from Arabic *'ilm al-raml* through medieval and Renaissance geomancers. Each figure carries planetary, elemental, and zodiacal attributions and a fortune-meaning, so that a generated chart can be read like a horoscope. The diagram is a convenient reference key to the system that underlies European geomantic divination, the *Book of Fate*, and occult revivals.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled cheat-sheet of all sixteen figures and their names — drop-in reference for implementing a geomancy reading system.

citations.text: S. Skinner, *Geomancy in Theory and Practice* (2011).

title: The Sixteen Geomantic Figures (Arabic classification)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Arabic tradition)
summary:
A classification chart of the sixteen geomantic figures of Arabic *'ilm al-raml* ('the science of the sand'), the binary tetragrams from which geomantic readings are built.

## Iconography
The figure arranges the sixteen [[motif:geomancy|geomantic figures]] — each a stack of four rows of one or two dots (e.g. *Via*, *Populus*, *Fortuna Major*, *Acquisitio*) — into a classified table. The one-or-two pattern of each row encodes a binary value; the four rows give 2⁴ = 16 possible figures.

## Significance
Geomancy generates these tetragrams by tallying random marks made in sand (or on paper) into odd or even, then derives 'mothers,' 'daughters,' 'nieces,' and 'witnesses' to answer a question. Born in the medieval Arab world, *'ilm al-raml* passed into Latin Europe as *geomantia* (Hugh of Santalla, Pietro d'Abano), into the Renaissance via Agrippa, and into living African practice as Ifá and *sikidy*. The sixteen figures are its fixed alphabet of fate.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 16-symbol binary divination set — trivially generated from random bits and historically deep across three continents.

citations.text: S. Skinner, *Geomancy in Theory and Practice* (2011).

title: Fuxi Order Grid and Circle of the Hexagrams
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: James Legge (engraving, Sacred Books of the East, 1882)
summary:
The Fuxi (Shao Yong) arrangement of the 64 hexagrams as both a square grid and an enclosing circle, engraved for James Legge's translation of the *Yi King* (Sacred Books of the East, vol. 16, 1882).

## Iconography
Within a great ring of [[motif:i ching|hexagrams]] sits a square block of the same 64 figures; together they display the 'former heaven' (Fuxi) order attributed to Shao Yong. Each hexagram is six yin/yang lines, and the layout makes the binary symmetry of the sequence visible at a glance.

## Significance
This is the diagram (the *Xiantian* or Fuxi arrangement) that underlies the binary reading of the *Yijing* and that reached Leibniz. In divinatory use the *I Ching* is consulted by building a hexagram from chance — yarrow-stalk counts or tossed coins — and interpreting its judgement, image, and changing lines. Legge's careful Victorian engraving made the classical Chinese oracle and its elegant combinatorics widely available to Western readers.

## For artists & game designers
A beautiful ring-plus-grid of all 64 states — usable directly as the selection diagram for a coin- or stalk-cast oracle minigame.

citations.text: James Legge, *The Yî King* (Sacred Books of the East, vol. 16, 1882).

title: Diagram of I Ching Hexagrams (Leibniz's copy, 1701)
work: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: Anonymous (after the Fuxi arrangement); copy owned by G. W. Leibniz
summary:
The diagram of the 64 *I Ching* hexagrams in the Fuxi 'natural' order, in the copy sent to Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz in 1701.

## Iconography
