Recorded finds like this document how widely the practice spread in post-medieval England.

## For artists & game designers
A pocket charm engraved with cryptic characters: the everyday wearable version of high-ceremonial magic, ideal as common 'minor talisman' loot.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Talismanic shirt (Khalili Collection)
work: Magical & Esoteric Objects and Ritual Instruments
creator: Anonymous (Islamic world)
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 IGO. Khalili Collections, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An Islamic talismanic shirt from the Khalili Collection, a garment covered with protective inscriptions to be worn for safety, especially in battle.

## Iconography
A cotton or silk **shirt** whose entire surface is inscribed in tiny script with **Qur'anic verses**, the ninety-nine names of God, prayers, and **magic squares** (*wafq*), often arranged in panels and roundels. Every inch of the textile is a field of protective writing.

## Significance
The **talismanic shirt** (Arabic *qamīs ṭilasm*, Ottoman *tılsımlı gömlek*) was worn under armour to shield the wearer through the power of sacred text and [[motif:talisman|magic squares]]. Such shirts were produced across the Islamic world, with notable Ottoman and Mughal examples; their making could take years, since the writing had to be executed at astrologically auspicious times. They are among the most spectacular wearable talismans of any tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Armour made of words: a shirt whose every thread is covered in protective script — a striking 'enchanted garment' that defends by inscription, not plate.

citations.text: Venetia Porter (ed.), *Arabic and Persian Seals and Amulets in the British Museum* (2011).
Khalili Collection, Hajj and the Arts of Pilgrimage.

title: De secretis mulierum (pseudo-Albertus Magnus), frontispiece
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Anonymous engraver (after pseudo-Albertus Magnus)
summary:
A frontispiece to *De secretis mulierum* ('On the Secrets of Women'), one of the popular 'books of secrets' circulated under the name of Albertus Magnus.

## Iconography
The printed leaf presents the work of pseudo-Albertus — a learned authority associated with the secrets of nature, generation, and natural magic. Such frontispieces lent the immense prestige of Albert the Great (c. 1200–1280), the Dominican philosopher and naturalist, to a body of pseudonymous occult and medical lore.

## Significance
The historical Albertus Magnus was a sober scholastic and student of nature, but his fame made him a magnet for spurious magical attributions: *De secretis mulierum*, the *Book of Secrets* (of herbs, stones, and beasts), and the grimoire *Le Grand Albert / Le Petit Albert* all travelled under his name. These pseudo-Albertine 'books of secrets' were among the most widely printed sources of popular natural magic and recipe-lore in early-modern Europe, as William Eamon has shown. The genuine and legendary Albert together made him a presiding figure of the magus tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Use a respected scholar's name on the cover of a dubious magic book — the in-world 'attributed grimoire' whose authority is half borrowed.

citations.text: William Eamon, *Science and the Secrets of Nature* (1994).
Pseudo-Albertus Magnus, *De secretis mulierum* (numerous early printings).

title: Astrolabe (the astrologer's instrument)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Photograph by Evan (Paris)
rights: CC BY 2.0 (photo by Evan, Paris). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An eighteenth-century brass astrolabe, the calculating instrument that stands behind every depiction of the astrologer-magus in his study.

## Iconography
The assembled astrolabe shows its pierced *rete* (a star-map of pointers) turning over engraved plates (*tympans*) marked with the lines of altitude and the houses for a given latitude. By setting the rete to a star or the Sun, the astrologer could read the time, find the ascendant, and cast a horoscope — the physical engine of horary and natal astrology.

## Significance
The astrolabe, perfected in the Islamic world and transmitted to Europe via Arabic learning, was for a thousand years the emblem and the tool of the astrologer. In magical practice the same instrument timed operations to the planetary hours and the favourable aspects on which talismans and conjurations depended. Where the necromancer has his circle, the astrologer-magus has his astrolabe; it is the recurring prop of the 'magus in his study' image. (Photograph licensed CC BY 2.0.)

## For artists & game designers
The astrologer's signature device: a turning brass star-map the player aligns to compute the right hour for a spell.

citations.text: J. D. North, *Stars, Minds and Fate: Essays in Ancient and Medieval Cosmology* (1989).

title: John Dee Performing an Experiment before Queen Elizabeth I
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Henry Gillard Glindoni
summary:
Henry Gillard Glindoni's painting of John Dee demonstrating his art before Queen Elizabeth I and her court — the magus as respected royal philosopher rather than hedge-conjurer.

## Iconography
Dee, robed and bearded, stands before a fire or brazier in a great chamber, gesturing as Elizabeth and her courtiers look on. He performs a 'natural experiment' — chemistry, optics, or astrology presented as wonder. Famously, an X-ray of the canvas revealed that Glindoni first painted Dee inside a ring of human skulls, which the artist or a patron later painted out, softening necromancer into natural philosopher.

## Significance
The picture dramatizes the double reputation of John Dee (1527–1609) — Elizabeth's learned adviser on navigation, calendar reform, and astrology, and, in legend, a conjurer of spirits. The painted-over skulls literally embody the Victorian ambivalence about whether Dee was scientist or sorcerer. The work is now at the Wellcome Collection. Glindoni specialized in costume-drama historical genre scenes.

## For artists & game designers
A model for the 'court magician' set-piece: the mage performing a controlled wonder before a watching monarch, the spectacle of magic as political theatre.

citations.text: Benjamin Woolley, *The Queen's Conjuror: The Science and Magic of Dr John Dee* (2001).

title: Edward Kelley Raising a Ghost (Dee and Kelley necromancy)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Robert Cross Smith ('Raphael')
summary:
The famous necromantic scene of John Dee and Edward Kelley raising the dead in a churchyard, the most reproduced image of Elizabethan ceremonial magic in action.

## Iconography
Two cloaked magicians stand within a protective double [[motif:magic circle|magic circle]] inscribed with names and signs, holding books and torches by night among the graves and a ruined church. A shrouded, risen corpse — the conjured shade — stands before them. The circle's perimeter is crowded with divine and angelic names that ward the operators from the spirit they have called.

## Significance
The engraving was popularized by the astrologer Robert Cross Smith ('Raphael') in *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (1825), which mythologized Dee and his scryer Edward Kelley as graveyard necromancers — a lurid Romantic legend rather than a record of their actual angelic 'actions.' It nonetheless became the canonical picture of necromancy, fixing the iconography of two magi, a warded circle, and a risen dead. Studies of Dee's reception by Deborah Harkness and György Szőnyi trace how the historical mathematician became this folk-image of the black magician.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive necromancer tableau: two operators, a double-warded circle, torches, graves, and a single risen shade — readable at a glance as 'raising the dead.'

citations.text: Robert Cross Smith, *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (1825).

title: Mephistopheles Presents Himself to Martha (Delacroix, Faust)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Eugène Delacroix
summary:
A lithograph from Eugène Delacroix's celebrated series illustrating Goethe's *Faust*, showing the devil Mephistopheles among mortals.

## Iconography
The sardonic, sharp-featured Mephistopheles — feathered cap, cloak, blade — insinuates himself into a domestic scene, the diabolical intelligence moving among the human characters of the drama. Delacroix's flickering lithographic line gives the tempter a restless, predatory grace.

## Significance
Delacroix produced seventeen lithographs for the 1828 French edition of Goethe's *Faust*; Goethe himself praised them. The series fixed the Romantic image of Mephistopheles as the elegant, ironic devil with whom the magician-scholar Faust has bargained — the spirit raised and bound being the very figure who damns the conjurer. The plates are a touchstone of nineteenth-century book illustration and of the Faust legend's grip on the Romantic imagination; the Met and other museums hold impressions.

## For artists & game designers
The template for a charismatic devil-familiar: not a monster but a witty, well-dressed spirit who is far more dangerous for being personable.

citations.text: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, *Faust* (Paris ed., 1828), illustrated by Delacroix.

title: Frontispiece, The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century (1825)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Internet Archive Book Images (after the 1825 edition)
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An illustration from *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (1825), the lavishly engraved compendium of astrology and magic edited by 'Raphael' (Robert Cross Smith).

## Iconography
The plate belongs to the dense emblematic apparatus of the book — magi, celestial figures, and the props of ceremonial magic and astrology assembled to advertise the 'whole art' of the stars and spirits. Such frontispieces presented the nineteenth-century magus as heir to Dee, Lilly, and Sibly.

## Significance
*The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* was a key vehicle of the early-Victorian occult revival, reprinting and illustrating older grimoire and astrological material for a popular market; it is the same volume that popularized the 'Edward Kelley raising a ghost' necromancy scene catalogued here. Its editor Robert Cross Smith ('Raphael') founded a lineage of *Raphael's* almanacs that ran for over a century. The work bridges the learned magic of the seventeenth century and the modern occult revival studied by Ronald Hutton and Alison Butler.

## For artists & game designers
A model for an in-world 'magician's manual' frontispiece: a crowded emblematic title plate promising mastery of stars and spirits alike.

citations.text: Robert Cross Smith ('Raphael'), *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (1825).
Alison Butler, *Victorian Occultism and the Making of Modern Magic* (2011).

title: Faust Title-page with the Conjuration (British Museum print)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Anonymous engraver
summary:
An engraved title-page print (British Museum) of the Faust legend, showing the scholar conjuring within his circle as a devil appears.

## Iconography
The magician stands inside an inscribed [[motif:magic circle|magic circle]] in his study, book and rod in hand, while a horned or grotesque spirit rises before him in answer to the conjuration. Such title-page vignettes condensed the whole Faust story — pact, evocation, damnation — into a single emblematic image of the man in the circle facing what he has called.

## Significance
The Faust *Volksbuch* (chapbook) of 1587 and its many translations and dramatizations (including Marlowe's *Doctor Faustus*) were furnished with woodcut and engraved frontispieces of exactly this scene: the over-reaching scholar conjuring Mephistopheles inside the circle. These prints, gathered in the British Museum and elsewhere, are the popular iconographic stock from which the 'magician in the magic circle' became a fixed European image.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal evocation frame: one operator, one inscribed circle, one rising spirit — the minimum visual grammar for 'a magician summons a demon.'

title: Sikidy: The Diviner Casting Geomantic Figures
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Anonymous (c. 1900 photograph/print)
summary:
A diviner performing *sikidy*, the Malagasy form of geomancy, arranging seeds into the grid of figures from which fate is read.

## Iconography
The *ombiasy* (diviner) sits before an array of seeds or grains laid out in columns and rows. By randomly grouping the seeds and reducing them in pairs, he generates a tableau of binary 'figures' — the same odd/even geomantic figures used across the Old World — and interprets their positions to answer the client's question. The image captures divination as a manual, deliberate operation.

## Significance
*Sikidy* is the Malagasy branch of the great geomantic family that runs from the Arabic *ʿilm al-raml* ('science of the sand') through medieval Latin *geomantia* to West African *Ifá* and European fortune-telling. All share the method of generating sixteen four-line figures from a random binary process and reading them in a chart of houses. Studies by Stephen Ellis and the anthropology of Malagasy divination document *sikidy* as a living system. It exemplifies 'the diviner at work' — magic as calculation rather than evocation.

## For artists & game designers
A tactile divination minigame: cast and pair tokens to build a binary chart, then read the resulting figures as an oracle.

citations.text: Stephen Ellis & Solofo Randrianja, *Madagascar: A Short History* (2009).
Wim van Binsbergen, on geomantic divination systems.

title: A Peep into Friar Bacon's Study (Rowlandson)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Thomas Rowlandson (print), publ. William Humphrey
summary:
Thomas Rowlandson's print *A Peep into Friar Bacon's Study*, a view into the conjurer's cabinet with its instruments of evocation.

## Iconography
The magician's study is crowded with the props of his art — books, skull, globe, magical apparatus — and the conjuring magician at work raising or commanding a spirit. As a Rowlandson production the image carries a satirical edge, but it faithfully assembles the standard furniture of the evocation scene: the study as the necromancer's laboratory.

## Significance
By the late eighteenth century the figure of Friar Bacon and the conjurer's study had become stock subjects for English print satire, even as antiquarian and Romantic interest in 'the black art' revived. Rowlandson (1757–1827), the great caricaturist, here treats the magician's cabinet as a scene of wonder and mockery at once. Such prints transmitted the visual inventory of the magus — circle, skull, grimoire, apparition — to a broad popular audience. The impression is held by the British Museum.

## For artists & game designers
A complete prop list for a wizard's study in one frame: skull, globe, open grimoire, alembics, and a half-summoned shape in the air.

title: The Necromancer; or, Harlequin Doctor Faustus (pantomime fleuron)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: John Rich (deviser)
summary:
A printed fleuron from *The Necromancer; or, Harlequin Doctor Faustus*, John Rich's enormously popular London pantomime of 1723.

## Iconography
The small engraved ornament condenses the Faust-necromancer theme for the stage: the conjuring magician and the diabolical apparatus of the pantomime's plot, in which Doctor Faustus raises spirits and is finally carried off by devils. As a typographic *fleuron* it is a miniature emblem rather than a full scene.

## Significance
John Rich's *Necromancer* (Lincoln's Inn Fields, 1723) was one of the first great English pantomimes and helped fix the Faust legend as popular spectacle — the necromancer's conjurations turned into stage machinery, transformations, and a hellish finale. Its success drove the eighteenth-century vogue for 'Harlequin' magic entertainments. The fleuron survives in the printed text catalogued in the English Short Title Catalogue (T147278).

## For artists & game designers
A reminder that the necromancer was also pure entertainment: stage-trap conjurations and a devil's exit make magic a show as much as a threat.

citations.text: John O'Brien, *Harlequin Britain: Pantomime and Entertainment, 1690–1760* (2004).

title: Miranda, Prospero, and Caliban (The Tempest)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: George Romney
summary:
George Romney's painting of Prospero with his daughter Miranda and the creature Caliban, from Shakespeare's *The Tempest* — the benevolent magus who commands spirits.

## Iconography
The robed Prospero, the deposed Duke of Milan turned island sorcerer, presides over his daughter Miranda and the monstrous Caliban. In the play his power flows from his books and his magic staff or mantle, and from the airy spirit Ariel whom he commands. Romney's Romantic treatment casts the magician as a grave, paternal figure of authority rather than a diabolist.

## Significance
Prospero is the literary apotheosis of the Renaissance magus — a learned prince whose 'art' (drawn, scholars note, from the natural-magic tradition of Agrippa and John Dee) raises tempests, binds spirits, and finally renounces magic by drowning his book. The figure has been read by Frances Yates and others as Shakespeare's portrait of the Hermetic philosopher-magician. Romney (1734–1802) painted several Shakespearean subjects for Boydell's Shakespeare Gallery.

## For artists & game designers
The 'good wizard' archetype: a robed master whose power lives in a book and a staff, and whose final act of strength is to give that power up.

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *The Occult Philosophy in the Elizabethan Age* (1979).

title: Faust in His Study (Rembrandt etching)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Rembrandt van Rijn
summary:
Rembrandt's celebrated etching traditionally called *Faust*: a scholar-magus rising from his desk before a glowing apparition of letters at the window.

## Iconography
A bearded scholar in his cluttered study turns toward a window where a radiant disk has materialized — a wheel of letters and a cabbalistic inscription (including INRI and an anagrammatic formula) hovering in light, a hand pointing within it. Books, a globe, and a skull surround him. The magus does not stand in a circle but receives a luminous vision, magic rendered as illumination rather than evocation.

## Significance
The plate (c. 1652) was already associated with Faust in the seventeenth century and was reused as a frontispiece to an edition of Goethe's *Faust*. Its glowing anagram has been read as a magical-cabbalistic formula, making it the great Baroque image of the learned magician at the threshold of revelation. The print is held at the National Gallery of Art and elsewhere; scholarship by Christopher White and H. Perry Chapman addresses its esoteric reading.

## For artists & game designers
A gentler vocabulary for spellcasting: not a summoned demon but a hovering ring of glowing glyphs — magic as light and text breaking into the scholar's room.

title: Friar Bacon's Brazen Head
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Anonymous woodblock engraver
summary:
A woodcut of the legendary Brazen Head built by Friar Roger Bacon — the prophesying brass automaton of medieval magical legend.

## Iconography
The scene shows the bearded friar-magician with his apparatus and the speaking head of brass, the oracular automaton that, the legend says, would utter the words 'Time is,' 'Time was,' 'Time is past' before shattering. A sleeping servant misses the moment, dooming the great work. The print belongs to the chapbook tradition of *The Famous Historie of Fryer Bacon*.

## Significance
The historical Roger Bacon (c. 1219–1292), Franciscan natural philosopher and experimenter, was transformed by later legend into a necromancer who, aided by Friar Bungay and a bound spirit, sought to build a wall of brass around England by means of a speaking head. Robert Greene's play *Friar Bacon and Friar Bungay* (c. 1589) dramatized the tale. The brazen head became the emblem of forbidden artificial knowledge — the medieval ancestor of the thinking machine.

## For artists & game designers
The original talking-oracle automaton: a constructed head that speaks true prophecy but only at the instant its maker is unprepared.

citations.text: Robert Greene, *Friar Bacon and Friar Bungay* (c. 1589).
William Newman, on medieval legends of artificial life.

title: King Solomon and Two Demons (Persian manuscript)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: 'Aliquli (illuminator)
summary:
A Persian manuscript painting of King Solomon enthroned with two demons (*divs*) brought into his service — the master who commands the spirits.

## Iconography
Solomon sits enthroned in royal majesty while two grotesque, horned *divs* (demons) attend or kneel before him, subdued. In the Islamicate Solomon cycle the king rules men, beasts, winds, and the *jinn* and *divs* alike, who labour at his command. The painting bears the name of the illuminator 'Aliquli and is held by the Walters Art Museum.

## Significance
Both biblical and Qur'anic tradition make Solomon (Sulaymān) the archetypal master of spirits, whose signet ring sealed the demons and compelled them to build the Temple and serve his will. This image of the king commanding bound demons is the root of the whole Solomonic grimoire tradition — the *Key of Solomon* and *Lesser Key* claim his authority over the seventy-two spirits. The Walters W.624 manuscript is one of many Persian witnesses to the Solomon-and-the-*divs* iconography studied by Marina Tolmacheva and others.

## For artists & game designers
The ideal 'spirit-binder king' image: a serene ruler flanked by subdued demons who do his building and his bidding.

citations.text: Qur'an, Sūrat Ṣād and Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ (on Sulaymān and the jinn).

title: Le Bateleur — The Magician (Dodal Tarot de Marseille)
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Jean Dodal
summary:
*Le Bateleur* ('the Magician' or juggler), the first trump of the Tarot de Marseille, in Jean Dodal's early-eighteenth-century Lyon pattern.

## Iconography
A standing figure in a wide brimmed hat holds a wand or baton aloft over a table strewn with implements — cups, coins, knives, dice. In the woodcut Marseille tradition he is the *bateleur*, the conjurer or sleight-of-hand showman; the four suit-tokens on his table came to be read esoterically as the magician's command of the four elements. His raised wand and the brim's lemniscate shape were later interpreted as signs of cosmic power.

## Significance
From the eighteenth century onward — through Court de Gébelin, Éliphas Lévi, and the Golden Dawn — the humble *Bateleur* was reinterpreted as 'The Magician,' the adept who unites the four elemental tools to channel the divine current ('as above, so below'). Dodal's deck (Lyon, c. 1701–15) is one of the canonical surviving Marseille patterns. The card became the tarot's emblem of the will-directed magician.

## For artists & game designers
The magician's loadout in one card: wand in hand, the four elemental tools laid out on the table, ready to be combined.

citations.text: Éliphas Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1856).

title: Saul and the Witch of Endor Raising Samuel
work: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: Anonymous engraver (Rijksmuseum)
summary:
An engraving of the Witch (medium) of Endor calling up the dead prophet Samuel at the request of King Saul — the great biblical scene of necromancy.

## Iconography
The robed woman of Endor performs her conjuration as the shade of the prophet Samuel rises, while the terrified King Saul recoils or kneels. The apparatus of necromancy — circle, smoke, gestures of summoning — frames the risen spirit, who delivers Saul's doom. The Dutch title on the Rijksmuseum print names Saul consulting 'the sorceress at Endor' to make Samuel appear.

## Significance
The episode (1 Samuel 28), in which the desperate Saul, having banned mediums, secretly visits the woman of Endor on the eve of battle, was the single scriptural warrant most debated in early-modern arguments about whether the dead can truly be raised. It anchored treatises on witchcraft and spirits by Reginald Scot, Jean Bodin, and others, and was endlessly engraved. The Rijksmuseum holds this and many related prints of the subject.

## For artists & game designers
The biblical proof-text for séance and necromancy alike: a medium, a frightened patron, and a risen prophet who answers only with bad news.

citations.text: Bible, 1 Samuel 28 (Saul and the medium at Endor).

title: Albumasar, De Magnis Coniunctionibus (Venice, 1515)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Abū Maʿshar al-Balkhī (Albumasar); printed Venice, 1515
summary:
A page from the 1515 Venice printing of *De Magnis Coniunctionibus* (On the Great Conjunctions) of **Albumasar**, the most influential single text of medieval astrology in its Latin form.

## Iconography
The printed page carries the apparatus of conjunction theory — text columns and astrological **diagrams** — under the name of [[motif:albumasar|Albumasar]]. The 1515 edition reproduces in print the doctrine that the Latin Middle Ages had absorbed centuries earlier through translation.

## Significance
Abū Maʿshar al-Balkhī (787–886), working in Baghdad, systematized the theory of **great conjunctions** of Saturn and Jupiter, used to date the rise and fall of religions and empires. Translated into Latin in the twelfth century (by John of Seville and Hermann of Carinthia), his works were foundational for Western astrology and for thinkers from Albertus Magnus to Pierre d'Ailly. This image dates to the surviving 1515 print, not the lost ninth-century original.

## For artists & game designers
A dense diagram-and-text page is the archetypal 'astrologer's authoritative tome' — useful as a lore prop or a predictive-mechanic interface.

citations.text: Charles Burnett, 'Abū Maʿshar,' in *The Cambridge History of Science* vol. 2.
Richard Lemay, *Abu Ma'shar and Latin Aristotelianism* (1962).

title: Armillary sphere (astronomical instrument diagram)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Harold Nelson (illustration)
summary:
An illustration of an **armillary sphere**, the ringed model of the heavens that was the emblematic instrument of pre-modern astronomy.

## Iconography
Nested metal **rings** represent the great circles of the sky — the celestial equator, the **ecliptic** with its band of the zodiac, the tropics and colures — turning around a central earth (or, later, sun). The device is at once a teaching model, a computing aid, and a symbol of cosmic order.

## Significance
Descending from Greek and Islamic astronomy, the [[motif:armillary sphere|armillary sphere]] embodied the spherical cosmos of Sacrobosco in three dimensions; it became the standard attribute of the astronomer and astrologer in art. Through its rings one read off the framework — equator, ecliptic, zodiac — on which every horoscope and calendar depended.

## For artists & game designers
The armillary is the perfect 'astronomer's tool' icon: a hand-held cosmos of brass rings, equally at home as a puzzle object, a navigation aid, or a wizard's instrument.

citations.text: James Evans, *The History and Practice of Ancient Astronomy* (1998).

title: Planispheric astrolabe, Iran, dated 984 CE (Museum of Islamic Art)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous (Islamic instrument-maker)
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0. Museum of Islamic Art, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A planispheric **astrolabe** made in Iran in 984 CE, among the earliest dated surviving examples of the master-instrument of medieval astronomy.

## Iconography
The brass disc carries the pierced **rete** — a star-map of pointers and an off-centre **ecliptic** ring — turning over engraved plates marked with the coordinate lines for a given latitude. By rotating the rete the user could compute the positions of sun and stars, tell time, and find the ascendant.

## Significance
The [[motif:astrolabe|astrolabe]], perfected in the Islamic world and transmitted to Latin Europe (notably via al-Andalus and figures like Gerbert of Aurillac), was indispensable to both astronomy and astrology: casting a horoscope began with finding the degree of the zodiac rising on the eastern horizon, a calculation the astrolabe performed directly. This 984 instrument is a landmark of the tradition.

## For artists & game designers
The astrolabe's pierced star-rete over scaled plates is a gorgeous, genuinely functional 'computation device' — ideal as an interactive dial puzzle or a diviner's tool.

citations.text: David A. King, *Astrolabes from Medieval Europe* (2011).

title: Encyclopedic manuscript with allegorical and medical drawings (bloodletting and zodiac)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A leaf from an encyclopedic medical manuscript combining allegorical and medical drawings, including the astrological apparatus of bloodletting.

## Iconography
The page joins figural **allegory** to practical **medical diagrams** — among them the vein-man and zodiacal charts that told a physician when and where to bleed. Such compendia bundled cosmology, calendar, and therapeutics on facing leaves.

## Significance
Encyclopedic medical codices of this kind circulated the linked doctrines of [[motif:melothesia|zodiacal melothesia]] and lunar timing through monastic and university medicine. The body, the heavens, and the calendar were treated as one system: the right moment for a remedy was an astronomical question. Digitized through the World Digital Library.

## For artists & game designers
A single page that bundles allegory, anatomy, and star-charts is a model 'physician's field manual' prop — dense, diagrammatic, and legibly arcane.

title: December–Capricorn, Der Naturen Bloeme of Jacob van Maerlant (KB KA 16)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Jacob van Maerlant (text); attributed to Gheraert van Lienhout
rights: Public domain. Koninklijke Bibliotheek, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The sign Capricorn for the month of December, companion image to the Sagittarius leaf in the same Middle-Dutch *Der Naturen Bloeme* (KB, KA 16).

## Iconography
**Capricorn** is rendered in its classical hybrid form — the **sea-goat**, fore-quarters of a goat tapering into a fish-tail — and paired with **December**. It rounds out the year's zodiacal sequence at the winter solstice.

## Significance
The goat-fish of [[motif:capricorn|Capricorn]] descends from Babylonian and Greco-Roman star-lore (the *aigokeros*) and marks the solstitial gate of the year, anciently the 'gate of the gods'. Its survival in a fourteenth-century Dutch natural encyclopaedia shows the deep continuity of the constellation figures from antiquity through medieval vernacular science.

## For artists & game designers
The goat-fish is an unusually striking hybrid — a distinctive silhouette for a winter, sea, or threshold-guardian creature.

citations.text: Jacob van Maerlant, *Der Naturen Bloeme* (ed. Eelco Verwijs).

title: Saturn on clouds (planetary deity), Gaspare Diziani
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Gaspare Diziani
summary:
An eighteenth-century Venetian image of **Saturn** enthroned on clouds by Gaspare Diziani — a late reception of the planetary-deity tradition descended from the medieval planet-rulers. (Dated to its actual creation, not to a lost medieval original.)

## Iconography
The aged, brooding god sits among **clouds**, attributes of [[motif:saturn|Saturn]] at hand — the **scythe** of harvest and time, the heavy melancholic posture. He is the planet imagined as a sovereign and an old man, lord of the slow, the cold, and the saturnine temperament.

## Significance
The identification of Saturn with melancholy, age, agriculture, and devouring Time runs from antiquity through the medieval Children-of-the-Planets cycles into Renaissance and Baroque art (famously analysed in Klibansky, Panofsky and Saxl's *Saturn and Melancholy*). This Diziani composition shows the planetary king continuing as a living artistic type long after the Middle Ages.

## For artists & game designers
The scythe-bearing old man on clouds is the definitive 'lord of time and decay' figure — directly usable for a Saturnine boss, deity, or melancholic patron.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

title: Halley's Comet on the Bayeux Tapestry
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous (Anglo-Norman embroiderers)
summary:
The scene from the **Bayeux Tapestry** showing the comet of 1066 (Halley's Comet) as an omen, a rendering reproduced in *Popular Science Monthly*.

## Iconography
A blazing, many-rayed **comet** hangs in the sky above a crowd of pointing onlookers; nearby, the newly crowned Harold sits uneasy on his throne while courtiers gesture at the **portent**. The embroidered caption reads *ISTI MIRANT STELLA* — 'these men wonder at the star'.

## Significance
The apparition of [[motif:comet|Halley's Comet]] in spring 1066 was read across Europe as a sign of upheaval, and the Tapestry frames it as a heavenly verdict on Harold's contested kingship before his fall at Hastings. Comets were the most dramatic of astrological **omens**, traditionally heralding the death of princes and the change of realms — a belief that persisted into the era of the printed comet-broadsheet.

## For artists & game designers
The rayed comet over a fearful court is the definitive 'ill omen' beat — a single sky-event that signals regime change or doom.

citations.text: David J. Bernstein, *The Mystery of the Bayeux Tapestry* (1986).
Roberta J. M. Olson, *Fire in the Sky: Comets and Meteors* (1985).

title: Planetary plate from Sphaerae coelestis et planetarum descriptio
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous (early-modern printed astronomicum)
summary:
A planetary diagram plate (no. 13) from the printed *Sphaerae coelestis et planetarum descriptio*, a descriptive atlas of the celestial sphere and the planets.

## Iconography
The plate sets out the **celestial sphere** and a planetary motion in geometric **diagram** form — circles, orbs, and the apparatus of [[motif:epicycle|epicyclic]] astronomy used to describe a planet's course. (The Commons title numbers but does not name the specific planet on this leaf, so I leave the identification general.)

## Significance
Descriptive planetary atlases of this type translated the Ptolemaic-Sacroboscan model of nested spheres into clean printed figures for teaching and computation. They are the technical substrate beneath astrology: before a horoscope could be cast, the positions had to be modelled by exactly such geometry of orbs and epicycles.

## For artists & game designers
A precise circles-and-orbits plate is ideal 'hard astronomy' set-dressing — the calculating, instrumental face of the cosmos rather than its mythic one.

citations.text: J. L. E. Dreyer, *A History of Astronomy from Thales to Kepler* (1906).

title: Luna plate from Sphaerae coelestis et planetarum descriptio
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous (early-modern printed astronomicum)
summary:
The **Luna** (Moon) plate from the printed *Sphaerae coelestis et planetarum descriptio*, diagramming the sphere and motion of the Moon.

## Iconography
The leaf — named *Luna* in its title — sets out the **lunar sphere**, the innermost of the planetary heavens in the geocentric scheme, as a circular **diagram** of orbs. The Moon stands at the boundary between the sublunary world of change and the unchanging heavens above.

## Significance
In astrological doctrine the [[motif:moon|Moon]] is the swiftest and most consequential of the planets for daily practice: its sign and phase governed the [[motif:melothesia|timing]] of medicine, bloodletting, planting, and travel. This descriptive plate gives the geometric back-end of that lore — the lunar orb whose computed position underwrote every almanac prognostication.

## For artists & game designers
A dedicated lunar-orbit plate suits a time, tide, or phase mechanic where the Moon's position drives in-game effects.

citations.text: J. L. E. Dreyer, *A History of Astronomy from Thales to Kepler* (1906).

title: Planetary plate (15) from Sphaerae coelestis et planetarum descriptio
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous (early-modern printed astronomicum)
summary:
A second planetary diagram plate (no. 15) from the printed *Sphaerae coelestis et planetarum descriptio*, continuing the descriptive survey of the planets.

## Iconography
As with its companion leaves, the plate renders a planet's sphere and motion as a geometric **diagram** of concentric circles and orbs. (The file title gives only a plate number, so the precise planet is left unstated here rather than guessed.)

## Significance
The series as a whole walks through the planetary spheres one by one, the printed counterpart to the manuscript planet-cycles. Where the Children-of-the-Planets images show the planets as gods and rulers, plates like this show them as objects of [[motif:cosmology|mathematical cosmology]] — the same bodies, the opposite mode of depiction.

## For artists & game designers
Pair this with a Children-of-the-Planets image to contrast the 'two faces' of a planet: mythic ruler versus measured orb.

citations.text: J. L. E. Dreyer, *A History of Astronomy from Thales to Kepler* (1906).

title: Mercury and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The planet **Mercury** with his 'children' from the luxurious *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), a high point of the Children-of-the-Planets tradition.

## Iconography
Mercury, the planetary deity, presides at the top of the page over the trades and arts he governs — scholars, clockmakers, painters, organists, scribes and merchants busy in a contemporary urban scene. This is the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] schema: each planet rules a temperament and a set of human occupations.

## Significance
The *De Sphaera* (c. 1470), associated with the Sforza court of Milan, presents each of the seven planets as a crowned ruler attended by the people born under it. The cycle visualizes astrological determinism in social terms — your planet shaped your character and your craft. It is one of the most sumptuous surviving examples of the genre.

## For artists & game designers
A planet-as-patron presiding over a townful of its 'children' is a vivid model for a faction, guild, or origin system keyed to seven celestial archetypes.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

title: Bloodletting Man under the influence of zodiac and planets
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous
date: later medieval / early modern (per Wellcome record)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A bloodletting man (*homo signorum*) showing the parts of the body governed by the zodiac signs and planets, from the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
A frontal nude figure is overlaid with the twelve **zodiac** signs, each linked by line or label to the body part it rules, from Aries at the head to Pisces at the feet. Such diagrams were practical tools for the [[motif:phlebotomy|phlebotomist]]: they told the surgeon which member it was dangerous to bleed while the Moon stood in the governing sign.

## Significance
The **bloodletting man** is the working-medical cousin of the courtly Zodiac Man, copied into almanacs, physicians' folding calendars, and books of hours across late-medieval and early-modern Europe. It encodes the doctrine of [[motif:melothesia|melothesia]] in a form a barber-surgeon could consult at the bedside.

## For artists & game designers
The lined-and-labelled body chart reads instantly as 'medical diagram of magical correspondences' — a strong UI model for an affliction or surgery mechanic.

citations.text: Charles Clark, 'The Zodiac Man in Medieval Medical Astrology,' *Journal of the Rocky Mountain MLA* (1979).

title: Calendar opening with labours of the months, Maastricht Book of Hours (BL Stowe MS 17)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous (Liège–Maastricht area)
rights: Public domain. The British Library, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A calendar opening from the Maastricht Book of Hours (British Library, Stowe MS 17), pairing the labours of the months with the zodiac.

## Iconography
The calendar leaves carry the **labours of the months** — seasonal occupations such as pruning, harvesting, and pig-slaughter — set beside the corresponding **zodiac** sign for each month. The familiar pairing aligns human work with the turning sky.

## Significance
The **labours / signs** cycle is one of the most widespread of all medieval image-programmes, running through portal sculpture, psalters, and books of hours. It expresses the agrarian and astrological rhythm of the year: each month has its task and its [[motif:zodiac|sign]], the calendar binding labour, season, and heaven into a single round.

## For artists & game designers
A twelve-cell cycle of occupation + sign is a perfect calendar or season system: each month already carries an activity and a celestial flavour.

citations.text: Bridget Ann Henisch, *The Medieval Calendar Year* (1999).

title: Constellation miniature from the Leiden Aratea
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Carolingian workshop, after Aratus (text) and classical models
summary:
A constellation figure from the **Leiden Aratea** (Leiden, UB, MS Voss. Lat. Q. 79), the luxurious Carolingian copy of the *Phaenomena* of Aratus in Latin.

## Iconography
The constellation is drawn as a full classical **personification** — a mythological body with the relevant stars marked upon it — set in a framed field of antique character. The figures preserve a late-antique pictorial tradition transmitted through the Latin *Aratea*.

## Significance
The *Phaenomena* of [[motif:aratus|Aratus]] (3rd c. BCE) was the standard ancient poem of the constellations; its Latin translations (Cicero, Germanicus, Avienus) carried the sky-pictures into the Middle Ages. The Leiden manuscript, made for the Carolingian court c. 816–840, is among the most faithful copies of an antique illustrated star-book and a key witness to how classical astronomy survived.

## For artists & game designers
Constellations rendered as living bodies with stars pricked onto them — a beautiful template for a 'living sky' or star-summoned-creature design.

citations.text: Ranee Katzenstein & Emilie Savage-Smith, *The Leiden Aratea* (1988).

title: The last lunar mansion of Scorpio (Wellcome Collection)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram depicting the last **lunar mansion** in the region of Scorpio, from the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The figure marks a station of the Moon among the fixed stars — one of the **mansions of the Moon** (Arabic *manāzil al-qamar*), the 28 segments through which the Moon passes in its monthly circuit. This one falls in the stretch of sky around **Scorpio**.

## Significance
The [[motif:lunar mansions|28 lunar mansions]] are a distinct system from the 12 solar zodiac signs, central to Arabic astronomy and to the talismanic magic transmitted to the Latin West through texts like the *Picatrix*. Each mansion had its own influences and was used to time the making of talismans and the launching of undertakings. Diagrams like this one served that operative, image-magic tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A 28-station lunar cycle is a finer-grained alternative to the 12 signs — ideal for a calendar or talisman-crafting system with many short, distinct windows.

citations.text: *Picatrix* (Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm), trans. Hashem Atallah / D. Pingree edition.

title: The Moon and her planet-children (De zeven planeten series), Rijksmuseum
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Netherlandish printmaker (Children of the Planets series)
date: early modern (16th–17th c.; per Rijksmuseum record)
rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving of **Luna** (the Moon) and her *planeetkinderen* from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, the early-modern descendant of the medieval Children of the Planets cycle.

## Iconography
The Moon rides her **chariot** across the upper sky while below, her 'children' pursue the lunar occupations — fowlers, fishers, millers, travellers, and those who work by water or wander by night. A verse caption names the planet's traits.

## Significance
The printed [[motif:children of the planets|Planetenkinder]] series carried a medieval manuscript scheme into the age of engraving, fixing for a broad public the link between each planet and a social type. Luna governs the changeable, the watery, and the restless — the traditional temperament of those born under the Moon. The Rijksmuseum sheet belongs to a complete seven-print set.

## For artists & game designers
The planet-in-chariot-above, children-below composition is a clean two-register layout for showing a ruling power and the lives it shapes.

title: Sphaera Mundi of Johannes de Sacrobosco (Ratdolt edition, Venice 1482)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Johannes de Sacrobosco (text); printed by Erhard Ratdolt
rights: Public domain (CC0). The Metropolitan Museum of Art, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram page from the *Sphaera Mundi* of **Johannes de Sacrobosco**, in the celebrated 1482 Venice edition printed by Erhard Ratdolt.

## Iconography
The woodcut shows the **celestial spheres** — concentric heavens enclosing the central earth — the standard geocentric diagram of the cosmos. Ratdolt's edition is famous for its clear, pioneering printed astronomical figures.

## Significance
Sacrobosco's *De sphaera* (c. 1230) was THE elementary astronomy textbook of the Latin West, taught in every university for four centuries. It expounded the spherical cosmos, the zodiac, the climes, and the celestial motions that underpinned all [[motif:zodiac|astrological]] practice. The 1482 Ratdolt print made these diagrams reproducible; the image here dates to that incunabulum, not to a medieval manuscript.

## For artists & game designers
The concentric-spheres diagram is the canonical 'map of the cosmos' — instantly readable as a worldview screen, cosmology menu, or planar map.

citations.text: Lynn Thorndike, *The Sphere of Sacrobosco and Its Commentators* (1949).

title: November–Sagittarius, Der Naturen Bloeme of Jacob van Maerlant (KB KA 16)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Jacob van Maerlant (text); attributed to Gheraert van Lienhout
rights: Public domain. Koninklijke Bibliotheek, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The sign Sagittarius for the month of November, from a Middle-Dutch copy of Jacob van Maerlant's encyclopaedic *Der Naturen Bloeme* (The Hague, KB, KA 16).

## Iconography
**Sagittarius** appears as the centaur-archer, bow drawn, paired with **November** in the calendar sequence. The miniature belongs to a month-by-month run of zodiac figures illustrating the natural-philosophical sections of the codex.

## Significance
Maerlant's *Der Naturen Bloeme* (c. 1270, after Thomas of Cantimpré) was the great vernacular Dutch encyclopaedia of nature; later illuminated copies such as KB KA 16 add the **zodiac** to its cosmological matter. The archer-centaur is the standard medieval form of [[motif:sagittarius|Sagittarius]], carrying the classical type into northern vernacular learning.

## For artists & game designers
A clean centaur-archer reference tied to a specific month — drop-in art for a zodiac-themed class, enemy, or seasonal event.

citations.text: Jacob van Maerlant, *Der Naturen Bloeme* (ed. Eelco Verwijs).

title: Astronomical volvelle from Blundeville's Exercises
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Thomas Blundeville (text); printed England
rights: Public domain. Library of Congress, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A printed **volvelle** — a rotating paper instrument — from *M. Blundeville His Exercises*, an English manual of practical mathematics and cosmography.

## Iconography
The page presents a **volvelle**: concentric discs of paper, joined at the centre and meant to be turned, bearing scales of the zodiac, the calendar, or planetary motions. Rotating the discs lets the reader read off a position or a date — a computation built into the book itself.

## Significance
Volvelles carried the calculating power of the [[motif:astrolabe|astrolabe]] onto the printed page, democratizing astronomical and astrological reckoning. Descended from medieval manuscript wheels, they flourished in early-modern almanacs and cosmographies (Apian's *Astronomicum Caesareum* is the grand exemplar). Blundeville's *Exercises* (first ed. 1594) made such instruments available to an English readership.

## For artists & game designers
A volvelle is a ready-made interactive dial — spin concentric rings to compute or unlock; a tactile UI metaphor with genuine historical pedigree.

citations.text: Suzanne Karr Schmidt, *Interactive and Sculptural Printmaking in the Renaissance* (2017).

title: The Anatomical Man (Homo signorum), Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Limbourg brothers
summary:
The celebrated *Homme anatomique* of the *Très Riches Heures*, the most famous medieval image mapping the twelve zodiac signs onto the human body.

## Iconography
Two back-to-back figures stand within an almond-shaped **mandorla** ringed by the twelve signs. Each sign is placed on the body part it was held to govern — **Aries** at the head, **Pisces** at the feet — the scheme of [[motif:melothesia|zodiacal melothesia]]. The four corners give the temperaments and elemental qualities (hot/cold, moist/dry).

## Significance
Melothesia — the doctrine that each zodiac sign rules a region of the body — governed medieval and Renaissance medicine, dictating when it was safe to bleed, purge, or operate on a given member. The Limbourg brothers produced this leaf for Jean de Berry around 1411–16; the manuscript is now Chantilly, Musée Condé MS 65. It is the canonical visualization of the **Zodiac Man**.

## For artists & game designers
A ready paper-doll of 'body zones governed by signs' — directly usable for a status, anatomy, or affliction system keyed to twelve influences.

citations.text: Jean Longnon & Raymond Cazelles, *Les Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry* (1969).
Harry Bober, 'The Zodiacal Miniature of the Très Riches Heures,' *JWCI* 11 (1948).

title: The Twelve Signs of the Zodiac, Dirc van Delft's Tafel van den Kersten Ghelove (Walters W.171)
work: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: Dirc van Delft (text); Dutch illuminator
rights: Public domain. The Walters Art Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A full-page miniature of the twelve zodiac signs from a copy of Dirc van Delft's *Tafel van den Kersten Ghelove* in the Walters Art Museum (W.171).

## Iconography
The twelve **signs** are arranged together on the page — the ram, bull, twins, crab, lion and the rest — as a compact survey of the zodiacal **wheel**. The image serves the cosmological chapters of a Christian encyclopaedic handbook, integrating the pagan signs into a devotional world-picture.

## Significance
Dirc van Delft, court theologian to Duke Albrecht of Bavaria, compiled the *Tafel* (c. 1400) as a Dutch summa of the faith that nonetheless included natural philosophy and the [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]]. Such inclusions show how thoroughly astrological cosmology was woven into orthodox medieval learning, the heavens read as the legible order of God's creation.

## For artists & game designers
A single sheet displaying all twelve signs is a ready-made selection wheel or zodiac-picker for a calendar, divination, or character-origin mechanic.

citations.text: K. R. Tervoort, *Dirc van Delft and the Tafel van den Kersten Ghelove*.

title: Pentagram and the human body (Agrippa)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
summary:
The pentagram inscribing the human body, from Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* — the canonical image of man as microcosm of the five planets and the heavens.

## Iconography
A nude figure stands spread-eagled, head and four limbs touching the five points of a [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]]; planetary signs are distributed around the figure and at the cardinal points. The proportion demonstrates that the human form contains the harmonic measures of the cosmos, with each point governed by a planet.

## Significance
Agrippa's *Three Books of Occult Philosophy* (1531–33) systematized Renaissance magic into natural, celestial, and ceremonial tiers, and this woodcut became its emblem: the doctrine that man is a little world bound by *correspondences* to the great one, so that operating on the right sign or number reaches the heavens. The image anticipates later Vitruvian and occult body-diagrams.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'as above, so below' diagram — map a character's five attributes to the five star-points and their planetary rulers.

citations.text: H. C. Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia libri tres* (1531–33).

title: Nota of the Ars Notoria (figure of logic)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Reconstruction after Bodleian MS Bodley 951, f. 10v (drawn by Haplo1990)
date: Original tradition 12th–13th c.; this reconstruction 21st c.
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (reconstruction by Haplo1990). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A reconstruction of a *nota* — one of the intricate prayer-figures of the *Ars Notoria*, the medieval art of acquiring knowledge and memory through ritual contemplation, after Bodleian MS Bodley 951.

## Iconography
The *nota* is a complex diagram of interlaced lines, circles, and bands filled with [[motif:divine names|divine names]], angelic names, and *verba ignota* (unknown words). Each figure corresponds to a liberal art — here a 'figure of logic' — and is gazed upon while reciting the associated orations so that the discipline is infused directly into the mind.

## Significance
The *Ars Notoria*, circulating from the 12th–13th century and often ascribed to Solomon, promised mastery of the liberal arts, eloquence, and memory without ordinary study. Its blend of devout prayer with theurgic figures made it deeply controversial; Thomas Aquinas condemned it as illicit. Julien Véronèse's critical edition is the standard scholarly treatment. (This image is a modern redrawing of a figure in the named manuscript.)

## For artists & game designers
Knowledge-as-magic: a 'study figure' the player meditates on to unlock a skill, the diagram literally writing the ability into the character.

citations.text: Julien Véronèse, *L'Ars notoria au Moyen Âge* (2007).

title: Pentacle of Solomon (The True Keys of Solomon)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Anonymous occultist
summary:
A Pentacle of Solomon from an 18th-century manuscript of *The True Keys of Solomon* (Les Véritables Clavicules de Salomon), one of the most copied grimoires of the Solomonic cycle.

## Iconography
The pentacle is a circular figure ringed with verses and divine names, enclosing geometric stars (hexagram and/or pentagram) and Hebrew or pseudo-Hebrew lettering. Each *Clavicula* pentacle is keyed to a specific operation — protection, treasure-finding, love, the binding of spirits — and is meant to be inscribed at the proper planetary hour on virgin parchment.

## Significance
The *Clavicula Salomonis* circulated in Latin, Italian, and French from the late medieval period; the 18th-century French recensions standardized the now-canonical set of planetary pentacles later popularized by S. L. MacGregor Mathers in 1888. The pentacles fuse Jewish theurgic name-magic with Christian ritual framing, and were always understood as licit only when used 'in the name of God.'

## For artists & game designers
A library of ready-made spell-sigils: each pentacle is a self-contained icon you can map to a discrete in-game effect.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (1888).

title: The Hand of Glory (Main de Gloire)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Modern illustration after grimoire tradition
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Albertus Parvus Lucius). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *Main de Gloire* (Hand of Glory), the macabre thieves' talisman of European grimoire folklore — a dried severed hand fitted as a candle-holder.

## Iconography
The charm is the pickled and dried hand of a hanged man, its fingers either holding or themselves serving as candles whose wax is rendered from the same corpse. Lit, the Hand of Glory was believed to freeze the inhabitants of a house motionless and to render its bearer unseen, making it the perfect burglar's aid.

## Significance
Recipes for the Hand of Glory circulate in early-modern French grimoires such as the *Petit Albert*, drawing on the older necromantic notion that the bodies of the executed retain potent magical virtue. The motif fuses learned grimoire instruction with popular witch-lore and became a fixture of folklore and gothic fiction. (This image is a modern depiction of the traditional object.)

## For artists & game designers
A grisly utility item: an equip that applies a 'sleep / unseen' status to NPCs in a room while held aloft.

citations.text: *Le Petit Albert* (Secrets merveilleux de la magie naturelle et cabalistique, 18th c.).

title: Magic Circle of the Heptameron
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: After Pseudo-Pietro d'Abano (Peter of Abano)
date: Attributed text 13th–14th c.; figure after early printed editions
summary:
The conjuring circle of the *Heptameron, or Magical Elements*, the compact ritual handbook attributed to Pietro d'Abano for summoning the angels of the days and hours.

## Iconography
The diagram shows nested circles inscribed with the names of God, angels, and the ruling spirit of the day, with crosses and a [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]] at the cardinal points and candle positions marked around the perimeter. The operator stands at the centre; the design wards him while compelling the summoned angel to appear in the triangle outside.

## Significance
The *Heptameron* (printed 1496, falsely ascribed to the physician Pietro d'Abano) supplied the practical scaffolding — circle, hours, planetary angels, conjurations — for much later European ritual magic, and was frequently bound with Agrippa's spurious *Fourth Book of Occult Philosophy*. Its day-by-day system of angelic governance made it a working manual rather than a theoretical treatise.

## For artists & game designers
A textbook summoning-circle layout: concentric name-rings, cardinal candles, central operator, external triangle for the conjured entity.

title: Magic seals, Harley MS 5596 (Hygromanteia tradition)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Anonymous (Byzantine Greek scribe)
summary:
A column of magic seals (*characteres*) from British Library Harley MS 5596, fol. 31r — a 15th-century Byzantine Greek magical manuscript central to the *Hygromanteia* (Magical Treatise of Solomon).

## Iconography
The leaf presents a vertical series of abstract spirit-signs: looped, barred, and ringed glyphs of the kind used to name and bind individual spirits. Each [[motif:characteres|charakter]] functions as a 'signature' by which a spirit is summoned and constrained — visually akin to the later Icelandic *galdrastafir*.

## Significance
Harley 5596 is one of the principal witnesses to the *Hygromanteia*, the Greek Solomonic handbook that fed directly into the later Latin *Clavicula Salomonis* and the *Key of Solomon* cycle. Its system of planetary hours, angel/demon pairings, and seals shows the Byzantine bridge between late-antique Greek magic and the Western grimoire tradition. Ioannis Marathakis's edition translates the text.

## For artists & game designers
A palette of abstract 'true-name' glyphs — assign one per summonable spirit so players collect sigils like keys.

citations.text: Ioannis Marathakis (ed.), *The Magical Treatise of Solomon, or Hygromanteia* (2011).

title: Seals of the Goetia (Lesser Key of Solomon)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Rune370, after the Lemegeton
date: Lemegeton compiled 17th c.; this rendering modern
summary:
A sheet of demon seals from the *Ars Goetia*, the first book of the *Lemegeton* (Lesser Key of Solomon), each glyph the binding signature of one of the seventy-two spirits.

## Iconography
The seals are abstract linear sigils — combinations of crosses, loops, dots, crowns, and barbs — one per demon (Bael, Agares, Vassago, and so on). The conjurer engraves the spirit's seal on a metal *lamen* worn on the breast; displaying it compels the spirit named to appear and obey.

## Significance
The *Ars Goetia* lists seventy-two demons with their ranks, legions, and offices, drawn largely from Johann Weyer's *Pseudomonarchia Daemonum* (1577) and reworked in 17th-century English manuscripts. Its seals are the most reproduced demon-sigils in Western magic, canonized by Mathers and Crowley's 1904 edition. (This image is a modern clean rendering of the traditional seals.)

## For artists & game designers
Seventy-two distinct, collectible 'monster sigils' — a built-in bestiary keyed to a sigil-based summoning system.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & Aleister Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia: The Lesser Key of Solomon* (1904).

title: Sefer Raziel HaMalakh (Book of Raziel the Angel)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Anonymous (attributed to the angel Raziel)
date: Compiled medieval; this printed edition Amsterdam 1701
summary:
A page from the *Sefer Raziel HaMalakh* (Book of Raziel the Angel), the Hebrew compendium of angelic and protective magic that the Latin West knew as the *Liber Razielis*.

## Iconography
The page combines Hebrew text with magical seals, divine and angelic names, and protective figures. The book is famous for its amulet-diagrams — circles and grids of holy names used to guard the home, ease childbirth, and ward off harm — which were copied onto parchment and hung as charms.

## Significance
Attributed to the angel Raziel, who supposedly gave the book to Adam, the *Sefer Raziel* gathers older Hekhalot and practical-Kabbalah material; a Latin *Liber Razielis* was assembled at the court of Alfonso X in the 13th century. The Amsterdam printing of 1701 made it a widely owned protective object in itself — the mere presence of the book in a house was thought to ward off fire and evil.

## For artists & game designers
A 'book that is itself a talisman' — an inventory item whose protective effect comes from possession, not reading.

citations.text: Sophie Page, *Magic in the Cloister* (2013), on the Latin *Liber Razielis*.

title: Magic square in Dürer's Melencolia I
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Albrecht Dürer's engraving *Melencolia I* (1514), which embeds a 4×4 magic square — the most famous talismanic number-grid in Western art.

## Iconography
The brooding winged figure sits amid the tools of geometry and craft; on the wall hangs a 4×4 square whose rows, columns, and diagonals all sum to 34, with the date 1514 set in its bottom row. As the *kamea* (magic square) of Jupiter, it was understood to counter the malefic, melancholic influence of Saturn that grips the figure.

## Significance
Renaissance magic, drawing on the *Picatrix* and Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (1531), assigned each planet a numbered square engraved on metal as a planetary talisman. Dürer places Jupiter's square against Saturnine *melancholia generosa* — the inspired-but-afflicted temperament of the genius. Panofsky and Klibansky's *Saturn and Melancholy* is the classic study.

## For artists & game designers
Number-grid as charm: surface a magic square as a puzzle whose solved state grants a planetary buff.

citations.text: H. C. Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1531), Book II.

title: Pentacles of Saturn (The Key of Solomon the Great)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), after anonymous Solomonic originals
summary:
Plate II of MacGregor Mathers's 1888 *Key of Solomon the King*, showing the seven Pentacles of Saturn — the planetary seals governing constraint, ruin, and the binding of spirits.

## Iconography
Each pentacle is a disc bordered with a verse (usually a Psalm) and filled with a geometric figure, Hebrew divine names, and the characters of the spirits of Saturn. Saturnine pentacles are dark in purpose: causing ruin, bringing answers in dreams, compelling refusing spirits, and protecting against earthquakes. The plate arranges them as a grid of operative seals.

## Significance
Mathers compiled his edition from several British Library *Clavicula* manuscripts, attaching each planetary set to its traditional powers. Saturn, slowest and most malefic of the seven classical planets, governs the most coercive magic. The plate became the canonical visual reference for the Solomonic pentacles in the modern occult revival and Golden Dawn circles.

## For artists & game designers
Use the seven-seal grid as a planetary 'skill tree' — Saturn for curses, debuffs, and binding mechanics.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (London, 1888).

title: Picatrix (Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm) manuscript leaf
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Anonymous (Arabic scribe)
date: Text 10th–11th c.; this MS later (Hamidiye Library MS 852, f. 45v)
summary:
A leaf from a late Arabic manuscript of the *Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm* — the 'Aim of the Sage,' known in the Latin West as the *Picatrix*, the foundational handbook of astral talisman-magic.

## Iconography
The page is dense Arabic text laying out the manufacture of talismans: the carving of planetary and decan images at elected astrological moments, the suffumigations, and the petitions addressed to planetary spirits to 'draw down' their celestial virtue into matter. Manuscripts of the work carry tables and occasional figures of the planetary spirits and zodiacal decans.

## Significance
Compiled in al-Andalus around the 11th century and translated into Castilian and Latin under Alfonso X in 1256, the *Picatrix* transmitted Hermetic and Sabian astral magic into Europe, shaping Ficino, Agrippa, and the entire Renaissance theory of talismans. David Pingree's edition of the Arabic and Latin texts is the standard reference.

## For artists & game designers
The ur-text of 'crafting under the right stars' — tie item enchantment to astrological timing and planetary correspondences.

citations.text: David Pingree (ed.), *Picatrix: The Latin Version of the Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm* (1986).

title: The Seal of Solomon (Lévi)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Éliphas Lévi
summary:
Éliphas Lévi's drawing of the Seal of Solomon — the interlaced double triangle that became the master emblem of correspondence between the higher and lower worlds.

## Iconography
Two overlapping equilateral triangles form a [[motif:hexagram|hexagram]], one pointing up and one down, often shaded so that light and dark interpenetrate. Lévi reads the figure as the union of the macrocosm and microcosm, spirit and matter, the active and passive principles — 'that which is above is like that which is below.'

## Significance
The hexagram-as-Seal-of-Solomon long predates Lévi, appearing on amulets and rings in Jewish, Islamic, and Christian magic as a sign of dominion over spirits. But Éliphas Lévi's 19th-century occult synthesis (*Dogme et rituel de la haute magie*, 1854–56) recast it as a key diagram of esoteric polarity, and it is his rendering that shaped the modern occult image of the seal.

## For artists & game designers
A clean, universally legible 'balance' or 'sealing' glyph — pair the up/down triangles with light/shadow mechanics.

citations.text: Éliphas Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1854–56).

title: Sigillum Dei (Seal of God)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: After Giorgio Anselmi (Georgius Parmensis)
summary:
A version of the *Sigillum Dei* (Seal of God), the elaborate concentric talisman of divine and angelic names that crowned the Solomonic and Honorian magical traditions.

## Iconography
The seal is built from nested geometry: an outer ring carrying the names of God and his angels, an inner heptagon or heptagram, and a five- or seven-pointed star at the centre. The interstices are packed with [[motif:divine names|divine names]], crosses, and letters, each compartment assigned to a particular spiritual power. The whole forms a closed cosmogram meant to compel obedience from spirits.

## Significance
The Sigillum Dei descends from the *Liber Juratus* (Sworn Book of Honorius, 13th–14th c.) and reached its most famous form as the *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* used by John Dee and Edward Kelley in their angelic scrying. The design encodes the names by which God governs creation; correctly drawn, it was held to grant the operator the vision of God and authority over angels. Studies by Jan Veenstra and Julien Véronèse trace its descent through the Honorian corpus.

## For artists & game designers
The perfect 'master ward' prop: a dense ring of names around a star, instantly readable as a high-tier protective or summoning glyph.

citations.text: Julien Véronèse, *L'Almandal et l'Almadel latins au Moyen Âge* (2012).
Jan R. Veenstra, on the *Liber Juratus* tradition.

title: Post-medieval talismanic pendant (PAS FindID 755270)
work: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: Anonymous; photo The Portable Antiquities Scheme
rights: CC BY 2.0 (The Portable Antiquities Scheme). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A post-medieval copper-alloy medallion or amulet-pendant recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme — a surviving physical object from the world that grimoires describe.

## Iconography
The pendant is a small disc of copper alloy, pierced for suspension and bearing inscribed marks — names, characters, or a seal of the sort prescribed by grimoires for protective and curative charms. Worn on the body, such a piece carried its inscribed magic continuously with its owner.

## Significance
Grimoires from the *Clavicula* to the *Petit Albert* instruct the operator to engrave seals and divine names on metal *lamens* or pendants, made at elected planetary hours, to attract or repel particular influences. Excavated and metal-detected finds like this one — catalogued through the British Museum's Portable Antiquities Scheme — show that such talismans were not merely textual but actually manufactured and worn.

## For artists & game designers
A grounded reference for craftable charm items: a plain pierced disc whose inscribed sigil determines its passive effect.

citations.text: The Portable Antiquities Scheme database, FindID 755270.

title: A Practitioner of Mesmerism Using Animal Magnetism
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A print of a mesmerist at work, directing animal magnetism upon a seated patient through the characteristic passes of the hands (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The magnetizer leans toward a seated subject, hands extended toward the patient's head and torso, fingers spread to project the fluid. The patient's posture — head tilted, eyes closing — signals the onset of the magnetic state.

## Significance
The [[motif:magnetic pass|magnetic pass]] was the basic technique of the operator: slow sweeping motions of the hands along the body, supposed to channel the [[motif:invisible fluid|fluid]] from magnetizer to patient and establish the [[motif:magnetic rapport|rapport]] that bound them. The image distils the whole practice to its essential gesture — the willed, fingertip transmission of an unseen force.

## For artists & game designers
A spell-casting pose ready for animation: hands that emit an invisible influence, the target's reaction keyed to the operator's gestures.

citations.text: Alison Winter, *Mesmerized: Powers of Mind in Victorian Britain* (1998).

title: Mesmer's Animal Magnetism Therapy (The Baquet)
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A scene of Mesmer's collective therapy around the *baquet*, the magnetic tub at the heart of his Paris clinic (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
Patients ring a low covered tub from which protrude bent iron rods; sitters press the rods to afflicted parts of the body, some linked by a connecting cord to share the [[motif:magnetic rapport|magnetic rapport]]. Attendants move among them as one or two slump into the convulsive [[motif:magnetic crisis|crisis]].

## Significance
The [[motif:the baquet|baquet]] was Mesmer's machine for distributing the [[motif:invisible fluid|invisible fluid]] to many patients at once — an oak vat filled with 'magnetized' water, iron filings, and glass, its rods conducting the supposed influence into the body. The room, with its dim light, music, and chains of joined hands, staged a powerful collective suggestion that produced fits, trances, and reported cures.

## For artists & game designers
A ritual-machine set piece: a central apparatus that channels an unseen force to a circle of linked participants, with crisis as the visible 'effect'.

citations.text: Robert Darnton, *Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France* (1968).

title: An Authentic Baquet de Mesmer (Surviving Object)
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous (photographed by Romainbehar)
summary:
A photograph of a surviving *baquet de Mesmer*, a rare physical example of the magnetic tub preserved at the Bibliothèque municipale de Lyon.

## Iconography
A low circular wooden vat, its lid pierced for the jointed iron rods that patients once applied to their bodies. Seen as an object rather than a scene, it shows the plain cooper's construction beneath the era's extravagant claims.

## Significance
Few genuine baquets survive, so this object anchors the mesmeric craze in material fact: an oak tub that, filled with 'magnetized' water, bottles, and iron filings, was held to store and dispense the [[motif:invisible fluid|animal-magnetic fluid]]. Where engravings show the [[motif:the baquet|baquet]] amid swooning crowds, the surviving artefact reveals the modest apparatus that focused so much Enlightenment controversy.

## For artists & game designers
A 'relic' prop: the actual device, weathered and demystified, that a player might recover and study apart from the spectacle that surrounded it.

citations.text: Robert Darnton, *Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France* (1968).

title: Title-page of James Braid's Neurypnology
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: James Braid
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The title-page of James Braid's *Neurypnology; or, The Rationale of Nervous Sleep* (1843), the book that coined 'hypnotism' (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
A plain printed title-page announcing Braid's treatise on 'nervous sleep considered in relation with animal magnetism'. The typography of a sober medical work signals its claim to rescue the phenomena from occultism.

## Significance
The Manchester surgeon James Braid argued that the 'magnetic' trance owed nothing to any fluid passing between operator and subject; it was a physiological 'nervous sleep' induced by fixed attention and the fatigue of the gaze. Renaming it [[motif:hypnotism|hypnotism]] (from Greek *hypnos*), he recast mesmerism as a subject for [[motif:rational physiology|physiology]] and psychology, a decisive step toward modern hypnosis.

## For artists & game designers
The 'rational reframing' beat: the document by which a feared occult power is redefined as a natural, teachable technique.

citations.text: James Braid, *Neurypnology; or, The Rationale of Nervous Sleep* (1843).

title: Portrait of Alessandro Cagliostro
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous (Rijksmuseum)
summary:
An engraved portrait of Alessandro, Count Cagliostro (Giuseppe Balsamo, 1743–1795), the most notorious occult adventurer of the mesmeric age (Rijksmuseum).

## Iconography
A bust-length portrait of Cagliostro, presented with the dignity of a man of mystery and learning — the standard engraved likeness that spread his fame across Europe.

## Significance
Though not a magnetizer himself, [[motif:cagliostro|Cagliostro]] was the era's paradigmatic [[motif:magus|magus]]: healer, alchemist, founder of an 'Egyptian' rite of Freemasonry, and purveyor of elixirs and prophecy. He moved through the same fashionable, occult-hungry salons that took up [[motif:animal magnetism|animal magnetism]], and his spectacular rise and disgrace (entangled in the Affair of the Diamond Necklace, dying in a papal prison) made him the symbol of Enlightenment-age occult charlatanry.

## For artists & game designers
The travelling-mystagogue archetype: a charismatic foreign 'count' trailing rumours of immortality, secret rites, and miraculous cures.

citations.text: Iain McCalman, *The Last Alchemist: Count Cagliostro, Master of Magic in the Age of Reason* (2003).

title: Charcot Hysteria Chronophotography (Salpêtrière)
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: After Jean-Martin Charcot (Salpêtrière school)
summary:
A chronophotographic study of hysteria from the circle of Jean-Martin Charcot at the Salpêtrière, where hypnosis and the convulsive 'grand hysteria' were photographed as clinical phenomena.

## Iconography
A sequence or single frame capturing a patient in the postures of the hysterical attack — the contorted, arching body that Charcot's school catalogued in serial images, the heir to the older convulsive [[motif:magnetic crisis|crisis]].

## Significance
In the 1880s Charcot revived hypnotism as a neurological subject, staging hypnotic and hysterical states before audiences and recording them with the new clinical [[motif:clinical photography|photography]]. His celebrated demonstrations — the direct line from Mesmer's crisis through Braid's nervous sleep — shaped the young Freud and made the Salpêtrière the theatre where mind, suggestion, and the body's spectacle converged.
