## For artists & game designers
A clinical-spectacle reference: the medical theatre where an altered state is exhibited and documented, blurring science and performance.

citations.text: Georges Didi-Huberman, *Invention of Hysteria: Charcot and the Photographic Iconography of the Salpêtrière* (1982; Eng. 2003).

title: Portraits of the Family of Johann Caspar Lavater
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A sheet of portraits of the family of Johann Caspar Lavater (1741–1801), the Zurich pastor whose *Physiognomy* made the reading of character from the face a science of the age (Zentralbibliothek Zürich).

## Iconography
A strip of profile likenesses of the Lavater family, the format echoing the silhouette and profile studies that filled his physiognomical works, where the line of brow, nose, and chin was scrutinised for the soul within.

## Significance
[[motif:johann caspar lavater|Lavater]]'s *Physiognomische Fragmente* (1775–78) systematised [[motif:physiognomy|physiognomy]] — the inference of inner character from outer form — and made it a craze parallel to mesmerism in the same decades. A friend of the sympathetic and 'magnetic' currents of the time, he lent the era's fascination with invisible forces a respectable, sentimental face. Physiognomy and phrenology together formed the broader 'science of the invisible self' around animal magnetism.

## For artists & game designers
A face-reading mechanic: profile portraits whose features the player learns to interpret for hidden traits, allegiance, or fate.

citations.text: Johann Caspar Lavater, *Physiognomische Fragmente zur Beförderung der Menschenkenntniß und Menschenliebe* (1775–78).

title: Le magnétisme animal, Mesmer ou les sots (Fleuron)
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Louis-Nicolas Mareschal
summary:
An engraved vignette (fleuron) from *Le magnétisme animal, Mesmer ou les sots* — 'Animal Magnetism, Mesmer or the Fools' — by Louis-Nicolas Mareschal.

## Iconography
A small printer's headpiece ornament from the title-matter of a satirical pamphlet, the kind of engraved fleuron set above the text. The very title yokes Mesmer to 'les sots', the fools who follow him.

## Significance
The pamphlet war over magnetism produced not only large caricatures but a stream of cheap printed satire whose [[motif:fleuron|ornamental vignettes]] carried the mockery into the book itself. The title *Mesmer ou les sots* exemplifies the literary [[motif:satire of magnetism|satire]] that, alongside the royal verdict, framed mesmerism as the gulling of the [[motif:credulity|credulous]].

## For artists & game designers
A period typographic asset: an authentic engraved ornament for mocking in-world 'pamphlets' that ridicule a charlatan's movement.

citations.text: Robert Darnton, *Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France* (1968).

title: The Magnetic Pass, the Mesmeric Sleep
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A nineteenth-century print depicting the magnetic pass inducing the 'Mesmeric sleep', the artificial somnambulic state (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The operator makes a downward pass before the face of a subject who has sunk, eyes closed, into trance. The composition isolates the moment of transition from waking to the lucid magnetic sleep.

## Significance
The '[[motif:mesmeric sleep|magnetic sleep]]' or [[motif:somnambulism|artificial somnambulism]] was the deeper state that displaced the early convulsive crisis as the prized phenomenon of magnetism. In it subjects appeared asleep yet responsive, sometimes credited with clairvoyance or insensibility to pain. This 'lucid sleep' is the direct ancestor of the hypnotic trance that Braid would later rename.

## For artists & game designers
A trance-induction beat: the precise gesture and the subject's collapse into a controllable sleep state — a mechanic for charm or interrogation.

citations.text: Alison Winter, *Mesmerized: Powers of Mind in Victorian Britain* (1998).

title: Les Effets du Magnétisme animal (French Satire)
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous (French printmaker)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A French satirical print, *Les Effets du Magnétisme animal*, lampooning the fashionable craze for animal magnetism (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
A crowded, comic scene of magnetism's 'effects': sitters around a tub, some swooning, others making free under cover of the trance, the operator presiding over a room of theatrical excess. The crisis is played for ridicule rather than awe.

## Significance
The magnetic vogue drew a flood of [[motif:caricature|caricature]] that mocked the [[motif:credulity|credulity]] of fashionable Paris and hinted broadly at the erotic licence the darkened séances afforded. Such satires, sharpened after the 1784 royal commissions ruled the fluid imaginary, made mesmerism a byword for fraud and folly even as the practice spread.

## For artists & game designers
A comic-relief tableau: the same ritual rendered as farce, exposing the gap between the operator's claims and what the crowd is really up to.

citations.text: Robert Darnton, *Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France* (1968).

title: De magnetiseur (The Magnetizer), Jan Jozef Verhaghen
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Jan Jozef Verhaghen (1726–1795)
summary:
*De magnetiseur* (The Magnetizer) by the Flemish painter Jan Jozef Verhaghen, a genre depiction of a magnetic operator and his subject.

## Iconography
A painted interior in which a magnetizer attends a seated figure, hands engaged in the work of the passes, observers looking on. The treatment is that of eighteenth-century genre painting, lending the new practice the intimacy of a domestic scene.

## Significance
That a Flemish painter took the [[motif:magnetizer|magnetizer]] as a genre subject shows how quickly animal magnetism entered the visual culture of the Low Countries and German lands as well as France. The work records the magnetic encounter not as caricature but as an everyday act of healing, the operator bending his will and hands to the transfer of the [[motif:invisible fluid|fluid]].

## For artists & game designers
A warmly lit reference for a 'magnetic consultation' scene — useful when the mood wanted is healing and intimacy rather than spectacle or fraud.

citations.text: Robert Darnton, *Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France* (1968).

title: Portrait of Franz Anton Mesmer
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A portrait of Franz Anton Mesmer (1734–1815), the Viennese physician whose theory of 'animal magnetism' gave its name to mesmerism.

## Iconography
A half-length likeness of a gentleman-physician of the Enlightenment, in powdered wig and formal coat, gazing out with composed authority. The print is the standard frontispiece type by which Mesmer was circulated to a curious public.

## Significance
Mesmer proposed that an invisible, magnetic-like [[motif:invisible fluid|fluid]] pervaded all bodies and that illness arose from its obstruction; the healer could redirect it by 'magnetizing' the patient. Trained in Vienna and famous in 1780s Paris, he became the storm-centre of a craze that two royal commissions (1784) investigated and dismissed. Whatever the verdict on the fluid, his practice opened the road from magnetic 'crisis' to somnambulism, suggestion, and ultimately hypnosis.

## For artists & game designers
A charismatic-doctor archetype: the authoritative healer whose 'cures' blur the line between medicine, theatre, and the occult.

citations.text: Robert Darnton, *Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France* (1968).

title: Le Mesmérisme Confondu (Caricature)
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous (French printmaker)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Mesmérisme Confondu* ('Mesmerism Confounded'), a caricature celebrating the discrediting of animal magnetism (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
A satirical composition in which the apparatus and adepts of magnetism are thrown into disarray, the print's title announcing the rout of the doctrine. Figures scatter from the tub as the pretensions of the magnetizers collapse.

## Significance
The royal commissions of 1784 — one including Benjamin Franklin, Lavoisier, and the physician Guillotin — tested magnetism by blinded trials and concluded its effects came from [[motif:credulity|imagination]], not any fluid. Prints like '[[motif:mesmerism confounded|Le Mesmérisme Confondu]]' turned that verdict into popular ridicule, a landmark in the history of placebo and of debunking by controlled experiment.

## For artists & game designers
A 'reveal' panel: the moment a celebrated power is publicly exposed, the believers fleeing as the apparatus is shown to be empty.

citations.text: Robert Darnton, *Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France* (1968).

title: The Basic Elements of Phrenology, Physiognomy and Palmistry
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A composite chart of 'the basic elements of phrenology, physiognomy and palmistry', the popular sciences of reading character from head, face, and hand (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The plate gathers a mapped [[motif:phrenological head|phrenological head]] partitioned into numbered organs of the faculties, physiognomic faces, and a palm divided into its lines and mounts — a one-sheet compendium of the body read as a text.

## Significance
[[motif:phrenology|Phrenology]], devised by Gall and popularised by Spurzheim and the Fowlers, mapped mental faculties onto bumps of the skull, and readily fused with mesmerism as 'phreno-magnetism' — magnetizing a phrenological organ to excite its faculty in a sensitive subject. Together with [[motif:physiognomy|physiognomy]] and palmistry it formed the nineteenth century's confident art of reading the invisible self from visible signs.

## For artists & game designers
A character-stat overlay: a head or hand divided into labelled regions, each tappable to reveal a personality 'faculty' the player can probe.

citations.text: Roger Cooter, *The Cultural Meaning of Popular Science: Phrenology and the Organization of Consent in Nineteenth-Century Britain* (1984).

title: Investigating the Od Rays (Reichenbach's Sensitive)
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Max Nentwich
summary:
An illustration of an experimenter investigating the 'Od rays' with a silver spoon and a gold ring suspended on a thread, after the work of Baron Carl von Reichenbach.

## Iconography
A figure conducts a test of radiation, holding a silver spoon and a gold ring hung from a thread as a kind of pendulum — instruments by which a 'sensitive' was thought to detect emanations from metals and bodies.

## Significance
The industrial chemist Baron Carl von [[motif:reichenbach experiment|Reichenbach]] (1788–1869) proposed an [[motif:odic force|odic force]] (the 'Od'), a vital emanation streaming from magnets, crystals, metals, and living things, visible as a glow to nervous '[[motif:sensitive subject|sensitives]]' in a darkened room. A late, would-be-scientific descendant of Mesmer's fluid, the Od became a touchstone for theosophy and psychical research even as mainstream science rejected it.

## For artists & game designers
A 'detect aura' mechanic: special-sensitive characters who perceive otherwise invisible emanations from objects, revealing hidden properties.

citations.text: Carl von Reichenbach, *Researches on Magnetism, Electricity ... in Relation to Vital Force* (Eng. trans. 1850).

title: Essai sur la théorie du somnambulisme magnétique (Fleuron)
work: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: Tardy de Montravel (T. D. M.)
summary:
An engraved vignette from the *Essai sur la théorie du somnambulisme magnétique* (1785) by Tardy de Montravel, an early theoretical treatise on magnetic somnambulism.

## Iconography
A printed title-page fleuron from a key text of the magnetic literature, the small ornament marking a serious theoretical essay rather than a satire.

## Significance
In 1784 the Marquis de [[motif:puysegur school|Puységur]] discovered that magnetism could induce, instead of convulsions, a calm 'artificial [[motif:somnambulism|somnambulism]]' in which the subject seemed asleep yet lucid. Followers like Tardy de Montravel theorised this lucid sleep and its supposed clairvoyance; this 1785 *Essai* is a document of that pivotal shift from the crisis to the somnambulic trance that reoriented the whole movement.

## For artists & game designers
A 'source text' prop: a credible period treatise whose title alone establishes the in-world science of the lucid magnetic sleep.

citations.text: Adam Crabtree, *From Mesmer to Freud: Magnetic Sleep and the Roots of Psychological Healing* (1993).

title: Codex Borbonicus (Complete Screenfold)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Aztec / Nahua tlacuilo)
date: c. 1500–1525 CE (very early colonial / late pre-Conquest)
summary:
A composite reproduction of the complete *Codex Borbonicus*, the great Aztec ritual-calendar screenfold held in the library of the French National Assembly (Palais Bourbon).

## Iconography
The long folded strip opens with a *tonalamatl* of twenty trecenas, each large compartment ruled by patron deities and the Lords of the Day and Night, followed by sections on the 52-year cycle, the New Fire ceremony, and the eighteen monthly (*veintena*) festivals.

## Significance
The Borbonicus is arguably the finest single Aztec manuscript, painted in pure native style on bark paper just before or just after the Conquest. Its [[motif:tonalamatl|tonalamatl]] is the canonical example used to explain how diviners read the 260-day count, and its New Fire scene documents the *toxiuhmolpilia*, the binding of the years every 52 years. Studied by Francisco del Paso y Troncoso and, more recently, Elizabeth Hill Boone.

## For artists & game designers
A single artifact that scrolls from a fate-almanac into a festival calendar is a model for an in-game "living" ritual book.

citations.text: Elizabeth Hill Boone, *Cycles of Time and Meaning in the Mexican Books of Fate* (2007).

title: Codex Borgia, Page 20 (Tonalpohualli Almanac)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican / Nahua-Mixtec tlacuilo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (facsimile rendering, Chaccard). Underlying codex public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A page from the *Codex Borgia*, the most splendid surviving Mesoamerican divinatory book, named for Cardinal Stefano Borgia and now in the Vatican Library.

## Iconography
The register packs the bar-and-dot numbers and twenty day-sign glyphs of the *tonalpohualli* into compartments, each ruled by a deity figure rendered in the flat, outlined polychrome "Mixteca-Puebla" style. Day signs such as Crocodile (Cipactli), Wind (Ehecatl) and Death (Miquiztli) interlock with patron gods and directional colours.

## Significance
The Borgia is the eponym of the "[[motif:tonalpohualli|Borgia Group]]" of pre-Conquest ritual screenfolds. Its almanacs let a diviner (*tonalpouhqui*) read the fate of a day, a birth, or an undertaking from the 260-day count, cross-tabulated with the nine Lords of the Night and the directional trees. Eduard Seler's monumental commentary (1904–09) remains foundational; Elizabeth Hill Boone has reframed these books as "narratives of fate."

## For artists & game designers
The compartment grid of day-glyph × deity × direction is a ready-made procedural lookup table for a divination minigame.

citations.text: Elizabeth Hill Boone, *Cycles of Time and Meaning in the Mexican Books of Fate* (2007).

title: Codex Borgia, Page 56 (Quetzalcoatl)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
summary:
A page from the central "narrative" section of the *Codex Borgia* featuring Quetzalcoatl, the Feathered Serpent.

## Iconography
The Feathered Serpent appears in his Ehecatl-Quetzalcoatl aspect, marked by the buccal mask of the wind god and a conch-shell "wind jewel" (*ehecailacacozcatl*). Plumed serpent forms, ritual paraphernalia and offerings fill the surrounding field in saturated reds, ochres and turquoise.

## Significance
Pages 29–46 of the Borgia form a celebrated cosmological cycle, and Quetzalcoatl recurs throughout as creator, wind, and Venus. As [[motif:quetzalcoatl|Quetzalcoatl]] he is the priest-god of self-sacrifice and the planet Venus; as Ehecatl he sweeps the roads for the rain gods. The ambiguous, much-debated "narrative" of these pages may depict a night-sea journey or a New Fire / Venus-related rite, as argued by Karl Anton Nowotny and later by Boone.

## For artists & game designers
A single deity with stacked aspects — wind, serpent, morning star, culture-hero — is a model for a shapeshifting patron god.

citations.text: Karl Anton Nowotny, *Tlacuilolli: Style and Contents of the Mexican Pictorial Manuscripts* (1961; Eng. 2005).

title: Codex Cospi (Bologna Screenfold)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
summary:
A view of the *Codex Cospi*, a pre-Conquest divinatory screenfold of the Borgia Group, preserved in the University Library of Bologna.

## Iconography
The deer-hide strip is painted on both sides with *tonalpohualli* almanacs: rows of day-signs and dots framed by directional deities and offerings, with one face given over to a Venus-related and ritual sequence.

## Significance
Cospi (formerly Codex Bologna) is one of the five members of the [[motif:tonalpohualli|Borgia Group]] and one of the very few Mesoamerican books to reach Europe in the sixteenth century, entering the collection of Marchese Ferdinando Cospi in 1665. Its almanacs and Venus pages parallel those of the Borgia and Vaticanus B, helping scholars reconstruct a shared Central Mexican divinatory tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A double-sided ritual strip — fate on one face, a planet-god cycle on the other — is a strong concept for a physical in-game relic.

title: Codex Laud, Folio 43
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
summary:
Folio 43 of the *Codex Laud*, a pre-Conquest divinatory screenfold of the Borgia Group, now in the Bodleian Library, Oxford.

## Iconography
Deity figures and day-signs are arranged in vivid compartments against bands of directional colour; death-and-fertility imagery (skeletal and life-giving gods paired) characterises several of the Laud's almanacs, including marriage and offering prognostications.

## Significance
Named for Archbishop William Laud, who gave it to the Bodleian in the 1630s, the Laud is a core member of the [[motif:tonalpohualli|Borgia Group]]. Its almanacs treat themes such as the fate of marriages and the pairing of death-gods with life-gods, illustrating how the 260-day count structured the most intimate life decisions. The reconstruction of its provenance and content owes much to C. A. Burland and Ferdinand Anders.

## For artists & game designers
Paired death/fertility gods governing marriage omens make an elegant dual-aspect card set for a fate deck.

title: Codex Ríos (Vaticanus A), Day-Sign Malinalli
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (indigenous painters, colonial Mexico)
place: Mexico / Italy (compiled in New Spain, completed in Italy)
summary:
The day-sign *Malinalli* ("Grass" / twisted grass) as drawn in the *Codex Ríos*, also called *Codex Vaticanus A* (Vatican Library, Vat. lat. 3738).

## Iconography
The twelfth of the twenty day-signs is shown as a stylised skull-and-grass glyph: a death's-head sprouting the curling blades of *malinalli* grass, the emblem of decay turning to new growth.

## Significance
The Ríos codex is an early-colonial encyclopedic manuscript, copied under Dominican supervision (associated with Fray Pedro de los Ríos), that glosses Aztec cosmology, the [[motif:tonalpohualli|day-count]], the heavens and the underworld in Italian. As a glossed copy it is invaluable for decoding pre-Conquest iconography, even as its colonial framing reinterprets it. Malinalli, ruled by Patecatl, was read as an unlucky, death-tinged sign.

## For artists & game designers
A "grass-from-a-skull" glyph is a compact death-and-rebirth icon for a calendar or tarot-style card.

citations.text: Ferdinand Anders & Maarten Jansen, *Religión, costumbres e historia de los antiguos mexicanos* (Codex Vaticanus A facsimile, 1996).

title: Codex Vaticanus B (3773), Page 24
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
summary:
A page from the *Codex Vaticanus B* (Vatican Library, Vat. lat. 3773), a pre-Conquest divinatory screenfold of the Borgia Group.

## Iconography
Dense compartments arrange the day-signs, numerical coefficients and presiding deities of the *tonalpohualli*, with directional and color schemes guiding the diviner's reading. The drawing is more abbreviated and schematic than the Borgia's, prioritising calculational clarity.

## Significance
Vaticanus B is one of the five core members of the [[motif:tonalpohualli|Borgia Group]] (with Borgia, Cospi, Laud and Fejérváry-Mayer). Its almanacs are unusually systematic, making it a key witness for reconstructing how Central Mexican diviners combined the 260-day count with the directions, the Lords of the Night, and Venus. Eduard Seler produced the standard early commentary (1902–03).

## For artists & game designers
The terse, calculational layout is a good reference for designing a readable in-game oracle table that still feels arcane.

title: Dresden Codex — Page with the Month Kankin
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Maya scribes)
summary:
A detail from the *Dresden Codex* featuring the glyph for the month *Kankin*, part of the Maya calendrical apparatus.

## Iconography
A framed Maya hieroglyphic block names the Haab month Kankin, set among the bar-and-dot numerals and almanac columns by which the codex's scribes laid out divinatory and astronomical tables.

## Significance
The Dresden Codex interweaves the 260-day *tzolkin* and the 365-day *haab* (whose eighteen named "months" include Kankin) into its almanacs and astronomical tables, including the Venus and eclipse cycles. Such month glyphs anchored omens and rituals to specific points in the solar year. The codex's calendrics were decoded by Ernst Förstemann, librarian at Dresden, whose work founded modern Maya epigraphy.

## For artists & game designers
Named "month" sigils that gate which events can occur are a tidy way to give an in-game calendar texture and lore.

citations.text: Michael D. Coe, *Breaking the Maya Code* (1992).

title: Dresden Codex — Venus Table (Facsimile)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Maya scribes); facsimile by Lacambalam
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (facsimile, Lacambalam). Underlying codex public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A facsimile of the famous Venus pages of the *Dresden Codex*, the finest surviving Maya book, held in the Saxon State Library (SLUB) in Dresden.

## Iconography
Columns of bar-and-dot numbers, Maya glyphs and almanac tables track the 584-day synodic cycle of Venus across its phases, accompanied by deity figures hurling spears — Venus as a malign spear-thrower whose heliacal rising threatened rulers, captives and crops.

## Significance
The Dresden [[motif:venus table|Venus table]] is the most celebrated piece of pre-Columbian astronomy, correlating Venus's appearances with the 260-day *tzolkin* to issue omens. It encodes a strikingly accurate long-term Venus calendar with correction mechanisms. Studied by Ernst Förstemann (who first cracked its numeration in the 1880s), Floyd Lounsbury and others, it proves that Maya divination was inseparable from precise astronomy.

## For artists & game designers
A planet that returns on a fixed clock to deal omen-driven damage is a clean astronomical event mechanic.

citations.text: J. Eric S. Thompson, *A Commentary on the Dresden Codex* (1972).

title: Codex Fejérváry-Mayer, Page 11 (Centeotl)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
place: Central Mexico (possibly the Gulf Coast / Mixteca)
summary:
A detail from page 11 of the *Codex Fejérváry-Mayer* showing the maize god Centeotl (Cinteotl), now in the World Museum, Liverpool.

## Iconography
The young maize deity is shown seated, painted with the facial markings and headdress associated with the patron of sustenance, set within a compartment of the divinatory count with its accompanying day-signs and colour fields.

## Significance
The Fejérváry-Mayer is a small, finely painted member of the [[motif:tonalamatl|Borgia Group]], best known for its opening cosmogram of the four directions and the world-tree calendar. Its interior pages, like this one, present *tonalamatl* almanacs assigning patron gods such as Centeotl — god of maize and human sustenance — to segments of the 260-day count, tying agricultural fertility to ritual time.

## For artists & game designers
A crop-god slotted into a calendar wheel ties planting cycles to a fate mechanic — fertility as a turn-based resource.

title: The Maya Codices (Madrid / Tro-Cortesianus)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Maya scribes)
summary:
A display of Maya codex pages including the *Madrid Codex* (Codex Tro-Cortesianus), the longest of the surviving Maya books, held in the Museo de América, Madrid.

## Iconography
The screenfold pages carry dense almanacs: rows of *tzolkin* day-glyphs and red-and-black numerals threaded between vignettes of gods sowing, weaving, hunting with snares, and tending hives, each scene tied to favourable or unfavourable days.

## Significance
The Madrid Codex is the great compendium of everyday Maya [[motif:divinatory almanac|divination]]: its almanacs govern agriculture, rain-making, beekeeping, hunting, weaving and disease, mapping the practical concerns of life onto the 260-day count. Reassembled from two fragments (Troano and Cortesianus), it complements the astronomical Dresden Codex by showing divination at the village level. Studied by Victoria and Harvey Bricker, among others.

## For artists & game designers
An almanac of "good and bad days" for each craft is a ready loot/luck system tied to an in-world calendar.

citations.text: Gabrielle Vail & Anthony Aveni (eds.), *The Madrid Codex: New Approaches* (2004).

title: Mictlantecuhtli, Aztec Lord of the Dead (Ceramic)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Mexica / Aztec sculptors)
rights: CC0 (photograph, Gary Todd). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A ceramic figure of Mictlantecuhtli, the Aztec lord of the underworld, of the type recovered from the House of the Eagles at the Templo Mayor, Tenochtitlan.

## Iconography
The god stands as a partly skeletal, fleshless figure with a grinning skull, clawed hands, and a liver or other organ spilling from the rib cage — the liver being the seat of the *ihíyotl* soul, drawn to the underworld. Curly hair set with paper rosettes and starry eyes complete the death-lord regalia.

## Significance
[[motif:mictlantecuhtli|Mictlantecuhtli]] ruled Mictlan, the lowest of nine underworlds, where the ordinary dead journeyed for four years. He governs the day-sign Itzcuintli (Dog) and one of the trecenas, and in the *Codex Borgia* he appears back-to-back with Quetzalcoatl in a cosmic life/death pairing. He embodies the Aztec conviction that death and regeneration are inseparable.

## For artists & game designers
A death-god with an exposed liver — the soul-organ — is a vivid, anatomically specific design for an underworld boss.

citations.text: Leonardo López Luján, *The Offerings of the Templo Mayor of Tenochtitlan* (1994).

title: The Aztec Sun Stone (Calendar Stone)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Mexica / Aztec sculptors)
rights: CC0 (photograph, Gary Todd). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *Piedra del Sol* (Sun Stone or Calendar Stone), the most famous Aztec monument, a 3.6 m basalt monolith now in the Museo Nacional de Antropología, Mexico City.

## Iconography
A central face — usually identified as the sun god Tonatiuh (or the earth-monster Tlaltecuhtli) — is framed by the glyph *Nahui Ollin* ("4 Movement"), whose four cartouches name the four previous world-ages destroyed by jaguars, wind, fire-rain and flood. A ring of the twenty day-signs encircles the center, bounded by two Fire Serpents (Xiuhcoatl) meeting at the base.

## Significance
The Sun Stone is a cosmogram of the [[motif:five suns|Five Suns]]: the present age, *Nahui Ollin*, is fated to end in earthquakes. It binds the 260-day count, the solar cycle and the myth of cosmic destruction into one carved disk, probably set horizontally as a sacrificial altar or *temalacatl*. It is the single most reproduced emblem of Aztec religion.

## For artists & game designers
A wheel that encodes both a calendar and a prophecy of the world's end is an iconic doomsday-clock motif.

citations.text: Khristaan D. Villela & Mary Ellen Miller (eds.), *The Aztec Calendar Stone* (2010).

title: Tlazolteotl, Eater of Filth (Codex Borbonicus)
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (Aztec / Nahua tlacuilo)
date: c. 1500–1525 CE (very early colonial / late pre-Conquest)
summary:
The goddess Tlazolteotl as depicted in the *Codex Borbonicus*, patroness of one of the twenty trecenas of the divinatory count.

## Iconography
The goddess wears the unspun-cotton headband and spindle-whorl headdress of the spinners and weavers, with black mouth-paint marking her as an "eater of filth." She is shown in the regalia of the *tonalamatl* compartment she governs.

## Significance
[[motif:tlazolteotl|Tlazolteotl]] ("filth deity"), also called Tlaelcuani, "eater of filth," both incited and absolved sexual transgression: she devoured human misdeeds, and a once-in-a-lifetime confession (*neyolmelahualiztli*) to her priests could wipe a person's offenses clean. Goddess of weaving, childbirth, lust and purification, she ruled the trecena beginning 1 Deer and the thirteenth day-sign, binding moral pollution to the diviner's calendar.

## For artists & game designers
A deity who is both the source of sin and its one-time absolution is a rich quest-giver for a confession or atonement mechanic.

citations.text: H. B. Nicholson, "Religion in Pre-Hispanic Central Mexico," *Handbook of Middle American Indians* vol. 10 (1971).

title: Tzitzimitl, Aztec Star Demon
work: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: Anonymous (indigenous painter, colonial Mexico)
summary:
A *tzitzimitl*, one of the Aztec star-demons, drawn in a colonial-period manuscript of the type of the *Codex Magliabechiano*.

## Iconography
The figure is a fearsome skeletal female with clawed hands and feet, a skirt of crossed bones, and severed-hand and heart ornaments. Such star-demons are shown frontally in an aggressive, descending posture.

## Significance
The [[motif:tzitzimitl|tzitzimime]] were stars personified as monstrous beings, associated with the goddess group around Cihuacoatl and Itzpapalotl. They were feared above all during solar eclipses and at the close of the 52-year cycle, when, if the New Fire failed to kindle, they were believed to descend and devour humankind. They thus express the Aztec dread of cosmic darkness and the precariousness of the world-age — the danger the New Fire ceremony was meant to avert.

## For artists & game designers
Sky-monsters that descend only during eclipses or a failed ritual make a perfect timed apocalyptic threat.

citations.text: Cecelia F. Klein, "The Devil and the Skirt: An Iconographic Inquiry into the Pre-Hispanic Nature of the Tzitzimime," *Ancient Mesoamerica* 11 (2000).

title: Seated Cybele / Magna Mater (Ny Carlsberg Glyptotek)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Marie-Lan Nguyen
place: Formiae, Italy; Ny Carlsberg Glyptotek, Copenhagen
summary:
A Roman statue of the enthroned Cybele, the Phrygian Great Mother (Magna Mater), flanked by her lions.

## Iconography
**Cybele** sits enthroned, wearing the **mural crown** (a turreted city-wall diadem) that makes her protector of cities, with a **tympanum** (frame-drum) at her side and **lions** at her feet or in her lap. The drum and lions evoke her ecstatic, wild mountain worship.

## Significance
The cult of **Magna Mater** was formally brought to Rome from Pessinus in 204 BCE on the advice of the Sibylline Books, the first oriental mystery cult officially installed in the city. Her self-castrated priests, the **Galli**, and the spring festival of the dying-and-rising consort **Attis** belonged to her mysteries, which included the bloody [[motif:taurobolium|taurobolium]]. She embodies the earth's fertility and the terror of untamed nature.

## For artists & game designers
The turret-crowned mother on a lion throne is an instantly readable 'great goddess / city-protector' archetype — civic power fused with feral wildness.

citations.text: M. J. Vermaseren, *Cybele and Attis: The Myth and the Cult* (1977).
L. E. Roller, *In Search of God the Mother: The Cult of Anatolian Cybele* (1999).

title: Dionysus and his thiasos — engraving after antique sources
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Engraving from a classical dictionary (1894), after antique models
rights: Public domain (no restrictions). Internet Archive Book Images, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A 19th-century engraving of Dionysus and his ecstatic following, illustrating the imagery of the Bacchic mysteries as transmitted to modern scholarship.

## Iconography
**Dionysus** appears amid his *thiasos* — the riotous band of **maenads** brandishing the **thyrsus** (the pine-cone-tipped fennel wand) and **satyrs** with wineskins and pipes. The vine, ivy crown, and dancing abandon mark the god of wine, ecstasy, and dissolution of the self.

## Significance
The Bacchic or **Dionysiac mysteries** promised their initiates a blessed afterlife through ritual union with the dying-and-rising god, and overlapped with [[motif:dionysiac-mysteries|Orphic]] eschatology. This printed image is a piece of *reception*: the means by which 19th-century antiquarians (such as the compilers of Smith's classical dictionaries) standardized and circulated ancient iconography for a wide readership.

## For artists & game designers
A compact reference for the 'ecstatic revel' party: god, wand-bearing dancers, and pipe-playing satyrs — the visual grammar of divine intoxication.

citations.text: W. Smith, *A Classical Dictionary of Greek and Roman Biography, Mythology and Geography* (rev. 1894).

title: Eleusinian votive relief — Demeter, Persephone and Triptolemos
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Greek, Attic)
summary:
An Attic votive relief from Eleusis showing Demeter, Persephone, and the hero Triptolemos — the divine triad at the heart of the Eleusinian Mysteries.

## Iconography
**Demeter** hands ears of **grain** to the youth **Triptolemos**, charging him to carry agriculture to humankind, while her daughter **Persephone** (Kore) stands by holding a torch. The composition echoes the celebrated Great Eleusinian Relief; the gift of grain stands for the gift of the [[motif:eleusinian-mysteries|mysteries]] themselves.

## Significance
The **Eleusinian Mysteries**, celebrated for nearly a thousand years, re-enacted Demeter's loss and recovery of Persephone, who spends part of the year below and part above — the myth of the seed that dies and returns. Initiates (*mystai*) who beheld the secret 'things shown' in the Telesterion were promised a better lot after death. The standard study is George Mylonas's *Eleusis and the Eleusinian Mysteries* (1961).

## For artists & game designers
The triad of grieving mother, returning daughter, and grain-bearing hero is a complete 'death-and-renewal' tableau — fertility myth condensed to three figures and a sheaf.

citations.text: G. E. Mylonas, *Eleusis and the Eleusinian Mysteries* (1961).

title: Statue of Isis from Hadrian's Villa (Musei Capitolini)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Daderot
rights: Public domain. Musei Capitolini, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Hadrianic marble statue of Isis from Hadrian's Villa at Tivoli, displaying the standard attributes of the goddess and her priestesses in Isiac procession.

## Iconography
The goddess wears the fringed mantle knotted between the breasts in the distinctive **Isis knot** (*nodus*), and her cult is identified by the attributes carried in Isiac rites: the **sistrum** (the sacred rattle) and the **situla** (the water-bucket holding Nile water). The half-Egyptian, half-Greek styling is typical of Roman images of [[motif:isis|Isis]].

## Significance
Apuleius (*Metamorphoses* XI) describes the great Isiac **procession**, the *Navigium Isidis*, in which votaries bore exactly these implements — sistra rattling, lamps and water-vessels carried — before the image of the saving goddess. Statues such as this stood in the Iseum and were paraded or imitated by her white-robed devotees. The Isiac cult offered personal salvation and an initiation 'voluntary death and rebirth by grace'.

## For artists & game designers
The knotted robe, rattle, and water-pail are a tight attribute-set that turns any draped figure into a recognizable priestess of a salvation cult.

citations.text: R. E. Witt, *Isis in the Ancient World* (1971).

title: Statue from the Sanctuary of Isis at Marathon
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by George E. Koronaios
place: Sanctuary of Isis, Brexiza, Marathon, Greece
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: George E. Koronaios). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An Egyptianizing cult statue from the Sanctuary of Isis at Brexiza near Marathon, part of Herodes Atticus's Isiac complex.

## Iconography
The over-life-size figure in **Egyptianizing** style guards the gate of the sanctuary, stiffly frontal in the pharaonic manner yet carved in Roman-period marble — a deliberate archaism marking the cult's Egyptian origin. Such statues attended the goddess **Isis** at the entrances of her temples.

## Significance
The cult of [[motif:isis|Isis]] was the most successful of the imported mystery religions, spreading from Ptolemaic Egypt across the whole Roman world. Apuleius's *Metamorphoses* XI gives the one detailed ancient account of an Isiac initiation. The Brexiza sanctuary, built in the 2nd century CE, shows how wealthy patrons recreated Egypt in miniature for the Greek-speaking faithful.

## For artists & game designers
A deliberately 'foreign and ancient' guardian statue at a temple gate — useful for signalling an exotic, imported cult within a familiar world.

citations.text: R. E. Witt, *Isis in the Ancient World* (1971).

title: Lion-headed Aion / Chronos — Mithraic relief (19th-c. drawing)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Unknown 19th-c. draughtsman (perhaps Félix Lajard), after a Roman relief
date: 19th-c. drawing after a Roman relief (2nd–3rd c. CE original)
summary:
A 19th-century drawing recording a Roman Mithraic relief of the lion-headed god, the enigmatic *leontocephaline* who personifies cosmic Time.

## Iconography
The **leontocephaline** stands frontally, naked and winged, his human body wound about by a great **serpent** whose head rests on his lion's skull. He grips **keys** and a sceptre, and the signs of the **zodiac** mark his body or the egg-shaped frame around him. The lion mouth, sometimes shown breathing fire, and the spiralling snake identify him as **Aion / Zurvan**, deified Time.

## Significance
The lion-headed figure is the second great image of the [[motif:mithraeum|Mithraic]] sanctuaries after the [[motif:tauroctony|tauroctony]], and the most debated. He has been read as Aion (boundless Time), the Iranian Zurvan, or a cosmic boundary-keeper holding the keys of the heavens. Cumont gathered the corpus; H. von Gall and R. L. Gordon refined its interpretation.

## For artists & game designers
The serpent-wound, key-bearing lion-man is a superb 'lord of time / gatekeeper of the cosmos' design with deep ancient pedigree.

citations.text: F. Cumont, *Textes et monuments figurés relatifs aux mystères de Mithra* (1896–99).
H. von Gall, 'The Lion-headed and the Human-headed God in the Mithraic Mysteries' (1978).

title: The Lovatelli urn — Eleusinian initiation of Herakles (drawing)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Drawing after the Roman cinerary urn (Lovatelli collection)
date: drawing after a Roman urn (1st c. CE / Augustan)
summary:
A drawing of the relief on the Lovatelli urn, the rare ancient image that actually shows the preliminary rites of Eleusinian initiation.

## Iconography
In three episodes, **Herakles** is initiated: he is purified with a sacrificed **piglet** held over his head, then **veiled** and seated on a fleece-covered stool while a priestess holds a **winnowing fan** (*liknon*) above him, and finally approaches **Demeter** enthroned with her snake. The sequence depicts the purification and *myesis* that preceded the great mystery.

## Significance
The **Lovatelli urn** (with the related Torre Nova sarcophagus) is one of the very few monuments to illustrate the otherwise secret machinery of the [[motif:eleusinian-mysteries|Eleusinian Mysteries]] — the piglet sacrifice, the veiling, the *liknon* of Dionysus. Mythically, Herakles' initiation justified the admission of non-Athenians; art-historically, it gives modern scholars their clearest glimpse of the rites' opening stages.

## For artists & game designers
A stepwise initiation sequence — wash, blindfold, present the sacred basket, meet the goddess — that reads as a built-in ritual tutorial.

citations.text: G. E. Mylonas, *Eleusis and the Eleusinian Mysteries* (1961).
K. Clinton, *Myth and Cult: The Iconography of the Eleusinian Mysteries* (1992).

title: Marino mithraeum — painted tauroctony fresco
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman)
summary:
The painted tauroctony of the Marino mithraeum near Rome, one of the few Mithraic cult-images rendered in fresco rather than carved stone.

## Iconography
Mithras in his red **Phrygian cap** and fluttering cloak slays the white **bull** at the centre, flanked by the torchbearers and framed by small **side panels** that narrate episodes of the Mithras-myth — the rock-birth, the water miracle, the hunt, and the banquet with Sol. The colour preserves details usually lost in stone reliefs.

## Significance
Discovered in 1962, the Marino fresco is prized because painting records the **polychromy** and the narrative side-scenes that flank the [[motif:tauroctony|tauroctony]]. These episodic panels are the closest thing the cult has to a 'comic strip' of its secret myth, otherwise reconstructed only from scattered reliefs.

## For artists & game designers
A central icon ringed by a sequence of small narrative panels is an efficient way to deliver a hero's whole backstory in one wall.

citations.text: G. Vermaseren, *Mithriaca III: The Mithraeum at Marino* (1982).
R. Beck, *The Religion of the Mithras Cult in the Roman Empire* (2006).

title: The Mithraic banquet of Mithras and Sol (after Cumont)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Engraving after a Roman relief, published by Franz Cumont
date: engraving after a Roman relief (2nd–3rd c. CE original)
summary:
A line engraving, published in Franz Cumont's foundational study, of the Mithraic banquet — the feast of Mithras and Sol that initiates re-enacted as a sacred meal.

## Iconography
**Mithras** and **Sol** recline together at a couch spread with the hide of the slain **bull**, served by the torchbearers and attendants in animal masks (the Raven and Lion grades). They share food and a **drinking horn**. The scene is the mythic charter for the communal meal of the cult.

## Significance
After the bull-slaying, myth held that Mithras and the Sun feasted on the victim and were reconciled; initiates repeated this **banquet** as a [[motif:mithraeum|communion]] on the benches of the mithraeum. Many tauroctony reliefs are double-sided or pivoting, with the banquet on the reverse, pairing the death of the bull with the meal it provides. Cumont's reproduction made the image central to early comparative debates with the Christian eucharist, now handled cautiously.

## For artists & game designers
A shared feast on the hide of the just-killed beast, attended by masked grade-officers, is a strong 'ritual aftermath' scene — sacrifice converted into communion.

citations.text: F. Cumont, *The Mysteries of Mithra* (Eng. trans. 1903).
R. Beck, *The Religion of the Mithras Cult in the Roman Empire* (2006).

title: Mithras born from the rock, ringed by the zodiac (Trier, CIMRM 985)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Carole Raddato
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (photo: Carole Raddato). Rheinisches Landesmuseum Trier, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A relief from Trier showing Mithras *petrogenitus* — the god rising newborn from the living rock, encircled by the signs of the zodiac.

## Iconography
The young **Mithras** emerges from the *petra genetrix*, the generative **rock**, already holding a **torch** and **dagger** and wearing the Phrygian cap. A ring of the twelve **zodiac** signs frames him, presenting the rock-birth as a cosmic event at the centre of the turning heavens.

## Significance
The **rock-birth** (*petra genetrix*) is the opening act of the Mithraic myth: the god is born not of a mother but of stone, on 25 December in some readings, a 'birth of the invincible sun'. The zodiac frame ties his nativity to the cosmos he governs, reinforcing the astronomical reading of the [[motif:tauroctony|tauroctony]]. CIMRM 985 is a securely catalogued example (Vermaseren).

## For artists & game designers
A figure emerging fully armed from solid rock inside a zodiac ring is a striking 'cosmic origin / spawn' image — birth as a stellar event.

citations.text: M. J. Vermaseren, *Corpus Inscriptionum et Monumentorum Religionis Mithriacae* 985.
R. Beck, *The Religion of the Mithras Cult in the Roman Empire* (2006).

title: Gold patera from the Pietroasele (Pietroasa) treasure
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (late antique, Gothic/Romano-Greek); photograph by Laci3
place: Pietroasele (Pietroasa), Romania; National Museum of Romanian History
summary:
The gold libation bowl (*patera*) from the Pietroasele treasure, a late-antique ritual vessel ringed with figures of seated deities around a central goddess — often discussed as an 'Orphic' cult bowl.

## Iconography
The shallow **patera** is worked in repoussé with a ring of sixteen **seated divinities** surrounding a raised central female **figure** holding a cup, with a knob (*omphalos*) at the bowl's centre. The pantheon-like assembly of gods around a presiding figure has invited readings as a cosmological or mystery-cult object.

## Significance
Found in 1837 as part of the rich Gothic-era **Pietroasa treasure**, the bowl belongs to the libation vessels used to pour offerings to the gods. Its dense divine assembly has been linked by some to [[motif:orphic-gold-tablet|Orphic]] or Gnostic cosmology, though its exact cult meaning is disputed; it remains a key monument of late-antique sacred metalwork on the Danube frontier.

## For artists & game designers
A ring of enthroned gods encircling a raised central figure makes a perfect 'council of the cosmos' relief for a ritual dish or shield-boss.

citations.text: R. Harhoiu, *The Treasure from Pietroasa, Romania* (1977).

title: Orphic gold tablet from Petelia (British Museum)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Greek, South Italy); photograph by Jononmac46
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Jononmac46). British Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Petelia tablet, a thin inscribed gold leaf buried with an Orphic initiate as a 'passport for the dead' (*totenpass*).

## Iconography
The tiny sheet of **gold** is engraved with Greek hexameters instructing the soul on its route through the underworld: it must avoid the white cypress and the first spring, and ask the guardians of the **Lake of Memory** (Mnemosyne) for cool water, declaring 'I am a child of Earth and starry Heaven.'

## Significance
Such [[motif:orphic-gold-tablet|gold lamellae]] (the so-called *totenpass* tablets, found at Petelia, Thurii, Hipponion, and Pelinna) are the most direct evidence for **Orphic–Bacchic** eschatology: the initiate, by reciting the right passwords and drinking from Memory rather than [[motif:lethe|Lethe]], escapes the cycle of rebirth and joins the blessed. The corpus is edited by Graf and Johnston.

## For artists & game designers
A literal 'cheat sheet for the afterlife' — an inscribed token whose text is the password the player must speak at a threshold guardian.

citations.text: F. Graf & S. I. Johnston, *Ritual Texts for the Afterlife: Orpheus and the Bacchic Gold Tablets* (2007).

title: The Orphic god Phanes — Modena relief
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photo published by Franz Cumont; crop by Wikimedia user
summary:
The famous Modena relief of Phanes (Protogonos), the Orphic first-born creator hatched from the cosmic egg.

## Iconography
A winged youth stands within an oval frame of the **zodiac**, his body wreathed by the coils of a **serpent**, flames issuing from his mouth, and animal heads (lion, goat, ram) sprouting around him. He emerges from the two halves of the split **cosmic egg**. This is **Phanes** / **Protogonos** ('the Shining', 'First-born'), the bisexual creator of the Orphic theogony.

## Significance
Phanes is born from the silver egg laid by Time (Chronos) and brings light and the gods into being — the Orphic counter-myth to Hesiod. The Modena relief is the single most complete surviving image of him, conflating Orphic, Mithraic (the [[motif:leontocephaline|zodiac-and-serpent]] frame), and Egyptian solar motifs. Its authenticity has been debated, but it remains the standard visual reference, reproduced by Cumont and Eisler.

## For artists & game designers
A radiant winged figure breaking from an egg inside a zodiac ring, wrapped in a serpent and crowned with beast-heads — a maximal 'creation deity' design.

citations.text: R. Eisler, *Orphisch-dionysische Mysteriengedanken in der christlichen Antike* (1925).

title: Votive hand of Jupiter Sabazios (Musée de Picardie, Amiens)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Bycro
place: Roman Gaul; Musée de Picardie, Amiens
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Bycro). Musée de Picardie, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A bronze 'hand of Sabazios', the votive right hand encrusted with symbols that was a signature object of the Sabazian mysteries.

## Iconography
The **bronze hand** is raised in the *benedictio Latina* gesture (thumb, index, and middle finger extended) and swarms with miniature symbols: a coiled **serpent**, a **pine cone** on the thumb, a **lizard** and **frog**, a tortoise, scales, and sometimes a tiny figure of the god or a mother and child. The whole forms a portable compendium of the cult's emblems.

## Significance
**Sabazios**, a Phrygian–Thracian sky-and-vegetation god assimilated to Jupiter and to Dionysus, was honoured in mysteries whose initiates handled the snake (*the god through the bosom*). The votive hands, found across the empire, were mounted on staffs in his sanctuaries; their meaning is reconstructed from their imagery, since the rites themselves were secret.

## For artists & game designers
A single hand-shaped reliquary covered in symbolic creatures is a memorable 'cult totem' object — an entire iconography packed onto one upraised hand.

citations.text: M. J. Vermaseren, *Corpus Cultus Iovis Sabazii* (1983).
E. N. Lane, 'Towards a Definition of the Iconography of Sabazius' (1980).

title: Mithraeum beneath the Basilica of San Clemente (Rome)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Edelmauswaldgeist
summary:
The well-preserved Mithraic *spelaeum* below the Basilica of San Clemente, showing the architecture of a mithraeum as a man-made cave.

## Iconography
A narrow vaulted chamber is lined with two long masonry **benches** on which initiates reclined for the cult meal; a freestanding **tauroctony altar** stands at the centre, its sides carved with Mithras and the bull. The ceiling is pocked to suggest the rough rock of a **cave**.

## Significance
The Mithraic temple was deliberately built as an artificial [[motif:cave|cave]], an image of the cosmos that Mithras was said to have created. San Clemente preserves the whole stratigraphy of conversion — a 1st-century building, a [[motif:mithraeum|mithraeum]], and the later Christian basilica stacked above — a vivid section through the religious history of Rome.

## For artists & game designers
The classic 'hidden cult chamber beneath the city': a low vaulted cave-room with flanking benches and a central altar — a complete underground shrine in one space.

citations.text: M. J. Vermaseren, *Corpus Inscriptionum et Monumentorum Religionis Mithriacae* (1956–60).
R. Beck, *The Religion of the Mithras Cult in the Roman Empire* (2006).

title: Mithraeum under Santa Prisca — initiation frescoes (Rome)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by MumblerJamie
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (photo: MumblerJamie). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Painted plaster from the mithraeum beneath the church of Santa Prisca on the Aventine, the most important surviving frescoes of any Mithraic temple.

## Iconography
The walls carry a **procession** of initiates and figures in the grades of the cult, painted above the side benches where members reclined. Surviving inscriptions in red name the seven [[motif:grades-of-initiation|grades]] — Raven, Bridegroom, Soldier, Lion, Persian, Sun-runner, Father — and verses celebrate the bull's saving blood.

## Significance
Discovered under Santa Prisca in 1934 and excavated by Vermaseren and van Essen, this **mithraeum** uniquely preserves both image and text of Mithraic ritual life. The frescoes show how a [[motif:mithraeum|mithraeum]] functioned as a sunken 'cave' modelling the cosmos, in which initiates ascended through grades toward the *Pater*.

## Significance and reception aside, the painted procession is among the rare direct windows onto what initiates actually saw.

## For artists & game designers
A banded narrative frieze running above seating — a ready template for a ritual hall whose walls teach the player the order of initiation.

citations.text: M. J. Vermaseren & C. C. van Essen, *The Excavations in the Mithraeum of the Church of Santa Prisca in Rome* (1965).
R. Beck, *The Religion of the Mithras Cult in the Roman Empire* (2006).

title: Bust of Serapis with the modius (Chiaramonti, Vatican)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman, after a Hellenistic type)
date: Roman copy (2nd c. CE) of a 4th-c. BCE prototype
summary:
A Roman bust of Serapis crowned with the grain-measure *modius*, in the Vatican's Chiaramonti collection, reflecting the influential cult statue of Alexandria.

## Iconography
The bearded, Zeus-like head wears the cylindrical **modius** (corn-measure) that marks **Serapis** as lord of abundance and of the underworld. The benevolent, brooding face follows the lost cult statue attributed to the sculptor **Bryaxis** for the Alexandrian Serapeum.

## Significance
**Serapis** was the deliberately syncretic god created under the early Ptolemies, fusing Osiris-Apis with Hellenic Zeus, Hades, and Asklepios to unite Greek and Egyptian subjects. With [[motif:isis|Isis]] he formed the leading divine pair of the Greco-Roman mystery world; his cult, with its promises of healing and salvation, spread to every corner of the empire before its temples fell in the Christian era.

## For artists & game designers
A single attribute — the corn-measure on the head — converts a generic bearded god into a specific lord of plenty and the dead; a lesson in identifying deities by one prop.

citations.text: J. E. Stambaugh, *Sarapis under the Early Ptolemies* (1972).

title: Taurobolium altar of the Magna Mater (engraving)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Engraving from G. Showerman, *The Great Mother of the Gods* (1901)
date: 1901 engraving (after a Roman altar, 2nd–4th c. CE)
rights: Public domain (no restrictions). Internet Archive Book Images, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An illustration of a taurobolium altar from Showerman's 1901 study of the Magna Mater, depicting the apparatus of the cult's bull-blood rite.

## Iconography
The carved **altar** bears the bucranium (bull's skull), garlands, and the implements of sacrifice, with a commemorative **inscription** recording that an individual 'received the taurobolium'. Such altars marked the performance of the rite and often the day the dedicant was, in the famous phrase, *in aeternum renatus* — 'reborn for eternity'.

## Significance
The **taurobolium** was the bloody initiation of the cult of [[motif:cybele|Cybele]] and Attis: a bull was slaughtered on a grating above a pit in which the initiate stood, drenched in its blood. Prudentius's hostile Christian description (*Peristephanon* X) is the fullest source. The rite, and the language of being 'reborn', made it a touchstone in old debates about pagan parallels to baptism — a debate now treated with much more caution by scholars such as R. Duthoy.

## For artists & game designers
A blood-stained altar over a sacrificial pit, inscribed 'reborn for eternity', is a stark prop for a violent rite of passage.

citations.text: G. Showerman, *The Great Mother of the Gods* (1901).
R. Duthoy, *The Taurobolium: Its Evolution and Terminology* (1969).

title: Mithras slaying the bull — tauroctony relief (Kunsthistorisches Museum)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Andreas Praefcke
summary:
The central cult image of Mithraism: the god Mithras slaying the cosmic bull, the *tauroctony* set up at the focal point of every mithraeum.

## Iconography
**Mithras**, a youth in Phrygian cap and billowing cloak, kneels on the back of a **bull** and drives a dagger into its shoulder while wrenching back its head. A retinue of creatures attacks the dying beast: a **dog** and **serpent** lap the blood, a **scorpion** grips the bull's testicles, a **raven** perches above. The torchbearers [[motif:cautes-and-cautopates|Cautes and Cautopates]] flank the scene with raised and lowered torches, and Sol and Luna look down from the corners.

## Significance
The tauroctony is the riddle at the heart of Mithraic studies. Franz Cumont read it as an Iranian creation-myth; later scholars, above all David Ulansey and Roger Beck, argued the figures map a **star-chart** (Taurus, Canis Minor, Hydra, Scorpius, Corvus), making Mithras a god who turns the cosmic axis. The bull's death releases fertility and was re-enacted as a [[motif:mithraeum|mystery]] of salvation for initiates.

## For artists & game designers
The single most reusable ancient 'boss kill' composition: a caped hero pinning and stabbing a great beast, ringed by symbolic animals — legible at a glance, dense with meaning.

citations.text: F. Cumont, *Textes et monuments figurés relatifs aux mystères de Mithra* (1896–99).
D. Ulansey, *The Origins of the Mithraic Mysteries* (1989).

title: Villa of the Mysteries — Dionysiac initiation fresco (Pompeii)
work: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Gary Todd
summary:
The great megalographic fresco of the Villa of the Mysteries at Pompeii, the fullest surviving depiction of a Dionysiac initiation.

## Iconography
Around the red-ground walls of a single room, near-life-size figures enact a rite: a bride is prepared, a boy reads a scroll, a **silenus** offers a bowl, a satyr gazes into a mask, the god **Dionysus** reclines against Ariadne, a kneeling initiate uncovers a veiled phallus in a winnowing basket, and a winged daemon raises a whip over a cowering woman who is **flagellated** while a nude bacchant dances.

## Significance
Painted in the Second Pompeian Style about 60–50 BCE, the cycle is the central monument for the **Dionysiac mysteries** and the most discussed of all ancient frescoes. Whether it shows a bridal preparation, a literal initiation, or both is unresolved; the ordeal of the [[motif:initiation|flagellation]] and unveiling dramatizes the passage from terror to blessed union with the god.

## For artists & game designers
A single room whose walls wrap the viewer in a life-sized initiatory drama — the gold standard for an immersive ritual environment.

citations.text: P. Veyne, *The Mysteries of the Villa: A Reading of the Dionysiac Frieze* (2009).

title: The Anima Mundi (World-Soul) — Fludd, Utriusque Cosmi Historia
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Robert Fludd; engraver Matthäus Merian
rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving from Robert Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617–21) depicting the structure of the macrocosm and the [[motif:world soul|Anima Mundi]] that binds it — Neoplatonic cosmology rendered as a Hermetic diagram.

## Iconography
Concentric bands of the created cosmos descend from the divine source through the angelic, celestial and elemental realms; in Fludd's famous related plate a nude female figure of Nature, chained at the wrist to the cloud of God above and leading an ape of art below, personifies the soul that animates the whole. [CHECK-RIGHTS]

## Significance
Fludd's encyclopaedia is the high-water mark of Renaissance Neoplatonic-Hermetic cosmology: the universe is a graded [[motif:chain of being|chain of being]] through which the One [[motif:emanation|emanates]], unified by the [[motif:anima mundi|World-Soul]] that Plato's *Timaeus* and Plotinus had set between the eternal and the corporeal. His system, drawing on Ficino and the *Corpus Hermeticum*, provoked the famous polemic with Kepler over whether the cosmos is to be read by mystical analogy or by mathematics.

## For artists & game designers
The complete 'great chain' cosmogram: banded realms from God to matter, joined by a chain — a ready-made cosmology screen.

title: Portrait of Cardinal Bessarion
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Justus van Gent / Pedro Berruguete
summary:
A portrait of Cardinal Basilios Bessarion (1403–1472) from the series of 'famous men' painted for the studiolo of the Duke of Urbino.

## Iconography
The bearded prelate is shown in three-quarter view in cardinal's robes, one of the uomini illustri assembled to embody learning and virtue.

## Significance
Bessarion, pupil of Plethon, was the Byzantine churchman who, after the Council of Florence, became a Roman cardinal and the great patron of Greek learning in Italy. His *In Calumniatorem Platonis* (1469) defended Plato against George of Trebizond's attacks and tempered Plethon's pagan Platonism into a form acceptable to Christianity, advancing the Neoplatonic [[motif:the one|One]] within orthodox bounds. His vast manuscript library — bequeathed to Venice as the seed of the Biblioteca Marciana — preserved much of the Greek Neoplatonic corpus for the West.

## For artists & game designers
The 'scholar-prelate' portrait — bearded, robed in cardinal red, dignified — the bridge between Byzantium and the Latin West.

title: The Wheel of Fortune and Boethius on his Sickbed (De consolatione philosophiae)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous illuminator
summary:
A miniature from a manuscript of Boethius's *De consolatione philosophiae* (Utrecht, UB Hs. 1335) pairing the Wheel of Fortune with the ailing Boethius on his sickbed.

## Iconography
Fortuna turns a great wheel on which crowned figures rise to the top (regno — 'I reign') and are flung down (regnavi — 'I have reigned'); alongside, Boethius lies stricken on his bed.

## Significance
The [[motif:rota fortunae|Wheel of Fortune]] is the *Consolation*'s most enduring image: Philosophy teaches that worldly goods spin on Fortune's wheel and are therefore worthless beside the changeless [[motif:the one|One]] that Neoplatonism sets above all becoming. The motif, fusing Stoic and Platonic providence, dominated medieval art and literature from Carmina Burana to the cathedral rose windows.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'rise and fall' device: a turning wheel with figures cresting and tumbling, labelled by station.

citations.text: H. R. Patch, *The Goddess Fortuna in Mediaeval Literature* (1927).

title: Boethius and Lady Philosophy (Consolation manuscript)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous illuminator
summary:
A medieval manuscript miniature of Boethius (c. 477–524 CE) visited in his cell by the personified Lady Philosophy, the central scene of the *Consolation of Philosophy*.

## Iconography
The imprisoned philosopher sits while a crowned woman — Philosophia, sometimes shown with a ladder of letters on her gown rising from Pi to Theta — leans toward him in instruction.

## Significance
Boethius wrote the *Consolatio* awaiting execution; in it Lady [[motif:lady philosophy|Philosophy]] leads him from grief to the vision of the supreme [[motif:the good|Good]], drawing on Plato, Plotinus and Proclus to argue that all things turn back toward the One and that Fortune's gifts are nothing beside divine providence. The work transmitted Neoplatonic cosmology to the entire Latin Middle Ages and was among its most copied and translated books.

## For artists & game designers
The model 'wisdom-figure visits the prisoner' tableau: crowned female mentor, despairing seated man, a cell.

citations.text: Boethius, *The Consolation of Philosophy*, trans. V. Watts (1969).

title: The Ptolemaic (Geocentric) System of Concentric Spheres (Cellarius, Harmonia Macrocosmica)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Andreas Cellarius; engraved by Jan van Loon
summary:
Andreas Cellarius's spectacular hand-coloured plate of the Ptolemaic geocentric universe from the *Harmonia Macrocosmica* (1660), showing the Earth ringed by the nested celestial spheres.

## Iconography
Concentric rings carry the Moon, Sun, planets and the fixed stars around a central Earth, the whole enclosed by the outermost heavens and crowned by the [[motif:the empyrean|Empyrean]]; cherubs and astronomers occupy the corners.

## Significance
This is the closest standard image to the Neoplatonic [[motif:concentric spheres|cosmos as interlocking circles]]: the diagram of nested spheres through which the Neoplatonists imagined divine power radiating outward from the centre (or, inversely, the soul ascending ring by ring back toward the One). The same scheme of graded, concentric heavens underlies the [[motif:celestial hierarchy|Dionysian hierarchies]], Dante's *Paradiso*, and the macrocosm of Renaissance Platonism — the visible architecture of [[motif:emanation|emanation]] and return.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'nested-spheres cosmos' diagram — a ringed universe map ready to repurpose as a cosmological wheel or skill tree.

citations.text: A. Cellarius, *Harmonia Macrocosmica* (1660); facsimile R. van Gent (2006).

title: Saints Dionysius the Areopagite and Hierotheos (icon)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous (Byzantine/post-Byzantine icon painter)
repository: Bank of Cyprus Cultural Foundation, Nicosia
summary:
A painted icon of Saints Dionysius the Areopagite and Hierotheos, the figures to whom the foundational corpus of Christian Neoplatonic mysticism was ascribed.

## Iconography
Two haloed bishop-saints stand in liturgical vestments, bearing gospel books — the standard hieratic icon type for the holy hierarchs.

## Significance
The writings of [[motif:dionysius the areopagite|Pseudo-Dionysius]] (c. 500 CE) — *On the Celestial Hierarchy*, *On the Divine Names*, *Mystical Theology* — recast Proclus's Neoplatonism in Christian terms, describing the [[motif:celestial hierarchy|nine angelic orders]] arranged in three triads through which divine light descends and creation returns to God. Transmitted under the name of Paul's Athenian convert, the corpus became the charter of medieval angelology and apophatic mysticism, shaping Eriugena, Aquinas and Dante's heaven. Hierotheos is named in the corpus as Dionysius's teacher.

## For artists & game designers
The 'holy hierarch' icon type — twin haloed bishops with gospel books — and the conceptual root of the nine-tier angelic hierarchy.

citations.text: Pseudo-Dionysius, *The Complete Works*, trans. C. Luibheid (1987).

title: Marsilio Ficino (with Landino, Poliziano and Chalkondyles), Ghirlandaio fresco detail
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Domenico Ghirlandaio
repository: Tornabuoni Chapel, Santa Maria Novella, Florence
summary:
A detail from Ghirlandaio's fresco *The Angel Appearing to Zacharias* in Santa Maria Novella, showing a group of Florentine humanists traditionally identified as Marsilio Ficino, Cristoforo Landino, Angelo Poliziano and Demetrios Chalkondyles.

## Iconography
Four scholars in contemporary dress stand conversing at the edge of the sacred scene — the donor-portrait convention by which Ghirlandaio inserted the living intellectual elite of Medicean Florence into a biblical narrative.

## Significance
Marsilio Ficino (1433–1499) was the architect of the Renaissance Neoplatonic revival: at the [[motif:florentine academy|Platonic Academy]] under Medici patronage he translated the complete Plato, Plotinus and the *Corpus Hermeticum* into Latin and wrote the *Theologia Platonica* and *De vita*, fusing Platonic [[motif:emanation|emanation]] with Christian theology and astral magic. This group portrait places him among the philological and philosophical circle through which his recovered Platonism reshaped European thought.

## For artists & game designers
The 'circle of scholars' cameo — contemporary intellectuals slipped into a sacred scene as living portraits.

citations.text: M. J. B. Allen & V. Rees (eds.), *Marsilio Ficino: His Theology, His Philosophy* (2002).

title: Hypatia of Alexandria (detail from Raphael's School of Athens)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio)
repository: Stanza della Segnatura, Apostolic Palace, Vatican
summary:
A detail from Raphael's fresco *The School of Athens* showing the white-robed figure traditionally identified as Hypatia of Alexandria, the only woman among the assembled philosophers.

## Iconography
A young figure in pale robes gazes out toward the viewer from the left foreground of the great architectural hall of philosophers, set apart by colour and direct gaze.

## Significance
Hypatia (c. 350/370–415 CE) was the Neoplatonist mathematician and philosopher who headed the Platonic school at Alexandria, teaching Plotinus and commenting on Diophantus and Apollonius. Lynched by a Christian mob in 415, she became the emblem of pagan learning destroyed by fanaticism. Raphael's inclusion of her — facing outward at the threshold of the [[motif:school of athens|School of Athens]] — canonised her within the Renaissance pantheon of sages.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal 'woman scholar among men': singled out by light robes and an outward gaze that breaks the scene.

title: Iamblichus, De mysteriis Aegyptiorum (Ficino's Latin edition)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Iamblichus; trans. Marsilio Ficino
summary:
A page from the Renaissance Latin edition of Iamblichus's *De mysteriis Aegyptiorum* (On the Mysteries of the Egyptians) in the translation by Marsilio Ficino, the foundational text of Neoplatonic theurgy.

## Iconography
A printed Latin page in humanist roman type, the running title naming the *Mysteria Aegyptiorum*, from the bundle of Neoplatonic translations Ficino issued through the Aldine press.

## Significance
Iamblichus of Chalcis (c. 245–325 CE) transformed Plotinian Neoplatonism by insisting that the soul ascends not by contemplation alone but through [[motif:theurgy|theurgy]] — ritual acts that draw down divine power. His *De mysteriis* defends Egyptian and Chaldean rites against Porphyry's skepticism and elaborates the procession of gods, henads and daemons through which the [[motif:the one|One]] is approached. Ficino's translation made theurgic Neoplatonism available to the Florentine Academy and to the whole Renaissance magical tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Reference for the 'rediscovered occult treatise': dense humanist print, Latin headings, the look of a magus's working library.

citations.text: Iamblichus, *On the Mysteries*, trans. Clarke, Dillon & Hershbell (2003).

title: Liber de Causis (manuscript, Venice Lat. 288)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous (after Proclus); 'Pseudo-Aristotle'
date: medieval manuscript (text 9th-century Arabic adaptation of Proclus)
summary:
A manuscript opening (Venice, Lat. 288, fol. 2r) of the *Liber de Causis* — the 'Book of Causes', the Neoplatonic treatise that medieval Europe long attributed to Aristotle.

## Iconography
A Latin manuscript page with rubricated incipit, opening the chain of propositions on the First Cause.

## Significance
The *[[motif:liber de causis|Liber de Causis]]* is an Arabic adaptation of Proclus's *Elements of Theology*, translated into Latin in the 12th century and read as Aristotle's crowning metaphysics until Aquinas, comparing it with the newly available Proclus, exposed its true Neoplatonic source. Its propositions on how all multiplicity proceeds from the [[motif:the one|First Cause]] by [[motif:emanation|emanation]] carried the core of Proclean Neoplatonism into scholastic theology, shaping Albert the Great, Aquinas and the doctrine of being itself.

## For artists & game designers
A 'misattributed grimoire of metaphysics' prop: austere Latin propositions, a rubricated opening, hidden Greek origins.

citations.text: *Liber de Causis*, trans. D. J. Brand (1984).

title: Diagram of Planetary Motion (Macrobian cosmology, after Somnium Scipionis tradition)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous
date: diagram (after the medieval Macrobian / Somnium Scipionis tradition)
summary:
A schematic diagram of planetary motion in the geocentric cosmos, in the tradition of the spheres-and-orbits figures that illustrated Macrobius's *Commentary on the Dream of Scipio*.

## Iconography
The paths of the planets are traced across a field representing the ordered celestial spheres turning about a central Earth — the diagrammatic form of cosmic order through time.

## Significance
Macrobius's *Commentarii in Somnium Scipionis* (early 5th century) was, with Calcidius's *Timaeus*, the great medieval handbook of Neoplatonic cosmology. Expounding Cicero's vision of the soul ascending through the planetary [[motif:concentric spheres|spheres]] to its celestial home, Macrobius taught the descent of the soul through the spheres at birth and its return at death, and the [[motif:harmony of the spheres|harmony of the spheres]]. Diagrams of this kind made the Neoplatonic ordered cosmos visible to medieval and Renaissance readers.

## For artists & game designers
A clean orrery-style schematic of nested planetary orbits — the geometry of the soul's celestial journey.

citations.text: Macrobius, *Commentary on the Dream of Scipio*, trans. W. H. Stahl (1952).

key_concepts: descent and return of the soul

title: The Monad (point within a circle)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous (modern vector after a classical symbol)
date: modern rendering of a classical symbol
summary:
The classical sign of the Monad — a single point enclosed in a circle — the simplest emblem of the Neoplatonic First Principle.

## Iconography
A dot at the centre of a perfect circle: the point is the undivided source, the circle its boundless extension.

## Significance
The [[motif:monad|Monad]] (Greek *monas*, 'unity') is the Pythagorean and Neoplatonic figure of the [[motif:the one|One]] — the unbegotten source from which all number, and so all reality, proceeds by [[motif:emanation|emanation]] and to which it returns. For Plotinus the One is beyond being and beyond predication; the point-in-circle gives that ineffable source its minimal visible form, the seed of every later diagram of concentric procession. The same sign recurs in Neopythagorean number theory and in Hermetic and alchemical symbolism (echoed in Dee's *Monas Hieroglyphica*).

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate minimal cosmological glyph — a centred point in a ring, instantly reading as 'origin / unity / source'.

citations.text: Plotinus, *Enneads* V.1, ‘On the Three Primary Hypostases'.

title: Portrait of Pico della Mirandola (Rubens)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Peter Paul Rubens (after an earlier likeness)
summary:
A portrait of Giovanni Pico della Mirandola (1463–1494), attributed to Rubens after an earlier likeness, preserving the image of the prodigy of the Florentine Academy.

## Iconography
A young man with long flowing hair, in three-quarter view, in the grave humanist portrait manner — the conventional image of the brilliant philosopher cut off young.

## Significance
Pico, the friend of Ficino, sought a *concordia* of all philosophies — Plato, Aristotle, the Hermetica, and above all the [[motif:christian kabbalah|Kabbalah]], which he was the first Christian to mine for Neoplatonic and apologetic ends. His *Oration on the Dignity of Man* and the 900 *Conclusiones* (1486), partly condemned by the Church, frame the human soul as a free microcosm able to ascend the [[motif:chain of being|chain of being]] toward the divine. With Ficino he made Florence the centre of Neoplatonic and Hermetic-Kabbalistic synthesis.

## For artists & game designers
The 'young genius' portrait — long hair, calm face, scholar's robe — the prodigy who died at thirty-one.

citations.text: S. A. Farmer, *Syncretism in the West: Pico's 900 Theses* (1998).

key_concepts: Oration on the Dignity of Man

title: Plato's Academy (Roman mosaic, Pompeii)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
