creator: Anonymous (Roman mosaicist)
place: Pompeii (Villa of T. Siminius Stephanus); now Naples
summary:
A Roman floor mosaic from Pompeii depicting a circle of philosophers — traditionally read as Plato and members of his Academy — gathered in learned conversation beneath a tree.

## Iconography
Seven draped figures sit and stand around a central sage who points with a rod at a celestial globe; a sundial sits atop a column and a walled city rises behind, framing the scene of philosophical instruction.

## Significance
The Academy that Plato founded in the grove of Akademos became, through its Neoplatonic successors at Athens and Alexandria, the institutional home of the tradition until Justinian closed it in 529 CE. This mosaic is among the very few ancient images of philosophical schooling, showing the cosmological teaching — the globe, the sundial — that fed the Neoplatonic concern with the order of the [[motif:concentric spheres|heavens]] and the soul's place within it.

## For artists & game designers
Reference for the 'open-air philosophy school': a seated circle, a pointing teacher, a globe and column under a tree.

title: Plato and Aristotle (detail from Raphael's School of Athens)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio)
repository: Stanza della Segnatura, Apostolic Palace, Vatican
summary:
The central detail of Raphael's *School of Athens*: Plato and Aristotle stride forward side by side, Plato pointing upward to the heavens, Aristotle gesturing toward the earth.

## Iconography
Plato (modelled on Leonardo) holds his *Timaeus* and raises a finger to the sky; Aristotle holds his *Ethics* and extends a level hand toward the ground — the opposed gestures of transcendence and immanence.

## Significance
The pairing crystallises the Renaissance reading of philosophy: Plato the metaphysician of the eternal [[motif:the forms|Forms]] above, Aristotle the analyst of the sensible world below. By placing the *[[motif:timaeus|Timaeus]]* in Plato's hand, Raphael flags the dialogue that the Neoplatonists read as the key to cosmic [[motif:emanation|emanation]] and the [[motif:world soul|World-Soul]]. The image, painted at the height of Ficino's influence, is the visual manifesto of Florentine Platonism.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'two masters, two worldviews' composition: one points up to heaven, the other down to earth.

citations.text: I. Hart, ‘Raphael's School of Athens', *Renaissance Studies* (2010).

title: Marble Bust of Plato (Roman copy)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous (Roman copy after a Greek original)
date: 1st century CE copy of a c. 350–340 BCE original
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons. (CC BY-SA 4.0, photo O. S. M. Amin)
summary:
A Roman marble bust of Plato (428/7–348/7 BCE), copied from a Greek original made within the philosopher's own lifetime — the source of the canonical Platonic likeness.

## Iconography
A dignified bearded head with full, swept hair and calm features, in the idealising portrait mode of a fourth-century master.

## Significance
Plato is the fountainhead of the whole Neoplatonic tradition. The doctrines the Neoplatonists systematised — the [[motif:the forms|Forms]], the [[motif:the good|Good]] as the sun of the intelligible realm in the *Republic*, the demiurge and World-Soul of the *Timaeus*, the soul's ascent in the *Symposium* and *Phaedrus* — all descend from his dialogues. Plotinus, Proclus and Ficino read themselves as his faithful interpreters; this bust is the face that the Academy gave the Western imagination.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical Plato head — broad brow, full beard, serene gaze — instantly legible as 'the founding philosopher'.

citations.text: G. M. A. Richter, *The Portraits of the Greeks* (1965).

title: Plato's Timaeus in Calcidius's Latin Translation (manuscript)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Calcidius (translator/commentator)
summary:
A medieval manuscript page of Plato's *Timaeus* in the partial Latin translation and commentary of Calcidius (4th century CE) — for centuries the only Plato the Latin West possessed.

## Iconography
A two-column Latin manuscript page of the *Timaeus* text with Calcidius's commentary, sometimes accompanied by geometric and astronomical diagrams of the cosmic soul.

## Significance
The *[[motif:timaeus|Timaeus]]* is Plato's account of how the divine craftsman (demiurge) fashions the cosmos by imposing mathematical harmony on chaos and animates it with a [[motif:world soul|World-Soul]] compounded from the Same and the Different. Through Calcidius this dialogue shaped the entire medieval Platonic cosmology — the school of Chartres, the macrocosm–microcosm analogy, the music of the spheres — and supplied the Neoplatonists their model of orderly [[motif:emanation|emanation]] from the One.

## For artists & game designers
The 'cosmogony codex': dense Latin columns and circle-diagrams of how the world was made.

citations.text: Calcidius, *On Plato's Timaeus*, ed. and trans. J. Magee (2016).

title: Plato's Cave
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Jan Saenredam after Cornelis Cornelisz van Haarlem
summary:
Jan Saenredam's 1604 engraving after Cornelis van Haarlem, the most famous visual rendering of Plato's Allegory of the Cave from Book VII of the *Republic*.

## Iconography
In a vast cavern, chained prisoners stare at shadows cast on the wall by figures and objects paraded before a fire; at the cave's mouth daylight and a freed figure ascending mark the world of reality beyond illusion.

## Significance
The [[motif:allegory of the cave|Allegory of the Cave]] dramatises the Platonic and Neoplatonic doctrine of ascent: the soul, bound to the shadow-world of the senses, must turn (periagoge) and climb toward the light of the [[motif:the good|Good]], figured as the [[motif:the sun|Sun]] outside the cave. For the Neoplatonists this was the very pattern of the soul's return to the One. Saenredam's print, with its Latin verses on truth and appearance, made the parable a staple of early-modern visual philosophy.

## For artists & game designers
The template 'illusion vs. reality' scene: chained watchers, firelit shadows, a blinding exit to daylight.

title: Gemistos Plethon (detail from Gozzoli's Procession of the Magi)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Benozzo Gozzoli
repository: Magi Chapel, Palazzo Medici Riccardi, Florence
summary:
A figure from Benozzo Gozzoli's *Procession of the Magi* in the Medici chapel, identified as the Byzantine philosopher Georgios Gemistos Plethon (c. 1355/60–1452/4).

## Iconography
An elderly bearded sage in rich robes rides amid the magnificent cavalcade of the Magi that winds through a Tuscan landscape — a contemporary scholar woven into the sacred procession.

## Significance
Plethon came to the Council of Ferrara–Florence (1438–39) and there, by lecturing on Plato and the differences between Plato and Aristotle, ignited the Florentine Platonic revival — it was reportedly his teaching that moved Cosimo de' Medici to found the [[motif:florentine academy|Platonic Academy]] under Ficino. A radical Neoplatonist who in his *Nomoi* proposed reviving a reformed pagan polytheism organised around the [[motif:the one|One]] and a hierarchy of gods, Plethon is the hinge between late Byzantine and Renaissance Neoplatonism. Bessarion was his pupil.

## For artists & game designers
The 'foreign sage in the procession' — an exotic bearded philosopher riding among Renaissance nobility.

citations.text: C. M. Woodhouse, *George Gemistos Plethon: The Last of the Hellenes* (1986).

title: Marble Portrait Bust of an Ancient Philosopher
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous (Greek/Roman)
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons. (CC BY-SA 4.0, photo Zde)
summary:
A Hellenistic marble portrait of a bearded philosopher from Delphi, of the contemplative type by which the ancient world imagined its sages — the very kind of image evoked for Plotinus (c. 204/5–270 CE), founder of Neoplatonism, who notoriously refused to sit for a portrait.

## Iconography
The heavily bearded head turns slightly, the brow furrowed in thought, eyes deep-set beneath a high forehead — the canonical Greek 'philosopher type'. The surviving marble is a Roman-era image of an unidentified thinker (museum label: 'maybe Plutarch').

## Significance
Plotinus taught that all reality flows by [[motif:emanation|emanation]] from a single ineffable source, the [[motif:the one|One]], through Intellect (Nous) and the [[motif:world soul|World-Soul]], and that the philosopher's task is the soul's return to that source. His pupil Porphyry records that Plotinus disdained the body so far that he would not have his likeness made — making every 'portrait of Plotinus' necessarily a borrowed contemplative type like this one. Such busts furnished the Renaissance with its image of the divinely-inspired sage.

## For artists & game designers
The default 'ancient philosopher' face: full beard, lined brow, inward gaze — read instantly as wisdom and asceticism.

title: Averroes and Porphyry in Disputation (manuscript miniature)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Monfredo de Monte Imperiali
summary:
A medieval manuscript miniature showing the Neoplatonist Porphyry (c. 234–305 CE) in imagined disputation with the Andalusian philosopher Averroes — an anachronistic pairing that registers Porphyry's central place in the scholastic curriculum.

## Iconography
Two robed scholars are seated face to face in gesture of debate, labelled with their names; the miniature comes from a herbal/philosophical compilation by Monfredo de Monte Imperiali.

## Significance
Porphyry was Plotinus's pupil, editor of the *Enneads*, and author of the *Isagoge* — the introduction to Aristotle's logic that became the standard textbook of the medieval West and the seed of the universals controversy. As a Neoplatonist he transmitted the doctrine of [[motif:emanation|emanation]] and the soul's ascent, while his anti-Christian polemic made him notorious. The medieval image of the disputing philosophers shows how late-antique Platonism survived embedded in the logic curriculum.

## For artists & game designers
A clean visual shorthand for 'the scholarly disputation': two seated, gesturing figures with name-labels.

citations.text: Porphyry, *Isagoge*; ed. and trans. J. Barnes (2003).

title: Synesius of Cyrene, Letters (Greek manuscript)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Synesius of Cyrene
date: manuscript (Byzantine copy of a c. 400 CE text)
summary:
A page of the Greek manuscript (Paris, BnF, Gr. 1040) preserving the *Letters* of Synesius of Cyrene (c. 373–414 CE), the Neoplatonist student of Hypatia who became a Christian bishop.

## Iconography
A column of Byzantine Greek minuscule on parchment, the text of Synesius's celebrated correspondence.

## Significance
Synesius studied at Alexandria under [[motif:hypatia|Hypatia]], to whom several of these letters are addressed, and fused Plotinian Neoplatonism with Christianity — his *Hymns* and treatise *On Dreams* describe the soul's ascent through the celestial spheres and the World-Soul. As Bishop of Ptolemais he embodies the late-antique passage of pagan Platonic philosophy into Christian thought. His letters are a primary source for the intellectual world of Hypatia's Alexandria.

## For artists & game designers
Authentic look of a Byzantine Greek letter-collection — useful as a 'lost correspondence' prop.

title: Tabula Cebetis (The Tablet of Cebes)
work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A painted *Tabula Cebetis* (Rijksmuseum SK-A-2372), the allegorical 'Tablet of Cebes' that maps the whole course of human life as a journey through walled enclosures toward true happiness.

## Iconography
Concentric ringed walls enclose successive precincts of life: at the gate a Genius instructs entering souls, Deceit offers her cup, Fortune turns on her ball, false opinions and pleasures crowd the lower courts, while a steep path leads up past True Education to the crowning citadel of Felicity. [CHECK-RIGHTS: image is PD]

## Significance
The *[[motif:tabula cebetis|Tablet of Cebes]]*, a Greek dialogue of the 1st century CE pseudonymously ascribed to Socrates' pupil Cebes, was hugely popular in the Renaissance as a moral-philosophical allegory of the soul's ascent. Its diagram of [[motif:concentric enclosures|concentric enclosures]] climbing toward the Good visually echoes the Neoplatonic ascent through graded realms, and it was repeatedly printed and painted from Holbein onward as a meditation on virtue, fortune and true education.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made allegorical level-map: ringed courts of vice and illusion climbing to a summit of true happiness.

citations.text: *The Tabula of Cebes*, ed. and trans. J. T. Fitzgerald & L. M. White (1983).

title: Ægishjálmur, the Helm of Awe (Lbs 2413 8vo)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous Icelandic scribe
rights: Public domain. National and University Library of Iceland, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A drawing of the *Ægishjálmur* — the 'Helm of Awe' or 'Helm of Terror' — from an Icelandic magical-stave manuscript held by the National and University Library of Iceland.

## Iconography
A radially symmetrical **stave**: from a central point project (typically eight) identical arms ending in tridents or forked, barbed terminals, the whole forming a snowflake-like figure. The design is built from repeated rune-like elements rather than written text.

## Significance
The **Ægishjálmur** is the most famous of the Icelandic [[motif:galdrastafir|magical staves]], named from the *aegis*-helm that the dragon Fáfnir claims in the *Fáfnismál* of the Poetic Edda — a power to instil terror and turn aside harm. In the post-medieval Icelandic grimoire tradition it was worn or drawn (sometimes in lead, or pressed to the forehead) for protection and dominance. It belongs to the manuscript magic catalogued by scholars such as Matthías Viðar Sæmundsson and Stephen Flowers.

## For artists & game designers
The eight-armed radial trident-glyph is the definitive 'protective sigil' silhouette — instantly readable as a ward and trivially scalable as a tattoo, shield boss, or HUD icon.

citations.text: Stephen E. Flowers, *The Galdrabók: An Icelandic Book of Magic* (1989).

title: The Björketorp Runestone (curse-stone), Blekinge
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Proto-Norse rune-carver); photograph by Harriks
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Harriks). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Björketorp stone, a towering Proto-Norse curse-stone standing within a stone setting in Blekinge, Sweden.

## Iconography
A tall, rough monolith carved with Elder/transitional **runes** on two faces. One face carries the cryptic word *uþarba-spá* and the warning that a 'prophecy of misfortune' lies hidden; the other pronounces that whoever breaks the monument will die by 'insidious death'.

## Significance
**Björketorp** is the most dramatic of the Blekinge [[motif:runestone|curse-stones]], sharing a near-identical magical formula with Stentoften. Its explicit threat — ruin and death to the violator — is a centerpiece of the argument that early runic writing could be a vehicle of binding magic and malediction. The standing stone's monumental scale within a ritual stone-setting reinforces its apotropaic, boundary-marking function. It is discussed at length by Krause, Antonsen, and McKinnell.

## For artists & game designers
A menhir that curses the unwary — towering, weathered, inscribed with a death-threat: a ready-made 'do not disturb' landmark for a haunted site.

citations.text: Wolfgang Krause & Herbert Jankuhn, *Die Runeninschriften im älteren Futhark* (1966).
Elmer H. Antonsen, *Runes and Germanic Linguistics* (2002).

title: The Franks Casket — front-left panel (British Museum)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Anglo-Saxon Northumbria)
rights: Public domain. British Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A panel of the Franks Casket (Auzon Casket), an early-eighth-century Anglo-Saxon whalebone box carved with runic inscriptions and legendary scenes, in the British Museum.

## Iconography
The **front-left scene** shows **Wayland the Smith** at his forge amid the bodies of his vengeance, beside the Adoration of the Magi, the two flanked and framed by a border of **Anglo-Saxon (Anglo-Frisian) runes** that name the whalebone and tell its origin in alliterative verse.

## Significance
The **Franks Casket** is the densest surviving juxtaposition of Germanic-pagan legend, Roman and Christian story, and runic writing — its inscriptions running in riddling, sometimes reversed and cipher-like script. Carved from the bone of a stranded whale, the box itself is treated almost as a charged object whose runes both narrate and, some argue, ward. It is a central monument for the study of [[motif:runes|runic]] literacy and Northumbrian visual culture, studied by Webster, Page, and others.

## For artists & game designers
A carved bone box whose every panel tells a different mythic story in pictures and runes — a model for a lore-dense artifact the player reads scene by scene.

citations.text: Leslie Webster, *The Franks Casket* (British Museum Objects in Focus, 2012).
R. I. Page, *An Introduction to English Runes* (1999).

title: Younger Futhark runic row
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Klafui (Swedish Wikipedia), diagram
summary:
A diagram of a Norse runic row (futhark), the alphabet that underlies all Germanic runic magic and inscription.

## Iconography
A columned chart of **runes** with their names and sound-values — the angular characters designed for cutting into wood, bone, and stone, named in order from *fe / fehu* onward, which gives the row its name *fuþark*.

## Significance
The **futhark** is the script-system whose very letters were credited with power: each rune bore a name and a meaning (wealth, ice, hail, the god Týr), and Old Norse texts such as *Hávamál* and *Sigrdrífumál* describe runes that heal, bind, curse, and protect. The Elder Futhark of the [[motif:runestone|early inscriptions]] gave way to the 16-character Younger Futhark of the Viking Age. Understanding the rune-row is the precondition for reading every amulet, bracteate, and curse-stone in this collection.

## For artists & game designers
The master key: a legible rune chart for building authentic inscriptions, spell-glyphs, or a runic 'language' system in a game.

title: Gold bracteates from Over Hornbæk (Nationalmuseet)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Migration Period); photograph by Marie-Lan Nguyen
rights: Public domain (photo: Marie-Lan Nguyen). Nationalmuseet, Copenhagen, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Migration-period gold bracteates from Over Hornbæk, Denmark, in the National Museum of Denmark — stamped gold pendants that often carry runic magical words.

## Iconography
Thin discs of **gold** stamped in relief and fitted with a loop and beaded rim for wearing. The imagery typically shows a large male head over a horse or beast, widely read as **Odin** as healer, sometimes accompanied by birds and short **runic** legends.

## Significance
**Gold bracteates** (5th–6th c.) are a major corpus of early Germanic amuletic art. Many bear the magic word [[motif:alu|*alu*]] or other runic formulae, and the recurring 'rider/head-and-horse' motif is interpreted as Odin healing Baldr's foal — the same scene as the later Merseburg Charm. They fuse Roman medallion form with Northern myth and runic magic, and are catalogued in the monumental *Ikonographischer Katalog* (IK) of Hauck and colleagues.

## For artists & game designers
Stamped gold pendants picturing a god and a horse, ringed with runes: luxurious wearable charms that double as miniature mythological scenes.

citations.text: Karl Hauck et al., *Die Goldbrakteaten der Völkerwanderungszeit* (IK, 1985–89).

title: Lukkustafir (luck-staves) from the Huld manuscript
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: After Geir Vigfússon, *Huld* manuscript (ÍB 383 4to)
summary:
*Lukkustafir* — 'luck-staves' — after the *Huld* manuscript, a famous nineteenth-century Icelandic compilation of magical signs.

## Iconography
A small set of compact **sigils** built from crossed lines, loops, and runic strokes. Each is a self-contained device meant to be inscribed and carried; together they form a row of distinct good-fortune marks.

## Significance
The **Huld** manuscript (ÍB 383 4to), compiled by Geir Vigfússon at Akureyri in 1860, is one of the principal late sources for Icelandic [[motif:galdrastafir|magical staves]], gathering the Vegvísir, the Helm of Awe, and dozens of *lukkustafir* and other charms from older lost exemplars. Its *lukkustafir* were drawn for luck and success. The manuscript is the single most influential channel by which Icelandic stave-magic reached the modern world.

## For artists & game designers
A palette of small, distinct luck-glyphs — ideal as collectible minor charms or as the iconography for a crafting/enchantment menu.

title: The Kvinneby Amulet (copper runic charm), Öland
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Viking Age); photograph by Ola Myrin, Statens historiska museum
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Ola Myrin, Statens historiska museum). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Kvinneby amulet, an eleventh-century copper plate from Öland incised with a runic protective spell, in the Swedish History Museum.

## Iconography
A small square **copper** plaque pierced for suspension, its faces crowded with **Younger Futhark** runes and a simple engraved image of a **fish**. The runes spell out a charm invoking the god **Thor** and his hammer to shield the bearer from a being named *þurs* (a hostile spirit) and from evil.

## Significance
The **Kvinneby amulet** is among the richest Viking-Age verbal charms, a rare survival of explicitly pagan protective magic naming [[motif:thor|Thor]] and asking that 'lightning hold all evil away from Bófi.' It shows the continuity between runic amulets and the later Christian and folk charm traditions, and is debated for its exact reading by scholars including Lena Peterson and Bernard Mees.

## For artists & game designers
A pocket metal ward invoking a thunder-god against a named demon, complete with a little engraved fish — a vivid, specific protective talisman.

citations.text: Mindy MacLeod & Bernard Mees, *Runic Amulets and Magic Objects* (2006).

title: The Lindholm Amulet (runic bone charm)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Proto-Norse)
summary:
The Lindholm 'amulet' (Lindholmen), a curved piece of bone incised with one of the earliest runic magical inscriptions.

## Iconography
A long, narrow strip of **bone** carved with a single line of **Elder Futhark** runes. The text opens with the rune-master's self-declaration (*ek erilaz sa wilagaz hateka* — 'I, the eril, am called the wily one') and closes with a run of repeated runes and the magic word **alu**.

## Significance
The **Lindholm amulet** is a key early witness to runic magic. The title *erilaz* (rune-master) and the formulaic word [[motif:alu|*alu*]] — variously linked to ale, protection, ecstasy, or taboo — recur on bracteates and amulets across the Migration-period North. The string of eight *a*-runes and three *t*-runes is widely read as a numerically charged magical sequence. It is a touchstone in the scholarship of operative runes (Krause, Flowers, MacLeod & Mees).

## For artists & game designers
A carved bone strip bearing a boast and a power-word: the prototype of the inscribed personal charm a caster carries on the body.

citations.text: Mindy MacLeod & Bernard Mees, *Runic Amulets and Magic Objects* (2006).

title: Thor's-hammer (Mjölnir) silver pendant
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Viking Age); recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme (Dot Boughton)
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (Portable Antiquities Scheme, Dot Boughton). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An early-medieval silver Thor's-hammer pendant recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme, a wearable emblem of the god Thor.

## Iconography
A cast **silver** pendant in the stylised shape of **Mjölnir**, Thor's hammer — a short-handled, broad-headed hammer worn inverted on a suspension loop, often decorated with punched or interlace ornament.

## Significance
The **Thor's-hammer** amulet is the signal object of Viking-Age devotion to [[motif:thor|Thor]], worn for protection and as a marker of pagan identity, frequently in pointed contrast to the Christian cross during the conversion period (some moulds even cast both at once). Hundreds are known from across Scandinavia and the Danelaw. As an apotropaic object the hammer channelled Thor's storm-power to guard the wearer, echoing the same invocation seen on the Kvinneby amulet.

## For artists & game designers
The hammer-pendant is the most recognisable Norse 'faith token' — a compact silver icon of divine protection and tribal identity, ideal as a quest reward or class emblem.

citations.text: Jörn Staecker, 'Thor's Hammer: Symbol of Christianization and Political Delusion,' *Lund Archaeological Review* (1999).

title: The Rök Runestone, Östergötland
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Viking-Age rune-carver); photograph by L'imaginaire
rights: Public domain (CC0, photo: L'imaginaire). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Rök Runestone in Östergötland, Sweden, the longest-known runic inscription and the foundational monument of Old Norse literacy.

## Iconography
A tall granite slab covered on multiple faces with dense lines of **Younger Futhark** runes, including passages in older runes, cipher-runes, and a transposition code. Raised by Varinn in memory of his son Vámóðr, it weaves in allusions to heroes, Theodoric, and cosmic riddles.

## Significance
The **Rök stone** (c. 800) is treated as the first piece of written Swedish literature and a key witness to early-medieval Germanic verbal magic and memory-craft. Its deliberate ciphers and riddling structure show runes used not merely to record but to encode and conjure — a learned, almost incantatory deployment of the script. Recent work (Holmberg, Gräslund et al.) reads it against a feared climate catastrophe. It stands at the head of the [[motif:runestone|runestone]] corpus.

## For artists & game designers
A monument that is itself a puzzle box: ciphered runes and riddles carved on stone — the archetype of an inscribed object the player must decode.

citations.text: Bo Ralph & Per Holmberg et al., studies of the Rök inscription (2007–2020).

title: The Stentoften Runestone (curse-stone), Sölvesborg
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Proto-Norse rune-carver); photograph by Helen Simonsson
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Helen Simonsson). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Stentoften stone, one of the Blekinge curse-stones, now sheltered in the church at Sölvesborg.

## Iconography
A dark stone bearing a Proto-Norse inscription in a mix of **Elder** and transitional **runes**, recording a gift of fields/cattle by HaþuwolafR and closing with a protective curse against anyone who would break or damage the monument.

## Significance
Stentoften belongs with the **Björketorp** and Gummarp stones as the Blekinge group of [[motif:runestone|curse-stones]] (c. 600 CE), among the clearest surviving examples of operative runic magic: the inscription pronounces ruin upon a violator and contains the cryptic, much-discussed magical word-formula shared with Björketorp. These stones are central evidence in the debate over whether runes carried inherent magical force, treated by Krause, McKinnell, and others.

## For artists & game designers
A literal cursed object: a stone whose carved words promise doom to whoever disturbs it — the template for a warded relic or tomb inscription.

citations.text: Wolfgang Krause & Herbert Jankuhn, *Die Runeninschriften im älteren Futhark* (1966).
John McKinnell et al., *Runes, Magic and Religion* (2004).

title: Stora Hammars picture stones, Lärbro, Gotland
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Viking-Age Gotland); photograph by Carl Gustaf Rosenberg
summary:
Picture stones from Stora Hammars at Lärbro, Gotland — among the most striking Viking-Age carvings, including a famous scene bearing the valknut.

## Iconography
Tall limestone slabs carved in registers with **ships**, warriors, and ritual scenes. The renowned Stora Hammars I panel shows a figure being sacrificed over an altar beside a hanged man, a bird, and the three-triangle **valknut** — widely read as a scene of cultic death dedicated to Odin.

## Significance
The **Stora Hammars** stones are key visual evidence for the darker, sacrificial side of Odinic religion and its magic: the [[motif:valknut|valknut]] appears precisely where the imagery suggests death-offering, supporting its interpretation as Odin's 'knot of the slain.' Together with the spear, the hanged figure, and the eagle, the scene connects to seiðr, war-magic, and the cult of the fallen. The stones are foundational to Lindqvist's and Andrén's work on Gotlandic cosmology.

## For artists & game designers
A carved narrative of ritual death under the valknut: the canonical reference for depicting an Odinic sacrifice or a grim battlefield rite.

title: Ardre VIII picture stone — Odin and Sleipnir (detail)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Viking-Age Gotland)
summary:
A detail of the Odin-and-Sleipnir scene from the Ardre VIII picture stone of Gotland, one of the actual carved monuments behind the familiar reconstruction.

## Iconography
The weathered limestone surface shows, in low relief, the rider on the **eight-legged horse** moving toward a hall, with a welcoming figure and interlaced ornament filling the field. The carving is shallow and abraded, typical of the surviving stones.

## Significance
The **Ardre VIII** stone (from Ardre church, Gotland) is among the most reproduced of the [[motif:picture stone|Gotland picture stones]], its Odin/Sleipnir register supplying the standard image of the god's otherworld ride. As a genuine eighth–ninth-century object it grounds the mythological iconography in real Viking-Age stone-carving, and its panels also depict scenes read as the Völund (Wayland) legend. It is documented in Lindqvist's corpus.

## For artists & game designers
The real, eroded stone behind the clean reconstruction — useful as a weathered-relic texture reference where the myth is half-legible in the rock.

citations.text: Statens historiska museum, Gotland picture stones.

title: Odin riding Sleipnir — Gotland picture stone (reconstruction)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (Viking-Age Gotland); rendering by Becherel
summary:
A rendering of the Gotland picture-stone scene of Odin arriving in the otherworld on his eight-legged horse Sleipnir.

## Iconography
A rider on an **eight-legged horse** — **Sleipnir** — approaches a hall where a figure (often read as a **valkyrie**) raises a drinking horn in welcome; below, a ship under sail carries the dead. The composition is the classic upper register of the Gotland image-stones.

## Significance
The scene depicts **Odin** and Sleipnir in a journey to **Valhalla**, the iconographic core of the Viking-Age picture stones of Gotland (Ardre VIII, Tjängvide, and others). These carved limestone slabs are primary visual evidence for the mythology that underlies Norse magic — Odin as the rune-winning, seiðr-knowing god — and for the cult of the slain. They are studied by Lindqvist's foundational *Gotlands Bildsteine* and by later scholars such as Andrén.

## For artists & game designers
The eight-legged horse bearing a rider to a horn-bearing welcomer is the definitive 'journey to the afterworld' image — a complete mythic tableau in one silhouette.

title: The Triquetra — triple-knot Germanic symbol
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (symbol diagram)
summary:
The *triquetra* or three-cornered knot, an interlace symbol found on Germanic runestones, coins, and later as an apotropaic sign.

## Iconography
Three overlapping **vesica** arcs forming a continuous trefoil **knot**, sometimes bound by an enclosing circle. Drawn as a single unbroken line, it has no beginning or end.

## Significance
The **triquetra** appears on Viking-Age objects and runestones (and on the Harald Bluetooth / 'Hand of God' coinage) and in insular manuscript art, where its unbroken three-fold form lent itself to ideas of completeness and protection. In the Northern context it sits alongside the [[motif:valknut|valknut]] as a triadic interlace charm, later absorbed into Christian Trinity symbolism and, more recently, into modern Norse-pagan and esoteric revival iconography. Like much knotwork its protective use is partly attested, partly reconstructed.

## For artists & game designers
A single endless three-loop knot: a versatile 'eternity/binding' glyph that reads as sacred across pagan and Christian settings alike.

citations.text: Hilda Ellis Davidson, *Gods and Myths of Northern Europe* (1964).
George Bain, *Celtic Art: The Methods of Construction* (1951).

title: The Valknut — Odin's knot of the slain (symbol)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Anonymous (symbol diagram, after Viking-Age models)
summary:
A clean diagram of the *valknut*, the interlocking three-triangle symbol associated with Odin and the cult of the dead.

## Iconography
Three interlocking **triangles** drawn as a single continuous (or three-linked) figure — a Borromean or unicursal knot. The form is geometric and abstract, with no figural content.

## Significance
The **valknut** ('knot of the slain', a modern coinage) appears on Gotland picture stones such as [[motif:stora hammars|Stora Hammars]] and on the Oseberg ship's bedpost, almost always in contexts of death and sacrifice, which is why it is read as a sign of Odin's power to bind and loose the fate of the fallen. Its exact ancient meaning is unrecorded, so scholars stay cautious, but it has become one of the most widely used emblems of Norse magical religion. Its triple-triangle structure invites comparison with other knot-symbols of binding.

## For artists & game designers
A single elegant interlace glyph reading instantly as 'death/fate magic' — endlessly usable as a faction mark, ward, or rune-tattoo.

citations.text: Hilda Ellis Davidson, *Gods and Myths of Northern Europe* (1964).
Tom Hellers, *Valknútr: Das Dreiecksymbol der Wikingerzeit* (2012).

title: Vegvísir, the Icelandic 'signpost' stave (Lbs 5472 I 4to)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: Jónas Jónasson (1856–1918), copyist
rights: Public domain. National and University Library of Iceland, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The *Vegvísir* ('that which shows the way') as drawn in a c. 1910 Icelandic magical-stave manuscript copied by Jónas Jónasson.

## Iconography
A figure of **eight radiating staves**, each arm distinct and ending in different runic terminals (hooks, crossbars, dots), arranged around a central hub like the spokes of a wheel. Unlike the regular Helm of Awe, each of the eight branches is individually elaborated.

## Significance
The **Vegvísir** is a wayfinding stave: the *Huld* manuscript glosses it with the promise that one who carries it 'will never lose his way in storms or bad weather, even when the way is not known.' It is a creature of the post-medieval Icelandic [[motif:galdrastafir|stave]] tradition rather than of the Viking Age, but has become a modern emblem of Norse magic. Its earliest attestations are 19th-century manuscripts such as the *Huld* (1860) and copies like this one.

## For artists & game designers
A navigation talisman with eight unique arms: a natural 'compass charm' whose asymmetry makes it more ornate and legible than the symmetrical Helm of Awe.

title: Sámi shaman's drum (after Ernst Manker)
work: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: After Ernst Manker (drawing of a Sámi noaidi drum)
rights: Public domain (after Ernst Manker). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram of a Sámi shaman's drum (*goavddis* / runebomme) after the ethnographer Ernst Manker, showing the painted figures of the drumhead.

## Iconography
The oval **drum membrane** is covered with red-painted **figures** arranged in zones — gods, the sun, animals, dwellings, and the worlds of the cosmos — over which a marker (the *árpa*) jumped as the drum was struck, its resting place read as an omen.

## Significance
The **noaidi** (Sámi shaman) used the drum both for divination and to induce the trance in which the soul journeyed between worlds — a living Northern-European shamanic practice long compared with Norse [[motif:seidr|seiðr]]. From the 17th century missionaries confiscated and burned hundreds of drums; Manker's twentieth-century catalogue is the principal record of their painted cosmologies. The drum is the central instrument of indigenous Sámi religion and a key comparandum for reconstructing Old Norse magic.

## For artists & game designers
A painted drumhead that is itself a map of the cosmos and an oracle device — strike it and read where the marker lands: a complete divination mechanic in one object.

citations.text: Neil Price, *The Viking Way: Magic and Mind in Late Iron Age Scandinavia* (2002).

title: Monkshood (*Aconitum napellus*), Köhler's *Medizinal-Pflanzen*
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Franz Eugen Köhler (chromolithograph)
summary:
A chromolithograph of monkshood (*Aconitum napellus*) from Köhler's *Medizinal-Pflanzen*, the hooded poison of werewolf and witch lore.

## Iconography
The **aconite** plant with its deeply cut leaves and tall spike of **blue-violet 'hooded' flowers**, the upper sepal forming the cowl that gives 'monkshood' its name, with the tuberous root drawn below.

## Significance
**Aconitum** ('wolfsbane') is one of the most lethal of European plants; its aconitine kills by cardiac arrest. Reputed in antiquity to have sprung from the slaver of Cerberus, it served as arrow-poison, as a charm against and a means of becoming werewolves, and — with the [[motif:nightshade|nightshades]] — as an ingredient of the witches' [[motif:flying ointment|flying ointment]], where it may have produced the numbness and racing heart read as flight.

## For artists & game designers
A beautiful blue hooded flower whose elegance hides one of nature's deadliest poisons — perfect for a deceptively pretty toxic plant.

citations.text: Michael J. Balick & Paul Alan Cox, *Plants, People, and Culture* (1996).

title: Male and female mandrake (*Männliche und weibliche Alraune*)
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (German herbal tradition)
summary:
A German herbal plate showing the male and female mandrake — the *Alraune* — as a paired human couple.

## Iconography
Two **mandrake roots** drawn as a **man and a woman**, one bearded, one with long hair, leaves and flowers springing from their heads. The doubling into sexes is explicit, each figure rooted in the soil.

## Significance
In German folk belief the **Alraun(e)** was more than a plant: a carved or naturally formed mandrake root kept as a household familiar, fed, clothed, and consulted, said to bring wealth, fertility, and protection to its owner. The male/female pairing reflects both the [[motif:doctrine of signatures|signature]] of the human form and the charm's role in love and fertility magic. The Alraun trade was lucrative enough to be widely faked from bryony root.

## For artists & game designers
A mandrake 'couple' that can serve as a pair of root-familiars — domestic magical beings rather than mere ingredients.

citations.text: Jacob Grimm, *Deutsche Mythologie* (1835), on the Alraun.

title: The barnacle-goose tree (zoophyte legend)
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (after early-modern sources); Wellcome Collection
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An illustration of the barnacle-goose tree showing the legendary 'development' of geese from a tree, from the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
A **tree** overhanging water from which hang **goose-barnacles**; the shells open and **geese** emerge, dropping into the sea below — the supposed life-cycle of the barnacle goose, half plant, half bird.

## Significance
The **barnacle-goose** legend held that these geese were not born of eggs but grew on trees or from rotting timber, emerging through stalked barnacles. Reported from Gerald of Wales through Gerard's *Herball*, it was a classic [[motif:zoophyte|zoophyte]] — a plant-animal — and a convenient theological loophole: a 'fish-fowl' that might be eaten in Lent. With the [[motif:vegetable lamb|vegetable lamb]] it epitomises the medieval wonder at the porous border between plant and animal.

## For artists & game designers
A tree that grows birds — a striking plant-animal hybrid straight from real medieval natural history.

citations.text: John Gerard, *The Herball* (1597), on the 'goose tree'.

title: Deadly nightshade (*Atropa belladonna*), Köhler's *Medizinal-Pflanzen*
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Franz Eugen Köhler (chromolithograph)
summary:
A chromolithograph of deadly nightshade (*Atropa belladonna*) from Köhler's *Medizinal-Pflanzen*, a central plant of the witches' pharmacopoeia.

## Iconography
The **belladonna** plant with its dull purple-brown bell flowers and glossy **black berries**, drawn whole with root, leaf, flower, and fruit.

## Significance
**Atropa belladonna** takes its genus name from **Atropos**, the Fate who cuts the thread of life — a frank acknowledgment of its lethality. Its atropine and scopolamine made it a key ingredient of the [[motif:flying ointment|flying ointment]], a beautifier (Italian women dilated their pupils with it — *bella donna*), and a poison of legend. With [[motif:henbane|henbane]] and [[motif:datura|datura]] it forms the deadly trinity of magical [[motif:nightshade|Solanaceae]].

## For artists & game designers
The glossy black berry that kills — an instantly readable poison plant with a name that already means 'fate' and 'beautiful lady'.

citations.text: Anthony John Carter, 'Myths and mandrakes,' *Journal of the Royal Society of Medicine* (2003).

title: Oswald Croll, alchemical physician of signatures
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous engraver (portrait of Oswald Croll)
summary:
A portrait of the Paracelsian physician Oswald Croll (Crollius, c. 1563–1609), author of the most influential early-modern treatise on the signatures of plants.

## Iconography
An engraved **portrait** of **Oswaldus Crollius**, the alchemical physician shown in scholar's dress. His name is attached to the *Tractatus de signaturis internis rerum* appended to his *Basilica Chymica*.

## Significance
Croll gave the [[motif:doctrine of signatures|doctrine of signatures]] its most systematic Paracelsian formulation: the *signatura* is the outward mark by which the inner, God-given virtue of a plant may be read. Eyebright for the eyes, heart-shaped leaves for the heart — the herb's form is its label. His *Basilica Chymica* (1609) made [[motif:alchemy|spagyric]] medicine and the signature doctrine central to seventeenth-century pharmacy.

## For artists & game designers
The alchemist-physician who codified reading cures from a plant's shape — a portrait to anchor a 'signature herbalism' system.

citations.text: Oswald Croll, *Basilica Chymica* with *De signaturis internis rerum* (1609).

title: Thornapple (*Datura stramonium*), Köhler's *Medizinal-Pflanzen*
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Franz Eugen Köhler (chromolithograph)
summary:
A chromolithograph of thornapple (*Datura stramonium*) from Köhler's *Medizinal-Pflanzen*, the spiny-fruited nightshade of the witches' brew.

## Iconography
The **datura** plant with its large white trumpet flowers and, most distinctively, the **spiny green seed-capsule** that gives 'thornapple' its name.

## Significance
**Datura stramonium** (thornapple, jimsonweed) is the New-World member of the magical [[motif:nightshade|Solanaceae]], rich in the same tropane alkaloids as [[motif:henbane|henbane]] and [[motif:belladonna|belladonna]] and so a natural addition to the [[motif:flying ointment|flying ointment]] and to delirium-inducing potions across many cultures. Its dramatic visions and frequent fatalities made it both a sacred entheogen and a notorious poison.

## For artists & game designers
A poison plant with a striking spiked fruit — visually distinct from the smooth berries of its nightshade cousins.

citations.text: Richard Rudgley, *The Encyclopedia of Psychoactive Substances* (1998).

title: The Glastonbury Thorn (*Crataegus monogyna* 'Biflora')
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (living tree); photograph by Rimstone
rights: Public domain (CC0, photo: Rimstone). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A Glastonbury Thorn, the winter-flowering hawthorn whose legend ties it to Joseph of Arimathea and the founding of English Christianity.

## Iconography
A **hawthorn tree** of the rare 'Biflora' form, which blossoms **twice a year** — once in spring and again around **Christmas**, the midwinter flowering that made it a marvel.

## Significance
Legend held that **Joseph of Arimathea**, coming to Glastonbury, thrust his staff into the ground at Wearyall Hill, where it rooted and flowered — the original **Holy Thorn**. Its uncanny Christmas blossoming was read as a standing miracle; sprigs were sent to the monarch each year, and Puritans hacked at the tree as superstitious. It stands at the meeting point of [[motif:apotropaic|sacred plant]] lore, relic-cult, and the broader English mythology of Glastonbury as the Isle of Avalon.

## For artists & game designers
A holy tree grown from a saint's staff that flowers at midwinter — a perfect 'miracle plant' tied to a sacred site.

citations.text: Richard Folkard, *Plant Lore, Legends, and Lyrics* (1884).

title: Hemlock (*Conium maculatum*), Curtis's *Flora Londinensis*
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: William Curtis & William Kilburn
summary:
A hand-coloured engraving of poison hemlock (*Conium maculatum*), from the circle of William Curtis's London flora.

## Iconography
The tall **hemlock** umbellifer: finely divided fern-like leaves, flat umbels of small white flowers, and the diagnostic smooth, **purple-spotted stem**.

## Significance
**Conium maculatum** is the hemlock of Socrates' execution-cup, its coniine causing ascending paralysis. In the witches' pharmacopoeia it joins [[motif:aconite|aconite]] and the [[motif:nightshade|nightshades]] in the [[motif:flying ointment|flying ointment]] and in Shakespeare's 'root of hemlock digg'd i' the dark'. Its blotched stem was sometimes read, by the [[motif:doctrine of signatures|doctrine of signatures]], as the mark of Cain or of poison itself.

## For artists & game designers
An innocuous-looking roadside weed — lacy white flowers, spotted stem — that is in fact a classic execution poison.

citations.text: Plato, *Phaedo*, on the death of Socrates.

title: Henbane (*Hyoscyamus niger*), Köhler's *Medizinal-Pflanzen*
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Franz Eugen Köhler (chromolithograph)
summary:
A chromolithograph of black henbane (*Hyoscyamus niger*) from Köhler's *Medizinal-Pflanzen*, one of the chief plants of the witches' flying ointment.

## Iconography
The whole **henbane plant**: sticky, lobed leaves, and the distinctive funnel flowers — dull yellow netted with **purple veins** — with the toothed seed-capsules, drawn with botanical precision.

## Significance
**Henbane** is among the principal tropane-alkaloid nightshades (with [[motif:belladonna|belladonna]] and [[motif:datura|datura]]) of the early-modern [[motif:flying ointment|witches' flying ointment]]. Its hyoscyamine and scopolamine induce delirium, the sensation of flight, and visions — pharmacological grist for the confessions of the witch-trials. Long used too as an anaesthetic and in beer, henbane sits at the crossroads of medicine, poison, and magic.

## For artists & game designers
The quintessential 'witch's weed': a real, deadly, vision-inducing plant with a sinister veined flower.

citations.text: Michael Pollan / Richard Rudgley, *The Encyclopedia of Psychoactive Substances* (1998).

title: Vervain (*Verbena*), *Hortus Sanitatis* woodcut
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (Hortus Sanitatis woodcutter)
summary:
A woodcut of vervain (*Verbena*) from the *Hortus Sanitatis*, the great printed herbal of 1491.

## Iconography
A simple, bold **woodcut** of the **vervain** plant — slender spikes of small flowers on a branching stem — in the schematic, emblematic style of the early printed herbals.

## Significance
The **Hortus Sanitatis** ('Garden of Health', Mainz, 1491) was the most comprehensive of the incunable natural encyclopaedias, treating plants, animals, and stones together. Its herb-entries preserved a wealth of magical lore: **vervain** in particular was the classical *herba sacra* and the medieval 'enchanters' herb', gathered with ritual for protection, love-magic, and the purification of altars. The Hortus's woodcuts shaped the look of the magical plant for centuries.

## For artists & game designers
A stark incunable woodcut of a magic herb — the template aesthetic for any 'old grimoire plant' illustration.

citations.text: Agnes Arber, *Herbals: Their Origin and Evolution* (1912).

title: Mandragora, from Fuchs's *De historia stirpium*
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Leonhart Fuchs (woodcut by Füllmaurer, Meyer & Speckle)
summary:
The mandrake (*Mandragora*) woodcut from Leonhart Fuchs's landmark herbal *De historia stirpium commentarii insignes* (1542).

## Iconography
A finely cut **mandrake plant**: a rosette of broad leaves, bell flowers, round fruit, and the thick, **forked root** rendered with new botanical accuracy rather than as a fully human figure.

## Significance
Fuchs's herbal, with its superb woodcuts by Heinrich Füllmaurer, Albrecht Meyer, and Veit Rudolph Speckle, marks the Renaissance turn toward observed, naturalistic botany. Yet the [[motif:mandrake|mandrake]] retained its occult charge: a narcotic [[motif:flying ointment|nightshade]] long tied to fertility magic, anaesthesia, and the [[motif:anthropomorphic root|man-shaped root]] of folklore. Fuchs's plate shows science and magic sharing the same plant.

## For artists & game designers
A scientifically 'real' mandrake — useful when you want the plant to read as botanically credible before its magic is revealed.

citations.text: Leonhart Fuchs, *De historia stirpium commentarii insignes* (Basel, 1542).
Frederick G. Meyer et al., *The Great Herbal of Leonhart Fuchs* (1999).

title: Fructus mandragorae (mandrake), Tacuinum Sanitatis
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (illuminator of the Tacuinum Sanitatis)
summary:
A page on the mandrake (*fructus mandragorae*) from a Tacuinum Sanitatis, the illustrated late-medieval handbook of health.

## Iconography
A leafy **mandrake plant** is shown with its forked, **man-shaped root** half-drawn from the earth, attended by figures harvesting it. The Tacuinum format pairs each plant or foodstuff with a note on its qualities and dangers.

## Significance
The **Tacuinum Sanitatis**, derived from the Arabic *Taqwīm al-ṣiḥḥa* of Ibn Buṭlān, circulated in luxury illustrated copies for north-Italian courts. Its [[motif:mandrake|mandrake]] page belongs to the wider medieval tradition that read the plant's bifurcated root as a human figure — male and female — and surrounded its harvest with ritual caution. The plant's narcotic alkaloids made it a genuine anaesthetic and a staple of the witches' [[motif:flying ointment|pharmacopoeia]].

## For artists & game designers
A courtly, naturalistic mandrake set in a working garden — a gentler counterpoint to the screaming-root horror imagery.

citations.text: Luisa Cogliati Arano, *The Medieval Health Handbook: Tacuinum Sanitatis* (1976).

title: The man of the mandrake, *Tractatus de Herbis*
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (medieval illuminator); photograph by Jl FilpoC
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Jl FilpoC). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A 'man of the mandrake' from a *Tractatus de Herbis*, depicting the mandragora root as a fully human male figure.

## Iconography
The **mandrake** is drawn as a **standing human figure**, leaves sprouting from the head, the body itself the root — the medieval convention that turned the forked taproot into a little man (and, in pairs, into male and female mandrakes).

## Significance
The *Tractatus de Herbis* tradition (richly represented in British Library manuscripts) is among the chief vehicles for the [[motif:anthropomorphic root|anthropomorphic mandrake]]. The root's resemblance to the human body underwrote both the [[motif:doctrine of signatures|doctrine of signatures]] — like cures like — and the lore of the screaming, dog-harvested root. Such images blur the line between botanical illustration and the figure of a homunculus.

## For artists & game designers
A mandrake rendered as a literal little person — the purest visual form of 'the plant that is also a man'.

citations.text: Minta Collins, *Medieval Herbals: The Illustrative Traditions* (2000).

title: Heuresis presents the mandrake to Dioscorides, Vienna Dioscorides
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (Byzantine illuminator)
summary:
An author portrait from the Vienna Dioscorides (*Codex medicus Graecus 1*, c. 512), showing the personification Heuresis ('Discovery') handing Dioscorides a mandrake root while the fatal dog lies at her feet.

## Iconography
The seated physician **Dioscorides** receives a forked **mandrake root** from the standing figure of **Heuresis**; below her, a **dog** writhes in death. The image encodes the legend that the mandrake, screaming as it is torn from the earth, kills whoever pulls it — so a dog is tied to the root and made to draw it out.

## Significance
The **Vienna Dioscorides** is the oldest and most splendid surviving illustrated copy of the *De Materia Medica*, made for the Byzantine princess Anicia Juliana. This folio is the canonical visual source for the [[motif:mandrake|mandrake]]-and-[[motif:uprooting dog|dog]] legend, repeated for a thousand years in herbals down to the early-modern witch-lore of the screaming root.

## For artists & game designers
The origin image for 'the dog that pulls the mandrake' — a ready-made tragic mechanic in which a creature is sacrificed to harvest a deadly plant.

citations.text: Pedanius Dioscorides, *De Materia Medica* (Vienna Dioscorides, Cod. med. gr. 1).
Leslie Brubaker, 'The Vienna Dioskorides and Anicia Juliana,' in *Byzantine Garden Culture* (2002).

title: Moly (*Allium moly*), the herb of Hermes against Circe
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (plant); photograph by Marie-Lan Nguyen
rights: CC BY 2.5 (photo: Marie-Lan Nguyen). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Allium moly*, golden garlic — the plant long identified with the mythical *moly*, the herb Hermes gave Odysseus to resist Circe's enchantments.

## Iconography
A flowering **Allium moly**: an umbel of bright **yellow star-flowers** above strap-like leaves, rising from a white bulb — the 'golden garlic' that botanists attached to Homer's name for the magical herb.

## Significance
In the *Odyssey*, **Hermes** plucks **moly** — 'black at the root, with a flower like milk' — and gives it to Odysseus as a counter-charm against [[motif:circe|Circe]], who has turned his men to swine. The plant 'hard for mortal men to dig' became the type of the magical antidote, debated by ancient and Renaissance botanists who variously identified it with garlic, snowdrop (whose galantamine genuinely counters anticholinergic poisons), or this golden *Allium*.

## For artists & game designers
The original 'anti-magic herb' — a single plant that protects the hero from a witch's transformation spell.

citations.text: Andrew Dalby, *Food in the Ancient World from A to Z* (2003).

title: Della Porta, *Phytognomonica* (1588) title page
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Giambattista della Porta
summary:
A page from Giambattista della Porta's *Phytognomonica* (Naples, 1588), a foundational treatise of the doctrine of signatures.

## Iconography
A printed page of the *Phytognomonica*, della Porta's work in which plants are read by their resemblances — a walnut for the brain, a scorpion-tailed seed against scorpion stings — the form of a thing disclosing its hidden virtue.

## Significance
Della Porta, the Neapolitan natural magician, systematised the [[motif:doctrine of signatures|doctrine of signatures]]: the belief that God stamped each herb with a visible 'signature' of the body part or ailment it heals. *Phytognomonica* extended his *Magia Naturalis* into a physiognomy of plants, linking macrocosm and microcosm by chains of [[motif:sympathy|sympathy]]. It is a key Renaissance bridge between magic and the emerging natural sciences.

## For artists & game designers
The textbook of 'a plant looks like what it cures' — a clean magical logic for designing herb effects from their appearance.

citations.text: William Eamon, *Science and the Secrets of Nature* (1994).

title: St John's wort (*Hypericum perforatum*), Lindman
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Carl Axel Magnus Lindman (chromolithograph)
summary:
St John's wort (*Hypericum perforatum*) from Lindman's *Bilder ur Nordens Flora*, the *fuga daemonum* or 'devil-chaser' of folk magic.

## Iconography
The **Hypericum** plant with its five-petalled **yellow flowers**, black-dotted margins, and leaves that appear **perforated** — pinpricks of translucent oil glands held up to the light.

## Significance
Gathered at **Midsummer** (St John's Eve), **St John's wort** was the foremost European [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] herb, hung over doors and worn to drive away the Devil, witches, storms, and the night-hag — its Latin epithet *fuga daemonum*, 'flight of demons'. The 'perforated' leaves and blood-red sap (held to be the saint's blood) gave it, by the [[motif:doctrine of signatures|doctrine of signatures]], its power over wounds and evil spirits alike.

## For artists & game designers
The classic protective herb — a sunny midsummer flower that is in fact a ward against demons, the bright counterpart to the poison plants.

citations.text: Carl Axel Magnus Lindman, *Bilder ur Nordens Flora* (1901–1905).
Richard Folkard, *Plant Lore, Legends, and Lyrics* (1884).

title: *The Upas, or Poison-Tree, in the Island of Java*, Francis Danby
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Francis Danby
summary:
Francis Danby's Romantic painting *The Upas, or Poison-Tree, in the Island of Java* (c. 1820), depicting the legendary tree that killed all who approached it.

## Iconography
A vast, blasted **landscape** strewn with bones and corpses, dominated by the solitary **upas tree** on a barren crag — a sublime vision of a poisoned valley where nothing lives.

## Significance
The **upas** (*Antiaris toxicaria*) is a real Javanese tree with a genuinely toxic latex used for dart-poison, but eighteenth-century travellers' tales (notably a 1783 hoax account) magnified it into a tree so venomous that its exhalations killed every living thing for miles — a poisoned desert ringed with skeletons. Erasmus Darwin's verse and Danby's [[motif:sublime|sublime]] canvas fixed it in the Romantic imagination as the type of the deadly plant.

## For artists & game designers
A single tree that poisons an entire valley — a ready-made 'death-zone' biome built around one malevolent plant.

citations.text: Erasmus Darwin, *The Loves of the Plants* (1789), on the upas.
Donald Beecher, 'The Legend of the Upas Tree,' in studies of early-modern marvels.

title: The Vegetable Lamb of Tartary (Borametz)
work: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: Anonymous (from Henry Lee, *The Vegetable Lamb of Tartary*, 1887)
summary:
The Vegetable Lamb of Tartary (*Borametz*), from Henry Lee's 1887 study of the legend — a plant that bore a living lamb as its fruit.

## Iconography
A stout **plant** rising on a single stalk, at whose top grows a woolly **lamb**, tethered by the stem at its navel; it grazes the grass within its reach until, exhausting it, both plant and lamb die.

## Significance
The **vegetable lamb** (*Borametz*, *Barometz*, the 'Scythian Lamb') was a celebrated [[motif:zoophyte|zoophyte]] of medieval and early-modern travel-lore, reported by Mandeville and others as growing in Tartary. Like the [[motif:barnacle goose|barnacle goose]] it dramatised the supposed continuity between plant and animal, and was later rationalised as a garbled account of the cotton plant. Henry Lee's 1887 monograph collected its whole tangled history.

## For artists & game designers
A plant whose fruit is a tethered, grazing lamb — one of the most charming and bizarre plant-animal hybrids in the Western imagination.

citations.text: Henry Lee, *The Vegetable Lamb of Tartary* (London, 1887).

title: The Rose Cross Lamen (Golden Dawn)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Modern redrawing (RootOfAllLight), after the Golden Dawn design
date: Golden Dawn design c. 1888 (modern vector rendering)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (modern rendering by RootOfAllLight; underlying Golden Dawn design is public domain). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Rose Cross Lamen of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, the breast-symbol of the Adeptus Minor grade (a modern vector redrawing of the c. 1888 design).

## Iconography
A cross of many colours bears a central rose of twenty-two petals (one for each Hebrew letter), with the elemental and zodiacal colours, a pentagram and hexagram, and the names of God arranged around the arms. The whole [[motif:lamen|lamen]] is a compressed map of the Golden Dawn's system of correspondences — letters, elements, planets, and Sephiroth in one badge.

## Significance
Designed within the Golden Dawn (largely by S. L. MacGregor Mathers) and worn by the Adeptus Minor of the inner Second Order (the R.R. et A.C.), the Rose Cross synthesises Rosicrucian symbolism with Kabbalah and the elemental scheme. It is among the most reproduced emblems of the order and a key image of the rosicrucian strand of the revival.

## For artists & game designers
A fully encoded 'rank lamen' — colours and symbols all carry meaning, ideal for a meaningful insignia.

title: Frontispiece to La Clef de la Magie Noire (Oswald Wirth)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Oswald Wirth (for Stanislas de Guaita)
summary:
The engraved frontispiece to Stanislas de Guaita's *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897), drawn by Oswald Wirth.

## Iconography
A dense allegorical composition of esoteric emblems — geometric figures, symbolic beasts, and the apparatus of [[motif:ceremonial magic|ceremonial magic]] — assembled as a visual overture to Guaita's treatise on the 'serpent of Genesis' and the misuse of occult power. Wirth's confident draughtsmanship binds the symbols into a single emblematic title-page.

## Significance
The collaboration between Guaita and his illustrator Oswald Wirth produced some of the most reproduced images of the French occult revival. As the gateway image to the second volume of Guaita's *Essais de sciences maudites*, the frontispiece advertises the book's theme — the dangers of black magic — and exemplifies the period's taste for the densely symbolic emblematic plate.

## For artists & game designers
A model 'grimoire title page': pack symbolic emblems into one frame to signal a book of dangerous knowledge.

citations.text: S. de Guaita, *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897), ill. O. Wirth.

title: The Pentagrams of La Clef de la Magie Noire (Goat and Microcosm)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Stanislas de Guaita
summary:
The two opposed pentagrams from Stanislas de Guaita's *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897): the point-up star enclosing the figure of Man, and the inverted star framing the head of the Goat of Mendes.

## Iconography
Left, an upright [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]] holds the human microcosm, head at the apex; right, an inverted pentagram is filled by the horned, bearded goat's head, with Hebrew letters around each. The colour plate makes vivid Lévi's doctrine of moral polarity — spirit-over-matter versus matter-over-spirit.

## Significance
Guaita (1861–1897), founder of the Kabbalistic Order of the Rose-Croix, gave this image its definitive form. The goat-in-inverted-pentagram with Hebrew letters spelling *Leviathan* later became, via Maurice Bessy's reproduction, the basis of the modern 'Sigil of Baphomet.' The plate is a cornerstone of the visual language of nineteenth-century ceremonial magic.

## For artists & game designers
The paired upright-Man / inverted-Goat stars are a complete white-versus-black magic system in one image.

citations.text: S. de Guaita, *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897).

title: L'Ermite (The Hermit) — La Clef de la Magie Noire
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Oswald Wirth (for Stanislas de Guaita)
summary:
*L'Ermite* (The Hermit), an emblematic plate by Oswald Wirth from Stanislas de Guaita's *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897).

## Iconography
A cloaked old man holds a raised lantern and a staff, a serpent coiling at his feet — the [[motif:the hermit|Hermit]] of the Tarot reworked as an emblem of esoteric prudence and the solitary seeker who lights the way through occult darkness. The lantern shields its flame, suggesting wisdom guarded and shared sparingly.

## Significance
Wirth, illustrator of Guaita's works and creator of the 1889 esoteric Tarot, here deploys the Hermit as a moral emblem within Guaita's treatise on black magic, where the figure stands for the cautious adept. The plate shows how the revival's authors moved fluidly between Tarot trump and book-illustration, treating each card as a portable allegory.

## For artists & game designers
The shielded-lantern hermit is the archetypal 'guide who lights the way' NPC — wisdom offered, never forced.

citations.text: S. de Guaita, *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897), ill. O. Wirth.

title: Inverted Pentagram (de Guaita)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Stanislas de Guaita
summary:
A clean rendering of the single point-down pentagram from the diagrams of Stanislas de Guaita's *La Clef de la Magie Noire*.

## Iconography
A five-pointed star with two points raised and one point directed downward — the orientation that nineteenth-century occultism read as the [[motif:inverted pentagram|inverted pentagram]], signifying the dominance of matter over spirit and, in Lévi's and Guaita's schemes, the sign of black magic and the sabbatic goat.

## Significance
The point-down/point-up distinction codified by Lévi and visualised by Guaita is the origin of the now-universal convention that an inverted pentagram is 'sinister.' Stripped to its bare geometry here, the figure shows how a simple change of orientation was loaded with the whole moral dualism of the occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
The minimal 'evil star' glyph — orientation alone signals corruption, no extra ornament needed.

citations.text: S. de Guaita, *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897); É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel* (1856).

title: The Banishing Pentagram with Divine Names (Golden Dawn ritual)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Modern diagram, after the Golden Dawn ritual
summary:
A diagram of the banishing pentagram with the four Hebrew divine names, illustrating the Golden Dawn's Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram (LBRP).

## Iconography
A five-pointed [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]] is shown with the four great Hebrew names of God — *YHVH*, *ADNI* (Adonai), *AHIH* (Eheieh), *AGLA* — assigned to the four quarters, together with the directions in which the star is traced to banish or invoke. The figure encodes the gestures and vibrated names of the ritual.

## Significance
The LBRP is the foundational practical rite of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn and the most widely transmitted ceremonial-magic exercise of the modern revival. Building on Lévi's pentagram doctrine, it ties the drawing of the star at the four quarters to the divine names and the elemental cross, framing the magician's working space. The diagram is a standard teaching aid for the ritual.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled spell-circle: four quarters, four names, traced star — a literal casting-diagram for a warding mechanic.

citations.text: I. Regardie, *The Golden Dawn* (1937–40); É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel* (1856).

title: The Pentagram of Éliphas Lévi
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Éliphas Lévi (Alphonse-Louis Constant)
summary:
Éliphas Lévi's celebrated pentagram, the diagram from *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1855–56) that fixed the meaning of the five-pointed star for modern occultism.

## Iconography
A single five-pointed star is inscribed within a double circle bearing Hebrew letters and Latin words (*Alpha*, *Omega*). Within and around the star are esoteric signs — the heads of the goat and the man, the symbols of the elements and planets. The figure encodes the doctrine that the point-up star (spirit ruling the four elements) is benign, while its inversion signals the triumph of matter.

## Significance
Lévi (Alphonse-Louis Constant, 1810–1875) was the pivotal figure of the French occult revival; his linking of the [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]] to the microcosmic Man and to the doctrine of moral polarity is repeated by nearly every later magician, from Papus and the Golden Dawn to Crowley. The diagram made the pentagram the master-glyph of will and the human form set against the cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'magic star' diagram — point-up versus inverted reads instantly as good versus corrupt magic.

citations.text: É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1855–56).

title: The Pentagram of the Microcosm (Transcendental Magic, p. 174)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Éliphas Lévi (Alphonse-Louis Constant)
summary:
The microcosmic pentagram from A. E. Waite's 1896 English edition of Lévi's *Transcendental Magic* (*Dogme et rituel*), showing the human body inscribed in a five-pointed star.

## Iconography
A nude man stands spread-eagled within a pentagram, head at the top point and limbs reaching the four lower points, surrounded by planetary and elemental signs and Hebrew letters. The image makes literal Lévi's claim that the [[motif:microcosm|human body]] is the measure of the magical universe — a Vitruvian Man recast as an occult talisman.

## Significance
This plate is the most reproduced of Lévi's diagrams and the direct source of the modern idea that the upright pentagram 'is' Man the microcosm. Carried into English by Arthur Edward Waite's translation, it shaped the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn's understanding of the pentagram and the doctrine of correspondences between the human form and the cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
Man-in-the-star: a ready emblem for binding a character's body to elemental or planetary powers.

citations.text: É. Lévi, trans. A. E. Waite, *Transcendental Magic* (1896).

title: Mantegna Tarocchi Figures (engraving history plate)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: After the Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (No restrictions; Internet Archive Book Images). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A 1908 book reproduction of figures from the so-called *Mantegna Tarocchi*, illustrating an early history of engraving (Internet Archive Book Images).

## Iconography
The plate reproduces one or more of the fifty engraved cards of the E-series — figures from the [[motif:mantegna tarocchi|Mantegna Tarocchi]] such as a Muse, a Liberal Art, or a cosmic principle — chosen by the 1908 author as specimens of fifteenth-century Italian line-engraving.

## Significance
The Mantegna Tarocchi was prized by early historians of engraving (Bartsch, Hind) as a landmark of the medium, and reproductions like this one carried its imagery to collectors and, indirectly, to occult writers. The series' ordered cosmos — estates, Muses, arts, virtues, planets — exemplifies the Renaissance encyclopaedic image-system that lies behind the esoteric Tarot the nineteenth century rediscovered.

## For artists & game designers
Public-domain line-engraved figures ready to repurpose as allegorical card art.

citations.text: A. M. Hind, *A History of Engraving and Etching* (1908/1923).

title: Prima Causa and Misero — Mantegna Tarocchi (engraving plate)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi (so-called 'Mantegna')
rights: Public domain. Bibliothèque nationale de France. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving plate of the so-called *Mantegna Tarocchi* (c. 1465) reproducing two cards: *Prima Causa* (the First Cause, no. 50) and *Misero* (the Beggar, no. 1), the top and bottom of the fifty-card series (BnF).

## Iconography
*Prima Causa* depicts the supreme cosmological principle — the [[motif:first cause|First Cause]] of the highest decade — as a radiant emblem of the source of all being; *Misero* shows the ragged beggar at the very bottom of the ladder of estates. Together they frame the entire ascending scheme from mortal poverty to the divine origin.

## Significance
The Mantegna Tarocchi is not a playing deck but an encyclopaedic engraved series in five decades — estates of man, Apollo and the Muses, the Liberal Arts, the cosmic principles and Virtues, and the planets and spheres. Wrongly attributed to Mantegna, it is a North-Italian humanist teaching device whose ordered cosmos profoundly shaped later esoteric image-systems and was admired and copied by Dürer.

## For artists & game designers
Top-and-bottom of a cosmic ladder on one sheet — a compact reference for a 'great chain of being' progression.

citations.text: J. A. Levenson et al., *Early Italian Engravings from the National Gallery of Art* (1973).

title: Il Diavolo (The Devil) — Minchiate of Florence
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: A. Baragioli (publisher), after the Minchiate pattern
summary:
*Il Diavolo* (The Devil), a trump from a Florentine Minchiate deck published by A. Baragioli, 1860–1890.

## Iconography
A horned, clawed [[motif:the devil|devil]] in the simplified, brightly coloured Minchiate style. The Minchiate is an expanded Florentine tarot of 97 cards, adding the four elements, the twelve zodiac signs, and the three theological and four cardinal virtues to the standard trumps — so its Devil sits within a far larger allegorical sequence than the Marseille deck.

## Significance
The Minchiate preserves an alternative, astrologically expanded tarot tradition that flourished in Tuscany from the sixteenth century into the nineteenth. With its zodiac and element cards it is the most cosmologically complete of historic tarots, and it interested occult writers seeking the 'original' astrological tarot. This late-nineteenth-century Baragioli edition shows the pattern still in production during the occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
A folk-bright devil from an astrological 97-card deck — a cheerier, less Gothic take on the trump.

citations.text: M. Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980), on the Minchiate.

title: La Casa del Diavolo (The Tower) — Minchiate of Florence
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: A. Baragioli (publisher), after the Minchiate pattern
summary:
*La Casa del Diavolo* (the House of the Devil — the Tower), a trump from a Florentine Minchiate deck published by A. Baragioli, 1860–1890.

## Iconography
A tower or building struck from above, with figures cast down — the Minchiate's version of the [[motif:the tower|Tower]] trump, here named *La Casa del Diavolo* ('the Devil's House'). The image carries the same meaning of sudden, divinely-sent [[motif:catastrophe|catastrophe]] as the Marseille *Maison-Dieu*, in the deck's lively Florentine colouring.

## Significance
The naming of the Tower as 'the House of the Devil' is distinctive to the Minchiate tradition and underlines how the same trump acquired different glosses across regional tarots. As part of the most astrologically elaborate historic deck, the card belongs to a sequence that, with its zodiac and element trumps, presented the period's fullest tarot cosmology. This late edition documents the Minchiate's survival into the era of the occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
'The Devil's House' as a name for the disaster card — a flavourful regional variant for a tower-collapse beat.

citations.text: M. Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980), on the Minchiate.

title: John Dee's Sigillum Dei Aemeth (wax seal, British Museum)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: John Dee (object); photograph CC0
rights: CC0 / Public domain. British Museum. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
John Dee's wax *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* ('Seal of God's Truth'), the inscribed talismanic disc on which his shewstone rested during the angelic conversations (British Museum).

## Iconography
A disc of wax engraved with concentric rings of [[motif:angel names|angelic names]], a heptagon and heptagram, and a complex array of letters and numbers derived from the names of God and the angels. Dee used it, with companion seals, beneath his crystal to consecrate the field of vision for scrying.

## Significance
The Sigillum Dei has a long medieval grimoire pedigree (the *Liber iuratus* / Sworn Book of Honorius), but Dee gave it the elaborated form preserved here, central to the [[motif:enochian|Enochian]] system he and Edward Kelley developed in the 1580s. Through Dee's manuscripts the seal passed into the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn and the wider occult revival, making this British Museum object one of the most important surviving instruments of Western ceremonial magic.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic, dateable consecration-seal prop — concentric rings of angel names ready as a 'ritual focus' object.

citations.text: British Museum (the Dee wax tablets); on Dee & Kelley, D. Harkness, *John Dee's Conversations with Angels* (1999).

title: Sigillum Dei Aemeth (Dee manuscript, Sloane 3188)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: John Dee
rights: Public domain. British Library, Sloane MS 3188. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
