The drawing of the *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* in John Dee's own working manuscript (British Library, Sloane MS 3188), the record of his angelic 'actions' of 1582–83.

## Iconography
Dee's pen diagram lays out the [[motif:sigillum dei aemeth|seal]] as concentric rings inscribed with the names of angels and the letters of the divine names, structured around a heptagon and heptagram. It is the design from which the wax seals were cut, recorded in the diary of his scrying sessions with Edward Kelley.

## Significance
Sloane MS 3188 (the *Mysteriorum Libri*) is the primary source for Dee's [[motif:enochian|Enochian]] system — the angelic language, the Great Table, and the consecrating seals. The manuscript was studied by the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, whose adepts (Westcott, Mathers) reconstructed Enochian magic from Dee's papers, carrying this sixteenth-century material into the heart of the nineteenth-century revival.

## For artists & game designers
The hand-drawn manuscript version is a more 'authentic notebook' asset than a clean printed seal — good for a found-grimoire feel.

citations.text: London, British Library, Sloane MS 3188 (Dee, *Mysteriorum Libri*).

title: Mato (The Fool) — Sola Busca Tarot
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
*Mato*, the Fool (card 0) of the Sola Busca Tarot, the earliest surviving complete 78-card tarot deck, engraved in northern Italy c. 1491.

## Iconography
A single figure in the deck's distinctive engraved style, named *Mato* ('the madman/fool'). The Sola Busca cards are unusual in depicting named classical and Roman heroes and an alchemical symbolism rather than the standard Tarot de Marseille images. The line-engraving and hand-colouring set it apart from woodcut decks.

## Significance
The Sola Busca is the oldest complete tarot deck known and the only fifteenth-century deck whose pip cards bear full scenic illustrations. A. E. Waite and Pamela Colman Smith studied photographs of it at the British Museum, and several Sola Busca pips directly inspired the scenic minor arcana of the 1909 Rider–Waite–Smith deck. It thus links the [[motif:earliest full deck|origins of tarot]] to the occult revival's most famous deck.

## For artists & game designers
The original scenic-pip deck — a model for illustrating every card, not just the trumps.

citations.text: S. Bartsch & others on the Sola Busca; British Museum impressions.

title: Catone — Sola Busca Tarot (trump XIII)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
*Catone* (Cato), trump XIII of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), the named-hero card that occupies the place of Death in the standard sequence.

## Iconography
A classical figure identified as [[motif:roman hero|Cato]] stands in the deck's fine engraved manner. Where the Tarot de Marseille shows a skeletal Death at trump XIII, the Sola Busca substitutes a named Roman worthy — part of its programme of replacing the conventional allegories with figures from antiquity.

## Significance
The Sola Busca's trumps form a gallery of Greco-Roman heroes (Alexander, Catullus, Nero, Cato), an erudite humanist reworking of the tarot sequence unlike any other surviving deck. Its alchemical undercurrents and classical learning made it a touchstone for later esotericists; the British Museum's photographic set of the deck was directly consulted by the makers of the Rider–Waite–Smith tarot.

## For artists & game designers
Named historical 'champions' standing in for abstract trumps — a template for a learned, character-driven deck.

citations.text: On the Sola Busca and its classical programme, museum catalogues.

title: Lentulo — Sola Busca Tarot (trump XVIII)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
*Lentulo* (Lentulus), trump XVIII of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), one of the deck's gallery of named antique figures.

## Iconography
A standing [[motif:roman figure|Roman figure]] named Lentulo, rendered in the deck's crisp engraved line and martial costume. As with the other Sola Busca trumps, a specific historical or legendary personage replaces the generic allegory of the ordinary tarot — here in the position the Marseille deck gives to the Moon.

## Significance
The substitution of Roman names (Lentulus among the Catilinarian conspirators or a Cornelii Lentuli) for the standard trumps reflects the deck's humanist, antiquarian conception and its likely commission for a learned North-Italian patron. The Sola Busca remains the single most important fifteenth-century deck for the history of esoteric tarot, since its scenic engravings reached the twentieth century through the British Museum and the Rider–Waite circle.

## For artists & game designers
Armoured named figures as trumps — period reference for a martial, history-themed card set.

citations.text: On the Sola Busca, museum and deck-history literature.

title: Nabuchodenasor (Nebuchadnezzar) — Sola Busca Tarot (trump XXI)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
*Nabuchodenasor* (Nebuchadnezzar), trump XXI of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491) — the named king who occupies the highest trump, the place of the World.

## Iconography
The Babylonian king [[motif:nebuchadnezzar|Nebuchadnezzar]] is shown as the deck's final and highest trump (XXI), where the ordinary tarot places *Il Mondo* (the World). The figure is engraved in the Sola Busca's elegant, slightly sinister Ferrarese manner, crowning the sequence of antique worthies.

## Significance
Replacing the cosmic World card with a specific biblical king is characteristic of the Sola Busca's idiosyncratic, learned programme. Nebuchadnezzar — builder of Babylon, dreamer of the great image, and the king humbled to beast and restored — is a rich choice for the summit of the trumps. The deck's survival and its study by the Rider–Waite–Smith circle make it a crucial bridge from the Renaissance to the modern esoteric tarot.

## For artists & game designers
A fallen-and-restored king as the 'final boss' trump — narrative weight built into a single card.

citations.text: Book of Daniel (Nebuchadnezzar); Sola Busca museum literature.

title: Seal of the Theosophical Society
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Theosophical Society (anonymous designer)
summary:
The seal of the Theosophical Society, the composite emblem of the movement founded by H. P. Blavatsky and Henry Steel Olcott in 1875.

## Iconography
A serpent biting its tail (the [[motif:ouroboros|ouroboros]]) encircles two interlaced triangles (the hexagram / Seal of Solomon), within which sit an ankh (*crux ansata*), and at the apex a swastika; the syllable *Om* and the motto 'There is no religion higher than Truth' complete the design. Each element is drawn from a different tradition — Egyptian, Hebrew, Indian — to assert a universal wisdom.

## Significance
The seal is a programmatic image of [[motif:syncretism|Theosophical syncretism]]: the claim that all religions share a single 'secret doctrine.' Theosophy was a major channel of the occult revival, carrying Eastern terminology and the idea of a perennial wisdom into Western esotericism and influencing later movements and modern art (Kandinsky, Mondrian). The emblem distils that universalising ambition into one badge.

## For artists & game designers
A syncretic faction crest in one glyph — ouroboros frame plus stacked symbols reads instantly as 'universal mystery order.'

title: The Kabbalistic Tree of Life (Kircher arrangement)
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: After Athanasius Kircher
summary:
The 'Kircher' arrangement of the kabbalistic Tree of Life, the standard diagram of the ten Sephiroth and their connecting paths adopted by the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn.

## Iconography
Ten circles (the [[motif:sephiroth|Sephiroth]], the divine emanations) are ranged in three vertical pillars and joined by twenty-two paths, each path later assigned a Hebrew letter and a Tarot trump. This particular geometry derives from the diagram in Athanasius Kircher's *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* (1652–54).

## Significance
Kircher's version of the Tree became the canonical form for modern Western occultism: the Golden Dawn mapped the twenty-two Hebrew letters, the planets and signs, and the Tarot trumps onto its twenty-two paths, making the Tree the master-diagram correlating Kabbalah, astrology, and Tarot. It is arguably the single most important structural emblem of the nineteenth- and twentieth-century revival.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made skill-tree: ten nodes, twenty-two connecting paths, each ripe for a correspondence or unlock.

title: Le Diable (The Devil) — Oswald Wirth Tarot, 1889
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Oswald Wirth
rights: Public domain. Bibliothèque nationale de France. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Diable* (The Devil), trump XV of Oswald Wirth's esoteric Tarot of 1889 (BnF impression).

## Iconography
A horned, winged, hermaphroditic devil stands upon a pedestal, one hand raised, a torch or flame above the brow, while two small bound figures are chained at its feet. The design echoes Lévi's [[motif:baphomet|Baphomet]] and the Devil of the Tarot de Marseille — the principle of material bondage and the dark counterpart of the Magician.

## Significance
Wirth's Devil makes the trump a deliberate occult cipher: the chained captives are loosely bound (bondage is voluntary), and the figure carries the elemental and astral signs the revival read into the card. It exemplifies how the 1889 deck re-coded the Marseille images as a kabbalistic and magical alphabet, with the Devil standing for the lower nature that the adept must master rather than a literal demon.

## For artists & game designers
A voluntary-bondage devil — captives loosely chained — is a richer 'temptation' beat than simple imprisonment.

citations.text: O. Wirth, esoteric Tarot deck of 1889.

title: Le Bateleur (The Magician) — Oswald Wirth Tarot, 1889
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Oswald Wirth
rights: Public domain. Bibliothèque nationale de France. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Bateleur* (The Magician), trump I of Oswald Wirth's esoteric Tarot of 1889, designed under the guidance of Stanislas de Guaita (BnF impression).

## Iconography
A standing conjuror works at a table bearing the implements of the four suits — cup, coin, sword, and wand — the tools of the four elements. His broad hat brim traces a lemniscate (the [[motif:infinity symbol|sign of infinity]]) and he raises a wand to heaven, embodying the magician who draws power from above to shape the world below.

## Significance
Wirth (1860–1943), a Swiss occultist in Guaita's circle, designed twenty-two trumps that grafted Hebrew letters, planetary signs, and kabbalistic correspondences onto the Tarot de Marseille pattern — making explicit the esoteric Tarot theorised by Lévi and Papus. His 1889 deck is a foundational document of the occult Tarot and a key influence on twentieth-century esoteric design.

## For artists & game designers
The four elemental tools laid out on a table is the clearest 'mage's workbench' icon in tarot.

citations.text: O. Wirth, *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge* (1927); deck of 1889.

title: Les Étoiles and La Lune (The Star and the Moon) — Oswald Wirth Tarot plate
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Oswald Wirth
summary:
A plate from Oswald Wirth's esoteric Tarot showing two of the celestial trumps side by side — *Les Étoiles* (The Star, XVII) and *La Lune* (The Moon, XVIII).

## Iconography
On the Star a nude maiden kneels pouring water from two vessels beneath a great star ringed by lesser stars; on the Moon a face-in-the-moon sheds drops over a landscape where two dogs bay and a [[motif:crayfish|crayfish]] crawls from a pool between two towers. The pair sets cosmic hope against nocturnal illusion.

## Significance
Wirth retained the traditional Marseille imagery of the celestial triad (Star, Moon, Sun) while overlaying his esoteric correspondences. The two cards illustrate the revival's reading of the Tarot's final ascent: after the catastrophe of the Tower, the Star offers astral hope and the Moon the perils of the unconscious path. The plate is a convenient witness to Wirth's full deck.

## For artists & game designers
Star-and-Moon as a paired hope/illusion diptych — useful for a 'night journey' sequence.

citations.text: O. Wirth, *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge* (1927); deck of 1889.

title: La Roue de Fortune (Wheel of Fortune) — Oswald Wirth Tarot, 1889
work: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: Oswald Wirth
rights: Public domain. Bibliothèque nationale de France. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*La Roue de Fortune* (The Wheel of Fortune), trump X of Oswald Wirth's esoteric Tarot of 1889 (BnF impression).

## Iconography
A great wheel turns on a stand, a sphinx couchant with a sword balanced at its summit while two animal-headed figures rise and fall on its rim — the dog-headed *Hermanubis* ascending and the monstrous *Typhon* descending, an arrangement borrowed from Lévi and Court de Gébelin. The wheel is the [[motif:wheel of fortune|Rota]] of fate and cyclic return.

## Significance
Wirth follows Éliphas Lévi's esoteric reading of trump X, in which the rising and falling figures and the balancing sphinx represent the equilibrium of opposing forces and the law of cycles. The card is a key example of how the occult revival reinterpreted the medieval Wheel of Fortune as a diagram of cosmic law rather than mere chance.

## For artists & game designers
Sphinx-atop-the-wheel with rising and falling beasts is a self-contained 'law of cycles' emblem.

citations.text: O. Wirth, esoteric Tarot deck of 1889; É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel* (1856).

title: *De re metallica* (Agricola, 1556 — title illustration)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Georg Agricola (Georgius Agricola)
summary:
A page from Georg Agricola's *De re metallica* (1556), the foundational treatise on mining and metallurgy whose underground world is haunted by mine-spirits.

## Iconography
A densely illustrated technical leaf showing mining and ore-processing, in the meticulous woodcut style that made the book the standard manual of the metal trades. The plate belongs to the celebrated series documenting shafts, pumps, furnaces and smelting.

## Significance
Agricola described the *daemones metallici* — the kobolds and *Bergmännlein* believed to inhabit mines — even as he sought to systematise mining as a rational craft. His subterranean realm is the empirical counterpart to the Paracelsian [[motif:earth|earth-elemental]], the gnome who lives within the rock.

## For artists & game designers
Authentic Renaissance mining apparatus and shaft architecture for any subterranean, ore-rich setting.

title: *De re metallica* — Book VIII, Plate 28 (smelting)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Georg Agricola (Georgius Agricola)
summary:
Plate 28 from Book VIII of Agricola's *De re metallica*, showing the processing of ore in the German mining country.

## Iconography
Workers tend furnaces and apparatus for crushing, washing and smelting ore, each tool labelled in the manner of a technical manual. The woodcut renders the labour of the mine and smelter with documentary precision.

## Significance
The smelting and assaying operations pictured here are the practical chemistry from which Paracelsian [[motif:tria-prima|spagyric medicine]] borrowed its methods of separation and purification. Agricola's empirical underworld is the rational face of the same earth that folklore peopled with kobolds and the gnome-elemental.

## For artists & game designers
Detailed furnace and ore-washing setups — reference for a working alchemical or industrial smelting scene.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De re metallica*, Book VIII (Basel, 1556).

title: *Basilica chymica* (Oswald Croll)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Oswald Croll (Crollius)
summary:
A leaf from Oswald Croll's *Basilica chymica* (1609), the most influential textbook of Paracelsian chemical medicine.

## Iconography
An early-modern printed page from the great Paracelsian pharmacopoeia, whose famous engraved frontispiece ranges portraits of chymical sages — Hermes, Geber, Morienus, Roger Bacon, Raymund Lull, Paracelsus — around the title. The plate presents the lineage of [[motif:alchemy|chymical]] authority Croll claimed.

## Significance
Croll codified the preparation of chemical remedies on Paracelsian principles, fixing the [[motif:tria-prima|tria prima]] of salt, sulphur and mercury at the centre of pharmacy. His *praefatio admonitoria* on the doctrine of signatures made the book a foundational text of spagyric medicine across seventeenth-century Europe.

## For artists & game designers
The model alchemical-medicine title page — a hall of sages framing a chemical doctrine.

title: Casting Horoscopes / Microcosm (Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Robert Fludd
summary:
An engraving from Robert Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617–21) on the casting of horoscopes, expressing the microcosm-macrocosm doctrine central to Paracelsian medicine.

## Iconography
A diagrammatic plate relating the human figure to the heavens and the apparatus of astrological calculation. The design embodies the principle that the body mirrors the cosmos.

## Significance
Fludd's vast two-cosmos history is the most ambitious visual statement of the [[motif:macrocosm-microcosm|microcosm-macrocosm]] correspondence that underwrote Paracelsian medicine, in which planets, metals and organs answer to one another. The Paracelsian physician read the patient as a small cosmos bound by sympathy to the great one.

## For artists & game designers
A ready cosmological diagram for a system where stars, metals and body parts are linked.

title: Gnome / Earth-spirit (book illustration, 1889)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Anonymous (Internet Archive Book Images)
summary:
A late-nineteenth-century fairy-tale illustration of a gnome, the earth-elemental that descends from the Paracelsian pygmy of the mines.

## Iconography
A small, bearded subterranean figure, the type of the gnome or dwarf, drawn for a household story-collection. The diminutive form and earthen setting mark the being of the element of earth.

## Significance
Paracelsus's *Liber de nymphis* names the gnome (pygmy) as the elemental who moves through solid earth as fish move through water, guarding mineral wealth. Folklore fused this with the mining *Bergmännlein* and *kobold*, and the [[motif:earth|earth-elemental]] became the familiar storybook gnome seen here.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal earth-spirit: small, bearded, keeper of underground treasure.

citations.text: Paracelsus, *Liber de nymphis, sylphis, pygmaeis et salamandris* (c. 1566).

title: *Integrae Naturae Speculum* (Fludd, 1617)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Robert Fludd
rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Fludd's famous *Integrae Naturae Speculum, Artisque Imago* (1617), the "Mirror of the Whole of Nature," diagramming the chain that links God, Nature and Art.

## Iconography
A nude female figure of Nature stands at the centre, a chain running from God's hand in the clouds to her wrist and from her other hand to an ape (Art) seated on the globe. Concentric rings ascend from the elements and minerals through plants, animals, humans, the spheres and the angelic hierarchies.

## Significance
The plate is the canonical image of the [[motif:great-chain-of-being|great chain of being]] and of the Paracelsian-Hermetic idea that human Art continues the work of divine Nature. Nature is *natura naturans*, mediating God's power to the lower world — the cosmic frame within which the alchemist and physician labour. Held by the Wellcome Collection under CC BY.

## For artists & game designers
A complete cosmological hierarchy in one image — gods, nature, the elements, and humanity's imitative craft, all chained together.

citations.text: Robert Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia*, vol. I (Oppenheim, 1617).

title: Nixen & Syrenen (Praetorius, 1666)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Thomas Cross (engraver), in Johannes Praetorius
summary:
A 1666 engraving of nixes and sirens from a compendium by Johannes Praetorius, showing the water-spirits as the early-modern German imagination pictured them.

## Iconography
Water-beings — nixes and sirens — depicted in or beside water, partly human and partly aquatic, in a small densely-worked engraving by Thomas Cross. The image catalogues the folkloric kin of the Paracelsian undine.

## Significance
Praetorius's marvel-collections gathered the folk water-spirits (*Nixen*) alongside learned matter, and they sit close to the Paracelsian undine of the [[motif:water|water-element]]. The plate shows how the technical elemental of occult medicine overlapped with vernacular belief in nixes and mermaids.

## For artists & game designers
A cabinet-of-curiosities take on water-spirits — folkloric, plural, slightly grotesque.

title: Paracelsus (Balthasar Jenichen, after Hirschvogel)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Balthasar Jenichen, after the 1538 Augustin Hirschvogel portrait
rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A sixteenth-century engraving by Balthasar Jenichen reworking the Hirschvogel likeness of "Aureolus Theophrastus Bombastus von Hohenheim, called Paracelsus."

## Iconography
The full Latinised name heads the plate; the physician appears in the familiar fur collar, the linework crisper and the framing more formal than the Birckmann issue. Engraved Latin verses typically surround the bust.

## Significance
Jenichen's plate is one of the proliferating Nuremberg-region reissues that fixed the Hirschvogel face as the standard image of the reformer through the later sixteenth century. Held by the Wellcome Collection, it is released under CC BY rather than as a bare public-domain scan, and is recorded accordingly.

## For artists & game designers
A sharper-line alternative to the Birckmann portrait, good for a crisp engraved "founder's plate" frontispiece.

title: Portrait of Paracelsus (after Augustin Hirschvogel, 1567)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: After Augustin Hirschvogel (print: heirs of Arnold Birckmann)
summary:
A widely-copied bust portrait of Paracelsus descending from Augustin Hirschvogel's 1538 likeness, the closest the iconography comes to a life portrait.

## Iconography
The physician is shown in three-quarter view, broad-faced and beardless, in a heavy fur-collared coat. Surrounding text gives his name and age and frequently the motto *Alterius non sit qui suus esse potest* — "Let no man belong to another who can belong to himself."

## Significance
No undisputed life portrait of Paracelsus survives; the Hirschvogel type, reissued by printers such as the heirs of Arnold Birckmann, became the canonical face transmitted through later editions of his works. It anchors the visual identity of the man who renamed himself "greater than Celsus" and reframed disease around the chemistry of [[motif:tria-prima|salt, sulphur and mercury]]. This is the source-type distinct from the eighteenth-century Cooper engraving.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive Renaissance-physician silhouette: fur collar, level gaze, lapidary Latin motto — instant alchemist-mentor portrait.

citations.text: Charles Webster, *Paracelsus: Medicine, Magic and Mission at the End of Time* (2008).

title: Portrait of Paracelsus (after Quentin Massys)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: After Quinten Metsys (Massys)
summary:
A painted half-length of Paracelsus traditionally associated with Quentin Massys, one of the richer painted versions of his likeness.

## Iconography
The physician sits in a fur-trimmed gown, the hands prominent and a ring on one finger, the face fuller and more careworn than the engraved Hirschvogel type. The painterly treatment gives flesh, fabric and shadow rather than the linear schematism of the prints.

## Significance
The attribution to Massys and the dating are debated, and the picture is best understood as part of the post-mortem portrait tradition that shaped how the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries pictured the founder of [[motif:tria-prima|chymical medicine]]. It complements the engraved portraits by giving the reformer a human, almost melancholic presence.

## For artists & game designers
Use for a warmer, weathered version of the alchemist — less icon, more tired practitioner with expressive hands.

citations.text: Andrew Weeks, *Paracelsus: Speculative Theory and the Crisis of the Early Reformation* (1997).

title: Paracelsus-Denkmal (Paracelsus Monument, Salzburg)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Photograph by 'Eweht' (monument: 19th-century sculpture)
summary:
A photograph of the Paracelsus monument at Salzburg, where the physician died in 1541.

## Iconography
A standing stone figure of Paracelsus on a pedestal, robed and contemplative, set in a public square. The sculpture commemorates the city's claim on the wandering physician, who ended his life there.

## Significance
The Denkmal is a document of [[motif:reception|reception]] rather than of the man himself: nineteenth-century civic culture reclaimed Paracelsus as a heroic forerunner of modern medicine and chemistry, monumentalising a figure once condemned as a heretic and charlatan. It marks the transformation of an outsider into local patrimony.

## For artists & game designers
Useful as a "legacy" beat — the rebel alchemist later cast in stone by the city that once rejected him.

citations.text: Charles Webster, *Paracelsus: Medicine, Magic and Mission at the End of Time* (2008).

title: Paracelsus, half-length, after Tintoretto (engr. F. Chauveau)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: François Chauveau (engraver), after Tintoretto
summary:
A seventeenth-century French engraving by François Chauveau claiming descent from a Tintoretto painting "from life," lettered *I. Tintoret ad vivum pinxit*.

## Iconography
A half-length figure in dark robes, turned slightly, framed by an engraved inscription naming both the supposed Venetian painter and the French engraver. The print's authority rests entirely on the *ad vivum* ("from the life") claim in its lettering.

## Significance
The Tintoretto attribution is almost certainly a later fiction lending prestige to the portrait, a common practice in the seventeenth-century print trade. As [[motif:reception|reception]], it shows how Paracelsus's fame had spread far beyond German lands, with French and Italian names attached to multiply its market value.

## For artists & game designers
A study in the manufactured pedigree — a portrait whose caption insists on an eyewitness origin it never had.

title: *Phytognomonica* (della Porta, 1588)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Giovan Battista della Porta
summary:
A page from Giovan Battista della Porta's *Phytognomonica* (1588), a key text of the doctrine of signatures.

## Iconography
An early-modern printed leaf from the treatise that reads the curative virtue of plants from their outward forms — leaves shaped like organs, roots like limbs. The book systematises the visual correspondences between herb and body.

## Significance
The doctrine of signatures held that God marked each plant with a sign of its medical use; della Porta's natural-magic version paralleled the Paracelsian *signatura rerum* later elaborated by Croll and Jakob Böhme. It grounded [[motif:correspondence|cosmic correspondence]] in a practical pharmacology of resemblance, distinct from the Crollius signatures held elsewhere.

## For artists & game designers
Visual logic for a magic system where an object's shape declares its power.

title: Salamander (from *The Story of Alchemy*)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: M. M. Pattison Muir (after an early-modern source)
summary:
A salamander emblem reproduced in Pattison Muir's *The Story of Alchemy and the Beginnings of Chemistry* (1902), transmitting an early-modern alchemical image to a popular readership.

## Iconography
A salamander shown frontally amid flame, in the stylised manner of a woodcut emblem. The figure is presented as an illustrative cut explaining the alchemists' fire-spirit to modern readers.

## Significance
The plate is a piece of early-twentieth-century [[motif:reception|reception]] that kept the Paracelsian [[motif:fire|fire-elemental]] in circulation, bridging the gap between the emblem-books and the modern occult revival. It exemplifies how popular histories of alchemy recycled and standardised the four-elemental iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A clean, legible salamander emblem suitable as an icon or sigil for a fire faction.

citations.text: M. M. Pattison Muir, *The Story of Alchemy and the Beginnings of Chemistry* (1902).

title: Salamander in the Fire
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
The salamander unharmed amid flames, emblem of the fire-elemental and of matter that survives the furnace.

## Iconography
A lizard-like salamander stands or coils within a bed of fire, its body untouched by the blaze that surrounds it. The image fuses the classical belief that the salamander was cold enough to quench fire with the alchemical reading of fire-dwelling spirit.

## Significance
In the Paracelsian scheme of the *Liber de nymphis*, the salamander is the being proper to the element of [[motif:fire|fire]], as the gnome is to earth and the undine to water. The motif also carries into alchemy as the spirit that endures calcination, and into heraldry and emblem-books as a symbol of constancy in trial.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical fire-spirit: a creature defined entirely by the flames it ignores.

citations.text: Paracelsus, *Liber de nymphis, sylphis, pygmaeis et salamandris* (c. 1566).

title: Salamander Device (BnF Français 934, f. 5v)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Noël Bellemare (illuminator)
summary:
An illuminated leaf from a French Erasmus manuscript (BnF Français 934) bearing the flaming salamander, the personal device of King Francis I.

## Iconography
A salamander surrounded by flames appears within the richly bordered page of Erasmus's *Paraphrase* on Matthew, illuminated by Noël Bellemare. The crowned salamander was Francis I's emblem, glossed by the motto *Nutrisco et extinguo* — "I nourish [good fire] and extinguish [bad]."

## Significance
The leaf shows the salamander circulating as a royal-imperial fire-symbol parallel to its alchemical and Paracelsian life as the [[motif:fire|elemental of fire]]. The same creature thus served simultaneously as monarchic propaganda and as a sign in the natural-philosophical imagination of the period.

## For artists & game designers
A luxury-manuscript treatment of the fire-spirit — gold border, royal flames — for a courtly rather than laboratory register.

citations.text: Bibliothèque nationale de France, MS Français 934, f. 5v.

title: La Sylphe (air-elemental)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Johnson & Berry (print)
summary:
A nineteenth-century print of a sylph, the airy elemental of the Paracelsian scheme as refashioned by Romantic and theatrical taste.

## Iconography
A light, often winged female figure suspended in air, the embodiment of weightlessness and grace. The aerial pose and diaphanous treatment mark her as the being of the element of air.

## Significance
The sylph is Paracelsus's air-elemental from the *Liber de nymphis*; the *Comte de Gabalis* (1670) and later the ballet *La Sylphide* (1832) made it a byword for ethereal femininity. The figure shows how the [[motif:air|air-element]] of occult cosmology was absorbed into salon and stage iconography.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive air-spirit silhouette: winged, weightless, perpetually rising.

title: Undine (book illustration)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A Romantic-era illustration of Undine, the water-nymph who gains a soul through human love, descendant of the Paracelsian undine.

## Iconography
A youthful female figure by or in water, slight and otherworldly, in the sentimental idiom of nineteenth-century book illustration. The setting of stream or shore marks her as a being of the watery element.

## Significance
Undine derives from Paracelsus's *Liber de nymphis*, where the undine is the soulless water-being who can acquire an immortal soul by marrying a human. Friedrich de la Motte Fouqué's 1811 novella made the figure a Romantic archetype, and this illustration belongs to the long [[motif:reception|reception]] that carried the [[motif:water|water-elemental]] from occult medicine into popular literature.

## For artists & game designers
The template water-spirit: ethereal, soul-seeking, bound to a body of water.

citations.text: Paracelsus, *Liber de nymphis, sylphis, pygmaeis et salamandris* (c. 1566).

title: Undine (Fouqué, 1811 edition)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Illustration to Friedrich de la Motte Fouqué's *Undine*
summary:
An illustration from the 1811 first edition of Friedrich de la Motte Fouqué's *Undine*, the work that fixed the water-elemental in the Romantic imagination.

## Iconography
A narrative scene from the novella, the water-spirit Undine among human figures in a landscape, rendered in the linear engraving style of the period. The plate accompanies the tale of her marriage, ensoulment and tragic return to the waters.

## Significance
Fouqué explicitly drew on Paracelsus, and his *Undine* became the conduit through which the Paracelsian [[motif:water|water-elemental]] entered nineteenth-century opera, ballet and painting. This first-edition plate documents the precise moment of that literary crystallisation.

## For artists & game designers
A period-accurate Romantic illustration of the water-spirit's love and undoing.

title: Die Wassernixe (Otto Ubbelohde)
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Otto Ubbelohde
summary:
Otto Ubbelohde's illustration of *Die Wassernixe* ("The Water-Nixie"), one of his celebrated drawings for the fairy tales of the Brothers Grimm.

## Iconography
A water-nixie figure at a well or spring, in Ubbelohde's clean Jugendstil-inflected line. The scene illustrates the Grimm tale in which two children fall down a well into the realm of the water-spirit.

## Significance
Ubbelohde's Grimm illustrations are among the most reproduced German fairy-tale images, and the *Wassernixe* keeps the [[motif:water|water-elemental]] alive in the folk register that runs parallel to the learned Paracelsian undine. It marks the elemental's full naturalisation into popular German storytelling.

## For artists & game designers
A spare, elegant well-and-water-spirit composition in an early-modern-revival line style.

citations.text: Jacob & Wilhelm Grimm, *Kinder- und Hausmärchen*, illus. Otto Ubbelohde (1907–09).

title: Zodiac Man / Vein Man
work: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: Anonymous (National Library of Medicine)
summary:
A Zodiac Man (homo signorum), mapping the twelve signs onto the body to guide bloodletting and surgery by the stars.

## Iconography
A frontal human figure with each zodiac sign placed over the body part it governs — Aries at the head, Pisces at the feet — often doubled as a "vein man" marking the points for phlebotomy. Lines or labels connect the celestial signs to the anatomy.

## Significance
The doctrine of *melothesia* held that each sign rules a region of the body, so a physician should not bleed or cut a part while the moon stood in its sign. This astrological surgery was standard practice that Paracelsian medicine inherited and reinterpreted through its [[motif:macrocosm-microcosm|microcosm]] doctrine.

## For artists & game designers
A classic occult-medical chart: the body as a map of the heavens, ready for an astrological surgery mechanic.

citations.text: National Library of Medicine, History of Medicine Division.

title: Berlin Chiromancy Hand (palmistry diagram)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A chiromantic hand diagram (the 'Berlin Chiromancy') mapping the lines and planetary mounts of the palm in the early-modern German tradition.

## Iconography
The open palm is inscribed with the principal [[motif:chiromancy|lines]] — life, head, heart, the Girdle of Venus, the Line of Saturn — and the fleshy *mounts* at the base of each finger and the thumb, each assigned to a planet: Jupiter, Saturn, Apollo/Sun, Mercury, Venus, the Moon, and the Mars regions. Names and abbreviations label the regions, turning the hand into an astrological chart.

## Significance
Palmistry treated the hand as a text written by the heavens at birth, legible to the trained chiromancer. Such labelled hand-cuts circulated widely in sixteenth- and seventeenth-century German and Latin manuals (Indagine, Cocles, Rothmann), standardising the correspondence of mounts and lines to the seven planets — a scheme that remains the backbone of palmistry to this day.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical palm-map — labelled mounts and lines tied to planets — drops straight in as a diegetic divination chart or a character-sheet overlay.

citations.text: J. ab Indagine, *Introductiones apotelesmaticae ... in chiromantiam* (1522).

title: Della Porta, De humana physiognomonia (human-animal heads)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: After Giambattista della Porta
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A page from Giambattista della Porta's *De humana physiognomonia libri IIII* (first published 1586) setting human heads beside animal heads to argue a correspondence of outward form and inner nature (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
Human profiles are juxtaposed with the heads of beasts so that a shared feature — a leonine brow, a broad bovine jaw, a sharp avian nose — implies a shared temperament. The paired woodcuts are the signature device of [[motif:physiognomy|zoomorphic physiognomy]], to be read across the gutter as visual syllogisms.

## Significance
Della Porta revived the pseudo-Aristotelian *Physiognomonica* and gave the human-animal analogy its definitive printed form. The book became the foundational image-source for the discipline, copied and adapted for two centuries — through Charles Le Brun's lectures on expression to J. C. Lavater's *Physiognomische Fragmente* — and, much later, distorted into the racial pseudo-sciences of the nineteenth century. As a divinatory art, physiognomy claimed to read disposition and fortune from the fixed lines of the face.

## For artists & game designers
The original 'beast-aspect' character sheet: pair a face with an animal to telegraph temperament before a word is spoken.

citations.text: G. della Porta, *De humana physiognomonia libri IIII* (1586).

title: Title page, De humana physiognomonia (della Porta)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Giambattista della Porta
summary:
The engraved title page of Giambattista della Porta's *De humana physiognomonia*, the work that codified Renaissance face-reading as a branch of natural philosophy.

## Iconography
The title is framed by an architectural-emblematic [[motif:frontispiece|frontispiece]] of the kind favoured by late-sixteenth-century learned books, announcing the four books (*libri IIII*) of the treatise. Della Porta's name appears as author; the design positions physiognomy among the respectable sciences rather than the vulgar fortune-telling trades.

## Significance
Della Porta (c. 1535–1615), better known for his *Magia naturalis*, was a Neapolitan polymath whose physiognomy attempted to ground the reading of character on Aristotelian and naturalist principles. The title page is the threshold of a book that shaped every later European physiognomic project; its very design — sober, classicising — was part of the argument that bodily signs could be studied systematically. The Inquisition's suspicion of della Porta's circle shows how close such 'sciences of signs' ran to forbidden divination.

## For artists & game designers
A period frontispiece template for an in-world occult treatise — the architectural frame signals 'serious forbidden science.'

title: Skeleton with Phrenological Skull
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Anonymous (Europe)
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A skeleton whose skull is marked into phrenological regions, illustrating the craniology of Gall and Spurzheim on the bone itself (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The cranium is divided into the bounded zones of the [[motif:faculties|phrenological organs]], drawn directly onto the bone so that the doctrine's claim — that brain organs swell the skull above them — is shown at its anatomical source. The pairing of marked skull with full skeleton gives the image a *memento mori* charge.

## Significance
Franz Joseph Gall's *organology* (later 'phrenology') located the faculties in specific brain regions and inferred their strength from cranial shape; the marked skull was the field's central evidentiary object. Though the localisation of bumps was discredited, Gall's underlying intuition that mental functions are seated in particular brain areas seeded modern neuroscience. As an image it stands at the boundary between divinatory body-reading and emerging anatomy.

## For artists & game designers
A marked skull is a striking prop bridging occult diagram and anatomy — equally at home on a diviner's shelf or an anatomist's table.

citations.text: F. J. Gall, *Anatomie et physiologie du système nerveux* (1810–19).

title: Zodiac Man (Homo Signorum / melothesia)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Anonymous
rights: No known restrictions. U.S. National Library of Medicine. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A 'Zodiac Man' (*Homo signorum*), the figure of medical astrology in which each sign of the zodiac governs a part of the human body (U.S. National Library of Medicine).

## Iconography
A standing figure is overlaid with the twelve [[motif:zodiac man|zodiac signs]], each placed on the body region it rules in the doctrine of *melothesia*: Aries at the head, Taurus the neck, Gemini the arms, Cancer the chest, descending through the trunk to Pisces at the feet. Lines or labels connect each sign to its bodily seat.

## Significance
The Zodiac Man was the indispensable working diagram of late-medieval and Renaissance medicine, used to determine safe times for bloodletting, surgery and purging — one avoided operating on a part while the Moon stood in its governing sign. Rooted in ancient *melothesia* and transmitted through almanacs and *Books of Hours*, it is the body read as a map of the heavens, the meeting point of astrology, medicine and the divinatory reading of the flesh.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive astrological body-map: twelve signs on twelve regions, ready as a wound-system, status-effect, or anatomy UI.

citations.text: C. Clark, 'The Zodiac Man in Medieval Medical Astrology' (1979).

title: Iridology Eye Chart (left iris)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Wikimedia contributors (after iridology tradition)
summary:
An iridology chart of the left iris, dividing the eye into radial sectors each said to correspond to an organ or region of the body.

## Iconography
The [[motif:iris|iris]] is mapped like a clock-face into labelled radial and concentric [[motif:zones|zones]], each sector assigned to a part of the body — brain, heart, liver, stomach, limbs — so that markings in a given zone are read as signs concerning that organ. It is the eye treated as a miniature chart of the whole person.

## Significance
Iridology, systematised in the nineteenth century by Ignaz von Péczely and later popularised by Bernard Jensen, holds that the iris records the condition and constitution of the body. Scientifically discredited as diagnosis, it belongs squarely to the long divinatory tradition of reading the macrocosm of the person from a single bodily microcosm — here the eye, as the palm is read in chiromancy and the brow in metoposcopy. This explanatory chart is a modern Creative Commons diagram, not a historical print.

## For artists & game designers
A radial body-map keyed to a single eye — an evocative diagnostic UI for a healer or seer who 'looks into' a character.

citations.text: I. von Péczely, founder of iridology (19th c.); B. Jensen, *Iridology* (20th c.).

title: Lavater, Essays on Physiognomy (animal-head plate)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: After Johann Caspar Lavater
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A plate from an English edition of J. C. Lavater's *Essays on Physiognomy*, continuing the della-Porta tradition of comparing human and animal heads (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The engraving ranges a series of [[motif:profile heads|profile heads]] — human and animal — for comparative reading of brow, nose and jaw. Lavater inherited the human-animal analogy but framed it within a sentimental-Christian science in which the face is the visible index of the soul's moral worth.

## Significance
Lavater's *Physiognomische Fragmente* (1775–78), translated and richly re-engraved across Europe, was the most influential physiognomic work ever published. It promised to read intellect and morality from facial form, drew sharp ridicule from G. C. Lichtenberg, and yet seeded both the silhouette craze and the later quasi-sciences of phrenology and criminal anthropology. As a divinatory art it claimed to disclose a person's inner destiny from their features.

## For artists & game designers
A ready comparative chart for assigning animal 'reads' to NPC faces in a judge-the-stranger mechanic.

citations.text: J. C. Lavater, *Essays on Physiognomy* (Eng. ed., 1789–98).

title: From Frog to Apollo (Lavater metamorphosis series)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: After Johann Caspar Lavater
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The head of a frog at an early stage of Lavater's famous 'frog-to-Apollo' physiognomic metamorphosis, in which a brutish animal profile is shown transforming by degrees into the ideal human head (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The plate shows one frame of a graduated [[motif:metamorphosis series|metamorphosis series]]: starting from the low, flat profile of a frog, successive heads slowly elevate the brow, refine the nose and lift the whole face toward the classical perfection of the Apollo Belvedere. The single contour is made to carry the whole scale of nature from beast to god.

## Significance
The frog-to-Apollo sequence is among the most reproduced and most criticised of all Lavater's images, dramatising his core claim that moral and intellectual worth ascend in step with facial form. It vividly exposes the dangerous side of physiognomy — a hierarchy of 'higher' and 'lower' faces that nineteenth-century race theory and criminology would seize upon. As divination it reads the soul's rank straight from the profile line.

## For artists & game designers
A built-in evolution slider: morph a face from amphibian to god in even steps for an in-game transformation or alignment scale.

citations.text: J. C. Lavater, *Essays on Physiognomy* (Eng. ed., 1789–98).

title: Goethe's Silhouette (Lavater)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Johann Caspar Lavater (attrib.)
summary:
A cut-paper silhouette of the young Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, of the kind J. C. Lavater used as the purest physiognomic record of a profile.

## Iconography
The black [[motif:silhouette|shadow profile]] reduces the sitter to a single unbroken contour — forehead, nose, lips, chin — stripped of colour and incident. For Lavater this outline was the most reliable trace of character, precisely because it removed the distractions of expression and surface and left only the bony, God-given line of the face.

## Significance
Lavater corresponded with and admired Goethe, who for a time collaborated on the *Physiognomische Fragmente* before recoiling from its dogmatism. The silhouette portrait, cheap and fashionable, owed its late-eighteenth-century craze largely to Lavater's insistence that the cut shadow could be 'read.' This image links the central text of modern physiognomy to one of its most famous (and ambivalent) contributors.

## For artists & game designers
The silhouette-as-soul: a single clean contour can carry identity and judgement — a strong minimalist portrait device.

title: A Profile Judged Harsh by Lavater
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: After Johann Caspar Lavater
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A single profile head that Lavater evaluated in his text as harsh and displeasing — a worked example of physiognomy applied to one face (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The plate isolates one [[motif:profile|profile]] for sustained scrutiny, the accompanying text parsing each feature — the set of the mouth, the angle of the forehead, the line of the nose — as evidence of temperament. It models the method in action: not a chart of types but a verdict delivered on an individual.

## Significance
This kind of case study is the heart of Lavater's project and of physiognomy as a divinatory practice: the confident pronouncement of inner character and worth from outward form. The very subjectivity of the judgement ('harsh and displeasing') is what Lavater's critics, above all Lichtenberg, attacked as arbitrary and prejudicial. Yet such face-verdicts proved enormously influential on portraiture, the novel, caricature and, ultimately, criminology.

## For artists & game designers
A template for a 'reputation read': present one profile plus a damning caption — the mechanic of judging a stranger on sight.

citations.text: J. C. Lavater, *Essays on Physiognomy* (Eng. ed., 1789–98).
G. C. Lichtenberg, *Über Physiognomik* (1778), a critique.

title: Man and Wolf (Le Brun, têtes d'expression)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: After Charles Le Brun (engr. Morel d'Arleux)
summary:
A physiognomic comparison of a human profile with the head of a wolf, after the drawings of Charles Le Brun, first painter to Louis XIV.

## Iconography
The human face is geometrically aligned with the [[motif:human-animal comparison|wolf]] head so that the angle of the brow and muzzle correspond. Le Brun drew a celebrated series pairing men with the lion, ox, eagle, camel and other beasts, plotting the relation with ruled construction lines that lend the analogy a quasi-scientific air.

## Significance
Le Brun's lectures to the Académie royale (1668 and after) on the expression of the passions and on human-animal physiognomy transmitted della Porta's method into French academic art theory, where it shaped the depiction of character and emotion. The plates were engraved and published well after his death (notably by Morel d'Arleux). They stand midway between Renaissance natural magic and Lavater's Enlightenment physiognomy, and fed directly into caricature.

## For artists & game designers
Le Brun's ruled construction lines are a literal recipe for morphing a human face toward an animal archetype — useful for beast-folk or were-creature design.

citations.text: C. Le Brun, lectures on expression and physiognomy (Académie royale, 1668ff.).

title: Métoposcopie: Signs of the Forehead (after Cardano)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: After Girolamo Cardano; trans. Claude Martin de La Vrendière
summary:
A face diagram of metoposcopic 'signs,' from the tradition of Girolamo Cardano's *Metoposcopia* in its French translation, reading the wrinkles of the forehead as planetary omens.

## Iconography
A human face is overlaid with horizontal [[motif:forehead lines|forehead lines]], each ruled line assigned to one of the seven planets — Saturn highest, then Jupiter, Mars, the Sun, Venus, Mercury, and the Moon lowest above the brows. The depth, length, breaks and crossings of the lines are read as the heavens' signature stamped on the individual.

## Significance
Metoposcopy — a specialised branch of physiognomy — was given its classic form by the polymath Girolamo Cardano (1501–1576), whose posthumously published *Metoposcopia* (Paris, 1658) supplied hundreds of forehead diagrams with prognostications, here transmitted via Claude Martin de La Vrendière's French version. The art rests on the early-modern conviction that astral influence is inscribed on the body and can be cast like a horoscope from the skin itself.

## For artists & game designers
A seven-line 'planetary forehead' overlay is a compact, exotic divination graphic for a seer NPC or a scanning UI.

title: Onychomancy Finger Chart (nail divination)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Wyatt Tyrone Smith
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Wyatt Tyrone Smith). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram of onychomancy — divination by the fingernails — keying each finger and nail-mark to a meaning (Portuguese-language version).

## Iconography
A spread hand is labelled finger by finger, the [[motif:onychomancy|fingernails]] flagged as the surfaces to be inspected. In the tradition, the spots, ridges, lunulae and discolorations of the nails — and which finger they appear on — yield omens of fortune, character or coming events, sometimes read with the nails oiled and held to the sun.

## Significance
Onychomancy is a minor sister-art to chiromancy, attested from antiquity (where it was associated with catoptromancy, reading reflections on an oiled nail) and surviving in folk practice. This modern explanatory chart documents the persistence of the belief that the body's smallest surfaces — even the nails — are inscribed with fate. As a contemporary diagram it is offered under a Creative Commons licence rather than as a historical print.

## For artists & game designers
A tiny, intimate oracle: read the marks on a single fingernail — a low-tech divination beat distinct from the grand palm chart.

citations.text: On onychomancy and oiled-nail catoptromancy, see histories of classical divination.

title: Palmistry Chart of the Right Hand (Salmon)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: William Salmon; engr. Frederick Hendrick van Hove
summary:
A detailed palmistry plate of the right hand (Plate XXII) engraved by Frederick Hendrick van Hove for a work of the English physician and occultist William Salmon (1644–1713).

## Iconography
The palm is finely engraved with its full apparatus of [[motif:palmistry|lines]] and mounts, each labelled and keyed to a planet, with the cardinal lines — of Life, Head (the 'natural'), Heart (the 'mensal'), Liver and Saturn — traced across the hand. The mounts of Venus, the Moon, Jupiter, Saturn, the Sun and Mercury rise at the finger-roots and thumb. It is a teaching diagram of professional precision.

## Significance
William Salmon was a prolific compiler of medical, alchemical and occult works for the English book trade; his richly illustrated treatises carried the planetary chiromancy of the Continental tradition to an Anglophone audience. The fine van Hove engraving exemplifies the seventeenth-century 'learned palmistry' that presented hand-reading as a respectable adjunct to astrology and medicine rather than a fairground trick.

## For artists & game designers
A high-resolution, fully-labelled palm chart — ideal as a zoomable in-game grimoire page or UI for a palm-reading interaction.

title: Phrenological Chart with Symbolic Head
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A nineteenth-century phrenological chart whose central head is filled with emblematic symbols standing for each of the cranial faculties (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The design embeds within the outline of a head a set of small [[motif:faculties|symbolic vignettes]], each occupying the skull-region of the faculty it represents — a mother and child for parental love, clasped hands for friendship, a dove for benevolence — making the abstract organs memorable and legible at a glance. The chart functioned as a wall-reference and advertisement for practical phrenology.

## Significance
Charts of this kind were the working tools and marketing of the itinerant phrenologist, who would 'read' a client's head and mark a printed chart with ratings of each faculty. They popularised the conviction that character is fixed, localised and measurable in the skull — the last great flowering of the bodily-divination tradition before scientific psychology displaced it.

## For artists & game designers
The symbol-per-region head is a polished template for a stat or skill diagram where each zone needs an instantly readable icon.

citations.text: On practical phrenology, R. Cooter, *The Cultural Meaning of Popular Science* (1984).

title: Phrenology (American Magazine head diagram)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A phrenological head diagram from the *American Magazine of Useful and Entertaining Knowledge* (1830s), dividing the skull into the numbered organs of the mind.

## Iconography
Profile and frontal heads are partitioned into the standard [[motif:faculties|phrenological organs]], each region labelled and numbered, with a key identifying the faculty — affection, firmness, ideality, cautiousness — supposedly seated beneath. The plate is a teaching diagram for amateur head-reading.

## Significance
This is phrenology in its popular-periodical phase, when the doctrine swept the United States as both parlour entertainment and earnest guide to education, hiring and marriage. Descended from Gall's craniology and physiognomy alike, it treated the head as a legible map of innate character. Its appearance in a general-interest magazine shows how thoroughly the divinatory reading of the body had been absorbed into nineteenth-century everyday life.

## For artists & game designers
A clean, period-authentic numbered head diagram — perfect as a diegetic examination screen or skill-tree visual.

citations.text: G. Combe, *The Constitution of Man* (1828); on popular phrenology.

title: The Symbolical Head (phrenology)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Popular Graphic Arts (Fowler tradition)
summary:
*The Symbolical Head*, a nineteenth-century phrenological chart illustrating all the 'developments' of the human head, each cranial region filled with a vignette of the faculty it governs.

## Iconography
The scalp is mapped into numbered zones — the [[motif:faculties|organs of the mind]]: Amativeness, Combativeness, Veneration, Benevolence, Acquisitiveness, and the rest — and within each zone a tiny pictorial scene illustrates the trait. The whole head becomes an illustrated atlas of character, to be 'read' by feeling the bumps of the skull.

## Significance
Phrenology, founded by Franz Joseph Gall and popularised by J. G. Spurzheim and, in America, the Fowler brothers, held that mental faculties are seated in discrete brain organs whose size, registered as cranial prominences, reveals character. Though discredited as science, it was a vast popular movement and a direct descendant of physiognomy as a divinatory reading of the body. The *Symbolical Head* was its single most reproduced emblem.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'stat map' for a head — a labelled diagram where every region is a trait, ready-made for a character-creation or examination UI.

citations.text: J. G. Spurzheim, *Phrenology, or the Doctrine of the Mind* (1825).

title: New Physiognomy (Samuel Wells, 1889)
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Samuel R. Wells
summary:
An illustration from Samuel R. Wells's *New Physiognomy; or, Signs of Character* (1889), a hugely popular American manual that fused physiognomy with phrenology for a self-improvement readership.

## Iconography
The plate presents heads — human and, in the comparative manner, animal — keyed to 'signs of [[motif:character|character]],' subtitled 'the human face divine.' Wells's book is densely illustrated with such cuts, teaching readers to diagnose temperament, talent and morals from facial form and proportion.

## Significance
Wells, partner in the Fowler & Wells phrenological firm, repackaged the European physiognomic tradition (della Porta, Lavater) for nineteenth-century America's vast market in practical self-knowledge. *New Physiognomy* ran through many editions and shows how the old divinatory art of face-reading survived into the modern era disguised as useful science — advice on choosing servants, spouses and business partners by their looks.

## For artists & game designers
A late, richly illustrated 'face-reading manual' aesthetic — Victorian self-help meets occult diagnosis.

citations.text: S. R. Wells, *New Physiognomy; or, Signs of Character* (1889).

title: Elements of Phrenology, Physiognomy & Palmistry
work: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A large composite instructional plate setting out 'the basic elements' of the three body-reading arts together — phrenology, physiognomy and palmistry (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
The sheet gathers a phrenological head, physiognomic [[motif:facial types|facial types]] (rows of differing noses, brows, mouths, chins), and a chiromantic hand into a single reference chart. Each art is reduced to its diagnostic vocabulary, so the viewer can cross-read skull, face and palm as one integrated system of bodily signs.

## Significance
By the late nineteenth century the three arts were routinely taught and marketed as a package — the complete 'science of character' for the self-improving public. This combined plate captures that synthesis: head, face and hand as three legible surfaces of one fate. It is the visual summary of the whole physiognomy-chiromancy domain, showing how the divinatory reading of the body cohered into a single popular discipline before scientific psychology and genetics displaced it.

## For artists & game designers
A one-sheet 'character-reading toolkit' — head, face and hand vocabularies side by side, a ready reference for any divination minigame.

citations.text: On the Victorian 'sciences of character,' see histories of physiognomy and phrenology.

title: The Aurora Borealis of 19 October 1726
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Philippe Simonneau (engraver)
summary:
An engraving by Philippe Simonneau recording the *aurora borealis* of 19 October 1726 as it appeared across the upper hemisphere of the sky around eight in the evening.

## Iconography
Great radiant beams and curtains of light fan upward across the night sky over a landscape and observers, the [[motif:aurora borealis|northern lights]] rendered as converging rays and luminous arcs — a documentary image of the apparition with its time and date inscribed.

## Significance
The aurora borealis, rare at lower latitudes, was long read as a fiery portent — 'burning skies,' 'armies in the heavens,' or blood-red warnings of war. By 1726 such displays were increasingly recorded as natural marvels to be observed and dated (Edmond Halley had theorised the aurora in 1716), yet the older sense of the [[motif:celestial portent|sky-sign]] persisted in popular reaction. This print sits at the meeting of wonder and natural science.

## For artists & game designers
Radiating curtains of auroral light over a small witnessing crowd is a sublime, non-threatening sky-spectacle — awe rather than dread.

citations.text: Edmond Halley, 'An Account of the late surprizing Appearance of the Lights seen in the Air' (1716).

title: The Pope-Ass and the Monk-Calf of Wittenberg (1523 broadsheet)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: After Lucas Cranach the Elder; text by Melanchthon & Luther
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0. Biblioteca Nazionale Centrale di Firenze (photo Francesco Bini). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The 1523 Wittenberg pamphlet-broadsheet *Deutung der zwo greulichen Figuren, Bapstesel zu Rom und Mönchskalb zu Freyberg*, by Philipp Melanchthon and Martin Luther, pairing two prodigies as anti-papal omens.

## Iconography
Two monstrous bodies face the reader: the [[motif:pope-ass|Pope-Ass]] (*Papstesel*) — the ass-headed, scale-bellied, claw-and-hoof-limbed Tiber Monster of Rome — and the [[motif:monk-calf|Monk-Calf]] (*Mönchskalb*), a deformed calf born at Freiberg in Meissen whose loose skin suggested a friar's cowl. Cranach's woodcuts present each as a single emblematic figure.

## Significance
Melanchthon decoded the Pope-Ass limb by limb as God's verdict on the Roman Church, and Luther read the Monk-Calf as an indictment of monasticism. The pamphlet is the founding text of Reformation 'prodigy exegesis,' turning the medieval *monstrum* (a sign 'shown' by God) into confessional propaganda — the monstrous birth as a printed sermon against the papacy.

## For artists & game designers
The paired-monster broadsheet — two grotesques captioned with their 'meaning' — is a model for propaganda-poster iconography in a setting's faction conflict.

citations.text: Philipp Melanchthon & Martin Luther, *Deutung der zwo grewlichen Figuren, Bapstesel und Mönchkalb* (Wittenberg, 1523).
Photograph © Francesco Bini (Sailko), CC BY-SA 4.0; BNCF Florence.

title: The Comet (Halley) in the Bayeux Tapestry
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Anonymous (Anglo-Norman embroiderers)
summary:
The scene in the *Bayeux Tapestry* (c. 1070s) where a blazing comet — later identified as Halley's — appears as an omen above the newly crowned King Harold, captioned *ISTI MIRANT STELLA* ('these men wonder at the star').

## Iconography
A group of pointing men marvel at a flaming star streaming across the upper border; in the scene beneath, the enthroned Harold receives ominous news, while ghostly ships in the lower border foreshadow the Norman invasion fleet. The [[motif:comet|comet]] hangs directly over the doomed king.

## Significance
The comet of 1066 — the apparition we now identify as Halley's — was read across Europe as a portent, and the Tapestry's designers made it the visual hinge between Harold's coronation and his fall at Hastings. It is the earliest depiction of Halley's Comet and a paradigmatic image of the comet-as-omen, fusing astrology and political prophecy in a single embroidered scene.

## For artists & game designers
A comet placed directly over a ruler, with a fleet ghosted below, compresses 'this reign is doomed' into one legible composition.

title: Cometary Forms (Hevelius, Prodromus Cometicus)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Johannes Hevelius
summary:
Engraved plates A and B from Johannes Hevelius' *Prodromus Cometicus* (1665), illustrating the observed forms and tails of comets.

## Iconography
The plates array a series of [[motif:comet|comets]] with their heads and streaming [[motif:comet tail|tails]] shown in varied shapes, recording the changing appearance of cometary bodies as Hevelius observed them — a systematic visual taxonomy of comet-forms rather than a single omen-image.

## Significance
Hevelius, the great Danzig astronomer, produced the *Prodromus Cometicus* and *Cometographia* (1668) to gather and theorise cometary observations, including the comet of 1664–65. His careful, instrument-based depictions exemplify the seventeenth-century shift from reading comets as divine [[motif:celestial portent|portents]] to measuring them as celestial objects — even as the older dread lingered in the popular broadsheets of the same years.

## For artists & game designers
A comparative plate of many comet shapes reads as a scholar's notebook — good set-dressing for an astronomer or natural-philosopher character.

title: A Crane-Headed Prodigy (Lycosthenes, Prodigiorum chronicon)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Conrad Lycosthenes (Conrad Wolffhart)
summary:
A woodcut of a crane-headed man from Conrad Lycosthenes' *Prodigiorum ac ostentorum chronicon* (Basel, 1557), the great printed encyclopaedia of prodigies.

## Iconography
A standing human figure bears the long-necked, long-beaked head of a crane — one of the [[motif:monstrous race|monstrous races]] and marvels the compilation illustrates, set as an isolated woodcut emblem of a wonder.

## Significance
Lycosthenes' *Chronicon* (1557) gathered every portent, monster, comet, and marvel from Creation to its own day into a single illustrated chronicle, drawing on Julius Obsequens and the broadsheet tradition and feeding directly into the *Book of Miracles* and later teratology. The crane-headed man belongs to the inherited canon of monstrous peoples (the *Plinian races*) reframed as [[motif:prodigy|prodigies]] — wonders that testify to the variety and warning-power of nature.

## For artists & game designers
The animal-headed humanoid is the oldest 'beastfolk' template; Lycosthenes' woodcut catalogue is a ready bestiary of single-feature hybrids.

title: A Many-Headed Hydra (Lycosthenes, 1557)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Conrad Lycosthenes (Conrad Wolffhart)
summary:
A woodcut of a many-headed hydra from Conrad Lycosthenes' *Prodigiorum ac ostentorum chronicon* (Basel, 1557).

## Iconography
A coiling serpent rears multiple necks and heads, the classical [[motif:hydra|hydra]] rendered as a printed wonder among the chronicle's catalogue of monsters and portents.

## Significance
Lycosthenes drew freely on classical sources, presenting mythic beasts like the hydra alongside reported contemporary prodigies, so that the boundary between ancient marvel and current omen dissolved. His *Chronicon* was the most influential wonder-encyclopaedia of the age, an immediate source for the *Book of Miracles* and for later collectors of [[motif:prodigy|prodigies]]; the many-headed serpent embodies the period's fascination with monstrous multiplicity as a sign of nature's (and God's) prodigious power.

## For artists & game designers
The multi-headed serpent is the archetypal 'cut one head, two grow back' boss — Lycosthenes shows its early printed iconography.

title: The Monk-Calf of Freiberg (Rijksmuseum print)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Anonymous (after Cranach prototype)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Rijksmuseum. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A print of the *Monk-Calf* (*Mönchskalb*), held by the Rijksmuseum — a deformed calf read by Luther as a sign against monasticism.

## Iconography
The malformed calf is shown standing, its hanging fold of skin draped like a [[motif:cowl|monastic cowl]] over its back and head, its posture and 'tonsured' appearance suggesting a friar. The print isolates the beast as a single legible prodigy.

## Significance
A deformed calf born at Freiberg in Meissen in 1522 was interpreted by Martin Luther in the 1523 pamphlet as the [[motif:monk-calf|Monk-Calf]], its cowl-like skin a divine mockery of the monastic orders. Paired with Melanchthon's Pope-Ass, it became one of the era's most famous [[motif:monstrous birth|monstrous births]], showing how natural deformities were enlisted as omens in confessional warfare and circulated widely as prints.

## For artists & game designers
An ordinary animal made uncanny by a single suggestive feature (skin draped like a robe) is a subtler horror than full chimerism.

title: A Monstrous Birth (Ambroise Paré, Des monstres et prodiges)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Ambroise Paré
summary:
A woodcut from Ambroise Paré's *Des monstres et prodiges* (first published 1573), the surgeon's celebrated treatise on monsters and marvels.

## Iconography
The plate depicts one of Paré's [[motif:monstrous birth|monstrous births]] — a body with abnormal or doubled members, presented as an illustrative case in his catalogue of natural and preternatural wonders.

## Significance
Paré, royal surgeon of France, gathered prodigies into a systematic work that hovered between the older theology of monsters-as-divine-signs and an emerging naturalistic teratology, proposing causes from the wrath of God to the imagination of the mother and 'too much' or 'too little' seed. *Des monstres et prodiges* is a hinge text in which the [[motif:prodigy|prodigy]] begins to migrate from omen toward object of medical study, even as the wonder remains.

## For artists & game designers
Paré's measured, almost clinical presentation of monsters offers a 'field-guide' visual register distinct from the lurid broadsheet — useful for a scholarly bestiary.

citations.text: Ambroise Paré, *Des monstres et prodiges* (1573); ed. Jean Céard (1971).

title: The Celestial Phenomenon over Nuremberg, 14 April 1561
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Hanns Glaser (printer)
summary:
The famous broadsheet printed by Hanns Glaser describing a *Himmelserscheinung* — a mass of objects seen battling in the sky over Nuremberg at dawn on 14 April 1561.

## Iconography
Above the city's skyline the sky teems with shapes: blood-red, black, and blue [[motif:globes and crosses|globes, crosses, and cylindrical 'rods']], crescents and a great black spear-like form, which the text says appeared to fight one another before many fell to earth as if burning. The sun rises at the centre amid the swarming forms.

## Significance
Glaser's account, set in letterpress beneath the woodcut, reports the spectacle as a divine warning to repentance — a classic *Himmelsschlacht* broadsheet of the kind that proliferated in Reformation Germany. Most likely a sun-dog and halo display elaborated by report, it became, centuries later, a celebrated item in UFO literature. In its own context it is a supreme example of the celestial-prodigy news-print.

## For artists & game designers
A chaotic swarm of abstract geometric forms (globes, rods, crosses) reads as 'unexplained sky event' far more uncannily than recognisable creatures.

citations.text: Hanns Glaser, broadsheet on the Nuremberg apparition (1561); Zentralbibliothek Zürich, Wickiana.
Jürgen Beyer, on early-modern prodigy broadsheets.

title: The Pope-Ass / Tiber Monster (Rijksmuseum print)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Anonymous (after Cranach prototype)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Rijksmuseum. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A print of the *Pope-Ass* (the Tiber Monster), held by the Rijksmuseum, depicting the composite monstrous body that Reformation polemic turned against the papacy.

## Iconography
The creature stands erect as a single full-length figure: an ass's head atop a scaly human-like torso, one hand a claw and one foot a hoof, the rump terminating in a grotesque second face and a tail. Each [[motif:pope-ass|incongruous member]] is an emblem awaiting interpretation.

## Significance
The Tiber Monster, reportedly washed up at Rome in 1496, was reinterpreted by Melanchthon in 1523 as the *Papstesel*, every limb glossed as a sign of ecclesiastical corruption. This print belongs to the wide diffusion of the image across the sixteenth century, by which a single 'monstrous birth' became one of the most reproduced anti-papal prodigies of the Reformation — the [[motif:monstrous birth|monstrum]] as durable propaganda.

## For artists & game designers
A standing, full-figure composite beast (animal head, mismatched limbs, second face on the rump) gives a memorable silhouette for an allegorical monster.

title: The Köllbigk Dancers Prodigy (Lycosthenes)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Conrad Lycosthenes (Conrad Wolffhart)
summary:
A woodcut from Lycosthenes' *Prodigiorum ac ostentorum chronicon* (1557) depicting the legendary cursed dancers of Köllbigk.

## Iconography
A ring of figures dances in a churchyard, locked in compulsive motion — the [[motif:dancing mania|dancers of Köllbigk]] who, by legend, were condemned to dance without rest for a year as punishment for profaning Christmas Mass.

## Significance
The Köllbigk dancers (a medieval miracle-legend set in 1021) were absorbed into the prodigy tradition as a wonder of divine chastisement, and later read against the historical outbreaks of [[motif:dancing mania|dancing mania]] (*choreomania*) in the Rhineland. Lycosthenes catalogues it among his chronicle of marvels, illustrating how the wonder-books blended hagiographic legend, social memory, and the reading of strange events as signs of God's judgement.

## For artists & game designers
A cursed, never-ending dance is a haunting affliction-motif — a crowd trapped in motion as a visible curse.

title: Cometary Observations, 1668 (Lubieniecki, Theatrum Cometicum)
work: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: Stanisław Lubieniecki
summary:
An engraved plate, *Figura observationum Cometicarum* (1668), from Stanisław Lubieniecki's *Theatrum Cometicum*, charting the observed path of a comet across the constellations.

## Iconography
A detailed [[motif:celestial map|celestial map]] traces the looping track of a [[motif:comet|comet]] against engraved constellation figures and a grid of coordinates, the comet's successive positions marked across the star-field. The plate combines astronomical record with the decorative constellation imagery of the baroque atlas.

## Significance
Lubieniecki's *Theatrum Cometicum* (Amsterdam, 1668) was a vast compilation of every recorded comet from antiquity onward, gathering observations and correspondence from astronomers across Europe — a monument straddling the older comet-as-omen tradition and the new mathematical astronomy that, with Halley and Newton, would soon tame the comet into a periodic body obeying gravity. It marks the transition of the comet from prodigy to predictable phenomenon.

## For artists & game designers
The overlaid comet-path on a constellation map is an elegant way to show a portent being studied rather than merely feared — science meeting omen.
