Sanguigno is a genial figure — hot and moist, governed by blood, the element of air and the planet Jupiter — associated with music, merriment, love, and a ruddy good health.

## Significance
Within Ripa's set of [[motif:four-temperaments|four temperaments]], the sanguine is the most fortunate, keyed to Jupiter and air. The scheme tied bodily humour to planetary rulership and season, the foundation of the astrological medicine and melothesia that governed Renaissance ideas of health, character, and the timing of cures.

## For artists & game designers
Air, a musical instrument, and rosy good cheer give a clear 'optimist / bard' read within a four-humour roster.

title: Sapienza (Wisdom), 1611 edition
work: Cesare Ripa's Iconologia — Dictionary of Personifications
creator: Cesare Ripa
summary:
The personification of **Wisdom** (*Sapienza*) from the 1611 illustrated *Iconologia* of Cesare Ripa.

## Iconography
Sapienza appears as a dignified female figure bearing the attributes of wisdom — a book, a flame or light signifying illumination, and a composed bearing that marks her as the embodiment of right understanding.

## Significance
Ripa's *Sapienza* draws on the biblical and Neoplatonic figure of [[motif:divine-wisdom|Wisdom]] (Sophia/Sapientia), the feminine divine knowledge that orders creation. The personification fed directly into the esoteric reverence for Sophia in Christian Cabala, Boehmist theosophy, and later Rosicrucian imagery.

## For artists & game designers
Light + book = wisdom; keep the light a small steady flame rather than a halo to read as inner illumination.

title: Sapienza (Wisdom), 1644 edition
work: Cesare Ripa's Iconologia — Dictionary of Personifications
creator: after Cesare Ripa (1644 English 'Tempest' edition)
summary:
The personification of **Wisdom** as rendered in the 1644 English-text edition of Ripa's *Iconologia*, a single-figure line plate.

## Iconography
Sapienza holds the attributes of true understanding — a book and a flame or lamp of intellect — in the spare engraved style of the Tempest edition, where each personification stands alone for ease of reference.

## Significance
The survival of [[motif:divine-wisdom|Wisdom]] across Ripa's editions, from Italian woodcut to English line engraving, shows how stable the attribute-set became: the flame of intellect and the book remained the fixed signs of *Sapientia* for generations of artists.

## For artists & game designers
A clean, copyable line version — good reference for a minimal 'wisdom' icon stripped to flame and book.

title: Scienza (Knowledge / Science)
work: Cesare Ripa's Iconologia — Dictionary of Personifications
creator: after Cesare Ripa (1644 English 'Tempest' edition)
summary:
The personification of **Knowledge** (*Scienza*) from Ripa's 1644 *Iconologia*, sure understanding of causes.

## Iconography
Scienza holds a book and a flame or light and is often winged, with a scepter — signs that true knowledge is certain, illuminating, and elevates the mind above mere opinion.

## Significance
Ripa distinguishes [[motif:ascent-of-the-mind|Scienza]] (demonstrative, certain knowledge) from Opinion and from the practical arts, mirroring the Aristotelian hierarchy of the disciplines that organized the early-modern map of learning within which alchemy, astrology, and natural magic sought their place.

## For artists & game designers
Wings + flame + book reads as 'enlightened knowledge'; remove the wings for an ordinary scholar.

title: Theologia (Theology)
work: Cesare Ripa's Iconologia — Dictionary of Personifications
creator: after Cesare Ripa (1644 English 'Tempest' edition)
summary:
The personification of **Theology** from Ripa's 1644 *Iconologia*, the discipline Ripa ranks above the other sciences.

## Iconography
Theologia is shown with a flame above her head (the light of revelation) and frequently a double face — one turned to the contemplation of God, one to the teaching of the world — together with the book of Scripture.

## Significance
Ripa's two-faced, fire-crowned [[motif:divine-and-revealed-knowledge|Theologia]] embodies the medieval idea of theology as the queen of the sciences, knowledge that descends from God. The motif of revealed light over the head recurs in Hermetic and Rosicrucian frontispieces depicting divine illumination of the adept.

## For artists & game designers
Flame-above-the-head is a compact glyph for 'revealed' or god-granted knowledge, distinct from a lamp the figure merely holds.

title: Verità (Truth)
work: Cesare Ripa's Iconologia — Dictionary of Personifications
creator: Cesare Ripa
summary:
The personification of **Truth** (*Verità*) from the 1603 *Iconologia* of Cesare Ripa, the 'naked truth' that conceals nothing.

## Iconography
Verità is figured nude (truth has nothing to hide), holding the sun (truth loves the light), an open book, and often the palm of victory or treading on the globe of worldly falsehood.

## Significance
Ripa's [[motif:naked-truth|naked Truth]] holding the sun is the canonical Western image of *Veritas*, drawing on the proverb *veritas filia temporis* ('Truth the daughter of Time'). The image was endlessly reused — from emblem books to political allegory — and supplied the visual logic for 'unveiling' as the disclosure of hidden truth, a metaphor central to alchemical and Hermetic revelation.

## For artists & game designers
Nudity + sun is the immediate sign of unconcealed truth; add a veil being lifted for the 'revelation' beat.

title: Emblem from Michael Maier, *Atalanta Fugiens*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Michael Maier (engraved by Matthäus Merian)
summary:
An emblem from Michael Maier's *Atalanta Fugiens* (Oppenheim, 1617–18), the celebrated alchemical emblem book that fuses image, epigram, and music (DPLA reproduction).

## Iconography
The page pairs a finely engraved emblematic scene — an allegorical figure acting out a stage of the alchemical work in a landscape — with a Latin motto, an explanatory epigram, and, in the full book, a three-voice musical fugue. The myth of Atalanta and the golden apples supplies the governing conceit of pursuit, delay, and capture.

## Significance
*Atalanta Fugiens* is the most sophisticated of the early-modern emblem books: each of its fifty emblems is a *fuga* in which the music chases its own theme as the alchemist chases the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]]. Maier, a physician at the court of Rudolf II and a defender of the Rosicrucian cause, here weds [[motif:alchemical emblem|emblematics]] to counterpoint, making the multimedia masterpiece of Hermetic art.

## For artists & game designers
A template for synaesthetic puzzle design: each emblem ties a picture, a motto, and a melodic line, so solving one channel hints at the others.

citations.text: H. M. E. de Jong, *Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems* (1969).

title: Emblem 21 (Squaring the Circle), Michael Maier, *Atalanta Fugiens*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Michael Maier (engraved by Matthäus Merian)
summary:
Emblem 21 of Michael Maier's *Atalanta Fugiens* (1617–18), the famous "squaring of the circle" figure of the alchemical work.

## Iconography
A philosopher with a compass inscribes a geometric construction: a man and a woman stand within a circle, around them a square, and around that a triangle, the whole enclosed again in a great circle. The motto instructs the adept to "make of man and woman a circle, thence a square, thence a triangle, and back to a circle" — and the philosophers' stone will arise.

## Significance
The emblem encodes the alchemical *quadratura circuli*: the [[motif:squaring the circle|squaring of the circle]] as a figure for resolving the four elements (square), the body-soul-spirit triad (triangle), and the unity of the stone (circle). It is among the most reproduced of all Hermetic diagrams and was singled out by C. G. Jung as a key emblem of the union of opposites.

## For artists & game designers
A nested circle–square–triangle motif makes an elegant sigil or summoning-ring whose layers must be drawn in the correct order.

title: The Philosophic Globe (*Philosophische Kugel*), Jacob Böhme
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: after Jacob Böhme (engraving in Theosophical Works)
summary:
The *Philosophische Kugel* or "Philosophic Globe" (also called the Wonder Eye of Eternity), the central diagram of Jacob Böhme's theosophy, engraved for his collected *Theosophical Works*.

## Iconography
Two intersecting circles or hemispheres form an eye-like figure divided into a light world and a dark world, with the heart of God and the regenerate soul at the point where they cross. Concentric labels in German map the eternal nature: wrath and love, fire and light, the wheel of the qualities turning between the two principles.

## Significance
Böhme (1575–1624), the "Teutonic philosopher," used the [[motif:philosophic globe|globe]] to picture his vision of God's self-revelation out of the unground (*Ungrund*) through opposing principles of darkness and light. The diagram condenses his difficult theosophy into a contemplative image and was hugely influential on later esotericism, Pietism, and Romantic thought, transmitted especially through the Amsterdam editions of his works.

## For artists & game designers
A two-world toggle: a single eye-shaped map whose light and dark hemispheres reconfigure the same locations into wrath and grace versions.

title: Emblematic Plate from Comenius, *Orbis Sensualium Pictus*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Johann Amos Comenius (Jan Komenský)
summary:
A plate from Johann Amos Comenius's *Orbis Sensualium Pictus* (The Visible World in Pictures, Nuremberg, 1658), the first widely used illustrated schoolbook.

## Iconography
The page sets a woodcut or engraved scene over a bilingual caption, each object in the picture keyed by a small number to its name in the surrounding text. Opening plates move from God and the cosmos down through the elements and creatures, building a visual encyclopedia of the world.

## Significance
Comenius (1592–1670), a Bohemian Brethren bishop linked to Andreae's reforming circle, designed the *Orbis Pictus* as the seed of his [[motif:pansophia|pansophia]] — a universal, divinely ordered knowledge in which word and image together restore the harmony of creation. Though a children's primer, its program of [[motif:word and image|picture-and-word]] correspondence belongs to the same emblematic and Rosicrucian-reformist impulse that shaped seventeenth-century Hermetic thought.

## For artists & game designers
A numbered hotspot codex: an illustrated world-page where every object is a clickable, named lexicon entry.

citations.text: Daniel Murphy, *Comenius: A Critical Reassessment of his Life and Work* (1995).

title: Heart Emblem from Daniel Cramer, *Emblemata Sacra*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Daniel Cramer
rights: No restrictions (institutional release). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A heart-emblem from Daniel Cramer's *Emblemata Sacra* (Sacred Emblems, Frankfurt, 1624), fifty engraved emblems of the regenerate Christian heart.

## Iconography
A single human heart is acted upon by divine agency — pierced, refined in fire, watered, planted, or crowned — within a roundel framed by a scriptural motto in Latin. A hand or beam from heaven works upon the heart while emblematic attributes (cross, rose, anchor, flame) mark each spiritual operation.

## Significance
Cramer's heart-emblems translate the alchemical refining of metals into the [[motif:devotional emblem|devotional refining]] of the soul, and his deliberate pairing of the [[motif:rose|rose]] and the [[motif:cross|cross]] resonated strongly with Rosicrucian and later Pietist piety. The *Emblemata Sacra* became one of the most reprinted Protestant emblem books, a bridge between Hermetic transmutation and Lutheran inwardness.

## For artists & game designers
A 'condition of the heart' status icon that visibly transmutes — pierced, burning, blooming — as the player's moral state changes.

citations.text: Peter M. Daly, studies in emblem literature (1998).

title: *Fama Fraternitatis* — Rosicrucian Manifesto
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Anonymous (Tübingen circle; assoc. J. V. Andreae)
summary:
A page of the *Fama Fraternitatis* (first published 1614), the founding Rosicrucian manifesto announcing the Brotherhood of the Rose Cross and the legendary Christian Rosenkreutz.

## Iconography
The reproduction shows the letterpress text of the manifesto rather than a pictorial plate: dense black-letter or roman type setting out the call for a "general reformation" and the story of the order. Such printed pages were the actual medium through which the Rosicrucian furore spread across Protestant Europe.

## Significance
The *Fama* — issued at Kassel and circulated with the *Confessio Fraternitatis* — narrates the discovery of the incorrupt body and luminous vault of [[motif:christian rosenkreutz|Christian Rosenkreutz]] and summons the learned to a reform of arts, sciences, and religion. Probably composed within the Tübingen circle around Johann Valentin Andreae, it ignited a wave of pamphlets, defences, and denunciations and stands at the head of the whole Rosicrucian tradition.

## For artists & game designers
An in-world found document: a printed manifesto whose discovery sets the player's faction and quest in motion.

citations.text: *Fama Fraternitatis des löblichen Ordens des Rosenkreutzes* (Kassel, 1614).

title: *Anima Mundi* (World Soul), Robert Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Robert Fludd (engraved by Matthäus Merian / de Bry workshop)
place: Oppenheim / German lands (for an English author)
summary:
An engraving of the *Anima Mundi*, the World Soul, from Robert Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia* (The History of the Two Worlds, Oppenheim, 1617–21).

## Iconography
A female figure personifying the soul of the world stands within concentric cosmic bands, linked by a chain to the divine name above and, below, to the "ape of nature" — the figure of Art imitating Nature. The hierarchy descends from God through the angelic, celestial, and elemental realms to the human and mechanical arts, every level bound to the next in a single graduated chain.

## Significance
Fludd's engravings, produced by the de Bry / Merian workshop, are among the most ambitious diagrams of [[motif:macrocosm-microcosm|macrocosm and microcosm]] in the Hermetic tradition. The [[motif:anima mundi|World Soul]] mediates between the Creator and creation, expressing the Neoplatonic and Rosicrucian conviction that one living spirit pervades the whole graduated [[motif:great chain of being|chain of being]].

## For artists & game designers
A vertical progression diagram — divine name at the top, mechanical arts at the bottom — that doubles as a skill-tree spine linking heaven, cosmos, and craft.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin, *Robert Fludd: Hermetic Philosopher and Surveyor of Two Worlds* (1979).

title: The Divine Names & Sephirotic Diagram, *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Anonymous (Rosicrucian compiler)
summary:
A coloured plate from the *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer* (1785) setting out the divine names and the order of emanation in concentric and radiating figures.

## Iconography
Concentric rings and rayed circles carry the names of God — the Hebrew Tetragrammaton and its permutations, together with German and Latin titles of the divine — arranged around a central sun or sacred monogram. The composition fans out from the unity of the Godhead through descending bands toward the created world, the whole drawn in fine engraved line and washed in transparent colour.

## Significance
The plate condenses the [[motif:christian kabbalah|Christian-Kabbalistic]] doctrine that creation proceeds by the unfolding of the [[motif:divine names|names of God]]. Rosicrucian theosophy absorbed this from Reuchlin, Agrippa, and the Lurianic currents transmitted through Christian Hebraists, mapping the sephirotic descent onto a contemplative diagram of light radiating from the centre.

## For artists & game designers
A radial UI of nested name-rings — rotating them to align the divine names could serve as a sigil-puzzle interface.

title: The Alchemical Mountain, *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Anonymous (Rosicrucian compiler)
summary:
A hand-coloured emblematic plate from the *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer* (Secret Symbols of the Rosicrucians, Altona, 1785–88), depicting the alchemical work as the ascent of a hidden mountain.

## Iconography
A conical mountain rises through a wild landscape; figures toil up its slopes toward a temple or treasure concealed at the summit. Emblematic creatures and bodies — among them a blindfolded wanderer and heraldic beasts such as the lion — populate the path, while Latin and German mottoes label the stages of the journey. Bright transparent watercolour over the engraved line is characteristic of the printed plates of this compilation.

## Significance
The [[motif:alchemical mountain|mountain of the adepts]] is one of the most enduring images of the Hermetic tradition: the long, dangerous ascent dramatises the *opus alchymicum* as a spiritual labour, the summit holding the [[motif:philosophers stone|philosophers' stone]]. The *Geheime Figuren* gathered emblems from earlier Rosicrucian and theosophical sources into a single luxuriantly coloured corpus that became, through later facsimiles, the most widely reproduced body of Rosicrucian imagery.

## For artists & game designers
A complete level map in one image: a tiered mountain whose switchbacks are stations of trial, each marked by an emblem-puzzle.

citations.text: *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer aus dem 16ten und 17ten Jahrhundert* (Altona, 1785–88).

title: The Citadel of Wisdom (Plate 8), Heinrich Khunrath, *Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Heinrich Khunrath
summary:
The great fold-out "Citadel of Wisdom" engraving (plate 8) from Heinrich Khunrath's *Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae* (Amphitheatre of Eternal Wisdom, Hamburg 1595 / Hanau 1609), here in very high resolution.

## Iconography
A circular fortress is ringed by many concentric walls pierced by gates; at the centre stands the temple or treasure of divine Wisdom. Around the perimeter are scattered the false paths and a monstrous figure of ignorance, while inscriptions name the moral and spiritual conditions for entry. Most gates are barred; only the rightly prepared seeker finds the single true way in.

## Significance
Khunrath's [[motif:citadel of wisdom|citadel]] is a theosophical labyrinth: the many gates correspond to disciplines and virtues, and the maze warns that mere learning or alchemical greed leads only to the monster, not the centre. Bridging Paracelsian alchemy, Christian mysticism, and the dawning Rosicrucian sensibility, the *Amphitheatrum* offered one of the most influential images of wisdom as a guarded, hard-won sanctuary.

## For artists & game designers
A dungeon-as-mandala: concentric ringed walls with twenty-one gates, all but one false, and a beast guarding the wrong turnings.

citations.text: Peter Forshaw, work on Heinrich Khunrath and the *Amphitheatrum* (2000s).

title: The Oratory–Laboratory, Heinrich Khunrath, *Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Heinrich Khunrath (engraved by Paullus van der Doort)
summary:
The celebrated "Oratory–Laboratory" plate from Khunrath's *Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae*, in which the alchemist's workroom and his place of prayer occupy a single sacred space.

## Iconography
On the left the adept kneels before an oratory tent inscribed with prayers; on the right stands the laboratory with its furnaces and vessels. Between them a central table bears musical instruments, and an inscription proclaims that nothing succeeds without God: "oratory and laboratory" must be joined. The hall is filled with Latin mottoes mapping prayer onto practical work.

## Significance
The plate is the visual creed of Khunrath's Christian alchemy: [[motif:ora et labora|ora et labora]] — pray and work — refuses any split between piety and the furnace. The union of [[motif:oratory and laboratory|oratory and laboratory]] became the emblem of the devout adept and shaped the Rosicrucian image of the laboratory as a temple, influencing later Pietist and theosophical understandings of the Great Work.

## For artists & game designers
A single room split into prayer-space and craft-space, where a 'devotion' meter and a 'craft' meter must both be tended for the operation to succeed.

title: Plate 12 of the *Mutus Liber* (The Wordless Book)
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Altus (Isaac Baulot, attrib.)
summary:
A plate from the *Mutus Liber* (The Wordless Book, La Rochelle, 1677), the alchemical treatise that conveys the entire Great Work through images alone, without explanatory text.

## Iconography
The engraving shows the adepts — typically a man and a woman — at their labour: gathering dew on cloths spread under sun and moon, tending vessels and furnaces, and praying, while angelic figures preside above. Numbers and symbols, not sentences, key the operations together across the book's fifteen plates.

## Significance
The [[motif:wordless book|wordless book]], signed only with the pseudonym "Altus" (often identified with Isaac Baulot), embodies the Hermetic principle that the deepest secrets are unspeakable and must be shown, meditated, and intuited rather than read. Its imagery of [[motif:dew collection|dew-gathering]] as the capture of the celestial 'spirit' made it one of the most studied of all purely pictorial alchemical works.

## For artists & game designers
A wordless tutorial: a sequence of image-only panels the player must interpret to learn the crafting steps, with no text prompts at all.

citations.text: Adam McLean, commentary on the *Mutus Liber* (1991).

title: Engraving from Michael Maier, *Symbola Aureae Mensae Duodecim Nationum*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Michael Maier
summary:
An engraving from Michael Maier's *Symbola Aureae Mensae Duodecim Nationum* (Symbols of the Golden Table of the Twelve Nations, Frankfurt, 1617), his defence of alchemy through its great authorities.

## Iconography
The plate stages an emblematic scene of philosophers or allegorical figures, captioned with Latin and ranged as if seated at a 'golden table' of the twelve nations that had produced alchemical sages. Each figure carries attributes identifying a tradition or master in the Hermetic lineage.

## Significance
The *Symbola Aureae Mensae* assembles twelve representative adepts — one for each 'nation' of the Hermetic past, from Hermes and Maria the Jewess to Western masters — to argue the antiquity and dignity of the Art against its detractors. Written in the same Rosicrucian moment as Maier's other works, it builds a visual genealogy that legitimised early-modern alchemy by anchoring it in a continuous chain of named authorities.

## For artists & game designers
A 'hall of masters' gallery: twelve seated authority-figures, each unlockable as a mentor granting a different alchemical specialty.

citations.text: Karin Figala & Ulrich Neumann, studies on Michael Maier (1990s).
Michael Maier, *Symbola Aureae Mensae Duodecim Nationum* (Frankfurt, 1617).

title: *Tabula Smaragdina* (Emerald Tablet of Hermes), Theosophical–Alchemical Plate
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Anonymous (after the Hermetic tradition)
summary:
An emblematic engraving of the *Tabula Smaragdina*, the Emerald Tablet of Hermes Trismegistus, rendering its terse Hermetic text as a single visual hieroglyph (Deutsche Fotothek reproduction).

## Iconography
The tablet is presented as a unified emblem: sun and moon, the heavens and the earth, and figures of the masculine and feminine principles are arranged so that the celestial above answers the terrestrial below. Latin sentences of the Tabula encircle or caption the scene, and alchemical signs knot the upper and lower worlds together at the centre.

## Significance
The Emerald Tablet — known in the Latin West from the twelfth century and printed throughout the early-modern Hermetic corpus — furnishes the axiom "that which is above is as that which is below" ([[motif:as above so below|as above, so below]]). It became the compressed creed of alchemy and Rosicrucian theosophy alike; pictorial versions like this one translate its sentences into a contemplative diagram of [[motif:macrocosm-microcosm|cosmic correspondence]].

## For artists & game designers
The sun-above / moon-below mirror layout is a natural template for a 'correspondence' mechanic where actions in one half of the screen are reflected in the other.

citations.text: Julius Ruska, *Tabula Smaragdina: Ein Beitrag zur Geschichte der hermetischen Literatur* (1926).

title: The Temple of the Rosy Cross, *Speculum Sophicum Rhodo-Stauroticum*
work: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: Theophilus Schweighardt (Daniel Mögling)
summary:
The famous winged, wheeled "Temple of the Rosy Cross" engraved for Theophilus Schweighardt's *Speculum Sophicum Rhodo-Stauroticum* (1618), the visual emblem of the invisible Rosicrucian fraternity.

## Iconography
A fortified collegiate building floats on wings and runs upon wheels, suspended from a divine hand reaching out of the clouds and inscribed with the name of God. Figures around it pray, study, and labour; mottoes proclaim the house mobile, hidden, and sustained from heaven. The structure is at once temple, college, and ark — a building that cannot be located or besieged.

## Significance
Schweighardt's image gives form to the central paradox of the early manifestos: the Rosicrucian Brotherhood is an [[motif:invisible college|invisible college]], present everywhere and nowhere, upheld only by God. Drawn during the manifesto furore that followed the *Fama* and *Confessio*, the winged temple became the defining icon of the movement and a touchstone for later esoteric and Masonic imagery.

## For artists & game designers
A mobile, hidden home base — a flying fortress that relocates and stays invisible to enemies until the player has earned entry.

title: Plate from the Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer
work: Secret Symbols of the Rosicrucians
creator: anonymous (Altona)
summary:
A coloured plate from the **Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer** ('Secret Symbols of the Rosicrucians', Altona, 1785–88) — the keystone visual document of the Rosicrucian tradition.

## Iconography
Dense concentric and emblematic diagrams in vivid colour — divine names ringing a central glory, the days of creation, the [[tradition:kabbalah|Sephirotic]] correspondences, the rose and the cross — each plate a self-contained meditation packing scripture, alchemy, and Kabbalah into one figure.

## Significance
Compiled from older Rosicrucian and Boehmenist material, the *Geheime Figuren* is the richest surviving compendium of [[tradition:rosicrucian]] imagery, transmitting the 17th-century manifestos' symbolic language into the 18th-century revival and, via the 1888 English *Secret Symbols*, into the modern occult movement.

## For artists & game designers
Maximal 'sacred diagram' density — concentric god-names, creation-wheels, rose-crosses. A trove for arcane murals and crafting-circle art.

citations.text: F. Hartmann (trans.), *Secret Symbols of the Rosicrucians* (Boston, 1888).

title: Amiens Cathedral interior (stalls and pavement)
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Gothic, 13th c.); photograph by Florestan
rights: CC BY 3.0 (photo: Florestan). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The interior of Amiens Cathedral seen from the triforium, the church that holds the great octagonal nave labyrinth of 1288 (re-laid 1894).

## Iconography
The view down across choir stalls and pavement shows the vast Gothic vessel whose floor carries an **octagonal labyrinth** centred on a now-lost plaque that named the cathedral's builders and bishop. The original blue-and-grey stone maze was destroyed in 1825 and reconstructed at the end of the century.

## Significance
Amiens belongs with Chartres and Reims in the small family of great French cathedral [[motif:labyrinth|labyrinths]]. Their shared placement in the nave, on the processional axis below the [[motif:rose-window|rose window]], shows the medieval architects treating the floor as a cosmological diagram to be walked, not only a surface to be crossed.

## For artists & game designers
An establishing 'cathedral interior' reference — vertical stone rhythm above, a patterned ritual floor below — for building awe-scaled sacred interiors.

title: Zodiac quatrefoils, Amiens Cathedral west front
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Gothic sculptors, c. 1220–30); photograph by Vassil
summary:
Carved **zodiac** signs and Labours of the Months in quatrefoils on the west front of Amiens Cathedral.

## Iconography
Paired panels run along the portal socle: each month shows a seasonal **Labour** (sowing, pruning, harvesting, feasting) above or beside its corresponding **zodiac sign**. The cycle binds the agricultural year to the turning heavens in a single sculpted calendar.

## Significance
The Zodiac-and-Labours program is one of the most widespread cosmological schemes of Gothic art, asserting that earthly time and labour are governed by the ordered motion of the stars. At Amiens it forms part of the great encyclopaedic portal sculpture that medieval viewers read as a [[motif:zodiac|stone summa]] of the world's order — astrology absorbed into Christian cosmology.

## For artists & game designers
A clean template for a 'calendar of the world': twelve paired sign-and-task panels give you a year-wheel that is both decorative and functional UI.

citations.text: É. Mâle, *L'art religieux du XIIIe siècle en France* (1898).

title: The Grotta Grande, Boboli Gardens (Buontalenti)
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Bernardo Buontalenti (and others), 1583–93; photograph by Кирилл Ч.
summary:
The **Grotta Grande** designed by Bernardo Buontalenti in the Boboli Gardens behind the Pitti Palace, a masterpiece of the Mannerist artificial **grotto**.

## Iconography
The façade and chambers dissolve architecture into dripping *spugne* (sponge-stone), stalactite-like concretions and half-emerging figures, so that ordered classical forms appear to melt back into primordial rock and water. Michelangelo's unfinished *Prisoners* once stood in the first chamber as if struggling out of the living stone.

## Significance
Renaissance garden grottoes staged a **hermetic** drama of matter and form: a descent into the earth-womb where chaos is shaped into life, charged with Neoplatonic and alchemical resonance and animated by hidden hydraulics. Buontalenti's grotto is the most influential example, heir to the [[motif:sacred-geometry|sacred-space]] tradition transposed from church to garden.

## For artists & game designers
The melting-architecture grotto — order collapsing into rock, figures trapped half-formed in the wall — is a superb template for an uncanny subterranean shrine or transformation chamber.

citations.text: N. Miller, *Heavenly Caves: Reflections on the Garden Grotto* (1982).

title: The labyrinth of Chartres Cathedral
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Gothic masons, c. 1200); photograph by Poulpy
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Poulpy). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The pavement labyrinth set into the nave floor of Chartres Cathedral, the most famous and best-preserved medieval church labyrinth.

## Iconography
A single unbroken (unicursal) path of black and white stone winds through **eleven concentric circuits** to a six-lobed rosette at the center, the whole roughly 12.9 metres across. There is only one way in and out; the walker is never lost, only turned. The form is laid into the western bays of the nave so the great west [[motif:rose-window|rose window]] mirrors it on the wall above.

## Significance
Medieval labyrinths were read as figures of the Christian life and of the pilgrimage to Jerusalem — Chartres' was reputedly walked as a substitute *chemin de Jérusalem*. Later esoteric writers (notably the Hermeticist Fulcanelli in *Le Mystère des cathédrales*) treated the cathedral labyrinth as an emblem of the alchemical [[motif:sacred-geometry|work]], the turning path standing for the difficult passage to the philosophical center.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made map: a single forced path with no dead ends generates pacing and ritual without puzzle-frustration — the journey, not the branching, is the content.

title: Cosmatesque pavement, Rome
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Roman Cosmati marble-workers (12th–13th c.); photograph by MumblerJamie
rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (photo: MumblerJamie). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A **Cosmatesque** church floor in Rome, of the type laid across the city's basilicas in the 12th and 13th centuries by the marble-working dynasties (Cosmati, Vassalletto) who gave the style its name.

## Iconography
Bands of geometric **guilloche** and interlace frame large discs of porphyry and serpentine — much of it cut from re-used antique Roman columns (*spolia*) — arranged in repeating roundel-and-ribbon schemes that lead the eye along the nave toward the altar.

## Significance
The Cosmatesque pavement turns the recycled stone of pagan Rome into Christian [[motif:sacred-geometry|sacred geometry]]: the porphyry once reserved for emperors becomes a path of measured circles drawing the worshipper to the sanctuary. These floors are the Italian parent of the [[motif:cosmati-pavement|Westminster pavement]] and a key chapter in medieval geometric ornament.

## For artists & game designers
Guilloche ribbons threading between porphyry discs make a tileable 'holy floor' texture that conveys both age and order; the spolia idea (old empire reused as sacred) is a strong worldbuilding note.

title: The Cosmati pavement, Westminster Abbey
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Roman Cosmati workmen under Odoricus (1268)
summary:
The Great Pavement before the high altar of Westminster Abbey, a **Cosmati** floor laid in 1268 by Roman craftsmen for Henry III.

## Iconography
The pavement is *opus sectile* / *opus alexandrinum*: discs of purple porphyry, green serpentine and coloured glass set in intricate interlace around a central **quincunx** of roundels. A now-fragmentary brass inscription once gave a numerological reckoning of the age of the world (the *primum mobile*, ending in 19,683 years), making the floor an explicit **cosmic diagram**.

## Significance
The Westminster pavement is the most northerly major Cosmati work and a rare case where the inscription tells us how a [[motif:sacred-geometry|sacred-geometry]] floor was *read*: as a model of the *macrocosmus*, the structure and duration of the universe, trodden by the monarch at coronation. It welds Pythagorean number-mysticism to medieval Christian cosmology.

## For artists & game designers
A floor that is secretly a clock of the cosmos — interlocking porphyry roundels carrying a hidden numerology — is a powerful 'sacred map underfoot' device.

citations.text: L. Grant & R. Mortimer (eds.), *Westminster Abbey: The Cosmati Pavements* (2002).
R. Foster, *Patterns of Thought: The Hidden Meaning of the Great Pavement of Westminster Abbey* (1991).

title: The Externsteine relief: the Descent from the Cross
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Romanesque, 12th c.); photograph (Dutch national heritage)
summary:
The monumental Romanesque rock relief of the **Descent from the Cross** carved into the natural sandstone pillars of the **Externsteine** in the Teutoburg Forest.

## Iconography
Nicodemus, on a bent palm-tree, lowers the dead Christ from the cross while the figure of God receives the soul above; **Sol and Luna** weep in the corners and a strange bowed 'tree of life' fills the lower register. A small chamber at the summit has a circular window aligned, some claim, on the midsummer sunrise.

## Significance
The relief (c. 12th c.) is genuine high-medieval art, but the Externsteine rocks became a magnet for **Germanic-nationalist and neo-pagan** speculation, especially in the early 20th century, when they were promoted as an ancient Saxon Irminsul sanctuary. The site is a textbook example of esoteric *reception* projected onto sacred stone — the scholarship of Wilhelm Teudt versus sober archaeology.

## For artists & game designers
A Christian relief carved directly into a wild rock formation, layered with later pagan legend, is a vivid model for a contested sacred site whose meaning shifts by who is looking.

citations.text: U. von Roeder, studies on the Externsteine relief.

title: The Externsteine relief (detail)
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Romanesque, 12th c.); photograph by m66roepers
rights: CC BY 2.0 (photo: m66roepers). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A closer, weathered view of the **Externsteine** Descent-from-the-Cross relief, showing the texture of the living sandstone into which it is cut.

## Iconography
The detail emphasises the deeply eroded figures and the curious **bent figure / tree** at the base of the composition, long debated as a 'bowed tree of life' or a deliberately broken Irminsul. The carving's exposure to weather has softened it into the rock itself.

## Significance
Seeing the relief at close range underlines what makes the Externsteine unique among [[motif:rock-relief|sacred-rock]] monuments: it is not a built wall but a natural crag turned into a Passion image, and its very ambiguity fed centuries of competing readings — Christian Calvary versus supposed Germanic sanctuary. It remains the most-cited German case of architecture-as-contested-myth.

## For artists & game designers
Weathered, half-legible carving on raw rock signals deep time and uncertain meaning — useful when you want a monument that the player cannot fully decode.

citations.text: W. Teudt, *Germanische Heiligtümer* (1929) — superseded but historically important.

title: A Green Man foliate-head carving
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (medieval mason); photograph by SiGarb
summary:
A carved **Green Man** — a human face sprouting and disgorging foliage — of the kind found on bosses, capitals and misericords throughout medieval churches.

## Iconography
Leaves and vine-tendrils issue from the figure's mouth, sometimes also from eyes and nostrils, so the head seems to grow into vegetation or to be devoured by it. The species (oak, hawthorn, acanthus) and the expression vary from serene to grimacing.

## Significance
The term 'Green Man' was coined by Lady Raglan in 1939, who linked the carvings to folk fertility figures; scholars since have stressed their roots in Roman foliate masks and their ambiguous Christian setting. The motif is read as a **vegetation spirit** smuggled into the church — an emblem of nature's cyclical death and rebirth, and one of the most-discussed [[motif:apotropaic-carving|liminal]] images of medieval art.

## For artists & game designers
The disgorging-foliage face is an instantly readable 'nature spirit' design that works at any scale, from a tiny boss to a boss-monster.

citations.text: Lady Raglan, 'The Green Man in Church Architecture', *Folklore* (1939).

title: The Lucca Cathedral wall labyrinth (Matthews engraving)
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: after the medieval relief; plate from W. H. Matthews (1922)
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A plate from W. H. Matthews' *Mazes and Labyrinths* (1922) recording the small carved labyrinth on the porch pier of the cathedral of San Martino at Lucca.

## Iconography
The Lucca labyrinth is a **finger maze** — a low relief cut into a wall pier beside the cathedral door, just large enough to trace with a fingertip. A Latin inscription beside it names the Cretan myth: *HIC QUEM CRETICUS EDIT DAEDALUS EST LABERINTHUS* — 'This is the labyrinth which the Cretan Daedalus built.' The classical [[motif:theseus-and-minotaur|Theseus and Minotaur]] story is thus written onto a Christian church.

## Significance
The Lucca carving shows the medieval church absorbing the pagan Cretan labyrinth as an allegory of sin and salvation: to trace the path with a finger before entering was to rehearse the soul's escape from error. Matthews' survey made such carvings part of the modern study of the [[motif:labyrinth|labyrinth]] tradition.

## For artists & game designers
The finger-maze at a threshold is a gesture mechanic in stone — a tiny interactive object that ritualizes entering a space.

citations.text: W. H. Matthews, *Mazes and Labyrinths: A General Account of Their History and Developments* (1922).

title: Christ in mandorla, south portal of Bourges Cathedral
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Gothic sculptors, c. 1160–70); photograph by GFreihalter
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: GFreihalter). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The tympanum of the south portal of Bourges Cathedral, showing **Christ in Majesty** enthroned within an almond-shaped **mandorla**.

## Iconography
The enthroned Christ raises his hand in blessing inside a pointed oval *mandorla* (the [[motif:vesica-piscis|vesica piscis]]), surrounded by the **tetramorph** — the winged man, lion, ox and eagle of the four Evangelists. The almond of light isolates the divine figure from earthly space.

## Significance
The mandorla is the geometric sign of glory and of the meeting of two worlds: formed by two overlapping circles, the *vesica piscis* was loaded by medieval and later esoteric writers with [[motif:sacred-geometry|sacred-geometry]] meaning (the proportion √3, the womb of forms, the union of heaven and earth). Set over a cathedral door it frames the threshold between the human and the divine.

## For artists & game designers
The almond/vesica frame around a numinous figure is the simplest possible 'this being is holy/other' device — a portal shape that reads instantly as a gateway between realms.

citations.text: É. Mâle, *L'art religieux du XIIe siècle en France* (1922).

title: Apocalyptic tympanum of Saint-Pierre de Moissac
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Romanesque sculptors, c. 1115–1130); photograph by Daniel Villafruela
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Daniel Villafruela). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The south-porch tympanum of the abbey of Saint-Pierre de Moissac, a supreme Romanesque vision of the Apocalypse.

## Iconography
A colossal **Christ in Majesty** sits enthroned, crowned and surrounded by the **tetramorph** and two attendant seraphim; ranged in three tiers around him are the **twenty-four Elders of the Apocalypse**, each turning toward the Lord with crown and viol, drawn from Revelation 4. Below, the famous scalloped *trumeau* writhes with interlaced beasts.

## Significance
Moissac stages the heavenly throne-vision of Revelation as a doorway, so that to enter the church is to step into the apocalyptic court. Its sculpture is a cornerstone of the study of Romanesque visionary art and of how architecture rendered eschatological [[motif:christ-in-majesty|cosmology]] in stone.

## For artists & game designers
The tiered throne-room — a single seated power ringed by twenty-four turning attendants — is a master template for a climactic 'court of the god' set piece.

citations.text: M. Schapiro, 'The Romanesque Sculpture of Moissac', *Art Bulletin* (1931).

title: Chimeras and gargoyles of Notre-Dame de Paris
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Viollet-le-Duc workshop (19th c., after Gothic precedent); Cornell University Library
summary:
A view from the towers of Notre-Dame de Paris across its parapet of brooding **chimeras** above the city.

## Iconography
The famous winged, horned figures — above all the pensive *Stryge* leaning on its hands — line the *galerie des chimères*. Strictly these contemplative monsters are *chimères* (purely decorative), distinct from the true **gargoyles**, the projecting beast-headed waterspouts that throw rain clear of the walls.

## Significance
The chimeras are largely a 19th-century invention of Eugène Viollet-le-Duc's restoration, but they crystallise a genuine medieval idea: the church edge guarded by hybrid monsters. Gargoyles and grotesques were widely understood as **apotropaic** — frightening figures stationed at a building's boundary to repel the demonic, while practically managing water.

## For artists & game designers
The rooftop guardian-monster overlooking a city is one of the most enduring Gothic images; note the working distinction between functional waterspout (gargoyle) and pure ornament (chimera).

title: The Reims Cathedral labyrinth (Cellier drawing)
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Jacques Cellier (after the lost pavement)
summary:
A 16th-century drawing by Jacques Cellier recording the octagonal pavement labyrinth of Reims Cathedral, which was destroyed in 1779.

## Iconography
Unlike the round Chartres design, the Reims labyrinth was an **octagon**, its turning path inset with the figures of the cathedral's four master builders at the corners — Jean d'Orbais, Jean-le-Loup, Gaucher de Reims and Bernard de Soissons. Cellier's drawing preserves both the geometry and the masons' portraits.

## Significance
Reims documents how the cathedral [[motif:labyrinth|labyrinth]] could double as a signed monument to the architects, binding the mystery of the design to the guild that laid it. The pavement was torn up because pilgrims and children treading its circuits disturbed services — a reminder that these floors were *used*, walked as devotional mazes, not merely contemplated.

## For artists & game designers
The octagonal variant with named makers at the corners is a model for a 'signed' level — a maze that credits and characterises its builders inside the geometry itself.

citations.text: C. Wright, *The Maze and the Warrior* (2001).

title: Labyrinth floor, House of the Labyrinth, Pompeii
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Roman mosaicist, 1st c. CE); photos by Peter Grunwald
summary:
The labyrinth pattern set into a *cocciopesto* (crushed-tile-and-lime) floor in the House of the Labyrinth at Pompeii, which gave the house its modern name.

## Iconography
A tight geometric **meander labyrinth** runs as a band in the pavement, the classical seven-circuit form built from interlocking right angles rather than curves. The same house preserves a famous mosaic of [[motif:theseus-and-minotaur|Theseus killing the Minotaur]] at the labyrinth's heart.

## Significance
The labyrinth as a *floor* motif predates the cathedrals by more than a millennium: Romans laid it at doorways and in atria, where its tangling lines were felt to confuse and turn back evil — an **apotropaic** function. The Pompeian examples are the crucial evidence that the Christian church labyrinth inherited a long pavement tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Proof that the maze-on-the-floor is ancient and domestic, not only ecclesiastical — useful for grounding a 'classical villa' set with a charged threshold.

title: Rose window, Chartres Cathedral
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Gothic glaziers, 13th c.); photograph by Rama
summary:
A **rose window** of Chartres Cathedral, whose 13th-century glazing is the most complete medieval glass programme to survive.

## Iconography
Stone tracery springs from a central medallion into a ring of petal-shaped lights and an outer band of small circles, each filled with figures, the whole dominated by the celebrated *bleu de Chartres*. The composition is built on twelve-fold and four-fold symmetries that radiate from the hub.

## Significance
Chartres' roses are the textbook example of the window-as-cosmos: the circular, numerically ordered field of light reads as a wheel of creation or a [[motif:rose-window|mandala]] of salvation. The same cathedral's pavement [[motif:labyrinth|labyrinth]] sits directly below the west rose, so the building stages a vertical dialogue between the wheel of light above and the wheel of the path below — a pairing dear to the esoteric literature on Chartres.

## For artists & game designers
Use twelve- and four-fold radial symmetry for a window or sigil that should read as 'cosmic order' at a glance; deep blue dominance signals the medieval sacred.

title: Rose window of Lausanne Cathedral (detail)
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: attributed to Pierre d'Arras (c. 1235); photograph by Patrickringgenberg
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Patrickringgenberg). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Detail of the south rose window of Lausanne Cathedral, an exceptional 13th-century *imago mundi* — a stained-glass diagram of the entire created world.

## Iconography
Within a square-and-circle frame the window arranges medallions of the **four elements**, the seasons, the months and their [[motif:zodiac|zodiac signs]], the winds, the rivers of Paradise and monstrous peoples of the earth's edges, all ordered around a central image of God/Annus. It is less a wheel of saints than a cosmographic chart.

## Significance
The Lausanne rose is the rare medieval window that is explicitly a **cosmological encyclopedia**, materialising the *imago mundi* tradition of 12th–13th-century learning. It makes literal what other [[motif:rose-window|rose windows]] imply: that the round church window models the structure of the cosmos, elements, zodiac and all.

## For artists & game designers
A single object that diagrams an entire world-model — elements, calendar, zodiac, edges — is a perfect 'lore wheel' or codex frame for a setting.

citations.text: E. Beer, *Die Rose der Kathedrale von Lausanne* (1952).

title: North rose window of Notre-Dame de Paris
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Gothic glaziers, c. 1250); photograph by Ibex73
rights: CC BY 4.0 (photo: Ibex73). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The north transept **rose window** of Notre-Dame de Paris, photographed after the 2019 fire and restoration, retaining much of its 13th-century glass.

## Iconography
A great wheel of stone tracery radiates from a central medallion into concentric rings of figures — Old Testament kings, judges and prophets surrounding the Virgin — set in the deep blues and reds of medieval glass. The radial symmetry turns the whole window into a turning circle of light.

## Significance
The rose window functions as a **cosmological mandala**: a circular diagram of order centred on a sacred figure, flooding the interior with coloured light understood, in the tradition of Abbot Suger and pseudo-Dionysian [[motif:light-mysticism|light mysticism]], as the divine made visible. Its kinship with the nave [[motif:labyrinth|labyrinth]] — wheel on the wall, wheel on the floor — was felt by medieval and esoteric readers alike.

## For artists & game designers
The radial stained-glass wheel is the archetypal 'sacred circle' UI element — a mandala that organises figures around a luminous center.

title: Carved foliage of Rosslyn Chapel (the 'maize' carving)
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Sinclair masons, mid-15th c.); photograph by Kjetil Bjørnsrud
rights: CC BY 2.5 (photo: Kjetil Bjørnsrud). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A detail of the dense stone carving of **Rosslyn Chapel** (Collegiate Church of St Matthew), the much-mythologised 15th-century foundation of the Sinclair family near Edinburgh.

## Iconography
Every surface of the chapel teems with foliate ornament, [[motif:green-man|Green Men]], angels, vines and the famous twisted **Apprentice Pillar**. This particular carving has been read by some as depicting New-World maize or aloe — fuel for claims of pre-Columbian voyages, though botanists dispute the identification.

## Significance
Rosslyn became a magnet for **Templar**, Masonic and Grail legend, amplified in modern popular fiction. Sober scholarship treats it as an exuberant late-Gothic collegiate church whose carving — over a hundred Green Men among them — sits within ordinary medieval iconography, not a coded esoteric cipher. It is thus a key case study in how sacred architecture *accretes* esoteric reception.

## For artists & game designers
A building so over-carved that it invites pattern-seeking is itself a design lesson: maximal ornament reads as 'hidden meaning' to players whether or not any is encoded.

citations.text: R. L. D. Cooper, *The Rosslyn Hoax?* (2006).

title: The Kilpeck Sheela-na-gig
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Romanesque, 12th c.); photograph by SiGarb
summary:
The celebrated **Sheela-na-gig** on the corbel table of the Romanesque church of SS Mary and David at Kilpeck — a squatting female figure displaying an exaggerated vulva.

## Iconography
The figure crouches and holds open her genitals with both hands, head oversized, body schematic. She sits among a famous series of corbels (animals, monsters, musicians) ringing the church exterior, placed high on the wall where the eye is drawn upward.

## Significance
Sheela-na-gigs are concentrated in Ireland and the British Isles and provoke ongoing debate: survivals of a pagan fertility or sovereignty goddess, **apotropaic** images warding evil with shock, or Romanesque warnings against lust. Their position on thresholds and exteriors links them to the [[motif:apotropaic-carving|protective]] grotesques carved to guard a building's edges.

## For artists & game designers
A blunt apotropaic guardian: the 'shock figure on the threshold' is a real medieval device for marking a boundary as dangerous to cross with ill intent.

citations.text: J. Andersen, *The Witch on the Wall* (1977).

title: Knight effigies, Temple Church, London
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (13th c.); photograph by AlanFord
summary:
The recumbent **knight effigies** in the round nave of the Temple Church, London — the English headquarters church of the **Knights Templar**, consecrated 1185.

## Iconography
Nine worn stone effigies of mailed knights lie on the floor of the circular nave, legs crossed, hands on swords or in prayer. The **round church** itself imitates the rotunda of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, the Order's spiritual model.

## Significance
The Templars' circular churches deliberately echoed Jerusalem, turning architecture into a portable Holy Land. After the Order's violent suppression (1307–1312) the Templars became the great seed-crystal of Western **esoteric reception** — claimed as ancestors by Freemasonry, Rosicrucianism and countless secret-society myths. The Temple Church is the most evocative surviving English monument of that real-then-legendary history.

## For artists & game designers
The circular nave ringed with sword-clutching stone knights is a ready-made 'order of warrior-monks' shrine — round plan plus recumbent guardians equals instant Templar atmosphere.

citations.text: M. Barber, *The New Knighthood: A History of the Order of the Temple* (1994).

title: The Tour Saint-Jacques, Paris
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (Flamboyant Gothic, 1509–23); photograph by Benh Lieu Song
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Benh Lieu Song). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The **Tour Saint-Jacques**, the lone Flamboyant Gothic bell-tower (1509–23) surviving from the demolished church of Saint-Jacques-de-la-Boucherie in Paris.

## Iconography
The richly pinnacled stone tower bristles with gargoyles and statuary and is crowned by a figure of Saint James; it once anchored a parish church on the Tower-and-shell pilgrimage route to Santiago de Compostela.

## Significance
The church of Saint-Jacques-de-la-Boucherie is bound to the legend of **Nicolas Flamel**, the 14th-century scrivener reputed to have achieved the [[motif:alchemy|Philosopher's Stone]]; he was a benefactor of the parish and, by tradition, buried there. Esoteric Paris guidebooks (and Fulcanelli's milieu) made the surviving tower a station of the city's **alchemical** topography. It later housed a meteorological station, an apt afterlife for a tower of the heavens.

## For artists & game designers
A single surviving tower from a vanished church — dense with gargoyles and wrapped in alchemical legend — is an ideal standalone landmark for an occult-city map.

title: The Mizmaze turf labyrinth, Breamore Down
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: Anonymous (medieval/early modern); photograph by Michael Coppins
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Michael Coppins). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Mizmaze on Breamore Down, one of the few surviving English **turf mazes** — a labyrinth cut into living grass rather than laid in stone.

## Iconography
The pattern is a unicursal labyrinth scored into the hilltop turf, its raised banks and trodden trenches forming circuits walked rather than viewed. Many English turf mazes echo the eleven-circuit Chartres design, linking the wild downland maze to the cathedral pavement.

## Significance
Turf mazes (called *mizmaze*, *Troy Town*, *Julian's Bower*) are the folk continuation of the church [[motif:labyrinth|labyrinth]] in the open landscape. Tradition holds that some were walked as penance or in spring 'maze-treading' games. Their survival on isolated hills is fragile, since they vanish if not re-cut.

## For artists & game designers
A labyrinth made of *negative space in the land* — no walls, just paths worn into ground — is a low-tech, atmospheric way to put ritual geometry into an outdoor scene.

title: Tympanum of the Pentecost, Vézelay (engraving)
work: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: after the Romanesque tympanum (c. 1120–1130); plate 1913
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving of the great inner tympanum of the abbey church of La Madeleine at Vézelay, one of the masterpieces of Romanesque portal sculpture.

## Iconography
An enormous **Christ in Majesty** sits enthroned within a [[motif:mandorla|mandorla]], rays streaming from his hands to the apostles at Pentecost. Around them, on the lintel and archivolt compartments, march the **monstrous races** of the world's edges — dog-headed Cynocephali, Pygmies, giants — together with the Labours of the Months and [[motif:zodiac|zodiac]] signs, summoning all peoples and all time to the gospel.

## Significance
Vézelay fuses cosmology, ethnography and mission into one doorway: the encyclopaedic Romanesque tympanum as a model of the whole world ordered around the radiant Christ. It is a touchstone for the study of medieval geographic and astrological imagination in church art.

## For artists & game designers
A single composition that frames a radiant central power, rings it with a zodiac/calendar, and populates the margins with monstrous peoples — a complete 'cosmic gate' in one tableau.

citations.text: É. Mâle, *Religious Art in France: The Twelfth Century* (1922).

title: Man inscribed in a pentagram (Agrippa)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
summary:
The figure of a man fitted into a five-pointed star, from Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia libri tres* (1533), a foundational image of Renaissance magical anthropology.

## Iconography
A standing man spreads his arms and legs so that head, two hands and two feet reach the five points of a [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]]; planetary glyphs are set around the figure and at its centre. The body's extremities define the star, marrying human anatomy to the geometry of the pentangle.

## Significance
Agrippa's plate translates the Vitruvian circle-and-square tradition into an explicitly occult key: the human form is the [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]] whose five points correspond to the planets and the quintessence. Together with his companion figure inscribed in a circle, it became the canonical Renaissance demonstration that the body's proportions encode the heavens. The pentagram here is a benign 'star of the microcosm', long before its later demonising.

## For artists & game designers
A template for a 'sign-of-man' glyph: body-as-star with correspondences arrayed at each point, ready to drive a system of bodily/planetary attributes.

title: Homo ad quadratum (Cesariano's Vitruvius, 1521)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Cesare Cesariano (after Vitruvius)
summary:
A woodcut of the proportional man from Cesare Cesariano's 1521 Como edition of Vitruvius, the first printed and illustrated translation of *De architectura* into Italian.

## Iconography
The nude figure stands within a square ruled into a fine measuring **grid**, arms outstretched, the navel marking the natural centre from which a circle is taken. The accompanying caption (preserved in the file title) describes inscribing a smaller square within the circle drawn at the umbilicus.

## Significance
Cesariano's plates are the earliest *printed* Vitruvian figures and circulated the circle-and-square ideal far more widely than Leonardo's private drawing. Their slightly stiff, grid-bound bodies show the Renaissance effort to make proportion measurable and reproducible, turning the [[motif:microcosm|microcosmic]] body into a teachable architectural module.

## For artists & game designers
The explicit measuring grid behind the figure is a clean reference for proportion overlays or 'blueprint' rendering modes.

citations.text: Vitruvius, ed. C. Cesariano, *Di Lucio Vitruvio Pollione de architectura* (Como, 1521).

title: Melencolia I
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Albrecht Dürer's master engraving *Melencolia I* (1514), the most analysed image of geometry and esoteric temperament in Western art.

## Iconography
A brooding winged figure sits amid the tools of geometry — **compass**, sphere, scales, a truncated polyhedron (the 'Dürer solid') and a great **4×4 magic square** whose rows, columns and diagonals all sum to 34 and whose bottom row encodes the date 1514. A comet, rainbow, hourglass and sleeping hound complete the scene.

## Significance
The engraving fuses Saturnine melancholy (the temperament of geometers and artists, per Ficino and Agrippa) with the instruments of sacred geometry. The [[motif:magic-square|magic square]] is the planetary square of Jupiter, traditionally a talisman against Saturn's gloom; the polyhedron and compass mark the contemplative who measures the world. It is the canonical image of geometry as both melancholic burden and divine gift.

## For artists & game designers
A whole inventory of esoteric props in one frame — solid, square, scales, compass — each a usable talismanic object.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky, F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

title: Underweysung der Messung (geometric construction)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
A plate from Albrecht Dürer's *Underweysung der Messung* ("Instruction in Measurement", 1525), the first printed geometry manual in German, in the Met's 1538 edition.

## Iconography
The woodcut shows **compass-and-straightedge constructions** — curves, polygons, spirals or conic sections — laid out as a teaching plate, with lettered points guiding the reader through the steps of drawing each figure exactly.

## Significance
Dürer wrote the *Underweysung* to give artists a rigorous geometric foundation, treating construction of regular polygons, the [[motif:divine-proportion|spiral]] and perspective as the painter's necessary science. It transmits Euclidean and Vitruvian geometry to the workshop and includes his approximate constructions of the pentagon and other figures central to proportion theory. The book made sacred and practical geometry a printed, learnable craft.

## For artists & game designers
A primary-source library of period-correct construction diagrams — drop them in as authentic 'arcane blueprint' textures.

title: Overlapping-circles study (Codex Atlanticus)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Leonardo da Vinci
summary:
A detail from Leonardo da Vinci's *Codex Atlanticus* (folio 459r) showing his studies of overlapping circles and rosette patterns — the geometry now popularly called the 'Flower of Life'.

## Iconography
Compass-drawn circles of equal radius overlap on a hexagonal lattice so their intersections trace petal-like **rosettes**, each circle passing through its neighbours' centres exactly as in the [[motif:vesica-piscis|vesica]] construction. Leonardo explored the areas and proportions generated by such packings.

## Significance
The overlapping-circle rosette is one of the most widespread ornamental motifs in world art, found from Assyrian thresholds to Roman mosaics, medieval manuscripts and Gothic tracery. Leonardo's notebook study treats it as a rigorous geometric problem rather than mere decoration. Modern esoteric writers retitled the figure the 'Flower of Life' and read it as a blueprint of creation; historically it is a compass exercise of remarkable generative power.

## For artists & game designers
The single rule — circle through neighbours' centres — tiles infinitely; perfect procedural ornament from one parameter.

citations.text: Leonardo da Vinci, *Codex Atlanticus*, Biblioteca Ambrosiana, Milan.

title: The divine monochord
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: After Robert Fludd
summary:
A monochord diagram in the tradition of Robert Fludd's 'divine monochord', the single-stringed instrument used to demonstrate the mathematics of musical harmony.

## Iconography
A single string is stretched along a ruled scale, its length divided to mark the consonant intervals — octave (2:1), fifth (3:2), fourth (4:3) and so on. In Fludd's celebrated version a hand reaches from a cloud to tune the great cosmic string spanning earth, the planetary spheres and the empyrean.

## Significance
The [[motif:monochord|monochord]] was the proof-instrument of Pythagorean harmonics: by dividing one string you derive every consonance from simple whole-number ratios. Fludd, Kepler and the wider Renaissance read these same ratios into the spacing of the [[motif:harmony-of-the-spheres|spheres]], making music the audible face of sacred geometry. One string thus measures the whole tuned cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
A single tunable string mapped to cosmic levels is an elegant interface metaphor — drag the node, change the world's pitch.

title: Integrae Naturae Speculum (Fludd)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Robert Fludd (engr. de Bry)
summary:
The *Integrae Naturae Speculum* ("Mirror of the whole of Nature"), one of Robert Fludd's grand cosmic plates from *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia* (1617).

## Iconography
**Concentric rings** descend from God — a Tetragrammaton in cloud holding a chain — through the angelic and stellar spheres, the planets, the elements, plants, animals and minerals. At the centre Nature is personified as a woman (Natura), chained by one hand to God and by the other leading an ape, who represents human art imitating nature. The whole is a single geometric cosmogram of the chain of being.

## Significance
Fludd's plate is the most encyclopaedic Renaissance image of the ordered cosmos as graded **concentric geometry**, every level nested by ratio beneath the divine. It binds the [[motif:macrocosm|macrocosm]], the [[motif:chain-of-being|great chain]] and number-mysticism into one diagram, expressing the Hermetic 'as above, so below' through layered circles.

## For artists & game designers
A finished template for a tiered cosmos screen: rings of being from mineral to divine, each a navigable layer.

citations.text: R. Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia*, vol. I (Oppenheim, 1617).

title: Utriusque Cosmi Historia (title page)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Robert Fludd (engr. de Bry / Merian)
summary:
The engraved title page of Robert Fludd's two-volume *Utriusque Cosmi … Historia* (the 'History of both worlds, the greater and the lesser'), 1617.

## Iconography
Within an elaborate architectural frame the title announces a work divided according to the **macrocosm** (the greater world) and the **microcosm** (man). Allegorical figures, personified winds and emblematic devices surround the cartouche, presenting the book as a temple of universal knowledge.

## Significance
Fludd's whole project rests on the Hermetic axiom that the great and small worlds mirror one another by the same divine [[motif:sacred-geometry|geometry]] and number. The title page programmatically frames the volumes — covering cosmology, music, the [[motif:monochord|monochord]], geomancy and the arts — as a single demonstration that man is a [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]] of the cosmos. It is the gateway image to the most ambitious esoteric encyclopedia of its age.

## For artists & game designers
The two-worlds framing device — greater world above, lesser world below, bound in one frame — is a clean structure for a split cosmology UI.

title: Logarithmic spiral of the nautilus
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Florian Elias Rieser (photograph)
date: modern photograph of a natural form
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Florian Elias Rieser). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A sectioned nautilus shell, the textbook natural example of the logarithmic (equiangular) spiral associated with the golden ratio.

## Iconography
The cut shell reveals a chambered **logarithmic spiral**: each successive chamber grows by a constant ratio, so the curve keeps the same shape as it expands — the *spira mirabilis* that so delighted Jacob Bernoulli he asked it be carved on his tomb.

## Significance
The golden / logarithmic spiral is the form esotericists most often read as nature's signature of the [[motif:divine-proportion|divine proportion]]. While the nautilus's true growth ratio is not exactly φ, the shell became the emblem of the idea that the same proportion governs sunflowers, galaxies and growth itself. It carries the long mystical claim that beauty and life unfold by geometric law.

## For artists & game designers
A constant-shape growth curve is ideal for procedural shells, horns, galaxies, or any self-similar expansion.

citations.text: T. A. Cook, *The Curves of Life* (1914).

title: Polyhedra of Harmonices Mundi
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Johannes Kepler
summary:
A plate of regular and semi-regular polyhedra from Johannes Kepler's *Harmonices Mundi* (1619), his great synthesis of geometry, music and astronomy.

## Iconography
Rows of solids — the five [[motif:platonic-solids|Platonic]] regulars together with Archimedean and star polyhedra — are drawn in ordered ranks. Kepler even discovered two regular star polyhedra (the small and great stellated dodecahedra) shown among these figures.

## Significance
In *Harmonices Mundi* Kepler argued that the same geometric ratios governing the polyhedra also generate musical consonances and the speeds of the planets — the literal 'harmony of the spheres'. The book contains his Third Law of planetary motion embedded in a Pythagorean vision of a cosmos tuned by geometry. These plates are the visual grammar of that world-harmony.

## For artists & game designers
A ready catalogue of buildable solids, each carrying a musical/planetary correspondence you can map to sound.

title: Nested Platonic solids (Mysterium Cosmographicum)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Johannes Kepler
summary:
Kepler's celebrated diagram of the five Platonic solids nested inside one another to fix the spacing of the planetary orbits, from *Mysterium Cosmographicum* (1596).

## Iconography
Within a great sphere a cube holds a tetrahedron, which holds a dodecahedron, icosahedron and octahedron, each separated by an inscribed and circumscribed **sphere**. The six gaps between spheres are meant to give the relative orbital radii of the six known planets around the Sun.

## Significance
The young Kepler believed God had built the solar system on the five [[motif:platonic-solids|Platonic solids]] — the only perfect regular polyhedra — so that geometry itself explained why there were exactly six planets. Though his later laws abandoned the model, it is a pure expression of the Pythagorean–Platonic conviction that number and form underlie the cosmos. It marks the threshold where number-mysticism turns into mathematical astronomy.

## For artists & game designers
Nested regular solids make an instantly legible 'orrery of pure form' — a cosmology you can build entirely from five shapes.

title: The Platonic-solid solar system (Tabula Orbium)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Johannes Kepler
summary:
A detail of the nested-solids model from Kepler's *Mysterium Cosmographicum*, showing the inner cluster of polyhedra and spheres that set the planetary distances.

## Iconography
The engraving zooms on the central polyhedra — octahedron, icosahedron and dodecahedron — stacked within their bounding **spheres**, the structure that in the full plate is enclosed by the cube and tetrahedron for the outer planets.

## Significance
This closer view makes clear how Kepler used inscribed and circumscribed spheres of the five regular solids as the geometric scaffolding of the Copernican system. It is the working detail behind his claim that the architecture of the heavens is built from [[motif:platonic-solids|Platonic geometry]] — the high-water mark of Renaissance polyhedral cosmology.

## For artists & game designers
Use as an exploded/cutaway companion to the full model: the same cosmos seen from inside, good for a zoom transition.

title: Polyhedron for De divina proportione
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Leonardo da Vinci (for Luca Pacioli)
summary:
One of Leonardo da Vinci's open 'skeletonic' polyhedra drawn to illustrate Luca Pacioli's *De divina proportione* (composed 1498, printed Venice 1509).

## Iconography
A solid is rendered as an open lattice of edges (the *forma vacua* or hollow form) hung as if suspended, so that the viewer sees through it and grasps its three-dimensional structure. Leonardo produced some sixty such figures, both solid and skeletal, for Pacioli's treatise.

## Significance
Pacioli's book is the great Renaissance hymn to the [[motif:divine-proportion|golden ratio]] (the 'divine proportion'), which he linked to God, the quintessence and the five [[motif:platonic-solids|Platonic solids]]. Leonardo's see-through polyhedra were a breakthrough in technical illustration and made the abstract perfection of the regular solids visible as buildable objects. The collaboration epitomises the Renaissance fusion of mathematics, art and mysticism.

## For artists & game designers
The hollow-edge rendering is a classic 'wireframe' aesthetic centuries early — ideal for holographic or blueprint-style assets.

title: The Lo Shu / mystic table magic square
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous (after Paul Carus)
summary:
An illustration of the Lo Shu 'mystic table', the 3×3 magic square in which every row, column and diagonal sums to 15, reproduced in Paul Carus's studies of number-symbolism (c. 1900).

## Iconography
Nine cells hold the numbers 1–9 arranged so each line of three sums to **15**; in the Chinese tradition the pattern was revealed on the shell of a sacred turtle from the river Lo. The plate sets out the grid and its numerical relations as a 'mystic table'.

## Significance
The [[motif:magic-square|magic square]] is the oldest known, the seed of a vast tradition of number-mysticism spanning China, the Islamic world (where such squares became planetary talismans) and Renaissance Europe via Agrippa. Its perfectly balanced sum made it a symbol of cosmic order — the numbers of heaven arranged so no direction outweighs another. It is sacred geometry in pure arithmetic form.

## For artists & game designers
A 3×3 balanced grid is a tiny, self-validating puzzle and a ready talisman glyph in one.

title: Metatron's Cube mandala
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Henrique José Teixeira Matos
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Henrique Matos). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A modern rendering of 'Metatron's Cube', a figure of thirteen circles connected by straight lines, popular in contemporary sacred-geometry practice.

## Iconography
Thirteen equal **circles** (the 'Fruit of Life' drawn from the flower-of-life lattice) are joined by every possible straight line between their centres, producing a dense symmetric web. Within these lines the outlines of all five [[motif:platonic-solids|Platonic solids]] can be traced, which is the basis of the figure's claim to contain the building blocks of form.

## Significance
Named for the archangel Metatron of Jewish mysticism, the figure is largely a modern (chiefly late-20th-century New Age) construction layered onto the much older [[motif:flower-of-life|flower-of-life]] geometry. It illustrates how historical overlapping-circle motifs are reinterpreted today as cosmic blueprints. Included here as a reception-era artefact showing the living afterlife of Renaissance sacred geometry.

## For artists & game designers
A single symmetric line-web that hides every Platonic solid is a compact 'unlock' graphic — reveal the solids one at a time.

citations.text: D. Melchizedek, *The Ancient Secret of the Flower of Life* (1999).

title: Portrait of Luca Pacioli (study)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Attributed to Jacopo de' Barbari (study by E. Fiorillo)
rights: CC0 (study: Ezio Fiorillo). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A comparative study based on the famous portrait of the mathematician Luca Pacioli (Naples, Capodimonte), the Franciscan friar who popularised the divine proportion.

## Iconography
Pacioli stands at a table demonstrating a theorem on a slate, an open Euclid before him, while a glass **rhombicuboctahedron** half-filled with water hangs suspended above — one of the polyhedra of his treatise. A richly dressed young man stands beside him. This image reproduces and compares details used to study the painting and the identity of the second figure.

## Significance
The original portrait is the iconic image of Renaissance mathematics: it shows geometry as a noble, almost devotional pursuit, with the suspended polyhedron flagging Pacioli's authorship of *De divina proportione* and his promotion of the [[motif:divine-proportion|golden ratio]]. The hanging water-filled solid is among the first naturalistic renderings of a semi-regular polyhedron in European art.

## For artists & game designers
