The suspended glass solid catching the light is a memorable 'sacred object' motif — a polyhedron as relic.

citations.text: N. MacKinnon, 'The Portrait of Fra Luca Pacioli', *The Mathematical Gazette* (1993).

title: Pentagram and the golden ratio
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous (geometric diagram)
rights: GFDL / public-domain geometric figure. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram showing how the pentagram (pentalpha) embodies the golden ratio at every intersection of its lines.

## Iconography
A five-pointed star is drawn within a regular **pentagon**; the diagram marks how each line is cut by the others so that the ratio of the whole segment to its larger part equals the larger part to the smaller — the golden ratio φ ≈ 1.618. The nested smaller pentagon at the centre repeats the proportion endlessly.

## Significance
The [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]] was the secret sign of the Pythagoreans precisely because it conceals the [[motif:divine-proportion|golden ratio]] in its self-similar structure. This made it an emblem of perfection and health (*hygieia*) in antiquity, and later a magical sign of the microcosm. The figure is the cleanest single demonstration that the 'divine proportion' lives inside the five-pointed star.

## For artists & game designers
The self-nesting pentagon/φ relationship gives infinite zoom for free — a fractal sigil from one shape.

title: Labyrinth mosaic (House of the Labyrinth, Pompeii)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous (Roman)
summary:
A floor mosaic from the House of the Labyrinth at Pompeii showing Theseus fighting the Minotaur at the centre of a geometric labyrinth.

## Iconography
At the heart of a square **maze** of right-angled meander corridors, Theseus grapples with the Minotaur; the surrounding mosaic renders the classical seven-circuit [[motif:labyrinth|labyrinth]] as an architectural plan of nested walls and turns, framed by geometric borders.

## Significance
The labyrinth is among the oldest sacred-geometry forms, a single winding path standing for the journey to a hidden centre and back. Roman house labyrinths combined the Cretan myth with the apotropaic power of a confusing geometry thought to trap evil. The form passed from such pavements into the medieval cathedral labyrinths, making this mosaic an ancestor of Chartres. Geometry here encodes a story of descent, confrontation and return.

## For artists & game designers
The maze-as-map with a boss at the centre is the original dungeon — pure geometry driving narrative pacing.

title: The Sator-Rotas square (Pompeii)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous (Roman)
summary:
The Sator-Rotas square scratched into a wall in the palaestra at Pompeii, a 5×5 word square that must predate the eruption of 79 CE.

## Iconography
Twenty-five letters spell the five Latin words **SATOR AREPO TENET OPERA ROTAS** in a grid that reads identically left-to-right, right-to-left, top-to-bottom and bottom-to-top — a perfect palindromic word [[motif:magic-square|square]] with TENET forming a cross at its centre.

## Significance
The square is the most famous letter-magic object of antiquity. Its letters can be rearranged into PATERNOSTER twice (sharing the central N) flanked by Alpha and Omega, which fed Christian and later cabalistic readings, while its symmetry made it a widely used apotropaic charm carved on churches and amulets across medieval Europe. It is sacred geometry in language — meaning locked by perfect bidirectional symmetry.

## For artists & game designers
A self-mirroring word grid is a built-in cipher puzzle and a protective-ward asset at once.

citations.text: D. Fishwick, 'On the Origin of the Rotas-Sator Square', *Harvard Theological Review* (1964).

title: Emblema XXI: squaring the circle (Atalanta Fugiens)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Michael Maier (engr. Matthäus Merian)
summary:
Emblem 21 from Michael Maier's alchemical emblem-book *Atalanta Fugiens* (1617), the classic figure of the alchemical squared circle.

## Iconography
A philosopher with a compass draws a great **circle** around a square, inside which stands a triangle, and inside that a smaller circle containing a man and woman. The motto runs: *Make of man and woman a circle, then a square, then a triangle, make a circle and you will have the Philosophers' Stone.* The geometry steps from circle to square to triangle and back to circle.

## Significance
Maier turns the geometer's impossible problem — [[motif:squaring-the-circle|squaring the circle]] — into an allegory of the Great Work: reconciling the four elements (square), the tria prima of body-soul-spirit (triangle) and the unity of the *prima materia* and the goal (the two circles). It is the most cited single image binding sacred geometry to alchemy.

## For artists & game designers
The nested circle→square→triangle→circle is a self-contained transmutation ritual you can stage as a literal puzzle sequence.

title: Squaring the circle (geometric study)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Don Daher
date: modern drawing of a classical problem
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (Don Daher). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A line drawing presenting approximate solutions to the squaring-of-the-circle problem, illustrating the famous correspondence between the Earth–Moon sizes and a circle of equal area to a square.

## Iconography
A **square** and a **circle** are superimposed so that their areas (or perimeters) are made nearly equal, with construction lines showing how the radius of the Moon and Earth fall out of the figure. The drawing is a didactic restatement of a problem unsolvable with compass and straightedge because π is transcendental.

## Significance
Since antiquity, [[motif:squaring-the-circle|squaring the circle]] symbolised reconciling the incommensurable — heaven (circle) with earth (square), spirit with matter. The 'Earth and Moon' construction popularised in esoteric geometry (notably by John Michell) treats the approximate solution as a hidden canon of measure. The image carries that long tradition of reading an impossible geometry as a sacred secret.

## For artists & game designers
A neat visual for an 'unsolvable but meaningful' relic — geometry that can be approached but never closed.

title: Vesica piscis construction diagram
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Anonymous (geometric diagram)
summary:
A clean construction diagram of the *vesica piscis* ("fish bladder"), the pointed-oval lens formed where two equal circles overlap through one another's centres.

## Iconography
Two circles of equal radius are drawn so each passes through the other's centre; the overlapping region is the almond-shaped **vesica** or mandorla. The diagram shows how the figure generates an equilateral triangle and the ratio √3, the basis for much medieval architectural layout.

## Significance
The vesica piscis is among the oldest sacred-geometry forms. Its almond enclosed the figure of Christ in Majesty as the [[motif:mandorla|mandorla]] in Romanesque and Gothic art, and its proportions (the 265:153 ratio of Euclid's first proposition) underlie cathedral plans and the Glastonbury 'Chalice Well' lid. It encodes generation, the meeting of two worlds, and the irrational √3 born from two simple circles.

## For artists & game designers
The overlapping-circle construction is the seed of the whole flower-of-life family — one rule that scales into endless ornament.

title: Vitruvian Man (Uomo Vitruviano)
work: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: Leonardo da Vinci
summary:
Leonardo da Vinci's pen-and-ink study of the ideal human figure inscribed simultaneously in a circle and a square, the single most famous image of Renaissance proportion theory.

## Iconography
A nude man is drawn with two superimposed pairs of arms and legs so that one stance touches the sides of a **square** (homo ad quadratum) and the spread-eagle stance touches a **circle** centred on the navel (homo ad circulum). Leonardo's mirror-script notes above and below transcribe the body's proportions. The figure appears to step within both figures at once, reconciling the two frames.

## Significance
The drawing visualises a passage in Vitruvius's *De architectura* (Book III) claiming the well-built body fits both perfect figures. For Renaissance Neoplatonists this made the human body the [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]], a measure binding earthly square and celestial [[motif:divine-proportion|circle]]. Leonardo's solution — different centres for circle and square — quietly corrects Vitruvius and embodies the humanist conviction that man is the measure of the cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
The overlapped-stance device is a compact way to show a figure inhabiting two geometric orders at once — useful for any avatar meant to mediate between worlds.

title: The Temptation of St Anthony (Callot)
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Jacques Callot
summary:
Jacques Callot's grand etching *The Temptation of St Anthony*, a theatrical panorama of an entire demonic army assaulting the hermit.

## Iconography
A vast infernal cavern opens at the centre, a winged dragon and a flying witch fill the sky, and ranks of [[motif:demon army|demons]] swarm in disciplined formation around the tiny figure of the saint. Callot stages the temptation as military spectacle, a battlefield of the supernatural with a gaping [[motif:hell-mouth|hell-mouth]] for an entrance.

## Significance
Callot, the great Lorraine etcher of the *Miseries of War*, brought his mastery of crowd scenes and tiny figures to the Anthony subject, producing one of the most ambitious temptation images of the Baroque. The orderly demonic 'army' reflects the early-modern systematisation of demonology — the infernal hierarchy marshalled like an état-major — the same impulse that catalogued the seventy-two spirits of the grimoires.

## For artists & game designers
A template for a 'demon legion' set piece: massed, ranked enemies pouring from a central hell-gate, with aerial threats overhead.

citations.text: Jacques Callot, *The Temptation of St Anthony* (etching, c. 1635).

title: The Temptation of St Anthony (Bresdin)
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Rodolphe Bresdin
rights: CC0 / Public domain. The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Rodolphe Bresdin's densely worked *Temptation of St Anthony*, a 19th-century visionary reprise of the medieval theme (Metropolitan Museum).

## Iconography
The saint is nearly lost within a swarming, hallucinatory tangle of foliage, beasts, and [[motif:demon swarm|demons]] rendered in Bresdin's famously minute, almost microscopic line. The whole sheet seethes with monstrous life, the temptation dissolved into an enveloping fantastic landscape.

## Significance
Bresdin, an eccentric printmaker admired by Baudelaire and a teacher of Odilon Redon, revived the Anthony subject for the Romantic and Symbolist imagination. His obsessive detail turns the desert father's trial into a meditation on the proliferation of the irrational — the demonic as an overgrowth that engulfs the soul. The theme links the medieval magician-saint tradition to the modern fascination with visionary and oneiric states.

## For artists & game designers
A model for 'maximal density' environment art, where the threat is the overwhelming, claustrophobic detail itself rather than any single monster.

title: The Tribulations of St Anthony
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Martin Schongauer
rights: Public Domain Mark. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Martin Schongauer's celebrated engraving of *St Anthony* borne aloft and tormented by a wheel of demons — the print the young Michelangelo copied in paint.

## Iconography
St Anthony is lifted into the air, clutching his book, encircled by [[motif:hybrid monsters|hybrid demons]] — feathered, scaled, beaked, horned, and clawed — that beat, bite, and drag at him. Each fiend is a virtuoso assemblage of animal parts forming a churning ring of torment.

## Significance
Schongauer's engraving (c. 1470s) was the most admired demon-swarm image of its age and became the template for centuries of temptation imagery, from Grünewald's Isenheim Altarpiece to Callot. Vasari records that Michelangelo, still a boy, copied it in colour, studying fish-scales at the market to render the demons. The print exemplifies the late-medieval conception of the devil as a multiplicity of monstrous forms assaulting the holy ascetic.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive reference for a 'demon swarm' aerial encounter — every monster a unique kitbash of beast parts.

citations.text: G. Vasari, *Lives of the Artists* (on Michelangelo's copy).

title: The Temptation of St Anthony (Bosch)
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Hieronymus Bosch
summary:
Hieronymus Bosch's *Temptation of St Anthony*, the supreme painted vision of a hermit besieged by demonic illusion.

## Iconography
The desert father is surrounded by a teeming world of [[motif:hybrid monsters|hybrid monsters]] — fish that walk, mounted demons, broken-eggshell ships, processions of the damned — across a burning, dreamlike landscape. The saint kneels or is hauled aloft, steadfast amid the diabolical theatre.

## Significance
Based on Athanasius's *Life of St Anthony*, the foundational account of eremitic struggle against demons, Bosch's panels (the Lisbon triptych is the most celebrated) became the touchstone for the temptation theme. Bosch transformed the desert assault into a cosmology of sin and illusion, where the demons embody the deceptions the magus-saint tradition warns against — magic as a snare of the devil. The work influenced everyone from Bruegel to the Surrealists.

## For artists & game designers
An inexhaustible bestiary of kitbashed demons and impossible architecture — the canonical reference for an 'illusory hell' encounter.

title: The Temptation of St Anthony (Follower of Bosch)
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Follower of Hieronymus Bosch
summary:
A *Temptation of St Anthony* by a follower of Hieronymus Bosch, one of the many workshop and imitator versions that spread the theme across the 16th century.

## Iconography
St Anthony endures a crowd of grotesque [[motif:demon swarm|demons]] — beaked, clawed, and chimeric — that pluck and torment him while he clings to faith. The composition repeats and reworks the Boschian formula of monstrous multiplicity in a barren landscape.

## Significance
Bosch's invention was so popular that dozens of variants by followers and imitators circulated in his lifetime and after, fixing the temptation of the desert father as a fashionable subject. These derivatives testify to the early-modern appetite for demonology as spectacle, and to the centrality of St Anthony as the model ascetic who resists the devil's [[motif:diabolical assault|illusions]] — the positive counterpart to the magician who succumbs.

## For artists & game designers
Useful as a 'second pass' on the demon-swarm theme: slightly cruder, more legible monsters that read clearly at small scale.

title: The Temptation of St Anthony (after Teniers)
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: After David Teniers the Younger (book engraving)
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving of *The Temptation of St Anthony* from Anna Jameson's *Sacred and Legendary Art* (1895), reproducing the much-painted Flemish treatment associated with David Teniers the Younger.

## Iconography
St Anthony is shown in his cave beset by small [[motif:grotesque imps|grotesque imps]] and a tempting female apparition — the demon disguised as a seductress, a stock motif of the seventeenth-century cabinet pictures of the subject. The mood is anecdotal and intimate rather than apocalyptic.

## Significance
David Teniers the Younger painted the Temptation of St Anthony many times, popularising a domesticated, collectable version of the theme for the Flemish market. Its reproduction in Anna Jameson's pioneering nineteenth-century iconographic study, *Sacred and Legendary Art*, shows how the magician-saint and desert-father imagery passed into the modern art-historical canon, framed as Christian symbolism for a Victorian readership.

## For artists & game designers
The 'seductress imp' variant is handy for a temptation scene built on allure and small mischievous demons rather than overwhelming horror.

citations.text: Anna Jameson, *Sacred and Legendary Art* (ed. of 1895).
David Teniers the Younger, *Temptation of St Anthony* (17th c., various versions).

title: Saints Cyprian and Justina
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A devotional image of *Saints Cyprian and Justina* of Antioch, the converted sorcerer and the virgin whose faith broke his spells.

## Iconography
The two martyrs are shown paired, each bearing the palm of martyrdom. Cyprian, once a pagan magus, is presented as a bishop or robed convert; Justina stands as a Christian virgin, the cross at her side the emblem that turned aside the [[motif:demon repelled|demons]] sent to seduce her.

## Significance
The hagiographic romance of Cyprian of Antioch tells how a celebrated sorcerer was hired to win the chaste Justina by magic, sent demons against her three times, and watched each one repelled by her sign of the cross. Confounded, Cyprian burned his magical books, was baptised, became a bishop, and was martyred with her under Diocletian. Because of his magical past he became the patron saint of magicians and necromancers, and his name was attached to a long tradition of grimoires (the Iberian-Brazilian *Libro de San Cipriano* / *Ciprianillo*). Scholars (e.g. Theodor Zahn) long ago noted his legend as a Christian reworking of the Faustian sorcerer-redeemed pattern.

## For artists & game designers
The paired sorcerer-and-saint is a ready 'redeemed antagonist' duo: a reformed mage and the holy figure whose virtue first defeated him.

citations.text: Theodor Zahn, *Cyprian von Antiochien und die deutsche Faustsage* (1882).

title: The Gadarene Demoniacs
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Léonard Gaultier
rights: CC0 / Public domain. National Gallery of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Léonard Gaultier's engraving of *The Gadarene Demoniacs*, the Gospel exorcism in which Christ casts a legion of demons into a herd of swine.

## Iconography
Christ confronts the possessed men among the tombs while the [[motif:demon expelled|expelled demons]], named Legion, drive the herd of [[motif:swine|swine]] headlong over a cliff into the sea. The little demon-figures streaming from the demoniacs into the pigs make the act of exorcism literally visible.

## Significance
The story (Matthew 8, Mark 5, Luke 8) is the New Testament's most dramatic exorcism and the scriptural charter for the Church's power over demons. The demon's confession — 'My name is Legion, for we are many' — supplied later demonology with its sense of demonic multiplicity, and the rite of casting out evil spirits, codified in the *Rituale Romanum* (1614), descends directly from such Gospel models. It is the canonical counter to sorcery: deliverance by divine authority rather than by spell.

## For artists & game designers
A clear visual mechanic for 'transferring' a possession — the swarm leaves one host and floods into another that is then destroyed.

title: The Anastasis (Harrowing of Hell), Lagoudera
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Anonymous (Byzantine fresco)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Dumbarton Oaks. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A detail of the head of Christ from the *Anastasis* (Harrowing of Hell) fresco at the Church of Panagia tou Arakos, Lagoudera, Cyprus (1192).

## Iconography
In the full [[motif:anastasis|Anastasis]] composition Christ tramples the shattered [[motif:gates of hades|gates of Hades]] and the bound figure of Death, hauling Adam and Eve up from their tombs. This detail isolates the serene, commanding head of the risen Christ, the visual focus of the descent into hell.

## Significance
The Anastasis is the standard Byzantine image of the Resurrection: rather than an empty tomb, it shows Christ's victory over hell, breaking its gates and freeing the righteous dead — the 'Harrowing of Hell' of the Gospel of Nicodemus. As the supreme icon of divine power over the infernal realm, it is the theological ground on which every saintly defeat of demons rests. The Lagoudera frescoes are among the finest Komnenian-era wall paintings to survive.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'breaking into the underworld' image — a hero shattering hell's gates and leading captives out — a ready climax for a descent quest.

citations.text: Dumbarton Oaks, Byzantine Photograph and Fieldwork Archives (CC0).

title: Hermes Trismegistus, Siena Cathedral Pavement
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Giovanni di Stefano
summary:
The marble-intarsia floor panel of *Hermes Trismegistus* in the pavement of Siena Cathedral (1488) — a pagan sage enthroned in a Christian church as a prophet of the true God.

## Iconography
[[motif:hermes trismegistus|Hermes Trismegistus]] stands robed and turbaned, handing a tablet to two figures and resting his hand on a second tablet inscribed in Latin and Italian, flanked by the words 'Hermes Mercurius Trismegistus contemporaneus Moysi' — 'contemporary of Moses.' He is presented as an ancient theologian, not a sorcerer.

## Significance
The panel embodies the Renaissance doctrine of *prisca theologia*, the belief — promoted by Marsilio Ficino, who translated the *Corpus Hermeticum* for Cosimo de' Medici in 1463 — that Hermes Trismegistus was an Egyptian sage contemporary with Moses who foresaw Christianity. Placing him at the cathedral's threshold, ahead of the Sibyls, dramatised the Christian absorption of pagan wisdom. (Isaac Casaubon would later, in 1614, redate the Hermetica to late antiquity.) It is the supreme image of the magus rehabilitated as prophet — the inverse of the magician condemned.

## For artists & game designers
A dignified 'pagan oracle in a holy place' design — a robed sage with inscribed tablets, perfect for a syncretic temple's gatekeeper.

citations.text: Frances A. Yates, *Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition* (1964).

title: Saint Justina
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Anonymous (Rijksmuseum print)
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Rijksmuseum. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving of *Saint Justina*, the virgin of Antioch whose chastity defeated the demons sent by Cyprian the magician (Rijksmuseum).

## Iconography
Justina is shown as a young woman with the attributes of a virgin martyr — palm and sword — her composure signalling the steadfast [[motif:chastity|chastity]] that, with the sign of the cross, turned back every demonic assault. She is sometimes conflated in art with St Justina of Padua, who shares the palm and is pierced by a sword.

## Significance
In the legend of Cyprian and Justina, Justina is the active spiritual force: it is her prayer and her cross, not any counter-spell, that defeat the conjured demons and ultimately convert the sorcerer himself. Her cult spread widely; St Justina of Padua became a major civic patron, her image (often with a unicorn or sword) painted by Moretto, Veronese, and others. The two Justinas blur in popular devotion, both standing for the virtue that unmakes sorcery.

## For artists & game designers
A 'faith as armour' archetype — a defenceless figure whose only weapon is a holy sign that nullifies enemy magic.

title: St Macarius the Elder (Macarius of Egypt)
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Fyodor Solntsev
summary:
A full-length icon of *St Macarius the Elder of Egypt*, the desert father famed for his battles against demons in the wilderness of Scetis.

## Iconography
Macarius stands frontal in monastic habit, an aged ascetic with an immensely long white beard — the conventional sign of the holy hermit's age and wisdom. The austere, full-length [[motif:byzantine icon|icon]] format presents him as a model of monastic sanctity.

## Significance
Macarius of Egypt (4th c.), a founder of the monastic settlement of Scetis, figures prominently in the *Apophthegmata Patrum* (Sayings of the Desert Fathers) and the *Lausiac History*, which recount his repeated [[motif:demon resisted|encounters with demons]] who tempt and threaten the hermits. The desert fathers' literature established the spiritual technology of resisting the devil — vigilance, prayer, the cross — that underlies the whole magician-saint tradition, where holiness, not counter-magic, defeats the demonic.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal 'old hermit master' design — gaunt, bearded, robed — for a mentor who teaches resistance to corruption.

title: The Fall of Simon Magus
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Jan Rombouts
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Museum M, Leuven. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
Jan Rombouts's panel of *The Fall of Simon Magus*, the sorcerer who tried to fly by demonic power and crashed at the prayer of St Peter.

## Iconography
Simon Magus is shown plummeting from the air, his [[motif:levitation|levitation]] collapsing as the [[motif:demons withdrawn|demons]] that bore him aloft are dismissed by St Peter's prayer below. The Apostle and the watching crowd witness the public ruin of false magic.

## Significance
Simon Magus, named in Acts 8 as the magician who tried to buy the power of the Holy Spirit (hence 'simony'), grew in apocryphal legend into the arch-sorcerer and antagonist of St Peter in Rome. The motif of his fatal flight — magic exposed as diabolical fraud the instant true holiness is invoked — is the paradigm the Cyprian legend repeats: the magus humbled by the power of Christ. He became the ancestral 'false prophet' of Western demonology.

## For artists & game designers
A dramatic 'power-cut' beat: an enemy sustained by borrowed magic abruptly falls the moment the source is severed.

title: Theophilus of Adana (Maastricht Hours)
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Anonymous (Liège-Maastricht miniaturist)
rights: Public domain. British Library. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A miniature opening from the *Maastricht Book of Hours* (BL Stowe MS 17) depicting the legend of *Theophilus of Adana*, the cleric who sold his soul to the devil.

## Iconography
The scene shows Theophilus doing [[motif:homage to devil|homage to the devil]] — kneeling, hands clasped between the demon's, as in a feudal oath — and sealing a written [[motif:charter|charter]] surrendering his soul. The marginal cycle continues to his repentance and the Virgin's recovery of the deed.

## Significance
The sixth-century tale of Theophilus, a steward who signs a pact with Satan to regain his office and is then saved when the Virgin Mary descends to Hell, snatches back the charter, and lays it on his breast, is the prototype of the Faust legend — 'the first Faust.' Translated by Paul the Deacon and dramatised across medieval Europe (Rutebeuf's *Miracle de Théophile*), it established the visual grammar of the diabolical contract: the kneeling homage and the signed document. It is the chief Marian-miracle counterpart to the Cyprian story of magic undone by grace.

## For artists & game designers
The feudal-homage pose plus a signed parchment is the cleanest possible 'soul-contract' iconography — and the Virgin's reclaiming of the deed is a built-in redemption mechanic.

citations.text: Paul the Deacon (trans.), *Miracle of Theophilus*; Rutebeuf, *Le Miracle de Théophile* (13th c.).

title: Theophilus and the Devil (Codex Gigas)
work: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: Herman the Recluse (attrib.)
rights: Public domain. National Library of Sweden. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A page from the *Codex Gigas* (the 'Devil's Bible') illustrating *Theophilus and the Devil*, set near the manuscript's famous full-page demon portrait.

## Iconography
Theophilus is shown before a horned [[motif:demon|demon]], enacting his pact — the cleric and the devil joined in the gesture of homage that seals the surrender of his soul. The image sits within the giant Bohemian codex whose adjacent leaf bears the most reproduced of all medieval devils.

## Significance
The *Codex Gigas* (early 13th c., from the Benedictine house of Podlažice) is the largest surviving medieval manuscript; legend says a condemned monk completed it in one night with the devil's help, in a real-life echo of the Theophilus pact. The placement of the Theophilus miracle beside the devil portrait and a page on confession underlines the book's theme of damnation answered by penance: the same arc — soul lost to a demon, soul recovered by grace — that governs the Cyprian and Faust legends.

## For artists & game designers
A period-authentic 'pact panel' you can pair with a monstrous devil portrait for a damnation-and-redemption set piece.

citations.text: *Codex Gigas* (early 13th c.), National Library of Sweden, MS A 148.

title: The Hexagram Seal of Solomon (Goetia lamen)
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: Lemegeton / Solomonic tradition (modern rendering)
rights: Public domain. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The hexagram **Seal of Solomon** as drawn in the *Lemegeton* (the *Ars Goetia*) — the lamen worn by the conjurer to command the seventy-two spirits.

## Iconography
A six-pointed star inside a double circle inscribed with divine names and Hebrew letters; a Tau or cross at the centre. Worn on the breast or set on the magician's ring, it protected the operator and compelled the bound spirit to obey.

## Significance
In the Solomonic legend, God gave Solomon a seal-ring by which he mastered the demons and made them build the Temple. The hexagram-with-names became the central authority-sign of grimoire magic, distinct from the planetary pentacles of the *Key of Solomon*.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'binding lamen' — the badge of authority a summoner wears to control what they call.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & A. Crowley (ed.), *The Goetia* (1904).

title: The Sigil of Lucifer (Grimorium Verum)
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: Grimorium Verum tradition (modern rendering)
rights: Public domain. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The **Sigil of Lucifer**, the spirit-seal given in the *Grimorium Verum* ('True Grimoire', purportedly 1517, in fact 18th-c. Italian/French).

## Iconography
An intricate line-glyph of crossed strokes, looping curves and a small inverted-cross/chalice motif — the abstract 'signature' by which the spirit Lucifer is named and summoned. Such sigils were copied onto parchment and shown to compel the spirit's appearance.

## Significance
The *Grimorium Verum* descends from the *Key of Solomon* but turns to the infernal hierarchy (Lucifer, Beelzebuth, Astaroth and their subordinates). Its sigils exemplify the grimoire principle that a spirit's name *as a drawn character* gives the magician a handle on the spirit itself. The glyph has had an outsized modern afterlife in popular occulture.

## For artists & game designers
One of the most recognizable 'demon sigils' — instantly legible as forbidden-magic iconography.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: The Pentagrammaton (YHShVH) in the flaming pentagram
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: Christian-Cabalist tradition (modern rendering)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The **Pentagrammaton** — the divine Name of five letters, YHShVH (Yeheshuah), formed by inserting the letter Shin (ש, fire/spirit) into the four-letter Tetragrammaton YHVH.

## Iconography
The five Hebrew letters Yod-He-Shin-Vav-He set one to each point of a pentagram. The added Shin at the centre transforms the unpronounceable Name of God into the pronounceable Name read by Christian Cabalists as *Jesus*.

## Significance
A cornerstone of **Christian Cabala**: Pico della Mirandola and Johann Reuchlin (*De verbo mirifico*, 1494) argued that the Tetragrammaton, made sayable by Shin, *is* the name of the Messiah — a Kabbalistic proof of Christianity. The flaming pentagram became the master-glyph of the later magical revival.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'name-of-power within the pentagram' device — sacred-word geometry.

citations.text: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola, *Conclusiones* (1486), the Cabalist theses.

title: The Sigil of Baphomet (goat pentagram)
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: after Stanislas de Guaita / Éliphas Lévi
rights: Public domain. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The **Sigil of Baphomet** — the goat's head set within an inverted pentagram, ringed by the Hebrew letters of *Leviathan*.

## Iconography
A downward-pointing pentagram (two points up) frames a goat's face; the five points touch the five Hebrew letters Lamed-Vav-Yod-Tav-Nun (LVYThN, *Leviathan*) read counter-clockwise. The downward star inverts the upright human-microcosm pentagram into a sign of matter and the bestial.

## Significance
The goat-in-pentagram derives from Stanislas de Guaita's *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897), itself building on Éliphas Lévi's Baphomet; the *Leviathan* lettering and the design were later adopted as the official Sigil of Baphomet of the Church of Satan (1966). It is the most widely recognized emblem of the modern Left-Hand Path.

## For artists & game designers
The single most legible 'dark occult' sigil in popular culture — use deliberately.

citations.text: Stanislas de Guaita, *La Clef de la Magie Noire* (1897).
Éliphas Lévi, *Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie* (1856).

title: The Seal of Jupiter (planetary sigil)
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: after H. C. Agrippa / Francis Barrett
rights: Public domain. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons (after Barrett, The Magus).
summary:
The seal of Jupiter, traced from the 4×4 magic square of the planet (constant 34).

## Iconography
The sigil winds through the *kamea* of Jupiter — a 4×4 grid whose every line sums to 34 — accompanied by the characters of Jupiter and of its Intelligence (Iophiel) and Spirit (Hismael).

## Significance
Jupiter's talisman, engraved on tin or silver, was the planet of expansion, fortune, sovereignty and benevolence — the great benefic. The same square is the one Dürer set into *Melencolia I*. Agrippa codified the seal; Barrett's *The Magus* carried it into the 19th-century revival that fed the Golden Dawn.

## For artists & game designers
A 'luck/prosperity' sigil with an exact mathematical pedigree — the geometry is reproducible from the number square.

citations.text: H. C. Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), bk. II.

title: The Seal of Mars (planetary sigil)
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: after H. C. Agrippa / Francis Barrett
rights: Public domain. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons (after Barrett, The Magus).
summary:
The seal of Mars, derived from the 5×5 magic square of the planet (constant 65).

## Iconography
A sharp line-figure threaded through the *kamea* of Mars, with the characters of Mars and of its Intelligence (Graphiel) and Spirit (Barzabel).

## Significance
The Mars talisman, engraved on iron at the planet's hour, governed war, courage, force and severance — its magic invoked for victory and protection but feared for its violence. Agrippa's seal, repeated by Barrett, became standard in ceremonial magic.

## For artists & game designers
The 'war/aggression' sigil of the planetary set — angular and martial by design.

citations.text: H. C. Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), bk. II.

title: The Seal of Mercury (planetary sigil)
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: after H. C. Agrippa / Francis Barrett
rights: Public domain. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons (after Barrett, The Magus).
summary:
The seal of Mercury, derived from the 8×8 magic square of the planet (constant 260).

## Iconography
A complex line-figure across the dense *kamea* of Mercury, with the characters of Mercury and of its Intelligence (Tiriel) and Spirit (Taphthartharath).

## Significance
The Mercury talisman, engraved on quicksilver-fixed metal, governed intellect, eloquence, commerce, writing and magic itself — Mercury being the planet of Hermes/Thoth. Agrippa's seal, via Barrett, became the magician's own planetary sign.

## For artists & game designers
The 'knowledge/communication' sigil — the busiest and most labyrinthine of the planetary seals.

citations.text: H. C. Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), bk. II.

title: The Seal of Saturn (planetary sigil)
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: after H. C. Agrippa / Francis Barrett
rights: Public domain. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons (after Barrett, The Magus).
summary:
The seal (*sigillum*) of Saturn, the geometric signature of the planet used to engrave talismans.

## Iconography
A looping line-figure traced across the 3×3 magic square (*kamea*) of Saturn, whose rows, columns and diagonals each sum to 15. The seal is the path drawn through the numbers of the spirit and intelligence; with it stand the characters of the planet and of its Intelligence (Agiel) and Spirit (Zazel).

## Significance
In Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (1533), each planet has a number-square and a set of seals derived from it; a Saturn talisman engraved on lead at the right hour was held to draw down Saturnine virtue (gravity, time, melancholy, binding). Francis Barrett's *The Magus* (1801) reproduced the system for the English occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal 'magic sigil' — a clean line-glyph born from a number grid; ideal for an engraved-talisman or planetary-magic system.

citations.text: H. C. Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia libri tres* (1533), bk. II.

title: The Seal of the Sun (planetary sigil)
work: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: after H. C. Agrippa / Francis Barrett
rights: Public domain. Vector rendering via Wikimedia Commons (after Barrett, The Magus).
summary:
The seal of the Sun, traced from the 6×6 solar magic square (constant 111; grand sum 666).

## Iconography
The sigil winds through the *kamea* of the Sun, attended by the characters of Sol and of its Intelligence (Nakhiel) and Spirit (Sorath, 666).

## Significance
The solar talisman, engraved on gold, was the seal of light, kingship, health and the heart of the cosmos — the most exalted of the seven. Its spirit-number 666 later drew apocalyptic associations. Agrippa fixed the design; Barrett transmitted it.

## For artists & game designers
The 'sovereign/solar' sigil — the centre of any seven-planet magic system.

citations.text: H. C. Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), bk. II.

title: Barruel, *Memoirs Illustrating the History of Jacobinism*
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Augustin Barruel (printed Hudson & Godwin)
summary:
A title page of Abbé Augustin Barruel's *Memoirs Illustrating the History of Jacobinism* (1797–99), the founding text of modern Illuminati conspiracy theory.

## Iconography
A printed letterpress title page announcing the work's claim to trace the French Revolution to a hidden conspiracy of philosophes, Freemasons, and Illuminati — typography, not image, doing the polemical work.

## Significance
The exiled French Jesuit [[motif:abbe barruel|Barruel]] argued that the [[motif:jacobinism|Revolution]] was the planned culmination of a triple plot — anti-Christian, anti-monarchical, and anti-social — orchestrated through Masonic lodges and Weishaupt's [[motif:anti-illuminati|Illuminati]]. Published almost simultaneously with John Robison's *Proofs of a Conspiracy*, it fixed the template of the secret-society [[motif:conspiracy theory|conspiracy theory]] that has recurred ever since.

## For artists & game designers
The in-world 'exposé' book whose lurid claims drive a witch-hunt subplot — a primary document players can read to learn (and mistrust) the official conspiracy.

citations.text: Augustin Barruel, *Memoirs Illustrating the History of Jacobinism* (1797–99).

title: Portrait of Alessandro Cagliostro (Rijksmuseum)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (Rijksmuseum)
summary:
An engraved portrait of Alessandro, Count Cagliostro (Giuseppe Balsamo, 1743–1795), the celebrated occult adventurer and founder of the Egyptian Rite of Freemasonry.

## Iconography
A bust-length engraving (Rijksmuseum) showing the magus in the dress of a fashionable eighteenth-century gentleman, his likeness sold as a print to a public fascinated by his fame.

## Significance
[[motif:cagliostro|Cagliostro]] founded the [[motif:egyptian rite|Egyptian Rite]], an esoteric Masonic system promising spiritual and physical regeneration through a pseudo-Egyptian ritual that admitted both men and women, presided over by himself as 'Grand Copht.' Healer, alchemist, and necromancer in repute, he was caught up in the Affair of the Diamond Necklace and died a prisoner of the Roman Inquisition — the archetype of the Enlightenment magus-charlatan.

## For artists & game designers
The charismatic mountebank-mage: an NPC whose miracles may be real or fraud, and whose Egyptian rite is a ready quest-cult.

citations.text: W. R. H. Trowbridge, *Cagliostro: The Splendour and Misery of a Master of Magic* (1910).

title: *A Masonic Anecdote* (James Gillray Satire)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: James Gillray (pub. Hannah Humphrey)
summary:
James Gillray's etching *Anecdote Maçonnique / A Masonic Anecdote* (1786), a satirical print bound up with the London scandals around Cagliostro and his rite.

## Iconography
A Georgian caricature in Gillray's biting manner, staging a lodge episode for ridicule — exaggerated figures, mock-ceremonial fuss, and the visual idiom of the print-shop satire published by Hannah Humphrey.

## Significance
Gillray's print belongs to the wave of [[motif:anti-masonic caricature|anti-Masonic and anti-Cagliostro satire]] that greeted the magus during his London sojourn, when his [[motif:egyptian rite|Egyptian Rite]] and his quarrels became fashionable scandal. Such [[motif:satirical print|satirical prints]] are a major channel through which the iconography of secret societies entered popular culture — as objects of mockery as much as awe.

## For artists & game designers
A period propaganda poster: an in-world caricature that lampoons a secret order, useful for showing public opinion turning against a faction.

citations.text: Richard Godfrey, *James Gillray: The Art of Caricature* (2001).

title: *Les Carbonari* (Scénario, BnF)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (BnF)
summary:
A plate from *Les Carbonari* (a nineteenth-century scénario held at the Bibliothèque nationale de France), depicting the Italian revolutionary secret society of the charcoal-burners.

## Iconography
A narrative print staging the Carbonari in their semi-Masonic guise — figures gathered around the emblems of the 'charcoal-burners' fraternity, whose rituals were dramatised for a curious public.

## Significance
The [[motif:carbonari|Carbonari]] ('charcoal-burners') were a network of revolutionary secret societies active in early-nineteenth-century Italy and France, organised in cells called *vendite* with initiation rites and oaths borrowed from Freemasonry. Dedicated to constitutional and nationalist causes, they played a key part in the uprisings of the 1820s–30s and in the long ferment of the [[motif:risorgimento|Risorgimento]], lending the era its model of the [[motif:political conspiracy|political secret society]].

## For artists & game designers
A cell-based revolutionary order — perfect for a clandestine-network plot where the player is initiated into one *vendita* and must coordinate with others unseen.

citations.text: R. John Rath, 'The Carbonari: Their Origins, Initiation Rites, and Aims,' *American Historical Review* (1964).

title: Eye in the Pyramid (Reverse of the Great Seal)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (US Great Seal reverse)
summary:
The reverse of the Great Seal of the United States — the radiant Eye atop an unfinished pyramid — as reproduced on the dollar bill, the emblem most often read as a 'secret-society' sign.

## Iconography
An [[motif:eye in pyramid|eye within a glory]] floats above a thirteen-stepped [[motif:unfinished pyramid|unfinished pyramid]], with the mottoes *[[motif:annuit coeptis|Annuit cœptis]]* ('He has favoured our undertakings') above and *[[motif:novus ordo seclorum|Novus ordo seclorum]]* ('A new order of the ages') below.

## Significance
Designed by a committee in 1782 (its imagery owing more to Renaissance and biblical sources than to any lodge), the eye-and-pyramid was later seized upon as proof of Masonic or Illuminati control of the republic. As a [[motif:conspiracy emblem|conspiracy emblem]] it is distinct from the lodge's devotional [[motif:eye of providence|Eye of Providence]]: here the eye reads as hidden power surveying a great design rather than as the watchful Architect of the lodge.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'they're watching' insignia: an eye crowning an unfinished structure, ideal for a faction that claims to be building a new world order.

citations.text: Richard S. Patterson & Richardson Dougall, *The Eagle and the Shield: A History of the Great Seal of the United States* (1976).

title: Jewel of the Memphis-Misraïm Rite (19th c.)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (Musée de la franc-maçonnerie)
summary:
A nineteenth-century jewel of the Rite of Memphis-Misraïm, the great Egyptian-styled fringe-Masonic system (Musée de la franc-maçonnerie).

## Iconography
A metal pendant jewel bearing the emblems proper to an officer of the rite — the symbolic device of the order suspended for wear in lodge, in the manner of all Masonic [[motif:officers jewel|officers' jewels]].

## Significance
The Rites of [[motif:egyptian rite|Misraïm]] and of [[motif:memphis-misraim|Memphis]], later fused, were elaborate [[motif:high-grade system|high-grade systems]] (running to ninety or more degrees) that wrapped Masonic ritual in an Egyptian-hermetic mythology. Flourishing in early-nineteenth-century Italy and France, this '[[motif:fringe masonry|fringe Masonry]]' became a vehicle for occult and esoteric currents, and in the next century fed directly into the founding of the Ordo Templi Orientis.

## For artists & game designers
A rank token for an elaborate order — a single jewel whose device encodes both the wearer's degree (one of dozens) and the rite's Egyptian theme.

citations.text: Gérard Galtier, *Maçonnerie égyptienne, Rose-Croix et néo-chevalerie* (1989).

title: Minerval Insignia of the Illuminati (Owl of Minerva)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
The Minerval insignia of the Bavarian Illuminati, the owl-of-Minerva device worn in the order's lowest 'Minerval' grade.

## Iconography
An [[motif:owl of minerva|owl]] perches on or above an open book, framed as a hanging insignia. The owl, bird of Minerva (Athena), is the emblem of wisdom; the book signals study and the pursuit of enlightenment proper to the grade.

## Significance
The Illuminati organised initiates through ascending grades, the first of which — the [[motif:minerval grade|Minerval]] — took its name and badge from Minerva, goddess of wisdom. The owl-and-book device condensed the order's self-image as a school of reason and secret learning. Reproduced from the order's exposed papers, this emblem has become, detached from its history, one of the most recycled signs in modern [[motif:illuminati emblem|conspiracy iconography]].

## For artists & game designers
A clean faction badge — owl plus book — that reads as 'secret academy' at any size; ideal for a knowledge-guild crest.

citations.text: René Le Forestier, *Les Illuminés de Bavière* (1914).

title: Seal of Martinès de Pasqually
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
The personal seal of Martinès de Pasqually (c. 1727–1774), founder of the Order of Knight-Masons Elect Priests of the Universe — the Élus Coëns.

## Iconography
A small engraved seal carrying the cipher and symbolic device of Pasqually, the kind of personal sigil used to authenticate the correspondence and charters of a theurgic order.

## Significance
[[motif:martines de pasqually|Martinès de Pasqually]] founded the [[motif:elus coens|Élus Coëns]] ('Elect Priests'), a high-grade Masonic system devoted to [[motif:theurgy|theurgy]] — ceremonial operations meant to achieve the soul's '[[motif:reintegration|reintegration]]' with the divine and to obtain visible signs (the *Passes*) of angelic contact. His doctrine, transmitted in the *Traité de la réintégration des êtres*, shaped both Saint-Martin's Martinism and Jean-Baptiste Willermoz's later rectified rite.

## For artists & game designers
A spell-charged signet: a personal sigil that authenticates a magus's letters and can be 'read' to identify the order behind a summons.

citations.text: Martinès de Pasqually, *Traité de la réintégration des êtres* (posth.).

title: Extracts from Robison's *Proofs of a Conspiracy* (Fleuron)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: after John Robison (extract pr. William Bentley)
summary:
A printer's fleuron and heading from an extract of Professor John Robison's *Proofs of a Conspiracy against all the Religions and Governments of Europe* (1797).

## Iconography
A narrow letterpress strip carrying a decorative fleuron (printer's ornament) and the running title of the Robison extract — the typographic furniture of the cheap reprints that spread the text.

## Significance
The Edinburgh natural philosopher [[motif:john robison|John Robison]] charged that Continental Freemasonry and the [[motif:anti-illuminati|Illuminati]] had been captured by a conspiracy aiming to overthrow church and state. Issued in the same years as Barruel's *Memoirs*, *[[motif:proofs of a conspiracy|Proofs of a Conspiracy]]* carried the [[motif:illuminati scare|Illuminati scare]] into the English-speaking world, where it fuelled an anti-Masonic panic in the early American republic.

## For artists & game designers
A fragment of a forbidden tract — the kind of half-page handbill a player finds nailed up, hinting at a larger banned book.

title: Plate from *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer* (1785)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (attrib. circle of Hinricus Madathanus)
summary:
An emblematic plate from *Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer* (Secret Symbols of the Rosicrucians), Altona, 1785 — the great picture-book of the Gold- und Rosenkreuzer.

## Iconography
A densely symbolic plate combining alchemical, kabbalistic, and Christian-theosophical devices — circles, divine names, geometric figures, and emblematic motifs arranged as a meditative diagram in the [[motif:emblematic plate|emblem-book]] manner.

## Significance
The *[[motif:secret symbols rosicrucians|Geheime Figuren]]* gathered the visual lore of the eighteenth-century [[motif:gold und rosenkreuzer|Gold- und Rosenkreuzer]], a Christian-Rosicrucian order of alchemists and Masons that flourished in the German lands and counted Frederick William II of Prussia among its initiates. Its hand-coloured plates fuse alchemy, Kabbalah, and the [[motif:rose cross|rose cross]] into a single esoteric synthesis that powerfully shaped later occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made grimoire spread: a dense symbolic plate that can serve as an unlockable 'codex page' whose figures hint at a multi-step alchemical recipe.

title: Louis-Claude de Saint-Martin (Portrait au physionotrace)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Gilles-Louis Chrétien
summary:
A physionotrace profile portrait of Louis-Claude de Saint-Martin (1743–1803), 'le Philosophe inconnu' and father of Martinism.

## Iconography
A fine profile engraving made by the physionotrace — the mechanical tracing apparatus of Gilles-Louis Chrétien — showing the mystic in sober late-Enlightenment dress, the bust framed in an oval.

## Significance
[[motif:louis-claude de saint-martin|Saint-Martin]] passed from the theurgic [[motif:elus coens|Élus Coëns]] of Martinès de Pasqually to a more inward, contemplative mysticism of '[[motif:reintegration|reintegration]]' — the soul's return to its divine source — set out in works signed 'le [[motif:le philosophe inconnu|Philosophe inconnu]].' His teaching, blended with Pasqually's theurgy, became the root of the later [[motif:martinism|Martinist]] orders. A reader of Jacob Böhme, he is a central figure of Christian theosophy.

## For artists & game designers
The quiet mystic-philosopher archetype — a contemplative mentor whose power is interior rather than ceremonial.

citations.text: Arthur Edward Waite, *The Unknown Philosopher: Louis Claude de Saint-Martin* (1901).

title: The Skull and Bones Tomb, Yale (Detroit Publishing Co.)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Detroit Publishing Co.
summary:
A photochrom of 'the Tomb,' the windowless brownstone hall of the Skull and Bones society at Yale University (Detroit Publishing Co., c. 1900).

## Iconography
A street view of the austere, almost windowless stone building — a deliberately forbidding, mausoleum-like facade that gives the senior society its nickname, 'the Tomb.'

## Significance
[[motif:skull and bones|Skull and Bones]], founded at Yale in 1832, is the most famous of the American [[motif:collegiate secret society|collegiate secret societies]] — a 'senior society' tapping fifteen students a year, meeting behind closed doors in [[motif:the tomb|the Tomb]], its [[motif:death's head|death's-head]] emblem and number 322 the stuff of enduring speculation. It exemplifies how the European model of the initiatic order was transplanted into elite American institutions.

## For artists & game designers
A windowless lodge-building as a location: an intimidating, secret-society headquarters whose blank facade invites the player to find a way in.

citations.text: Alexandra Robbins, *Secrets of the Tomb: Skull and Bones, the Ivy League, and the Hidden Paths of Power* (2002).

title: Baron Karl Gotthelf von Hund (Strict Observance)
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A portrait of Baron Karl Gotthelf von Hund und Altengrotkau (1722–1776), founder of the Rite of Strict Observance, the Templar-revival system of high-grade Freemasonry.

## Iconography
A formal portrait of the baron in the dress of an eighteenth-century nobleman, identifying the founder of the order rather than displaying its regalia.

## Significance
[[motif:karl von hund|Von Hund]] claimed initiation into a secret order continuing the medieval [[motif:templar revival|Knights Templar]], governed by hidden '[[motif:unknown superiors|Unknown Superiors]]' whom he could never produce. His [[motif:strict observance|Rite of Strict Observance]] dominated German Masonry for a generation with its chivalric grades and Templar legend, until the Convent of Wilhelmsbad (1782) quietly abandoned the Templar claim. The episode is the classic case of the 'unknown superiors' that haunt esoteric orders.

## For artists & game designers
The leader bound to absent masters — an order whose authority rests on superiors no one has ever met, a built-in mystery and possible betrayal.

citations.text: René Le Forestier, *La Franc-maçonnerie templière et occultiste* (1970).

title: Masonic Temple, Chicago — Home Office of the Knights Templar
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Anonymous (*History of Chicago*)
summary:
A print of the Masonic Temple in Chicago described as the home office of the Knights Templar, headquarters of the Masonic chivalric order that revived the Templar name.

## Iconography
An architectural view of the great fraternal hall, captioned as the seat of the [[motif:knights templar|Knights Templar]] — the imposing 'temple' building proclaiming the order's wealth and civic standing.

## Significance
The Masonic [[motif:templar revival|Knights Templar]] (in the United States, the crowning order of the York Rite) is a Christian chivalric degree-system that adopted the medieval Templars' name, regalia, and legend without literal descent from them. By the late nineteenth century its [[motif:commandery|commanderies]] paraded in plumed uniforms and built grand halls; this Chicago temple shows how thoroughly the [[motif:neo-templar order|neo-Templar]] revival had embedded itself in American civic life.

## For artists & game designers
A fortress-like guild hall: a grand, named headquarters that signals a wealthy, ceremonial chivalric order at the heart of a city.

citations.text: Mark A. Tabbert, *American Freemasons: Three Centuries of Building Communities* (2005).

title: Adam Weishaupt, Founder of the Bavarian Illuminati
work: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: Christoph Wilhelm Bock
summary:
An engraved portrait of Adam Weishaupt (1748–1830), professor of canon law at Ingolstadt and founder, in 1776, of the Order of the Illuminati.

## Iconography
A bust-length engraving in the Enlightenment manner: the sitter in coat and ruffled stock, soberly framed, identified as the head of the Bavarian order — the conventional learned-man's likeness rather than any occult emblem.

## Significance
Weishaupt founded the *Bund der Perfektibilisten*, soon the [[motif:illuminati founder|Illuminati]], as a rationalist-republican brotherhood that infiltrated Masonic lodges to spread Enlightenment and anti-clerical ideals. Suppressed by the Bavarian elector in 1784–87, the order's confiscated papers were published and became the seed of the enduring [[motif:secret society|Illuminati conspiracy]] myth. Weishaupt himself fled to Gotha and lived out his life as a writer.

## For artists & game designers
A respectable scholar whose mild portrait belies a clandestine network — a useful 'hidden mastermind' face whose ordinariness is the point.

citations.text: René Le Forestier, *Les Illuminés de Bavière et la franc-maçonnerie allemande* (1914).

title: The Thirty-Two Paths of Wisdom
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Aviyud613 (modern diagram after the Sefer Yetzirah)
summary:
A diagram of the 'thirty-two wondrous paths of wisdom' — the ten sefirot together with the twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet — that open the *Sefer Yetzirah*.

## Iconography
The scheme enumerates the thirty-two paths: ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] (the numbered emanations) and the twenty-two connective [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] that link the nodes of the Tree of Life. Each path is labelled, displaying the combined numerical-and-alphabetic structure by which the *Sefer Yetzirah* says the world was formed.

## Significance
The opening line of the *Sefer Yetzirah* — 'by thirty-two wondrous paths of wisdom God engraved... and created His world' — became the architectural key of later Kabbalah, where the twenty-two letters are mapped onto the twenty-two paths joining the ten sefirot of the Tree of Life. This 10+22 structure underlies the Golden Dawn's tarot-and-path system and modern Western Kabbalah. (This is a modern explanatory diagram, not a historical manuscript.)

## For artists & game designers
A literal node-and-edge graph: ten nodes, twenty-two labelled edges — a ready skill-tree topology with built-in lore.

citations.text: Aryeh Kaplan, *Sefer Yetzirah: The Book of Creation* (1990).

title: The Hebrew Alphabet (Alefbet)
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Immanuel Giel
summary:
A clear chart of the twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet — the raw material that the *Sefer Yetzirah* treats as the building-blocks of creation.

## Iconography
The twenty-two [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] from Aleph to Tav are set out in order with their forms, including the five final (sofit) variants. It is a plain alphabetic reference rather than an esoteric diagram, but it displays exactly the set of signs the *Sefer Yetzirah* divides into three mothers, seven doubles, and twelve simples.

## Significance
In the *Sefer Yetzirah*, these twenty-two letters are not merely phonetic but cosmogonic: God 'engraved, carved, weighed, permuted, and combined' them to form all that exists. Knowing the letter-set is the precondition for *tzeruf* (permutation) and gematria, the operative techniques of Jewish letter-mysticism. The alphabet is thus the indispensable index for the whole tradition.

## For artists & game designers
The base 'rune set': twenty-two glyphs that a letter-magic system can draw on for spells, names, and constructs.

title: Number-Mysticism Plate (Kircher, '666')
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
An Athanasius Kircher plate engaging the number 666 through letter-number calculation, an example of the gematria/arithmology that parallels the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s numerical mysticism.

## Iconography
The plate works the famous apocalyptic number through the equivalence of letters and numbers, displaying names and words resolved into their numerical sums. It belongs to the family of Kircher's combinatorial and number-mystical diagrams in which alphabets, names, and numbers are tabulated and computed.

## Significance
[[motif:gematria|Gematria]] — assigning numerical values to letters and reading equivalences between words of equal sum — is, with *notarikon* and *temurah*, one of the three classic techniques of Hebrew letter-mysticism rooted in the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s teaching that letters are also numbers. Kircher (1602–1680), in his *Arithmologia* and *Oedipus Aegyptiacus*, transmitted this number-mysticism to learned Christian Europe. (The Hebrew alphabet's number-values, not the Greek isopsephy of '666', are the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s concern, but the technique is the same.)

## For artists & game designers
A worked example of name-to-number magic: any word resolves to a value that can gate spells or unlock secrets.

citations.text: Athanasius Kircher, *Arithmologia sive De abditis numerorum mysteriis* (1665).

title: Hebrew Letters with Traditional Pronunciations
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Squee3
summary:
A detailed table of the Hebrew letters with their names and traditional pronunciations across Jewish communities — a key to the sounds and names the *Sefer Yetzirah* manipulates.

## Iconography
Each of the [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] is listed with its name (Aleph, Bet, Gimel...) and its pronunciation in different reading traditions. Because every Hebrew letter is also a number, such tables implicitly carry the numerical values that underlie [[motif:gematria|gematria]].

## Significance
The *Sefer Yetzirah* groups the letters by sound and articulation — the three mothers correspond to a triad of elements, the seven doubles to letters pronounced two ways (hard/soft), the twelve simples to single sounds. A pronunciation table thus encodes the very phonetic distinctions on which the treatise's classification of the alphabet depends. Letter-names and values are also the substrate of *notarikon* and gematria.

## For artists & game designers
A sound-and-value key: pair each glyph with a phoneme and a number to drive a spoken-name or numeric magic system.

citations.text: *Sefer Yetzirah*, on the three mothers, seven doubles, twelve simples.

title: Plate from Kircher's *Arithmologia* (1665)
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Athanasius Kircher
summary:
A plate from Athanasius Kircher's *Arithmologia sive De abditis numerorum mysteriis* (Rome, 1665), his treatise on the hidden mysteries of numbers, including their Kabbalistic and magical uses.

## Iconography
The engraving sets out number-tables, combinatorial schemes, and name-and-number figures of the kind Kircher used to expound the 'secret virtues' of numbers — magic squares, letter-number correspondences, and arithmetical diagrams that fold Hebrew [[motif:gematria|gematria]] into a universal science of number.

## Significance
Kircher's *Arithmologia* is a key early-modern channel by which the Hebrew letter-mysticism behind the *Sefer Yetzirah* — the doctrine that letters are numbers and that creation is combinatorial — entered Christian learning. It sits alongside his *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* in treating Kabbalah as part of a *prisca theologia* uniting Hebrew, Egyptian, and Pythagorean number-wisdom. Daniel Stolzenberg and others survey Kircher's Kabbalistic sources.

## For artists & game designers
A period 'grimoire of numbers': tables and squares that can serve as puzzle-locks or the rule-sheet for a numeric magic system.

citations.text: Daniel Stolzenberg, *Egyptian Oedipus: Athanasius Kircher and the Secrets of Antiquity* (2013).

title: Title page of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Mantua, 1562)
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Printed by Ya'akov ben Naphtali ha-Kohen Gazzolo (Mantua)
summary:
The Hebrew title page of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Book of Formation) as printed at Mantua in 1562, one of the foundational early printings of the text with its classic commentaries.

## Iconography
A framed Hebrew title page announcing the *Sefer Yetzirah*, set in the dense vocalized type of a 16th-century Mantua press. The layout presents the short treatise together with the cluster of commentaries (such as those attributed to Saadia Gaon, Abraham ben David, and others) that the Mantua editions gathered around the core text.

## Significance
The *Sefer Yetzirah* teaches that God formed the cosmos through 'thirty-two wondrous paths of wisdom' — the ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] and the twenty-two [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] of the Hebrew alphabet — divided into three mothers, seven doubles, and twelve simples. The Mantua printings were decisive in fixing the text and its commentary tradition for early-modern readers and the later Christian Kabbalists who drew on it. Gershom Scholem and Tzahi Weiss are standard guides to its textual history.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic 'arcane primer' cover: dense sacred type framed as the opening of a book of world-making.

citations.text: Tzahi Weiss, *Sefer Yeṣira and Its Contexts* (2018).

title: Text page of the *Sepher Yetzirah* (Mantua, 1562)
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Printed by Ya'akov ben Naphtali ha-Kohen Gazzolo (Mantua)
summary:
An interior text page of the *Sepher Yetzirah* in the 1562 Mantua printing, showing the treatise's terse mishnaic Hebrew surrounded by commentary.

## Iconography
Vocalized Hebrew set in the column-and-gloss layout typical of the Mantua edition: the brief aphoristic chapters of the *Sefer Yetzirah* on the page, expounded by the marginal and interlinear commentaries that the edition collected. The text rehearses the doctrine of the three mother letters (Aleph, Mem, Shin), the seven doubles, and the twelve simples.

## Significance
The body of the *Sefer Yetzirah* maps the [[motif:hebrew letters|twenty-two letters]] onto the elements, the seven planets, and the twelve signs of the zodiac, making the alphabet the combinatorial machinery of creation. This is the seed of all later Kabbalistic *tzeruf* (letter-permutation) practice and of the Golem legend, in which inscribed letters animate matter. Scholem treated the work as the bridge between late-antique cosmology and medieval Kabbalah.

## For artists & game designers
A page of 'source code' for a world: short combinatorial rules mapping letters to elements, planets, and signs.

title: Leaf from Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* (1517)
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Johannes Reuchlin
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A leaf from Johannes Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* (On the Art of Kabbalah, 1517), the foundational Christian-Kabbalah treatise that brought Hebrew letter-mysticism into Renaissance humanism.

## Iconography
The page interweaves Latin dialogue with [[motif:hebrew letters|Hebrew letters]] and divine names, in the manner Reuchlin used to expound Kabbalistic doctrine — including his famous derivation of the *Pentagrammaton* (YHSVH) from the [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]] by inserting the letter *shin*. The leaf shows the textual machinery of name-and-letter speculation set into a printed humanist book.

## Significance
Reuchlin (1455–1522), the great Christian Hebraist, drew on the *Sefer Yetzirah*, the *Sefer Raziel*, and Abulafian letter-mysticism to argue that the manipulation of Hebrew letters and divine names was the highest wisdom. *De arte cabalistica* and his earlier *De verbo mirifico* made the letter-magic of the Jewish tradition respectable for Renaissance scholars and shaped Agrippa and later occult philosophy. Charles Zika and Wilhelm Schmidt-Biggemann survey his project.

## For artists & game designers
A Renaissance 'cross-tradition codex': Latin learning splicing in Hebrew glyphs — a model for hybrid magical writing.

title: Kabbalistic Tree of Life with Sefirot and Letters
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: RootOfAllLight (modern diagram)
summary:
A clean modern Tree of Life showing the ten sefirot and the twenty-two lettered paths in the three-column layout, illustrating the combined number-and-letter cosmology of the *Sefer Yetzirah*.

## Iconography
The ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] descend through the pillars of mercy, severity, and the reconciling middle; the connecting paths carry the [[motif:hebrew letters|twenty-two letters]]. The whole presents the 10 + 22 = 32 structure of the 'paths of wisdom' as a single articulated system.

## Significance
The three-pillar Tree with lettered paths is the standard reference diagram of Western esoteric Kabbalah, descending through the Renaissance Christian Kabbalists and the *Kabbala Denudata* into the Golden Dawn. It visually fuses the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s letter-doctrine with the sefirotic Tree of the *Zohar* and Luria. (Modern explanatory image.)

## For artists & game designers
A polished, legible version of the lettered Tree — drop-in art for a balance-of-forces magic system with left/right/centre columns.

title: Tree of Life with the Twenty-Two Lettered Paths
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: Eliyak (modern diagram)
summary:
A schematic Tree of Life in which the twenty-two connecting paths are each assigned a Hebrew letter, visualizing the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s pairing of letters with the structure of emanation.

## Iconography
Ten named [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] are arranged in the three-pillar configuration and joined by twenty-two paths, each path bearing one of the [[motif:hebrew letters|Hebrew letters]]. The diagram renders the *Sefer Yetzirah* claim that letters are the connective tissue of creation as a concrete map of nodes and lettered edges.

## Significance
The assignment of the twenty-two letters to the twenty-two paths of the Tree of Life is the great synthesis of the *Sefer Yetzirah* with the medieval sefirotic Tree; it was systematized by the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, which keyed each lettered path to a tarot trump, planet, or sign. The diagram is the everyday working tool of modern Kabbalistic magic. (Modern explanatory image.)

## For artists & game designers
The canonical lettered skill-tree: each edge tagged with a glyph that can carry an element, planet, or card.

citations.text: Israel Regardie, *The Golden Dawn* (1937–40), on the lettered paths.

title: The Tetragrammaton with Masoretic Vowel-Points
work: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: AnonMoos (diagram)
date: Modern diagram of a traditional subject
summary:
A diagram of the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) showing the various Masoretic vowel-pointings attached to the four sacred letters — the raw material of the name-permutation mysticism descended from the *Sefer Yetzirah*.

## Iconography
The four letters *Yod–He–Vav–He* of the [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]] are displayed with the different vowel-points the Masoretes placed on the Name (typically the vowels of *Adonai* or *Elohim*, signalling the substitute word to be read aloud). The figure makes visible how the unpronounceable Name was pointed in the written text.

## Significance
The *Sefer Yetzirah* describes God 'permuting' the letters; later Kabbalists such as Abraham Abulafia built whole contemplative systems on permuting and vocalizing the [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] of the divine Name (*tzeruf* and *hokhmat ha-tseruf*). The vowel-points are central to that practice, since each pointing yields a different meditative pronunciation. The Name's 42- and 72-letter expansions extend the same logic. Moshe Idel's studies of Abulafia are standard.

## For artists & game designers
A 'configurable true name': one fixed set of consonants whose changing vowel-pattern alters its power — a built-in combination lock.

citations.text: Moshe Idel, *The Mystical Experience in Abraham Abulafia* (1988).

title: Les Clavicules de R. Salomon, Vol. II (manuscript)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (French Clavicula scribe)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A page-opening from *Les Clavicules de R. Salomon* (The Keys of Rabbi Solomon), Volume II — a French *Veritable Clavicules* manuscript held by the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The spread shows French ritual text facing finely drawn pentacles and seals, each labelled with its operation and ringed with divine names and Hebrew or pseudo-Hebrew characters. The high-resolution scan captures the inked figures, marginal notes, and the manuscript's careful bilingual presentation.

## Significance
The French recension known as *Les Véritables Clavicules de Salomon* was among the most copied grimoires of the 18th and 19th centuries, attributing the work to 'Rabbi Solomon' and standardizing the set of planetary pentacles that Mathers later edited into English. The Wellcome's illustrated volumes are a key witness to this francophone branch of the tradition.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic spellbook spread — facing pages of cursive incantation and hand-drawn seals, ideal as an in-game 'grimoire' asset.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (1888), on the French recensions.

title: Dragon figure (Clavis Inferni, Sloane 3854)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Cyprianus / Clavis Inferni scribe)
summary:
A coloured dragon emblem from the *Clavis Inferni* (Key of Hell), the illustrated *Cyprianus* grimoire (British Library Sloane MS 3854).

## Iconography
The figure is a coiled or rampant [[motif:dragon|dragon]], painted in the manuscript's vivid reds and golds, set among the seals and circles that structure the book. In this tradition the dragon stands for the chained infernal power the magician claims to govern through the names of God and the angel Metatron.

## Significance
The full title — *Clavis Inferni sive magia alba et nigra approbata Metratona* ('Key of Hell, or white and black magic approved by Metatron') — frames its bold imagery as licensed by the highest angel. The dragon and serpent motifs link the manuscript to a wider Northern grimoire iconography of bound chaos, and make the *Clavis Inferni* one of the most visually memorable of all surviving ritual books.

## For artists & game designers
A period-authentic dragon sigil-emblem — a heraldic 'bound beast' device rather than a naturalistic monster.

citations.text: Stephen Skinner & David Rankine, studies of the *Clavis Inferni* (Sloane MS 3854).

title: King of the spirits (Clavis Inferni, Sloane 3854)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Cyprianus / Clavis Inferni scribe)
summary:
A coloured figure of one of the four spirit-kings from the *Clavis Inferni* (Key of Hell), the late-18th-century manuscript magical handbook associated with the *Cyprianus* tradition (British Library Sloane MS 3854).

## Iconography
The painting shows a robed regal figure — one of the kings of the four directions — rendered in ink and watercolour with the vivid, slightly naive style typical of the manuscript. The *Clavis Inferni* pairs such figures with circles, seals, and dragons in red, black, and gold to map the spirits ruling the cardinal points.

## Significance
The *Clavis Inferni sive magia alba et nigra approbata Metratona* is a short, richly illustrated grimoire attributed to 'Cyprianus,' organizing its magic around the four kings — Oriens, Paymon (Paimon), Egyn, and Amaymon — who govern east, west, north, and south. It is one of the most visually striking surviving ritual manuscripts and a key witness to the late Solomonic and Nordic 'black book' traditions.

## For artists & game designers
A painted 'spirit-king' boss portrait in authentic period style — colour-coded to a cardinal direction for a four-kings encounter set.

citations.text: Stephen Skinner & David Rankine (eds.), *The Goetia of Dr Rudd / Clavis Inferni* studies.

title: Pentagram seal (Clavis Inferni, Sloane 3854)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Cyprianus / Clavis Inferni scribe)
summary:
A coloured pentagram-seal page from the *Clavis Inferni* (Key of Hell), the illustrated late-18th-century *Cyprianus* grimoire (British Library Sloane MS 3854).

## Iconography
The page centres a [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]] within a ring of names and characters, painted in the manuscript's signature red, black, and gold. The five-pointed star anchors the protective and commanding power of the figure, surrounded by the letters and seals that bind it to a particular operation.

## Significance
The *Clavis Inferni* is unusually pictorial for a grimoire, presenting its magic as a sequence of boldly coloured seals, circles, and emblematic figures rather than dense text. Its pentagrams and characters belong to the same Solomonic family as the *Clavicula* pentacles but in a vivid Northern-European manuscript idiom. Stephen Skinner and David Rankine have published facsimile studies of the manuscript.

## For artists & game designers
A painterly, period-authentic pentagram prop — strong reference for hand-coloured ward art in red, black, and gold.

citations.text: Stephen Skinner & David Rankine, studies of the *Clavis Inferni* (Sloane MS 3854).

title: Gold seal from Cyprianus (18th-c. black book)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Scandinavian Cyprianus scribe)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A gold-painted magical seal from an 18th-century *Cyprianus* — the Scandinavian 'black book' branch of the Solomonic tradition — held by the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The seal is a circular figure brushed in gold, filled with characters, crosses, and divine names. The lavish use of gold marks it as a high-status, high-power [[motif:magic seal|seal]], to be copied for an important operation; the surrounding script names the spirits and holy powers it commands.

## Significance
