The *Cyprianus* or *Svartebok* ('black book') was the Nordic folk-magical descendant of the learned Solomonic grimoires, attributed to a legendary penitent magician 'Cyprian.' Such manuscripts mixed *Clavicula*-style seals with charms for healing, theft-detection, and treasure-finding, carrying elite ritual magic into vernacular practice. The Wellcome holds several illustrated examples.

## For artists & game designers
A gilded 'rare' seal variant — use gold to signal a higher-tier or legendary version of an otherwise ordinary sigil.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: The Archangel Metatron from Cyprianus (18th c.)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Scandinavian Cyprianus scribe)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A painted figure of the archangel Metatron from an 18th-century *Cyprianus* black book of the Solomonic tradition, held by the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The angel is depicted frontally with wings and robes, surrounded by his name and attendant characters in the manuscript's vivid colours. As the supreme angel of the divine presence, [[motif:metatron|Metatron]] is invoked to authorize and empower the operations of the book.

## Significance
Metatron — the 'lesser YHWH' of Jewish mystical lore who stands nearest the throne of God — was a favourite guarantor of grimoire magic; the *Clavis Inferni*, indeed, declares itself 'approved by Metatron.' His prominent portrait in a Nordic black book shows how thoroughly Kabbalistic angelology was absorbed into vernacular Solomonic practice. The Wellcome's *Cyprianus* preserves this striking angelic image.

## For artists & game designers
A folk-painted patron-angel portrait — a 'sponsor deity' figure whose presence in a spellbook licenses the player's magic.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Pentagram seal from Cyprianus (18th-c. black book)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Scandinavian Cyprianus scribe)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A pentagram seal from an 18th-century *Cyprianus* 'black book' of the Solomonic tradition, in the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The page sets a [[motif:pentagram|pentagram]] within a ring of characters and divine names, drawn in the bold, untrained hand typical of vernacular magical manuscripts. Each point and angle of the star carries letters that key it to the protective or commanding purpose of the seal.

## Significance
The pentagram was the all-purpose ward and command-figure of the Solomonic books, and its appearance in a Nordic *Cyprianus* shows how the learned seal-magic of the *Clavicula* filtered down into folk grimoires. These black books were genuinely used — for protection, healing, and luck — and survive in numbers across Scandinavia. The Wellcome's examples document the popular reach of the tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A rougher, folk-hand pentagram — useful when you want a ward that reads as homemade or peasant-magic rather than scholarly.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: John Dee's Seal of God (Sigillum Dei Aemeth, wax disc)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: John Dee (designed); British Museum object
summary:
John Dee's actual wax *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* — the inscribed disc on which his scrying table and crystal rested — photographed as a physical object in the British Museum.

## Iconography
The pale wax disc is carved with concentric rings of [[motif:divine names|divine and angelic names]], an outer band of letters and numbers, an inner heptagon and heptagram, and a central pentagram, all derived from the *Liber Juratus* (Sworn Book of Honorius). Unlike the printed Sigillum Dei diagrams, this is the worn, three-dimensional ritual instrument itself.

## Significance
Dee used several such wax seals in his angelic conversations with the scryer Edward Kelley in the 1580s: a large central disc and four smaller feet supported the *mensa* on which the *shew-stone* sat. The surviving British Museum examples are among the most important physical relics of Renaissance angel magic, and differ materially from the engraved Anselmi-derived prints of the same seal. Deborah Harkness's *John Dee's Conversations with Angels* is the standard study.

## For artists & game designers
The object form of a famous magic glyph — a worn carved wax disc, showing how a ritual diagram looks as a handled, physical relic.

citations.text: Deborah E. Harkness, *John Dee's Conversations with Angels* (1999).

title: Circle of Solomon and Triangle of Art (Goetia)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: After the Lemegeton; Mathers & Crowley edition (1904)
summary:
The protective Circle of Solomon and the external Triangle of Art from the *Ars Goetia*, first book of the *Lemegeton* (Lesser Key of Solomon), in the form printed by Mathers and Crowley.

## Iconography
The great circle is built from concentric bands inscribed with the names of God, of the archangels, and of the spirits of the days and hours, with a hexagram and pentagram set within. Outside it stands the [[motif:triangle of art|Triangle of Art]] — a triangle ringed with the names *Michael*, *Anaphaxeton*, and *Tetragrammaton* enclosing a circle — into which the conjured spirit is compelled to appear and be bound while the magician stands safe at the centre.

## Significance
The paired circle-and-triangle is the single most reproduced apparatus of Western ceremonial magic. The circle wards the operator; the triangle is the 'box' that constrains the evoked spirit to visible manifestation. The system descends from the Greek *Hygromanteia* and the Latin *Clavicula Salomonis* and was canonized for the modern revival by S. L. MacGregor Mathers and Aleister Crowley's 1904 *Goetia*.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive summoning-arena layout: a warded circle for the caster plus an external bind-triangle where the entity is forced to materialize.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & Aleister Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia: The Lesser Key of Solomon the King* (1904).

title: Goetic Circle of Pacts (Lesser Key of Solomon)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: After the Lemegeton
date: Lemegeton compiled 17th c.; this rendering modern
summary:
A second, more elaborate goetic circle from the *Lemegeton* tradition, laying out the full ring of divine names, hours, and seasonal angels used in the conjuration of spirits.

## Iconography
The diagram is a wheel of concentric bands: an inner cross or hexagram, then rings bearing the secret names of God, the archangels governing the four cardinal points, and the spirits of the hours and the four seasons distributed around the rim. The whole forms a closed cosmogram that fixes the operator within a complete map of celestial governance.

## Significance
Where the simple circle-and-triangle gives the bare working frame, this fuller variant shows the doctrine behind it: that to command a spirit the magician must invoke the entire hierarchy of names — divine, angelic, planetary, and temporal — by which God orders creation. Such circles, copied and recopied in 17th–19th-century English and continental manuscripts, vary in detail but share this layered name-architecture.

## For artists & game designers
A denser, lore-rich circle variant for a high-tier ritual — readable as 'the full version' of the basic summoning glyph.

citations.text: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), *The Lesser Key of Solomon: Lemegeton Clavicula Salomonis* (2001).

title: The Grand Grimoire (title page)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (attributed to 'Antonio Venitiana del Rabina')
summary:
The title page of the *Grand Grimoire* (Le Grand Grimoire), the notorious early-modern French handbook of black evocation and infernal pacts.

## Iconography
The printed title page announces the work and its false provenance — a supposed manuscript of King Solomon recovered and printed — framed in the plain typographic style of the cheap *Bibliothèque bleue* chapbooks through which it circulated. The text promises power over the infernal spirit Lucifuge Rofocale and the secret of the *clavicle* of Solomon.

## Significance
The *Grand Grimoire*, also issued as *Le Dragon rouge* (The Red Dragon), is the darkest of the popular French grimoires: it teaches the magic circle of black evocation and the drawing up of a pact with Lucifuge Rofocale, prime minister of Hell. Though pretending to ancient Solomonic origin, it is an 18th–19th-century compilation that became infamous in folklore as the most dangerous of all magic books. Owen Davies treats its publishing history in detail.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic 'forbidden book' cover — the title page alone signals a high-stakes, pact-with-the-devil questline.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Seal of Lucifuge Rofocale (Grand Grimoire)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (Grand Grimoire / Red Dragon tradition)
summary:
The conjuring seal of Lucifuge Rofocale, the infernal minister with whom the operator of the *Grand Grimoire* makes his pact.

## Iconography
The sigil is an abstract linear glyph — loops, crosses, and barbs forming the 'signature' of the spirit — of the kind drawn on the pact and shown during evocation to compel the demon's appearance. Like the [[motif:demon seal|Goetic seals]], it functions as a binding true-name in graphic form.

## Significance
Lucifuge Rofocale ('the one who flees the light') is named in the *Grand Grimoire* as the prime minister of Hell, controller of the world's wealth, who must be bargained with through the dread *Grand Conjuration*. His seal is among the most reproduced of the French 'black magic' sigils, central to the book's reputation as a manual for selling one's soul for treasure. The figure belongs to the same sigil-logic as the Solomonic spirit seals.

## For artists & game designers
A single 'devil's signature' glyph — the icon you stamp on an in-game pact or contract item.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).

title: Pentacles of Jupiter (Plate IV, Key of Solomon)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), after anonymous Solomonic originals
summary:
Plate IV of MacGregor Mathers's 1888 *Key of Solomon the King*, showing the seven Pentacles of Jupiter — the planetary seals of fortune, honour, riches, and the gaining of favour.

## Iconography
Each pentacle is a disc bordered with a verse (commonly a Psalm) and filled with a geometric figure, Hebrew divine names, and the characters of the spirits of Jupiter. The Jovial seals are benevolent in purpose: to acquire wealth and dignities, to win at law, to gain visions and protection. The plate arranges the set as a grid of operative seals.

## Significance
Mathers compiled his edition from several British Library *Clavicula Salomonis* manuscripts, keying each planetary set to its traditional virtues. Jupiter, the 'greater benefic' of classical [[motif:planetary magic|planetary magic]], governs expansion, prosperity, and lawful authority — so its pentacles are the grimoire's instruments of gain. The plate became a canonical reference for the modern occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
The 'buff / fortune' branch of a planetary seal system — Jupiter for wealth, luck, and favour mechanics.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (London, 1888).

title: First Pentacle of the Moon (Key of Solomon)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: After S. L. MacGregor Mathers's Key of Solomon
summary:
An isolated rendering of the First Pentacle of the Moon from the *Key of Solomon*, the lunar seal whose special virtue is to open all things that are shut or barred.

## Iconography
The disc shows a hand issuing from a cloud and holding a key, surrounded by Hebrew versicle-letters and the divine names proper to the [[motif:moon|Moon]]. The verse drawn from Psalm 107 — 'He hath broken the gates of brass, and cut the bars of iron in sunder' — frames the figure, declaring its power to break open locks, gates, and bindings.

## Significance
The planetary pentacles of the *Clavicula Salomonis* each pair a Scriptural verse with a figure that enacts it; here the key-in-hand and the prison-breaking Psalm make the seal a master 'opener.' This single-pentacle rendering circulates widely as a clean reference image, isolating one seal from Mathers's crowded plates for clearer study.

## For artists & game designers
A literal skeleton-key glyph — the in-game 'unlock' or 'break-binding' sigil, complete with its hand-and-key icon.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (London, 1888).

title: Pentacles of Mars (Plate VI, Key of Solomon)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), after anonymous Solomonic originals
summary:
Plate VI of MacGregor Mathers's 1888 *Key of Solomon the King*, showing the Pentacles of Mars — the planetary seals of war, courage, destruction, and resisting enemies.

## Iconography
Each disc is bordered with a Psalm verse and packed with a martial figure, Hebrew divine names, and the characters of the spirits of Mars. The Martial seals serve coercive and defensive ends: to excite ruin and discord, to render the bearer invulnerable, to terrify foes and stop bleeding. The plate ranks them as a grid of operative seals.

## Significance
Mars, the 'lesser malefic,' governs the violent and forceful operations of [[motif:planetary magic|planetary magic]]. In the *Clavicula* scheme each planet's pentacles match its astrological nature, and the Martial set supplies the tradition's combat and protective magic. Mathers's plate fixed the canonical look of these seals for the Golden Dawn and the later revival.

## For artists & game designers
The 'combat / aggression' branch of a planetary seal tree — Mars for offense, invulnerability, and fear effects.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (London, 1888).

title: Pentacles of Mercury and the Moon (Plate XI, Key of Solomon)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), after anonymous Solomonic originals
summary:
Plate XI of MacGregor Mathers's 1888 *Key of Solomon the King*, carrying the last Pentacles of Mercury and the Pentacles of the Moon — seals of knowledge, opening, travel, and lunar mystery.

## Iconography
The sheet joins the Mercurial discs to the Lunar set: pentacles bordered with verses and filled with figures, Hebrew divine names, and the characters of the spirits of Mercury and the Moon. The Mercurial seals confer knowledge, open locks and bindings, and command spirits of intellect; the Lunar seals govern travel, dreams, water, and the opening of doors.

## Significance
Mercury rules intellect, speech, and trickery, the Moon imagination, flux, and the night-side of magic. In [[motif:planetary magic|planetary magic]] their pentacles supply the grimoire's operations of knowledge, communication, and journeying. Mathers's arrangement again lets two planets share a sheet, completing the seven-planet cycle that defined the modern Solomonic canon.

## For artists & game designers
The Mercury/Moon 'utility' seals — unlocking, scouting, dreams, communication, and safe-travel effects.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (London, 1888).

title: Pentacles of Mars and the Sun (Plate VII, Key of Solomon)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), after anonymous Solomonic originals
summary:
Plate VII of MacGregor Mathers's 1888 *Key of Solomon the King*, completing the Pentacles of Mars and opening the Pentacles of the Sun — the solar seals of glory, liberty, and divine vision.

## Iconography
The plate carries the last Martial pentacles alongside the first Solar ones: discs bordered with verses and filled with radiant figures, Hebrew divine names, and the characters of the spirits of the Sun. The Solar seals serve to gain dominion and honour, to free a captive, to obtain the favour of the great, and to behold spirits and the glory of God.

## Significance
The Sun, source and king of the seven planets, governs sovereignty, fame, and illumination in [[motif:planetary magic|planetary magic]]. Its pentacles are the grimoire's instruments of authority and spiritual vision. Mathers arranged the planetary plates so that some share a sheet — here Mars passes into Sol — reflecting the manuscript layouts he worked from.

## For artists & game designers
The solar 'authority / revelation' seals — light-based buffs, charisma, freeing allies, and revealing hidden entities.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (London, 1888).

title: Pentacles of the Sun and Venus (Plate IX, Key of Solomon)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), after anonymous Solomonic originals
summary:
Plate IX of MacGregor Mathers's 1888 *Key of Solomon the King*, carrying the last Pentacles of the Sun and the Pentacles of Venus — the seals of love, grace, and concord.

## Iconography
The sheet joins the final Solar discs to the Venusian set: pentacles bordered with verses and filled with figures, Hebrew divine names, and the characters of the spirits of Venus. The Venusian seals serve to obtain grace and honour, to incite love, to bring concord between man and woman, and to compel the spirits of Venus to friendly service.

## Significance
Venus, the 'lesser benefic,' rules attraction, beauty, and harmony in [[motif:planetary magic|planetary magic]], so her pentacles furnish the tradition's love- and favour-magic. As with Plate VII, Mathers lets two planets share a sheet, mirroring the *Clavicula* manuscripts. The plate is part of the canonical 1888 visual set adopted across the modern revival.

## For artists & game designers
The Venusian 'charm / social' seals — attraction, persuasion, pacifying foes, and reconciliation mechanics.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (London, 1888).

title: Magical Circles and Seals of Solomon (19th-c. manuscript)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (19th-century Clavicula scribe)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A manuscript leaf gathering several magical circles and seals of Solomon, from a 19th-century *Clavicula Salomonis* in the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The page arranges multiple discs side by side: small protective circles, planetary pentacles, and abstract spirit-seals (*characteres*), each ringed with Hebrew or pseudo-Hebrew lettering and divine names. Together they form a practitioner's reference sheet — a compact menu of the figures to be inscribed for particular operations.

## Significance
Solomonic manuscripts routinely collect the seals onto summary plates so the operator can copy the correct figure at the elected planetary hour. This sheet shows how the tradition was actually used: as a working catalogue of interchangeable [[motif:magic seal|seals]] keyed to discrete purposes. The Wellcome holdings preserve a substantial body of these late hand-copies.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made 'sigil sheet' — a grid of distinct seals you can crop individually as collectible spell-icons.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (1888).

title: The Magical Circle of Solomon (19th-c. manuscript)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (19th-century Clavicula scribe)
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection).
summary:
A hand-drawn Magical Circle of Solomon from a 19th-century manuscript of the *Clavicula Salomonis*, held by the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The circle is composed of concentric rings filled with divine and angelic names, crosses, and characters, with stations marked for the operator and for ritual lamps or pentacles. The hand of the copyist is plainly visible in the slightly irregular lettering — this is a working drawing from a practitioner's book, not a printed plate.

## Significance
The *Key of Solomon* survived less in print than in a long chain of hand-copied manuscripts, many of them 18th- and 19th-century. Such copies, like those Mathers used for his 1888 edition, preserve the circle in its lived, variable form. The Wellcome Collection's Solomonic manuscripts document this manuscript afterlife of the tradition into the modern era.

## For artists & game designers
A hand-inked circle with an authentic 'grimoire page' texture — ideal when you want a ritual that looks copied by hand rather than machine-printed.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (1888).

title: A Magical Seal of Solomon (19th-c. manuscript)
work: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: Anonymous (19th-century Clavicula scribe)
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A single magical seal of Solomon drawn in full on its own leaf, from a 19th-century *Clavicula Salomonis* manuscript in the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The seal is a large concentric figure: an outer ring of divine and angelic names, an inner band of Hebrew letters, and a central geometric star or character. Drawn at full page size, it is the kind of seal meant to be transferred onto virgin parchment or metal and consecrated for a specific operation.

## Significance
Isolated, full-page seals like this served as the master copies from which a working pentacle would be reproduced. The detail and care of the drawing reflect the *Clavicula*'s insistence that a pentacle be inscribed exactly, in the right colours and at the right planetary hour, or it would have no virtue. The Wellcome manuscript preserves the seal in this presentation form.

## For artists & game designers
A single clean, high-resolution seal — perfect as a focal ritual prop or a UI icon for one powerful spell.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (ed.), *The Key of Solomon the King* (1888).

title: Spiritualism and Theosophy (Hours with the Ghosts, 1897)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Anonymous (book illustration, Internet Archive)
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An illustration from *Hours with the Ghosts, or, Nineteenth Century Witchcraft* (1897), an illustrated investigation into the phenomena of spiritualism and theosophy.

## Iconography
The plate belongs to a richly illustrated exposé-cum-survey of séance-room and [[motif:theosophy|Theosophical]] phenomena — mediums, manifestations, and the apparatus of spirit communication — typical of the turn-of-century literature that catalogued and scrutinised the occult revival.

## Significance
Henry Ridgely Evans's *Hours with the Ghosts* exemplifies the sceptical-investigative genre that grew up alongside spiritualism and Theosophy, surveying their 'phenomena' with a conjuror's and debunker's eye. Theosophy, founded in 1875 by Helena Blavatsky and Henry Olcott, gave the occult revival its most ambitious doctrinal system, absorbing spiritualist phenomena into a grand scheme of subtle bodies, masters, and cosmic evolution. Works like this document the dense, contested print culture in which belief and exposure circulated together.

## For artists & game designers
A window into how the period itself illustrated and catalogued 'ghost phenomena' — a source for an in-world investigator's casebook aesthetic.

citations.text: H. R. Evans, *Hours with the Ghosts, or, Nineteenth Century Witchcraft* (1897).

title: The Cottingley Fairies
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Elsie Wright & Frances Griffiths (published by A. Conan Doyle)
summary:
The Cottingley fairy photographs as published in *The Strand Magazine* (1920), the images of two girls with 'fairies' that famously convinced Sir Arthur Conan Doyle.

## Iconography
A young girl is photographed gazing at a group of winged [[motif:fairies|fairies]] dancing before her — in fact cardboard cut-outs copied from a popular illustrated book and propped up with hatpins. The composition's innocent realism is precisely what gave the hoax its power.

## Significance
Taken by cousins Elsie Wright and Frances Griffiths in 1917, the photographs were championed by Conan Doyle, an ardent spiritualist, in his 1920 *Strand* article and his book *The Coming of the Fairies* (1922). They became a cause célèbre of the post-war occult enthusiasm. Only in the 1980s did the elderly cousins admit the fairies were paper cut-outs — a definitive case study in how photographic 'evidence,' wishful belief, and a famous endorser combine to sustain a hoax.

## For artists & game designers
The textbook example of a faked wonder photograph — flat cut-out 'fairies' in a real scene — a model for in-world forged evidence.

citations.text: A. Conan Doyle, *The Coming of the Fairies* (1922).

title: Crewe Circle Spirit Photograph (Hope)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: William Hope / Crewe Circle
summary:
A Crewe Circle spirit photograph associated with William Hope, one of the images later cited in the exposure of his methods as a hoax.

## Iconography
The plate shows sitters overlaid with a ghostly [[motif:spirit photography|'extra']] — a faint superimposed face or form floating in the frame. The cloudy, luminous quality marks the deliberate secondary exposure used to produce the apparition.

## Significance
The Crewe Circle, the group of spirit photographers around William Hope, produced large numbers of such 'extras' for grieving clients in the years around the First World War. Harry Price's 1922 investigation for the Society for Psychical Research demonstrated that Hope substituted prepared plates, and images like this became evidence in the controversy. The episode is a key case study in the history of photographic fraud and the sociology of belief, dividing even prominent spiritualists.

## For artists & game designers
A documented example of a 'haunting' built from a swapped plate — good source material for an in-game forgery puzzle.

citations.text: H. Price, *Cold Light on Spiritualistic Phenomena* (1922).

title: The Davenport Brothers and the Spirit Cabinet
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Anonymous (book illustration, 1869)
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An 1869 book illustration of the Davenport brothers, Ira and William, the American mediums famous for their 'spirit cabinet' demonstrations.

## Iconography
The brothers are shown bound with ropes inside a wooden cabinet hung with musical instruments. Once the doors closed, the [[motif:spirit cabinet|spirit cabinet]] would erupt with floating instruments, bells, and disembodied hands — proof, the brothers claimed, of spirit agency.

## Significance
The Davenports toured America and Europe in the 1850s–60s, presenting their rope-tied cabinet act as genuine mediumship. The escapologist's secret — slipping the ropes — was widely suspected, and a young Harry Houdini, who knew Ira late in life, regarded their act as conjuring. The spirit cabinet became a defining theatrical form of physical mediumship and a direct ancestor of stage magic's escape acts. It marks the porous boundary between séance and showmanship.

## For artists & game designers
The original 'magic box' set piece — a person bound in a cabinet that produces impossible phenomena once closed.

citations.text: T. L. Nichols, *A Biography of the Brothers Davenport* (1864).

title: Eva Carrière Producing Ectoplasm (30 November 1912)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Baron Albert von Schrenck-Notzing (photographer)
summary:
A séance photograph of the medium Eva Carrière (Marthe Béraud) exuding 'ectoplasm,' taken on 30 November 1912 during the investigations of Baron Albert von Schrenck-Notzing.

## Iconography
The entranced medium is shown with a pale, filmy substance issuing from her mouth or hands — the [[motif:ectoplasm|ectoplasm]] believed to be the raw material from which spirits formed. In several plates the 'teleplasm' resolves into faces; here it appears as a fibrous, cloth-like emanation.

## Significance
Schrenck-Notzing photographed Eva Carrière repeatedly and published the results in *Phänomene der Materialisation* (1914), presenting ectoplasm as a genuine psychic phenomenon. Sceptics, then and since, identified the 'ectoplasm' as chewed paper, cheesecloth, and cut-out magazine faces regurgitated or concealed by the medium. The Eva C. plates are the canonical images of materialisation mediumship and a central exhibit in the history of psychical research and its debunking.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'ectoplasm' visual — a pale, cloth-like substance pouring from a trance medium — for any séance or possession scene.

title: The Fox Sisters
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: N. Currier (lithograph after a daguerreotype by Appleby)
summary:
An 1852 lithograph (N. Currier, after a daguerreotype) of the Fox sisters — Margaret, Kate, and their elder sister Leah — whose 'spirit rappings' launched modern spiritualism.

## Iconography
The three young women are shown in a formal group portrait, the standard mid-century lithographic likeness sold to a public fascinated by their celebrity. The image carries none of the séance drama — it is the calm public face of the movement's founders.

## Significance
In 1848 at Hydesville, New York, the young Fox sisters reported communicating with a spirit through coded [[motif:spirit rapping|rappings]], sparking the spiritualist movement that swept America and Europe. They became famous public mediums. In 1888 Margaret confessed the raps had been produced by cracking the joints of their toes — then partly recanted — leaving their legacy permanently contested. They remain the conventional starting point for any history of modern spiritualism.

## For artists & game designers
The origin-story characters of spiritualism: ordinary young women whose 'knocking code' began a global movement — a strong NPC archetype.

citations.text: B. Weisberg, *Talking to the Dead: Kate and Maggie Fox and the Rise of Spiritualism* (2004).

title: Kathleen Goligher and Alleged Ectoplasm
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: William Jackson Crawford (photographer)
summary:
A photograph by W. J. Crawford of the Belfast medium Kathleen Goligher, showing the alleged ectoplasm and 'psychic structures' he believed supported séance-room levitations.

## Iconography
The seated medium is shown with a pale mass of [[motif:ectoplasm|ectoplasm]] descending toward the floor — described by Crawford as 'cantilever' rods of psychic substance that lifted tables. To later eyes the material reads plainly as muslin or fabric concealed about the medium's person.

## Significance
William Jackson Crawford, a Belfast engineer, conducted years of experiments with the Goligher Circle and published *The Reality of Psychic Phenomena* (1916), theorising ectoplasmic 'levers.' After his death his findings were discredited; the photographs are now read as evidence of fraud rather than physics. The Goligher case is a notable instance of an engineer-investigator persuaded by séance-room manipulation, and a staple of the literature on the psychology of deception.

## For artists & game designers
Illustrates the 'invisible psychic machinery' conceit — ectoplasmic struts lifting furniture — a literal mechanism behind a haunting.

citations.text: W. J. Crawford, *The Reality of Psychic Phenomena* (1916).

title: Spirit Photograph by William Hope
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: William Hope
summary:
A spirit photograph by William Hope (1863–1933), the Crewe-based medium whose images of ghostly 'extras' beside living sitters made him a leading and controversial figure of post-war British spiritualism.

## Iconography
A living sitter is shown with a faint, semi-transparent face or figure — the 'extra' — superimposed nearby, often haloed in a cloudy aureole. As with all [[motif:spirit photography|spirit photographs]], the apparition is produced by exposing the plate twice or pre-loading a ghost image.

## Significance
Hope founded the Crewe Circle of spirit photographers and flourished amid the wave of bereavement following the First World War, when grieving families sought contact with the dead. He was famously exposed in 1922 by Harry Price of the Society for Psychical Research, who caught him switching photographic plates — yet Hope retained ardent defenders, including Sir Arthur Conan Doyle. His career encapsulates the collision of grief, belief, and fraud at spiritualism's twentieth-century peak.

## For artists & game designers
A study in the 'manufactured apparition' — useful reference for how a faked ghost was actually staged on a photographic plate.

citations.text: H. Price, *Cold Light on Spiritualistic Phenomena* (1922).

title: Georgiana Houghton, Spirit Drawing (Grand Jubilee print)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Georgiana Houghton (tradition); printed S. Chism, Boston
summary:
A printed sheet in the Houghton collection associated with the spiritualist medium and artist Georgiana Houghton, whose mediumistic drawings are pioneering works of abstract art.

## Iconography
Houghton's characteristic [[motif:spirit drawing|spirit drawings]] are dense, swirling webs of fine coloured lines, often built around sacred-geometric cores and titled as the work of named spirit guides (deceased relatives, Renaissance masters, and angelic beings) who, she said, moved her hand. Each was accompanied by an explanatory text decoding its symbolism.

## Significance
Georgiana Houghton (1814–1884) produced her swirling 'sacred symbolism' watercolours from the 1860s through automatic, spirit-guided drawing — works of pure abstraction decades before Kandinsky. Long overlooked, she is now recognised, alongside Hilma af Klint, as a key figure in the prehistory of abstract art arising directly from spiritualist practice. Her 1871 London exhibition presented these mediumistic images to a baffled public. She exemplifies the deep link between mediumship and the birth of non-representational art.

## For artists & game designers
Proof that 'automatic' spirit-guided mark-making yields a distinctive dense, web-like abstract style — a usable visual language for channelled art.

citations.text: Georgiana Houghton, *Evenings at Home in Spiritual Séance* (1881).

title: Astral Body of a Savage Man (Leadbeater)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Charles Webster Leadbeater (designer)
summary:
An aura plate from C. W. Leadbeater's *Man Visible and Invisible* (1902), illustrating the clairvoyantly-perceived astral body of an 'undeveloped' (so-labelled 'savage') man.

## Iconography
A human figure is surrounded by an ovoid [[motif:aura|aura]] of murky, muddied colours, each tint coded by the book's key to passions and faculties — the dull reds and browns marking, in Leadbeater's scheme, a coarse and undeveloped nature. The colours and clarity of the egg-shaped aura were held to register a person's moral and spiritual evolution.

## Significance
*Man Visible and Invisible* systematised the Theosophical doctrine of subtle bodies and the coloured aura, giving the aura its now-familiar visual form. The book's evolutionary and racial framing — ranking 'types' of humanity by aura — reflects the period's pervasive and now-rejected racial hierarchies, an uncomfortable but historically significant feature of early Theosophical literature. As image, its colour-coded aura diagrams shaped a century of New Age visualisation.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'character aura' chart: an egg of coded colours around a figure, readable as an alignment or status display.

citations.text: C. W. Leadbeater, *Man Visible and Invisible* (1902).

title: Magnetism and Mesmerism (New-York Dissector, 1845)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Anonymous (journal illustration)
rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An illustration from *The New-York Dissector* (1845), a journal of 'medicine, surgery, magnetism, mesmerism and the collateral sciences,' documenting mesmeric practice in mid-century America.

## Iconography
The plate shows the practice of [[motif:mesmerism|mesmerism]] — a magnetiser inducing a trance in a seated subject by passes of the hands or fixed gaze. Such imagery accompanied the lectures and demonstrations through which 'magnetic' healing and clairvoyance toured the United States.

## Significance
By the 1840s mesmerism had crossed the Atlantic and merged with phrenology ('phreno-magnetism') and incipient spiritualism, supplying the trance technique and the idea of a magnetic rapport through which a sensitive could perceive hidden things. The very journal title — joining magnetism and mesmerism to orthodox medicine while promising the 'mysteries and fallacies of the faculty' — captures the contested status of these practices. American mesmerism formed an immediate matrix for the spiritualist explosion of 1848.

## For artists & game designers
A period American depiction of 'putting someone under' by magnetic passes — useful for a hypnotist or trance-induction scene.

citations.text: On American mesmerism, see R. Fuller, *Mesmerism and the American Cure of Souls* (1982).

title: Le Baquet de Mesmer (Mesmer's Magnetic Tub)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: After Franz Anton Mesmer (tradition)
rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An illustration of *le baquet de Mesmer*, the magnetic tub around which Franz Anton Mesmer's Parisian patients gathered to receive the curative flow of 'animal magnetism' (Wellcome Collection).

## Iconography
Elegant patients sit in a circle around the [[motif:baquet|baquet]] — a covered wooden tub filled with 'magnetised' water, iron filings, and bottles — gripping protruding iron rods and joined by a connecting cord. The scene captures the theatrical, salon setting in which magnetic 'crises' (convulsions, swoons) were induced.

## Significance
Mesmer's doctrine of animal magnetism (1770s–80s) held that an invisible fluid pervaded the cosmos and that its blockage caused illness, treatable by the magnetiser's passes and apparatus. A 1784 French royal commission (including Franklin, Lavoisier, and Guillotin) found no such fluid, attributing the effects to imagination — an early landmark of placebo and suggestion research. Mesmerism nonetheless fed directly into hypnotism and, via its trance states and rapport, into the spiritualist and psychical movements of the next century.

## For artists & game designers
The original group 'energy-channelling apparatus' — patients wired to a magnetic tub — a striking proto-scientific occult device.

citations.text: R. Darnton, *Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France* (1968).

title: Mary Todd Lincoln with the Spirit of Abraham Lincoln
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: William H. Mumler
summary:
William H. Mumler's most famous spirit photograph, showing the widowed Mary Todd Lincoln seated while the ghostly figure of her assassinated husband, Abraham Lincoln, stands behind her with hands on her shoulders.

## Iconography
The sitter is photographed in standard cabinet-card pose; behind and above her a translucent figure with Lincoln's features hovers, hands resting on her shoulders. The 'extra' is rendered faint and luminous, the signature look of [[motif:spirit photography|spirit photography]] achieved through deliberate double exposure or an underdeveloped second plate.

## Significance
Mumler pioneered commercial spirit photography in Boston around 1861, claiming his plates captured the dead. Tried for fraud in New York in 1869 (and acquitted for lack of conclusive proof), he nonetheless became the genre's defining figure. This portrait of the grieving First Lady — taken under the name 'Mrs. Lindall' — is the most celebrated example, emblematic of how spiritualism offered consolation to a generation bereaved by the American Civil War. It remains the locus classicus for debates over photography, belief, and deception.

## For artists & game designers
The template for the 'photographed ghost': a faint translucent figure behind a solemn living sitter, instantly legible as a haunting.

citations.text: L. Kaplan, *The Strange Case of William Mumler, Spirit Photographer* (2008).

title: Ouija Talking Board (Kennard Novelty Company)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Elijah Bond & Charles Kennard (Kennard Novelty Company)
summary:
An early Ouija talking board manufactured by the Kennard Novelty Company, the firm that commercialised and patented the device in 1890.

## Iconography
The flat board is printed with the alphabet, the numerals 0–9, and the words YES, NO, and GOODBYE. A heart-shaped pointer (the [[motif:planchette|planchette]]) is moved across the surface by the sitters' resting fingers to spell out messages, ostensibly from spirits.

## Significance
The Ouija board emerged from the spiritualist craze for spirit communication, patented by Elijah Bond and produced by Charles Kennard; the name was reputedly supplied by the board itself in a séance. Its apparent motion is now explained by the ideomotor effect — small unconscious muscular movements of the sitters. Transformed from a séance instrument into a mass-market parlour game (later a Parker Brothers / Hasbro product), it became the most enduring popular artefact of the spiritualist movement.

## For artists & game designers
The instantly recognisable spirit-communication interface: alphabet board plus sliding planchette, a ready diegetic UI for contacting the dead.

citations.text: R. Murch, on the history of the Ouija board and the Kennard Novelty Company.

title: Eusapia Palladino Levitating a Table
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: H. Mairet (photographer)
summary:
A séance photograph of the Italian medium Eusapia Palladino apparently levitating a table, taken during one of the many scientific investigations of her phenomena.

## Iconography
The medium sits at a small table that rises from the floor while sitters hold her hands and feet to control for trickery. The [[motif:table levitation|levitating table]] was the signature feat of Palladino's repertoire of telekinetic effects, along with rapping, cold breezes, and touches from unseen hands.

## Significance
Eusapia Palladino (1854–1918) was the most investigated medium of her era, examined by commissions including Cesare Lombroso, Charles Richet, the Society for Psychical Research, and others. Results were notoriously mixed: she produced effects that baffled some researchers but was repeatedly caught freeing a hand or foot to fake phenomena, most damningly in the 1910 Feilding sittings and at Columbia University. Her career is the central case in the scientific scrutiny of physical mediumship.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal séance levitation — a table lifting under controlled hands — a clean visual for a 'controlled test of the paranormal' scene.

citations.text: H. Carrington, *Eusapia Palladino and Her Phenomena* (1909).

title: Phrenological Chart of the Faculties
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Anonymous (popular graphic arts)
summary:
A nineteenth-century phrenological chart mapping the regions of the skull to the mental 'faculties' or 'organs' of the mind.

## Iconography
A head in profile is divided into numbered and labelled zones — Amativeness, Benevolence, Veneration, Combativeness, and dozens more — each [[motif:phrenology|organ]] held to govern a trait whose strength could be read from the bumps of the skull above it. The chart is the standard reference image of the phrenologist's practice.

## Significance
Phrenology, founded by Franz Joseph Gall around 1800 and popularised in America by the Fowler brothers, claimed that character could be read from cranial shape. Though discredited as science, it was enormously influential, spreading a vocabulary of localised mental 'faculties' and feeding the same Victorian appetite for reading the hidden self that drove physiognomy, mesmerism, and spiritualism. 'Phreno-magnetism' explicitly fused phrenology with mesmeric trance.

## For artists & game designers
The labelled-head diagram is a perfect diegetic 'character stat sheet' — map traits to skull regions for a pseudo-scientific UI.

citations.text: J. van Wyhe, *Phrenology and the Origins of Victorian Scientific Naturalism* (2004).

title: Spiritualist Planchette (Robert Dale Owen)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Kirby and Co. (maker)
summary:
A spiritualist planchette (this example given to Matthew Wilson by the reformer and spiritualist Robert Dale Owen), the small wheeled writing device used to receive messages from spirits.

## Iconography
The heart-shaped wooden board rests on two small castors, with a pencil mounted through a hole at its point. Sitters lay their fingertips lightly on the [[motif:planchette|planchette]]; it rolls across paper and the pencil traces words and figures attributed to spirit control.

## Significance
The planchette became popular in the 1850s–60s as an instrument of automatic writing, predating and later inspiring the lettered Ouija board (the Ouija's pointer is itself called a planchette). It gave physical form to the spiritualist doctrine of [[motif:automatism|automatism]] — the belief that an external intelligence could guide the hand. Like the Ouija, its motion is now attributed to the ideomotor effect. Robert Dale Owen, a prominent advocate, helped popularise such devices in America.

## For artists & game designers
The pre-Ouija writing oracle: a wheeled board with a pencil that scrawls spirit messages — a tactile, low-tech séance prop.

citations.text: R. D. Owen, *Footfalls on the Boundary of Another World* (1860).

title: Spirit Rapping Diagram (Henry Novra)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Henry Novra (attributed)
summary:
A mid-nineteenth-century 'spirit rapping' diagram, attributed to Henry Novra, illustrating the séance phenomenon of mysterious knocks answering questions.

## Iconography
The diagram depicts a séance table and sitters, schematising how [[motif:spirit rapping|spirit raps]] were said to occur — knocks sounding in reply to the alphabet or to yes/no questions. Such plates often doubled as exposés, showing how the sounds could be produced by concealed mechanical or bodily means.

## Significance
Spirit rapping, the phenomenon that launched the Fox sisters and modern spiritualism, became the movement's foundational 'evidence': coded knocks by which the dead supposedly communicated. A whole literature of explanation and debunking grew up around it, from toe-cracking confessions to hidden devices. Diagrams like Novra's belong to the Victorian genre of rational explanation aimed at the rapping craze, documenting both the practice and the sceptical response.

## For artists & game designers
A period schematic of how a séance 'worked' — useful reference for staging or explaining a knocking-spirit sequence.

citations.text: On the rapping craze, see histories of Victorian spiritualism.

title: Thought-Form of the Music of Gounod (Besant & Leadbeater)
work: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: Annie Besant & C. W. Leadbeater (designers)
summary:
A plate from Annie Besant and Charles Leadbeater's *Thought-Forms* (1905) depicting the supposed clairvoyantly-perceived form generated by the music of Gounod.

## Iconography
Swirling bands and architectural masses of luminous colour rise above a church, rendering music as a visible [[motif:thought-forms|thought-form]]. Each hue carries a coded meaning from the book's colour key — affection, devotion, intellect — so that sound becomes a coloured edifice in the astral atmosphere.

## Significance
*Thought-Forms* claimed that thoughts and emotions produce coloured shapes perceptible to trained clairvoyants, and supplied a systematic colour-symbolism to read them. A landmark of Theosophical art theory, the book's abstract colour-forms are frequently cited as a direct influence on early abstract painters — Kandinsky, Mondrian, and others — who sought to make the spiritual visible. It bridges occult revival and modernist abstraction.

## For artists & game designers
A founding template for 'visualising sound/emotion as coloured energy' — directly applicable to synesthetic UI and aura effects.

title: Names and Seal of the Intelligence and Spirit of Jupiter (Agrippa)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
summary:
The Jupiter divine, intelligence and spirit names with their derived seals, as set out in Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia*.

## Iconography
The figure pairs the Hebrew **names** governing [[motif:jupiter|Jupiter]] — the divine name, the intelligence (Iophiel) and the spirit (Hismael) — with the line-traced [[motif:planetary seal|sigils]] generated from the planet's 4×4 [[motif:magic square|kamea]]. Names and seals are shown together as a working table.

## Significance
This is the heart of Agrippa's method: each planetary name is converted, letter by letter via *gematria*, into a numbered path across the square, and that path becomes the engraved sigil. Showing the names beside the figures exposes how the [[motif:magic square|kameas]] mediate between Hebrew angelology and operative talisman-making — the synthesis of Kabbalah and astral magic that defines the Renaissance tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A name-plus-derived-glyph table is a ready 'true name → sigil' mechanic for a summoning or binding system.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia libri tres* (1533).
Christopher I. Lehrich, *The Language of Demons and Angels* (2003).

title: Character of Mercury (Agrippa)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
summary:
The **character (seal) of Mercury** from Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (1533).

## Iconography
An intricate composite glyph, traced from the 8×8 Mercury [[motif:magic square|kamea]] (constant 260), forms the *signum* of [[motif:mercury|Mercury]] — the most elaborate of the planetary characters.

## Significance
The Mercury seal was engraved for eloquence, learning, commerce, and skill — the mercurial virtues. Agrippa's Book II gave the dense order-8 square and the sigils of the intelligence Tiriel and spirit Taphthartharath, fixing the figure for all later practitioners. As the planet of writing and number, Mercury appropriately bears the most complex of the seven seals.

## For artists & game designers
The most ornate Agrippan seal — perfect as a 'high arcana / mastery' crest for a magician's guild or skill cap.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia libri tres* (1533).

title: Character of the Sun (Agrippa)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
summary:
The **character (seal) of the Sun** as given in Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (1533), in a clean digitized rendering.

## Iconography
A single composite glyph, traced from the solar 6×6 [[motif:magic square|kamea]], forms the *signum* of [[motif:sun|Sol]] — the figure to be engraved whole on a solar talisman.

## Significance
This is the form that fixed the planetary characters for the whole later tradition: Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (Book II) tabulated for each planet its square, its divine and angelic names, and the seals of its intelligence and spirit. The Sun's character drew the planet's royal, vivifying virtue for talismans of honour and health. Barrett's 1801 plates and the modern occult revival descend directly from these figures.

## For artists & game designers
A crisp, authoritative version of the solar emblem — the 'source text' look, good for a lore document or an inscribable relic.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia libri tres* (1533).
Christopher I. Lehrich, *The Language of Demons and Angels* (2003).

title: Seal of the Spirit of the Sun (Sorath), Agrippa
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
summary:
The sigil of the **Spirit of the Sun** (Sorath) from Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia*.

## Iconography
A line-figure traced through the solar [[motif:magic square|kamea]] spells the spirit-name Sorath, whose number 666 is the sum of the order-6 square. It is the forceful, darker aspect of the solar virtue, twin to the intelligence Nakhiel.

## Significance
The solar spirit Sorath is the most numerologically famous of the planetary spirits: 666, derived from the 6×6 [[motif:sun|Sun]] square, drew the attention of later commentators linking the kameas to the 'number of the beast'. Within the talismanic system, however, Sorath is simply the [[motif:planetary seal|spirit]] paired with Nakhiel — the raw versus the governed solar power. Agrippa's figure is the standard source.

## For artists & game designers
A solar dark-twin sigil with a built-in 666 hook — a natural 'corrupted sun' or solar-eclipse motif.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia libri tres* (1533).

title: Character of Venus (Agrippa)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
summary:
The **character (seal) of Venus** from Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (1533).

## Iconography
A single woven glyph, drawn from the 7×7 Venus [[motif:magic square|kamea]] (constant 175), forms the *signum* of [[motif:venus|Venus]] for engraving on a copper talisman.

## Significance
Agrippa's Venus character is the canonical form of the love-and-concord seal, made in the planet's hour to attract affection, beauty, and reconciliation. Book II of *De occulta philosophia* supplied the square, the angelic names, and the intelligence (Hagiel) and spirit (Kedemel) sigils that all later sources copied. The Venus talisman is the great charm of harmony in the tradition.

## For artists & game designers
The authoritative Venus emblem — a love- or peace-token glyph in the 'source manuscript' style.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia libri tres* (1533).

title: Religious amulet or talisman pendant (PAS FindID 865151)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (English maker)
rights: CC BY 2.0 (Portable Antiquities Scheme). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraved **amulet/talisman pendant** recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme (FindID 865151), a second surviving worn talismanic object.

## Iconography
The small pierced pendant bears engraved devices and characters of an apotropaic, talismanic kind, made to be suspended and worn for protection or influence. The PAS record classes it between religious amulet and magical [[motif:talisman|talisman]] — the categories overlapped in practice.

## Significance
Worn [[motif:talisman|amulets]] like this are the everyday end of the spectrum whose learned end is Agrippa's planetary seals. The same impulse — to fix a benign power in a portable object — runs from the [[motif:picatrix|Picatrix]]'s astral images to the cheap engraved charm. Two such finds together show how widespread the practice remained in post-medieval England.

## For artists & game designers
Pair this with the other PAS pendant for a believable 'common vs. crafted' tier of protective charms in a loot system.

citations.text: Don Skemer, *Binding Words: Textual Amulets in the Middle Ages* (2006).

title: Astrological diagram inside a book cover (1801)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A hand-drawn **astrological diagram** pasted inside a book cover, dated 1801, from the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The diagram lays out astrological **correspondences** — zodiac signs, planetary symbols, and associated figures — in the manner of a working reference for casting charts or timing operations. Its placement inside a binding marks it as a practitioner's personal aide-mémoire.

## Significance
Diagrams of this kind condensed the [[motif:astrology|astrological]] data a talisman-maker needed — the rulerships and timings on which [[motif:talisman|talismanic]] practice depended — into a single consultable sheet. Surviving in a private book, it testifies to the persistence of operative astrology into the nineteenth century, the same revival milieu that produced Barrett's *Magus*.

## For artists & game designers
A hand-inked correspondence chart tucked in a cover is an evocative 'practitioner's cheat-sheet' prop — personal, used, and arcane.

title: The Five Points of a Planetary Square (construction diagram)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (occult-revival)
summary:
A diagram naming the structural points of a planetary magic square, from *The Mystic Test Book* — a key to how sigils are derived from a kamea.

## Iconography
The figure labels the salient positions of the grid (centre, corners, and intermediate points) used when laying out a square and when tracing lines across its numbers. It is a working diagram rather than a finished talisman.

## Significance
The operative power of a [[motif:magic square|kamea]] lies not only in its summing numbers but in the *paths* drawn through it: connecting the numbered cells in sequence produces the looping [[motif:planetary seal|sigils]] of the planetary intelligence and spirit. This construction sheet exposes the geometry behind that derivation, making explicit the method Agrippa's tables assume.

## For artists & game designers
A 'how the sigil is built' diagram is perfect UI for a crafting screen where the player traces a path across a grid to forge a seal.

citations.text: L. W. de Laurence (comp.), *The Mystic Test Book* (1907).

title: Seal of the Intelligence of Jupiter (Iophiel)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The sigil of the **Intelligence of Jupiter** (Iophiel), traced from the 4×4 Jupiter kamea.

## Iconography
A looping path across the implied square spells Iophiel, the benign presiding power of [[motif:jupiter|Jupiter]]. Turning-points are marked as nodes along the line.

## Significance
Jupiter's intelligence governs the planet's fortunate, expansive, magnanimous virtue. Its sigil, derived from the order-4 [[motif:magic square|kamea]] (constant 34), was engraved on talismans for prosperity, honour, legal success, and the favour of rulers — the classic 'jovial' boon. It is paired with the planet's spirit Hismael in the Agrippan scheme.

## For artists & game designers
The Jupiter intelligence-seal is the 'fortune-bringer' glyph — a graceful sigil for a luck buff or patronage mechanic.

title: The Magic Square of Jupiter (4×4 kamea)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (after Agrippa)
summary:
The 4×4 magic square assigned to **Jupiter**, the talismanic grid most famous to art history through Dürer.

## Iconography
Sixteen cells hold 1–16 so that every line sums to **34**. This is the order-4 square; a closely related arrangement appears in Albrecht Dürer's engraving *Melencolia I* (1514), where the bottom row even encodes the date.

## Significance
[[motif:jupiter|Jupiter's]] square underwrites the planet's beneficent, expansive virtue and yields the seal of its intelligence **Iophiel** and spirit **Hismael**. Engraved on tin or silver in the planet's hour, the Jupiter table was the classic talisman for fortune, increase, and favour with the great. Dürer's borrowing of the square shows how thoroughly the kameas had entered learned visual culture by the sixteenth century.

## For artists & game designers
The Jupiter square is the 'luck / prosperity' charm of the set — a clean 4×4 motif for a fortune or boon mechanic with a real art-historical pedigree.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: Seal of the Intelligence of Mars (Graphiel)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The sigil of the **Intelligence of Mars** (Graphiel), traced from the 5×5 Mars kamea.

## Iconography
A sharp, angular line-figure threads the implied square to spell Graphiel, the presiding intelligence of [[motif:mars|Mars]]. Its restless geometry suits the planet's fiery character.

## Significance
Graphiel governs the channelled, ordering side of the martial virtue — courage and victory rather than blind destruction. Derived from the order-5 [[motif:magic square|kamea]] (constant 65), the seal was engraved on iron for triumph in conflict and the routing of enemies, balanced against the planet's malefic spirit Barzabel. Mars's talismans were the most warily handled of the seven.

## For artists & game designers
An angular, aggressive sigil — the natural 'war-seal' to brand on weapons or a combat-buff icon.

title: The Magic Square of Mars (5×5 kamea)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (after Agrippa)
summary:
The 5×5 magic square assigned to **Mars**, the talisman-grid of the war-planet.

## Iconography
Twenty-five cells hold 1–25 so that every row, column and diagonal sums to **65**; the total is 325. The order-5 square sits between Jupiter's and the Sun's in the ascending planetary series.

## Significance
From [[motif:mars|Mars's]] square are drawn the seal of its intelligence **Graphiel** and spirit **Barzabel**, and the constant 65. The Mars talisman — traditionally cut on iron in the planet's hour — was made for victory, courage, and the overthrow of enemies, but warned against for its violent, choleric, 'infortunate' character. It is the martial member of the seven-fold talismanic set.

## For artists & game designers
The Mars square is the obvious 'combat / aggression' charm: a red-iron 5×5 sigil for a weapon-enchant or berserk mechanic.

citations.text: L. W. de Laurence (comp.), *The Mystic Test Book* (1907).

title: Seal of Mercury
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The **Seal of Mercury**, the composite character of the planet of mind and exchange, after Barrett's *The Magus*.

## Iconography
An intricate glyph gathers the lines of the dense 8×8 Mercury [[motif:magic square|kamea]] (constant 260) into the *signum* of [[motif:mercury|Mercury]] — fittingly the most elaborate of the planetary characters.

## Significance
Mercury's seal was engraved in fixed metal or written in its colours in the planet's hour for eloquence, learning, commerce, swift travel, and skill in the arts — the mercurial gifts. Its intelligence is Tiriel and its spirit Taphthartharath. As the planet of writing and number, Mercury is aptly the one whose square is largest among the inner planets and whose seal is the most complex.

## For artists & game designers
The most ornate sigil of the set — ideal as a 'master / arcane knowledge' emblem for a scholar faction or skill-tree crest.

title: The Magic Squares of the Seven Planets (Barrett, The Magus, 1801)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Francis Barrett (after Agrippa)
summary:
The plate of the seven planetary magic squares from Francis Barrett's *The Magus* (1801), reproducing the *tabulae* of Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia*.

## Iconography
Seven number-grids ascend in size with the planetary spheres: the 3×3 of **Saturn**, the 4×4 of **Jupiter**, the 5×5 of **Mars**, the 6×6 of the **Sun**, the 7×7 of **Venus**, the 8×8 of **Mercury**, and the 9×9 of the **Moon**. In each, every row, column and diagonal sums to the same 'magic constant'; Hebrew numeral forms accompany the figures.

## Significance
These [[motif:magic square|kameas]] (Arabic *wafq*, Hebrew-influenced *kamea*) are the mathematical heart of astral talisman-magic. From the grid one derives the planet's seal, the sigils of its [[motif:planetary seal|intelligence and spirit]], and the numbers engraved on a planetary talisman. Agrippa drew the scheme from the Arabic tradition (the magic squares appear in al-Buni and the *Picatrix* milieu); Barrett's English plate carried it into the modern occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
Seven nested number-grids of rising order make an elegant tech-tree or crafting-table motif — each planet a tier with its own constant and derived sigil.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), bk. II.

title: Seal of the Intelligence of Saturn (Agiel)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The sigil of the **Intelligence of Saturn** (Agiel), traced from the 3×3 Saturn kamea, after Barrett's *The Magus*.

## Iconography
A looping line-figure dotted at its turning-points runs across the implied magic square, its path spelling out the planet's intelligence-name in the [[motif:planetary seal|number-letters]] of the grid. The 'intelligence' is the beneficent presiding power of the planet.

## Significance
Agrippa pairs each planet with a virtuous **intelligence** and a darker **spirit**, whose sigils are generated by connecting the cells of the planetary [[motif:magic square|square]] that correspond to the letters of their Hebrew names. [[motif:saturn|Saturn's]] intelligence Agiel (sum 45) governs the favourable, ordering side of the saturnine virtue; its sigil was engraved on talismans to invoke that power while restraining the malefic.

## For artists & game designers
The dotted, looping seal is the canonical 'magic sigil' shape — instantly legible as an arcane glyph for a spell, ward, or summon.

title: The Magic Square of Saturn (3×3 kamea)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (after Agrippa)
summary:
The 3×3 magic square assigned to **Saturn**, the smallest and most ancient of the planetary kameas.

## Iconography
Nine cells hold the numbers 1–9 so that every row, column and diagonal sums to **15**; the whole sums to 45. This is the same arrangement as the Chinese *Lo Shu*, the oldest known magic square, here read as the seal-source of the slowest planet.

## Significance
In the talismanic scheme each planet owns a square whose order matches its rank from Saturn outward. [[motif:saturn|Saturn's]] 3×3 grid yields, by tracing lines between its numbers, the sigils of the planet's [[motif:planetary seal|intelligence (Agiel) and spirit (Zazel)]] and the constant 15 that names them in *gematria*. A Saturn talisman was engraved with this square to draw down the planet's heavy, binding, melancholic virtue.

## For artists & game designers
The 3×3 of Saturn doubles as a ready puzzle grid — fill nine cells to a constant of 15 — with built-in esoteric flavour.

citations.text: L. W. de Laurence (comp.), *The Mystic Test Book* (1907).

title: The Magic Square of Venus (7×7 kamea)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (after Agrippa)
summary:
The 7×7 magic square assigned to **Venus**, talisman-grid of the planet of love and concord.

## Iconography
Forty-nine cells hold 1–49 so that every line sums to **175**. The order-7 square is the second-largest of the planetary tables, exceeded only by Mercury's and the Moon's.

## Significance
[[motif:venus|Venus's]] square yields the seal of its intelligence **Hagiel** and spirit **Kedemel**, and the constant 175. Engraved on copper in the hour of Venus, the talisman was made to win love, friendship, fertility, and reconciliation. Its larger, denser grid reflects Venus's higher place in the planetary order than the swift inner planets below.

## For artists & game designers
The Venus square is the 'charm / attraction' talisman — a copper 7×7 sigil for a social, seduction, or peacemaking mechanic.

citations.text: L. W. de Laurence (comp.), *The Mystic Test Book* (1907).

title: The last lunar mansion of Scorpio (Wellcome Collection)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous
rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A diagram of a **mansion of the Moon** in the region of Scorpio, from the Wellcome Collection — an image from the operative lunar-mansion tradition.

## Iconography
The figure marks one station of the Moon among the fixed stars: one of the **manāzil al-qamar**, the 28 segments through which the Moon passes in its monthly circuit, here falling near **Scorpio**.

## Significance
The [[motif:lunar mansions|28 lunar mansions]] form a system distinct from the 12 solar signs, central to Arabic astronomy and to the talismanic magic of the [[motif:picatrix|Picatrix]] and Agrippa. Each mansion carried its own influence and prescribed image, and the Moon's passage through it set the precise time for making a [[motif:talisman|talisman]] or beginning an undertaking. Diagrams like this served that finely-timed image-magic.

## For artists & game designers
A 28-station lunar cycle is a higher-resolution alternative to the 12 signs — ideal for a talisman-crafting system with many short, distinct windows.

title: Frontispiece of a 17th-century Picatrix (Hagia Sophia MS 2443)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (Ottoman copyist of the Ghayat al-Hakim)
summary:
The frontispiece of a seventeenth-century Arabic manuscript of the *Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm* — the **Picatrix** — held in the Hagia Sophia collection (MS 2443), Istanbul.

## Iconography
The opening leaf carries the decorated title and incipit of the *Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm* (The Goal of the Sage), the Arabic handbook known in the Latin West as the **Picatrix**. The page is the threshold to the most important medieval manual of astral image-magic.

## Significance
Compiled in Arabic in al-Andalus (10th–11th c.) and translated for Alfonso X of Castile in 1256, the [[motif:picatrix|Picatrix]] taught how to draw planetary and stellar spirit into engraved [[motif:talisman|talismans]] made under precise astrological conditions — the right planet, hour, [[motif:lunar mansions|lunar mansion]], and suffumigation. It transmitted the magic squares, the planetary images, the 28 mansions, and the fixed-star magic that Agrippa and the whole Renaissance tradition inherited.

## For artists & game designers
An ornate Arabic grimoire frontispiece is a perfect 'forbidden manual' prop — the in-world source of every talisman recipe.

citations.text: *Picatrix: The Latin Version of the Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm*, ed. David Pingree (1986).
Liana Saif, 'The Cosmology of the Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm,' (2021).

title: Latin Picatrix (Kraków, Jagiellonian Library MS 793, fol. 189r)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (Latin copyist; trans. tradition of Alfonso X)
summary:
A fifteenth-century **Latin Picatrix** leaf (Kraków, Jagiellonian Library MS 793, fol. 189r), a witness to the Western transmission of the astral-magic handbook.

## Iconography
The opening of two text columns in a Gothic book hand carries the Latin *Picatrix*, the version Europe knew. The page is plainer than the illustrated Arabic copies — the West received the work chiefly as text — but it transmits the same talismanic doctrine.

## Significance
The Latin [[motif:picatrix|Picatrix]] descends from the translation made for Alfonso X of Castile (1256). It circulated, often secretly, among Renaissance scholars and was a key (if dangerous) source for Ficino, Pico, and Agrippa. The Jagiellonian copy belongs to the same Central-European milieu that nurtured learned magic; it shows the [[motif:talisman|talismanic]] tradition entering the universities and courts of Latin Christendom.

## For artists & game designers
A sober two-column Latin manuscript is the 'scholar's forbidden copy' — contrast it with an ornate Arabic original to show a text crossing cultures.

citations.text: David Pingree, 'Some of the Sources of the Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm,' *JWCI* 43 (1980).

title: Talismanic figure ('the man') in a Picatrix manuscript (Hagia Sophia MS 2443, fol. 84r)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (Ottoman copyist of the Ghayat al-Hakim)
summary:
A talismanic human figure from a seventeenth-century *Picatrix* (Hagia Sophia MS 2443, fol. 84r) — one of the astral **images** the text instructs the magician to make.

## Iconography
A standing figure is drawn on the page as a model **astral image**: the *Picatrix* prescribes such figures — a man in a given posture, with given attributes, made of a given material under a given star — to be fashioned into a [[motif:talisman|talisman]] for a specific end. This leaf preserves one such prescribed image.

## Significance
The operative core of the [[motif:picatrix|Picatrix]] is its catalogue of images: for each planet, decan, and [[motif:lunar mansions|lunar mansion]] it gives a figure to engrave or model, the time to make it, and the effect it draws down. These talismanic figures — descended from late-antique Hermetic and Sabian star-worship — are the direct ancestors of the planetary and decan images later systematized by Agrippa.

## For artists & game designers
A 'recipe figure' showing exactly what to inscribe is an ideal crafting blueprint — pose plus material plus timing equals effect.

title: Late Arabic Picatrix manuscript (Hamidiye Library MS 852, fol. 45v)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (Arabic copyist of the Ghayat al-Hakim)
summary:
A text leaf (fol. 45v) from a late Arabic copy of the *Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm* (Picatrix) in the Hamidiye Library, MS 852.

## Iconography
The page carries the Arabic prose of the [[motif:picatrix|Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm]], the dense instructional text in which the talismanic recipes — planet, hour, [[motif:lunar mansions|mansion]], image, suffumigation — are set out. It represents the manuscript body within which the famous figures are embedded.

## Significance
The survival of the *Picatrix* in numerous Arabic copies of varying date shows the long life of astral magic in the Islamicate world, parallel to and feeding the Latin reception. The text fuses Neoplatonic cosmology with the practical fabrication of [[motif:talisman|talismans]], and remained a live magical handbook for centuries. Leaves like this are the textual substrate of the whole tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Close Arabic script in a worn codex reads instantly as 'authentic spellbook page' — strong set-dressing for a magical-text prop.

citations.text: Maria Mavroudi, in *Occult Sciences in Byzantium* (2006).

title: Seal (Character) of Jupiter
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The composite **Seal of Jupiter**, the planet's overall character, after Barrett's *The Magus*.

## Iconography
A single woven glyph gathers the lines of the Jupiter square into the *signum* of [[motif:jupiter|Jupiter]], the figure cut whole onto a jovial talisman.

## Significance
The Jupiter seal was the most sought-after of the planetary characters, engraved (with the [[motif:magic square|4×4 kamea]] of constant 34) on tin or silver in the hour of Jupiter to draw fortune, growth, and benevolent authority. Of all the planetary talismans it carried the most unambiguously beneficent reputation in the Agrippan-Picatrix tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A single 'prosperity emblem' icon — ideal as a wealth-faction crest or a blessing-spell glyph.

title: Seal (Character) of Saturn
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The composite **Seal of Saturn** — the planet's overall 'character', distinct from its separate intelligence and spirit sigils — after Barrett's *The Magus*.

## Iconography
A single complex glyph gathers the lines of the planet's square into one figure, the *signum* or character of [[motif:saturn|Saturn]] to be cut whole onto a talisman or lamen.

## Significance
Beyond the paired intelligence and spirit, each planet has a comprehensive **seal** summarizing its virtue. Engraved with the [[motif:magic square|kamea]] on the reverse and made in the planet's day and hour, such a talisman was held to channel the full astral influence of the body. Saturn's seal served operations of limitation, time, agriculture, and deep meditation in the saturnine key.

## For artists & game designers
The single all-in-one planetary character is the cleanest icon-form of the set — a drop-in emblem for a faction, school, or element keyed to Saturn.

title: Seal of the Intelligence of the Sun (Nakhiel)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The sigil of the **Intelligence of the Sun** (Nakhiel), traced from the 6×6 solar kamea.

## Iconography
A broad looping line spells Nakhiel across the implied order-6 square (constant 111). The Sun's seal is among the most prized, the planet being the heart and king of the heavens.

## Significance
The solar intelligence Nakhiel governs the favourable, life-giving, royal virtue of the [[motif:sun|Sun]]. Engraved in gold in the Sun's hour, its talisman was made for honour, health, success, and the favour of the powerful — the most generally auspicious of all planetary charms. Its spirit-counterpart is Sorath. The 6×6 [[motif:magic square|kamea]] from which it derives sums to 666 in total, a number later much remarked upon.

## For artists & game designers
The solar intelligence-seal is the 'glory / kingship' glyph — a radiant emblem for a sun-faction, healing buff, or royal sigil.

title: Seal of the Intelligence of Venus (Hagiel)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The sigil of the **Intelligence of Venus** (Hagiel), traced from the 7×7 Venus kamea.

## Iconography
A softly looping line spells Hagiel across the implied order-7 square (constant 175). The figure is the presiding intelligence of [[motif:venus|Venus]].

## Significance
Hagiel governs the concordant, loving, fruitful virtue of Venus. Engraved in copper in the planet's hour, its talisman was made to draw love, friendship, beauty, and reconciliation, paired against the planet's spirit Kedemel. Venus's seals are the classic charms of attraction in the [[motif:magic square|kamea]]-talisman tradition.
