## For artists & game designers
The Venus intelligence-seal is the 'charm / allure' glyph — an emblem for a social buff, love-quest token, or peace mechanic.

title: Seal of the Spirit of Mars (Barzabel)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The sigil of the **Spirit of Mars** (Barzabel), the violent counterpart to the planet's intelligence, after Barrett's *The Magus*.

## Iconography
A second jagged path through the Mars square spells Barzabel, the forceful and dangerous **spirit** of [[motif:mars|Mars]] — its choleric, destructive aspect.

## Significance
Barzabel embodies the unrestrained malefic force of Mars, invoked (with caution) in operations of overthrow, severance, and martial dominance. As with every planet, the [[motif:planetary seal|spirit]] is the dark twin of the intelligence, both sigils built from the single order-5 [[motif:magic square|kamea]]. The pairing let the magician address either the governed or the raw power of the planet.

## For artists & game designers
The Mars spirit-seal is the 'dark / berserk' variant of the war-glyph — a curse-sigil or rage-mode emblem.

title: Seal of the Spirit of Saturn (Zazel)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: after Francis Barrett, The Magus
summary:
The sigil of the **Spirit of Saturn** (Zazel), the darker counterpart to the planet's intelligence, after Barrett's *The Magus*.

## Iconography
A second line-figure threaded through the Saturn square spells the spirit-name Zazel. Where the intelligence-seal traces the planet's benign power, the **spirit** is its forceful, constraining, potentially malefic aspect.

## Significance
In the Agrippan scheme every planet has both an [[motif:planetary seal|intelligence and a spirit]], each sigil derived from the same [[motif:magic square|kamea]] but tracing a different name. [[motif:saturn|Saturn's]] spirit Zazel embodies the binding, deadening, fatal side of the saturnine — invoked in operations of restraint, cursing, or necessity. The intelligence/spirit pairing structures the whole planetary-talisman tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Paired 'benign vs. malefic' sigils for the same planet give a built-in good/dark alignment toggle for a single power source.

title: Post-medieval copper-alloy talismanic pendant (PAS FindID 755270)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (English maker)
rights: CC BY 2.0 (Portable Antiquities Scheme). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A post-medieval copper-alloy **talismanic pendant** recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme (FindID 755270) — a surviving physical astral talisman.

## Iconography
The small cast medallion is engraved with magical characters of the kind drawn from the planetary [[motif:magic square|kameas]] and seal-tradition. Made to be worn, it is an actual **object** of the talismanic practice the manuscript figures describe.

## Significance
Most talismans were ephemeral — written on paper, cut in soft metal, and lost — so a surviving engraved [[motif:talisman|amulet]] is precious evidence that the bookish doctrine of Agrippa and the [[motif:picatrix|Picatrix]] was actually carried out. Worn against ill fortune or for a desired influence, such pendants bridge learned magic and everyday apotropaic practice. The find-record dates and locates it without over-claiming its exact source.

## For artists & game designers
A real worn talisman — small, hand-engraved, slightly crude — is a far more convincing 'magic item' reference than a clean printed sigil.

citations.text: Don Skemer, *Binding Words: Textual Amulets in the Middle Ages* (2006).

title: Talismanic Scroll (The Metropolitan Museum of Art)
work: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: Anonymous (block-printed)
rights: Public domain (CC0). The Metropolitan Museum of Art, via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A block-printed **talismanic scroll** in the Metropolitan Museum of Art — a long protective talisman of the Islamicate tradition.

## Iconography
The narrow scroll is densely filled with calligraphic text, magic [[motif:magic square|squares]], and rows of talismanic characters and devices, printed from woodblocks for repeated production. Such scrolls were carried, worn in a case, or kept for protection.

## Significance
Talismanic scrolls are the Islamicate cousins of the planetary [[motif:talisman|talismans]] of Agrippa: both fix protective and beneficent power in inscribed signs, and both deploy magic squares among their devices. Block-printing allowed mass production of amulets long before the European printing of occult figures. The Met's scroll shows the breadth of the magic-square and character tradition beyond the learned Latin sphere.

## For artists & game designers
A long, scrollable strip of squares and characters is a striking 'cumulative ward' object — unrolled to reveal layer after layer of protection.

citations.text: Yasmine Al-Saleh, 'Amulets and Talismans from the Islamic World,' Met Heilbrunn Timeline.
Venetia Porter, *Arabic and Persian Seals and Amulets* (2011).

title: The High Priestess (Rider-Waite-Smith Tarot)
work: Tarot — A Visual History
creator: Pamela Colman Smith & A. E. Waite
summary:
**The High Priestess** (Trump II) of the **Rider-Waite-Smith** tarot (1909) — guardian of the hidden mysteries.

## Iconography
A veiled woman crowned with the lunar disc sits between two temple pillars marked **B** and **J** (*Boaz* and *Jachin*). A veil of pomegranates hangs behind her; a crescent moon rests at her feet; a scroll marked TORA lies in her lap.

## Significance
She is esoteric knowledge held in reserve — the veil not yet lifted. The pillars (severity and mercy) and pomegranate veil tie the card to the [[tradition:kabbalah|Kabbalistic]] Tree and Temple symbolism. With the Magician she forms the first polarity of the Major Arcana: manifest will and hidden wisdom.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal 'keeper of secrets' — veil, moon, twin pillars. Strong reference for a seer or a mystery-gate.

citations.text: A. E. Waite, *The Pictorial Key to the Tarot* (London, 1911).
R. Place, *The Tarot: History, Symbolism, and Divination* (2005).

title: The Magician (Rider-Waite-Smith Tarot)
work: Tarot — A Visual History
creator: Pamela Colman Smith & A. E. Waite
summary:
**The Magician** (Trump I) from the **Rider-Waite-Smith** tarot (1909) — drawn by **Pamela Colman Smith** to A. E. Waite's design, the most influential tarot deck ever made.

## Iconography
A robed magus stands at an altar bearing the four suit-emblems (wand, cup, sword, pentacle — the four elements). One hand raises a wand to heaven, the other points to earth: *as above, so below*. A **lemniscate** floats over his head; roses and lilies bloom at his feet.

## Significance
Waite and Smith, both of the **Golden Dawn**, recast the tarot as a Hermetic-[[tradition:kabbalah|Kabbalistic]] initiatory system; Smith's narrative pip cards fixed the modern visual language of [[tradition:divination|tarot]]. The Magician is the adept who channels divine power into the world.

## For artists & game designers
The template 'mage' card: figure, altar of the four elements, infinity halo.

citations.text: A. E. Waite, *The Pictorial Key to the Tarot* (London, 1911).
S. Kaplan, *The Encyclopedia of Tarot*, vol. 3 (1990).

title: A Trump of the Visconti-Sforza Tarot
work: Tarot — A Visual History
creator: workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
A trump from the **Visconti-Sforza** tarot (Milan, c. 1450) — among the earliest surviving tarot cards, hand-painted on gold ground for the ducal court.

## Iconography
The richly gilded, courtly figures of the early *trionfi* (triumphs). The deck predates any occult interpretation: these are painted allegories of triumph in the Petrarchan mode, made as a luxury game.

## Significance
The Visconti-Sforza cards are the origin point of the entire [[tradition:divination|tarot]] image-tradition. Only in the 18th century (Court de Gébelin, Etteilla) were the trumps reinterpreted as a secret Egyptian-Hermetic book; everything down to the [[work:tarot|Rider-Waite-Smith]] descends, visually, from these triumphs. They anchor the 'reception' question at the heart of this archive: an image *becomes* occult.

## For artists & game designers
Gold-ground Renaissance court allegory — reference for a luxury card set or the 'original' of a later occult deck.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (New York, 1966).

title: Queen of Swords — Visconti-Sforza Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
summary:
The Queen of Swords (*regina di spade*) from the Visconti-Sforza tarocchi, a hand-painted Milanese deck attributed to Bonifacio Bembo, c. 1450–1480.

## Iconography
A seated or standing crowned queen holds an upright sword, set against the deck's characteristic gold-tooled ground and bearing the heraldry of the Visconti and Sforza houses. The court cards of this suit personify the rank of queen within the martial suit of swords.

## Significance
The Visconti-Sforza is the most complete and luxurious of the surviving fifteenth-century Italian *tarocchi*, made for the ruling family of Milan. Unlike later mass-produced woodcut decks, these were luxury miniatures. Court cards such as this queen show how the suit signs ([[motif:sword|swords]], cups, batons, coins) and the four court ranks predate the divinatory tarot and descend from earlier playing-card traditions.

## For artists & game designers
A model for a gold-ground court-card aesthetic—heraldry, permanence, and dynastic display rather than fortune-telling.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

title: Trump V (Jupiter / Hierophant) — Renault Tarot de Besançon
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Renault (Besançon workshop)
summary:
Trump V of the Tarot de Besançon as printed by the Renault workshop, occupying the position the Marseille deck assigns to the Pope.

## Iconography
A woodcut, stencil-coloured trump in the Marseille manner, here from the Renault edition of the Besançon pattern. In this regional variant the fifth trump is Jupiter rather than *Le Pape*.

## Significance
The Renault firm of Besançon was among the long-lived French cardmakers who kept the Besançon pattern in production into the nineteenth century. The survival of complete Renault sheets lets scholars track how the [[motif:marseille pattern|Marseille template]] mutated regionally over two centuries.

## For artists & game designers
Note the flat stencil palette—red, blue, yellow, flesh—typical of cheap mass-produced French tarots.

title: Trump XIII (Death) — Renault Tarot de Besançon
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Renault (Besançon workshop)
summary:
The thirteenth trump, Death, from the Renault edition of the Tarot de Besançon.

## Iconography
A skeletal reaper swings a scythe through a field of severed heads, hands, and feet—the standard Marseille image of the “nameless arcanum.” In most Marseille decks the card carries no title.

## Significance
Death as a mowing skeleton fuses the medieval [[motif:memento mori|*memento mori*]] and the *danse macabre* with the tarot's allegorical sequence. Its placement at XIII, traditionally an unlucky number, and its frequent namelessness made it a focus of later esoteric speculation about transformation rather than literal death.

## For artists & game designers
The scattered crowned heads in the soil are the key detail—death levels king and commoner alike.

title: Jupiter — Tarot de Besançon (Trump V)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (Besançon workshop)
summary:
Jupiter, the trump that replaces the Pope (V) in the Tarot de Besançon, the Protestant-regional variant of the Tarot de Marseille.

## Iconography
The classical god Jupiter is enthroned with his eagle and thunderbolt, occupying the position the standard Marseille deck gives to *Le Pape* (the Hierophant). In the Besançon pattern the two ecclesiastical trumps—the Pope (V) and the Popess (II)—are swapped for the pagan deities Jupiter and Juno.

## Significance
The Besançon variant emerged in the largely Protestant and Imperial-leaning cardmaking centres of eastern France and the Rhineland, where the explicitly Catholic figures of Pope and Papess were unwelcome. Substituting [[motif:jupiter|Jupiter]] and Juno secularised the deck while keeping the trump count intact—a vivid case of confessional politics reshaping a divinatory iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A ready example of “reskinning” a card to dodge censorship while preserving game structure.

title: Trump XVI (The Tower / La Maison-Dieu) — Renault Tarot de Besançon
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Renault (Besançon workshop)
summary:
The sixteenth trump, the Tower (*La Maison-Dieu*), from the Renault Tarot de Besançon.

## Iconography
A crenellated tower is struck at the crown by a bolt or burst from the sun, its top blown off, with two figures tumbling head-first amid scattered coloured spheres.

## Significance
The French title *La Maison-Dieu* (“the House of God”) is debated—possibly a corruption, possibly a reference to a hospice or to the Tower of Babel. In the [[motif:marseille pattern|Marseille tradition]] it is the image of sudden catastrophe and divine overthrow; the occult revival later read it as the shattering of false structures.

## For artists & game designers
The falling bodies and exploding crown make this the most kinetic image in the trump sequence—pure disaster.

title: Trump XXI (The World / Le Monde) — Renault Tarot de Besançon
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Renault (Besançon workshop)
summary:
The twenty-first and highest trump, the World (*Le Monde*), from the Renault Tarot de Besançon.

## Iconography
A dancing nude or lightly draped figure stands within a laurel mandorla, framed in the four corners by the [[motif:tetramorph|tetramorph]]—the man/angel, eagle, lion, and ox of the four evangelists.

## Significance
The World closes the trump sequence as the image of completion and cosmic order. The surrounding tetramorph links the card to the visionary creatures of Ezekiel and Revelation and to the four elements; the central figure is variously read as the soul, the *anima mundi*, or Christ in glory.

## For artists & game designers
The mandorla-plus-four-corners layout is one of the oldest framing devices in Christian art—instantly legible as “totality.”

title: The Chariot — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
summary:
The Chariot from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) tarocchi, among the earliest surviving tarot cards, c. 1442–1447.

## Iconography
A crowned lady rides a triumphal car drawn by horses, on the deck's tooled gold ground. The triumph (*trionfo*) is the structural metaphor of the whole game—each trump a higher “triumph” in procession.

## Significance
The Cary-Yale deck, held at Yale's Beinecke Library, is unusual for its expanded structure—it includes the three theological virtues and female as well as male court ranks. As one of the three great Milanese hand-painted decks, it documents the tarot at its courtly origin, before standardisation. The [[motif:triumphal car|chariot]] derives directly from Petrarch's *Trionfi* and Renaissance triumphal pageantry.

## For artists & game designers
A primary source for the “triumph” as procession—the root metaphor behind the entire trump ladder.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

title: Charity (Caritas) — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
summary:
Charity (*Caritas*), one of the three theological-virtue trumps unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti tarocchi.

## Iconography
Charity is personified as a woman nursing or tending children, often with a flame or pelican, the standard attributes of caritas in Italian art.

## Significance
Standard tarot retains only the four cardinal virtues' subset (Justice, Strength/Fortitude, Temperance); the Cary-Yale deck additionally includes the three [[motif:theological virtue|theological virtues]] Faith, Hope, and Charity, evidence that early tarot structure was not yet fixed. Their presence suggests an originally moralising, didactic programme behind the trump sequence.

## For artists & game designers
Charity's nursing-mother type is the visual opposite of the deck's martial court cards—useful for an allegory of nurture.

title: The Emperor — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
summary:
The Emperor from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) tarocchi, c. 1442–1447.

## Iconography
A bearded, crowned emperor sits enthroned holding an orb or sceptre, attended and set against gold, often with the imperial eagle. He embodies temporal sovereignty at the head of the secular trumps.

## Significance
The Emperor and Empress pair anchors the lower trumps in the order of worldly rule, above which rise the Pope and Popess (spiritual rule) and then the cosmic and eschatological cards. The Cary-Yale Emperor's [[motif:imperial|imperial regalia]] reflect the Visconti claim to dignities granted by the Holy Roman Emperor.

## For artists & game designers
The orb-and-throne formula is the universal shorthand for legitimate worldly power.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

title: The Empress — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
summary:
The Empress from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) tarocchi, c. 1442–1447.

## Iconography
A crowned and richly robed empress is seated in state, frequently holding an imperial shield bearing an eagle, on the deck's gold-tooled ground. She is the female sovereign of the trump procession, paired with the Emperor.

## Significance
In the Milanese decks the Empress and Emperor reflect the Visconti family's imperial pretensions; the heraldic eagle ties the card to dynastic propaganda as much as to allegory. As one of the earliest surviving Empress cards, it shows the [[motif:imperial|imperial]] iconography that would persist, simplified, all the way into the woodcut Marseille tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Note how heraldry doubles as characterisation—the shield tells you whose Empress this is.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

title: Faith (Fides) — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
summary:
Faith (*Fides*), one of the three theological-virtue trumps unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti tarocchi.

## Iconography
Faith is personified as a woman holding a chalice and/or cross, the conventional attributes of *Fides* in Renaissance allegory, set on the gold ground.

## Significance
Faith, Hope, and Charity appear in the Cary-Yale deck but in no later standard tarot, making this deck a key witness to the fluid early state of the trump sequence. Their inclusion alongside the cardinal virtues points to a structured ladder of [[motif:theological virtue|virtues]] as part of the game's original moral architecture.

## For artists & game designers
Faith's chalice-and-cross attributes make her readable at a glance even without a caption.

title: Hope (Spes) — Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (attributed)
summary:
Hope (*Spes*), one of the three theological-virtue trumps unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti tarocchi.

## Iconography
Hope is shown as a woman, hands raised in prayer or reaching upward, sometimes with an anchor, looking toward heaven—the standard personification of *Spes*.

## Significance
Together with Faith and Charity, Hope rounds out the theological virtues that the Cary-Yale deck adds to the trump set. Their appearance only here—and never in the Tarot de Marseille line—demonstrates that the canonical 22-trump structure was a later consolidation of more varied early programmes. The [[motif:theological virtue|virtues]] anchor the game in late-medieval moral allegory.

## For artists & game designers
Hope's upward gaze and anchor are a compact emblem of aspiration—useful far beyond tarot.

title: Death — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
summary:
Death from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
Death appears as a skeleton—here often mounted or striding with a bow or scythe across fallen bodies—an early Italian version of the trump that later Marseille decks render as the nameless reaper.

## Significance
The Death trump links the tarot to the pan-European [[motif:memento mori|*memento mori*]] and *Triumph of Death* imagery popular in fifteenth-century Italy (compare the Palermo and Pisa frescoes). The Ferrarese rendering is among the earliest surviving tarot Deaths.

## For artists & game designers
Mounted, archer Death is a striking alternative to the standing scythe-bearer—more huntsman than mower.

title: The Emperor — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
summary:
The Emperor (*Empereur*) from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
A crowned emperor is enthroned holding a sceptre, the imperial eagle nearby, courtiers below. He heads the trumps of worldly authority.

## Significance
The seventeen “Charles VI” cards survive at the BnF and exemplify the high quality of [[motif:ferrara|Ferrarese]] card painting in the Este period. Comparison of this Emperor with the Milanese Visconti versions lets scholars map regional variation in the earliest trump iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A second-region take on the Emperor—useful for showing how the same allegory drifts between workshops.

title: The Hermit (Time) — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
summary:
The Hermit—here in its older guise as Time—from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
A bearded old man leans on crutches or a staff and holds an hourglass, the attribute of [[motif:father time|Time]] (*il Vecchio*, “the Old Man”). Only later did the lantern replace the hourglass and the figure become the lamp-bearing Hermit.

## Significance
This card preserves the trump's earliest meaning: not solitary wisdom but Time the devourer, one of Petrarch's *Trionfi*. The shift from hourglass to lantern over the following centuries is a textbook case of iconographic drift reshaping a card's meaning.

## For artists & game designers
Give your “hermit” an hourglass instead of a lamp and you instantly evoke the older, darker Time.

citations.text: Gertrude Moakley, *The Tarot Cards Painted by Bonifacio Bembo* (1966).

title: The Pope — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
summary:
The Pope from the so-called “Charles VI” tarot, a late-fifteenth-century Ferrarese deck (Bibliothèque nationale de France).

## Iconography
The Pope sits enthroned in the papal tiara, raising a hand in blessing, attended by kneeling figures. He is the spiritual counterpart to the Emperor in the trump hierarchy.

## Significance
Long misattributed to a deck painted for King Charles VI of France in 1392, these seventeen surviving cards are now dated c. 1470–1480 and assigned to a [[motif:ferrara|Ferrarese]] workshop, possibly connected to the Este court. They form one of the most important non-Milanese witnesses to early Italian tarot iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A reminder that confident attributions can be wrong by a century—always check the redating.

title: The Sun — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
summary:
The Sun from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
A radiant sun-disc with a face shines above a figure below—in this early deck often a single woman holding a distaff, rather than the twin children of later Marseille versions.

## Significance
The celestial trumps (Star, Moon, Sun) form a luminous sequence near the top of the ladder. The Ferrarese Sun shows how variable these images were before standardisation: the [[motif:sol|solar disc]] is constant, but the figures beneath it differ markedly from deck to deck.

## For artists & game designers
The distaff-spinner under the sun is a quieter, more domestic image than the leaping children of the Marseille Sun.

title: The World — “Charles VI” (Estensi) Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (Ferrarese workshop)
summary:
The World, the highest trump, from the “Charles VI” Ferrarese tarot, c. 1470–1480.

## Iconography
Two putti or figures hold up a great orb on which is painted a walled city in a landscape under heaven—the world entire, displayed as a globe. This differs sharply from the later mandorla-and-tetramorph Marseille World.

## Significance
The “Charles VI” World shows the medieval cosmographic image of the inhabited earth—a [[motif:globe|globe]] bearing a fortified city—as the culmination of the triumph sequence. Its survival is crucial evidence that the World card's iconography was once quite different across Italian regions.

## For artists & game designers
A city-on-a-globe held aloft by genii is a gorgeous, under-used emblem of “the whole world.”

title: L'Empereur (The Emperor) — Jean Dodal Tarot de Marseille (IIII)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Jean Dodal
summary:
L'Empereur (trump IIII) from the Jean Dodal Tarot de Marseille, printed in Lyon c. 1701–1715.

## Iconography
The Emperor sits in profile, legs crossed, holding a sceptre topped with an orb, an eagle-blazoned shield at his side—the canonical Marseille pose of worldly authority.

## Significance
The Dodal deck is one of the earliest and most influential exemplars of the “Tarot de Marseille type I” (TdM I) pattern. Its slightly archaic woodcut style and Italianate name-spellings make it a key witness to the standard French tarot before the Conver workshop fixed the “type II” standard. This entry extends the project's existing Dodal majors (Bateleur, Papesse, Chariot). The crossed-leg [[motif:marseille pattern|Emperor pose]] became iconic.

## For artists & game designers
The profile, crossed-legs Emperor is the signature Marseille posture—instantly “tarot.”

title: Le Monde (The World) — Pierre Madenié Tarot de Marseille (XXI), 1709
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Pierre Madenié
summary:
Le Monde (trump XXI), the highest trump, from the dated 1709 Tarot de Marseille of Pierre Madenié of Dijon.

## Iconography
A dancing figure within a laurel mandorla is framed by the four creatures of the [[motif:tetramorph|tetramorph]] (angel, eagle, lion, ox), in the classic Marseille configuration.

## Significance
The Madenié deck of 1709 is one of the most important securely-dated early Marseille tarots and a benchmark for the “type II” (TdM II) pattern later codified by Nicolas Conver. Its precise date makes it an anchor point in the chronology of French tarot production. (This Commons image is a CC BY-SA restoration of the surviving deck.)

## For artists & game designers
A securely dated reference if you need a “1709 Marseille” look pinned to a real year.

title: Misero (The Beggar) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Conditions of Man 1)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Misero*, the Beggar (no. 1, lowest of the Conditions of Man) in the Mantegna Tarocchi E-series.

## Iconography
A ragged beggar walks with a staff, beset by two dogs—the bottom rung of the social ladder, captioned MISERO. The decad rises from him through Servant, Artisan, Merchant, Gentleman, Knight, Doge, King, Emperor, to Pope.

## Significance
The Beggar opens the Mantegna sequence's [[motif:conditions of man|Conditions of Man]], a hierarchy of estates that mirrors the wider cosmic ladder of the deck. The same figure (as *Misero*) became the model for the Fool/Beggar in several later tarot traditions.

## For artists & game designers
The dogs nipping at the beggar are the detail that makes “lowest of the low” read instantly.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

title: Charita (Charity) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Genii & Virtues 38)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
summary:
*Charita*, Charity (plate 38), among the Genii and Virtues of the Mantegna Tarocchi.

## Iconography
Charity is personified holding a flame or tending children, the standard attributes of *caritas*, the highest of the theological virtues.

## Significance
Charity appears in the same decad as the cardinal virtues and the cosmic genii, integrating the [[motif:theological virtue|theological virtues]] into the deck's cosmological scheme. The Mantegna virtue figures were widely copied and helped standardise virtue iconography across Renaissance Europe.

## For artists & game designers
Flame-bearing Charity makes a warm counterweight to the colder cardinal virtues in a virtue set.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

title: Genius of the World (Genius Mundi) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Cosmic Principles 33)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Genius of the World (*Genius Mundi*, no. 33) from the Mantegna Tarocchi's decad of Cosmic Principles and Virtues.

## Iconography
A winged genius personifies the animating spirit of the cosmos, set among the abstract principles—Cosmico (cosmos), Tempo (time), and the virtues—that bridge the human ranks and the celestial spheres.

## Significance
The “Genius of the World” gives visual form to the Neoplatonic *anima mundi*, the world-soul that pervades and orders creation. Its inclusion shows how the Mantegna deck functions as a Renaissance cosmological diagram, ascending from [[motif:conditions of man|man]] through abstract principles to the planets and the *primum mobile*.

## For artists & game designers
A personified “world-spirit” is a perfect hub-figure for a cosmology-themed deck.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

title: Geometria (Geometry) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Liberal Arts 24)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (CC0, Metropolitan Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Geometria* (XXIIII), Geometry, from the Liberal Arts decad of the Mantegna Tarocchi.

## Iconography
Geometry is personified as a woman holding a compass and measuring instruments, often bending to measure the ground—the attribute of the geometer—captioned GEOMETRIA.

## Significance
Geometry belongs to the quadrivium, the mathematical half of the [[motif:liberal arts|seven liberal arts]]. The compass-wielding figure ties the card to the Renaissance ideal of the universe as measurable and the artist-engineer as measurer—an image that resonates from Mantegna through to later Masonic and esoteric uses of the compass.

## For artists & game designers
The compass-and-ground-measuring pose is the visual root of countless later “architect/geometer” emblems.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

title: Imperator (The Emperor) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (CC0, National Gallery of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Imperator*, the Emperor, from the so-called Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series), engraved in Ferrara c. 1465.

## Iconography
A crowned emperor sits enthroned with orb and sceptre, attendants below, captioned IMPERATOR. He belongs to the deck's first decad, the “Conditions/Ranks of Man,” which ascends from Beggar to Pope to Emperor.

## Significance
Despite the name, the Mantegna Tarocchi is not a tarot and not by Mantegna: it is a fifty-card engraved encyclopedia of the cosmos in five decads—ranks of man, [[motif:muses|Apollo and the Muses]], the liberal arts, the cosmic principles/virtues, and the planets and spheres. It profoundly shaped Renaissance print culture and was copied across Europe.

## For artists & game designers
A model for an “encyclopedic” card set—every card a rung on a ladder from earth to the *primum mobile*.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters* (Mantegna Tarocchi).

title: Re (The King) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Conditions of Man 8)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Re*, the King (no. 8 in the Conditions of Man), from the Mantegna Tarocchi E-series.

## Iconography
A crowned king is enthroned with sceptre and orb, captioned RE, just below the Emperor and Pope in the rank ladder.

## Significance
The King sits near the top of the [[motif:conditions of man|estates ladder]] that forms the first decad of the Mantegna series. The careful gradation of King, Emperor, and Pope reflects the late-medieval theory of the two swords—temporal and spiritual power—made visual.

## For artists & game designers
King-then-Emperor-then-Pope is a clean three-step escalation of authority for any ranked card set.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

title: Rhetorica (Rhetoric) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Liberal Arts 23)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Rhetorica*, Rhetoric, one of the seven Liberal Arts in the third decad of the Mantegna Tarocchi.

## Iconography
Rhetoric is personified as a woman with attributes of eloquence—often a sword or scroll and a commanding gesture—captioned RHETORICA, among the arts of the trivium and quadrivium.

## Significance
The [[motif:liberal arts|Liberal Arts]] decad (Grammar, Logic, Rhetoric, Geometry, Arithmetic, Music, Poetry, Philosophy, Astrology, Theology) makes the medieval curriculum into a visual encyclopedia. The Mantegna images became standard models for personifying the arts in later Renaissance prints and decorative cycles.

## For artists & game designers
A full personification set of the liberal arts—ideal reference for an “academy” or scholarly faction.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

title: Sol (The Sun) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Firmaments of the Universe 44)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Sol*, the Sun (no. 44), from the Mantegna Tarocchi's fifth decad, the planets and firmaments of the universe.

## Iconography
The Sun is personified as the radiant solar deity—Apollo/Sol—driving a chariot across the heavens, captioned SOL. The decad runs through Moon, Mercury, Venus, Sol, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn to the Eighth Sphere, *Primum Mobile*, and *Prima Causa*.

## Significance
The planetary decad presents the [[motif:planets|seven planets]] in the Ptolemaic order as the upper rungs of the cosmic ladder. Personifying Sol as a charioteer follows classical and medieval planet-deity iconography that pervaded astrological art.

## For artists & game designers
The charioteer-Sun is the canonical way to draw the planet rather than the disc.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

title: Temperantia (Temperance) — Mantegna Tarocchi (E-series, Cosmic Principles & Virtues 34)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi
rights: Public domain (CC0, Cleveland Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Temperantia*, Temperance (no. 34), one of the virtues in the Mantegna Tarocchi's fourth decad of Cosmic Principles and Virtues.

## Iconography
A woman pours liquid between two vessels—the standard attribute of [[motif:temperance|Temperance]], the act of mixing and moderating—captioned TEMPERANTIA.

## Significance
The Mantegna series groups the cardinal and other virtues with cosmic principles such as Genius and the Muses, presenting morality as woven into the structure of the universe. This pouring-vessels image is the direct ancestor of the Temperance card in the tarot proper.

## For artists & game designers
Two vessels and a careful stream of water = “moderation” with no text required.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch: Early Italian Masters*.

title: Postumio — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump II)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
*Postumio*, trump II of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), one of the named-hero trumps that replace the standard Popess.

## Iconography
A classical/Roman figure named Postumio stands in martial dress in the deck's engraved style. The Sola Busca trumps form a gallery of antique worthies rather than the Marseille allegories.

## Significance
The substitution of Roman and biblical heroes (Postumio, Catone, Nerone, Nabuchodenasor, and others) for the conventional trumps marks the Sola Busca as a humanist and antiquarian project, plausibly carrying an [[motif:alchemy|alchemical]] subtext, made for a learned patron rather than for ordinary play.

## For artists & game designers
Replacing each trump with a historical figure is a clean way to theme a deck around a single culture.

title: Sesto — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump VI)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
*Sesto*, trump VI of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), part of its gallery of named antique heroes.

## Iconography
A classical warrior-figure named Sesto stands in the deck's distinctive engraved manner, occupying the position the standard deck assigns to the Lovers.

## Significance
The Sola Busca's reassignment of subjects—here a Roman *Sesto* in place of the Lovers—demonstrates how freely the trump programme could be reimagined in fifteenth-century Italy. The cycle's coherence and its dense [[motif:alchemy|symbolism]] have led some scholars to read it as an alchemical-initiatic sequence.

## For artists & game designers
The engraved hatching and antique armour give a cool, antiquarian alternative to the bright woodcut tarots.

title: Bocho — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump XIV)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
*Bocho*, trump XIV of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), one of its named-hero trumps.

## Iconography
A named figure, *Bocho* (often identified with the Numidian king Bocchus), stands in the engraved Sola Busca style where the standard deck shows Temperance.

## Significance
The Sola Busca trumps draw on Roman history and the Jugurthine War among other sources, turning the trump ladder into a parade of antiquity. The depth of its learned programme and possible [[motif:alchemy|alchemical]] coding make it the single most studied early deck after the Visconti-Sforza.

## For artists & game designers
Obscure historical names invite worldbuilding—each card is a hook for a backstory.

title: Ipeo — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump XVII)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
Trump XVII (named *Ipeo*) of the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), occupying the position the standard deck gives to the Star.

## Iconography
A named classical figure stands or rides in the Sola Busca's fine engraved manner, again replacing the conventional celestial allegory with an antique personage.

## Significance
Because the Sola Busca substitutes named heroes for the upper trumps as well as the lower, its sequence severs the usual link between trump number and standard image—a vivid demonstration that the “canonical” 22 trumps were not yet fixed. Its [[motif:alchemy|engraved symbolism]] later fascinated the founders of the Golden Dawn.

## For artists & game designers
Decoupling card position from expected image keeps players guessing—useful for a deck with hidden lore.

title: Seven of Discs (Coins) — Sola Busca Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photograph of PD engraving). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
The Seven of Discs (Coins) from the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), a fully illustrated pip card.

## Iconography
Rather than seven coins arranged decoratively, the card stages a small scene incorporating the seven discs—the hallmark of the Sola Busca's [[motif:scenic pip|scenic pips]].

## Significance
The Sola Busca is the only fifteenth-century deck with scenic illustrations on every numbered card. This Seven of Discs is among the pips whose composition has been compared with the Rider-Waite-Smith minor arcana, the deck that made scenic pips standard in modern tarot. (The Commons image is a CC BY-SA photograph of the public-domain engraving.)

## For artists & game designers
A direct fifteenth-century precedent for telling a tiny story on a “number” card.

title: Knight of Swords — Sola Busca Tarot
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
The Knight of Swords from the Sola Busca Tarot (c. 1491), British Museum impression.

## Iconography
A mounted knight brandishes a sword, rendered in fine line-engraving. The Sola Busca court cards, like its trumps, are individualised antique-style figures.

## Significance
The Sola Busca is the earliest deck whose [[motif:suit of swords|suit and court cards]] are fully and individually illustrated, not merely arranged repetitions of the suit sign. This scenic treatment of the minor arcana was unique until the British Museum's photographs inspired Pamela Colman Smith's fully illustrated pips four centuries later.

## For artists & game designers
The Sola Busca proves that even “number” and court cards can each carry a unique scene.

citations.text: Sofia Di Vincenzo, *Sola-Busca Tarot* (1998); British Museum.

title: Panfilio — Sola Busca Tarot (Trump I)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Anonymous (North Italian engraver)
summary:
*Panfilio*, trump I of the Sola Busca Tarot, the earliest surviving complete 78-card deck, engraved in northern Italy c. 1491.

## Iconography
A named figure (Panfilio) stands in the deck's fine line-engraved, hand-coloured style. Unlike the Marseille trumps, the Sola Busca trumps depict named classical, Roman, and biblical heroes rather than the standard allegories.

## Significance
The Sola Busca is the only fifteenth-century deck to survive complete, and the only one whose pip cards bear full scenic illustrations. Its [[motif:alchemy|alchemical]] and antiquarian programme sets it utterly apart; A. E. Waite and Pamela Colman Smith studied British Museum photographs of it when designing the scenic minor arcana of the 1909 Rider-Waite-Smith deck.

## For artists & game designers
Named-hero trumps turn an abstract allegory deck into a roster of characters—great for a story-driven set.

citations.text: Sofia Di Vincenzo, *Sola-Busca Tarot* (1998); British Museum impressions.

title: The Star — Jacques Vieville Tarot (XVII)
work: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: Jacques Vieville
summary:
The Star (XVII) from the Jacques Vieville tarot, Paris, c. 1650.

## Iconography
The Vieville Star departs from the Marseille norm: instead of a kneeling woman pouring water under stars, it shows a standing figure (an astronomer or shepherd) gesturing toward a great star with a compass or staff.

## Significance
Vieville's deck, produced in Paris around the same time as the Noblet tarot, represents an alternative northern-French line that did not become the dominant Marseille standard. Its idiosyncratic trumps—like this [[motif:the star|Star]]—are invaluable for showing the diversity of seventeenth-century French tarot before standardisation around the Marseille pattern.

## For artists & game designers
An astronomer-with-compass Star is a fresh alternative to the ubiquitous water-pouring maiden.

title: Le Chariot (The Chariot) — Dodal Tarot de Marseille (VII)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Jean Dodal
summary:
*Le Chariot*, trump VII of the Tarot de Marseille in Jean Dodal's Lyon woodcut pattern, c. 1701–1715.

## Iconography
A crowned prince stands upright in a canopied [[motif:triumphal car|triumphal car]] drawn by two horses, holding a sceptre, his shoulders often marked with crescent emblems. The car evokes the Roman *triumphus* and the *trionfi* (triumphs) from which the trumps themselves take their name.

## Significance
The Chariot images worldly victory and command — the conqueror riding in triumph, an idea rooted in Petrarch's *Trionfi* and the Roman triumphal procession. In the Marseille sequence it crowns the first septenary of trumps. Later esoteric readers (Lévi onward) made it the card of the will mastering opposed forces, reading the two horses as the duality the charioteer must control. Dodal's early printing fixes the standard French iconography.

## For artists & game designers
The canopied triumphal car with two horses is the archetypal 'victory / driven will' card — momentum and command in one image.

title: La Papesse (The High Priestess) — Dodal Tarot de Marseille (II)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Jean Dodal
summary:
*La Papesse*, trump II of the Tarot de Marseille in Jean Dodal's Lyon woodcut pattern, c. 1701–1715.

## Iconography
A seated woman wears the triple papal [[motif:tiara|tiara]] and holds an open book on her lap, a veil behind her. She is the female pope, counterpart to *Le Pape*, a figure with no obvious place in Catholic orthodoxy.

## Significance
The Papess is one of tarot's enduring puzzles: variously linked to the legend of Pope Joan, to the Visconti family's ancestor the Umiliata 'popess' Manfreda, or simply to an allegory of the Church or Faith. In Dodal's woodcut she carries forward the Visconti *Papessa* into the printed tradition. The eighteenth-century occultists renamed her the High Priestess and made her the keeper of the hidden book of wisdom — the reading still standard today.

## For artists & game designers
The seated, book-bearing 'female pope' is the archetype of the hidden-knowledge keeper — a librarian-of-secrets card.

citations.text: G. Berti, on the Manfreda hypothesis; M. Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980).

title: Mantegna Tarocchi — Engraved Figure (E-series)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Master of the E-Series Tarocchi (so-called 'Mantegna')
rights: CC0 / Public domain. Cleveland Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
An engraving from the so-called *Mantegna Tarocchi*, the encyclopaedic fifty-card didactic series of c. 1465 (Cleveland Museum of Art impression).

## Iconography
A single allegorical figure stands frontally with its identifying attributes, labelled and numbered in the manner of the whole series. Each plate of the set personifies one rung of a cosmic ladder — a [[motif:personification|personification]] of an estate of man, a Muse, a Liberal Art, a cosmic principle, or a planetary sphere — rendered in the crisp early-Italian engraving line of the Ferrarese workshop.

## Significance
The *Mantegna Tarocchi* is not a true playing tarot but a humanist teaching device: fifty engravings in five decades that map the whole hierarchy of being from the beggar up to the Prime Cause. Misattributed to Mantegna, it issued from the Ferrarese cultural circle and was admired and copied across Europe — Dürer drew after it. It is a key witness to the image-systems that stood behind the emergence of tarot's allegorical sequence.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled, single-figure 'concept card' from the dawn of the form — period-accurate reference for an allegorical deck of ideas.

citations.text: J. A. Levenson et al., *Early Italian Engravings from the National Gallery of Art* (1973).

title: Le Bateleur (The Magician) — Dodal Tarot de Marseille (I)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Jean Dodal
summary:
*Le Bateleur*, trump I of the Tarot de Marseille in the woodcut pattern of Jean Dodal, the Lyon cardmaker, c. 1701–1715.

## Iconography
A standing [[motif:juggler|mountebank]] works at a small table spread with cups, balls, knives and coins, a wand in one hand and a token in the other, his broad hat brim looped like a lemniscate. He is the conjuror or market-square trickster, lowest-numbered of the trumps.

## Significance
Dodal's deck is one of the earliest dated examples of the standardised Tarot de Marseille pattern and a foundational document of the tradition that the eighteenth-century occultists would inherit. *Le Bateleur* — the sleight-of-hand showman — was reinterpreted by Court de Gébelin and Lévi as the Magus, master of the elements (the suit-tokens on his table read as the four 'magical weapons'). The card thus marks the hinge between the fairground juggler and the occult magician.

## For artists & game designers
The juggler-at-his-table is the original 'trickster / showman' card — props laid out as the four suits, ready to become a mage.

title: Le Chariot (The Chariot) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (VII)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Chariot*, trump VII of the Tarot de Marseille in Nicolas Conver's 1760 pattern.

## Iconography
A crowned prince stands erect beneath a starred canopy in a [[motif:triumphal car|triumphal car]] drawn by two horses that face outward in opposite directions; crescent epaulettes adorn his shoulders and a sceptre is in his hand. The fuller Conver cut sharpens the regal, frontal symmetry.

## Significance
The Chariot embodies triumph, will, and worldly mastery, descending from the Roman *triumphus* and Petrarch's *Trionfi* that gave the trumps their name. Esoteric tarotists read the two divergent horses as the opposing forces the charioteer's will must yoke. Conver's 1760 deck made this the canonical image of the card for all subsequent occult and divinatory tarot.

## For artists & game designers
Two horses pulling in different directions under one driver — a tidy visual metaphor for will mastering conflict.

title: Le Diable (The Devil) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (XV)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Diable* (The Devil), trump XV of the Tarot de Marseille in Nicolas Conver's 1760 pattern.

## Iconography
A horned, bat-[[motif:bat wings|winged]] devil stands on a pedestal, brandishing a torch, with two small horned imps chained by the neck at its base. The figure is androgynous, taloned, and crowned with antlers — a composite of medieval demonic imagery.

## Significance
The Devil enters the trump sequence just before the Tower, the pair forming the nadir of the moral ascent. The chained imps signify bondage to vice and the flesh; esoteric readers (Lévi pre-eminently, who modelled his famous *Baphomet* partly on such imagery) made the card the emblem of material entrapment and the dark forces of nature. Conver's woodcut crystallised the iconic Marseille Devil that nineteenth-century occultism elaborated.

## For artists & game designers
The winged devil with two chained imps is the canonical 'bondage / temptation' card — captives bound to a tempter.

citations.text: É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1856).

title: La Force (Strength) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (XI)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*La Force* (Strength / Fortitude), trump XI of the Tarot de Marseille in Nicolas Conver's 1760 pattern.

## Iconography
A woman wearing a wide lemniscate-brimmed hat calmly closes — or opens — the [[motif:open jaws|jaws]] of a lion with her bare hands, mastering the beast without effort. She personifies the cardinal virtue Fortitude.

## Significance
Fortitude/Strength is the third surviving cardinal virtue in the Marseille trumps. Its imagery — a serene woman subduing a lion — descends from medieval personifications of Fortitude (sometimes shown breaking a column) and signifies moral strength, the gentle mastery of brute passion. In the historic Marseille order it is numbered XI; the Golden Dawn later renumbered it VIII, swapping it with Justice for astrological reasons. Conver's woodcut is the standard form.

## For artists & game designers
A woman quietly opening a lion's jaws is the perfect 'gentle mastery over force' image — strength as composure, not violence.

title: Le Jugement (Judgement) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (XX)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Jugement* (Judgement), trump XX of the Tarot de Marseille in Nicolas Conver's 1760 pattern.

## Iconography
An [[motif:angel|angel]] leans from a cloud sounding a trumpet (often hung with a cross-banner) while below, three naked figures rise from a tomb or the earth with hands raised — the dead summoned to resurrection at the Last Judgement.

## Significance
Judgement renders the Christian *Dies Irae*: the angel's trumpet calling the dead to rise, a near-universal motif of medieval and Renaissance art here distilled to a single trump. Penultimate in the sequence, before the World, it signifies awakening, rebirth, and the final reckoning. The plainly biblical imagery shows how deeply the trumps are rooted in ordinary Christian iconography, whatever esoteric meanings (resurrection, the call to a higher life) were later layered on. Conver's woodcut is the canonical Marseille form.

## For artists & game designers
The trumpeting angel raising the dead is the archetypal 'awakening / resurrection / final call' card.

title: La Justice (Justice) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (VIII)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*La Justice*, trump VIII of the Tarot de Marseille in Nicolas Conver's 1760 pattern.

## Iconography
A crowned woman sits frontally enthroned holding upright [[motif:scales|scales]] in one hand and a vertical sword in the other — the immemorial attributes of Justice, here unblindfolded and facing the viewer directly.

## Significance
Justice is one of the three cardinal virtues that survive among the Marseille trumps (with Temperance and Fortitude/Force), embedding classical-Christian moral philosophy in the sequence. Its numbering as VIII (with Strength at XI) is the historic Marseille order, which the Golden Dawn would later swap. Conver's clear woodcut fixed the standard image; the sword-and-scales pairing remains the universal emblem of judgement and equity.

## For artists & game designers
The frontal sword-and-scales figure is the clearest possible 'judgement / law' icon — no caption required.

title: Le Monde (The World) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (XXI)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Monde* (The World), trump XXI and highest of the Tarot de Marseille, in Nicolas Conver's 1760 pattern.

## Iconography
A dancing nude or lightly draped figure stands within an oval laurel [[motif:mandorla|wreath]], a wand in each hand, framed at the four corners by the angel, eagle, bull, and lion — the [[motif:four living creatures|four living creatures]] of Ezekiel and the Apocalypse (the four evangelists / fixed signs of the zodiac).

## Significance
The World crowns the trump sequence as the image of cosmic completion. Its four-creature frame derives from the Tetramorph of Christian art (Ezekiel 1, Revelation 4), read astrologically as the fixed signs Aquarius, Scorpio, Taurus, Leo. The central dancer signifies the perfected soul or the *anima mundi* at the centre of creation. This Marseille design, unlike the earlier Visconti World, became the template the occultists elaborated, and remains the most widespread World image.

## For artists & game designers
A dancer in a wreath ringed by the four Tetramorph beasts is the ultimate 'completion / cosmos / victory lap' capstone card.

title: Le Pape (The Hierophant) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (V)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Pape*, trump V of the Tarot de Marseille in Nicolas Conver's 1760 Marseille pattern.

## Iconography
The Pope sits enthroned in the triple [[motif:papal tiara|tiara]], raising one hand in benediction and holding a tall cross-staff, with two tonsured acolytes seen from behind kneeling before him. The scene depicts the conferring of blessing or teaching.

## Significance
The Pope, counterpart to the Papess, embodies spiritual authority and orthodox doctrine in the trump hierarchy. The two acolytes make explicit his role as teacher and mediator of sacred knowledge — which is why the occultists renamed him the *Hierophant*, the initiating priest of the mysteries. Conver's 1760 woodcut is the standard form transmitted to the esoteric tradition.

## For artists & game designers
The enthroned pontiff blessing two kneeling acolytes is the archetypal 'teacher / initiator' card — authority transmitting doctrine.

title: La Papesse (The High Priestess) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (II)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*La Papesse*, trump II of the Tarot de Marseille in the pattern of Nicolas Conver's celebrated 1760 Marseille deck.

## Iconography
The Papess sits enthroned in the triple [[motif:tiara|tiara]], an open book in her lap and a veil hung behind her, in the cleaner, fuller line of the mid-eighteenth-century Conver woodblocks. The composition is essentially that of Dodal, refined.

## Significance
Conver's 1760 deck is the single most influential edition of the Marseille pattern, the version most reproduced and the base text for nineteenth- and twentieth-century esoteric tarot. *La Papesse* — the enigmatic female pope — passes through it to become the occultists' High Priestess, guardian of the secret book between the pillars. Comparing the Conver and Dodal Papesse shows the standardisation and slight elaboration of the woodcut pattern over half a century.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive Marseille 'priestess of the hidden book' — the canonical reference image for the archetype.

title: Le Soleil (The Sun) — Conver Tarot de Marseille (XVIIII)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Nicolas Conver (after); Tarot World Project (photo)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Tarot World Project, after the 1760 Conver deck). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
*Le Soleil* (The Sun), trump XVIIII of the Tarot de Marseille in Nicolas Conver's 1760 pattern.

## Iconography
A great human-faced [[motif:radiant face|sun]] sheds straight and wavy rays and falling droplets over two children who stand before a low brick wall, one touching the other. The pair beneath the radiant sun read as concord, innocence, and shared warmth.

## Significance
The Sun is the brightest of the celestial triad (Star, Moon, Sun) that closes the trump sequence before Judgement and the World, and traditionally the most auspicious card of the deck — signifying clarity, joy, vitality, and revealed truth. The two children were variously read as innocence regained, human fellowship, or (by the alchemically minded) the conjoined principles. Conver's 1760 image is the standard Marseille Sun.

## For artists & game designers
Two children under a beaming face-sun is the deck's purest 'joy / clarity / good outcome' card.

citations.text: É. Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (1856).

title: Death — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (XIII)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
Death, trump XIII of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, a Milanese hand-painted card of c. 1450.

## Iconography
A gaunt skeletal figure of [[motif:death|Death]] strides forward, in the Visconti tradition often shown drawing a great bow or carrying a scythe, trampling the bodies of the high and low alike. The image draws directly on the *memento mori* and *Danse Macabre* art that pervaded fifteenth-century Europe.

## Significance
Death the leveller — taking pope and king as readily as peasant — is the trump's plain medieval message, central to the moral allegory of the *trionfi*. Petrarch's *Trionfi*, in which Death triumphs over Chastity only to be overcome by Fame, lies behind the very name and structure of the trump sequence. The card needed no occult gloss: it spoke the universal language of mortality before later readers recast it as 'transformation.'

## For artists & game designers
A striding, bow-bearing skeleton trampling the great — a stark *memento mori* leveller for a mortality or plague theme.

title: The Emperor — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (IV)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
The Emperor, trump IV of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, a hand-painted Milanese card of c. 1450.

## Iconography
A bearded emperor sits enthroned in profile, crowned and holding the orb of dominion, the imperial [[motif:imperial eagle|eagle]] displayed on his regalia, all against the tooled gold ground. The dignified profile pose echoes the portrait medals of Renaissance princes.

## Significance
With the Empress, the Emperor embodies temporal authority within the trump hierarchy. In the Visconti-Sforza commission the figure flatters the deck's ducal owners, asserting their place in the imperial order. The card's restrained, portrait-like majesty contrasts with the schematic woodcut Emperor of the later Marseille pattern and shows the original courtly register of the trumps.

## For artists & game designers
A profile, medal-like 'sovereign' portrait — a dignified ruler card with built-in heraldic gravitas.

title: The Empress — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (III)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
The Empress, trump III of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, painted for the Milanese court c. 1450.

## Iconography
A crowned empress sits enthroned holding a shield charged with the imperial [[motif:imperial eagle|eagle]], her robes and the gold ground proclaiming sovereignty. The dynastic heraldry ties the figure directly to the Visconti-Sforza patrons, who saw their own rank mirrored in the trump.

## Significance
The Empress and Emperor head the 'estates' of the trump sequence, the worldly powers beneath the spiritual Pope and Papess. In a deck commissioned by ruling dukes, these cards were not abstract allegories but flattering reflections of the court itself, complete with family arms. The trump preserves the original courtly self-image from which tarot's later, universalised meanings (the Empress as fertile mother-principle) were abstracted.

## For artists & game designers
An enthroned, heraldic 'sovereign lady' — a ready model for a queen/ruler card carrying real dynastic arms.

title: The Traitor (The Hanged Man) — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (XII)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo / Antonio Cicognara (attrib.)
summary:
The Traitor — the card later called the Hanged Man — trump XII of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, c. 1450.

## Iconography
A young man hangs upside down by one ankle from a wooden frame, his other leg crossed behind and his hands often bound or hidden, perfectly composed and calm. The pose is that of the *pittura infamante*, the [[motif:shame penalty|shame-painting]] by which Italian cities publicly depicted traitors hung by the foot.

## Significance
In its original context the trump was understood as 'the Traitor' (*il Traditore*) or 'the Penitent,' invoking a real judicial-pictorial custom of dishonour rather than the mystical 'suspended wisdom' the occultists later read into it. This grounds one of tarot's most reinterpreted cards in concrete fifteenth-century Italian practice. The serene face, however, already invites the inverted, contemplative readings that Éliphas Lévi and his successors would develop.

## For artists & game designers
The inverted-hanging-by-the-foot figure as a *shame penalty* — historically a punishment for traitors, not a yogi: a sharp framing for a 'disgrace' beat.

title: Love (The Lovers) — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (VI)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo / Antonio Cicognara (attrib.)
summary:
Love (the Lovers), trump VI of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, a Milanese card of c. 1450.

## Iconography
A man and woman stand or join hands beneath a canopy, with a blindfolded [[motif:cupid|Cupid]] above drawing his bow. Some readers identify the couple as a betrothal portrait of the Visconti-Sforza dynasty itself. The scene is courtly and matrimonial rather than the moral 'choice between two women' the Marseille card would later depict.

## Significance
The Love trump in the early deck celebrates dynastic union and the power of Amor, in keeping with the wedding-gift character often proposed for such luxury packs. The shift from this single embracing couple to the Marseille *Amoureux* — a youth choosing between two women under Cupid's arrow — is one of the clearest illustrations of how the trump's meaning migrated from celebration of marriage to allegory of moral choice.

## For artists & game designers
A betrothal-portrait 'union' card with blindfold Cupid — a romance beat distinct from the later 'choice' iconography.

title: The Moon — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (XVIII)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
The Moon, trump XVIII of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, a Milanese hand-painted card of c. 1450.

## Iconography
A standing woman holds a crescent [[motif:crescent|moon]] aloft, against the workshop's gold ground. There are none of the Marseille card's baying dogs, towers, or crayfish; the early Moon, like the Star and Sun beside it, is reduced to a single courtly figure presenting the heavenly body.

## Significance
The simplicity of the fifteenth-century celestial trumps throws into relief the elaborate symbolism added later. Where the Tarot de Marseille reads the Moon as a nocturnal landscape of instinct and illusion, and the occultists made it the card of the unconscious, the Visconti image is iconographically neutral — evidence that the rich 'traditional' meanings are accretions of the printed and esoteric eras rather than original to the deck.

## For artists & game designers
A single-figure lunar emblem — pairs with the matching Sun and Star cards as a clean celestial trio.

title: The Pope — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (V)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo / Antonio Cicognara (attrib.)
summary:
The Pope, trump V of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, a Milanese hand-painted card of c. 1450 (Pierpont Morgan group, with later restorations attributed to Cicognara).

## Iconography
The Pope sits enthroned in the triple [[motif:papal tiara|tiara]], one hand raised in benediction, against the workshop gold ground. He is the spiritual counterpart to the Emperor, the highest of the worldly authority-trumps, paired in the sequence with the Papess.

## Significance
That a fifteenth-century court deck should include a Pope and a Papess (the future High Priestess) is historically striking and was later cited in debates over the deck's symbolism. In the trump hierarchy the Pope embodies the Church's authority above the temporal rulers. The card descends, through the Marseille *Pape*, into the occultists' 'Hierophant.' Its presence underlines that the trumps began as a ladder of social and cosmic estates, not an esoteric cipher.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'high priest / pontiff' figure — tiara, blessing hand — for an ecclesiastical authority card.

title: The Star — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (XVII)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
The Star, trump XVII of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, a hand-painted Milanese card of c. 1450.

## Iconography
A robed woman stands holding a great eight-pointed [[motif:eight-pointed star|star]] aloft in her hand, against the gold ground of the Visconti-Sforza workshop. The early Star lacks the nude water-pourer and stream of the later Marseille card; here she is a dignified courtly figure simply presenting the star.

## Significance
The celestial trumps — Star, Moon, Sun — already form a sequence in the fifteenth-century deck, but their imagery is far simpler than the elaborate symbolism the Tarot de Marseille and, later, the occultists would attach. The Visconti card shows the trump in an early, unfixed state, before Court de Gébelin and Lévi read it as the pouring goddess of hope and astral influence. It is prime evidence for how much later 'tradition' was in fact eighteenth-century invention.

## For artists & game designers
A minimalist 'star' card — one figure, one star — useful where the busy Marseille composition would be too much.

title: The Sun — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (XIX)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
The Sun, trump XIX of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, hand-painted at Milan c. 1450.

## Iconography
A winged child (*putto*) floats among clouds bearing a great radiant [[motif:radiant sun|sun]] with a human face, set against the tooled gold ground. The composition differs sharply from the Marseille Sun, which shows two children before a wall beneath the rayed star; the early card gives a single celestial spirit carrying the sun across the heavens.

## Significance
The Sun belongs to the deck's celestial triad and, like its fellows, appears here in an iconography not yet codified. The bearing-putto motif links the card to Renaissance personifications of the planets and to the cosmological imagery of the courtly workshops. The contrast with later patterns is a textbook case of how tarot's 'fixed' symbolism actually shifted from deck to deck over three centuries.

## For artists & game designers
A winged spirit carrying the sun — a clean heraldic 'daylight / clarity' icon, gold-ground luxe.

title: Temperance — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (XIV)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
Temperance, trump XIV of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, a Milanese hand-painted card of c. 1450.

## Iconography
A woman pours liquid between two vessels, the gesture of [[motif:tempering wine|tempering]] (mixing water with wine), the classic emblem of the cardinal virtue Temperance, against the gold ground. She is one of three virtue-trumps in the deck, alongside Fortitude and Justice.

## Significance
Temperance is one of the four cardinal virtues that appear among the trumps, embedding a scheme of moral philosophy in the card sequence. Its inclusion shows the trumps as a visual catechism of medieval-Renaissance ethics as much as a game. The pouring-vessels image passed almost unchanged into the Tarot de Marseille and thence to every later deck, making it one of the most stable of all tarot icons across five centuries.

## For artists & game designers
The water-into-wine pouring gesture is the universal 'balance / moderation' emblem — instantly legible without text.

title: The Wheel of Fortune — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (X)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
The Wheel of Fortune, trump X of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, a Milanese hand-painted card of c. 1450.

## Iconography
A great wheel turns, ridden by figures who rise, reign, and fall — typically labelled in the medieval scheme *regnabo* (I shall reign), *regno* (I reign), *regnavi* (I have reigned), *sum sine regno* (I am without rule). A blindfolded [[motif:fortuna|Fortuna]] often turns the hub. The image sits on the workshop gold ground.

## Significance
The trump directly transmits the medieval *Rota Fortunae* — the goddess Fortune raising and casting down kings, an image canonised by Boethius's *Consolation of Philosophy* and ubiquitous in Gothic art. Its presence shows the trumps drawing on the standard moral-allegorical repertoire of the late Middle Ages. The Marseille pattern would later replace the human riders with hybrid animal figures and a crowned sphinx.

## For artists & game designers
The medieval four-figure Wheel (rise/reign/fall/ruin) is a richly legible 'cycle of fortune' diagram for a luck or fate mechanic.

title: The World — Visconti-Sforza Tarot (XXI)
work: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: Bonifacio Bembo (attrib.)
summary:
The World, highest trump of the Visconti-Sforza tarot, hand-painted for the Milanese court c. 1450 and attributed to Bonifacio Bembo.

## Iconography
Two winged *putti* hold aloft a great orb within which floats a walled city above a sea, crowned by a star. The image is laid over a tooled [[motif:gold ground|gold ground]] in the luxurious International Gothic manner. Unlike the later Marseille World, there is no dancing figure or four evangelist-beasts: the early card shows the cosmos itself, the earthly city held up beneath the heavens.

## Significance
The Visconti-Sforza deck is among the earliest and finest surviving *trionfi*, a bespoke luxury pack made for the rulers of Milan long before any documented divinatory use. As the culminating trump, the World signified completion and the totality of creation. Its iconography of a city-in-a-sphere shows how malleable the early trump images were before printing standardised them; the climbing 'four beasts' World of the Marseille pattern is a later development.

## For artists & game designers
A 'completion / cosmos' capstone card distinct from the modern dancing-figure World — useful as a luxury, gold-ground alternative.

title: Aaron invested with the breastplate (Hoshen)
work: The Temple of Solomon & Sacred Architecture as a Model of the Cosmos
creator: Anonymous (Rijksmuseum impression)
summary:
An engraving showing Aaron, the first high priest, being vested with the jeweled breastplate (*Hoshen*) of his sacred office.

## Iconography
Aaron stands in the full priestly regalia — the long robe, the ephod, the turban — as attendants fasten the square gemmed breastplate over his heart. The scene stages the moment of investiture, when an ordinary man is clothed into the role of mediator between Israel and God.

## Significance
The high priest's vestments were read by Philo and Josephus as an image of the universe: the blue robe the heaven, the bells its harmonies, and the twelve-stoned [[motif:hoshen|breastplate]] the zodiac borne over the heart. To robe Aaron was to robe him in the cosmos, so that the one who entered the sanctuary carried creation itself before the divine presence. Christian Hebraists and kabbalists alike dwelt on this priestly microcosm.

## For artists & game designers
An investiture scene — ordinary figure transformed by donning a coded garment — is a strong visual for a character gaining a sacred class or role.

citations.text: Philo, *De vita Mosis* II; Josephus, *Jewish Antiquities* III.7.

title: The Temple of Solomon in Adrichom's plan of Jerusalem
work: The Temple of Solomon & Sacred Architecture as a Model of the Cosmos
creator: Christian van Adrichom
summary:
A detail of the Temple of Solomon excerpted from Christian van Adrichom's celebrated large-scale plan of ancient Jerusalem.

## Iconography
The temple precinct is drawn in tilted bird's-eye view: a walled rectangular court with corner towers enclosing ranked buildings and the sanctuary, lettered keys tying each structure to a printed legend. It is cartography rather than elevation — the holy house located within the surveyed city.

## Significance
Adrichom's *Theatrum Terrae Sanctae* (1584) reconstructed first-century Jerusalem as a numbered, legible map, and his temple became a standard reference image copied for two centuries. The project expresses the antiquarian conviction that the [[motif:temple-of-solomon|sacred topography]] of scripture could be recovered as exact, locatable space — the holy city as data.

## For artists & game designers
A keyed, lettered precinct plan shows how to make a sacred site navigable: every building indexed to a legend the player can consult.

citations.text: C. van Adrichom, *Theatrum Terrae Sanctae et Biblicarum Historiarum* (1584).

title: The Ark and cherubim in the Holy of Holies
work: The Temple of Solomon & Sacred Architecture as a Model of the Cosmos
creator: Anonymous (in 'The Rites and Worship of the Jews', 1890)
rights: No known copyright restrictions. Via Wikimedia Commons (Internet Archive Book Images).
summary:
A wood-engraving of the Ark of the Covenant overshadowed by the great cherubim of the inner sanctuary.

## Iconography
The golden Ark stands beneath two towering winged cherubim whose wings meet overhead to canopy the mercy seat. In Solomon's Temple these guardian figures were colossal — some fifteen feet tall — their wingspans stretching from wall to wall of the windowless cubic [[motif:holy-of-holies|Holy of Holies]].

## Significance
The cherubim were the temple's living throne-bearers, derived from the winged composite guardians of the ancient Near East.
