The page is written entirely in an exotic-looking script of abstract symbols, Greek and Roman letters, and diacritics, with no plain text apart from the word 'Copiales 3' that gave the manuscript its name. The dense, calligraphic ciphertext resembles an arcane alphabet.

## Significance
The Copiale Cipher is one of cryptography's great success stories. In 2011 Kevin Knight, with Beáta Megyesi and Christiane Schaefer, broke its [[motif:homophonic substitution|homophonic substitution]] using machine-translation techniques, revealing a German-language ritual book of an eighteenth-century [[motif:secret society|secret society]] preoccupied with eye-surgery symbolism and initiation. It stands as the counter-example to the Voynich: a comparably forbidding enciphered manuscript that modern computational cryptanalysis did solve.

## For artists & game designers
A solvable cipher prop: a beautiful abstract alphabet that, unlike the Voynich, can actually be cracked to deliver a real reward.

citations.text: Kevin Knight, Beáta Megyesi & Christiane Schaefer, 'The Copiale Cipher' (2011).

title: The Dorabella Cipher (Edward Elgar, 1897)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Edward Elgar
summary:
The Dorabella Cipher, a short enciphered note the composer Edward Elgar sent in 1897, still unsolved.

## Iconography
Three lines of an invented shorthand: a string of squiggles each made of one, two, or three semicircular pen-strokes set at varying angles, like rotated letter 'E's or fragments of arcs. Eighty-seven characters in all, with no spacing into obvious words.

## Significance
Elgar sent the note to Dora Penny ('Dorabella'), a young friend, who never managed to read it; it has resisted cryptanalysts ever since. The glyphs appear to be a [[motif:rotated glyphs|rotation cipher]] of a small alphabet of arcs, but the message — too short to give cryptanalysts much purchase — remains undeciphered. Elgar's fondness for puzzles (he also embedded the 'Enigma' in his *Variations*) makes the cipher a beloved minor mystery of [[motif:secret writing|secret writing]].

## For artists & game designers
A tiny rotation-cipher font: arcs at set angles you can use for short, crackable in-game notes.

citations.text: Jerrold Northrop Moore, *Edward Elgar: A Creative Life* (1984).

title: The Holy Table of John Dee (Enochian Tables of Practice)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: John Dee (after his angelic 'actions' with Edward Kelley)
summary:
A rendering of the Holy Table of John Dee, the engraved table of practice bordered and filled with the characters of his 'angelic' Enochian system.

## Iconography
A square table-top is inscribed around its rim with a band of Enochian letters and, across its surface, a grid of small squares each holding a character or number; the central field would have held the wax [[motif:sigillum dei aemeth|Sigillum Dei Aemeth]]. The whole functions as a ritual instrument keyed to Dee's angelic alphabet.

## Significance
John Dee (1527–1608/9), Elizabeth I's mathematician and astrologer, recorded with the scryer Edward Kelley an entire 'angelic' language — [[motif:enochian table|Enochian]] — delivered through grids of letters that the angels supposedly dictated cell by cell, often backwards as a safeguard. Whether mystical revelation or an elaborate constructed cipher, Dee's lettered tables sit squarely in the tradition of [[motif:secret writing|secret writing]] and angelic cryptography that runs from Trithemius onward.

## For artists & game designers
A glyph-grid altar: a board whose squares the player fills letter-by-letter to 'summon' an effect, with reversed entry as a built-in safety puzzle.

citations.text: Deborah E. Harkness, *John Dee's Conversations with Angels* (1999).

title: The Pigpen (Masonic) Cipher Grid
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Masonic / Rosicrucian tradition)
summary:
A key to the Pigpen or Masonic cipher, the geometric grid-substitution code long used in Masonic and Rosicrucian circles.

## Iconography
The diagram shows the construction grids — a pair of tic-tac-toe grids and a pair of X-shaped grids, some dotted — from whose cells each letter takes the shape of the lines (and dots) enclosing it. The resulting 'alphabet' is a set of angular, gate-like glyphs.

## Significance
The [[motif:pigpen cipher|pigpen cipher]] (also 'Freemason's cipher') is a simple monoalphabetic [[motif:grid substitution|grid substitution]] in which letters are replaced by the fragment of grid surrounding them. Used by Freemasons to keep records private and engraved on Masonic gravestones, it appears across [[motif:rosicrucian code|Rosicrucian]] and fraternal contexts. Easily broken, it survives more as a badge of secrecy than as serious cryptography.

## For artists & game designers
The most legible 'secret alphabet' to letter signage with: instantly readable as 'hidden writing' and trivially decodable by an engaged player.

title: The Rohonc Codex (Undeciphered Hungarian Manuscript)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous
summary:
Pages from the Rohonc Codex, an undeciphered manuscript in an unknown script held by the Hungarian Academy of Sciences.

## Iconography
The spread shows dense rows of an unfamiliar, hooked alphabet — far more symbols than any natural script needs — interspersed with simple line illustrations of apparently religious scenes (crosses, figures, churches). The text appears to run right-to-left and bottom-to-top.

## Significance
The Rohonc Codex is the Voynich's eastern cousin: a roughly 450-page book in an [[motif:unknown alphabet|unknown script]], surfacing in nineteenth-century Hungary, that has resisted decipherment. Theories range from a genuine devotional text in a cipher or invented alphabet, to a forgery by the antiquarian Sámuel Literáti Nemes. Recent work by Levente Zoltán Király and Gábor Tokai argues it is an enciphered religious compilation, but no reading is universally accepted.

## For artists & game designers
Another fully-formed mystery script with matching pictures — a model for a 'found' sacred book in an invented faith.

citations.text: Levente Zoltán Király & Gábor Tokai, studies of the Rohonc Codex (2018–2020).

title: The Shepherd's Monument, Shugborough (with the O.U.O.S.V.A.V.V. Inscription)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (after Poussin); Shugborough Estate
summary:
The Shepherd's Monument at Shugborough Hall, Staffordshire, carrying the unsolved O·U·O·S·V·A·V·V cipher inscription.

## Iconography
A carved stone relief — a mirror-reversed version of Poussin's *Et in Arcadia Ego*, in which shepherds gather at a tomb — is set into a rustic arch. Beneath it runs the enigmatic sequence of letters **O U O S V A V V**, framed by a **D** and **M**, that has never been satisfactorily decoded.

## Significance
The [[motif:shepherds monument|Shepherd's Monument]] (c. 1748–1763) pairs an Arcadian tomb scene with a [[motif:cipher inscription|letter cipher]] whose meaning has eluded everyone from casual visitors to professional codebreakers, and even attracted Grail and Templar speculation. Proposed readings — a Latin devotional acrostic, a lover's memorial — remain conjectural, making it Britain's most famous monumental cipher.

## For artists & game designers
A landmark puzzle object: an outdoor carving whose letters seed a quest, with the reversed-painting motif as a built-in visual clue.

citations.text: A. J. Morton, *The Shugborough Inscription* (research surveys, 2010s).

title: Trithemius, *Polygraphiae* (1518) — First Printed Book on Cryptology
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Johannes Trithemius
summary:
A copy of Johannes Trithemius's *Polygraphiae* (1518), held at the National Cryptologic Museum and often called the first printed book on cryptology.

## Iconography
The open volume shows the printed [[motif:cipher table|cipher tables]] for which the work is famous: long columns of substitute words and Latin-prayer alphabets by which a message is enciphered into seemingly pious text, prefiguring the *tabula recta* of the polyalphabetic cipher.

## Significance
*Polygraphiae libri sex* was published posthumously in 1518 and is widely regarded as the first printed book wholly devoted to [[motif:polygraphy|cryptography]]. Its tables — including the famous 'Ave Maria' cipher that hides letters inside columns of devotional words — and the recta table later developed by Bellaso and Vigenère made Trithemius a founder of Western cryptology, the respectable twin of his disguised *Steganographia*.

## For artists & game designers
A period-accurate codebook prop: real Renaissance cipher tables a player can use to encode or crack messages in a historical setting.

citations.text: Johannes Trithemius, *Polygraphiae libri sex* (1518); David Kahn, *The Codebreakers* (1967).

title: Steganographic Chart in the Hand of John Dee (Trithemius's *Steganographia*)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Johannes Trithemius; chart copied by John Dee
summary:
A cryptographic chart relating to Johannes Trithemius's *Steganographia*, written out in the hand of the English magus John Dee.

## Iconography
The chart sets out a grid of names, letters, and tabulated correspondences in a careful sixteenth-century hand. Trithemius's *Steganographia* presents its cryptography under the guise of conjuring angels and spirits: tables of barbarous spirit-names that, decoded by the hidden key, yield ordinary enciphered messages.

## Significance
Trithemius's *Steganographia* (written 1499, printed 1606) is the foundational work of [[motif:steganography|steganography]] — the art of hiding the very existence of a message. Its disguise as a manual of [[motif:angelic names|angel magic]] was so convincing that the book was placed on the Index and read for centuries as a genuine grimoire, until scholars showed the 'spirit names' were a cover for a real cipher. That John Dee copied this chart links the cipher tradition directly to Renaissance occult philosophy.

## For artists & game designers
The perfect 'spell that is really a code': a page of conjuration that, read correctly, hides a plain-text clue.

citations.text: Johannes Trithemius, *Steganographia* (comp. 1499, pr. 1606); Noel L. Brann, *Trithemius and Magical Theology* (1999).

title: Voynich Manuscript, Balneological (Bathing Nymphs) Page
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408)
summary:
A page from the 'balneological' or biological section of the Voynich Manuscript, crowded with small nude female figures in green pools and tubes.

## Iconography
The page teems with tiny nude women standing in basins and barrels linked by a plumbing of pipes and channels through which a greenish liquid seems to flow; clusters of stars and looping conduits connect the pools, all annotated in the unreadable script. The effect is part bath-house, part anatomical or alchemical schematic.

## Significance
The [[motif:nymphs in tubes|biological section]] is the strangest part of the Voynich, variously read as a treatise on women's medicine and balneology (the healing use of baths), as an allegory of the body's inner vessels, or as an alchemical scheme of fluids. The interconnected tubes have invited comparisons to anatomy, to the bath-cures of medieval medicine, and to the *vasa* of alchemical circulation — none confirmed.

## For artists & game designers
A plumbing puzzle made flesh: a network of pools and pipes whose flow the player reroutes, each chamber occupied by a figure that reacts to the current.

citations.text: Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (Yale University Press, 2016).

title: Voynich Manuscript, Herbal Page (Unidentified Plant)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408)
summary:
A page from the 'herbal' section of the Voynich Manuscript, showing one of the codex's many unidentified plants beside its undeciphered text.

## Iconography
A single plant fills the page: a coloured drawing of stem, leaves, and an exaggerated root system, surrounded and labelled by lines of the manuscript's enigmatic script. The largest section of the book, the herbal pages each pair one plant with a block of writing in the manner of a medieval herbal — yet almost none of the species can be securely identified with a real plant.

## Significance
The Voynich Manuscript (Yale, Beinecke Rare Book Library, MS 408) is the most famous undeciphered book in the world. Radiocarbon dating of its vellum places it c. 1404–1438. The herbal section apes the form of the medieval and early-modern [[motif:herbal diagram|herbal]] tradition, but its plants are botanical fantasies or so stylised as to defy identification, deepening the puzzle of whether the book encodes real knowledge, an unknown language, or an elaborate cipher or hoax.

## For artists & game designers
A template for an alien field-guide: realistic herbal layout filled with invented species and an unreadable script the player must slowly decode.

citations.text: Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (Yale University Press, 2016).

title: Voynich Manuscript, Pharmaceutical Container (Folio 89v)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408); digitised by Yale University Library
summary:
A drawn 'pharmaceutical' container from folio 89v of the Voynich Manuscript, one of the ornate jars that line the apothecary section.

## Iconography
The image isolates a single tall, decorated vessel — drawn like a glazed apothecary jar or *albarello* with a banded foot and lid — of the kind set in columns down the margins of the pharmaceutical pages, alongside detached roots and leaves and short blocks of labelled text.

## Significance
The [[motif:pharmaceutical jar|pharmaceutical section]] of the Voynich shows rows of elaborate jars beside fragments of plants, exactly as a medieval *materia medica* or apothecary's manual would pair drug-containers with their ingredients. The vessels' resemblance to fifteenth-century maiolica pharmacy jars has been used to help date and place the manuscript, even as the recipes they imply remain unreadable.

## For artists & game designers
An inventory of crafting vessels: ornate containers each tied to a (currently unknown) ingredient and effect — perfect labelled loot icons.

citations.text: Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (Yale University Press, 2016).

title: Voynich Manuscript, 'Recipes' Text Page (Folio 107r Detail)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408)
summary:
A detail from folio 107r of the Voynich Manuscript, in the dense 'recipes' or 'stars' section where short paragraphs are each marked by a marginal star.

## Iconography
The crop shows tight, almost unbroken lines of the manuscript's looping script, divided into short paragraphs each tagged in the margin by a small drawn star. With few illustrations, these pages read as a list of brief entries — hence the nickname 'recipes.'

## Significance
The [[motif:recipe text|recipes section]] (sometimes called the 'stars' pages) is the most purely textual part of the codex, its star-marked paragraphs suggesting a series of short prescriptions or instructions. Statistical analysis of these pages has fed every theory of the script — natural language, cipher, glossolalia, or meaningless hoax — without producing an accepted reading.

## For artists & game designers
A scroll of cryptic recipes: star-bulleted entries the player collects and must decode to unlock craftable effects.

citations.text: Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (Yale University Press, 2016).

title: Voynich Manuscript, Cosmological Foldout (Folio 86v Detail)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408)
summary:
A detail from the great cosmological foldout of the Voynich Manuscript (folio 86v), part of the nine-rosette 'map' that unfolds across several leaves.

## Iconography
Large circular rosettes — elaborate roundels filled with cells, spokes, towers, and looping ornament — are joined to neighbouring circles by causeways and tubes, the whole reading like a schematic map of linked places. One rosette shows what look like castellated towers; others hold radiating patterns and rings of script.

## Significance
The nine-rosette [[motif:rosette map|cosmological foldout]] is the manuscript's single largest and most ambitious diagram, spread over a folding sheet. It has been interpreted as a cosmic map, an itinerary of cities, or a diagram of the elements and spheres; the apparent towers on one rosette have even prompted attempts to locate a real place. Like every other page, its purpose is unproven.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made world map: linked circular regions joined by causeways, each rosette an explorable hub on a fold-out overworld.

citations.text: Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (Yale University Press, 2016).

title: Voynich Manuscript, Detail of Folio 72r (Zodiac Figure)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408); digitised by Jason Davies
summary:
A detail from folio 72r of the Voynich Manuscript, showing the central figure of one of the astronomical zodiac medallions.

## Iconography
The close-up isolates the heart of a zodiac wheel: a central roundel containing a zodiacal emblem, framed by the inner ring of compartments and curving labels. Folios 70–73 of the manuscript carry the run of zodiac diagrams; this detail from f72r lets the central device be read apart from its crowded rings of figures.

## Significance
Isolating the medallion shows how the Voynich follows the convention of placing a sign's emblem at the hub of a [[motif:calendar wheel|calendar wheel]]. The folios run through the zodiac from Pisces and Aries onward, several split across paired diagrams. As with every page, the script naming the figure resists all attempts at decipherment, from William Friedman's wartime study groups to modern computational analysis.

## For artists & game designers
A zoomable map-pin: the central emblem of a wheel that expands into a full ringed minigame when selected.

citations.text: Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (Yale University Press, 2016).

title: Voynich Manuscript, Script Detail (Folio 1r)
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408)
summary:
A close detail of the Voynich Manuscript's opening folio (1r), showing the looping, fluent script that no one has been able to read.

## Iconography
The retouched crop isolates a few lines of 'Voynichese': a flowing hand of perhaps two dozen recurring glyphs, written confidently left-to-right with consistent ligatures and word-like groupings, but corresponding to no known alphabet or language.

## Significance
The Voynich [[motif:undeciphered script|script]] is the crux of the whole mystery. Its statistics behave partly like natural language (it obeys word-frequency laws) and partly unlike it, fuelling rival theories — an unknown language, a cipher, an artificial 'glossolalia,' or a meaningless fabrication. From William and Elizebeth Friedman to modern information theorists, the best cryptanalysts of the twentieth century failed to break it, which is why it anchors the tradition of unsolved [[motif:secret writing|secret writing]].

## For artists & game designers
A constructed alphabet ready to copy: a coherent glyph set you can letter signs and scrolls with for an instantly 'unreadable' world.

citations.text: William F. Friedman & Elizebeth S. Friedman, studies of the Voynich script (1940s–50s); Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (2016).

title: Voynich Manuscript, Zodiac Wheel with Star-Holding Nymphs
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408)
summary:
Another zodiac folio of the Voynich Manuscript, its central medallion encircled by rings of small figures each grasping a star.

## Iconography
The page is organised as a wheel: a central emblem inside a roundel, surrounded by two or more concentric rings divided into compartments, each holding a small figure — frequently a nude woman emerging from a tub or barrel and holding a star aloft. Curving lines of the manuscript's script run along the rings and around the rim.

## Significance
The star-holding [[motif:star-holding nymphs|nymphs]] of the Voynich zodiac wheels are among the codex's most distinctive images, fusing the astronomical diagram with the bathing-figure motif of the 'biological' section. Scholars such as Nick Pelling and the contributors to the Beinecke's facsimile have noted resemblances to fifteenth-century German calendar and astrological-medical traditions, without resolving the meaning of the text.

## For artists & game designers
A constellation-assembly mechanic: figures handing up stars to populate a sky, each ring a tier of difficulty or season.

citations.text: Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (Yale University Press, 2016).

title: Voynich Manuscript, Astronomical / Zodiac Medallion Page
work: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: Anonymous (Beinecke MS 408)
summary:
A page from the 'astronomical' or zodiac section of the Voynich Manuscript, built around a central medallion ringed by small human figures.

## Iconography
A large circular diagram dominates the page: a central roundel containing a zodiacal emblem, encircled by concentric bands in which dozens of small nude or robed figures — many holding stars — stand in compartments, each with a brief label in the unreadable script. The design recalls a calendar wheel keyed to a sign of the zodiac.

## Significance
The Voynich [[motif:zodiac medallion|zodiac]] folios pair each sign with rings of star-holding 'nymphs,' a scheme with loose parallels in late-medieval astronomical and calendrical manuscripts. The labels around the rim have encouraged speculation that they name days, degrees, or astrological influences, but like the rest of the book they remain undeciphered.

## For artists & game designers
A living calendar wheel: a rotating zodiac dial whose ringed figures could each trigger a different seasonal event or astral effect.

citations.text: Raymond Clemens (ed.), *The Voynich Manuscript* (Yale University Press, 2016).

title: The Bewitched Groom
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Hans Baldung Grien
summary:
Hans Baldung Grien's late woodcut *The Bewitched Groom* (c. 1544), a tour-de-force of foreshortening and one of art's most enigmatic witchcraft images.

## Iconography
A groom lies sprawled on his back in dramatic foreshortening across a stable floor, a currycomb and pitchfork beside him. A great horse looms over him, and an old hag thrusts a flaming torch through a window at upper right. Whether the man is bewitched, dead, or dreaming is left unresolved.

## Significance
The print is read as a meditation on the artist's own mortality and on the disordering power of the [[motif:hag at window|witch]] — the hag intruding from outside the rational world. The Stocker coat of arms on the wall and Baldung's monogram have fed interpretations as a self-portrait-in-allegory. Scholars such as Linda Hults treat it as the summa of Baldung's witch imagery.

## For artists & game designers
Extreme foreshortening of a fallen figure, viewed from its feet, instantly conveys collapse, death, or supernatural defeat.

citations.text: Linda C. Hults, *The Witch as Muse* (2005).
Metropolitan Museum of Art, object record.

title: The Witches (Witches' Sabbath)
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Hans Baldung Grien
summary:
Hans Baldung Grien's *The Witches* (1510), a celebrated chiaroscuro woodcut and one of the defining images of the witches' sabbath.

## Iconography
Nude witches cluster around a steaming pot from which fumes and a forked stick rise; one rides a goat backwards through the air above. Bones, a raised covered dish belching vapour, and wildly streaming hair fill the scene. The tone block lends the print a lurid, nocturnal glow.

## Significance
Baldung, a pupil of Dürer, fused eroticism, the grotesque, and learned demonology into the era's most influential [[motif:witches sabbath|sabbath]] image. The cooking pot and rising vapours allude to weather magic and the ointments said to enable flight. Linda Hults and Charles Zika emphasize how Baldung's witches express anxieties about female sexuality and disorder as much as any literal belief.

## For artists & game designers
Vapour as a compositional device — smoke that carries objects and figures upward — turns a static coven into a scene of active, churning magic.

citations.text: Linda C. Hults, *The Witch as Muse* (2005).

title: The Four Witches
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Albrecht Dürer's engraving of 1497, traditionally called *The Four Witches*, showing four nude women gathered in a chamber.

## Iconography
Four idealized nude women stand in a tight circle; a small devil with flames peers through a doorway at lower left, and a skull and bone lie on the floor. A sphere overhead bears the date 1497 and Dürer's AD monogram. The women's purpose is deliberately ambiguous — the title is a later tradition rather than Dürer's own.

## Significance
The print fuses the classical motif of the [[motif:nude women|grouped nudes]] (echoing the Three Graces or a Judgement of Paris) with the menacing devil and bones that invite a witchcraft reading. It exemplifies how Dürer translated humanist study of the nude into images charged with moral and demonic suggestion. Art historians continue to debate whether the four are witches, goddesses, or an allegory of discord.

## For artists & game designers
Ambiguity is the lesson: a single small demon and a skull reframe an otherwise classical group as something sinister.

citations.text: Erwin Panofsky, *The Life and Art of Albrecht Dürer* (1943).

title: Witch Riding Backwards on a Goat
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Albrecht Dürer
summary:
Albrecht Dürer's engraving (c. 1500–01) of an old witch riding backwards on a he-goat, hair streaming against the wind, accompanied by tumbling putti.

## Iconography
A wizened, naked woman sits astride a horned goat facing its tail, brandishing a distaff. Her hair and the goat's coat blow forward — opposite to her direction of travel — signalling that she generates her own storm. Four winged putti scramble below; Dürer's monogram appears reversed, reinforcing the theme of inversion.

## Significance
The [[motif:goat|goat]] is the Devil's beast and the witch's mount; riding it *backwards*, against the wind, encodes the world-turned-upside-down logic of witchcraft. The distaff marks the figure as a woman whose domestic tool has become an instrument of malefice. Charles Zika reads the print as crystallizing the gendered, weather-making witch of German art.

## For artists & game designers
Reversed motion — hair and cloth blowing the 'wrong' way — is a subtle visual cue that a figure bends natural law.

citations.text: Linda C. Hults, *The Witch as Muse* (2005).

title: A Witches' Kitchen
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Frans Francken the Younger
summary:
*A Witches' Kitchen* by Frans Francken the Younger (early 17th century), a crowded cabinet painting of witches at their preparations.

## Iconography
In a vaulted interior lit by candle and fire, witches young and old mix potions, consult open magical books, and tend a cauldron amid skulls, bones, and oddments of the occult. Demons and monstrous creatures lurk in the shadows; figures stream up toward a chimney for the flight to the sabbath.

## Significance
The Antwerp 'witches' kitchen' became a popular cabinet-picture genre that let collectors enjoy the demonic as exotic spectacle. Francken assembles an inventory of magical apparatus — the [[motif:grimoire|book]], cauldron, and skulls — that codified what a witch's workshop should contain. The genre reflects both real demonological literature and the art market's taste for the marvelous.

## For artists & game designers
A single room packed with labelled occult props — book, cauldron, skull, candle — is an efficient way to establish a 'witch's workshop' location at a glance.

citations.text: Jane P. Davidson, *The Witch in Northern European Art* (1987).

title: Buen viaje (Bon Voyage), Caprichos 64
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Francisco Goya
summary:
Plate 64 of Goya's *Los Caprichos* (1799), *Buen viaje* ("Bon voyage"), depicting a winged demonic figure speeding off into the night with companions.

## Iconography
A cloaked, winged being launches through pitch darkness, trailed by a knot of indistinct flying figures. The aquatint plunges most of the sheet into black, isolating the airborne shapes against the void.

## Significance
The plate belongs to the cluster of *Caprichos* (60–66) devoted to witches and demons departing for or returning from nocturnal mischief. Its ironic farewell salutes the [[motif:night flight|night-flight]] of malign spirits — Goya using the rich blacks of aquatint to evoke the unknowable dark into which superstition projects its terrors. As with the series generally, the target is human folly, not real demons.

## For artists & game designers
Near-total darkness with only edges of a figure catching light is a powerful way to render menace 'departing' — the unseen is scarier than the shown.

citations.text: Metropolitan Museum of Art, object record.

title: Linda maestra! (Pretty Teacher!), Caprichos 68
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Francisco Goya
summary:
Plate 68 of Goya's *Los Caprichos* (1799), *Linda maestra!* ("Pretty teacher!"), showing an old witch and a young one flying together on a single broom.

## Iconography
Two nude witches, one aged and one youthful, ride a broomstick through a dark sky; an owl flies alongside. The ironic title casts the hag as a 'teacher' instructing her pupil — the broom-flight as a lesson in vice.

## Significance
The plate satirizes procuresses and the corruption of the young under the guise of [[motif:broomstick flight|witch initiation]]. Like the rest of the *Caprichos*, it uses the inherited iconography of the night-flying witch as a vehicle for moral and political commentary rather than belief. The pairing of old instructress and young initiate became one of Goya's most reproduced witch designs.

## For artists & game designers
Pairing a mentor and apprentice on one mount compresses a whole relationship — teaching, corruption, lineage — into a single silhouette.

title: Volaverunt (They Have Flown), Caprichos 61
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Francisco Goya
summary:
Plate 61 of Francisco Goya's *Los Caprichos* (1799), *Volaverunt* ("They have flown"), a satirical etching of bewitched flight.

## Iconography
A serene young woman rises into the air, balanced on the heads of three crouching, bat-cloaked figures, a butterfly-like shape at her own head. She floats with eerie composure against a blank aquatint sky.

## Significance
Goya turns the old [[motif:flight|witch-flight]] into Enlightenment satire: the image mocks credulity, vanity, and the giddy 'flightiness' of fashionable society as much as any literal witchcraft. The *Caprichos* deploy witches and demons as instruments of social and political critique, a key moment when witch imagery is repurposed against superstition itself. Scholars read *Volaverunt* alongside Goya's other sabbath and flight plates as ironic, not credulous.

## For artists & game designers
Goya shows that supernatural levitation reads most uncannily when the figure is calm and the background empty — restraint, not spectacle.

citations.text: Janis Tomlinson, *Goya: A Portrait of the Artist* (2020).

title: Matthew Hopkins, Witch Finder Generall
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Anonymous (publ. Richard Royston)
summary:
The woodcut frontispiece to Matthew Hopkins's *The Discovery of Witches* (1647), in which the self-styled "Witch Finder Generall" presides over two confessing witches and their named familiar spirits.

## Iconography
Hopkins stands at left in cloak and hat; two seated old women name their familiars, which are depicted as small animal imps with grotesque labels — "Holt," "Jarmara," "Vinegar Tom" (a greyhound with an ox's head), "Sack & Sugar," "Newes," "Pyewacket," and others. Each creature is captioned.

## Significance
The image documents the distinctively English theory of the witch's [[motif:familiars|familiar]] — an animal imp suckled at the witch's mark — that drove Hopkins's East Anglian campaign of 1645–47, the deadliest in English history. The labelled menagerie turned demonology into something like a folk bestiary and remains the iconic image of the English witch-hunt.

## For artists & game designers
Naming and captioning each familiar creature individualizes a coven's 'pets' and is a tidy template for a companion/summon roster.

title: The Burning of Witches (Malleus Maleficarum tradition)
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Anonymous
summary:
A woodcut of witches being burned, reproduced in connection with the *Malleus Maleficarum* (Montague Summers's edition), representing the lethal end of the witch trial.

## Iconography
Condemned witches are consumed by flames at the stake while officials and onlookers attend. The crude woodcut belongs to the broadsheet tradition that illustrated executions for a popular audience.

## Significance
The *Malleus Maleficarum* ("Hammer of Witches," 1486/87) by Heinrich Kramer codified the procedures of witch-hunting and, with the new printing press, spread the persecuting mentality across Europe. Execution woodcuts like this one supplied the public face of that machinery — the [[motif:burning at the stake|stake]] as the inevitable conclusion of accusation. (This particular plate is a later reproduction associated with the text rather than an original 1487 title-page.)

## For artists & game designers
The stake-and-crowd composition is the visual shorthand for institutional persecution — the human cost behind the more decorative sabbath fantasies.

title: Witches in Flight (Molitor, De lamiis)
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Anonymous woodcutter, for Ulrich Molitor
summary:
A woodcut from Ulrich Molitor's *De lamiis et phitonicis mulieribus* ("On Witches and Diviner Women," first printed 1489), depicting witches in flight, partly transformed into animals.

## Iconography
Figures ride through the air on a forked cooking-stick; their heads have become those of beasts, illustrating the belief that witches travelled to the sabbath transformed or in animal guise. The crude woodcut line is typical of early German incunabula.

## Significance
Molitor's treatise was one of the first printed books on witchcraft and the first to be illustrated, so its woodcuts became the foundational visual vocabulary for the witch. Notably, Molitor himself was sceptical that such flights and transformations were real, treating them as diabolical illusion — yet the pictures, reprinted for decades, hardened the very stereotype the text questioned, as scholars including Charles Zika have shown.

## For artists & game designers
Partial animal transformation — a human body with a beast's head mid-flight — is a vivid, readable way to depict shapeshifting in progress.

citations.text: Ulrich Molitor, *De lamiis et phitonicis mulieribus* (1489).

title: The Witches' Weigh House, Oudewater
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Photograph (Martinvl)
rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo by Martinvl). Via Wikimedia Commons.
summary:
A photograph of the historic balance scale at the *Heksenwaag* (Witches' Weigh House) in Oudewater, where suspected witches were weighed to disprove accusations.

## Iconography
The great wooden beam balance and weighing platform of the Oudewater weigh house, the institution at the centre of the Dutch 'weighing of witches'.

## Significance
Folk belief held that witches were unnaturally light — able to fly — so weighing could expose them. Uniquely, Oudewater issued official certificates that an accused person's weight was *normal*, effectively clearing them; reputedly no one weighed there was ever condemned. The [[motif:weighing of witches|weighing ordeal]] thus became, in the Dutch Republic, a tool of exoneration rather than persecution, a notable counter-current in the European witch panic. (This item is a modern photograph of the surviving site, under CC BY-SA, not a historical print.)

## For artists & game designers
A giant public balance scale is an evocative ordeal-device prop: trial by weight makes for a tense, legible game mechanic.

citations.text: Hans de Waardt, on the Oudewater Heksenwaag and Dutch witch trials.

title: Witches at their Incantations
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Salvator Rosa
summary:
Salvator Rosa's *Witches at their Incantations* (c. 1646), a horizontal nocturne of necromantic rites, now in the National Gallery, London.

## Iconography
Across a frieze-like nocturnal landscape, witches and demons perform grisly rites: a hanged corpse, a skeleton, dismembered limbs, candles, scrolls inscribed with charms, and figures bent over books and instruments. Torchlight picks out the macabre incidents against deep shadow.

## Significance
Rosa, a poet and rebel as well as painter, made witchcraft and necromancy signature themes, cultivating a reputation for the sublime and the sinister. The scattered apparatus — [[motif:scroll|charm-scrolls]], corpses, and bones — draws on classical necromancy (Lucan's Erichtho) as much as contemporary witch-belief, appealing to learned patrons' taste for the dark and erudite.

## For artists & game designers
A wide, friezelike composition lets you string many small rituals across one canvas, reading like a sequence of vignettes in a single nocturnal scene.

citations.text: Jane P. Davidson, *The Witch in Northern European Art* (1987).

title: The Witches' Sabbath (after Teniers)
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Follower of David Teniers the Younger
summary:
*The Witches' Sabbath*, a 17th-century painting by a follower of David Teniers the Younger, in the Flemish demonic-genre tradition.

## Iconography
An old witch reads from a magical book within a candlelit chamber swarming with grotesque hybrid demons, toads, and flying monsters. Smoke and apparitions rise around her as a conjuration unfolds, the picture given over to inventive monstrosity.

## Significance
Teniers and his circle made a speciality of demonic and witchcraft cabinet pictures, descending from Bosch and Bruegel. These scenes treat the [[motif:witches sabbath|sabbath]] as a stage for fantastical invention rather than documentary demonology, satisfying collectors' appetite for the bizarre. The 'follower of' attribution reflects how successful compositions were repeated by the workshop and imitators.

## For artists & game designers
The lone reader at a book surrounded by swarming small monsters reads instantly as 'summoning gone large' — quantity of creatures conveys the spell's power.

citations.text: Jane P. Davidson, *The Witch in Northern European Art* (1987).

title: Witches Riding Brooms (Les Vaudoises)
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Martin Le Franc (text); anonymous illuminator
summary:
A marginal illumination in a manuscript of Martin Le Franc's poem *Le Champion des Dames* (c. 1451), showing two women flying on a broom and a stick — among the earliest images of broomstick flight.

## Iconography
Two women in long gowns and head-coverings are borne aloft, one astride a household broom, the other on a plain wooden staff. The marginal caption labels them *Vaudoises* — "Waldensians" — the heretical sect onto which the new crime of diabolical witchcraft was being projected.

## Significance
The image predates the witch-hunt's great print era by half a century and shows the [[motif:broomstick flight|flying broom]] already fixed as the visual sign of the witch. Its identification of witches with the Waldensian heresy (*vauderie*) reflects the fifteenth-century fusion of heresy-hunting and the emerging stereotype of the night-flying sabbath, documented by historians such as Norman Cohn and Richard Kieckhefer.

## For artists & game designers
The humble broom plus an ordinary woman in everyday dress is the most economical witch signifier there is — no warts or pointed hat required.

citations.text: Martin Le Franc, *Le Champion des Dames* (c. 1440–42; ill. ms. c. 1451).

title: Description and Figure of the Witches' Sabbath
work: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: Jan Ziarnko, for Pierre de Lancre
summary:
Jan Ziarnko's large engraving *Description et figure du Sabbat des Sorciers* (1613), the fold-out plate added to Pierre de Lancre's *Tableau de l'inconstance des mauvais anges et démons*.

## Iconography
A panoramic, numbered scene of the sabbath: the horned Devil enthroned receiving homage, a banquet of the damned, a ring of dancers, a queen and great lords, children offered up, cauldrons, and witches departing through the air on brooms and beasts. A printed key letters and numbers each episode.

## Significance
De Lancre, a magistrate who conducted ferocious witch trials in the Basque Labourd, commissioned the plate to make the [[motif:witches sabbath|sabbath]] systematically visible — a near-encyclopedic catalogue of its alleged components. As Stuart Clark notes, such images lent the learned demonology of the sabbath a spurious documentary authority.

## For artists & game designers
A numbered 'map-key' overlay turns a busy scene into a legible diagram — useful when a single image must convey many simultaneous events.

citations.text: Pierre de Lancre, *Tableau de l'inconstance des mauvais anges et démons* (1613).

title: The Witches' Sabbath (Hans Baldung Grien)
work: Witchcraft & the Sabbath
creator: Hans Baldung Grien
summary:
**Hans Baldung Grien's** chiaroscuro woodcut of 1510 — the founding image of the early-modern **witch**.

## Iconography
Naked hags gather around a steaming, fuming cauldron in a night wood; one rides a goat backward through the air; bones, a fork, and conjured weather-vapours swirl around them. Baldung, a pupil of Dürer, gives the scene a learned, eroticised, deliberately disturbing charge.

## Significance
Made as the witch-hunt theory was hardening (the *Malleus Maleficarum* was a generation old), Baldung's print helped *invent the visual stereotype* — cauldron, night-flight, sabbath assembly — that print culture spread across Europe (Iconclass 13B3). It marks the moment **witchcraft becomes an image**. Charles Zika traces exactly this machinery.

## For artists & game designers
The template witches' coven: cauldron, goat, night-flight, conjured storm.

citations.text: L. Hults, 'Baldung and the Witches of Freiburg', *Art Bulletin* 69 (1987).
